whose relation he may be. I shall appoint
none but old bachelors, childless widows,
and orphans. Any man who has a relation
in the world need not apply for an office un-
der my administration. I have a few rela-
tives of my own, holding office now, but they
shall be promptly kicked out as soon as I
am elected. One brother-in-law has a little
coal office on the river. He must give it
up.   A third cousin on my neighbor’s side
drinks too much occasionally, and gets office
foot.  I shall give him notice to quit. An-
other relation has a hankering after George
Eliss’ “ Office.” It won’t do him any good.
You see I am determined to reduce the
“relative” expenses of the Government.
[end of column; start of new column]
I engage not to accept any gift, unless it
be the highest office in the gift of the peo-
ple. If over I am called “ Our present Chief
Magistrate,”-it won’t be a chief magistrate
of presents. Not being a man of command-
ing presence anyhow, there would probably
be few presents that I could command.
What few natural gifts I may have, how-
ever, I shall endeavor to retain. They are
not worth making any fuss about.
  I am not only in favor of woman’s rights,
but of woman’s rights and lefts. I am in
favor of women voting providing they vote
for me. I see no reason why a woman
should not hold an office, except, perhaps,
the difficulty of getting hold of it. Nor
should there be any bar to a woman’s accu-
mulating property and supporting the family
if she wants to.
   I may be asked how I would treat the In-
dians. I wouldn’t “ treat” them at all.. .They
have been treated too much and too often.
My private opinion, however, is that it will
be a treat when there isn’t an Injun left.
  I stand by the old constitution that has
been tried. Few men have tried their con-
stitution more than I have tried mine. I
accept the amendments, every one of them.
When it comes to amends, I am ready to
shout “ amen” as loud as anybody.
I understand there is an ambitious man
named George Francis Train, who aspires to
be President on his promise to free Ireland.
I engage not only to free Ireland but make
Irish whisky free in the bargain. I shall at
least be able to tie George Francis in the.
popular vote unless one of us is kept away

   I shall inaugurate a wholesale emancipa-
tion businesses soon as I am inaugurated. No
goods retailed at the White House when I
am President.    I engage to emancipate the
women from the thraldom of fashion, to
give the “ boys” their rights, and abolish

from the elevating and purifying influences

I trust I am not making myself too free.


﻿A BRUTAL SPANISH PROCLAMATION.—
The Madrid correspondent of the “Indepen-
dence Belge” states that General Buceta, in
assnming command of the troops at Malaga,
addressed them in the following most re-
markable terms :   “ Soldiers, I am informed
that the inhabitants of Malaga may perhaps
attempt an insurrectional rising in connec-
tion with the election; in this case, do not
suppose that I shall send you into the street
to obtain possession of the barricades.  No;
we shall march out of the city, and from
thence the artillery will destroy it. We
shall then return and march through its
ruins, which will be strewn with corpses.
Let no quarter be given; I won’t have any
prisoners made, as they might manage to
escape.  I wish that you may be able to
say :  ‘ General, I have killed so many men
without making a single prisoner.’  My,
opinion is that to hold in check one-half of
the population, it is necessary to kill the
other half. I speak to you in the name of
the national Sovereign.”


﻿A WOMAN, at Pittsfield, Mass., a few
days ago, took a photographer to the tomb
where the body of her deceased brother lay,
and they stood the corpse up against the
door and took its portrait.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿ABOUT WOMEN.
		
	THE Empress of Austria is said to be the
most beautiful woman in Europe.
  THE religious department of a St. Louis
paper is in charge of Miss Fanny Holy.
  A PROVIDENCE lady lecturer concludes it
won’t pay. Only two tickets were sold.
  THE Michigan University at Ann Arbor
has sixty-three young women students.
  MAGGIE MITCHELL has made her re-entry
to the dramatic world on the stage of a Bos-
ton theatre.
  MRS. Fanny Frazer Foster is considered
one of the most successful singing teachers
in the United States.
  A BALL was recently given at Marion,
New York, at which every lady present
wore a Dolly Varden costume.
  NEW HAVEN has a ladies’ reading room,
furnished with piano, tables, handsome car-
pet, local and weekly papers, etc.
  A TERRE HAUTE lady aged threescore
years and ten has attended every circus
within reach since she was five years old.
  MRS. Florence Donahue, a lady of Chicope,
Mass., not yet thirty years old, is mother of
fifteen children.  She weighs 249 pounds.
  MISS Ellen F. Starbuck has been the con-
fidential clerk to the last three Governors of
Massachusetts—Andrews, Bullock, and Claf-
lin.
  ’TIS said that a Hindoo in the city of Al-
labad (“ city of God”) has a diamond worth
an untold amount and weighing above 100
carats.
  FANS for the convenience of lazy women
have been attached to sewing machines and
are worked by the power which runs the
machine.
  MRS. Jane Farley, in a fit of jealous des-
pondency, cut her throat in a friend’s house
in New York, lately, dying in a few
minutes.
  HALF the depositors in the Massachusetts
savings banks are women, and $26,000,000
is the sum total of the amount deposited by
these delightful darlings.
  MADAM Kibrisli Pasha, widow of the late
Grand Vizier Kibrisli Mehamet Pasha, will
issue an autobiographical work called
“Thirty Years in a Harem.”
  A LADY who was inclined to favor ,the
woman suffrage movement, went to Wash-
ington, had a chat with Dr. Mary Walker,
and came back bitterly opposed to it.
  MRS. Dr. Mary J. Studley ranked third in
a recent competitive examination for five
positions on the house staff of the New York
Charity Hospital. She will not have a place,
however, on the ground that the medical
board “deem it inexpedient to appoint a fe-
male to such a position.”
   A BOSTON bride, whose groom remonstra-
ted with her at the wedding for indulging
in a rather indiscriminate bestowal of her
maiden kisses on a number of her male
friends, remarked with natural naivete that
the gentlemen in question had been in the
habit of kissing her all her life, and she
didn’t see why they should stop now.
  MRS. Peter Gronda, of Mackinac, is the
prolific mother of twenty-three children,
the grandmother of fifteen children, the off-
spring of a son, and thirteen more the off-
spring of a daughter.  Another daughter
has twelve more little ones, and still others
of Mrs. Gronda’s brood have flourishing
families. This multitudinous mother is vig-
orous and hearty, and uses snow-shoes in
her frequent ten-mile tramps.


﻿AN ADVENTURE,
			
	ON AND IN THE WATER.
			
Editor “ Woman’s Exponent :"
	Perhaps a brief notice of our boat excur-
sion and the little adventure we met with,
on Sunday, June 2d, might be somewhat
interesting to a portion, at least, of your
readers. Indeed the adventure itself proved
very interesting, intensely so, to us, before
we got through with it.
Mr. H. was going to start to work on the
railroad on Monday morning, so he wished
us all to go down to his farm to spend Sun-
day and take dinner. he came for us with
his horses and wagon, and all hands went, 
	After dinner it was proposed that we
should have a ride in the boat. Accordingly,
four of us, ladies, and two small children,
seated ourselves in the boat for a ride, and
Mr. H. took the position of oarsman. He
had gallantly rowed twice across the little
lake, or big pond, and we were just starting
out again, when “ the treacherous bark”
capsized and threw us all overboard into the
water. The boat went over the head of one
lady, but Mr. H. succeeded in shoving it off
from her, and out further into the water,
where the two ladies who were in the great-
est danger, being farthest from our hero, (Mr.
H.) could get hold of it; which they did, and
thus kept themselves and the infant they
had with them from sinking. Mr. H. first
gave the little boy he had taken to a lady
on shore; then helped the lady who had
been slightly hurt by the boat, to get on
shore; then myself, and then he went to
the rescue of those who were still clinging
to the boat. Through the mercies of a kind
Providence, we were all saved, and none of
us were seriously injured, not even our pre-
cious children.
	We conclude, though, that Sunday is not
the day for dinners and boat excursions;
and that if we are able to go anywhere on
that day, the proper place to go is to the
house of the Lord, to learn of His goodness
and feast upon His pleasing word.
	True, we had that day a great manifesta-
tion of His long-suffering and marvelous
kindness toward the children of men; yet
it might be looked upon as a warning, I
think. The scene was certainly an exciting
one. Imagine the position for a moment!
Smithfield.	M. G. H.

----
﻿AN ADVENTURE,
ON AND IN THE WATER.
Editor “ Woman’s Exponent
	Perhaps a brief notice of our boat excur-
sion and the little adventure we met with,
on Sunday, June 2d, might be somewhat
interesting to a portion, at least, of your
readers. Indeed the adventure itself proved
very interesting, intensely so, to us, before
we got through with it.
	Mr. H. was going to start to work on the
railroad on Monday morning, so he wished
us all to go down to his farm to spend Sun-
day and take dinner. He came for us with
his horses and wagon, and all hands went. 
	After dinner it was proposed that we
should have a ride in the boat. Accordingly,
four of us, ladies, and two small children,
seated ourselves in the boat for a ride, and
Mr. H. took the position of oarsman. He
had gallantly rowed twice across the little
lake, or big pond, and we were just starting
out again, when “ the treacherous bark”
capsized and threw us all overboard into the
water. The boat went over the head of one
lady, but Mr. H. succeeded in shoving it off
from her, and out further into the water,
where the two ladies who were in the great-
est danger, being farthest from our hero, (Mr.
H.) could get hold of it; which they did, and
thus kept themselves and the infant they
had with them from sinking. Mr. H. first
gave the little boy he had taken to a lady
on shore; then helped the lady who had
been slightly hurt by the boat, to get on
shore; then myself, and then he went to
the rescue of those who were still clinging
to the boat. Through the mercies of a kind
Providence, we were all saved, and none of
us were seriously injured, not even our pre-
cious children.
	We conclude, though, that Sunday is not
the day for dinners and boat excursions;
and that if we are able to go anywhere on
that day, the proper place to go is to the
house of the Lord, to learn of His goodness
and feast upon His pleasing word.
	True, we had that day a great manifesta-
tion of His long-suffering and marvelous
kindness toward the children of men ; yet
it might be looked upon as a warning, I
think. The scene was certainly an exciting
one. Imagine the position for a moment!
	Smithfield.			M. G. H.

		﻿AN ADVENTURE,
			   
	   ON AND IN THE WATER.
			   
Editor “ Woman’s Exponent:"
   Perhaps a brief notice of our boat excur-
sion and the little adventure we met with,
on Sunday, June 2d, might be somewhat
interesting to a portion, at least, of your
readers. Indeed the adventure itself proved
very interesting, intensely so, to us, before
we got through with it.
   Mr. H. was going to start to work on the
railroad on Monday morning, so he wished
us all to go down to his farm to spend Sun-
day and take dinner. he came for us with
his horses and wagon, and all hands wept, 
   After dinner it was proposed that we
should have a ride in the boat. Accordingly,
four of us, ladies, and two small children,
seated ourselves in the boat for a ride, and
Mr. H. took the position of oarsman. He
had gallantly rowed twice across the little
lake, or big pond, and we were just starting
out again, when “ the treacherous bark”
capsized and threw us all overboard into the
water. The boat went over the head of one
lady, but Mr. H. succeeded in shoving it off
from her, and out further into the water,
where the two ladies who were in the great-
est danger, being farthest from our hero, (Mr.
H.) could get hold of it; which they did, and
thus kept themselves and the infant they
had with them from sinking.  Mr. H. first
gave the little boy he had taken to a lady
on shore; then helped the lady who had
been slightly hurt by the boat, to get on
shore ; then myself, and then he went to
the rescue of those who were still clinging
to the boat.  Through the mercies of a kind
Providence, we were all saved, and none of

cious children.
  We conclude, though, that Sunday is not
the day for dinners and boat excursions;
and that if we are able to go anywhere on
that day, the proper place to go is to the
house of the Lord, to learn of His goodness
and feast upon His pleasing word.
   True, we had that day a great manifesta-
tion of His long-suffering and marvelous
kindness toward the children of men ; yet
it might be looked upon as a warning, I
think.  The scene was certainly an exciting
one.   Imagine the position for a moment!
   Smithfield.	M. G. H.


﻿INTELLIGENCE AND INTEGRITY.
			    
   It seems a pity, sometimes, to see persons who
are highly gifted with a noble portion of intelli-
gence, give way to evil passions, yield to the pur-
suasive voice of temptation, and fall into vice. The
query very often arises, How can they, knowing so
well the difference between the proper and improper
course, act as they do ? But the question is easily
answered.  They lack integrity; the love of truth,
the appreciation of justice and right. Yet, by their
eloquence, their ingenuity, the pleasing display of
their superior intellectual abilities, they often win
friends and obtain favors, where others, possessed
of genuine integrity and trustworthy principles, but
less brilliant in ideas and consequent appearance,
are passed by unnoticed.
   Where both these elements richly abound and
are finely cultivated in one being, we find superior-
ity indeed.  And that superiority all should, and
all may, obtain, by carefully cultivating the funda-
mental parts of these two pre-eminent principles.
For, certain it is, the Lord in His infinite wisdom
and mercy has ordered a seed of each to be planted
in every little perfect human form, that all who de-
sire it, may be both wise and good.			L.


THE first colored lawyer has been admit-
ted to practice in South Carolina.

	THE United States Government employs
over 3,000 women.


﻿THE RIGHT WAY FOR WOMEN.
			
  A thoughtful female writer has published
the following reflections:—“After toiling
all day, how refreshing to a weary man is
the peaceful quiet of home! A woman
ought to study, in everything, the comfort
of those who are passing their days shut out
from the pleasant green fields and shady
lanes, toiling where the sunbeams cannot
reach them, to cheer their sinking hearts,
in order that she may live in comfort; such
a thought should guide the actions of every
mother, wife, and sister.
    Cleanliness and a judicious expenditure of
funds are most admirable qualifications in a
wife, yet do not constitute entire happiness;
they are only parts of the ingredients em-
ployed in forming domestic happiness.
Many women never think of conversing on
any subject beyond dress and flirtations, so
the father, husband, or brother, goes out in
search of a friend with whom he can discuss
more interesting matters.
   It is a great loss to females not to be able,
on all occasions, to converse on general sub-
jects.  We are happy to state that children
of the working classes are now, in many
towns, amply provided with the means of
securing an excellent education, despite pe-
cuniary deficiency ; an education that will fit
them to hold, with credit, superior situa-
tions. They are taught to think and to rea-
son upon all learned subjects as children, so
that in the days of maturity they may enjoy
and add to the enjoyment of an intelligent
circle of friends.”


﻿THE CORONADO ISLANDS.

	One of the first sights that attracts the
stranger on his arrival here, and one that
the old residents of San Diego never tire
looking at, is the group of islands known as
the Coronados.  In plain view from every
portion of the city, with nothing beyond
them but the waters of the immense Pacific,
they form an attractive and graceful con-
trast to the high hills south and east and the
mesa land north of the town. They are al-
ways beautiful, but are especially so when
the atmosphere is intensely clear, as it was
yesterday.  The first day of May was, even
for a San Diego day, unusually lovely.  Its
pleasant breezes made every one feel cheer-
ful, and drew many into the open air away
from their occupations to sit and gaze with
delight on the islands, which seemed, al-
though miles away, to be within a hundred
yards of the beach, and looked as if nothing
intervened between them and the shore but
a narrow strip of blue water.  But a still
more attractive phase under which the
group of islands can be seen, is when the
atmosphere causes them to change their ap-
pearance, producing the most beautiful mir-
ages imaginable. On Tuesday an opportu-
nity was afforded to observe these atmos-
pheric effects. The rapid changes in the
appearance of the Coronados as seen from
the city on this day were wonderful; and
the shapes were as puzzling and the opin-
ions of observers as conflicting as were those
of Polonius, who saw a camel, weasel and
whale in the same cloud. These curious
mirages are not rare, but may be seen fre-
quently.  Another peculiarity connected
with these islands is that at times they are
not visible from the city, although with the
exception of a slight and almost undiscerni-
ble haze, the day may be perfectly clear,
with not a single cloud in the sky.—“San
Diego Union.”


WOMANS- EXPONENT.


﻿WHEN'Mrs. Isabella Beecher Hooker was
pleading before the Willimantic Selectmen
for the admission  of women as voters, she
stated that she believed one of the gentle-
men before her was in sympathy with the
movement (referring to Mr. Lincoln, the
town clerk). Mr. Lincoln asked her for the
reasons for believing so, and she said she
could see it in the twinkle of his eye.  "I
beg your pardon,” said Mr. Lincoln, “but
that eye is a glass eye.”
  MISS CUSHMAN gave an evening’s read-
ing in Chicago, some time ago, for which
she was paid $1,500—very generous remu-
neration for an hour’s labor.  A correspond-
ent of the  "Christian Union” says that “in
spite of the mouthings and mannerisms that
are not good because they are not nat-
ural, it was better than any sermon on do-
mestic bickering to hear her read ‘ Betsey
and I are Out.’ ” He then proceeds to ask
how the church can hope to make a good
fight against the theatre when it has failed
to find a fitting place for this kind of talent,
which is so powerful when used in the
cause of truth and purity.

		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

		A UTAH LADIES' JOURNAL. 

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

  They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them ; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen, and
countrywomen in reply.

  Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves ?    "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"

  For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has boen
commenced.

  The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief.and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.

   It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

   Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best 
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but 
little is known of the self-sacrificing .labors of these 
Societies.  In WOMAN'S EXPONENT'S a department ;
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other 
matters of interest connected with their workings; 
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the 
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which.will receive 
due attention.

  Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she,has leisure from
her numerous duties.

  WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
   The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in 
Utah.

   TERMS, Strictly in Advance:	’		r
1 Copy one year,	$200 ; . 	1 Copy six months,    $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months,  9.00

  No subscription received for less than six months. 
  It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents 
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a 
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting 
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

   Address all communications and subscriptions, 
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City, 
Utah.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 

VOL. I. 		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JULY 1, 1872.		NO. 3.


﻿NEWS AND VIEWS.
		    
  DIED at Geneva—Indirect Damages.
  SENSATIONAL preaching continues fashion-
able and attractive.
  THE Hyers sisters led the colored chorus
in the Boston Jubilee.
  MORAL Massachusetts has an average of
11,000 prisoners always on hand.
  LONDON is said to have 2,000 “Christian
Jews.” They keep store open on Sundays.
  THE “Big Jamboree” is the Washington
style of speaking of the Boston Jubilee. Is
Washington jealous?
  CONVICTS in Detroit write dime novels,
which is little better business than they
were engaged in before.
  POLITICAL corruption is being exposed to
the scorching light of critical exposure, which
reveals a large amount of previously con-
cealed rotteness.
  GEORGE SAND is now at work upon a
“Life of Christ.” Novel readers and lovers
of romance will probably learn something
new of the Scriptures now.
  THE BAND of the British Grenadier
Guards warmly welcomed to Boston on the
anniversary of the battle of Bunker Hill, is
one of the events of the times too notable to
be overlooked.
  IN LONDON alone, since last August, there
has been nearly 10,000 deaths by small-pox.
Nor has that dreadful disease been confined
to England and the United States, but has
ravaged Europe as well. Job’s complaint is
becoming widely known.
  A CHANGE in-the Utah Federal officials,
it is intimated, is likely to be made after
the November elections. Blessings of such
magnitude should be carefully announced
in advance to prepare the public for receiv-
ing them with a moderate degree of enthu-
siasm.
  A FEW prominent Free Traders reject Dr.
Greeley, because of his protection views,
and having hammered together a platform
of the old materials have set up candidates
thereon, the principal difficulty about the
matter being that the candidates decline to
accept.
  ONE HALF of the servant girls in the city of
Philadelphia are said to be drunkards. If
true, give them a chance to become honest
wives and keepers of their own houses, in-
stead of having to keep irregular hours wait-
ing on fashionable mistresses, and that evil,
like many others, may disappear.
  ON THE 9th inst. the National Democratic
Convention will meet in Baltimore, and the
indications are strong that it will make no
nominations, but endorse those made at
Cincinnati. In this matter the Democratic
leaders have been led by the rank and file of
the party which is evidently tired of follow-
ing the same men to defeat through years
coming on years.
  WHAT a terrible commentary is that on
the manner in which justice is administered
in New York, conveyed in the following
from a paper published in that city? “Three
hundred and fifty murders last year, and but
one man suffering the extreme penalty of

the law. He, poor Jack Reynolds, half-
witted, without money or friends, was hung
as an awful example!”
  A NEW fire-arm of terribly destructive
power, and called the “Magazine rifle,” is
announced as the latest addition to life-
destroying inventions. Invented by Captain
Meigs, of Lowell, Massachusetts, weighing
only half a pound more than the regular
Springfield rifle, it is capable of discharging
fifty bullets in fifty seconds; and promises to
be as effective in reducing the population as
railroad accidents or steamboat explosions.
  ANOTHER PRESIDENTIAL ticket, and no-
body injured! Groesbeck, of Ohio, and
Olmsted, of New York, were nominated by
a small band of Free-Traders on the 21st ult.
The latter declined the honor immediately;
the former, with an evident hankering after
the post of standard-bearer, intimated that
the Democratic convention at Baltimore
must speak before he would listen. The
nominating body was. respectable but not
strong enough.	
  TWO TERRIBLE disasters have occurred on
this continent since the last issue of the
EXPONENT. A steamboat explosion on the
Mississippi river hurled thirty-five human
beings into eternity; and a railroad accident
in Canada bruised, battered and butchered
sixty-five more, of whom over thirty died,
most of them in horrible agony. A weak
boiler, or a defective rail, and nobody to
blame, is the usual verdict; while death in
horrid form mocks at the advances of science
as recklessness disregards or defies them.
  PUBLISHED dispatches last week accused
Mrs. Emily Pitts Stevens of drawing a pistol
on a gentleman at an anti-Woman’s Suffrage
meeting in San Francisco; and subsequently
stated that she had published a card deny-
ing the allegation. While regretting that
the agitation and discussion of so important
a subject is not conducted with less acrimo-
ny and more courteous good feeling on both
sides, we await a more lengthened statement
of the affair in the next number of the
“Pioneer,” of which Mrs. Stevens is editor,
before accepting as correct that which has
been telegraphed concerning it.
  TO TEMPT a woman to evil and then seek
a divorce because she has fallen before the
temptation, has been the cowardly and
criminal course pursued by more husbands
than court records have shown. But a late
decision on the matter will mar its future
efficacy. A suit for divorce was recently
tried in London before Lord Penzance, when
the woman showed that she had been led
into evil through the plots of an agent of
her husband. Lord Penzance ruled that the
husband was responsible for the acts of his
agent, and refused the divorce. A wise and
upright Judge!
  TENNIE C. CLAFLIN has gained the
coveted position, and is now a full-blown
Colonel. She was elected to that position by
a colored regiment of New York. There
have been women wno have led armed men
to conflict and victory, as did Boadicea the
ancient Britons and Joan of Arc the dis-
pirited French; but they were moved to ac-
tion by other impulses than a morbid passion
for notoriety. Tennie C. Claflin is a sad
spectacle of a talented woman setting mod-

esty, purity of feeling, and womanly grace
at defiance, that she may secure the ap-
plause of the reckless and the indorsement
of those who set proper restrictions at defi-
ance.
  THE practical character of the present ago
is fatal to many ancient and venerable theo-
ries. Rev. James R. Dunne, of Boston, who
has been traveling in Palestine announces
that the Dead Sea is not the sad, sluggish
sheet of water it has been represented. Its
waters though heavy and bitter are clear
and rippling, its wavelets sparkle on the
beach, and its “shores are green with shrub-
bery and bright with flowers.” So too the
idea that Great Salt Lake is untenanted with
life is proved a fallacious illusion. A mem-
ber of Professor Hayden’s scientific explor-
ing expedition announces that it teems with
animal life.  The Dead Sea of the cast, and
its counterpart in the west, it appears, have
both been misrepresented.
  THE JESUITS have been deprived of all
rights of citizenship in Prussia by recent
statutory enactment. There is a bitter strag-
gle going on in Germany between church
and state, the government proving itself the
stronger by the passage of this statute.
Bismarck is determined that no ecclesiasti-
cal authority shall successfully lift itself
against the secular government, and in this
he is sustained by the “Old Catholic” party
which does not accept the dogma of infalli-
bility. He sees that the Jesuits are insinu-
ating and aggressive, yet he gives them
strength by the very act which deprives
them of citizenship; for when men are thus
punished for simply belonging to a religious
order, sympathy is evoked in their behalf
and their logic becomes irresistable when
their opponents thus admit their inability
to meet it by counter-argument.
  THE following communication was re-
ceived and read at the National Republican
Convention on June 6th, as appears by the
minutes of that day’s proceedings: No. 333
Walnut street, 6th month, 5th, 1872.—Dear
Friend: I am assured that over sixty of the
ablest women of the land are ready to take
the field for the Republican party If the Con-
vention now in session will adopt some plank
that will even allow them this opportunity
as honest, serious workers for human rights.
Below you will find a copy of the plank,
which, if adopted; will be the occasion of an
enthusiasm only equalled by that of 1856 for
Fremont.		Very respectfully,
					E. M. DAVIS.
To Robert Purvis, Delegate to the Republi-
  can Convention.
  Headquarters Woman’s Suffrage Dele-
gates, No. 333 Walnut street, Philadelphia,
June 5th, 1872.—To the National Republi-
can Convention—Gentlemen: In behalf of
the women of this nation, one-half the entire
people, I ask you to put a plank in your
platform that shall assert the duty of the
National Government to protect women citi-
zens in the exercise of their right to vote,
and thereby make it possible for women
possessed of true self-respect to advocate the
claims of the Republican party to the suf-
frages of the people.
  Respectfully,   	SUSAN B. ANTOHNY.
President National Woman’s Suffrage Asso-'
clation.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿THE FEMALE RELIEF SOCIETY.
				
			WHAT IS IT?
				
	It is an institution formed to bless
	The poor, the widow, and the fatherless;
	To clothe the naked and the hungry feed,
	And, in the holy paths of virtue, lead;

	To seek where anguish, grief and sorrow are,
	And light the torch of hope eternal, there;
	To prove the strength of consolation’s art.
	By breathing comfort to the mourning heart;

	To chase the clouds that shade the aspect where
	Had mis’ry dwells, and wake up pleasure there;
	With open heart t’ extend the friendly hand;
	To hail the stranger from a distant land;

	To stamp a with’ring impress on each move
	That virtue’s noble self would disapprove;
	To put the tattler’s coinage, scandal, down,
	And make corruption feel its scathing frown;

	To give instruction where instruction’s voice
	Will guide the feet and make the soul rejoice;
	To turn the wayward from their recklessness,
	And aid them in the path of happiness.

	- It is an order fitted and designed
	To cultivate and elevate the mind;
	To seek the needy in their lone abode,
	Supply their wants, and lead them up to God.
										E.R.S.


F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
			
  THE Female Relief Society of the Six-
teenth Ward of this city, held its annual
meeting on June 15th, in the Ward school
house; and the following summary of the
minutes will be found interesting:
  After singing the second hymn, Mrs. Z.
D.  YOUNG made remarks: “Obedience is
better than sacrifice, and to hearken than
the fat of rams.” I feel my inability to in-
struct; yet, we are all dependant on one
Father. Although, according to the flesh,
we have different parents, brothers and
sisters, kindred ties and associations, by
which we are linked together here; yet, in
the grand chain of the Eternities we all be-
long to one family, and all look to the same
great God for aid and comfort. If there ever
was a time when we should be faithful,
watchful, and careful, it is now. Mothers,
look after your daughters; teach them to be
modest, industrious, and virtuous; to honor,
with all the other principles pertaining to
the Priesthood of God, the law of celestial
marriage, which is for the redemption and
salvation of many. I pray God to bless you,
my sisters, both young and aged; and help
us all to keep the faith.
   ELDER JOSEPH F. SMITH, being present
by invitation, was requested to address the
meeting. A very brief synopsis of his re-
marks is contained in the following: I may
say,  my sisters, I hardly expected to be
here I look upon these Societies and their
work as things of great importance; and some-
times wonder why so long a time elapsed after
the break-up at Nauvoo, before a reorganiza-
tion was made. I look at it as being just as
essential as any other organization of the
Priesthood. Upon mothers in Israel devolve
great responsibilities. To them belongs the
care of their children; to nourish, feed, dress
and instruct; and in a great measure to edu-
cate. Upon you, mothers, depend the train-
ing of the future generation. You form their
minds, thoughts, aspirations. I know by
experience you can destroy or make. You
can tell what a mother is when you see her
children at play. In Cache valley, there was
a number of little girls playing keeping-
house and making mud-butter. One was
playing mother and dictating. When there
was a nice, large, roll of butter made, she
laid it on one side, and said, “Now, that is
to go to the Tithing Office, it is better than
the rest.” That told a great story for the
little girl's mother. The child showed her
teaching. If children treat each other kindly

and courteously, it is the spirit that is in-
stilled into their hearts by their mothers at
home. We often hear children speak in a
cross, commanding way. What does that
argue? That it is the way the mother does.
The father is away, the mother at home,
where she may teach her children to be
mild and loving, honest, gentle and good.
Mothers have a better chance to lead their
children in the proper way than the fathers.
They have opportunities afforded for cutiva-
ting the blessings of faith. I love to hear a
woman pray. They can approach nearer to
the Lord than men can, as a general thing;
or my mother did. I could not do as my
mother could. These societies are not only
calculated for a means of relieving the wants
of the poor, comforting the afflicted etc., but
to correct and improve yourselves and each
other.
  Much more valuable instruction was given
by Elder Smith and others, followed by sing-
ing and prayer after which the meeting ad-
journed.
  The Society financially is in a sound con-
dition, as we note by the financial report
presented by the Secretary, Presendia L.
Kimball. It shows that during the four
years $727.65 was received in cash, mer-
chandise and produce, of which $201.86 was
disbursed, leaving $527.79 in the treasury
at date of report. Among the disbursements
for the fiscal year ending June 15th, 1872,
was one item of $50 donated to assist the
poor to immigrate; and other items for
clothing bestowed upon those who needed
it.
  We are indebted to Mrs. Mary A. Maughan,
of Logan, Cache county, for the following
interesting report:
  “The anniversary meeting of the Female
Relief Society was held in the Logan Hall
May 18th, Mrs. M. A. Maughan, President
of the Society, presiding. There was a good
attendance of the members, but in conse-
quence of sickness in the family of the Secre-
tary, the financial report was not read.
  Spirited and interesting remarks were
made by Bishop W. B. Preston, and Elder
Moses Thatcher on the duties of mothers in
rearing and educating their sons to become
good and wise men in the Kingdom of God;
and the daughters to acquire the accomplish-
ments necessary to enable them to become
useful as helpers in the great work which is
before us; and instructing them how to be-
come ornaments in society. They also spoke
in commendation of the great good that laid
been accomplished by the F. R. Society of
this place; and felt to encourage and bless
us in our efforts to relieve the needy.
  The meeting was then adjourned until
the monthly meeting of the Society, which
was held on the first Monday in June, when
the financial report was presented, showing
that since the organization of the Society
$1,453.02 had been gathered by donations,
profits on labor, etc., of which a great
amount had been disbursed to the poor; and
for other charitable purposes, in helping to
sustain the Kingdom; leaving a balance in
the Treasury. It is the purpose of the So-
ciety as fast as possible to make the adorn-
ment of their persons the workmanship of
their own hands.
   I heartily congratulate you on the able
manner in which your little paper is
gotten up, wish you great success, and can
assure you that your paper, will be warmly
welcomed and highly appreciated by the
ladies of Logan.”
  Accompanying the report nnsa handsome
subscription list.
				
MRS. M. A. BOICE and Mrs. Emily Hep-

worth, President and Secretary of the F. R.
Society at Oxford, Cache county, kindly
forward a report of their little Society,
which shows that although few in numbers,
they take an active interest in the promo-
tion of good, relieving the wants of the des-
titute among them; helping to immigrate
the poor from other countries, to send mis-
sionaries abroad to preach the gospel, to
build railroads, etc. A meeting was held
on June 16th, at which Bishop Lake and
others made appropriate and interesting, re-
marks.
   They also send congratulations on the
favorable debut of the EXPONENT.


﻿A SENSIBLE WOMAN.
			
  A New England girl twenty-seven years
of age, who was disappointed in a love affair
in early life, and had afterwards steadily re-
fused the least attention from any man, sur-
prised her friends a short time ago by get-
ting married.
  Being an only child at the death of her
parents, which took place some five years
ago, she was left sole inheritor and manager
of a handsome fortune. She asked no aid
from anyone, but with two old and trusty
servants, a gardener and housekeeper, she
lived on quietly in her own home, and in a
highly creditable manner conducted her own
affairs. Soon after entering upon this new
stage of life, she loaned quite a large sum of
money to a young merchant, then almost in-
solvent.  He struggled bravely to redeem
his pledges, and very much care-worn, he
at last became almost discouraged and would
have yielded to bankruptcy, he says, had it
not been for his frequent visits to his fair
creditor and the cheering advice she always
gave him. At last, after having vainly
striven for more than four years to rise
above his misfortunes, he relinquished all
hope. The first one he called upon, to make
this anounccment, was his lady creditor.
Old Sharkey, the gardener, witnessed their
interview; for she never allowed both her
servants to withdraw when a gentleman
was present.
   She still tried to encourage the merchant
as before, but could not succeed this time;
he was too far gone. She offered him more
money, which he refused to take; also . to
forgive the debt and the interest then due on
it, which was likewise manfully declined, in
part, with promises for some future .time
when all would be refunded.
  After he had taken his leave of the lady,
she said to the gardener, “Call him back,’
Sharkey, I’ve concluded to .become his
wife.”
  “His wife!” repeated Sharkey, “why, he
has never said anything of the kind . to
you!”‘		-	’
   “I know it,” replied the lady, “and-that's
the reason I like him. He looks love at me
all the time, but never ventures to speak;
and he has proved himself honest, grateful
and noble. Call him back, Sharkey, and
then go and ask the parson to step over here
a few minutes.”
  Sharkey obeyed, the parson came, and
the wedding took place. The merchant’s
debts are all paid up, and he and his heroic
bride live very happily in their peaceful
and beautiful home.
   Women are not all fools, are they?


﻿A San Francisco journal, states that more
than half that city is given up to demoraliz-
ing practices, more ruinous to the souls and
bodies of the unfortunate than is any system
of savagery among the wild men of the
desert.  We do not dispute the sad state-
ment.  But what an atmosphere to live in!


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿LET ME THINK THUS.
				
Let me not think that the world is all selfishness,
  Care’ess and grim and of dark, sombre hue;
Let me believe there are hearts still affectionate,
  Still undeceiving, unsordid and true.
Though often death’s undisputed authority,
  Claims the most gifted, the freest from guile,
Yet their dear spirits still make earth their dwelling
	place:
  Let me think thus, and he happy the while.
Let me not think that our God, unremittingly,
  Leads us poor mortals in ignorance here,
Thirsting for knowledge, expecting and wondering:
  One day with light He'll envelope our sphere.
No more dark shadows of doubt and disparagement;
   All will be sunshine, Jehovah’s glad smile;
Freely we’ll drink at the Fount of Intelligence:
  Let me think thus, and be happy the while
									L L


﻿HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
			
  TO COLOR SLATE COLOR.—Boil yellow
oak bark in an iron kettle till the strength is
extracted. Take out the bark, then add a
very little copperas, and you have a pretty
color with no expense.
  SCALLOPED CHICKEN. — Mince chicken
with lean ham and a little pepper mixed, in
scallop shells or a flat dish, add two large
spoonfuls of cream, cover with crumbs, set
before the fire to brown, with a little butter
on top.
   APPLE FLOAT. —One pint of stewed ap-
ples ;  when cold, sweeten and flavor to
taste. Just as you want to send to table,
add the beaten whites of four eggs, lightly
stirred into it.  With cream this makes a
nice dessert.
   BATH, CAKES.—Mix well together a quar-
ter of a pound of butter, half a pound of flour,
two large eggs, and a tablespoonful of yeast;
set the mixture before the fire to rise, and
when this has been effected, add two ounces
of finely powdered sugar, and half an ounce
of carraways. Roll the paste out into little
cakes; bake them on tins.
   DOLPETTES OF COLD MEAT.—Prepare the
meat as for a hash—or some hashed meat
that has become cold will answer the pur-
pose—add to it some bread-crumbs, enough
to stiffen the consistency, mix it together
with the yolk of eggs, shape it into small
balls, dip them into egg, roll them in bread-
crumbs and grated cheese, and fry them
brown. Glaze them, or serve them with
tomato sauce.
   THE FOLLOWING dressing for salad is very
much liked by some, but does not suit the
taste of others. It is also said to be excel-
lent over sliced tomatoes in the Summer.
Take the yolk of one fresh egg and mix with
it two tablespoonfuls of olive oil very slowly;
add one and one-half spoonfuls of mustard,
three spoonfuls of salt, a little pepper, and
last of all two spoonfuls of vinegar. Beat
the white of the egg to a stiff froth and
lightly stir in.
   DUCHESSES.—-Take half a dozen potatoes,
boil them, pass them through a sieve, and
work into them in a bowl one gill of cream
and the yolks of three eggs; add pepper, salt,
and nutmeg to taste, and some parsley chop-
ped fine. When they are well mixed and
smooth, take them up by tablespoonfuls, roll
each in a ball, flatten it, and flour it slightly;
lay them all in a sauce pan with plenty of
butter melted, and cook them slowly ; turn
them over when one side is done, and serve
hot as soon as both sides are colored.
  OYSTER PIE.—Cover a.well-buttered deep
plate or tin—a soup plate answers perfectly
—with puff paste ; lay an extra layer around
the edge of the plate, and bake it very
nearly enough. That done, fill the pie with
oysters, seasoning with nutmeg, pepper, salt
[next column]
and butter; dust in a little flour amongst
them, and cover all with a thin puff paste.
Bake quickly ; when the top crust is done
the oysters will be done also. If to be  eaten
cold, let it cool untouched, in the plate or
pan. It is quite as good in this way as hot,
and is excellent for picnics or for traveling.
  MISS BEECHER has remarked that there
is nothing which so distinctly marks the
difference between weak and strong minds
as whether they control circumstances or
circumstances control them. And Mrs.
Abell says that with many well meaning
women, eating, drinking, clothing, cleanli-
ness and order of a household constitute the
sum total of all life ; while the moral, the
social and intellectual interests are wholly
neglected. But, with assistance suitable to
the labor, a proper improvement of time
and wise arrangement of plans, these best
and most exalted duties need not be slight-
ed, and will not unless an undue importance
is attached to the grosser and lower pleas-
ures of the appetite and personal adornment.
There are some so rigid in their adherence
to a system that they never allow anything
to interfere with it. Even the sacred claims
of friendship and hospitality must be sacri-
ficed to "washing on Monday ;" and a cold
and frigid reception is often given to a friend
whose visit might interfere with the iron-
ing on the succeeding day.
  But a true, right-hearted woman will not
take upon her one or two virtues only, but
will in kindness embrace them all, reflecting
always that “ it is not all of life to live.”


﻿WHY I DO NOT CALL.
			
Dear “Exponent:”
   Having a little explanation to make to a
couple of my friends, and thinking it might
prove rather a good hint to some others, I
would like to offer the same through your
columns, if not objectionable ; and will en-
deavor to be very brief and explicit.
  A short time ago, Miss Alice---(a friend
who was and is very dear to me) and myself
were walking along the street together and
chatting in a lively manner, when suddenly
Alice stopped short and said in a low tone,
“Hush, let’s walk more slowly!”
  “ Why ?” I asked, and she answered,
“ Because there’s Mrs. K. crossing the street
and I do not wish to meet her, for I don’t
like her, and she always makes so much of
me.”
   We walked slowly, but Mrs. K. saw Alice
and waited at the corner to speak to her. I
was a little surprised at Alice’s remark, for
I had got it into my head that she was so
good and charitable that she never allowed
herself to dislike anyone. I was still more
surprised presently.  As we came up Mrs.
K. held out her hand with a smile ; Alice
smiled back again, warmly clasped the prof-
fered hand, and said, as if delighted at the
meeting, “ How do you do ? I haven’t seen
you for such a long time !”
    “ I know it,” said Mrs. K. “ Why don’t
you come over and see me?”
   Alice replied, “Simply because I can’t
find time to go anywhere; but you must
come and see me, won’t you ? Do come !”
   Mrs. K. said she would, and we passed on.
  “That’s the way she talks to folks she
don’t like !” I said to myself, “That’s just
the way she talks to me!”
  Yes, when she left me, it was with an af-
fectionate and persuasive injucntion that I
should come very soon to see her. She al-
ways did so.
   The more I reflected on it the more it
seemed to me that if. my dear, loving little 
[end of column]
friend could speak as she did about Mrs. K.,
and then meet her so cordially, and say such
pleasant things to her, she might do the
same by me. I don’t know but she means
it all when she says “come and see me ! now
do !” in such emphatic tones; but I’m afraid
she does not; so I have not been to see her
lately.  She has been anxious to know why.
I have kept it to myself till now, not telling
even my mother; but if Alice reads this she
will be in doubt no longer as to why I did’nt
call.
  The other part.of my explanation is for 
the benefit of Mrs. R. (an elderly lady) in
particular; and to all who may need a like
hint in general. Mrs. R. is such a good-na-
tured, affable, kind-hearted woman, that it
always did me good to call on her. All for-
mality was laid aside, and I could be so much
at home in her house, and always felt that I
was welcome there ; for her words were kind
and motherly, just such as put one at ease,
because they carry with them the assurance
that the speaker means what she says.
  I went there one day, not many minutes
before dinner time, feeling very weary and
faint, having taken a long walk, and having
still a longer one to take. Mrs. R. was par-
ticularly glad to see me and wanted to know
why I had delayed calling so long. I sat
down, threw my hat on to the center table,
and took up the album.
   “O ! ma, here comes mother N.,” called
out Mrs. R.’s little daughter.
  “Well I won’t have any mother N. here
to-day ! I’ve had enough of her lately, and
can’t put up with her any more !” said Mrs.
R. with much earnestness, and I could see
she meant what she said, or thought I could,
though I had never before seen her so
moved.
  Mother N. appeared at the door. “How
do you do, mother N. ? Sit down, dinner
will soon be ready ; how tired you look !”
   How surprised I was ! Mrs. R. had re-
gained her usual cordial manner and pleas-
ant tone, and greeted mother N. the same as
she had greeted me.	
   I turned the leaves of the album over in a
hurry, and arose with hat in hand just as
dinner was announced.
  “You’re not going?”
  “ Yes, I must !”
  “ Oh, not before dinner; it’s all ready.”
  “ But I must hurry; mother will won-
der—”
  “You ! O, now!”
  “ Good bye.” And I haven’t called on
Mrs. R. since, though I often meet her and
she professes to feel real bad because I do
not come and see her. Of course as  can-
not tell whether she means it or not, I do
not like to be suspicious, yet have no wish
to be so credulous as to be silly.
							FRANK.
   The explanation seems to be plain enough
Miss Alice and Mrs. R. will doubtless under-
stand it.—[ED. EXPONENT.


It has been found that the clothes fitted
to  new recruits in the English army soon
become too small across the chest, owing, it
is stated, to the increased development given
by gymnastic and military exercises. It is
very probable that the same result is pro-
duced upon fashionable .ladies’ brains, by
the wonderful exercises they undergo upon
desultory subjects. Many of their hats are
unbecomingly small.


﻿Professor Goltz, of Konigsberg, in his ex-
periments upon the nervous centre of frogs,
finds that if you take out the brain, the
creature will croak as if pleased. Frogs
must be as easily pleased as some men.

			SALT LAKE CITY, JULY 1, 1872.


﻿SPLINTERS.
	It is reported that Russia contemplates
raising a new loan of £10,000,000......A two
story hotel, with a French roof, to accommo-
date two hundred guests, is projected at the
summit of Mount Washington......Ole Bull
thinks that by proper culture, Leaven-
worth will produce a violinist second to none,
in the person of Miss Ella Frew....A stone
was recently quarried on Lake Superior that
is 110 feet long and 8 1/2 feet thick......Fields
of corn, potatoes and other farm produce,
and the buds on fruit trees, were completely
destroyed in Los Angeles and neighboring
vicinity, by April frosts......Many of the
prizes in the Boston high school are taken
by girls...The Marble Hill (Mo.) “True
Democrat,” has for its motto, “A Red-hot
Democratic Family Newspaper.”.....The num-
ber of Republicans in the country is esti-
mated in round figures at 3,300,000; of Dem-
ocrats 3,000,000......In the iron furnace of
York, Pennsylvania, a short time since, the
flywheel, while in motion, burst asunder and
flew in all directions, killing three men and
leaving the mill in ruins......Alphonzo Ad-
hemer predicts another deluge, A. D. 7253.
......The Nevada county, (Cal.) Angora goat
company now have over 500 goats of that
breed......The Prussian Minister of War has
issued a decree for the organization of a
military corps of aeronauts......The commer-
cial writers say that 1872 will be distin-
guished for its immense trade......Eddy-'
ville, Ky., with a population of eight hun-
dred, had, lately, one hundred cases of
measels......A grandson of Daniel O’Connell,
is lecturing in Pennsylvania on the “War-
riors and Bards of Ireland”......The eight
hour movement in San Francisco is said to
be assuming business-like and solid propor-
tions......A tower, 100 feet high, is to be
erected and surmounted with a statue of
Miles Standish, at Duxbury, Mass......In
Alabama the circulation of the New York
“Tribune” is said to have increased fifty
per cent. since the nomination of Greeley.
......The San Francisco “Pioneer,” of June
13th, contains the platform of the Equal
Rights party......The Scandinavian colony,
another Union Pacific achievement which
was started last year, is said to be already a
success......There are ten female superin-
tendents in Kansas......One of the women
in the house of the Good Shepherd, St.
Louis, is a carpenter and shoemaker......
Clark, the balloonist, who fell from a balloon
at Memphis, died from the effects, less than
a week afterward......Prince Bismcrck is go-
ing to the Isle of Wight......The Emperor
William, of Germany, is said to be gradually
sinking into the grave.....The Bairds, who
stand at the head of the iron masters in
Scotland, make as clear profit annually $2,-
500,000......Nilsson sketches landscapes
nicely......Over ten million pairs of shoes
were manufactured in Lynn, Massachusetts,
last year......Myriads of locusts are appear-
ing in the western portion of North Caro-
lina......Pope Pius IX is eighty years old......
The Gould and Curry shaft, in Nevada, has
attained a depth of 1,821 feet, the deepest
on this coast......It is estimated that the sur-
plus wheat crop for exportation, in Califor-
nia, this year, will reach 600,000 tons......
Thiers was a printer’s “devil” in 1825, and
is still bothering the editors......Hon. Chas.
Buckalew, nominated by the Democrats for
Governor of Pennsylvania, is said to be op-
posed to the nomination of Greeley at Balti-
more......Iowa farmers this year, have to
contend with wire worms, cut worms, grub
worms, army worms, chintz bugs and Hes-
sian flies......The Czar of Russia, it is re-
ported, was cool in his demeanor toward
[end of column; start of new column]
Lieut. Fred. Grant, on the occasion of the
latter’s recent visit to Moscow......The vicini-
ty of Prague, in Bohemia, has been visited
by a great flood, involving an appalling de-
struction of life and property........ A London
telegram announces that so far from the Per-
sian famine being over, it is even now worse
than ever......Kaiser William is ready to
deliver his decision on the San Juan bound-
ary question as soon as requested......Cana-
da has abolished the duty on tea and coffee,
except when imported from the United
States......Under the treaty giving Germany
control of the Luxembourg railway, that
power stipulates that it will never use the
roads for the transportation of war munitions,
except in time of peace......The boat with
which the Cambridge crew so badly beat the
Atlantas has been carefully laid away in the
Crystal Palace.....Thiers’ demeanor during
the debate on the French Army bill, greatly
irritated members of all parties in the As-
sembly. Some of the deputies charge him
openly with all the arrogance of the first
Napoleon....Religious out-door services are
to be held in Franklin Square, New York,
Sabbath afternoons. Rev. Stephen H. Tyng,
Jr., has volunteered his services......The
population of Great Britain is placed at 31,-
000,000; that of France at 37,000,000; and
that of Russia as high as 70,000,000......Prof.
Wise proposes to start from Boston, on the
Fourth of July, for a trans-Atlantic balloon
voyage......A file of the London “Times” is
said to be worth not less than £3,000......
Half the depositors in the Massachusetts sav-
ings banks are women......Red Cloud stated
his theory for the settlement of the Indian
question in a very few words, in Cooper In-
stitute. He said, “I can protect the western
country myself!” (Applause, and war-
whoops!)


THE "ENSLAVED" WOMEN OF UTAH.
				______

	Much lachrymose and silly sentimentality
has been uttered and published relative to the
"enslaved" condition of the women of Utah. It
dates back to before the time that Buchanan
sent an army and a Governor here to inflict
punishment for the distruction of court records
that are still intact, and to free the "oppressed
women" who were held in "servile bondage"
by their "male-tyrants." That Governor came
and found the records untouched. Issued
his proclamation offering protection to the
"enslaved women" of the Territory, yet they
neglected to avail themselves of his aid, though
it had a strong military force to give it effect.-
Years passed, and still the cry was raised that
these poor women were trammeled and held in
bonds; and the necessity of extending the elec-
tive franchise to them above all the sisterhood
of the United States was loudly urged. Pos-
sessed of that weapon with which to fight their
oppressors at the polls, it was held that a won-
derful revolution would be speedily manifested
in Utah; for were not the women disgusted with
their condition, desirous of fleeing from their
husbands, fathers and brothers, and eager to 
adopt the fashionable follies, vices, and wicked-
bess wich "adorn" the civilization of the 
century!
	The right of suffrage because theirs, not 
through the efforts of these purely philanthropic
souls who were so eager for their emancipation,
but by the act of the representatives of the "ty-
rants," in legislative body assembled; and still
the ardent natures of those zealous individuals
who were so anxious to "befriend" the women
of Utah were not satisfied. They became as
anxious to have deprived of the elective
franchise as they previously had been that it 
might be bestowed upon us. As the gubernatorial
proclamation referred to had been neglected by
the women to whom it was addressed, so their
votes were cast with and for the men whom in
long years of trial they had proved and found
worthy. And when in February 1870, they
met in mass conventions all over the Territory, 
before the right to vote had been conferred upon
them, the voice of nearly twenty thousand wo-
men-wives, mothers, sisters and daughters of
these "tyrants"-was a united protest against
the calumnies of years heaped upon the men of
Utah, whom they honored and in whom they
had confidence. 
	Now the manner of attack has changed, but
the object is the same; the sentimentality is not
so lachrymose, yet the venom accompanying it
is as malignant as ever. The women of Utah
having destroyed the force of the previous
charges made, are themselves attacked, their
veracity impugned, and their honesty of purpose 
questioned. A few busy-bodies, by means dis-
reputable because dishonorable, have brought
themselves before the attention of the country
as representing the women of this Territory, 
and have published statements which will not 
bear the scrutiny of investigation. Their prin-
ciples are repudiated by nineteen-twentieths of
the women of Utah, while their alleged facts
are exaggerations, misconceptions, and direct
mistatements. Yet when the evidence of their
disingenuousness are published, they seek to
shelter themselves veracity, and claim that they, only,
of all the women of Utah, are to be believed.
	We have lived in Utah a number of years,
have had ample opportunity of understanding 
the situation here, have participated in this 
"slavery," have exercised the franchise being
legally authorized to do so, and are better in-
formed on the views, feelings and sentiments of
our sisterhood in this Territory than those who
eagerly parade their sentimentality before the 
country. With the knowledge thus obtained
through years of experience, we denounce the 
incorrect and vindictive statements still made
concerning men, women and matters in Utah;
and regret that any woman should be engaged
in such work. If the women of Utah are "slaves"
their bonds are loving ones and dearly prized.
They are to-day in the free and unrestricted ex-
ercise of more political and social rights than
are the women of any other part of these Uni-
ted States. But they do not choose, as a body
to court folly, pander to senseless passions, en-
courage vice because it may come with smiling
visage and polished exterior; which make mankind
happy in any condition of existence, for the
tinselled covering of hollow sentimentality, and
the guidance of unstable theorists, or creatures
of romance and slaves of passion.
	Life is a solid reality, drawing its existence
from the past, having its influence on the future. 
As such we view it, and labor to exert that in-
fluence for the elevation of human-kind, ment-
ally, morally and physically.-

﻿   	HOME AFFAIRS.
				______
	A GRATIFYING novelty in judicial proceed-
ings in Salt Lake County, just now, is the ses-
sion of a grand jury summoned according to law
by the Probate Court.
	WILL COUNTY School Superintendents be
kind enough to furnish for the EXPONENT re-
ports of the condition of education in their
several counties?
	THE HIGH waters have fallen throughout the
Territory, leaving abundance for irrigation; and
the prospects for the coming harvest are ex-
ceedingly cheering.   
	A COMMUNICATION from Goshen Valley was
received too late for notice in the present issue.
The same explanation applies to the report of
the Third Ward, F. R. Society of Provo, which
will appear in the next number.
	THE BERGER Family of bell-ringers, with
Sol Smith Russell, are to appear again at the
theatre this week, and will without question be
largely patronized. They are chaste and artistic
performers, and one attractive feature of their
programmes is the appearance of a highly ac-
complished cornet band of ladies.
	IN THE general Retrenchment meeting held in
this city, June 22nd, the President, Miss E. R.
Snow, recommended each Society to have a
Secretary and assistant Secretary, so that in
case of indisposition or other hinderances occur-
ring to prevent either one from attending a
meeting, the other might be there and the min-
utes be regularly kept. This rule is a good one
for Societies outside this city to adopt, as well
as for the different Ward Societies in the city.
	BY A judicial blunder, or for ulterior reasons
not given to the public, the term of the Third
District Court of Utah has been permitted to
lapse. The ad interim Judge, Strickland, ad-
journed it by telegraph, from Provo, until to-
day, instead of attending personally as the stat-
uto requires. Not a few imgaine that the Judge
could not be ignorant of a law so well known,
and that personal chagrin and malice and not
the public interest, were only consulted In the
matter.
	“INDEPENDENCE DAY” is close at hand, and
yet little is said of it. The people of Utah are
warm in their feelings of loyalty to the Consti-
tution and the country, and reverence for the
founders of this great Republic lies shrined in
their heart of hearts; but they desire to cele-
brate the anniversary of freedom as a free peo-
ple, or would prefer not to celebrate at all. By
proclamation they where prohibited from doing
so a year ago, and that proclamation, illegal
and insulting as it was, has never been an-
nulled. We presume they can observe the
day in a quiet fashion, with less outward show
yet as much genuine honor for the occasion, as
their fellow citizens in any part of the country.
	ILLUSTRATIONS of the astounding liberality
of thought evinced by many people, where
“Mormonism” is concerned, now and then crop
out. A man a few days ago stepped into the
store of Messrs. Campbell & Patterson, general
agents for the EXPONENT, and seeing copies of
this periodical on the counter lifted one and in-
quired: “Is this in favor of the Mormons or
against them?” “Rather in their favor,” was
the response; on which he threw it indignantly
down, remarking, “Then, I don’t want it.” In
justice to the stranger—for he was one—the rest
must be told. A sharp remark from one of the
firm called him to a sense of his want of polite-
ness, when he purchased a copy of the first
number, took it away with him; and soon after
returned to secure a copy of the second number,
It improved to his taste, evidently, with the
reading.
	A TERRIBLE tragedy occurred in this city
during the past week, lengthy accounts of
which appeared in the daily papers. Charles
L. Dolson, a married man, sought a divorce
from his wife, and endeavored to have Mary
Obey obtain a divorce from her husband, who
was his partner, that he might marry her.
Whatever relations existed between Dolson and
the woman have not yet been made public, hut
she had left her husband and was residing in
St. Louis, where Dolson corresponded with her.
She refused to accede to his desires, upon which
he became as abusive as he had been beforetime
endearing. Oliver C. Obey, the husband, ob-
tained a knowledge of the facts, and feelings of
violent anger between the men, who were both
gamblers, was the result. Dolson circulated a
printed card, reflecting in gross terms on Obey
and his wife. On Tuesday night last the men
met in the post office in this city, near the de-
livery windows, when Obey killed Dolson,
shooting him with five bullets from a pistol.
He was arrested, stood an examination before
Judge Clinton, and was held to bail in $2,000 to
appear for trial. It was a case of ungoverned
passion, unbridled lust, and sanguinary revenge
from begining to end; and on the part of one a
fearful termination to an unenviable life.

﻿


﻿WOMEN INVENTORS.
				____
	Why women do not invent, is a problem
an anonymous English writer has solved, to
his own satisfaction at the least. He thinks
that the cause of feminine uninventiveness 
is to be found in hereditary laws.  Women
have always been drudges.  Men have been
the explorers, the inventors, the actors of
the world from the first, while women have
simply looked on, followed and obeyed. Con-
sequently they inherit an uninventive con-
stisution, and the inertia of a thousand gen-
erations has descended in entail upon the
women of to-day. All of which is exceed-
ingly plausable, provided the fact corres-
ponds with the assumption. But is it a fact
that woman is naturally less inventive than
man?  We have no proof that she is not.
True she makes few inventions. But she
has few occasions and still fewer opportuni-
ties for making them. She does not get that
practical knowledge of machinery that comes
only from long and constant usage, which is
necessary as the first condition of making
improvements.  As she is not an artisan she
fails to get the requisite mastery of tools to
enable her to see how to put them to yet un-
known uses.  And still further, the stress
of necessity and the spur of mechanical am-
bition are not laid upon her. Scarcely one
mechanic in a hundred ever invents any-
thing. Until a hundred women are thor-
oughly trained to mechanical arts and spend
years in actual mechanical labor it is unfair
to expect one woman to invent anything.
Yet in our Patent Office there are some fifty
models of inventions made by women. In
her own familiar work of making dresses
and ordering the household, woman shows
inventiveness of a high order, which if it
had had the requisite training and field for
its exercises might have achieved brilliant
results. The hereditary theory failing
to account for the fact, the theory and as-
sumption drop together.  We have known
not a few women who have invented noble
traits and amiable qualities for men who
were intolerably stupid and vulgar, and had
their inventions return to plague them ever
after.—“Golden Age.”


A correspondent of the New York
“Tribune” predicts that central Illinois will
become the garden spot of the world. One
of them most likely.


﻿CARELESSNESS AND ITS RESULTS.
	Accounts of accidents by fire, drowning,
children being run over by teams, etc., are
becoming more and more frequent, notwith-
standing the constant efforts that are made,
and the new inventions that are being
continually brought to light, for the preven-
tion of such painful incidents.
	Now and then there seems to be an
almost supernatural force at work against
some individual, or company of individuals,
who have no chance or power to resist, and
are consequently obliged to submit to disap-
pointments, disasters and, frequently, death.
	Bnt much more common is it, that mis-
haps occur as a result of recklessness, or in-
judicious calculations, than otherwise. Nu-
merous instances are recorded of loving and
faithful mothers stepping out of their rooms
for a few minutes only, leaving their precious
little darling, or darlings, where a kettle of
scalding-hot water might be reached, or a
consuming fire be kindled, and returning to
find their little ones writhing in the agonies
of death, or already dead; or perhaps doomed
to wear out a wretched existence on earth,
rendered an object of pity to all beholders.
The sympathies of a community, the tears
of a world could be no compensation for the
loss sustained in that moment of affliction;
yet a little prudence and forethought might
have prevented the cause for sorrow.
	Broken limbs, crushed heads, and distress-
ing deaths, are too often the effects offathers
leaving their teams improperly secured,
with children in the vehicles to be run away
with.
	Children are sent generally, or permitted
to go out, to play that the house may be rid
of their noise; and not being duly cautioned
against running dangerous risks, or being
themselves forgetful and heedless, it fre-
quently occurs that they are run over in the
street, drowned, or made the victims of ac-
cidents in some other way.
	The loss of proqerty might be averted
many times were discretion observed as it
should be.
	With all the hazardous recklessness that
now abounds, is it any woner that so many
accidents are constantly occurring?


SLEEP.
	If no other reward could be anticipated,
for a good, substantial day’s work, and the
careful keeping of a clear conscience, a night
of calm, refreshing sleep is a compensation
not to be ignored by the most ambitious and
energetic.
	How is it that laborers in literature, and
in fact in nearly all the pursuits of life aside
from the farm and housework, have so for
converted their days into nights as to per-
form the greater part of their work by arti-
ficial light, and take their rest and sleep
while the sun shines?
	When Frederic the Great and one of his
literary friends conceived the idea of so far
overcoming and remodeling nature in them-
selves as to live and labor without sleep, the
absurdity of such an undertaking was clearly
illustrated by the attempt and failure they
made.  And it has been alleged by careful
demonstrators that a person in good health
will not take more sleep than nature re-
quires, and that from six to eight hours in
every twenty-four should be given to sleep
to aid the continuance of health and to pro-
mote longevity.
	Is it not very probable that one great
cause of the immature deaths in the present
age, may be attributed to a lack of indulg-
ence in this exhilarating restorative at the
proper time?
