﻿ABOUT WOMEN.
	Young ladies have now the necktie
mania.
	“Pooh ! who cares for Beecher,” says
Fanny Fern, “I used to go to school to his
sister.”
	A Burlington, Iowa, girl got show-
struck while a circus was exhibiting there,
and went off with it.
	A “woman’s rights” man, rather bashful,
and a little religious, asks his wife to “ say
grace” when they have company.
	Twenty thousand women keep body and
soul together in Switzerland by their skill
at the finer work of watch-making.
	Ida Lewis having married or otherwise
sacrificed her claims to public attention, a
Mrs. Clifford appears as her successor in the
capacity of an aquatic heroine.
	Mrs. Woodhull speaks freely of the abuse
and misrepresentations of which she had
been and is yet the victim, but says these
persecutions have but purified her.
	Mrs. Drummond, the Quaker preacher,
was asked whether the spirit ever inspired
her with the thought of getting married.
“No, my friend,” said she, “but the flesh
has.”
	Miss Kellogg is said to have a larger
repertoire than any living prima donna.
She is letter and note perfect in twenty-
five operas, in most of which she has sung
with success.
	A German woman, living in Goodhue
Co., Minnesota, yoked herself along side of a
cow this spring, and the two together hauled
a drag during the day after a seeder, which
was run by her husband with a span of
horses. 
	Mrs. Charles Kean, of London, was so de-
lighted with Kate Field’s elocution that she
besought the fair lecturer to adopt the stage.
But she does not propose to forsake the lec-
ture Field for the uncertain triumph of the
Boards quite yet.
	A medical college for the education of
women has been chartered by the New York
legislature, and two or three women of
wealth have agreed to furnish the college
with buildings, and secure its future useful-
ness by liberal funds.
	Somebody has said that life is an epigram
of which death is the point. Kate Sanborn
thinks it would be well if many of our novel-
ists would consent to starve the public for a
few years, and devote their diluted energies
to composing epigrams.
	The whole story of man’s regard for wo-
man, in countries where she is uneducated,
is told in the simple language of the-Modean
of Siberia, who, at the marriage ceremony,
places the bride on a mat, saying to the
bridegroom there, “Wolf, take thy lamb!”
	A servant girl at Pittsfield, Mass., it is
related, was so much impressed the other
night by dreaming that some one was robbing
the clothes line, that she got up and looked
out of the window, when she saw a man car-
rying off some clothes.  She was so over-
come by the singularity of the affair that she
let him go and did not tell the story till the
next day.
	Mrs. Olive Logan Sykes has come back
from the south-west, bringing with her, it
is said, the MS. of a volume which will ut-
terly demolish Stephen Pearl Andrews’ so-
cial pantarchy and Mrs. Woodhull’s conjugal
theories.  Those who have hesitated to
marry for fear that the institution would
collapse just as they had fairly invested in
it, may now take courage and apply for a
certificate.


﻿OBJECTS OF DRESS.
			      ______
	
	If women had but one object in selecting
and arranging their costumes—to make a
pleasing display, how far short of meeting
that object the fashionable ladies of to-day
would come, with their puffs and panniers,
their bends and bawbles,false hair,disfigured
forms, cramped feet, paint-spoiled complex-
ions and general bogus apppearance !
	But there are, really, two other objects in
dress, which should be held paramount to
this one, yet they are not generally so looked
upon.  The first object should be health, and
next to that convenience.   Yet these two
features are now so entirely overlooked in
ladies’ toilets, as to be trampled on and mar-
tyred without mercy ; merely for the sake
of adopting fashions that are absurd and
only serve to make their foolish votaries ap- 
pear ridiculous ; so that not one object, nei-
ther health, comfort nor true adornment, is
obtained.
	What time and means are spent for no
purpose except to make of ourselves exactly
what we do not wish to be ; poor, wretched,
unsightly invalids! If we had in view no
future, and lived only in and for the present
moment, would there not be more sanity in
our spending the little time alloted us here
in doing as much good in the world as we
can, taking measures to promote health and
happiness while we stay and to preserve life
as long as possible, than in taking the oppo-
site course as many are now doing ? And
if we look forward to another and higher
state of being than we now live in, how
shall we be prepared for it if we fritter away
our present existence in experimenting on
how the person may be arranged to carry
the most gewgaws and look the most
distorted ; working ourselves to death to
obtain all this fashionable nonsense, or
drawing the money for it from the pockets
of our fathers, brothers, or husbands, who
are sufficiently yielding to honor our whims,
while they may mourn over, our mania for
dress while being driven into bankruptcy
and despair!
	Daughters and sisters, where are your
hearts ! Wives and mothers, where are
your brains !
	Can not a consistency be arrived at and
held to in these matters, which will aid
greatly in bringing about a general reform ?
Or has the majority of womankind become
too selfish, sordid and heartless to conceive
of any higher aim than that of bringing dis-
grace upon herself, her lineage and her sex
by committing suicide by means of a slow
and miserable process!


THOUGHTS.
			       ____
FOR A BRANCH OF THE RETRENCHMENT
			ASSOCIATION.
			       ____
	Do we realize and  keep in mind, that
most of us, if not all, have formed many
good resolutions and covenanted before the
Lord to try and carry them out? What is
it to try to do a thing that the Lord has cer-
tainly commanded, and which our sense of
morality and our knowledge—though limited
—of the ways of God, teach us is pleasing in
His sight?
	To really try, trusting in the Lord for
power to accomplish His purposes concern-
ing us, is virtually to consumate the thing
designed. We may think we try—we may
say we have tried—to put away some idle
habit, some vain fancy, unwise passions, un-
due and perhaps causless jealousies, while
in reality we have merely been wishing,
and that not very strongly, that such and
such imperfections existing in our present
organizations, were overcome, or that we
had never possessed them. If a very quick-
tempered person realizing the folly of being 
easily provoked, says that she would give 
anything if she only could control her tem- 
per, and in a moment afterward flies into a
passion, on little or no provocation; and when 
cautioned against giving way to anger and 
saying unreasonable things, is silenced there- 
by, or expresses penitence; she thus shows 
her sincerity in desiring to overcome this 
natural failing. And you may be sure, if
she will not allow herself to become discour- 
aged, but continues to try, she will by and 
by reap the reward of a happy conquerer.
	But if, on the other hand, when reminded
that she must first use all her exertions to 
govern herself if she wishes to become able 
to do so with ease, she proves ungrateful for 
the interest taken in her welfare, and disre-
gards the kindly advice given, then you 
may justly suppose that the declaration con- 
cerning her temper was inconsiderately 
uttered, and that she does not really care to
overcome it or she would anxiously endea-
vor to do so.	
	These and similar rules for self-govern- 
ment are applicable to all proclivities of hu- 
man nature, as well as to the temper; and 
any propensity that flesh is heir to, may 
prove either a curse or a blessing to its pos- 
sessor, as it is governed or allowed to govern.
	To each of us is granted the inestimable 
privilege of working for and obtaining all
the blessings and rejecting the curses. But, 
in order to enjoy this privilege to the fullest
extent, we must never cease striving to
overcome, while there remains within our
beings one perceptible fault. Neither should
we take offense at kindly-offered criticisms,
which, if properly received, may prove of
much value to us, and for which we should
be grateful.
	We wish to attain to that perfection which
will fit us to dwell in the presence of our
Heavenly Parents, and to be associated
“with the noblesse of all Eternities.” It is a 
great object to achieve; and none of us will
be able to bear more than our own infirmi-
ties. We cannot overcome the least imper- 
fection for a neighbor, nor can we trust a
neighbor to do anything of the kind for us.
Yet, we can often greatly assist each other,
by gently and wisely criticising at the right
time and in the proper manner. Except in
some rare cases it is always unnecessary, and
unwise to speak of faults to any except those 
who possess them, and who alone can and 
should overcome them. And in doing this
we should be careful to, if possible, never
wound the feelings of the person we desire
to benefit.	
	When criticised, instead of seeking to ex-
culpate ourselves, let us look more closely 
to our thoughts and ways, and see if the 
hint is not really applicable; ever bearing in
mind that “it is easier to mend a fault than
to hide it.”			F.S.


In a paper read before the British Associ-
ation, Dr. Wilson argues that habitual crim-
inals do not possess such an amount of in-
tellect as to enable them to discriminate
between right and wrong, and that the
majority of them are devoid of common
sense; they are of a low type of intellectual
development, and some of them are unable
to surmount even the rudimentary difficul-
ties of education. Dr. W. states that he has
made several hundred separate measure-
ments, and all showed a cranial deficiency,
especially in the anterior lobe of the brain.
It is easy and charitable to believe the
Doctor’s logic correct.

﻿MISCELLANEOUS.
			    _____
	THE four principle smelting works for
the treatment of gold and silver ores in the
United States, and outside of Colorado, are
at San Francisco, California, Reno, Nevada,
Omaha, Nebraska, and Newark, N. J.
	THE yearly revenue of the Order of Odd
Fellows in the United States is now about
$3,500,000, and the average annual outlay
for purposes of relief is $1,000,000. The re-
mainder is reserved as a fund to be called
into use whenever required. The latest of-
ficial statistics of the Order are as follows:
Number of members, 757,941; number buried,
49,019; total relief paid, $16,542,256; total
receipts, $40,234,417; amount invested and
funds on hand, $23,690,170.
	FLORENCE NIGHTINGALE is said to be a
plain, simple woman, quite destitute of ro-
mance, poetry, or sentiment—no “Evangel-
ical” in belief or habit, no “professor,” least
of all a devotee—yet when she is thinking
how she can help the sick and wounded,
how she can improve the administration of
hospitals, her face takes on an expression of
pure, spiritual beauty, her eyes kindle with
enthusiasm, she is transported out of her-
self. The soldiers in the hospitals at Scutari
adored her as an angel, were happy if they
could but touch the hem of her garment,
and sent imploring looks after her shadow.
	HENRY WARD BEECHER says : “ Work.
There is some tendency among the laboring
classes to reduce the hours of work to eight.
I like it, and I abhor it. I like it, because
it is a sign of the growing intelligence of
the working classes; and I abhor it, because
it would seem that work was hateful. I
don’t believe that common people are going
to work out their fortunes by collusion, ex-
cept by hard knocks. I don’t believe that
the majority of them will be able, at eight
hours’ work, to educate their children and
to leave their families in a better condition.
I do not wish to say anything against the
working man—I who have the blood of
blacksmiths in my veins; but I want to
praise work.”
	LARGE TELESCOPE.—There is in process
of manufacture at Cambridgeport, Massachu-
setts, for the use of the United States Naval
Observatory at Washington, the largest re-
fracting telescope in the world. The object
glass is twenty-six inches in diameter, and
the telescope when completed will cost $46,-
000. McCormick, of McCormick reaper
fame, has ordered an instrument of the same
size, to be presented by him to the Lee and
Washington University of Virginia, his
native State. The next refracting telescope
in point of size is owned by Newhall, of
Gateshead, England. The telescope so
much prized by Harvard University has an
object glass of about fifteen inches diameter,
and the one connected with the Chicago
University has a diameter of eighteen and a
half inches.
	WILL MOTHERS heed the following:	A
very distinguished Paris physician says: “I
believe that, during the twenty years I have
practiced my profession, 20,000 children
have been carried to the cemeteries, a sacri-
fice to the absurd custom of exposing their
arms. Put the bulb of a thermometer into
a baby’s mouth, and the mercury rises to
ninety degrees. Now, carry the same to its
little hand; if the arm be bare and even
cool, the mercury will sink to fifty degrees.
Of course, all the blood that flows through
these arms must fall from ten to forty de-
grees below the temperature of the heart.
Need I say, when these currents of the
blood flow back to the chest, the child’s vi-
tality must be more or less compromised?
And need I add that we ought not to be
surprised at the frequently recurring affec-
tions of the tongue, throat, or stomach?”
	HERE is how Theodore Tilton talks in
the “Golden Age” of the woman recogni-
tion plank in the Philadelphia platform :
“It is something that the women got a rec-
ognition at the Philadelphia Convention.
Of course everybody knows that the woman
plank is the thinnest bit of pasteboard imag-
inable, and means about as much as “ Very
truly Yours,” at the end of a letter; and we
never heard of a woman’s basing a suit for
breach of promise on this complimentary
appendage to an ordinary epistle. The wo-
men who see the dawn of the millennium in
the nebulous generalities of the Philadelphia
platform, must be in a desperate plight, and
like the ancient maiden we hear of, and al-
ways regarded as apocryphal, who exclaimed
out of her impatience, ‘Anything in the
shape of a husband, good Lord !’ If Mrs.
Livermore and Lucy Stone and Miss Gordon
can wax eloquent over Grant, their enthusi-
asm must be irrepressible.”
	"THERE ARE so many of us. We are
like ‘the blades of grass,' as Red Cloud says.
We cannot all be supported, depend and
cling as we may. We must look out and do
for ourselves.
	“It is now pretty generally admitted that
a woman may know something of business
—of bookkeeping, banking, and even law—
without loosing the peculiar charm and
beauty of her womanhood ; that such stud-
ies as are pursued here do not necessarily
rub the bloom from the peach of maidenly
innocence, the frost from the plum of mod-
est reserve, the gold-dust from the wing of
the feminine soul. True womanhood is not
to be essentially changed by changed pur-
suits and conditions. There is a tough vi-
tality about tender womanhood. It is a
tree, whose roots take hold on eternal life.”
	[Extract from Grace Greenwood’s address
in behalf of the Lady Graduates of the
Washington Business College, June 17,
1870.]
	THE "GOLDEN AGE" says: When the
Englishman wants money for a charitable or
religious purpose he collects together a large
number of wealthy old gentlemen, and after
getting them into a good humor by good
feeding and warming their hearts to a glow
of generosity by plenty of wine, depletes
their purses to an important extent. The
public dinner is really a gentle kind of gar-
roting. At these dinners, which are for the
most part insufferably dull, no woman is under
any circumstances admitted, though of late
years, by a daring innovation, ladies have
been admitted to the galleries to look down
and see their male friends eat, themselves
being regaled by a little wine and sand-
witches handed around. It was into one of
these solid and stupid dinner parties that
our brilliant countrywoman, Miss Kate Field,
was smuggled the other day, without being
scarcely noticed by any one present, until
after the usual heavy toasts and heavier re-
sponses about the Queen and Royal Family
and Army and Navy; then the usual senti-
ment to women was proposed and our fair
revolutionist rose with easy grace, and with
sparkling wit, with winning voice, and
twinkling eyes, won them from their first
stupor of fright; then one and another began
to snicker at her sallies; then followed one
or two hearty laughs, and finally the whole
table was in a roar. The effect of her speech
was electrical, and already some of the solid
Englishers who attend those dinners confess
that they would be more attractive were
ladies invited to partake of them, and then
to open their lips afterward.

﻿MISCELLANEOUS.
The four principle smelting works for
the treatment of gold and silver ores in the
United States, and outside of Colorado, arc
at San Francisco, California, Reno, Nevada,
Omaha, Nebraska, and Newark, N. J.
The yearly revenue of the Order of Odd
Fellows in the United States is now about
$3,500,000, and the average annual outlay
for purposes of relief is $1,000,000. The re-
mainder is reserved as a fund to be called
into use whenever required. The latest of-
ficial statistics of the Order are as follows:
Number of members, 737,9-11; number buried,
9,010; total relief paid, $10,542,236; total
receipts, $10,234,417; amount invested and
funds on hand, $23,690,170.
Florence Nightingale is said to be a
plain, simple woman, quite destitute of ro-
mance, poetry, or sentiment—no “Evangel-
ical” in belief or habit, no “professor,” least
of all a devotee—yet when she is thinking
how she can help the sick and wounded,
how she can improve the administration of
hospitals, her face takes on an expression of
pyre, spiritual, beauty, her eyes kindle with
enthusiasm, she is transported out of her-
self. The soldiers in the hospitals at Scutari
adored her as an angel, were happy if they
could but touch the hem of her garment,
and sent imploring looks after her shadow.
Henry "Ward Beecher says : “ Work.
There is some tendency among the laboring
classes to reduce the hours of work to eight.
I like it, and I abhor it. I like it, because
it is a sign of the growing intelligence of
the working classes; and I abhor it, because
it would seem that work was hateful. I
don’t believe that common people are going
to work out their fortunes by collusion, ex-
cept by hard knocks. I don’t believe that
the majority of them will be able, at eight
hours’ work, to educate their children and
to leave their families in a better condition.
I do not wish to say anything against the
working man—I who have the blood of;
blacksmiths in my veins but I want to
praise work.”
Large Telescope.—There is in process
of manufacture at Cambridgeport, Massachu-
setts, for the use of the United States Naval
Observatory at Washington, the largest re-
fracting telescope in the world. The object
glass is twenty-six inches in diameter, and
the telescope when completed will cost $46,-
000. McCormick, of McCormick reaper
fame, has ordered an instrument of the same
size, to be presented by him to the Lee and
Washington University of Virginia, his
native State. The next refracting telescope
in point of size is owned by Newhall, of
Gateshead, England. The telescope so
much prized by Harvard University has an
object glass of about fifteen inches diameter,
and the one connected with the Chicago
University has a diameter of eighteen and a
half inches.
Wile mothers heed the following: A
very distinguished Paris physician says: “I
believe that, during the twenty years I have
practiced my profession, 20,000 children
have been carried to the cemeteries, a sacri-
fice to the absurd custom of exposing their
arms. Put the bulb of a thermometer into
a baby’s mouth, and the mercury rises to
ninety degrees. Now, carry the same to its
little hand; if the arm he bare and even
cool, the mercury will sink to fifty degrees.
Of course, all the blood that flows through
these arms must fall from ten to forty de-
grees below the temperature of the heart.
Need I say, when these currents of the
blood flow back to the chest, the child’s vi-
tality must be more or less compromised?
And need I add that we  ought not to be
surprised at the frequently recurring affec-
tions of the tongue, throat, or stomach?”
Here is how Theodore Tilton talks in
the “Golden Age” of the woman recogni-
tion plank in the Philadelphia platform :
“It is something that the women got a rec-
ognition at the Philadelphia Convention.
Of course everybody knows that the woman
plank is the thinnest bit of pasteboard imag-
inable, and means about as much as “ Very
truly Yours,” at the end of a letter; and we
never heard of a woman’s basing a suit for
breach of promise on this complimentary
appendage to an ordinary epistle. The wo-
men who see the dawn of the millennium in
the nebulous generalities of the Philadelphia
platform, must be in a desperate plight, and
like the ancient maiden we hear of, and al-
ways regarded as apocryphal, who exclaimed
out of her impatience, ‘Anything in the
shape of a husband, good Lord !’ If Mrs.
Livermore and Lucy Stone and Miss Gordon
can wax eloquent over Grant, their enthusi-
asm must be irrepressible.”
“There are so many of us. We are
like the blades of grass, as Rod Cloud says.
We cannot all be supported, depend and
cling as we may. We must look out and do
for ourselves.
“ It is now pretty generally admitted that
a woman may know something of business
—of bookkeeping, banking, and even law—
without loosing the peculiar charm and
beauty of her womanhood ; that such stud-
ies as are pursued here do not necessarily
rub the bloom from the peach of maidenly
innocence, the frost from the plum of mod-
est reserve, the gold-dust from the wing of
the feminine soul. True womanhood is not
to be essentially changed by changed pur-
suits and conditions. There is a tough vi-
tality about tender womanhood. It is a
tree, whose roots take hold on eternal life.”
Extinct from Grace Greenwood’s address
in behalf of the Lady Graduates of the
Washington Business College, June 17,
l870.
The “Golden Age” says: When the
Englishman wants money for a charitable or
religious purpose lie collects together a large
number of wealthy old gentlemen, and after
getting them into a good humor by good
feeding and warming their hearts to a glow
of generosity by plenty of wine, depletes
their purses to an important extent. The
public dinner is really a gentle kind of gar-
roting. At these dinners, which are for the
most part insufferably dull, no woman is under
any circumstances admitted, though of late
years, by a daring innovation, ladies have
been admitted to the galleries to look down
aud see their male friends eat, themselves
being regaled by a little wine and sand-
witches handed around. It was into one of
these solid and stupid dinner parties that
our brilliant countrywoman, Miss Kate Field,
was smuggled the other day, without being
scarcely noticed by any one present, until
after the usual heavy toasts and heavier re-
sponses about the Queen and Royal Family
and Army and Navy; then the usual senti-
ment to women was proposed and our fair
revolutionist rose with easy grace, and with
sparkling wit, with winning voice, and
twinkling eyes, won them from their first
stupor of fright; then one and another began
to snicker at her sallies; then followed one
or two hearty laughs, and finally the whole
table was in a roar. The effect of her speech
was electrical, and already some of the solid
Englishers who attend those dinners confess
that they would be more attractive were
ladies Invited to partake of them, and then
to open their lips afterward.


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
			—
  THE Wisconsin tobacco crop for 1872 is
estimated at 10,000,000 pounds.
  THE Odd Fellows of Indiana, in even
numbers, are 20,000 strong.
  THE biggest sale of real estate ever made
in Minnesota was that of 31,500 acres in
Todd and Otter Tail counties sold the other
day to a London firm for $80,000.
   KOOPMANSCHAP has gone to wreck at
last, on the Mongolian wave, with liabilities
of $260,000 and any quantity of assets in
Chinamen’s notes, quotable at a cent a pound
for old paper.
  IN 1871 the iron mills of Illinois rolled
40,026 tons of rails, iucluding 2,800 of sheet
rails, and rounded 51,152 tons, making a to-
tal production of 91,178 tons of railroad bars,
which places Illinois next to Pennsylvania
in the manufacture of rails.
   FRANZ ABT works a whole year in Ger-
many for about $1,500, while in this coun-
try twice that sum is lavished in his enter-
tainment almost nightly.  America must be
to him the Eldorado of his dream days.
  THE total membership of the Orthodox
Quakers at present in the United States is
57,105, being an increase of 715 in 20 years.
   AT THE Methodist General Conference it
was stated the other day that the church
has $70,000,000 of property, and that before
long it will amount to $100,000,000.
  THERE are 215 cadets at the Naval Acad-
emy, 39 of whom were appointed by the
President, four are naval apprentices, three
Japanese students, one is from the District
of Columbia, and 169 from the various
States.
  THE American Printworks at Fall River,
Mass., are arranging to suspend operations
for a season on account of the price of goods.
The amount of business done at the works,
requires $2,100 a day to carry it on, and
nearly $3,000,000 worth of cloth is in process
of printing from the time it goes into the
mill before it is finished.
   THERE are over 650,000 scholars in atten-
dance at the public schools in Illinois.
Teachers number over 20,000. School
houses, log, frame, brick and stone, 10,773.
Expenditures for school purposes for the
past year, $7,000,000.
   LEXINGTON, Ky., has nine hemp facto-
ries, employing an average of about sixty
hands each. The average amount of hemp
consumed per day the year round by these
firms, is from twenty to thirty tons, necessi-
tating the planting by the farmers to supply
this demand of about 30,000 acres of the
best land.
   THE following are the dimensions of the
great bridge now being erected between
New York and Brooklyn: Total length,
5,682 feet; length of central span, 1,600 ;
elevation above high water, 130 ; Width of
bridge floor, 80 feet; height of towers above
high water, 268 feet; base of towers at wa-
ter line, 121 by 56 feet.
  THE Vulcan Iron Works, Carondolet, Mo.,
employs 800 men in relays of five hours
each. The furnaces are turning out from
seventy to eighty tons of pig iron daily.
One of them is making pig iron directly
from pure. Iron Mountain (Mo.) ore, to fill a
contract with the Bessemer Steel Works at
Chicago. The rail mill is making forty tons
per day. The largest vertical, direct-acting,
blowing engine west of Pittsburg is here ;
its steam cylinder has a diameter of five feet
and stroke of nine feet; the blowing cylin-
der, a diameter of ten feet and stroke of nine
feet. Its capacity is 20,000 cubic feet of air
per minute.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿The ladies of the Congregational Society
of San Mateo, realized from their “Mother
Goose party” over $300.


﻿Ohio has enacted by law that eighty-
eight cubic inches of bituminous coal shall
weigh just sixty pounds avoirdupois.


A Connecticut company is putting up a
factory for the building of carriages made
entirely of India rubber, excepting the axles
and tires. A decided superiority is claimed
for the material over wood.


﻿In 1787 the Emperor of China issued the
following decree: “All persons of the female
sex are forbidden, upon any pretext what-
ever, to enter a temple or quit their houses,
except in cases of absolute necessity.
Fathers, husbands, sons or relatives are
commanded to keep them at home on pain
of being themselves severely punished.”
What a believer in equal rights! The men,
most likely, had as hard a time of it as the
women.


The Massachusetts Labor Union Conven-
tion met in Boston on the 28th ult. Wendell
Phillips offered a series of resolutions, which
were adopted after a long debate, approving
all labor associations, announcing the belief,
expediency and necessity of strikes, but
denouncing violence; advocating congres-
sional establishment of labor unions; declar-
ing that all dividend paying companies
should be obliged to divide half of the net
profits among employes, and that imported
pauper contract work should be dutyable.

[next column]
		   WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

		A UTAH LADIES' JOURNAL.

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them ; and 
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.

	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"

	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.

	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

  Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings; 
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.

	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.

	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.

   TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year, 	  $2.00	1 Copy six months, $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months, 9.00

	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a 
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


Woman's Exponent.
									

VOL. I		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JULY 15,1872.﻿	NO. 4.


NEWS AND VIEWS.	
			    _____
	Dead At last—The Freedmen’s Bureau.
	In November, which? Grant or Greeley?
	The Boston Jubilee did pay ex-
penses after all, it is averred. Happy Gil-
more! Thrice happy Boston!
	Newspaper Blackguarding and gross
misrepresentation are among the chief char-
acteristics of the Presidential campaign, as
usual.
	Gilmore’s Grand gathering of the
musical clans at Boston closed on the
Fourth. The sad refrain comes across the
continent that the Jubilee did not pay.
	Chicago With an eye to thrift and pru-
dence, grants licenses for the side of fire-
crackers, and assesses a fine of ten dollars
for exploding them. That is municipal
wisdom unalloyed.
	England Has discovered a new liquor to
get drunk on. It is composed of naptha and
ether, will save wheat and barley, and may
possibly fill prisons, madhouses and graves
as rapidly as older stimulating beverages.
	Fifteen Hundred dollars for each ap-
pearance, and expenses paid, was the tune
to which Madame Peschka Leutner sung at
the Jubilee. She is reported to have “a
magnificent organ,” and her notes are un-
questionably golden ones.
	Harvard college has appointed a com-
mittee to consider the propriety of admit-
ting girls; and Dartmouth is exercised be-
cause the woman of the age demands ad-
mission. The world moves, and women
may claim the right, by and by, to have the
best education which the first seat of learn-
ing in the country can impart.
	Kentucky has another hero, a four-
footed one, “Longfellow” by name, that won
the Monmouth cup at the late Long Branch
races; and Kentucky is as enthusiastic over
the fleet quadruped as if it were a gifted
son of the State, who had performed some
noble deed which would reflect honor on
himself, his family and the place that gave
him birth.
	The Stokes trial for the murder of Fisk
draws to a close, and the usual insanity
fiction has been set up as a plea by the de-
fense. If a few of these prominent cases of
“insanity,” as developed in murders, were
confined for life in a well-conducted mad-
house, there would be fewer instances of it
being set up as a plea to save the hangman
of a job. Will Stokes be executed? Very
unlikely.
	Miss Nellie Grant is the champion
traveler. The telegraphic news lately an-
nounced she was in Switzerland, and the
next night the same reliable source of in-
telligence had her in London at a session of
the House of Lords. Doubtless Miss Nellie
is an estimable young woman, but the way
she is talked of in the newspapers must be a
source of great annoyance in the future
when she comprehends fully how ridiculous
they have sought to make her appear with
their tawdry toadying.
	The first State election of the present
campaign will be held in North Carolina on
August 1st. It is expected that the Grant
party will tarry the State, for it is alleged
that large sums of money are being used for
that purpose. The statement speaks vol-
umes concerning the “patriotism,” “intelli-
gence” and “love of country” which ani-
mate the voter man, bleached or colored. If
the allegation be correct, and we have not
seen it disputed, the integrity and safety of
the country merely depend upon a man or
party rich enough to buy power.
	Mrs.	 Hartvie proposes, as Mrs.
Gorden, when she stepped on the platform
in Cincinnati and boldly claimed her right
to speak, was insulted by the “hoodlums”
of the assembly and forced to withdraw,
that in future women go one hundred in
number and stand up all together, like an
iron ring, and surround and protect their
elected epeaker. That would be rather sen-
sational, and, a pleasant “out” for some of
us; but. who would pay the expenses, and
look after the babies while we were about
it, is a question that will bear consideration.
	Three American women, unaccustomed
to the proprieties which are deemed essen-
tial in many places outside of the United
States, went walking fashionably dressed at
a late hour, out in Edinburgh, Scotland, to
visit Holyrood Palace. Their stylish dress
attracted attention, and a mob gathered
around them, a couple of policemen having
finally to afford them protection and escort
them to a place of safety. There is a little
moral attached to this, which the public can
furnish to it themselves.
	The nomination of Horace Greeley and
B. Gratz Brown, a couple of editors, made
at Cincinnati in May, has been repeated at
Baltimore. On Wednesday last, the 10th
inst., the National Democratic Convention,
on the first ballot, gave Greeley 686 votes
out of 732, and then made his nomination
unanimous. Brown, on the first ballot, re-
ceived 713 votes, when his nomination was
also made unanimous. These two men,
therefore, are the chosen candidates of two
great Conventions, and as opposed to Grant
and Wilson have strong hopes of election to
the highest offices within the gift of the
people. Women cannot vote in the States,
or they would be even stronger.
	Jennie Bronson has been the wife of a
Mr. Holbrook, of New Orleans. But a di-
vorce came between them, and a new Mrs.
Holbrook took the place formerly occupied
by Jennie. Quite recently, while the second
Mrs. H. was making her toilet, Jennie
entered her dressing room and fired at her
twice with a pistol. The two women clutched,
when the divorced woman took a bay rum
bottle and battered it into pieces over the
other’s head. She then demolished furni-
ture and fixings, routed the domestics, and
was finally removed to prison, leaving a
dying woman behind. This should be
charged to a madly jealous disposition and
an ungoverned temper; but it might be
charged to matrimony and divorce just as
sensibly as some people charge every do-
mestic difficulty in Utah to plural marriage.
	On Wednesday last, the 10th inst., in
the National Democratic Convention at
Baltimore, Horace Greeley, a life-long an-
tagonist of the Democratic party, was
unanimously nominated as their candidate
for the Presidency of the United States. It
was one of the most startling political acts
that our country has yet seen. And in ac-
cepting that nomination Mr. Greeley said,
the time would yet come when it would be
acknowledged that they were none the less
Democrats because they nominated him, and
that he was none the less a Republican be-
cause he accepted the nomination. There is a
strong combination of political elements all
over the country, not long ago bitterly op-
posed to each other, but now united in the
determination to make Mr. Greeley the
next President of the United States, and the
indications point to their success.
	Three leading National Conventions
have lately been held, at Cincinnati, Phila-
delphia and Baltimore, and the advocates of
woman suffrage endeavored to present their
claims before each. At Cincinnati they were
rather ungracefully treated. At Philadel-
phia a quasi recognition was given to their
demands. And as the Baltimore Conven-
tion adopted the Cincinnati platform, no ac-
knowledgement was made there of the right
of woman to the ballot. The cause of wo-
man suffrage evidently finds little sympathy
with any of the great-political parties. The
Philadelphia allusion to the services of wo-
man and her rights, was included in the
platform for political effect, but so transpar-
ent as to be seen through at a glance. Had the
Republican party meant in good faith what
their platform set forth, their Congressmen
could easily have passed an amendment to
the Constitution conferring the suffrage
upon woman, and sent it to the Legislatures
of the different States for ratification, as
they did with the one enfranchising the
negroes. Women will gain a just recogni-
tion of the right demanded when the peo-
ple at large concede it, regardless of politi-
cians or party platforms.
	Some five years ago a Miss Barber, of
Milford, Massachusetts, conceived the ro-
mantic idea of carrying civilization and re-
finement to the Indians by marrying a chief
and accompanying him to his home in the
wilderness. She was married to Squatting
Bear, a young chief of the Brule Sioux, who
was at Washington, in 1867, and went with
him to his hunting-ground in Dacota. There
she found that Fennimore Cooper’s glowing
descriptions of the noble red man, if not
highly colored pieces of prose poetry, had
no reality among the Sioux. She found
several other Mrs. Squatting Bears occupy-
ing the lodge of her dusky liege-lord. In-
stead of being approached with reverence
as a being of a superior race, and listened to
with eagerness while she poured noble sen-
timents into the ears of those dwellers of
the plains, she was treated like the other
squaws, compelled to do the usual drudgery
of an Indian’s wife, beaten like a dog, and
compelled to witness the murder of one of
his squaws by her savage husband. She
tried to escape, was captured, nearly killed,
and sold to a Cheyenne chief for three
ponies. After being with the Cheyennes
until last Spring, she succeeded in escaping
to Fort Benton, and subsequently returned
to Massachusetts. Romance often makes sad
havoc of the future of young women, but it
is seldom that it weaves a web of such bar-
barous sufferings as in this instance.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.

	Mrs. A. M. Frodsham, of Ogden, continues
to manifest a lively interest in our journal,
to which she politely forwards the following
synopsis of the report of a meeting held by
the F. R. Society of that city, on the 27th of
June:
   “We were much pleased and gratified to
have the company of sisters Eliza R. Snow,
Zina D. Young, Bathsheba W. Smith and
sister Horne, from Salt Lake City, at our
meeting. The President being absent, her
Counselors, Mrs Martha Bingham and Mrs.
Sarah Herrick, took charge of the meeting,
which was opened by some excellent sing-
ing followed with prayer by Mrs. A. M.
Frodsham; and singing again. The meet-
ing was addressed by sisters Z. D. Young,
B. W. Smith and I. Horne, who gave some
excellent counsel and words of encourage-
ment; after which sister E. R. Snow said:
We are all engaged in one great work, the
redemption of mankind. In this work wo-
man hikes a most important part. Upon her
rests the responsibilty, in a great measure,
of giving character to all societies; and this
is for each one of us. God is giving us
opportunities to prove ourselves here. It is
essential that we work for our dead. Work
seems to be the watchword of this genera-
tion. The training of our children is one of
the most weighty duties we have to per-
form; to teach them the difference between
right and wrong, between good and evil in
all respects, so far as we have learned our-
selves to comprehend. She closed by invok-
ing the blessing of God upon those present.
	Sister Richards briefly expressed her feel-
ings, saying she had been much interested
and edified. Donations were taken for the
emigration of the poor to the amount of
sixty-eight dollbrs and some cents, and more
will be collected, it is presumed, to make as
much as $100.”

	Of the Third Ward F.R. Society, of Provo,
we receive a brief but pleasant account from
Mrs. M. John, Secretary.
	A meeting was held on the 6th of June
at the house of Mrs. Tanner, President of
the Society.  A long and severe illness had
prevented Mrs. Tanner’s meeting with the
Society for a length of time. General satis-
faction was expressed by the sisters present,
that her life had been spared and her health
improved so that she could again be able to
meet with them; and she expressed herself
as being truly thankful to God for His good-
ness, and to the brethren and sisters for
their faith and prayers, which had been ex-
ercised in her behalf. The Society seems to
be in a very flourishing condition, Mrs.
John concluding: "Happy to say there are
none needing assistance in the Ward at
present."

	The following from Mrs. Elmira S. Taylor,
Secretary of the 14th Ward F. R. Society of
this city, is brief but comprehensive:
	I send you a brief report of a meeting of the
F. R. Society of the14th Ward, held in the
Society Hall, June 26th. We were favored
with the presence of our Bishop, Brother
Thomas Taylor, who gave us some excellent
instructions, and whose remarks were most
encouraging. He said  he was one with the
Society, that he highly appreciated their
efforts, and would do all in his power to
enable them to carry on their laudable work.
	Sisters E.R. Snow, Bathsheba W. Smith
and Sarah Richards were also present, and
gave much wise and good counsel.
	There are many poor in the Ward who
are assisted by the Society.  The attendance
at meetings is small, but those who do at-
[new column]
tend are energetic and faithful, and feel
blest in the discharge of their duties. Too
much praise cannot be accorded to our
President, Mrs. I. Horne, as she is most in-
defatigable in her efforts to advance the
interests of the Society, and ameliorate the
condition of the poor.

	From Mrs. A. S. Rogers, Secretary of the
F. R. Society of Farmington, we receive a
pleasant report of the fourth annnal meeting
of that Society, held June 19th. Much
valuable instruction was given, and great
satisfaction expressed for the progress the
Society has made, by those who addressed
the meeting, namely; Sisters E. R. Snow,
P. L. Kimball and Z. D. Young, from Salt
Lake City, Elders Arthur Stayner, Job
Welling, Hector Haight, Ezra Clark, John
Tibbits, and the President, Mrs. S. J.
Holmes.
	The financial report shows that since the
organization of the Society $822.85 has been
taken in; $348.85 given to the poor, and
$181.84 donated for public purposes; leaving
a handsome balance in the Treasury.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
				
  POLITENESS IS contagious, and a kind
word, a gracious smile, and a delicate tact
to make sunshine in the heart, are felt and
will have their influence over the conduct and
character of others, which is secretly inspir-
ing them with a love for virtue. A lady
never appears to so much advantage as when
doing the honors of her home. There she
has opportunity for the full development of
her character and. a display of the charms
which are truly her highest ornaments. The
care of an accomplished and well-bred wo-
man will so regulate an establishment, that
visitors may at any time be received with-
out apologies.
  COUNTERPANES THAT are neat and light,
and easy to wash, can be made in this way:
Take new cotton cloth; make it longer than
a sheet, and if for a wide bed, two breadths
and a half; before being wet, draw in candle-
wick, in diamonds of about a finger apart,
and cut off to leave ends on the out side
about half an inch long; then wash and
bleach them, and they are durable and look
very tidy. One can be made in a day and a
half or two days with a needle that has a
very large eye, like the packing needle.
Draw the wick in double, and if done true
the diamond will be formed.
  NITRE MOISTENED with water is said to be
an excelled wash to remove freckles from
the skin.
  DISSOLVE, FOR fruit cream, half an ounce
of isinglass in warm water enough to cover
it; add to this a pint of cream, .sweetened
with four spoonfuls of sugar, and boil it.
When cool, lay some apricot, raspberry or
peach preserves in a glass dish and pour the
cream over it.
  STRONG ALUM water, often applied, is a
great enemy to warts, and seldom fails to
drive them away without danger or damage
to the afllicted party. To cut or pick a wart
until it bleeds freely, and then put on it a
little quick lime is a sure cure, and not
painful as one would think who had not
tried it.
  ANY HARD, steel tool will cut glass with
great facility when kept freely wet with
camphor dissolved in turpentine. -The rag-
ged edges of glass vessels may also be thus
easily smoothed by a flat file.
  TO MAKE hard gingerbred, take one quart
of molasses, two cupfuls of sugar, three
quarters of a pound of butter and lard, one
cupful of ginger, a teaspoonful of black pep-
[next column]
per and a tablespoonful of cloves, cinnamon
and allspice.
  DURABLE WHITEWASH. —Whitewashed
walls will not rub off if the lime is thus pre-
pared: Mix half a pailful of lime and water;
take half a pint of flour, make a starch of it,
and pour it hot into the whitewash; stir it
well, and add a little salt.
  JELLY.—To make a quart, soak one oz. of
gelatine in a pint of cold water for twenty
minutes, then add the same quantity of
boiling water, stir until dissolved; add the
juice and peel of two lemons, with enough
sugar to sweeten; have ready, well beaten,
the white and shell of one egg stir these
briskly into the jelley, then boil for two
minutes without stirring it; remove it from
the fire, and allow it to stand two minutes,
then strain through a close flannel bag;
this jelly may be flavored or colored
according to taste.
   COTTAGE PUDDING.—Warm two and a
half tablespoonsful of butler, stir in a teacup
of white sugar and two eggs. Put two tea-
spoonsful of cream tartar in one pint of flour,
add a teaspoonful of soda dissolved in a cup
of milk; flavor with lemon or vanilla. Bake
three-quarters of an hour in a moderate
oven, and serve hot with rich hot sauce.
  MORNING BISCUITS.—One quart of flour,
half a teaspoonful of salt, two tablespoonsful
of yeast, and one pint of sour milk, with
half a teaspoonful of soda dissolved in it.
Work this into a dough; then rub into the
dough half a cup of butter. Knead well; cut
off some bits; shape them into biscuits; lay
them in the bake pan, cover closely with a
broadcloth, and let them stand over night,in a
warm place in the Winter, and a cold place
in the Summer. Bake in the morning for
breakfast.
  BREAD PUDDING.—One pint of fine bread
crumbs, one cup of sugar, two tablespoonsful
of molasses, half a pound of suet chopped
fine, one coffee-cup of raisins, half a rind of
preserved orange-peel or citron cut thin and
fine, very little nutmeg and cinnamon, two
teaspoonsful cream tartar, one small tea-
spoonful soda, one teaspoonful of salt. Stir
in milk enough to make it thick as pound-
cake; beating all thoroughly together; put it
into a buttered pudding mould, and boil
three hours. Be careful to keep the water
boiling all the time.
		————————


﻿“MUST I pray too, mother?” asked a two
year old boy, with flaxen hair, and mild
blue eyes, as the family were called together
for morning prayers. “Yes, dear, of course
you must,” answered his mother. “What
shall I pray for?” continued the little quer-
ist. “For whatever you want most,” was
the response; and while his father was offer-
ing up his prayer, the little fellow was over-
heard to whisper loudly hut reverently,
“Pray, Father, give me some candy! Pray,
O, Father, give me some candy!”  It is
needless to say that the innocent and sweet
petition was heard and promptly answered.


﻿NOT THE MATERIAL FOR A SHAKERESS.
—Mary Hogan was too pretty a girl to be a
Shakeress. They tied her hair and ears
back, and distorted her fair proportions by
uncomely dress, but she still refused to be
plain. Brother Jackson, warmed out of cold
faith by love and the weather, eloped with
and married Mary a few days ago. When
interviewed by some of the scandalized
brethren, Mary is reported to have said:
“You can make your apple sass and war-
rant it to keep; but gals ain’t apples, and
you.can’t bile ’em down so they won’t sour
on your old rules about marrying.”—“Wash-
ington Star.”


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


NEARER HOME.

	One of Alice Cary’s songs has a story con-
nected with it, of a young man in a gamb-
ling house at Hong Kong, who sang one
verse while idly shufiling the cards. The
young man’s companion, old in sin, was so
rebuked by the sweet sentiment of the song
that he arose from the play and with tears
in his eyes said, “Come, Harry; here’s what
I won from you; go and use it for some good
purpose. As for me, as God sees me, I have
played my last game, and drank my last
bottle. I have misled you, Harry, and I am
sorry. Give me your hand, my boy, and
say that, for old America’s sake, if for no
other, you will quit this infernal business.”
	The gentleman who told the story
(originally published in “The Boston Daily
News”) saw these two men leave the gamb-
linghouse together, and walk away arm in
arm; and he remarked: “It must be a
source of great joy to Miss Cary to know
that her lines, which have comforted so many
Christian hearts, have been the means of
awakening in the breasts of two tempted
and erring men on the other side of the
globe, a resolution to lead a better life.”
Here is the song:
	One sweetly solomn thought
	  Comes to me o’er and o’er;
	I’m nearer my home to-day
	  Than I ever have been before;

	Nearer my Father’s house,
	  Where the many mansions be;
	Nearer the great white throne,
	  Nearer the crystal sea;

	Nearer the bound of life,
	  Where we lay our burdens down;
	Nearer leaving the cross,
	  Nearer.gaining the crown. 

	But the waves of that silent sea
	  Roll dark before my sight,
	That brightly the other side
	  Break on a shore of light.

	O, if my mortal feet
	  Have almost gained the brink,
	If it be I am nearer home
	  Even to-day than I think,

	Father, perfect my trust.
	  Let my spirit feel in death
	That her feet are firmly set
	  On the Rock of living faith.


﻿WOMAN'S DETERMINATION.
				
   Among the evidences of woman’s deter-
mination to enlarge her resources, is notable
that of a lady who acts as coal agent for a
large firm in this city. She solicits orders
from merchants and private families, and is
so successful as to be enabled thereby to sup-
port comfortably an infirm father and
mother, a little child, an insane brother and
herself. One old conservative gentleman
whose ideas of woman’s sphere do not ex-
tend beyond the hearthstone, when told that
a lady wished to see hint about ordering
coal, showed his disgust by exclaiming on
her entrance to his presence:
   “Woman in the coal business! What
next, I wonder. The men will have to take
in dress-making to compete with them."
  “Beg your pardon, sir,” answered the
lady, “But the men have already done so,
and met with success too. There is----,
who takes in washing and has it done by
men too.”
  The old gentleman looked slightly dumb-
founded at this retort, but quickly recover-
ing, he smiling, acknowledged the “corn,”
and gave her a liberal order for his gear’s
supply of coal, both for his business and
private residence as well.—“New York Ex-
press.”


﻿FLOWERS BY THE WAYSIDE.
			    —
 Whatever purifies also fortifies the heart.
 Charity is an eternal debt, and without
limit.
 A beautiful external life symbolizes a
beautiful soul.
 It is easier to be wise for others than for
ourselves, yet such wisdom is wasted.
 Eagerness to grasp, often strangles and
destroys pleasure.
 Innocence confers freedom to the mind,
and leaves it open to every pleasing sensa-
tion.
  “Errors, like straw, upon the surface flow;
but he who seeks for pearls must dive be-
low.”
  The chief misfortunes in life can be traced
to some vices or follies committed by the
sufferers.
 The spirit of love, like the fragrance of
flowers, may be inhaled while the object is
out of sight.
  The veil which covers from our sight the
events of succeeding years, is woven by the
hand of mercy.
  The most forlorn and helpless sinner was
once an innocent child, possessed of untold
possibilities of good.
  Whatever useful or engaging endowments
we possess, virtue is requisite to their shin-
ing with proper lustre.
  Diligence, industry and a proper im-
provement of time, will never fail of gain-
ing great and just rewards.
  The desire to improve denotes a liberal'
mind, and is connected with many accom-
plishments and many virtues.
 They who have nothing to give but sym-
pathy can often afford relief by imparting
what they feel.
 It is as difficult for revenge to act without
exciting suspicion, as for a rattlesnake to
stir without making a noise.
  Many mistake the admiration for the
practice of virtue, and are not so much good
friends as the friends of goodness.
  Restraint is the golden rule of enjoyment;
we appreciate no pleasures properly, unless
occasionally debarred from them.
  A temperate spirit and moderate expecta-
tions are excellent safeguards of the mind
in this uncertain and changing world.
  In the darkest night there still is light in
the sky somewhere shining up from the
sun or coming down from the distant stars.
  There is nothing except simplicity of in-
tention and purity of principle that can
stand the test of near approach and strict
examination.
  Mere intellectual acuteness, if divested,
as it often is, of all that is comprehensive of
goodness, is more revolting than the most
helpless imbecility.
  The.best preparation for all the uncertain-
ties of futurity, consists in a well-ordered
mind, a clear conscience, and a cheerful sub-
mission to the will of Heaven. 
  To be wise in our own eyes, to be wise in
the opinion of the world, and to be wise in
the sight of our Creator, are three things so
very different as rarely to come together.
  If you take a great deal of pains to serve
and benefit your fellow creatures, and after
all they scarcely thank you for the trouble
you have taken, do not be angry and make
a loud noise about the world’s ingratitude;
for then it would seem that you cared
more about the thanks than the blessings
you sought to bestow.


WHAT’S THE HURRY?
	The question of labor is likely to be one of
vast interest during the remaining years of
the present century. The idea seems to be
universally conceded by the laboring class,
that too much time is required for work and
not a sufiicient amount granted for rest and
recreation.
	Certain it is, that all denominations, parties
and sects are hurrying through the world
with a rapidity that daily increases, and be-
comes each year, seemingly, more and more
irrestable. Many of those who are wealthy
would drive their workwomen to hard
physical labor all the time, day and night,
were it in their power; so anxious are they
to-grasp all in their reach, and to have their
arms extended and strengthened so that
they may reach and grasp and secure still
more. And many of those who are poor are
so eager to become rich in a hurry, that they
are almost opposed to spending time and
dilligence enough at any kind of work to
make it profitable, either to themselves or
their employers. A calm, quiet course of
procedure is apparently considered of little
account in the effort to accomplish anything
now-a-days; it is all hurry and bustle, elec-
tric speed for every thing that is done.
Every one you meet with is tired, but still
in a hurry to get some piece of work off
hands. No one has time to rest; the balance
would get ahead if one rested a little. Wait
a minute, poor, old world! Your sage head
must be dizzy and confused with such ceas-
less turning, day after day and year after
year, for so many generations! Why rush
along so furiously? It must be almost time for
the “new Heaven and new earth” to make
their appearance, any how; you are coming
to an end fast enough. What’s the hurry?
								   G.


﻿GIRLS, DON’T TALK SLANG!
				
  An exchange says: Girls, don’t talk
slang! If it is necessary that any one in 
the family should do that, let your big
brother, though I would advise him not to ;
talk “pigeon English” when there is an
elegant, systematized language that he can
just as well use; but don’t you do it. You
have no idea how it sounds to ears unused
or averse to it, to hear a young lady, when
she is asked to attend some place of amuse-
ment, answer, “Not much;” or, if requested
to do something she does not wish to, “Can’t
see it!”
   Not long ago I heard a Miss, who is edu-
cated and accomplished say, in speaking of
a yonng man, that she intended to “go for
him;” and when her sister asked her assist-
ance at some work, she answered, “Not for
Joe.”
  Now, young ladies of unexceptionable
character and really good education, fall
into this habit, thinking it shows smart-
ness to answer back in slang phrases; and
they soon slip flippantly from their tongues
with a saucy pertness that is neither lady-
like nor becoming. Young men who talk i
in that way do not care to hear it from the
lips they love or admire—it sounds much
coarser then; and, really, slang does not
save time in use of language as an abbrevi-
ation: “No” is shorter and much more de-
cided than “not much;” “l am sure,” is quite
as easily said as “I’ll bet.”
  More than one promising wedding has
been indefinitely postponed by such means,
for, however remiss young men may be in
such things themselves, they look for better
things in the girls of their choice—and it
does not help them to mend a bad habit to
adopt it.

creased 3 1/2 per cent. in the decade from 1860
to 1870.......At Long Branch, a grand fair is
to be held this Summer, for the benefit of
the French Liberation Fund...........Cut
worms are destroying the imported Havana
tobacco, now growing in Connecticut.....

which met June 19th, was the largest ever
held in the State......The annual reduction
of tariff duties, taxes and interest by the
acts of the Congress lately adjourned, it is
estimated, amounts to $51,823,761......A
great many cattle are reported dying in
Clark Co., Alabama, from an unknown dis-
ease...Missoula county, M. T., it is said, will
produce more grain this season, by one
half, than it ever did before......A visitor to
the Charleston, Massachusetts State prison,
reported that eight out of every ten of the

directly or indirectly by liquor...M’me.

homes, and that is the reason why society
is so demoralized......It is stated of a promi-
nent military general that “his sword was
never drawn but once and that was in a
raffle"....The scheme of excavating a railroad

are no longer inseparable companions......

tons a year, and the American about 2,000,-
000......At Cincinnati, a few days since,

been making a raid into Sonora, killing and
