﻿SUMMER MORTALITY;
			
  For some seasons past an unusual mortality
has prevailed in this city, particularly among
children, in the latter part of the summer, es-
pecially in the months of August and Septem-
ber; and occasionally continuing through Octo-
ber. At one time it has appeared as diptheria;
at another.as scarlet fever; but more generally
the fatal disease has taken the form of an
epedemic bowel complaint, and the little
victims to it have had the causes of their death
variously reported as cholera-infantum, teeth-
ing, and bowel-complaint.
  Why this mortality should come thus period-
ically and fill numerous children’s graves is a
subject which medical skill has so far failed to
furnish satisfactory reasons for. Some persons
attribute it to the eating of unripe fruit; others
to miasmatic vapors arising from stagnant wa-
ter near the southwestern portion of the city,
and some again to other causes. But while the
source of the mortality is not satisfactorily un-
derstood, there is little doubt that much sick-
ness and many deaths maybe prevented by moth
ers exercising due care in observing the laws of
health. A regular condition of body is neces-
sary to the preservation of health, and this is
more especially so during the latter part of
Summer and early Autum, when the human
system is relaxed and more or less enervated
from continued heat. Mothers who are nursing
cannot be too careful as to what they eat and
drink, if they desire the infants that draw sus-
tenance from them to enjoy good health. Fruit
or vegetables which they are unaccustomed to


use should be very sparingly partaken of; so
with watermelons or anything calculated to
relax the system; for laxity and constipation
are equally dangerous. To nurse an infant
when the body is overheated is injurious, nor
can it be done without evil results to the babe.
And the eating ot much pastry or highly
seasoned food is also productive of injury and
often of serious consequences. A regular and
temperate diet, exercising care not to partake
too freely of anything which is not ordinarily
used, is best for the preservation of health for
all, and more particularly nursing mothers.
  A sad and often fatal mistake made by many,
and growing out of maternal fondness, is the 
giving children any kind of food that may be
on the table for which they express a desire. A
little one seated in its mother's lap will ask or
make signs for something of which she is par-
taking and she cannot refuse. The childish
wish is gratified. It may be meat, or pie, or
something else which its tender stomach is
utterly incapable of digesting with ease. The
child is taken sick; its mother does not know
what is the matter with it; the disease con-
tinues, and before proper remedies are applied
it has seized fatal hold of its victim and another
little grave is filled, while a weeping mother
murmers against Providence for robbing her of
her babe.
  Mothers, we say in sorrow, that this is too
often the ease. And many a child, whose bones
are now mingling with the dust, might have
been to-day in the possession of life and vigor-
ous health had proper care been taken in the
matter of diet. Think of it, now that Summer
is here.


“MORMON WOMEN.”

  An extract from the San Francisco “Pioneer”
under the above title, and comments on it by a
lady of this city, will be found elsewhere in
this number. The editor of the “Pioneer”
evidently desires to be deceived relative to
the sentiments of the “intelligent women of
Utah,” those women who have proved their in-
telligence as organizers and colony-founders
along with men whose troubles and privations
they have shared and in whose prosperity they
participate. There are no people so difficult to
undeceive and as they who are self-deceived and
are satisfied with their assumed knowledge.
The “Pioneer” may be assured that the “Mor-
mon women” have long since been waked “up
to a full realization of their grave situation,”
and have solid reasons for holding that its editor
and those in whose agitation she so manifestly
sympathizes, would do well to pass through a
similar process of “awaking up;” for the con-
dition of women the world over is far from
what it should be, nor were the day-dreams so
often indulged in realized would it be greatly
improved.  But we desire to let this matter be
discussed by others of the “Mormon women” of
Utah, either over their own signatures or not
as best pleases them, satisfied that there are
hundreds of intelligent women in Utah who
can present facts and reasons on the subject
which the editor of the “Pioneer” would profit
by carefully considering and studying.
  The columns of the EXPONENT are open to
the “Mormon "Women” that they may express
themselves freely on this and other matters in
which they have been abundantly misrepre-
sented.


﻿ON SATURDAY last a meeting was held in the
tabernacle to nominate candidates for the com-
ing election. As the women of Utah possess
the suffrage, we shall treat at greater length
on the nominees next issue than we have
space to do now.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HOME AFFAIRS.
			 —
 THERE APPEARS to be a misunderstanding
on the part of numbers of our readers, in regard
to the Editor of the EXPONENT, “Mr. L. L.
Green, Dear Editor,” and “Editor Woman’s
EXPONENT, Dear Sir,” are honorable appella-
tions, but we are not entitled to either “Mr.” or
“Sir,” being simply a woman, which our name
as it now appears over the editoriaI department
will indicate.
  WE MUST repeat again that subscribers in
this city who desire their papers delivered at
their residences, can be so accommodated by
the payment of twenty-five cents for the year
to the carrier; and they should send in their
names immediately. Those who do not wish
it delivered can get it after the first and fifteenth
of each month by calling at Campbell & Patter-
son’s East Temple Street.
  ON SATURDAY evening last a Greeley and
Brown ratification meeting was held at the
City. Hall, in this city, at which nearly five
thousand people were present, including many
ladies. Two bands of music were in attendance,
an artillery salute was fired, and stirring
speeches were made. The indications were very
decisive that if Utah had a State government
Its electoral vote would be given for Mr. Gree-
ley by a very large popular majority.
  MRS. DUNIWAY, editor of the “New North-
west,” Portland, Oregon, delivered an interest-
ing lecture in the tabernacle in this city on
Monday night, the 8th inst., choosing for her
subject “Human Rights.” Reference will be
ound made to it elsewhere, and the circum-
stances which brought Mrs. Duniway into the
editorial sanctum and the lecture field. She is
an earnest woman, and in her lecture advanced
some excellent ideas clothed In a garniture of
oloquent language
   THE NATIONAL Anniversary has come and
gone since our last issue. Independence Day
passed without a formal celebration in this
city, as a result of the narrow-mindedness of
ofiicial authority which would prevent a free
people from celebrating as they should the
birth-day of freedom. But in their “heart of
hearts” the people carried their love for liberty
and their reverence for the great men gone, just
as fondly as though they had engaged in all the
outward seeming of patriotism which so often
characterizes Fourth of July celebrations.
  HON. W. H. HOOPER, retires from the politi-
cal field as Delegate to Congress, after having
faithfully served the Territory for ten years in
that position. He retains the unabated confi-
dence and warmest wishes for his success of the
thousands whose votes were cast for him at
each election, for he has proved himself an in-
defatigable, energetic, and honorable public
officer. Hon. Geo. Q. Cannon is nominated for
Delegate, a gentleman in every way qualified
to fill the place, and with a record as clear as
the crystal water which is his principal bever-
age.	
  ALMOST EVERY day subscriptions are being
received for the Exponent, the senders asking
to have the papers sent from the beginning of
the volume. We regret being unable to meet
these requests affirmatively, as the issue of the
first and second numbers is exhausted. The desire
to possess the history of the first organization of
the Female Relief Society In Nauvoo, written,
by Miss Eliza R. Snow, is one great reason why
so much anxiety is manifested to obtain the
numbers in which it appeared; and to meet
this desire we purpose, after some little time,
republishing it for the benefit of those who
have not yet read it.	
[new column]
  WEDNESDAY WEEK, the Twenty-fourth inst.,
is the anniversary of the day when the Pioneers,
under President Brigham Young, entered this
valley. The occasion is held in warm remem-
brance by the people of Utah, for it opened a
new era in their history. Here, after having
borne for sad years the pitless storm of persecu-
tion, culminating in the martyrdom of their
Prophet and Patriarch and their being driven
beyond the confines of “civilization,” they
found rest and peace, and under the blessing of
Heaven the little one has become a nation. No
wonder they celebrate the day with fervor and
earnestness.
		 ————————


﻿WOMAN'S STATUS.
			   —
  With the EXPONENT'S permission I will
follow up the subject which appeared In
your first number, under the title “Woman’s
Rights, and Wrongs.”
   The status of women is one of the ques-
tions of the day. Socially and politically it
forces itself upon the attention of the world.
Some who are so conservative that they op-
pose every change until they are compelled
to accept it, refuse to concede that woman is
entitled to the enjoyment of any rights other
than those which the whims, fancies or jus-
tice, as the case may-be, of men may choose to
grant her. The reasons which they cannot
meet with argument they decry and ridicule,
an old refuge for those opposed to correct
principles which they are unable to contro-
vert. Others, again, not only recognize that
woman’s status should be improved, but are
so radical in their extreme theories that they
would set her in antagonism to man, assume
for her a separate and opposing existence,
and to show how entirely independent she
should be would make her adopt the more
reprehensible phases of character which men
present, and which should be shunned or im-
proved by them instead of being copied by
women. These are two extremes, and be-
tween them is the “golden mean.”
  We do not have to go back very far in
the history of the Anglo-Saxon race to find
a time when women were viewed and treat-
ed as chattels. The father had absolute
power in everything, except in the matter
of life and death, over his daughter. The
husband had equal power over his wife.
She was his property, to the last article of
raiment worn by her. And there are not
wanting instances within a comparatively
recent date where women have been sold
with a halter around the neck, and passed
quietly and almost uncomplainingly into
the possession of their purchasers. Even
now, among the lower classes of the Eng-
lish, the wife speaks of her husband as “my
master,” not “my husband;” and this by
traditionary force In cases where the man is
anything but master, being the veriest slave
of a woman’s will.
   Coming to America the status of woman
among the same classes may be something
higher, but it is not so well defined in any
grade of society. See the manner in which
ladies—a term for which I have little rever-
ence or respect—are treated in all public
places! The most obsequious deference is
ostensibly paid to their slightest wish. The
profane man, from whose lips in the company
of men alone oath follows oath, one scarcely
waiting on the other, will not utter an of-
fensive word in the presence of woman. She
must not be allowed to stand if a seat can be
found by a man vacating it. She must be
preserved from the slightest blast of trouble,
petted, carressed, dressed to attract atten-
tion, taught accomplishments that minister
to man’s-gratification; in other words, she
must be treated as a glittering and fragile
[new column]
toy, a thing without brains or soul, placed
on a tinselled and unsubstantial pedestal by
man, as her worshipper. This elevation of
status is by courtesy, not by right. Let one
of those idols choose to step beyond the
bounds which society prescribes, and she is
hurled from her position, and flung in the
mire of social degradation. What legal rights
can she fall back  upon? What power has
she to work her own restoration? In some
of the States of the Union she can acquire
and control property, and this is a great step
in advance. Yet she is far from possessing
the power to rise aboe untoward circum-
stances that she would possess, if she enjoyed
polilical rights and influences. For “society,”
or any social combination, in this country,
is composed of people a part of whom pos-
sess political influence, and that very in-
fluence gives them social power. An indi-
vidual man possesses but a single vote; but
he may have talent and ability, and can lead
the votes of others; and he is a power, even
though he be known to be corrupt nnd in-
temperate, and his honor and honesty are
questionable. The individual woman may
possess equal talents and ability, but she has
not a single vote, she cannot lead votes,
she is not a political power of any kind, and
society only respects her so long as she con-
forms to its rules.
  Woman’s true status is that of companion
to man, yet protected by law in the posses-
sion of those rights which will enable her to
act in an independent sphere, should he
abuse his position and render the union un-
endurable. To discuss the position thus laid
down would occupy more space than your
limits will allow at the present, so I will
take it up again.				E.


﻿MRS. DUNWAY'S LECTURE.
					—
  Mrs. Duniway, an able champion of human
rights, has favored our city with a passing
call, and our citizens with a public expres-
sion of some of her views. She is no ex-
tremist, except in her reverence for the
sacred office of motherhood.
  As Milton placed Adam and Eve, she
places man and woman, hand in hand in all
the walks of life. While being in-
terviewed she gave an interesting sketch of
the manner of her conversion to this, to
some, objectionable doctrine. She said that
while it was her only ambition to be a good
wife and mother, her husband missed his
financial balance, and about the same time
met with a personal injury, which drove
her to the school room and thence to a mil-
linery store. She competed disadvantage-
ous with men. Around her were other
women struggling similarly to keep their
heads’ above water, and claiming her assist-
ance.
   At the close of a day’s labor in which
sympathy and aid had been called in different
directions, and disasters and snubs had been
unusually prevalent, she approached the
low couch on which her invalid husband
was lying, and seating herself on the carpet
laid her head against him; and after re-
hearsing the difficulties of the day, she said
“Why are these things?” Her noble and
generous husband (that is what she called
him) said, “My dear, these things must in
the nature of things be so until woman
takes her proper position as the social, and
political equal of man.” She said an inspira-
tion seemed to enable her to comprehend
that moment what she had heard him talk
many times before.			RHEUMA.


THE MAN who was hemmed in by a crowd
has been troubled with a stitch in his side
ever since.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿ABOUT WOMEN.
			   —
  UPWARD of sixteen American widows are
sojourning at Dresden.
  LADIES are being admitted to the practice
of dentistry in Germany.
  LADY Beaconslield, Disraeli’s wife, is ill
beyond hope of recovery!
  THE ladies of Paris, Ky., are forming an
anti-speak-evil-of-your-neighber society.
  A BLIND woman in Iowa has learned to
thread a cambric needle with her teeth and
tongue.
  THE wickedest woman in Boston is named
Jemima Jakes. She claims to have yanked
fifty husbands from the domestic hearth.
  THE eye of a good mother is like the sun;
it shines on a world that weuld be dark
without, and its brightness is that of love.
  THE fees of a Wyoming paper for mar-
riage notices are “as high as the ecstacy and
liberality of the bridegroom may prompt.
  THE stillborn daughter of Mrs. Bates, the
Nova Scotia giantess, was twenty-eight
inches long, and weighed eighteeen pounds.
  THE citizens at Battle Creek, Mich., have
rewarded Miss Emma Pearl, of that city,
with a fine gold watch, for taking care of a
small-pox afllicted family.
  AT Coburg it is believed that Queen Vic-
toria will abdicate at an early day, and then
pass the remainder of her life at Castle
Kosenau, where Prince Albert was educated.
  THE Berger sisters may be numbered
among the very few women that appear on
the stage at the present day, who earn the
money and the applause which they re-
ceive.
  MRS. Edmund Dexter, who created a
furore at the St. Louis Saengerfest, is a Cin-
cinnati brunette, of self-possession, embon-
point, and pleasant presence—a light edition
of Parepa.
  SOME of the young Quakeresses, who were
dressed in modern style, were admonished
by the venerable Lucretia Mott, at a recent
meeting, to beware of the follies of fashion
and dress in becoming simplicity and dig-
nity.
  BARBARA Ubryk, the story of whose
long imprisonment as a nun attracted so
much attention a few years ago, has now re-
covered from her severe and protracted suf-
ferings, and is living quietly and happily at
Cracow.
  NO wonder the fair sex disliked Dr. John-
son when he said: People flock to hear a
woman preach, not because she preaches
well, but because she preaches anyhow.
Just as they go to see a dog walk on his
hind legs, though he does not walk on them
nearly so well as a man.
  THE women of Damascus, Illinois, take
part in the discussion of the farmers’ club
meeting. A recent conundrum was: “Shall
we dress for comfort or show?” It was, of
course, decided to dress for comfort, and
then they all went away, and at the next
meeting each appeared with a “three-
decker” head-dress and a “Dolly Varden”
suit.
  A FASHION reporter describes some of
the toilets at a recent party at Cheyenne:
“The belle of the evening was Miss W. She
dressed faultlessly in a linsey-woolsey of the
palest shade of ‘cafe au lait,’ cut ‘entrain’
and trimmed with Chicago relics. Miss H.
wore a ‘polonaise’ made of blue jeans,
postilioned in the back, cut ‘vis-a-vis’ with
a ‘detree’ bias, and gored in the most san-
guinary manner. She wore no jewel but
consistency.”


MORMON WOMEN.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Under the above heading I find the fol-
lowing in the San Francisco "Pioneer" of
July 4th:
	"We are pleased to see the intelligent
women of Utah coming to the front and de-
nouncing Polygamic life, and are giving the
lie to some former statements that these
women were happy under a system which
strikes at the root of womanhood, blighting
the affections and hopes, and making her a
slave to priestcraft—teaching a woman that
she is most happy when most oblivious of
every instinct of her nature. But this new
agitation among the Mormon women them-
selves, who are at last awaking up to a full
realization of their grave situation, will do
more than anything that has transpired
lately to shake the priestcraft in Utah to its
utmost depths. Let them open their mouths
and testify to the wrongs they suffer, and
their lives will no longer be but a dreary
show."
	As a "Mormon Woman" I beg a little
space to notice this paragraph.
	What definition the "Pioneer" may give
to the word "intelligent" I am unable to
say. If it is that element existing in the
composition of some narrow minds, which
protrudes itself into public notice in the
form of the grossest misrepresentations,
meddling with affairs in which it has no in-
terest but to injure those concerned, but
little need be said. Yet if my idea of in-
telligence be correct, that it is a quality of
true understanding, something which is cal-
culated to create good for its possessors and
their fellow men, to establish Truth and
Virtue and aid in uprooting error and cor-
ruption, then the "Pioneer" has been grossly
deceived, and its "pleasure" is grounded on
a great misunderstanding. For the "in-
telligent women of Utah" have not taken
the stand which, it seems, the "Pioneer"
would be pleased to have them take. On
the contrary, the intelligent Mormon wo-
men comprehend more clearly to-day than
ever before how elevated is their position
as honered wives and mothers, and the
partners of good and noble men. As for
their hopes and affections being blighted,
there is no intelligent Mormon woman that
could not say with sincerity of heart that the
better she learns to understand the religion
she professes, the brighter and purer do her
hopes and affections become. And instead of
being oblivious of any instinct of her nature,
she has a fuller realization of her true rela-
tionship to God and the works of His crea-
tion, than is possessed by those who un-
appreciated and senseless whinings over
her wrongs she can only despise, while she
pities the ignorance, willful though it may
be, of such as seek to crowd themselves out
of their proper sphere into one which they
will fail to be satisfied with, or hold with
honorable dignity, should they ever obtain
it in the illegitimate way they have adopted.
							TRUTH.

----
﻿MORMON WOMEN.
Editor Exponent:
Under the above heading I find the fol-
lowing in the San Francisco “Pioneer” of
July 4th:
“We are pleased to see the intelligent
women of Utah coming to the front and de-
nouncing Polygamic life, and are giving the
lie to some former statements that these
women were happy under a system which
strikes at the root of womanhood, blighting
the affections and hopes, and making her a
slave to priestcraft—teaching a woman that
she is most happy when most oblivious of
every instinct of her nature. But this new
agitation among the Mormon women them-
selves, who are at last awaking up to a full
realization of their grave situation, will do
more than anything that has transpired
lately to shake the priestcraft in Utah to its
utmost depths. Let them open their mouths
and testify to the wrongs they suffer, and
their lives will no longer be but a dreary
I show.”
As a “Mormon Woman” I beg a little
space to notice this paragraph.
What definition the “Pioneer” may give
to the word “intelligent” I am unable to
say. If it is that element existing in the
composition of some narrow minds, which
protrudes itself into public notice in the
form of the grossest misrepresentations,
meddling with affairs in which it has no in-
terest but to injure those concerned, but
little need be said. Yet if my idea of in-
telligence be correct, that it is a quality of
true understanding, something which is cal-
culated to create good for its possessors and
their fellow men, to establish Truth and
Virtue and aid in uprooting error and cor-
ruption, then the “Pioneer” has been grossly
deceived, and its “pleasure” is grounded on
a great misunderstanding. For the “in-
telligent women of Utah” have not taken
the stand which, it seems, the “Pioneer”
would be pleased to have them take. On
the contrary, the intelligent Mormon wo-
men comprehend more clearly to-day than
ever before how elevated is their position
as honored wives and mothers, and the
partners of good and noble men. As for
their hopes and affections being blighted,
there is no intelligent Mormon woman that
could not say with sincerity of heart that the
better she learns to understand the religion
she professes, the brighter and purer do her
hopes and affections become. And instead of
being oblivious of any instinct of her nature,
she has a fuller realization of her true rela-
tionship to God and the works of His crea-
tion, than is possessed by those whose un-
appreciated and senseless winnings over
her wrongs she can only despise, while she
pities the ignorance, willful though it may
be, of such as seek to crowd themselves out
of their proper sphere into one which they
will fail to be satisfied with, or hold with
honorable dignity, should they ever obtain
it in the illegitimate way they have adopted.
Truth.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
   Under the above heading I find the fol-
lowing in the San Francisco “Pioneer” of
July 4th:
   “We are pleased to see the intelligent
women of Utah coming to the front and de-
nouncing Polygamic life, and are giving the
lie to some former statements that these
women were happy under a system which
strikes at the root of womanhood, blighting
the affections and hopes, and making her a
slave to priestcraft—teaching a woman that
she is most happy when most oblivious of
every instinct of her nature. But this new
agitation among the Mormon women them-
selves, who are at last awaking up to a full
realization of their grave situation, will do
more than anything that has transpired
lately to shake the priestcraft in Utah to its
utmost depths. Let them open their mouths
and testify to the wrongs they suffer, and
their lives will no longer be but a dreary
I show.”
   As a “Mormon Woman” I beg a little
space to notico this paragraph.

to the word “intelligent” I am unable to

protnules itself into public notice in the

telligence be correct, that it is a quality of
true understanding, something which is cal-

their fellow men, to establish Truth and

would be pleased to have them take. On
the contrary, the intelligent Mormon wo-
men comprehend more clearly to-day than
ever before how elevated is their position
as honored wives and mothers, and the
partners of good and noble men. As for
their hopes and affections being blighted,
there is no intelligent Mormon woman that
could not say with sincerity of heart that the

she professes, the brighter and purer do her

she has a fuller realization of her true rela-
tionship to God and the works of His crea-

appreciated and senseless winnings over

of their proper sphere into one which they
will fail to be satisfied with, or hold with
honorable dignity, should they ever obtain
it in the illegitimate way they have adopted.
								TRUTH.


WOMAN’S VOICE.
			     —
  Under this heading we propose publishing
such communications, or extracts from them,
forwarded by ladies, as do not assume
the character of regular articles. Women
of Utah, speak for yourselves! 
  The following is from a private communi-
cation from an old friend, and a subscriber
to the EXPONENT.
  Seeing I am an old-fashioned piece of
goods I shall begin with an excuse. Can’t
you hear the miserable subterfuge—“I’ve


been to busy!” Bah! How I hate an ex- 
cuse! I very seldom believe the person who 
makes one, do you? I read somewhere, 
that “Excuses were lies,” and believe to 
some extent that it is true. The truth with
me is, I don’t like writing. The thought
of taking up a pen to write makes me shiver. 
How shall I get over this dislike? Can you 
tell me?	
  I like the EXPONENT so far very much; I
it tells of sound sense—which, you know, I I
like.	
   We “Mormon women” ought to write and 
tell the world—whether it is pleased to be-
lieve us or not—that we are not the poor, 
oppressed beings we are represented to be.  
I have lived in the great world, you know; 
I have lived here thirteen years, and I re- 
alize that I have not been oppressed here; 
but have been free to come, free to go, free 
to work or let it alone. I was not as free 
in England, nor in the States. I would not 
go to live in either place, or any where else
in preference to this free, wild, mountaineous 
country, with all its deficiencies.	
   Were I a ready writer I could illustrate 
some of the abuses thatsome women have
to put up with at the hands of the “lords of 
creation” in New York and London. In the 
latter place, when a girl of seventeen, I 
hired with a man and his wife to do house- 
work. I was not allowed to go out-side the
door, save once in six weeks from two in the 
afternoon until ten in the evening. When 
I did wrong I was reprimanded; and when I
I did right, the threat of sending me away 
without a character was kept as the sword 
hanging by a hair over my poor head. This 
kept me in subjection for three long years— 
the bitterest of my life. Yet my experience 
was nothing, compared with what many a I
wretched girl possessed of more meekness 
and less resolution than myself is compelled
to put up with, or starve, or do worse. In. 
New York, I went to get sewing to do, and
was treated shamefully by a man, or a being 
who styled himself such. Chicago was better 
to me. I liked that city the best of any I 
ever lived in. But there, men seemed to : 1
look upon woman as a mere plaything, to be
thrown away when they were tired of her. 
   Yet hear their cant and humbug about  
the “downtrodden women of Utah!”   O!  
but you know I hate writing!
				—			MARY.
  This comes from Goshen, dated June, 27:	
  I am glad that the ladies of Utah have 
the privilege of speaking for themselves. I 
have often wished that I might tell the na-  
tions of the earth they are deceived concern- 
ing us. And now, I will say, I am free;
that I know my friends around me are free; 
that the religion we have embraced makes 
us free, and gives us blessings and ad- 
vantages which are not enjoyed by women
elsewhere.						S. R.


﻿BOYS USING TOBACCO.	
				—
	A strong and sensible writer says a good
sharp thing and a true one, about boys who
use tobacco:
	It has utterly spoiled and utterly ruined
many thousands of boys. It tends to soften-
ing and weakening of the bones and the
whole nervous fluid. A boy who smokes
early and frequently, or in any way uses 
large quantities of tobacco, is never known 
to make a man of much energy, and gener-
ally lacks muscular and physical, as well as
mental power. We would particularly warn
boys who want to be anything in the world
to shun tobacco as a most baneful poison. It
injures the teeth, it produces an unhealthy
state of the throat and lungs, hurts the
stomach, and blasts the brain nerves.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿NOT ALL IN BRINGING UP.
				    —
		It isn’t all in bringing up,
		  Let folks say what they will;
		You silver-wash a pewter cup—
		  It will be pewter still.
		E’en he of old, wise Solomon,
		  Who said "train up a child,”
		If I mistake not, raised a son,
		Gay, rattle brained and wild.

		A man of mark, who fain would pass
		  For lord of sea and land,
		May have the training of an ass,
		  And bring him up full grand:
		May give him all the wealth of lore
		  Of college and of school,
		Yet after all make him no more
		  Than just a decent fool.

		Another, raised by penury
		  Upon her bitter bread,
		Whose road to knowledge is like that
		  The good, for heaven, must tread,
		Has got a spark of Nature’s light,
		  He’ll fan it to a flame.
		Till in its burning letters bright
		  The world may read his name.

		If it were all in bringing up,
		  In counsel and restraint,
		Some rascals had been honest men—
		  I'd been myself, a saint.
		Oh! ’tisn’t all in bringing up,
		  Let folks say what they will;
		Neglect may dim a silver cup—
		  It will be silver still.


MISCELLANEOUS.
			  ——
  AN INDIANA farmer, after trying to trap,
poison and shoot the rats that overrun his
premises, bought two goats and gave them
the range of yards and stables. Within a
week every rat emigrated and stayed away
until the goats were sold—nearly two years
afterwards—when they all came back with
all their friends. A second edition of goats
were procured, and since then not a rat has
been seen on the premises.

    BEAUTIFUL SENTENCE.—“I expect to
pass through this world but once. If, there-
fore, there is any kindness I can do to
any fellow-being, let me do it now. Let me
not defer nor neglect it, for I will not pass
this way again.” Most men live as if they
expected, perhaps, to come around again,
when they will do the kindness they neglect
to do during the present life. Concerned for
self, they fail to love their neighbors, and
distribute none of those sweet charities
which are to our fellow men as the flowers
of spring and the singing of birds. Let the
words of the Quaker stimulate us to do what
we have to do “now.” We will not travel
this way again.
  SAP IN PLANTS.—A Botanist says that
the sap in plants or trees goes up in certain
pipes in the wood or stalk, and when it gets
to the leaves, it turns about and goes back
toward the ground through other pipes in
the live portion of the bark. The sap that
goes up has a great deal of water in it, much
of which Is left with the leaves, so that the
sap which goes down in the bark carries
back to the ground but a comparatively
small quantity of water. Through the pores
of the leaves water is let off into the air.
The sap is not perfect until it has visited
the leaves and been touched by the air,
after which it goes to all portions of the
plant, down through the very roots, and
from it every part of the plant grows. The
new layer of wood which is made from the
inner bark in trees, every year, is formed
from some of this aired sap.
   A BIG CLOCK.—The large clock at the
English parliament house is the largest one
in the world. The four dials of this clock
are twenty-two feet in diameter. Every half
minute the point of the minute hand moves
nearly seven inches. The clock will go eight
and a half days, but only strikes for seven
[new column]
﻿and a half, thus indicating any neglect in
winding it up. The mere winding up of the
striking mechanism takes two hours. The
pendulum is fifteen feet long; the wheels are
of cast iron; the hour bell is eight feet high
and nine feet in diameter, weighing nearly
fifteen tons, and the hammer alone weighs
more than four hundred pounds. This clock
strikes the quarter hours, and by its strokes
the short-hand reporters in the parliament
chambers regulate their labors.  At every
stroke a new reporter takes the place of the
old one, while the first retires to write out
the notes that he has taken during the pre-
vious fifteen minutes.
  A “REPARABLE LOSS.”—The most re-
markable strike we have so far heard of is i
that of five hundred women in the north of
England and against the high price of butch-
er’s meat, potatoes and milk. A lower price
was fixed for those necessaries, and it was
resolved to burn every woman in effigy who
purchased at more than the strike rates.
One weak-minded sister exclaimed, “I must
have milk for my bairn.” “I have a bairn,”
retorted the strong-minded chairwoman,
Mrs. Hetherington, “and can make it some
flour that will answer the same purpose. If
we lose a bairn it is a reparable loss which
can be easily overcome. I propose that we
cease purchasing butcher’s meat, &c., until
the butchers, &c., reduce their prices in ac-
cordance with our terms.” The resolution
was carried with “great applause.”—“Ex-
change.”
  CRUSADE AGAINST WAR.—An eastern
paper says: Mrs. Julia Ward Howe has
crossed the Atlantic to enlist the active sup-
port of the women of England in a crusade
against war. The object of the proposed
crusade is a praiseworthy one, though the
plan is not clearly understood. Are women
to organize into bands of Maids of Saragossa,
and rush with dishevelled hair between con-
tending armies, or are they to moderate the
martial ardor of their husbands by gentle
persuasion to stay at home? The latter plan
would seem to have its disadvantages, for
in case of war between England and France,
if the English women should be successful
in inducing their male defenders to stay at
home, and the French women should not,
the benefits of a peace policy would be rather
one-sided.  It is not surprising that Mrs.
Howe has not met with much success among
the robust damsels of Albion, who have
heard more than they have seen of the evils
of war.  She should try Mexico.
   AN ICICLE TWO THOUSAND MILES LONG.
—According to a letter from St. Johns, N.
F., in the New York “Post,” the Arctic re-
gions have this year thrown off an ice spur
of an unprecedented magnitude, which is
drifting down into the Gulf Stream, and
chilling its tropical temperature. The
writer says that since last January enormous
fields of ice,sometimes 200 miles in breadth,
have been passing the shores of Newfound-
land in almost continuous streams. The
thickness of this field of ice is from twenty
to thirty feet. The distance betwen Baffin’s
Bay, where the ice fields are formed, and
the waters of the Gulf Stream, where the
masses are dissolved, is from 1,500 to 2,-
000 miles. It may be affirmed, without any
exaggeration, that a river of ice, varying
from 50 to 200 miles in breadth and 2,000
miles in length, has been for three months
pouring incessantly its contents into the
tepid waters of the Gulf Stream. Last
winter was fearfully severe, and the quantity
and thickness of the ice unprecedented.
The irruption of this immense mass of ice
into our latitude may account for the cold
and backwark season we then experienced.


﻿FACTS AND. FIGURES.
			—
  IOWA marble is of superior quality.
  CARRARA, Italy, yawns with 168 marble
querrics.
  KEY WEST sends pine-apples by the ship-
load to Baltimore.
  MORE poets flourish successfully in Austria
than in England.
  THERE are over 500,000 affiliated Masons
in the United States.
  PORTUGAL, in a hundred years, has not
quarreled with any power.
  FIVE nations are working this year to raise
flags on the North Pole.
  A DISCOVERY of emeralds has been made
on the Guinea coast of Africa.
  THE latest foreign labor news is to the
effect that 12,000 German miners have
struck.
  TIGER TAIL, a son of the famous Seminole
Chief of the same name, peddles pumpkins
at Key West.
  THE annual expense of protecting the six
thousand inhabitants of Arizona from the
Indians is $4,000,000.
  A NATURAL spring that will intoxicate,
situated near Kern River, Cab, is delighting
the Indians of that vicinity.
  NEW JERSEY raised by a local taxation,
last year, for school purposes, $2,375,000,
which was an average of $14 a pupil actually
attending school.
  THERE is said to be $25,000,000 invested
in silk manufacturies in this country, and
the productions of these manufactures sell
at $30,000,000.
  AN East Saginaw' pastor declines an ad-
dition of $200 to his salary on the ground
that it is more than he wants to do to collect
the salary that he already has.
  THERE are over 650,000 scholars inatteiul-
ance at the public schools in Illinois. Teach-
ers number over 20,000. School houses, log,
frame, brick and stone, 10,773. Expend-
itures for school purposes for the last year,
$7,000,000.
  ST. VINCENT, Fla., is an island of 7,000
acres, owned and occupied for the past three
years by Mr. Hatch, a gentleman of intelli-
gence and fortune, formerly Mayor of Cin-
cinnati, who chooses to lead here the life of
a hermit, solitary and alone, save with a
servant or two, and seeing few visitors.
  IMMIGRATION into all parts of the State is
large. The influx is now setting in in full
tide. Not less than 75,000 people will be
added to the population of Nebraska in the
year 1872, and in less than ten years a mil-
lion souls will occupy this magnificent region,
whose arable area is fifty per cent, larger
than that of Illinois.
  THE library of the University of Stras-
bourg now numbers 200,000 volumes. The
old library had. from 300,000 to 700,000
volumes, and was so loosely managed that
no catalogue was ever made of it, nor is
there now a leaf of it extant. Any man in
Strasbourg or the province, of Alsace may
draw books. The city, however, is attempt-
ing to found an opposition  library, and is
soliciting aid through Europe.
  SAMUAL A. HITCHCOCK, of Brimfield, has
just given $100,000 to Amherst College and
$50,000 to Phillips Academy, at Andover.
Each of these institutions has at a previous
time received $75,000 from Mr. Hitchcock.
At Andover he established the Hitchcock
professorship of Hebrew language and
literature, and at Amherst the professor-
ship of natural theology and geology, also
bearing his name, besides six scholarships.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


TASTE.
			   —	
    ﻿That fine, sensitive faculty which enables
us to perceive and enjoy the beautiful, ex-
cellent, or elegant, in nature and art, cannot
be too carefully cultivated. A person who
is entirely unacquainted with Nature, can-
not be a person of good taste, not knowing
whether the production of art resembles
Nature or not; and unless this knowledge is
possessed to some extent, no real satisfac-
tion can be received from the imitative arts.
A lively imagination, clear and distinct ap-
preciation of things, good sense, and the
Iove of virtue, delicacy and correctness are
some of the features to be gained, before
beauty, sublimity, harmony, sweetness and
all those characteristics which speak such
volumes of rapturous delight to the educated
eye and ear, can be received or appreciated.
It is said, and is undoubtedly correct in a
gereral sense, that the toilet of a lady be-
tokens her taste; that to dress simply and
without ostentation is a mark of modesty;
to wear many bright, glaring colors, a flaunt-
ing, red, yellow, or sky-blue hat covered
with ribbons, or as much jewelry as can be
loaded on, gives evidence of an uncultivated
mind and taste. A waist too small for the
natural proportions of the body is styled the
worst possible deformity, and must be the
workings of a narrow, unexpanding mind
and bad taste, if produced by sinning against
the laws of nature by tight lacing.

			WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

			A UTAH LADIES' JOURNAL.

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

   They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them ; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.

   Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves ?  “ It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"

   For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.

	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.

	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.

	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah:

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year, 	    $2.00	1 Copy six months, 	 $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months, 9.00

	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

  Address all communications .and subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


Woman's Exponent.
VOL. I.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, AUGUST 1, 1872.


﻿NEWS AND VIEWS.
		     
  GONE to rest—The “New York Standard.”
  TO LEARN the value of money—try to
borrow some.
  GRANTS wanted for everything, even for
the National head.
  IT is considered too revolting for soldiers
to shoot a woman, in Paris, and Cantinicer
Gugard, convicted of firing on Bishop Jural,
is to be guillotined.
  EDITH O’Gorman, lecturing on the
Sisters of Charity and Mercy, pronounces
them little better than prostitutes. She must
be crazed and is to be pitied.
  POLITICAL interests are looked after
eveywhere, and party feeling manifested,
even at the Peace Jubilee, where the French
and German bands were coldly civil to each-
other.
  “LIKE begets like.” Seventeen years
ago, a Judge, in Georgia, sentenced John
Dolton to be hung; and a few days ago the
same Judge performed the shine office for
John Dolton’s son.
  SOME enlightened women hold that the
Father designed for man to eat of the for-
bidden fruit; but seeing the consequences,
he had not fortitude to meet the requisition,
so his wife had to do it for him.
  JUAREZ is reported to have been a man
of much firmness, an able General and
politician, a faithful and energetic Presi-
dent; who won the hearts of all, both friends
and foes. Will his successor prove as true
and as beneficial to his country?
  A DELEGATE in the Wisconsin Demo
cratic convention, after struggling vainly in
opposition to the nomination of Greeley,
says, “When we come to die, next Nov-
ember, we shall wish we had not forsaken
the path of virtue.” Honest fellow!
  MRS. Livermore sensibly remarks that
she is in favor of the Republican party,
whether it gives the women Suffrage or not,
even if it does not recognize them at all; be-
cause it advocates all other good things,
and our cause will come in due time.
  MRS. Kellison, an Iowa preacher, recently
delivered a lecture, having for her text,
“Love divided into four parts; L for Love,
O for Obedience, V for Victory and E for
Eternity.” The text is decidedly novel, and,
doubtless, the discourse was elaborate and
entertaining.
  A PORTION of the jewels belonging to the
Empress Eugenie were sold at  auction, a
short time ago, in the rooms of Messrs.
Christie, Mason & Co. The amount realized
was upwards of $250,000 in gold. Nothing
for her—but wouldn’t half the sum make
some poor people rich!
  IT is reported that since the fiasco at
Baltimore, Mrs. E. Cady Stanton,: Mrs.
Laura De Force Gordon, Miss Anthony, and
nearly all the conspicious Women Suffragists
have concluded to support Grant and Wil-
son. Changes will occur, but they are not
always improvements.
  IN Iowa there is no provisions of the law
Which excludes women from holding any
office to which they may be elected. In
several Counties women are holding
elective offices. When the right of suffrage
[new column]
is granted to the women there, whata happy 
State Iowa will be!
  MISS J. F. Ripley has the honor of being
the first woman who has surpassed the men
in the contest of Greek scholarship; for
which she takes the first prize at the State
University of Missouri. This will be en-
couraging to the advocates of a liberal educa-
tion for women.
  GRATITUDE one of the happiest and most
noble attributes with which man has been
endowed, is finely evinced in the character
of the Liberal Democratic candidate for Vice-
President, B. Gratz Brown, in his speech at
New Haven, in which lie acknowledges
that he owes the honors and compliments
he receives as much if not more to his
“Alma mater” than to himself.
  PRIVATE Instructions, to little folks, and
home influences have long been allowed to
be best rendered by woman, and now her
capabilities assume a broader and higher
sphere of action. Mrs. Ellen Webster, the
first woman elected to a county office
in Kansas, has just taken the oath and
been qualified as Superintendent of public
instruction in Harvey County.
  IN March, 1871, an American woman,
Mrs. Emily Gould, opened, in Rome, at her
own expense and with the co-operation of
some of her generous lady-compatriots, a
free school for poor boys and girls. In which
are taught both Italian and English; geogra-
phy,arithmetic,history and the first elements
of the physical sciences. Here is wisdom
and a proper usage of time, means and in-
fluence
  A LADY in this City, over sixty years of
age, finds in the “Portage Co., (O.) Demo-
crat” “A Poetic Address” delivered atMan-
tua on the Fourth of July, 1815, at a celebra-
tion in which she participated, when eleven
years old; and so well does her memory
serve her that she repeats line after line of
the poem without glancing at the paper.
Fifty-seven years is quite a time for a simple
little poem to sleep in an active brain, and
then wake up when called upon, as fresh
and bright as it was when laid away.
  ONE of the most cruel and heartless
punishments that can be inflicted upon
child is to place it in a position where fear
of danger is sustained, whether real or imag-
inary. Don’t do it you who have the care
of youth. Mark the awful consequences
which followed such a transaction, not long
since, in Detroit. A four-year old boy was
left by his parents alone in the house,
though he screamed loudly and begged to
be taken with them, saying he was afraid of
bears and wolves. When they returned, it
was to find their boy insane, and it is feared
he will be a consummate idiot all his life.
  THE orthodox people of England, who
hoped the illness of the Prince of Wales
would cause a radical reform in his course
of life, are getting disheartened again. They
began to be encouraged from his saying in
a speech, that he hoped, by the aid of God
to be of use to his country. But the same
evening he went to see Toole in a dark
play, and laughed inconsiderately, and has
since been to Ascot and other races, and
shows every sign of goihg back into the
graceless fellow he used to be. It is a little
[new column]strange that England, with such a head as
Queen	Victoria, does not concede at once
and fully to the Woman Suffrage movement.
  IT IS now reported that Chinese archives,
having been researched, show that the
architects who designed and the engineers
who built the great wall were women. No
doubt but the statement is correct; but even
if it were not, women must have the credit
of being mothers to the great architects and
engineers which is quite as good. For true
women are more thankful, if possible, for
the glorious deeds enacted by their
noble sons than for any notable work
of their own hands. And they do not
desire that women should be credited with
every worthy action that is performed; it is
enough that no hero, however great, ever
existed without a mother. 
	"THE City of broken hearts," is the
original, romantic name which Mrs. Duni-
way gives to the "Mormon burying ground,"
of which she says: "The largest class of
occupants are 'first wives', who died in their
heroic efforts to become reconciled to the
wonderufl beauty of the practical workings
of the Mormon faith." How could she
have found that out? Even had she taken
the time to walk through the grave yard
and inspect the tombstones and head boards,
(which she doesn't say she did,) the "wicked
Mormons," are not in the habit of inserting
upon these simple monuments the cause of
death; no more than they tell what Brigham
Young does with all the property he takes 
from "apostate women, and their daughters."
  THE "Memphis Appeal" of July 2, con-
tains a striking letter written in March,
from Jackson, Miss., by Bennet Jones to
Dr. Dameron; in which the former coolly
and plainly asks the latter to send his (the
Doctor's) wife to him. He tells Dameron
among other things, that he and Mggie
(the Doctor's) wife to him. He tells Dameron,
among other things, that he and Maggie
(the Doctor's wife) love each other devoted-
ly, and that in the sight of God she belongs
to him. This letter was a portion ot the
testimony evinced in favor of Dameron,
who was acquitted in his trial at the Crim-
inal Court for shooting Jones, who, it also
appears had a wife living. A couple of
divorce cases might have prevented the
murder, but Dameron failed to see the
beauty of such proceedings and took the
next best and most popular way of getting
out of domestic difficulties.
  THE "Pioneer" pronounces the incident
relative to Mrs. Emily' Pitts Stevens and
her pistol, of very little importance, and
states that at the meeting at Platt's Hall,
where the occurence form which the ex-
citement was raised took place, ex-Assem-
blyman Meeker, without just cause, openly
reprimanded those who were earnest in
their applause of sentiments expresses, co-
inciding with their own views; even calling
out Mrs. Stevens name. For which insult,
at the close of the meeting the lady de-
manded an apology.  Meeker refused to
comply, whereupon, some on handed Mrs.
Stevens a small derringer, which she
thoughtlessly put into her pocket. She is
decidedly opposed to the use of fire-arms
and had no thought of using the derringer.
This is a better and more consistent story
than the oner told by California papers, soon
after the incident occurred.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


AN ADDRESS FROM THE YOUNG
			LADIES.
			    —
WRITTEN ON THE OCCASION OF A RETRENCHMENT
 ASSOCIATION PARTY, GIVEN ON THE TWENTY-
 FOURTH OF JULY, 1872, BEING THE TWENTY
 FIFTH ANNIVERSARY OF THE ENTRANCE OF
 THE PIONEERS INTO THE VALLEY OF THE GREAT
 SALT LAKE.	
		
  When ca led upon, in such a kindly way,
We, from our heart, a few good things can say:
'Tis good to be a Saint of God, and live  
In the pure light His loving mercies give; 
'Tis good to gather at a time like this,
And yield awhile to animated bliss;
'Tis good to mark the change which now appears,
Among the Saints in five and twenty years. 

They came to Utah—came with heart and mind
Giv’n to their God. and to His will resigned;
They settled here—’t has oft been sta ed how,
We know the difference ’tween then and now.
Good is the picture illustration paints
Of God's protection to His faithful Saints;
How, ’mid privations stern, and trying toil.
He blest their labors, blest the seed and soil.

  'Tis good for us to know the Gospel Truth,
Which brightly beams upon us in our youth;
And that our patients have that faith embraced, 
Which will unite the humble, pure and chaste;
And banish all who love, and revel in
The ways of vice, licentiousness and sin:
And it is good that we need not disguise,
The holy feelings which -such thing's despise.

  We may and do.despise the seeming man,
Who seeks to baffle the Almighty’s plan;
Which is to save, exalt and elevate.
Woman, with man, made noble, wise and great:
We cannot, in false friendship, seem to clothe
The dread we bear for deeds we justly loathe!
Yet with compassion’s voice would fain recall,
Vice’s wretched dupes who gaze, and yield and fall!

  We know that we are free—our freedom gives
That confidence, for which proud woman lives;
For we have pride, not that which fumes and flaunts
O’er fashion’s follies and her favorite haunts; 
But ignorance and fripp’ry thrown aside,
True dignity and virtue are our pride;
Our motto Honor, Truth our whole defense,
Our aim to perfect rich intelligence.

  Hence, we regard those who are Saints indeed,
A[-] having right our youthful steps to lead; 
And never to ought else may we submit,
Or in the least descend to follow it.
We feel that we are safe, while being led
By trusty followers of our lawful Head;
And, sooner wou'd we all unite to-day
With one GOOD MAN than one be led astray.

   Not lacking charity, would gladly see,
All mortals healthy, happy, blessed as we;.
Possessing, morals which alone can make 
The sweetest joys of which mankind partake:.
Grateful to God for His parental care,
Anxious that all the same good gifts should share;
And closely follow where our helmsman steers,
To lives Eternal in Celestial spheres.

   Ere we conclude, we wish to humbly pay,
A generous tribute to the passing day; 
Oft clothed in gladness may it reappear,
Still, to the Saints held sacred, sweet and dear,
And crown anew, with love and peace each year,
The home of every good, brave Pioneer;	
Their friends, their neighbors, kindred great and small,
Wives, sons and daughters, Heaven, bless them all!
				—
			    SONG.
				—
  The object of Retrenchment, for us, 1$:
	Not to sit us down in sadness,
	  With our faces grim and long;
	Not to chase the smile of gladness,
	  Not to hush the voice of song.
	Not to miss one pure, true pleasure,
	 Worthy space in heart or brain;
	Not to lose a single treasure—'
	 But a precious store td gain.

	Ladies, who o'er idle fashions,
	  Waste their time and strength and thought,
	Feeding vain and selfish passions,
	  'While their lives amount to nought;
	Know not of the untold sweetness
	   Wrought for those who cultivate,
	Powers of worth which in completeness,
	   Ev’ry virtue will create.	

	Scoffers laugh and mock their plainness.
	  Casting slurs and jeers thereat;
	Pause they for the worlds insaneness?
	  No, they have not time for that;
[new column]

	'Tis enough they fill their missions,
	   Fraught with noble womanhoos;
	Wake their souls and dispositions,
	   Sweet and rich and pure and good.

	Ot to think that f shion's bubbles,
	  From one care could set us free,
	Is, like courting causeless roubles,
	   Nonsense to the first degree:
	Let no honest mind be darkened,
	   Future scenes will shortly prove
	Who to wisdom's voice hath hearkened,
	  Aided every godlike move.

	﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
			  —
  The Young Ladies’ Retrenchment Assocl
ation of the Eleventh Ward, of this city,
held a highly creditable meeting on Thurs-
day, ‘July- 18th. After singing and prayer,
articles from the Bible, Book of Mormon
“Millennial Star” and other works were
read, by officers and members of the Associ-
ation, “Selfishness” was the subject chosen
at the meeting previous, to be spoken upon
Many animated and instructive remarks
were made, and rich hints given upon that
topicand others. The genuine spirit of
purity and freedom which pervaded the
meeting, seemed to render all present joy-
ous and happy. The manner in which those
meetings, held every Thursday afternoon,
are conducted and attended, is very com-
mendable, both to the President Mrs. Mary
Freeze, and the members of the Society.
				—
  Mrs. Amelia, M. Frodsham writes from
Ogden City July 20th, 1872: The Sisters
came over from Lynne and Marriott settle-
ments, both belonging to one precinct, and
asked of and were granted by Bro. F.D.
Richards, the privilege of holding a two
days' Conference of the F. R. Society, when
they issued invitations to some of the Ogden
Sisters. I, for one can testify  the two days'
meeting, and to me it is a testimony that
the formation of the Society is of God. 
	The Conference convened the first day,
July 16th, at Lynne; Sister Ann Becking-
ton, President of the joint Society of Lynne
and Marriott settlements, presiding. Sisters
present from Ogden, President Mrs. Harriet
Brown, Secretary Mrs. A. M. Frodsham,
Treasurer Miss Rosalthie Canfield, Sisters
Jane Richards, Maria Ballantyne, Eveline
Farley and others. Meeting opened with
singing, and prayer by Mrs. M. Ballantyne.
Minutes read by Mrs. Perry, Secretary.
President Mrs. Beckington spoke, welcom-
ing the Sisters from Ogden, and stating that
they had collected, in the Society $100 for
the Emigration Fund. During the forenoon
meeting, addresses were made by Sister
A. M. Frodsham, President H. Brown,
Sisters J. Richards and M. Ballantyne from
Ogden; and Sister Tracy, of Lynne. All
felt well and bore faithful testimonies to the
truth of the Latter-day work, and the good-
ness of God to His children. The meeting
closed with singing, and prayer by Mrs.
Tracy. 
  Met again at 2 p.m. Called to order;
singing; prayer by President Mrs. Becking-
ton; singing by the Scandinavian  Sisters in
their own tongue. 
	Elders F. D. Richards, Herricks and Farr
were expected at the meeting, but as they
did not arrive President Mrs. Beckington
called on the Sisters belonging to her Society
to speak. The Scandinavian Sisters bore
their testimonies, and instructing and en-
couraging remarks were made by Sister
Bingham and several others.  "The Spirit
of God like a fire is burning" was sung.
Other remarks followed, and the meeting
adjourned to meet to-morrow at 10 a.m., at
Marriott settlement. 
[new column]
	Dismissed with singing, and prayer by
President Mrs. Brown.
	At 10 a.m., July 17, Conference convened,
pursuant to adjournment, in the school
house at Marriott settlement; Sister Beck-
ington presiding.  Singing; prayer by Sister
Ballantyne; singing by the Scandinavian
Sisters in their own tongue; minutes read
and approved. Sisters Beckington, Ballan-
tyne and Frodsham made remarks. Miss
R. Canfield read a report of the Ogden
Society, from the EXPONENT, and after-
ward addressed the meeting.  She was fol-
lowed by Mrs. Jane Richards, Mrs Emily
Richards, Miss J. Richards and President
Mrs. Beckington.  After which the meeting
adjourned until 2 p.m.  Prayer by Mrs. 
Frodsham. 
	At 2 p.m., the Conference again con-
vened.  Meeting opened with singing;
prayer by Mrs. Hannah Baird; singing by
the Scandinavian Sisters.  Speakers: Sisters
Beckington, Baird and Cordon.
    At about 3 o'clock, Elder Richards arrived.
He stated that he had been detained by the
fire in Ogden.  He felt very much pleased
to see the deep interest manifested by the
Sisters in seeking to do good; they are not
sent abroad to the world, but have missions
at home which are great and responsible.
Sending for the poor is the beginning of
their redemption.
   Elder Harrison spoke a short time.
   It was then moved, seconded and carried
that Mrs. A. M. Frodsham send a report of
this Conference to the "EXPONENT."
  Conference adjourned "sine die."
  Benediction by Elder Marriott.
  The very interesting and beneficial, two 
days' meeting, of which the foregoing is a
brief  synopsis, was inaugerated and con-
ducted by Sisters Beckington, Tracy, Baird
and Parry, of Lynne and Marriott settle-
ments. 
			___
	The following is from Mrs. Maria Parkin-
son, of Wellsville, Cache County, under date
July 25th:
  This branch of the Female Relief Society
is in a very healthy condition.  After de-
ducting $102.05 for the relief of the poor for
the year, we have on hand $352.23. We have
also donated $50 to the P.E. Fund.  When
the fact that for the last six years we have
had the terrible plague of grasshoppers to
contend with is taken into consideration, we
think we have done well.  Our efforts have
been blessed; the poor have been comforted,
and the labors of our Bishop greatly relieved.
It is a labor of love on which our Father in
heaven smiles approvingly.


﻿PROVIDENCE MADE both tear and  laugh-
ter, and both for kind purposes; for .as laugh-
ter enables mirth and surprise to breathe
freely, so tears enable sorrow to vent Itself
patiently. Tears hinder sorrow from be-
coming despair and madness; and laughter
is one of the very highest privileges’of. rea-
son, being confined to the human species.


﻿“MADAM,” SAID a husband to his young
wife in a little altercation which will spring
up in the best regulated families, “when a
man,and his wife, have quarreled and .each
considers the other at fault, which of the
two ought to advance towards reconcilia-
tion?”  “The best natured, and wiser of
the two,” said the. wife, putting up her
mouth for a kiss which was given with
unction.


The Baroness Nathaniel de Rothschild is
an industrious and accomplished artist.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


SOME WOMEN imagine they look well in
anything, and consequently go about in the
dowdiest kind of style.  Arms out at the
elbows are endurable, but when it comes to
sour hair and toes out of stockings and shoes,
it is too much.


MY SISTER, LEONORA A. MORLEY.
			----
DEDICATED TO HER FRIENDS IN BRIGHAM
  CITY, WHERE SHE DEPARTED THIS LIFE,
  FEBRUARY 11, 1872.

  'Tis hard to part with those we dearly love,
But parting comes to all.
					   No purer tie-
No holier sympathy warms human breast,
Than that of loving sisterhood, where heart
To heart is joined and interwoven with
A long, well tested and unbroken chain
Of mutual confidence—a confidence
Unstirred by envy, jealousy, or breach
Of sacred trust: Where the broad stream of thought
Flows unabridg'd: where each can think aloud.
Such was the love inspiring confidence,
Strengthened as years accumulated with
My sister and myself. Ours was the sweet
Reciprocation where each sentiment
Found safe repository—safe as heaven's
Eternal archives.
					   But my sister's gone!
I feared—I felt—I knew she soon would go,
But when beside her bed I watch'd, and saw
The last faint breath which fed the spring of life
Exhaled, it seemed frail nature's tend'rest cord
Was rent asunder, and a crushing sense
Of loneliness, like solitude's deep shade,
In that unguarded moment made me feel
As though the lights of earth had all gone out,
And left me desolate.
					 I knew't was false—
I knew that many noble, loving ones,
And true, remained; but none can fi’l
The vacant place—it is impossible;
Th' endearing ties we hold as saints of God,
The ties of consanguinity, secured
By sacred cov'nants which the Priesthood bind
On earth, and 't is recorded in the heavens,
We shall perpetuate beyond the grave;
Eternal union with the cherished ones
Will crown the glory of immortal lives.
  True love may multiply its object mosts
Extensively without diminishing
Its strength, but love accepts no substitute.
   When the fond mother lays her darling down
In death's cold, silent sleep, though others may
Be added to her arms, the vacancy
Remains until the resurrection shall
Give back her child.
						My sister faithfully
Life's changeful battle waged—her life was full
Of years—her years were fill'd with usefulness:
Her trust was in the living God, who hears
And speaks as He was wont to hear and speak.
She loved the Gospel and exemplified
It in her life. Her heart knew no deceit,
Her lips ne'er moved with fulsome flattery,
Her tongue with guile. Other positions of
Responsibility, as well as those
Of wife and mother she has nobly filled.
Her sun went down in peace.  For her death had
No sting—the grave will have no victory—
Her noble spirit lives and dwells above.
  The casket rests—The pure component part,
The' eternal portion of the human form,
In life combined with the gross element,
Sleeps in the bosom of our mother Earth,
Secure from nature's changing processes—
Despite decomposition's complex skill.
  But the gross, earthly substance, that which is
Both tangible to mortal sight and touch—
Formed with affinities that downward tend
Strength'ning our hold on this our lower life,
Clinging to earth as like adheres to like
This, soon as life becomes extinct, by dint
Of one of nature's fundamental laws,
The law of restitution, has commenced,
And separates till all is disengaged—
Till every particle shall be restored
Back to its native element, to be
Transformed in infinite varieties
To new creations and in other forms—
Through every grade of life and being here,
From earth and herbage up to brute and man—
From land to land—from clime to clime transferred;
Leaving the pure unchanging element
( When all that is corruptible has been
Dissolved and passed away) to rest—to sleep
Until the glorious resurrection morn.
And then come forth in triumph from the tomb
And clothe the spirit with immortal bloom.
  Adieu my sister, we shall meet again,
And live on earth when Jesus Christ shall reign.
							 ELIZA R. SNOW

acquaintance, and often cuts them too.


IT'S THE way with mites—there is not
one of them that does not think himself the
entire cheese.


A MISER grows rich by seeming poor; an
extravagent man grows poor by seeming
rich.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS
				—
 A WOMAN who attempts to carry out-
plans of system, order and economy, is con-
stantly liable to have them crossed, and her
expectations violated by the inexperience
or negligence of those about her. The busi-
ness of housekeeping consists of items so
small that for want of care and order, the
whole often becomes a confused mass, and
everything must, inevitably go wrong. Al-
though, at such times, an orderly house-
keeper is apt to be irritated and feel like
giving vent to her irritability in harsh, re-
proving words, a calm, decided spirit is the
best calculated to meet such difficulties, for
the restoration of complete discipline. Scold-
ing does not do much good, and a con-
tinuous stream of complaint and reproof is
generally met by sullen silence or imperti-
nence from a child or a domestic. Anger is
likely to prevent any resolution of future
amendment, or any regret for past misde-
meanor. A woman should regard her duties,
as dignified, important and difficult, and her
mind may be soothed and even elevated by
a sense of the far-reaching influence of her
example. If she feels that she is a cypher
and that it makes little difference how she
performs her duties, she has far less to
sustain her than if she feels the importance
of her station and the weight of her respon-
sibilities.
	FOR HOUSE PLANTS.—A good fertilizer is
made by dissolving one ounce of glue in one
gallon of water and use for moistening the
plants.
	TO CLEAN DECANTERS.—The best way to
clean decanters, after wine has stood in them
for some time, is to wash them out with a
little pearl-ash and warm water, adding, if
necessary, a spoonful or two of fresh slacked
lime.  A few very small cinders will facili-
tate the action of the fluid against the sides
of the vessel.
	FOR HOARSENESS.—Take the whites of
two eggs, beat them and add two spoonsful
of white sugar; grate in a little nutmeg and
then add a pint of luke-warm water. Stir
well and drink often. Repeat the prepara-
tion if necessary, and it is said to cure the
most obstinate case of hoarseness in a com-
paratively short time.
	WHITE of eggs for burns is now used ex-
tensively, and seems to be preferable to
collodion or cotton. Seven or eight success-
ive applications of this substance, sooths
pain; and it effectually excludes the air
from the burn.
	LEMON SPONGE.—Dissolve one ounce of
isinglass in a pint of boiling water; when
cold, strain it and add the juice of three
lemons, strained; two ounces of white sugar,
the rind of a lemon cut very thin; beat all
together and strain it.  When nearly cold,
add the whites of two eggs well beaten and
whisk it until it becomes of the consistency
of sponge; wet a mould and put in the
sponge. It should be partly settled before
the whisking is commenced, or it will take
longer to do.
	RAISED CAKE WITHOUT EGGS.—Stir to-
gether a large coffee cupful of light brown
or white sugar and half a cupful of butter;
add half a pint of sweet milk arid half a pint
of warm water; stir in flour enough to make
a thick batter and half a cupful of yeast; set
it to rise over night and in the morning stir
in a cupful of chopped raisins or currants,
and a teaspoonful each of cinnamon, cloves
and nutmeg. Put into baking pans; let it
rise until perfectly light, and then bake
slowly.


ONE WAY LIFE IS BLIGHTED.
			—
	A part of the misery in the world
arises from persons, not understanding each
other; sometimes from selfish indifference
on the subject, sometimes from thinking
that to do so is petty and puerile. Two
people, for instance, are thrown into inti-
mate daily relations. One understands no
suffering that is not purely physical. If he
is not hungry, if he is not thirsty, if he is
not cold, if he has a roof over his head, if he
is not bothered,  whether-necessarily. or
otherwise his life is rounded off. To shed 
a tear for lack of sympathy, or appreciation,
or love, after that is to him lunacy, and to
be treated as such, with a hard, Gradgrind
non-recognition worthy of such a creed.
Now, imagine a sensitive nature, delicately
susceptible to the “little things”—miscalled
so—which make up our lives, grieving over
rough or indifferent words, where tender 
recognition should have come at the right
moment, like medicine—a tenderness which
costs nothing, perhaps only a simple word
or caress, or look, even; hungering day after
day for something besides the hard practi-
calities of life—imagine such a person keep-
ing back as far as possible the unshed tears,
conscientiously, nevertheless, doing duty—
labor—day after day, week after week,
month after month, with a heavy heart; and
the other eating; sleeping, drinking, as in-
different to the suffering caused as if life
and loyalty were fulfilled by these  latter
conditions.
	It is very poor comfort to answer that dif-
fering natures have differing limitations;
just as one vessel may hold a gallon and
another a pint, and each be useful in its
way. Would it not be well for this stolid
nature, voluntarily choosing such relation-
ship, to ask the question whether there may
not be real suffering in these cases, though
he himself “Never felt so, and therefore
can’t understand it?” Is it not duty, as
well as wisdom, in such juxtaposition, to
make an effort to “understand it,” and not
coolly to deny its existence, or pronounce it 
a sham, because he has only one nerve
sensitive to pain or pleasure where the
other has a hundred?
	It is pitiful to think how, for the want of
this, some natures, like prisoned birds, beat
themselves against the bars of a relentless
fate, till, bruised and maimed, they drop, at
least for the timebeing, into a hopeless des-
pair, their songs stifled, their wings clipped,
and darkness and silence reigning, where
brightness and music might so easily have
come.	
	Ali, it is blessed that in another world
there will be no limitations. Else how could
Heaven be, with natures one half paralyzed,
as here, for want of Space to expand—chilled,
stunted, frowned upon, denounced, for lack
of that charity without which all else is
comparatively valueless?
	“I never felt so; it is all nonsense.” I
neVer yet saw those cruel words in-
scribed on a tombstone as the cause of death.


ONE THING brings upanother, especially if
the first thing is an emetic.


IF YOU want to test the ingenious tortures
of the inquisition, place a pretty girl in the
house with a lot of old spinsters to live. If
they do not get her on the rack, within a
month, it will be an exceptional case.
