﻿SPLINTERS.

	London has four thousand miles of streets.
.........Harvesting is about over in Virginia...
Twenty ocean steamships now ply between
New Orleans and Europe......On the first
day of July, the women of Illinois were
made eligible to hold appointive offices in
that State.....The Press Association in Lon-
don are disputing as to which first sent to
America the news of the result of the late
boating contest......The strikers in New
York have cut down the building going on
there nearly one-half, as compared with last
year......The Woman Suffragists of Missouri
are a unit for Grant and Wilson........A wood-
en water pipe factory is to be started in
Salem.......The Caledonian Society in Lon-
don gets up fancy balls in which some of
the guests attire themselves to represent
the ancient Picts and Scots......Gen. Grant
has placed in office twenty-three blood rela-
tives and eighteen who are relatives by
marriage......The woman movement has not
sensibly modified the social atmosphere of
St. Louis.......General Porter will shortly
make public a list of Democrats who are
going to stump for Grant......A Catacazy
pamphlet is published at Long Branch, in
which the ex-Minister endeavors to vindi-
cate himself from charges brought against
him by Sectretry Fish, and says he was not
dismissed from his position, but relieved at
his own request........The yacht “Josephine”
recently capsized in a gale, off Bedhoes’
Island, and sank; passengers and crew were
saved.........A Boston special has not the
slightest doubt that General Banks has de-
termined to abandon the administration
and array himself on the side of the Democ-
racy.......The first success of the Woman
Suffrage movement in England is said to
be the result of a surprise, being earned
through Parliament before conservative
people got their eyes open......A writer
from St. Louis says, that city confidently
expects to become the capital of the United
States and to supersede Boston as the hub
of the universe.......Ladies are to be admitted
to the Young Men’s Christian Union, of
Chicago, and the name is to be changed to
the Christian Union.....Ladies in square
cut bodices are not admitted to Queen Vic-
toria’s drawing rooms......The Board of Vis-
itors, at West Point, in their annual report,
animadvert severely upon many things
about the academy.....Six Chinamen were
killed and two others severely wounded, a
few days since, by the explosion of a blast,
on the new road between Carson and Reno.
........An extensive coal bed is being de-
veloped on Reynolds’ Creek, near Cabbing-
ton’s, Idaho.......It is reported that A. T.
Stewart has given $30,000 to aid in the
election of Greeley.......At an influential
meeting of English Catholics, in London,
the action against the Pope, of the Italian
Government, and the new German law pro-
scribing the Jesuits, were condemned.......A
school house in Copenhagen, Denmark, is
furnished for one thousand children......One
of the rumors concerning the shooting of
McMullin is, that it grew out of some diffi-
culty in the Moyamensing Association.......
A number of Roman coins have recently
been found in Wales.....For a poem of 1,-
500 lines, the “Overland Monthly” pays
Joaquin Miller $2,000.......Romeo, the big
elephant, died lately from the effects of an
operation to cure his diseased leg......Nearly
200 cases of sunstroke occurred in one day
of July, in New York, of which sixty were
fatal...Two Japanese youngsters are being
educated at Forest Grove university, Oregon.
......The “Lady Elgin” is the name of a
neat little journal published by the ladies of
[new column]
the Elgin Watch manufactory........A $44,-
000 Court house is to be erected this season
at Modesta......The question of the succes-
sion to the Turkish throne, still creates
much excitement in Turkey.......A Salem,
Massachusetts paper says, General Grant’s
greatest fault is that he stands in the way of
so many candidates to the Presidency......
The Gerke vineyard in Tahama County,
California, covers an area of 150 acres, all in
a full state of cultivation.......Mr. M. G. Up-
ton, leading editor of the “Alta California”
for ten years, has retired, and Mr. Frank
Soule succeeds him.......Fans made of the
scales of fish have been introduced and will,
doubtless, soon be very popular......Billy
Edwards, the light-weight champion, and
Batt Mullins, an English bruiser, have been
matched for $1,000 a side and the light-
weight championship of America......A cor-
respondent of the Washinton “Patriot” says
that the title of L. L. D. bestowed on Mr.
Grant means “Lover of Large Donations.....
The printers’ strike in Milwaukee created
greater excitement than any event since
the Chicago conflagration......A patent for
ear, cheek and chin muffs has been granted
to William Powellware, of New York.


﻿FACTS.
			    —
	A great many people who visit Salt Lake
City and remain over night, or a couple of days
and nights, go away imagining themselves
tolerably well informed with regard to affairs
in Utah. An account of a visit to Salt Lake
and what was learned, appears in some news-
paper; and after reading it, no one need be in
doubt as to what sort of person or persons the
writers have been entertained by while here; if
there appears a shadow of truth in the article.
Once in a while an humble visitant sees only
good in us, and illustrates the Mormons as be-
ing all happy, all virtuous, intelligent, temper-
ate, moral and industrious. This, however, is
rarely the case, as we are most likely to be
misjudged in a less pleasant style.  Even those
who wish to be unprejudiced, and treat with
candor what they are led to observe, have their
eyes blinded and their minds darkened concern-
ing Utah and the Mormons, and represent un-
truths which they themselves may believe to
be correct.
	The Mormons do not claim to  have
reached a standard of perfection yet. They do
not assume to have overcome the world, its
evils, temptations and sufferings. They never
insinuate that there are no bad people among
them; no inebriates, liars, thieves, fools, scound-
rels and hypocrites. They are ever ready to
confess that there are numbers, professing to be
Latter-day Saints, whom his Satanic majesty
might blush to acknowledge as his relatives.
To say they are all contented would not be
true; that none in Polygamy find it a cross
would be false. But that as a people, and mak-
ing the statement in a general sense, the Mor-
mons are the most temperate, industrious,
chaste and generous in their desires and


practices, and, in consequence of this, the most
felicitous in their everyday life, of any class of
people extant, is one principle to which they
adhere. And one proof of the efficacy of the
basis upon which this faith is built, is evinced
by the spirit manifested in the reports of meet-
ings held by women outside and inside of Mor-
mondom. Minutes of meetings held in San Fran-
cisco and other places, show that the women
are dissatisfied with their present position and
wish to burst the bonds of tyranny in which
they consider themselves bound. While min-
utes of those held in Utah speak of peace, good-
will, satisfaction and thankfulness for the great
blessings with which the majority of the Mormon
women consider themselves surrounded. And
yet, these very women who are so anxious to
free themselves, misrepresent the women of
Utah as “broken-hearted,down-trodden, misera-
ble creatures,” whom they imagine have no
sense or spirit.
	If their position is happier for them than
ours would be, we are willing that they should
maintain it, and improve it as fast and as
much as possible. We have no thought of ex-
changing with them, or drawing over to their
views and opinions; for while in the possession
of our senses, we prize our freedom too highly
to yield it so unprofitably; and only regret the
waste of sympathy lavished upon us, who
neither need nor appreciate it.


﻿   WHY WOMEN SHOULD VOTE.

	While many women care nothing about the
ballot or politics, and would avert the trouble
of voting were the right granted to them with-
out their asking for it; others have formed
themselves into serried bodies of contending,
pugnacious clamorers, whose vociferations are
difficult to be traced or understood. Yet it is
supposable that they think, by the purveyance
of the power to vote for those they believe most
worthy and best calculated to be placed as dic-
tators and rulers over the people, they should
be able to render that purgation to the world
which is so manifestly necessary.
	But were that power which they so enthusi-
asticly claim the right to possess, bestowed up-
on them, how many would be truly efficient to
wield it in the proper channel? Not that wo-
men have less than men of the natural endow-
ments requsite for forming a clear and irrepre-
hensible judgment; but they have not culti-
vated those faculties. Their attention has been
given to other things; and they are, therefore,
unqualified, the majority of them, to act wisely
in the capacity they so earnestly seek.
	That men abuse the powers they hold, is not
an evidence that women would not use those
powers in an empirical manner if permitted to
use them at all. The fact that the motives of
women are purer, their sentiments more re-
fined and elevated than those of men, should be
sufficient testimony that the right of suffrage
ought to be conceded to them. Not that they
may teach men wisdom by their superior intel-
ligence, but that they may exert their more
beneficient and chaste influences in endeavor-
ing to purify the social atmosphere.


﻿IT is believed that Texas alone, will
within a comparatively few years produce
a larger quantity of the great agricultural
staples than are now produced in all the
other States of the Union.


﻿Study contentment. In these days of in-
ordinate greed and self-indulgence, keep
down the accursed spirit of grasping. What
they don’t have makes thousands wretched.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	IMPROVEMENTS in our City and harvesting
in its surroundings are visible everywhere.

	AN ADDRESS “in behalf of the twenty-four
young ladies” and an interesting communica-
tion from St. George, both contributed by
“Flora,” are unavoidably held over, as are also
several other articles and correspondence.

THE LADIES’ Retrenchment Association Ball,
given on the evening of the Twenty-fourth of
July, in the Thirteenth Ward assembly rooms,
was a success. After paying all the incidental
expenses, the Committee found they had a
small surplus of $22.00—which they deposited
in the P. E. Fund.

	WHOSE IS IT?—A young lady wishes to make
enquiry through the “EXPONENT,” whether
the owner of a pretty gray and white, half-
grown kitten, that followed her from some
house on Second East street, early on the
morning of the 25th of July, would like to
ascertain its whereabouts. Information con-
cerning it may be obtained at Bishop L. D.
Young’s, 18th Ward.

 -     THE PAST two weeks has been a period
fraught with care and sorrow to many in differ-
ent parts of the City. Numerous accidents have
occurred, many of them of a very serious
nature, and some fatal. Quite a number of
deaths from sickness are also reported. To the
exceedingly dry and hot weather which has
been experienced may be accorded much of the
cause for ill health which has been and is still
complained of.

	THROUGH THE courtesy of Bishop L. D.
Young, we have had the pleasure of perusing
an interesting letter from Brother Edward
Stevenson, Missionary to the United States,
written at Polk City, Iowa. The writer repre-
sents himself and Elder Porter as enjoying
their Missionary labors, being favorbly received
in that portion of the country. His letter,
directed to Bishop Young, is addressed, within,
to “Elder L. D. Young, and the Home Mission-
ary Staff;” and he cordially greets them all.

	THE ORGANIZATION of a physiological class,
which took place on the afternoon of Thursday,
July 25th, under the supervision of Mrs. Sarah
Kimball; in the 15th Ward F. R. Society hall,
in this City, promises to be an opening for the
pursuit of much valuable information and con-
sequent good results for our ladles. There is
scarcely any, perhaps no other branch of educa-
tion which can be made so practically and in-
dividually useful, especially to women, as the
study of physiology. Yet it has heretofore
been sadly neglected; and even ridiculed by
many unthinking people.

	THOSE NAMES presented to the public as can-
didates for the various offices to be filled at the
coming election, have, without doubt been well
canvassed and considered by those who are de-
sirous of participating in the electoral enact-
ments. Ladies, as well as gentlemen should
understand the importance attached to giving
support to a candidate to any office, by which
the whole or a portion of the community is to
be wronged or benefitted. To be sure we are
voting to sustain good officers, is the only way
wo can know we are not voting to sustain bad
ones.

	ALL INNOCENT attempts at home progress
and self improvement should be generously
sustained and encouraged. Yet, care must be
taken, that charity does not overreach its legit-
imate limits and pronounce acquirements of
thought and manner of gesticulation good when
they are really crude, or excellent while only
[new column]
moderate. The performance of the young folks
at the theatre last evening was, for the most
part very well executed; the haste manifested
throughout the entire presentation of the drama,
being excusable. The duet by the young flut-
ists and the song, “Dolly Varden” were very
happily rendered.


﻿A WOMAN'S VIEWS.

EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	There appeared an article in the “Deseret
News” of July 10th, on the seduction of a
Miss Hardy by Wm. L. Davis, with com-
ments by a writer in the “New York
Standard.”  The writer says that Miss
Hardy was as bad as her seducer, and if
taken before a jury of women would be pro-
nounced the worst of the two. I, a woman,
would solemnly decry such an unworthy
statement. I am not a young woman; I
have traveled considerably and found the
nature of woman much the same in all
places where I have been. That woman
desires honorable marriage is true; but se-
duction, never! She never wishes her own
downfall; it would be contrary to all reason.
	Man has asserted his superiority and right
to rule; woman has received this idea, and
believed him most religiously. Is this
wrong? Woman sets up a shrine and
places some man there as the Deity, often
to her own sorrow. That women often fall
is sadly true; and that when they have
fallen, they often become utterly reckless is
also a truth. But are they the ones most to
blame? I think not. I know some men
try to destroy young girls; and that when
one of them succeeds he glories in the de-
struction he has wrought and taunts his
his victim with her folly, and spreads
her darkened name far and wide leaving
her nothing but degredation for the
rest of her miserable life. If she escapes
uncontaminated, what then? She and the
Lord only know of the villain’s damning
purposes!
	Can the writer in the “Standard” deny
these facts? I know he cannot. And if I
read his nature aright, as exhibited in his
article, he is not an innocent man—or he
would never libel his mother’s sex. Surely
my judgment is just, for by his own words
have I judged him.
	If Miss Hardy was placed before a jury of
sensible women what would be their sen-
tence? I do not hesitate to say that the de-
stroyer would have been shot dead instead
of only wounded in the hip; for they would
have sorrowed over the downfall of a sister
and striven to reclaim her. Do not think so
meanly of woman as to believe she does not
feel the shame that is brought on her by
the sins of her sister woman. It is as a stab
with a sharp knife. A man can never know
the pain.
	The “Standard” says there would be a fine
time if all the fathers of the miserable be-
ings that ply their horrible trade in New
York would shoot down their visitors.  I
think it would be a great blessing if all the
pure fathers would follow the example of
Mr. Hardy. Let me tell the “Standard”
that one.very fruitful cause of evil in wo-
men is in the evil their fathers have com-
mitted before them. You cannot gather
good fruit from a corrupt tree.
	GRANTSVILLE.				MARY.


﻿AN INCIDENT.
				—
		"Teach them correct principles,
	And they will govern themselves.”

	We give the following little incident as
it was told to us by a young teacher, con-
sidering the illustration worthy of notice to


many mothers as well as teachers of the
young:
	“While attending to the more advanced
classes, I gave the small children liberty to
play a portion of the time in the yard sur-
rounding the school house. One day a little
girl left her play and came in to tell me a
very pitiful story. Said she:
	“ ‘Neils has found two bright, pretty
shells, where we were playing, and has
given them both to Hannah, and they won’t
let me have one of them; mustn’t they?’
	“ ‘Did you ask Hannah if she would
please to let you have one of them?’ I
asked.
	“ ‘I am going to say that to her now,’
said Maggie; and away she ran, but presently
returned again with a sorrowful face, and
the sad tidings:
	“ ‘She won’t let me have it, Miss ——,’
	“ ‘Well, I’ll tell you what, Maggie,’ I re-
sponded; ‘put your arms around Hannah’s
neck, kiss her, and say, ‘Hannah, won’t
you let your dear little class-mate have one
of those shells?’
	“Maggie scampered away again, but was
back in a moment, this time holding the
shell up triumphantly, and exclaiming.
“See! school-ma’am, isn’t it pretty?”
	“It was pretty and I said so, and the glad
little creature was off to her play again,
smiling and singing as hapily as ever; and
she and Hannah have been the best of
friends ever since.
	“I have inveriably found it the best way
to let the little ones settle their own diffi-
culties, if they can reasonably; and I never
interfere, unless highly necessary,
more than to advise.”
	The same teacher remarks that one day
she praised a bright, clean little face and
smoothly combed head, and told the owner
she wanted a kiss from such a tidy, sweet,
little boy, whenever he came to school in
the morning; and that afterwards she was
never troubled with dirty faces or un-
combed heads; or if one did happen to make
its appearance occasionally, all  the reproof
necessary was for her to say “I have no
kiss for you to-day.” That was better than
scolding.


﻿KIND WORDS.
				—
	The Utah “Pomologist" says, in the last
number to hand:
	THE WOMEN'S EXPONENT.—No’s 1 and
2 are before us, and we give it a hearty
welcome. Its editorials are plain, sound and
logical, and its selections pithy, newsy and
well chosen. The character, object and aim
of the EXPONENT is widely different from
that of the political, strong-minded wo-
man’s rights journals of the day. Every
family in Utah should give it support. Long
may it wave, an exponent of the sentiments
and feelings of the many good women of
Utah.	
	And the Council Bluffs “Record and
Farmer” has this in regard to our little
journal:
	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.—The above is the
title of a new semi-monthly publication, in
octavo form, now being published at Salt
Lake City, Utah, by ladies of Mormon faith,
in that Territory, who, in their Prospectus
for the paper, say: “It is better to repre-
sent ourselves than to be misrepresented by
others.” And it is to this end they have
begun the publication of the WOMAN'S
EXPONENT, a journal neat in typographical
appearance and replete with reading matter
of interest. Success to the enterprize of the
ladies.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


ABOUT WOMEN.
				   —
	A Georgia lady has woven a balmoral out
of the hair of a rabbit 
	DOUGLASS Jerrold said; “Eve ate the
apple that she might indulge in dress”.
	MRS. DR. Tarr takes the place of her hus-
band, recently deceased, as editor of the
Denton (Md.) “Journal.”
	A BUFFALO woman has raised  $2,500
worth of strawberries, this year, from two
acres of ground.
	MRS. Tupper of Des Moines has been
made professor of apiarian science in the
Iowa Agricultural College.
	A YOUNG lady in Ohio, Maggie Reid by
name, has distinguished herself as a model
farmer.  There is some sense in that.
	IN Indiana women plow, plant, hoe and
harvest, preach and chop their own wood.
What do the men busy themselves with?
	THE Michigan State Woman Suffrage
Association has raised six hundred dollars,
and its Secretary, Mrs. M. Victor, is in the
field as a lecturer.
	MISS Foley, an American sculptor at Rome
has been about the most successful artist in
the Holy City, during the past season. Most
of her work has gone to England.
	A TENNESSEE girl broke an arm on the
eve of her wedding day, but pluckily went 
through the ceremony with her arm in a
sling at the appointed hour.
	MISS Eliza Hete, of Cumberland, Md., 23
years of age, who lost the power of speech
five years since by the effects of a cold, and
had tried the best medical skill in vain,
suddenly found her voice restored Sunday
while humming a hymn tune sung by the
choir at the service.
	MRS. Somerville, the well-known author-
ess of the “Connection of Physical Sciences.”
now in her ninety-second year, was present
and saw the late eruption of Vesuvius.
	A CALIFORNIA paper reports that a hotel
chamber-maid laid aside her broom a few
days ago, and calling for a settlement, told
her employer she had been dabbling in
stocks for a year or more, and had something
more than $200,000 as the result.
	A GENTLEMAN, recently married in
Chicago, presented his bride at the wedding
with the original transcript of one of the
first dispatches ever sent over the first tele-
graph line, from Baltimore to Washington.
It was the announcement to the lady’s
grandmother of her birth, and read, “Only
a girl.”
	THE annual commencement of Wesleyan
Female College, Wilmington, took place on
June 20. Nine young ladies were gradu-
ated, among whom only Miss Pickles, Miss
Huntzinger, and Miss Housten took the full
collegiate course. The Baccalaureate, by
President Wilson, was a frank and honest
production, and very much in keeping with
the circumstances. The degree of A. M.
was conferred in course upon Miss Walters,
of Media, and Miss Galliner, of Philadel-
phia.
	THE Philadelphia Board of Education
have lately had under consideration a mo-
tion to strike out of their by-laws the rule that,
“no female teacher shall be elected to the
position of principal to a male grammar
school.” It was argued that a woman who
had the learning, strength and capacity to
teach, is as capable of filling this position as
a man, and that this is proved by the fact that
they do fill satisfactorily most, if not all the
subordinate places as teachers and assistant
teachers.


FLOWERS BY THE WAYSIDE.
				—
	Truth needs no flower of speech. 
	True dignity never descends to a mean
action.
	Sensitive hearts may suffer most, but the
wisest are the happiest.
	Kind words are like delicious perfumes;
generous actions like a rich and wholesome
repast.
	As iron is most strongly united by the
fiercest flame, so the firmest friendship is
often formed in mutual adversity.
	Make your friends well pleased with
themselves, and you need have no fears but
they will feel kindly toward you.
	Politeness never calls for a sacrifice of a
single principle of truth, sincerity or honor;
but grows out of the purest benevolence.
	A tried and proved character for truth,
honor and honesty, is the best capital in the
long run, and gives the largest interest.
	Professions of propriety may win the
flattery of the unwary, but the friendship of
the wise is only gained by the practice of
principles.
	To “make the best of it” is one way of
going through the world; to “make the
worst of it” is another. The latter course
is working hard for poor pay.
	The blossom cannot tell what becomes of
its odor, nor man what becomes of his in-
fluence and examples, as they roll away be-
yond his ken, on their perilous mission.
	“Who noble ends by noble means obtains,
or failing smiles in exile or in chains, like
good Aurelius let him reign, or bleed like
Socrates, that man is GREAT INDEED.”
	The external misfortunes of life, disap-
pointments, poverty and sickness, are light
in comparison to those inward distresses of
mind occasioned by folly, passion and
guilt.
	When upon rational and sober enquiry
we have established our principles, let us
not suffer them to be shaken by the scoffs
of the licentious or the cavils of the scepti-
cal.
	Memory presides over the past, action
over the present. The first is a rich temple
hung with glorious trophies, and lined with
tombs; the other should have no shrine but
duty.
	Recreation is to the mind what whetting is
to the scythe—it sharpens the edge, which
would otherwise grow dull and blunt; but
constant whetting wears the edge entirely
away.
	To tell your own secrets is generally folly,
but the folly is without guilt; to communi-
cate those with which we are entrusted is
always treachery, for the most part com-
bined with folly.
The only way to meet affliction is to pass
through it solemnly, slowly, with humanity
and faith, as the Israelites passed through
the sea. Then its very waves of misery
will divide, and become to us a wall on the
right side and on the left, until the gulf
narrows before our eyes, and we land safe
on the opposite shore.
	Flowers are the delight of childhood and
a pleasure in old age. They typify all that
is beautiful in sentiment, and the language
they express is emblematical of the tenderest
thoughts and feelings of men. Romance and
poetry are blended with their history. How
many a faded violet there has been and is
now, yellow and discolored with age, whose
withered leaves tell the story of a human
heart more eloquently than human language
can express!


HOW TO GET AN ORCHARD.
			 	     —
	After selecting the ground for an orchard;
go to work and dig and haul the stone and
stumps from it. Plough deep and level the
land off, and you are ready for your fence.
Put on a good, substantial fence, that will
not admit any creatures only by the way of
the gate or bars, and always keep the fence
in good repair, and suffer no cattle to enter
the enclosure, only for the purpose of labor.
This fence built you will plough your ground
again, and harrow and work in manure to
your liking. Sow your ground and seed
down the same. Pick up all the stones and
sticks that lie on top of the ground, and
take them away so that it will leave a
smooth surface.
	Select the best trees you can find; those
that are young, straight and sound. When
you take them up be careful and do not
break any of the roots, for they have none
to part with. When set they want to be so
near each other that they will shade the
ground well. When it is necessary to cut
limbs, cut them when small. Let the lower
limbs be so high from the ground that a tall
man can pass under them and not hit his
beaver. When you prune, be careful and
let no two limbs in the top chafe each other;
cut off one of the two.
	Now for the management of the ground.
You must keep all ploughs and hogs’ snouts
out of the surface of the ground of your
orchard. I know most every one says (and
practices) ploughing, sowing, mowing, and
hauling crops out of their orchard, but I say
it is a bad practice. Let the crop of grass go
back into the ground every year, and that
will keep the ground mellow and rich, and
will protect the roots of the trees from the
droughts of the season and the cold chills of
the fall and spring, and also help to keep
them warm, rugged, and healthy. By so
doing, they will bear well and live long.
You want your ground in a condition that
will bear thirty or forty hundreds to the acre.
[?—Ed.]	Just before you gather your ap-
ples, you can cut the grass down and spread
it, or you can roll it down, just as you please,
so that it will be smooth to pick up your ap-
ples. If you think more nutriment to the
trees is wanted, you can haul and spread
manure on the ground, but do not allow the
surface of your orchard soil to be broken, or
any crops to be taken from it.
	If you should want a wash for your trees
at any time, first scrape the trunks, and
then put on fish oil. This is the best wash
that can be found.
	Our orchards are all dying out, and what
is the cause? It is owing to their treat-
ment, as I have before said; ploughing up
and cutting off the roots, exposing them to
sudden hard droughts and severe frosts, and
robbing them of nutriment which tends to
impoverish and disorder them, and brings
on decay and short life. Feed and clothe
the trees well, and keep them warm, and
you will do this by not breaking the surface
of the soil or taking the foliage away.—
"Maine Famer."


GOOD ADVICE.—Don’t be discouraged if
occasionally you slip down by the way, and
others tread on you a little. In other words,
don’t let a failure or two dishearten you; ac-
cidents will happen, miscalculations will
sometimes be made, things will turn out
different to our expectations, and we may
be sufferers. It is worth while to remember
that fortune is like the skies of April, some-
times cloudy, and sometimes clear and
favorable.

season is on “Successful People.”


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


LITTLE MARY'S WISH.
					—
"I have seen the first robin of spring, mother dear, 
	And have heard the brown darling sing,
You said, ‘Hear it and wish, and ’twill surely come
	true;'
	So I've wished such a beautiful thing!

I thought I would like to ask something for you;
	But I couldn’t think what there could be
That you’d want while you had all these beautiful
	things—
	Besides, you have papa and me!

So I wished for a ladder; so long that ’twould stand
	One end by our own cottage door,
And the other go up past the moon and the stars,
	And lean against Heaven’s white floor.

Then I’d get you to put on my pretty white dress,
	With my sash and my darling new shoes,
And I’d find some white roses to take up to God—
	The most beautiful ones I should choose.

And you and dear papa would sit on the ground
	And kiss me and tell me 'Good bye;'
Then I'd go up the ladder far out of your sight,
	Till I came to the door in the sky!

I wonder if they keep the door fastened tight?
	If but one little crack I could see,
I would whisper, ‘Please, Father, let Mary come in,
	She’s as tired as she can be!’

And then, when the angels had opened the door,
	He would say, ‘Bring the little child here,’ 
But He’d speak so softly I’d not be afraid;
	And He’d smile just like you, mother dear!

He would put his kind arms round your dear little girl,
	And I’d ask him to send down for you,
And papa, and cousin, and all that I love—
	O dear! don’t you wish ’twould come true?"

The next spring time, when the robins came home,
	They sang over grass and flowers
That grew where the foot of the ladder stood,
	Whose top reached the heavenly bowers.

And the parents had dressed the pale little child
	For the flight to the summer land,
In a fair white robe with one snow-white rose
	Folded tight in her pulseless hand.


﻿EXAMPLES FOR CHILDREN.
			    —
	The chief care of women, especially
those belonging to the Church of
Jesus Christ, should be, that their
children are properly taught. They
should realize, too, that the examples
which they set for them are most likely to
mark their steps through life. Yet how
many of us, often forget this all important
subject, instead of keeping it constantly in
view, as a safeguard against anger, fretful-
ness, jealousy and envy, and many other
evils that might be mentioned.
	A few years ago, I one night dreamed of
being at church and hearing President
Young chide the Sisters concerning their
clothing, and I looked at the sleeve of my
dress; it was white as snow; for which I felt
a deep sense of satisfaction and gratitude.
My eye wandered downward to the bottom
of my skirt, and I saw two rows of black
trimming there, which annoyed and per-
plexed me. How could I get that ugly
trimming off? If I ripped it off the stain
and stitches would look even worse than the
trimming. With the mortification and
anxiety caused by these reflections, I awoke.
“Well,” I said to myself, “I have been
fretting and worrying over nothing; and by
making myself cheerless and disagreeable—
which is the black trimming—I have dis-
quieted my home, and cast a gloom over
the pure minds of my dear, sweet children;
which bad example will leave a hateful stain
that must be carefully erased in the right
way, or it will grow upon my children,
making them even more irritable and un-
loveable than I have been.” We cannot
take too much pains, mothers, in seeking to
live near the Lord and to cherish a peaceful
and loving spirit, that we may impart the
same to our offsprings, both before and after 
their birth.						NORA.


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
				—
	BALTA is a Russian city of 15,000 inhab-
itants, where, until recently, not a single
school was in existence.
	A TERRE Haute, Ind., professional sheep-
shearer says that he has not seen such fine
fleeces in the last fifteen years as this year’s
clip shows.
	THE managers of the New York and Erie
railroad have issued an order forbidding the
sale of spirituous liquors at the restaurants
along the road.
	AN English lady in the city of Birming-
ham has been made to pay a fine of L20 for
giving an undeserved “recommendation”
to a discharged domestic.
	THE model cheese factory of Utica made
last season 211,500 lbs.,. of cheese, which
sold for $21,776.81. The business will be
largely increased another season.
	THE Lincoln “Statesman” says: A care-
ful canvass of the political status of the
Lunatic Asylum taken yesterday gives the
following result: Grant, 39; G. F. Train,
4; Greeley, 1. Strickland is their unanimous
choice for Congress.
	A NEW cave in Schoharie County has re-
cently been explored for a mile. It has
statacutes, statagmites and all the rest of
those fellows. Near the centre is a dome
about a hundred feet high.
	IT is estimated that there are 400 saloons
and groceries in San Francisco, which dis-
pense annually 20,000 barrels of lager, 500,-
000 gallons of wine, and 2,000,000 gallons of
the more stimulating fluids, the total value
of which is $10,000,000.
	THE industrial and commercial growth of
Italy during the past few years has been re-
markable. Genoa alone, with a population
of only 150,000, has now twenty-four great
banking houses, with a capital of 162,900,-
000 francs; twenty corporations, with a capi-
tal of 42,328 francs, and forty-five insurance
companies.
	THE great pyramid weighs 12,760,000,-
000 tons, if anybody wants to know. Ac-
cording to Herodotus, it took the labor of
100,000	men twenty years to build it. To
show the mechanical value of modern im-
provements Dr. Lardner affirms that 480 tons
of coal, with an engine and hoisting ma-
chine, would have raised every stone to its
position.
	THE total wool product of the world for
1871 has been given at 1,121,519,000 pounds.
The largest contributing countries were as
follows: United States, 177,000,000 pounds;
England, 160,000,000 pounds; Australia,
152,000,000 pounds; La Plata, 138,000,000
pounds. These four countries furnish 627,-
000,000 pounds, or over 50 per cent. of the
total product.
	THE entire area of Manhattan Island is
14,500 acres. Taking out 1,800 acres set
aside for public parks, and 1,638 acres left
for streets and boulevards, and we have 11,-
062 acres left for building purposes. Of
these 11,062 acres, 7,253 acres are now occu-
pied by 101,000 houses, which leaves only
3,809 acres to be occupied! These 3,809
acres, at twenty building lots to the acre,
would make 76,180 more building sites on
the island. The last census gives our popu-
lation at 942,992. If we increase during the
next twenty years as fast as we have for the
last twenty years, our population will have
reached 1,600,000, or 100,000 more than
enough to cover every inch of ground from
the Battery to Harlem River.—“New York
paper."


FARMERS’ BOYS.
			    —
	There is a native manliness and indepen- 
dent nobility about farmers’ boys that is 
rarely ever witnessed in those who grow up
in cities. The city boy may be more pol-
ished, and perhaps a little quicker with his
mother tongue, but sound him and you will
find that he has not the depth of mind 
or the manliness of character that are gener-
ally possessed by the hardy country boy. 
The latter is nature’s child, he is strong, 
fearless and independent, his faculties are 
healthy and active, and he readily masters 
the lessons that are spread out so abundantly 
around them. He studies the works and
beauties of nature, and being unhampered by
the rules of conventionality, he seeks the
natural course of things he does not under-
stand, depending upon himself for the. solu-
tion of his most difficult problems; thus he
becomes self-reliant, common-sense and
practical, and when he goes out into the
world he deals with men on those princi-
ples. He understands human nature, too,
as he has learned it from his own disposi- 
tion and his keen observation of the con- 
duct of other people, and, his common-sense 
telling him that human nature is and al-
ways has been the same, he knows, exactly
how to approach men when he wishes to
gain his purpose.	
	The greatest men of every calling and all 
ages have been farmers’ sons, and they will
be until the end of time; and the secret of 
their success is their self-reliance, united 
with industrious, temperate habits. They 
have neither the time nor the means to study
the sciences and fine arts.
	There is still a brighter future for farm- 
ers’ boys. The great improvements that 
have been made in farming implements do 
away with a large portion of the heavy and 
disagreeable labor that formerly could not
be avoided, and enable one man to do as 
much work as two or three could in earlier
periods, so that now farmers have more time
to read and improve their minds and be- 
come familiar with events that are transpir- 
ing in the world. The great increase in books
and periodicals and the low prices at which 
they are offered furnish additional facilities
for obtaining knowledge, and if farmers take
advantage of them as they should, we will 
soon find our most polished gentlemen and 
cultivated scholars among the youth of the 
country.—“St. Joseph Reflector.”


DURING HIS short stay in Paris the Prince 
of Wales took occasion to pay a visit to Mrs. 
Johnson, of Baltimore, formerly Miss Har-
riet Lane, who was staying for some weeks
at the Hotel Westminster in that city. The 
compliment was not only consistent with a
truly royal courtesy, but was merited by the 
recipient, whose claim upon the recognition 
of the representative of the British throne
was well established during the years that
she presided over the White House, at 
Washington, and at the residence of the
American Minister in London. As niece
of the bachelor President, she did the
honors of the White House with a delicate
courtesy and a gentle dignity that gained
the respect as well as the admiration of state
visitors from abroad, equally with that of
the people. It was a true Republican Court
that Miss Lane established, over which she
ruled without pretensious display or foolish
extravagance, but with an elegant grace
that gave an easy supremacy. It was she
who welcomed and entertained the Prince
of Wales on his visit to America, and we
can easily believe that the return call of the
Prince in Paris was prompted as much by
inclination as by royal courtesy.


WOMAN’S’ EXPONENT.


﻿ANECDOTE OF SEWARD.
				   —
	Not long ago a gentleman was telling me
a Senatorial anecdote of, and dating back to,
the ante-bellum days. Mr. Seward had made
a speech—something about the telegraph—
when Mr. Toombs, of Georgia, rose to reply,
and made a speech of personal abuse of
Seward. He wrought himself up into a rage
and lashed about in the most aggressive
manner. He finished and took his seat. As
Mr. Seward rose from his chair every eye
was bent upon him with the greatest anxi-
ety. With calm, measured step, he walked
towards Mr. Toombs. It was noticed that
his right hand was underneath the rear
pocket of his coat. There was apprehension
that he was concealing a pistol, and Mr.
Toombs' friends crowded around him. When
Mr. Seward reached him, he drew out his
hand, and opening his snuff-box, politely in-
vited his adversary to take a pinch of snuff.
"My God!" said Mr. Toombs, "Mr. Seward,
have you no feelings?" "Take a pinch of
snuff; it will soothe your agitation." He
then returned to his seat without any allu-
sion to Mr. Toombs or his speech, made an
able argument in favor of his measure,
which carried. Coolness and self-possession
will always win the victory over hot temper
and passionate invective. —"Washington
letter."

[new column]
		   WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

		A UTAH LADIES' JOURNAL.

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest in mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them ; and 
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.

	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"

	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the public-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.

	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings; 
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.

	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.

	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in in
Utah.

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year,  $2.00	1 Copy six months, $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months, 9.00

	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a 
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


Woman's Exponent.
Vol. I.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, AUGUST 15, 1872.	No. 6.﻿


NEWS AND VIEWS.

 THE usual deluge of summer “poetry” is
being poured out..
  EX-EMPRESS EUGENIE finds warm sym-
pathy in cold Scotland.
  A FAILURE.—PlautamouFs comet to put
in an appearance on the 12th, insf.
  PRESIDENT GRANT and would-be President
Greeley are “swinging around the circle,”
and their admirers are feting" them.
  POLITICAL parties charge political .corrup-
tion on each other, and the facts reveaL ah
alarming amount of truth in the charges,
 MR. JEFFERSON, the actor, is.recovering
his eyesight, and Rip Van Winkle,.as.per-
sonated by him, may again delight thou-
sands,
  SUSAN B. ANTHONY has announced-her-
self strongly in favor of President Grant’s
re-election. Miss Anthony has done wiser
things.
  THE Presidential campaign has given
Utah, a breathing spell from her persecutors.
Every blessing should be thankfully ac-
knowledged.
  POLITICIANS changing parties and tiie
people pressing on to the accomplishment of
a settled political purpose, arc among the
signs of the times.
  A RECENT magazine says: “ Fifteen dol-
lar will buy a lovely homestead of eighty.
acres in Florida.” But is it worth the money?
And how much of a fortune does it take to
make the homestead profitable.
  GARIBALDI, it is said, has quit soldiering
I and turned novelist, and is preparing for the
press a novel the scenes of which nrc'laid in
France during the late war. “ Take away
the sword, states may be saved without it.”
  LADY Wentworth, of England, has left
the roof of her husband, and Lord Went-
worth advertises her as if they were “vulgar
plebians.” The leveling tendencies of the
age have struck oven the haughty English
aristocracy.
  THE rejection of the side-saddle for ladies’
riding is warmly agitated just now, without
making much headway, A revolution in
dress will precede the change, and then
sensible women will cry halt, and consider
the matter.
  "LET us have peace" was the cry on
which General Grant was elected to the
Presidency in 1868; and still the Republic
cries and yearns for good peace and has not
yet found it. Election watchwords are as
hollow as worthless reeds.
  BALDNESS, it is said, is becoming fearfully
prevalent among the ladies as a result of 
wearing chignons. Where nature's orna-
ment of hair is cultivated in place of mon-
strosities of fashion, this deformity is
avoided. But fashion makes fools of mil-
lions.
  RUSSIA stands no newspaper nonsense. A
report was circulated in the United States
that the Grand Duke Alexis was secretly
married; and two Russian editors have been
fined and imprisoned for alluding to the mat-
ter. It is a reflection on the profession, but
sad to say a truth, to add that if all the edi-
tors in this country that publish false reports

were imprisoned, the penitentiaries would
have to be enlarged.
   BOSTON shipped 489,500 gallons of Med-
ford rum to heathen Africa in the last six
months. With a sufficient stock of mission-
ary tracts and red flannel jackets Africa
should be in a fair way to be "Christianized"
and "civilized." Bnt that Boston thus ex-
porst the gospel of rum to the heathen is a 
stain on the "Hub" which it should cover
with the mantle of charity for the short-
comings of other place less pretentions in
regard to piety, literature, and the higher
virtues.
  FIVE young ladies of New York announced
through a leading newspaper the other day
that they would ride in a public part of the
city, on horseback, in the style men use—
without side-saddles.  Whether the exploit
was an illustration of moral courage, or a
speciman of "fast" life, the public can deter-
mine ; but the act of itself shows that the
modesty which our great-grandfathers ad-
mired in our great-grandmothers is not so
popular as it might be to the benefit of well-
ordered society.
  HON. John A. Bingham, who was so anx-
ious last session of Congress to have some
harsh, prescriptive, anti-Mormon bill passed,
has been kindly permitted by his constitu-
ents to retire after the 4th of March next.
They declined to nominate him, and he can
now pass to the living tomb of politicans
with Cullom, Colfax and others, who sought
to make political capital by pressing a cru-
sade against the people of Utah, and became
bankrupt by the operation.
  AN English astronomer once said that a
comet striking an ex-
press train going forty miles an hour would
have on the train. M. Plantamour, the Swiss
astronomer, evidently did not think so when
he predicted fearful results to this planet by
its coming into collision with a comet on the 
12th instant. If the comet encountered the
earth at the date specified, the blow must
have been a light one, as nobody appears to
have felt it.
  DID Stanley, the New York "Herald"
correspondent, find Dr. Livingstone, or did
 he not? Did he write the letters attributed
to him, or were they written in the "Herald"
office in New York? Is there anything
genuine about Stanley's account of finding
Livingstone, or is the whole, a "bohemian's"
fabrication, bred in a vivid imagination and
designed to secure cheap notoriety for
wonderful journalistic enterprise? These
are questions of the hour. And the news-
paper that can so thrill the pulse of the
civilized world with a fabrication—if it be
one—has won a triumph of notoriety that
respectable journals will not envy.
  THE Emperors of Germany, Russia, and
Austria, are soon to meet in Berlin, and the 
ex-Emperor Napolean is going to drink the
waters of Carlsbad. Emperors do not con-
gregate in this manner to exchange courtes-
ies. There is danger to the peace of Europe
in their gathering together, and millions of
homes may be filled with mourning because
of the meeting of Willhelm, Alexander and
Francis Joseph. Such are the fruits of
imperial ambition; and such will be the

fruits of a lust for power while men are the
slaves of passions which prompt them to
work woe in the world that they may be
great and raised far above their fellows.
  "THE Sleeping Beauty of Tennessee" is
the title given to a woman in that State who
presents one of those physical phenomena
which baffle science and apparently set all
ordinary laws of nature at defiance. She
is described as white as marble, thirty-
one years ago, and having slept for
twenty-three years, awaking thirteen times
every twenty-four hours and remaining
awake exactly seven minutes each time,
during which she eats, drinks and talks. It
is said no earthly power can make her keep
awake a second longer than seven minutes
at a time; and that she breathes only at in-
tervals of five minutes. The circumstances
connected with the peculiar case are related
at length in the Louisville "Courier Jour-
nal" and bear marks of genuineness. Where
is the pundit or savant that can give a satis-
factory explanation of the phenomenon?
  IT is reliably stated that "trichina spira-
lis" has been found in a ham imported to
California from Chicago. The action of these
parasites upon the human system is fatal,
and death is preceeded by tortures of the most
horrible character. They breed in the in-
testines, in the flesh, and through the entire
body of their victim, making it a nursery for
death-dealing insects. In seeing how widely
hog's flesh is used, and thinking on the un-
clean nature of the animal and the serious
consequences that often follow eating it, we 
sometimes think of Dr. Adam Clark's
"grace," when asked to "return thanks"
over a meal where a young roast pig was on
the table. He had an aversion to swine,
but a politeness prevented his declining to
ask a blessing, and in the course of it he
said: "Lord, if Thou canst bless under the
Christian dispensation, what Thou didst
curse under the Jewish dispensation, bless
that pig!" Avoid the use of hog's flesh as
much as possible; but when it must be used
endeavor to learn how the animal was fed,
and be sure to have the flesh thoroughly
cooked. 
	THE first gun of the Presidential cam-
paign was fired at North Carolina, and no-
body seem to be aware whether anyone was
hurt by the discharge. First it was alleged
the State had gone Republican; then Demo-
cratic; then again 'Republican; and both
parties have rejoiced hugely over it in turn.
Seemingly, the Republicans, by a lavish ex-
penditure of money and the use of all the
means at the command of the administra-
tion have elected their candidates for the
Governorship and the several State offices;
while the Democrats have elected a majority
of members of Congress and of the State
Legislature. This virtually makes it a victory
for neither side, so that each can count honors
and rejoice in having won something. The
next elections of interest are those of Cali-
fornia and Maine, the former on September
2, the latter on September 9; with Vermont
coming off on September 3, but as it is cer-
tain to go for Grant less interest attaches to
it. In a month from the present date
politicians will begin to figure with a greater
degree of certainty on the chances of Grant
and Greeley in the November struggle.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿ASKING A BLESSING.
			—
"Ay; but wait, good wife, a minute;
	I have first a word to say:
Do you know what day to-day is?
	Mother, ’tis our wedding-day!

"Just as now, we sat at supper
	When the guests had gone away;
You sat that side, I sat this side,
	Forty years ago to-day!.

"Then what plans we laid together:
	What brave things I meant to do!
Could we dream to-day would find us
	At this table—me and you? 

"Better so, no doubt—and yet I
	Sometimes think—I cannot tell—
Had our boys—ah, yes! I know dear;
	Yes, He doeth all things well.

"Well, we’ve had our joys and sorrows;
	Shared our smiles as well as tears;
And—the best of all—I've had your
	Faithful love for forty years!

"Poor we’ve been, but not forsaken;
	Grief we’ve known but never shame—
Father, for Thy endless mercies
	Still	we bless Thy Holy Name!"


﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
			  —
	SMITHFIELD, Cache County, July 31, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	I congratulate you on the success of your
paper. I am very much pleased with it,
and am always anxious to see each coming
number. I wish you prosperity in every
good work.					J.S.C.
	We herewith send you a financial report
of our Society, since its organization. We
held our annual meeting on the 13th of
May; had a good attendance, and all felt
well. Our Society is in a prosperous condi-
tion. Since our organization we have re-
ceived in donations and increase of value on
property $858.50; which we have disposed
of as follows: Given to the poor, $319,06;
paid for the educating of dependent children,
$183.15; to assist in gathering the poor,
$77.00; for public expenses, $50.00, and
have a balance now in the Treasury of$229.-
29. A share in the railroad, and shares in
the Co-operative store are included in our
property on hand.
		MRS. E. T. MOREHEAD, President,
		MRS. JANE S. COLEMAN, Secretary,
		MRS. MARGARET STORY; Treasurer.
				   ——
	The following report is from the 17th ward,
Salt Lake City:
	The semi-annual meeting of the fifth year
of the F. R. Society of the 17th ward, was
held in the Union Hall, Thursday, August 6.
	The semi-annual report, which was read
by the Secretary, showed the society to be
in good order. The sum of $158.44 had been
disbursed to the needy during the past six
months.
	We have a large class of Scandinavian sis-
ters who devote some hours a week to the
study of the English language, and are
taught by Sister Julia Howe, who realized
the amount of good that would accrue to
them through a knowledge of the English
language.
	During the meeting the sum of $50.00 was
donated to the Perpetual Emigration Fund.
President Mrs. M. A. Hyde suggested that
a synopsis of the proceedings of the meeting
be sent to the EXPONENT which, being put
to vote, passed unanimously.
	A good feeling prevailed and all felt to do
all they could to aid the poor and suffering.
	Since the organization of the Society, four
years and a half ago, we have raised in dona-
tions, $2,667.42 1/2; disbursed t othe poor,
$1,797.15; and have at present in the treas-
ury, in cash, upwards of $300.00.
			MRS. G. A. ALDER, Secretary.
-


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
				—
	INSTABILITY AND a love of change,
among domestics is an evil in our country,
which might be remedied in a great
measure, if employers, especially the mis-
tresses of families, would take more pains
to make their residences agreeable and to
attach domestics to their families by feelings
of gratitude and affection. It is not merely
by giving them good food, comfortable
rooms, presents and privileges that the at-
tachments of domestics are secured. These
things are necessary, but not all that is re-
quired. A kindly interest should be mani-
fested for the hapiness and welfare of the
domestic. Make her feel that you are really
her friend, and can afford, occasionally, to
make little sacrifices to accommodate her
feelings, which may be as sensitive and as
susceptible of enjoyment as your own.
	IT DOES not speak well for womankind,
that washer-women, and those who go out
to day’s work, prefer to work for gentlemen.
Yet they positively aver that gentlemen
find less fault with their work and are much
more liberal in their prices for work than
ladies. The Golden Rule should be kept
in view—ladies should once in a while wash
and get up their own collars and ruffles that
they may be able to consider the real esti-
mate of the labor, and not underrate it when
settling with the poor creatures, who are
thus obliged to work to gain a livelihood.
	PUDDING.—Take a pound of stoned raisins,
six eggs, a wine-glass of rum or brandy,
some beef marrow, a pound of corn flower
or maizena, some sugar, two tumblers of
milk, citron or lemon peel, and nutmeg to
taste; mix perfectly; and add in bread-
crumbs until you get sufficient consistence. 
Tie tightly in a cloth and boil four hours.
	POTATO SOUP.—Boil two or three pounds
of potatoes, mash them well, add slowly
good broth, sufficent for your tureen; let
this well boil, and then add some spinach,
sorrel, a little parsley, lemon, thyme, mint
and sage, all chopped fine; boil all five min-
utes; pepper and salt to taste; just before
taking off the fire, add two well-beaten eggs.
	RENDERING FABRICS INCOMBUSTIBLE.—
Dissolve borax in hot water, soak the cloth-
ing in the liquid, and afterward let it dry.
If will not be impossible to inflame it, al-
though it will burn away with a slow com-
bustion. Alum has been recommended for
the same purpose, but it is injurious to the
clothing; the carbonate of potash may also


be used, but it is apt to contract moisture
from the air, and thus render the clothes
damp.
	STEWED CALF'S FEET.—Divide the feet
at the joints, and split the claws, put them
into a stewpan, and pour in cold water to
cover them completely. If a thick slice of
the lean of an unboiled ham or knuckle of
bacon be laid at the bottom of the pan it
will greatly improve the whole preparation.
When the boiling takes place, throw in a
little salt, and after the scum has been re-
moved, add some parsley, celery, onion,
cloves, mace, and pepper. Stew softly until
the flesh parts entirely from the bones, take
it from them, strain part of the gravy, skim
off the fat, flavor with catsup, and thicken,
when it boils, with flour and butter; put in
the flesh of the feet, and serve immediately.


MISCELLANEOUS.
			   —
	CHEERFULNESS.—What a world of sun-
shine, beauty and sweetness is concentrated
in this one attribute. Innocence and chas-
tity are its constant companions, for it can-
not in its refined state, exist where there is
impurity, or vulgarity. When it brightens
the countenance and wreathes the lips with
smiles, even a plain, commonplace face ap-
pears to good advantage; while without its
warm and genial expressions, the most fault-
less features are unlovely. Whether mani-
fested in looks, words or actions, it is the
outpourings of a frank and generous heart;
and is actuated by virtuous and unselfish
principles. No intelligence, however su-
perior, can make a person an agreeable com-
panion without the maintenance of some de-
gree of pleasantry. But with a reasonable
share of it, some people render themselves
fascinating, and their company very desira-
ble though their intellectual developments
may be meagre and their education limited.
Rude and boisterous gaiety, that which is
calculated to excite, weary and create after
dissatisfaction, is no part of genuine cheer-
fulness. But happy tears as well as quiet
laughter may be the overflowings of inno-
cent joyousness.
	THE GREAT BELL IN RUSSIA.—The
largest bell is in Russia, in the city of Mos-
cow. The Russians call it “the king of
bells.” It is nearly twenty feet high, sixty
feet around at the bottom, two feet thick,
and weighs four hundred thousand pounds.
Just think of a round room the shape of a
bell, twenty feet across, with walls two feet
thick, and you can have some idea of how
big the bell is. Would you like to ring it?
It takes twelve men to ring it. They tug
and pull, and it booms and tolls so as to al-
most deafen one. Its tongue or clapper is
fourteen feet long. When they were making
this big bell, some of the nobles and rich
people were so proud of it, that they put
gold and silver rings in with the metal.
	A VIGOROUS old agriculturist, living near
Abilene, Kansas, while hoeing corn the other
day, chopped his toe off with his hoe, but
never stopped to pick it up until he had
finished the row. He then went back and
found the amputated appendage, brushed
the dirt off and carried it to the house,
where with the help of his wife and some
fruit jar wax, he replaced it and then went
to work again. He is a self-made man, be-
longs to a Greeley Club, is a deacon in a
church, never imbibes in the the ardent,
neither swears nor chews gum.
	A GLANCE, a twinkle, or an earnest look
often expresses volumes when there is no
time for words. Eyes are not only given for
sight but for the expression of ideas.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿WOMAN’S VOICE.
				      —
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Somehow, it seems to me that if all those
“strong minded” women would but allow
themselves to consider, they could hardly
help seeing that, at present, there cannot
be devised any better system for the con-
traction of the social evil, than the Mormon
principle of plurality of wives.
	They believe the majority of women to be
superior to the majority of men. How then
can they hope to procure a suitable husband
for every good and pure woman, save by
letting men who are worthy take more than
one worthy woman each, or many of them,
since there are a great many more women
than men in different parts of the world?
	They believe in equal rights for all.
What equality is there to be found in a
society where only one woman out of a
great number is to be favored with a moral,
temperate husband? While the rest must
remain in “single blessedness,” and die
without having fulfilled their mission to the
earth. Or else, marry poor, worthless,
drunken vagabonds who will desert them
and perhaps their helpless children, when-
ever they tire of them.
	How is it that they cannot see that it
would be better to have a husband that can
be trusted, and a father for the children,
that they need not be ashamed to own?
With all my weakness and selfish feelings,
I can see that women in the world have
much more cause for anxiety and trouble
than we have in Utah.
	Our F. R. Society is not doing much now.
We haven’t had a meeting for ever so long.
I feel sorry to have things so neglected, for
I think we should be awake and at work
for the general good of mankind, ourselves
included; but it is not my place to make any
move unless so directed. So I wait patiently,
saying good words and encouraging where
ever I can, and feeling assured that all will
be well.							F. S.
	Dear sisters, who have so nobly stepped
forward to stem the tide of arrogance, ignor-
ance, and assumption; seeing in your paper
an invitation to come and do likewise I offer
a few of my scattered ideas for your columns.
My feelings have been aroused, or, in other
words, I have “been set to thinking” by
some remarks I saw in a late paper relative
to “Mormon women awakening up to a full
realization of our grave situation.” Have
we ever been asleep? I trust not. There is
no thinking woman among us but what will
say our situation is grave; has ever been
grave. My mind wanders back in retrospect
to the days when I was “one of Christ’s
little ones, too” and I could give many
things from memory to show that our situa-
tion was grave. The days of anxiety and
nights of fear in Nauvoo; the days of sick-
ness and destitution in the scorching sun,
as we traveled through Illinois, Iowa or
Missouri. When at Council Bluffs the flower
of our little band was taken from us to fight
on the plains of Mexico for the land we now
inhabit—fathers, husbands, and brothers;
and their bones were strewn by the wayside
a sacrifice of loyalty to their country; leav-
ing only the old and infirm, and boys too
young to stand the fatigues of camp life, to
support and protect the women and children.
Our situation then was truly grave. Beneath
burning suns on the sandy plains of the
“great desert,” and amid the chilling cold
and eternal snows of the Rocky Mountains,
it was still grave. We may have tried to
slumber and bury our wrongs in forgetful-
ness, but it was like sleeping in a nest of
mosquitoes; there were too many bills pre-
[new column]
sented (not excepting Cullom’s) to permit us
a peaceful repose.
	But why renew this subject, or repeat
these things? Many are tired of hearing
them, because we are not afraid to open our
mouths and testify to the wrongs we suffered.
How have our affections and hopes been
blighted? I will tell you. When amid all
this privation and sorrow we have reared
our little daughters, according to the best of
our circumstances and ability, they have
been snatched from us by the hands of liber-
tines and villains. When the onward course
of “civilization” has washed its waves on
our rock-bound shores and brought for our
sons the billiard table, the gambling saloons
and the dens of infamy; when the people
would make laws to take from us our hus-
bands and protectors, leaving us a prey to
officers and politicians, the base and- un-
principled, to fill houses of wretchedness
and ill-fame; our children illegitamized, our
names a by-word, then, sisters of Zion, our
hearts may bleed, for that is the system
that strikes at the root of womanhood and
blights our affections and hopes. See our
sons, bright and noble, not disgracing
the image of their Creator! See our daughters,
fair and gentle, types of noble womanhood,
fit to become the mothers of a noble race,
exalted in health, in dignity, in purity and
virtue!  We have obeyed the command-
ments of God and the dictates of our own
conciences, and this is our reward. We are
tender mothers and honored wives. Have
we fainted or faltered by the wayside?
Perhaps we have. How many of our
Christian sisters, fearing pain or dreading
the cares of maternity, have done the same
and committed their names in cowardice
and shame to the grave of oblivion? But to
those who have nobly contended against the
tide of ignorance, error and public opinion—
who have fought the good fight and won
the victory, how great must be their satis-
faction, as they receive the blessings of God
and the approbation of their own hearts!
	PROVO. 						M. J. T.


﻿BROTHERS.
				—
	Girls, are not your brothers generous and
loving to you? It is, unqestionably, very
much your own fault if they are not.
	If brothers are not what they should be,
the influence of good-natured, sweet-tem-
pered, pure-minded sisters has much power
as an incitement to reflection and reform.
Few men are naturally so very vile that
the gentle, persuasive voice and affection-
ate ways of a true woman, have no weight
with them. Yet, sometimes, brothers are
mean, cross, and altogether worthless; and
it is not in the reach of mothers or sisters to
reclaim them. And even such ones some-
times get wives with tender hearts to break.
	I have frequently heard elderly ladies say
to young ones, “Never marry a man who is
not kind, and thoughtful of the welfare and
comfort of his mother or sister.” This is
excellent advice, to which sensible girls
mostly give head. Yet, even such men
are sometimes won over to a beautiful con-
sistency by a prudent, cheerful wife whose
affectionate words and ways are the only
disapprovals he gets for his cross ones. A
man should be as careful in selecting a
wife, if he would enjoy life under the influ-
ence of hymen’s power, not to get one who
is ungrateful, and regardless of her father or
brother’s happiness.
If a man has been brought up with a
mother and sisters who were unreasonable
and extravagant in their demands for atten-
tion, he will naturally have acquired the


habit of making refusals, and look to be
found fault with for doing so; and anything
else will seem out of place to him. It is as
unfair for sisters and wives to want to be
petted and indulged all the time to keep
them in good humor, as it is for brothers
and husbands to be ungracious and arbitrary
and never deign to gratify the wishes of
those who depend so much upon them.
		“There’s no belief in man,
		Not even in a brother;
		So girls, it you must love.
		Love one another.’'
	These words are said to have been found
in a lady’s scrap-book at a hotel. I don’t
like them—they are too deprecatory of one
of the ablest and most devoted supports we
ever find—a brother.
	What should we do without broth-
ers? Those who will confide in us to the
very last; and even ask our advice in
matters that other people might think us
too frivolous and childish to have ever
thought of.
	There is belief in man; and until some
one is found who will take still greater re-
sponsibilities upon himself for your sake,
young lady, cling closely to your brother
and be thankful for him. And if you know
he is not the sort to make a good, noble
husband for some chaste creature, either coy
or dignified, (for all good men cannot be
suitable companions to all good women,
there is so great a variety of tastes and
sentiments,) help him to become so, for
that is what God designed him to be.
								FRANK.


﻿TRUTH WITH CHILDREN.
				—
	“Suffer little children to come unto me,
and forbid them not; for of such is the King-
dom of Heaven.”
	These beautiful words of our beloved Re-
deemer, are worthy the contemplation of
every intelligent mind. After living twenty
or thirty years upon the earth, how far have
we receded from the innocence of infancy,
and how carefully and faithfully must we
watch ourselves, to recall its charitable, un-
suspicious and confiding trust!
	How thoughtless are parents ever to take
a course to change and embitter that pure,
heavenly trust in their children! Yet it is
often done, and in various ways. For in-
stance: A mother promises to dress her little
girl’s doll, presently. How often hour after
hour slips away, and still the promise is not
fulfilled. Still the child’s perfect trust in her
mother’s words, keeps her hopeful; and the
little task is postponed, laid aside for some-
thing of more importance, until the entire
day passes, or perhaps two or three days;
and although the promise may be often re-
peated, it is still unfulfilled.
	From such unwise examples children learn,
not only to be distrustful of others, but also
to make promises themselves that they do i
not even intend to perform. In many cases,
older people make a practice of telling chil-
dren what they may consider “white lies,”
to persuade or frighten them into being obo-
dient. But every falsehood that is calcula-
ted to implant within the bosom of a child
seeds of deception and distrust, is a black
one and should be studiously avoided. It is
better to enforce discipline among children
by the firm exercise of parental authority
than to inculcate the poison of deceit into
their young, guileless minds. But to control
them by the gentle powers of firm, cheerful,
yet earnest and truthful persuasion, is better
still.									LUNIE.


WE should guard against small vices as  
well as great errors.
