﻿SPLINTERS.	
				—
	One of the chief objects of attention and
sensational conversation in London, now, is
Gladstone and his great triumph........Miss
Amy Sedgwick, the popular London actress,
will visit this country next season......A
new comedy, in five acts, from George Sand,
is among the recent additions to stage litera-
ture....Elinski, a Polish exile, is lecturing
in Oregon and gives a history of the late
revolution in his own land.......A turkey in
Scipio, New York, has laid one hundred
eggs in one hundred days, never missing a
day......Ochre is used in making bricks and
pottery ware in Kansas.....Senator Brown-
low says if Greeley and Brown are elected
we are placed back where we were in 1861,
when the traitors fired upon Fort Sumpter.
......Boston gave a grand banquet to the
Japanese Embassy, August 2nd......At To-
peka, on the 27th of July, Mrs. Thomas
Mattingley drowned herself and a three
year old child, on account of domestic un-
happiness......The wedding presents of Miss
Nilsson, married in London to M. Boussand
amount, in value, to $60,000....Since the
death of President Juarez, the French think
of renewing diplomatic relations with
Mexico......General Sherman attended th
funeral of the son of the Duke of Aumale at
Paris......Senator Schurz charges Grant with
using Federal arms to influence the States
and overawe them......Heavy mining strikes
are reported in Pioche Dictrict.....About
1,000 acres of cotton was planted in Califor-
nia this season......Disraeli’s wife, Lady Bea-
consfield is ill beyond hope of recovery.....
In a competitive examination at New York,
the nomination for a cadetship at the Na-
val Academy was won by a ragged news-boy.
......A Missouri paper makes calculations
that Grant will receive a majority of
10,000 in that State......The value of the
gold now extant is estimated at upwards of
$5,000,000,000.....All record of the late de-
vastation in Paris is reported to be disap-
pearing......The “American Settler” is the
name of a paper lately started in London....
Among the peasantry in France, it is said,
not one in a hundred can read or write......
A California inventor has produced a rail-
road tie of vegetable fibre, held in asphal-
tum......The contract for building a seminary
to cost one thousand eight hundred and
forty dollars, has been let at Napa, Califor-
nia......Thiers will send one hundred thou-
sand men to suppress the mining riots on
the northern frontier of France,if necessary.
......George E. Pugh, an eminent Ohio Cop-
perhead, tenders his services as a stumper for
Greeley and Brown......One hundred thou-
sand dollars of diamonds are said to have
been lately received in San Francisco, from
New Mexico, discovered years ago by Kit
Carson......A tailor in California, named
Ledig, shot his wife twice the other day.
The wounds are supposed to be mortal; he
then shot himself and expired......A San
Francisco youth, named Harley, has been
committed to trial for burning the faces of
small boys with nitrate of silver......Ex-
tremely hot weather is reported, all through
the 'Western States...... Rumor says the
“colored folks” are nearly all for Greeley....
In company with his family, Greeley will
visit his old home in New Hampshire and
probably remain a month or more.....
A sarcastic paper says the West Point
cadets are pleading to let their moustaches
grow......Independence Hall, in Philadel-
phia, is being restored as nearly as possible
to its condition in 1776......Two men in St.
Charles, Michigan, have become maniacs by
exposure to a hot sun while buggy-riding.
[new column]
......The “diamond fields” of Arizona are the
latest attraction to excited hunters after pre-
cious stones......A Kansas woman has been
paralyzed by the sting of a wasp.......Du-
buque has a religious society for children.
called “The Seeds of Mercy”......A female
horse-thief in male attire has been caught In
Michigan......Bret Hart is said to be engaged
to write one article a month for the “Atlan-
tic” at $10,000 a year......The youngest bride
of the season, at Niagara, is a young lady of
Stonington, Connecticut, aged fourteen. She
left her skipping-rope at home......Nicholas
C. Sennat, a once prosperous merchant who
was ruined by the Chicago fire, committed
suicide in St. Louis recently because of his
reverses......A farmer named Williams was
thrown in front of a mowing machine a few
days ago in Suffolk county, New York, and
was cut to pieces...."Who carried North
Carolina in the late election is still an open
question......A Fon-du-Lac, Wis., woman
has sent a bill of $200 to the saloon keepers
of the town, being the amount legally due
her for a beating inflicted by a drunken
husband......General Banks, of Massachu-
setts, and General Farnsworth, of Illinois,
are among the latest prominent Republicans
that have come out for Greeley.


IGNORANCE AND BIGOTRY.
				——
	The San Francisco “Pioneer,” edited by Mrs.
Emily Pitts Stevens, is devoting some space to
Utah affairs and developing an inordinate
quantity of meaningless sentimentality in
treating of them. The last effort in this direc-
tion, in the issue of the 8th inst., starts out by
asserting that “tyranny and fanatacism go hand
in hand in Utah” which might justly be sup-
plemented by the assertion that ignorance and
bigotry go hand In hand everywhere, and nota-
bly so in the “Pioneer” office. The article in-
dulges in such sweet and loving phrases as
"simple devotee," "unscrupulous tyrant," "the
doctrine of polygamy savoring of every foul at-
tribute of slavery," "this canker upon the body
of Christendom," "humiliations which these
Mormon women bear," "spreading leprosy,"
and so on. The article is one of those tirades
in which strong adjectives are compelled to do
duty for sound reasons. Narrowed down to its
actual value, it is simply half a column of un-
reasoning abuse, for there is not an argument,
nor the shadow of one, in it from the opening
word to the closing sentence. It assumes that
"polygamy" is "a canker upon the body of
Christendom," and like scores of such efforts
rests satisfied with the assumption. The fetid,
festering, gangrene spots upon "the body of
Christendom" are sufficiently numerous to call
for the surgical aid of these would-be doctors of
morality without troubling themselves serious-
ly relative to the marital relations of Utah; but
evidently the "Pioneer" and others of the
same class, are more anxious to hold up their
hands in affected horror against an imaginary
evil and point to an assumedly sickly condition
of society at a distance, than to look upon the
[new column]
pestilence of corruption which surrounds them
at home, striking down the fairest and the
loveliest, victims of man's lust and woman's fol-
ly. The "Pioneer" is terribly exercised because of
the "humiliations these Mormon women bear,
mingled with an inward sense of shame and
outrage;" but as the "humiliations" only exist
in the imagination of the "Pioneer" and others
of like stripe, she can spare her sympathy until
it is solicited. And when she uses the language
"mingled with an inward sense of shame and 
outrage," she is impertinent, and would be in-
sulting if her bigotry were not the result of
ignorance, which few sounding sentences can-
not hide.
	We have closely watched the practical work-
ings of plural marriage for years, having been
brought into daily contact with it; and have
formed very decided opinions on the subject.
And when some of these people who are so fond
of indulging in abuse concerning "the cruelty
of Brigham Young," as this writer phrases it,
and the "downtrodden women of Utah," as
they are pleased to call us, will advance some
solid reasons based on scripture, morality,
physiology, or social science against plural 
marriage, we shall be pleased to weigh them
and give them due consideration. But neither
impertinence, abuse, nor slander will commend
them to our judgment. And when the editor
of the "Pioneer" can point us to as pure, as
happy, and as temperate a community in all
things, where she resides, as can be found in
Utah; to men in whom women can so confide
without having their confidence abused, and to
women as chaste, take them as a whole; then
we may possibly begin to have some faith in
the excellence of theories which the experience
of centuries has proven hollow and worthless.
And, further, when the editor of the "Pioneer"
can point to such a condition of society as a re-
sult of her efforts aiding those "honest preach-
ers" of whom she speaks, we will be prepared
to accord her and them more credit than that of
which the present condition of society indicates
they are worthy.


A RIGHT RECOGNIZED.
				—
Before the women of Utah enjoyed the elec-	
tive franchise those who clamored loudest for 
its being extended to them were parties who
had political "axes to grind," and who were
filled with the bitterest feelings of animosity
against the people of Utah. They foolishly
imagined that if our sex in this Territory had the	
legal right to vote, they could lead us by the arts
which politicians are accustomed to use, to turn	
against men whom we had tested through long
years of trouble and sorrow, and follow their 
lead. Upon no other hypothesis can their urg-
ent demands on Congress to bestow the right of 
suffrage upon us be explained. For when the 
Territorial legislature did enact such a measure,
and it became law by the signature of acting- 
governor Mann and the approval of Congress; 
and when they found that we could think, and
that thinking we intelligently supported the 
candidates of our choice, they were even more
clamorous that Congress should pass a law de-
claring illegal that which they had beforetime
sought to have legalized. They contended that
the exercise of the franchise byus would not stand
the test of the courts, and endeavored to have our
votes declared a nullity. But the decision of the
Supreme Court of the United States in the
Engelbrecht case declared, in plainness, what
power was vested ih a Territorial Legislature.
Then, finding that the highest tribunal of the
country had declared that the acts of the Legis-
lature, when not disapproved by Congress, were
of binding force; and as the elective franchise


was so conferred upon the women of Utah and
was consequently valid, their next move was to
practically recognize that which they had so
violently denied and disputed.  At the general
election, on the 5th inst., the candidate put for-
ward by these same individuals was voted for
by the ladies who would not support the Peo-
ple’s ticket; and thus quietly but pointedly they
acknowledged a right they could no longer gain-
say. But what shall be thought of men who,
knowingly, refuse to recognize every right to
others which they claim to exercise themselves,
and yet talk of their respect for republican gov-
ernment and the glorious principles enunciated
In the Declaration of Independence! Their
political honesty of to-day is on a par with
the “philanthropy” of a few years ago that
urged an extension of the right of suffrage to
the women of Utah; and both are arrant hypoc-
risy!


﻿	WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


HOME AFFAIRS
			    —
	THE ELECTION has come and gone, and the
majority of votes polled for the People's Ticket
shows that the people were alive to the import-
ance of the occasion.
	THE THEATRE in this city has been re-opened
and is now lighted with gas, which works a
wonderful change in enhancing the appearance
of the interior, and rendering the performances
more pleasant and agreeable.
	INDIAN DIFFICULTIES in the Sanpete region
are threatened; and yet the Indians say they
only want a little food and some blankets. Has
Government suddenly grown poor? Or does it
tie the hands of the Indian agent so that he is un-
able to do anything for these "red wards" of the
"great father?"
	AN ABUNDANT harvest has blessed the agri-
culturists of Utah this year, as a general thing;
and though grasshoppers have caused destruc-
tion in some parts of Summit and Morgan
counties, there is much cause for thankfulness
that the scourge has been limited when com-
pared with the past few years.
	MATTERS ARE progressing for the erection of
water works in this city, and the work of sur-
veying for laying the piping has commenced.
The water will be carried from City creek to the
principal parts of the city, in sufficient volume
to meet any demand for extinguishing fires,
and for household uses, being purified and
filtered in its passage from the creek for the
latter purpose. It is an important improve-
ment.
	PREPARATIONS ARE being made for a Terri-
torial Fair, to be held in this city on the 3rd,
4th, and 5th of October next. We hope the
ladies will do as they have done at previous
Fairs; let it be seen how much their handiwork
aids in home development, It will be an excel-
lent opportunity for the Relief Societies to show
speciments of work which willing hands, in-
spired by warm hearts, do in the cause of benev-
olence.
	A FEARFUL crime is reported from Weber 
county. Two little boys seized a young girl
who was out hunting a cow on horseback, drag-
ged her from the horse, violated her person after
she had violently struggled against them, and
then pushed her into a slough where she was
drowned, because she said she said would tell
her mother. The heart sickens while thinking of
the deed, and every feeling of humanity and
justice asks, what punishment should the crim-
inals receive. They are in prison.
	AN IMPRESSION prevails in some places that
subscriptions for the EXPONENT can only com-
mence with the beginning of a volume. This
[2nd column]
﻿is a mistake which we wish to correct. There
are no continued articles in the EXPONENT, and
subscriptions can begin at any time. Those
who subscribe will receive the paper for the
term for which they pay, whether six months
or a year, commencing with the number which
they first receive. The only continued article
yet published was the history of the first organ-
ization of the Female Relief society in Nauvoo,
by Sister Eliza R. Snow, which appeared in
the first and second numbers; and this, as we
we have before stated, it is the intention to re-
publish at an early day, that those who did not
receive the numbers in which it appeared may
be enabled to obtain it. The rapid increase in
circulation of the EXPONENT is most gratify-
ing, and if the friends of the little journal will
continue to assist us as they have done, we
hope by the close of the present volume to make
such improvements in it as will render it in
every way more efficient in accomplishing the
objects for which it was started. While thank-
ing all who have aided in extending its circula-
tion for their efforts in its behalf, we would still
request a continuance of those efforts, until the
EXPONENT is found in every family in Utah.


﻿FROM ST GEORGE.
			—
	ST. GEORGE, July 25th, 1872.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I, for one appreciate and admire the EX-
PONENT, and with pleasure note your energy,
progress and success, in this enterprise—
the voice of the “downtrodden;”' I being
one of them, speak whereof I know.
	St. George though not a great, populous,
nor yet an extensively commercial city, is
one of the handsomest and most quiet and
pleasant of all the cities of Utah. Our build-
ings, enclosures and other improvements,
generally and specially, show taste in de-
sign, and skill in workmanship, and may we
not generally reckon the intellectual status
of a place by its taste and industry? I think
so, and so I like St. George. Yesterday was
celebrated here by all citizens and many
visitors with much energy and zeal, our
sex having taken a prominent part. I send
you a copy of the programme which was
fully carried out, and I herewith enclose
copy of address, read by Miss Woodbury on
the occasion, composed by Miss Cottam and
Miss Woodbury. Its reading, elicited rounds
of applause, and congratulations for the au-
thors. The coronation ceremonies were
quite formal and imposing and one of the
finest features of the celebration.
				—
			ADDRESS
ON BEHAlF OF THE TWENTY-FOUR YOUNG LADIES.
				—
	“Having descended from a glorious ances-
try whose historic annals embrace the re-
gal power of David and his successors, the
princely Integrity of Joseph, and the beauti-
ful and devout Queen Esther who plead for
exiled Israel; as heirs of the same royal line
we have been brought to this goodly land.
	We have chosen and crowned our Queen,
and gladly celebrate the day that, through
our fathers, we enjoy the freedom of these
mountains and valleys. As daughters of
Zion we wish to demonstrate the apprecia-
tion of our rights and privileges. Removed
from the contaminating vices of the world,
in the peaceful vales of Deseret, surrounded
by our fathers, mothers, brothers, sisters,
and those who are near and dear to us, we
can live in the peaceful enjoyment of the
blessings which have been so bountifully
bestowed upon us. We are looked upon by
the women of the world, as not enjoying
the rights and privileges that they enjoy,
and as being degraded and downtrodden.
[2nd column]
What they consider a curse we consider a
blessing; and what they consider as de-
grading, we count as elevating. They pre-
fer to live in luxury and elegance, scarce
considering the degrading means adopted
to bring these; while we prefer becoming
honorable wives and mothers in Israel. Can
we be considered as degraded while enjoy-
ing the respect and esteem of honorable
men and women, and the confidence and
love of all we hold most dear? Let
disinterested persons answer and judge,
whether it is the women of Utah or those of
the world that need pity and condolence.
What greater blessings can woman wish for,
than we enjoy? True, we do not possess the
useless fineries and elegancies of the fashion-
able world; yet we have that which is of far
greater value to us—the enjoyment of our
religion, and the society and confidence of
the truly good—the saints of God; which is
more to us than all the pleasures that wealth
can afford. We live in innocence and sim-
plicity, and with industry and frugality, en-
joy with a clear conscience the comforts re-
sulting from industry, by the kindness of
our great Father.
	The world may have its own ideas of com-
fort; we have ours, and wish to enjoy them
unmolested. As for their sympathy, it has
oftener been shown in murdering our
Prophets and best men, and driving us and
dispoiling us of our property, than other-
wise.
	All we now ask, in the name of our
Queen, is liberty of thought, liberty of ac-
tion and the privilege of serving God accord-
ing to the dictates of our own consciences,
and our holy religion, which by the help of
the great King of Israel, we are determined
to sustain.”
	Weather is very warm and dry, mercury
going up to over 100° daily and sometimes
105°. We have very fine ripe peaches,
plums, pears and apples; and grapes of earli-
est kinds begin to ripen. All these are de-
licious at the cool evening hour, when fra-
grance from the rose and lily and a thousand
other flowers is wafted by the breeze that
gently stirs the foliage overhead, of our
“vine and fig tree,” the almond, prune,
acacia and the elm; after the fierce solar
heat has departed. Although some of the
“downtrodden women of Utah” might wish
to escape for a day with their “masters” to
the shady mountain glen, hear the sighing
of the pines, and take draughts from the pure
nectar springs, we know of none who wish
to make a wider escape from their “tyrant
husbands,” nor to leave far behind the fine
fruits, beautiful flowers and delicious even-
ings of our “Dixie;” and I may be pardoned
for here observing that although the “pecu-
liar institution” of our people is here widely
practiced (as any one might surmise by the
great number of childres of all ages exhibited
in the procession yesterday), we never
knew a more quiet, orderly cormmunity, or
one where less domestic troubles were ex-
perienced.						FLORA.
	The programme of the days proceedings
sent us, is highly interesting but too
lengthy for publication in our columns.—ED.


“I HEV allurs observed that a whining
dog is sure to get lickt in a fight. No cur
of well reggerlated morals kan resist the
temptation to bite a cowardly purp that tries
to sneak off with his tale between his legs.
The whinin’ business man is just so. A
good ringing bark is wuth more to put green-
bax in a man’s pocket than forty-two years
of whinin’ ”.—“Josh Billings.”

it is set in humanity.

﻿ABOUT WOMEN.
			_____
	THE ladies of Albany are about to organ-
ize a Grant and Wilson Club.
	MRS. Greeley is said to be unable to walk,
and is in such poor health as to give Gree-
ley great anxiety about her.
	MRS. Stowe is announced for public read-
ings at New Haven in the Fall. She is
spending the Summer at Hampton Beach.
	THERE are six ordained female pastors in
the Universalist Church in the United
States. This is the only church where wo-
men are admitted to ordination.
	BY a new law, married women in Penn-
sylvania may purchase sewing-machines
without the consent of their husbands, and
the contract will be binding.
	MRS. Lincoln, the widow of the President,
has presented to the State of Illinois a very
valuable oil portrait of the late Colonel E.
D. Baker, a favorite son of Illinois.
	LAURA De Force Gordon is making her-
self famous in the East as an earnest and
eloquent pleader for the cause of Human
Rights.
	SOME Detroit women, of the practical
missionary stamp, lately visited a sick and
widowed neighbor, shingled her house,
planked her side-walk, picketed her fence,
painted one room and papered two, and split
and piled a load of slab wood.
	“HENRIETTA,” said a landlady to her
new girl, “when there’s bad news, particu-
larly private afflictions, always let the board-
ers know it before dinner. It may seem
strange to you, Henrietta, but such things
make a great difference in the eating in the
course of a year.”
	A MARRIED woman of the Shawnee
Indians made this beautiful reply to a man
whom she met in the woods, and who im-
plored her to love and look on him: “Old
man, my husband, who is forever before my
eyes, hinders me from seeing you, or any
other person.
	AN unhappy citizen of Cleveland, was re-
cently entrusted by his wife with the fol-
lowing message to her dressmaker: “Tell
her, dear, that if she thinks it would look
better with ten bias flounces without puff-
ing, and box plaited below the equator,
which should be hem-stitched gudgeons up
and down the seams, with a gusset stitch
between, she can make it up in that way in-
stead of fluting the bobinette insertion, and
piecing out with point applique as I sug-
gested yesterday.”
	A YOUNG HEROINE.—The Royal Humane
Society has awarded its medal, with suita-
ble testimonials, to a young woman, named
Annie Lord, the daughter of a bathing at-
tendant at Lynmouth, Devonshire, who, at
the imminent risk of her  life, saved a
young lady, named Fremlin from drowning.
The circumstances, as elicited by the Com-
mittee of the Society, were briefly as follows:
	Miss Fremlin, accompanied by another
lady, her sister-in-law, went into the sea to
bathe. The sea was very rough at the time,
as it was just after the turn of the tide, and
Miss Fremlin’s companion was struck by a
wave and carried out of her depth. Miss
Fremlin immediately hastened to her assist-
ance, but was herself swept out to sea, and
was in imminent danger of drowning, as no
one was near who was able to swim. Their
perilous position was witnessed by the bath-
ing attendant, who shouted for assistance,
when Annie Lord, who was in a cottage
near, hearing her mother’s cries for help,
ran to the rescue of the two ladies. She
undressed herself as she ran, divesting her-
[2nd column]
self of everything but her chemise, and
bravely plunged into the water. In the
meantime a crowd of people gathered on 
the beach with ropes, etc., but none were
bold enough to swim out. This, however,
Annie Lord did, and soon reached Miss 
Fremlin, who by this time had been carried
some distance out, and was perfectly help-
less, although her companion, whom she
had endeavored to save, had by this time
struggled to shallow water. Grasping Miss
Fremlin by her hair, Annie Lord supported
her, and swam, pushing the drowning lady
before her towards the shore; but Miss
Fremlin was quite insensible, and but for
the heroic perseverance of the young wo-
man, Annie Lord, must have inevitably
been drowned. The other lady, too, was
happily saved.


﻿SIMILARITIES.
				____
	When quite young, I remember reading a
story of some physicians, who experimented
on a young man, to ascertain if it were pos-
sible to frighten a person into a disease, of
which no symptoms were manifest, by mak-
ing him believe that he had taken the dis-
ease and was likely to die in consequence.
They made a joke of the experiment, car-
ried it a good way, and the poor boy was
actually scared into a fit of sickness, while
in a state, physically, of perfect health.
	To-day, while reading some articles, this
little anecdote was brought to my mind, and
I mentally compared our would-be physi-
cians, the moral, highminded (!) people of
the day, to those experimentists. We are,
in a word, all right, but these quacks would
like to frighten us into hysterics over our
situation,and make us believe we are crushd,
trampled on, benighted, and about to die.
One of them, (a lady editor,) says in an art-
icle headed “The wolf in sheep’s clothing,”
“Mormon women are powerless to help them-
selves out of their Church ; it is sad to say
that some of them are so blinded by ignor-
ance and bigotry as to consider that they are
serving God by suffering daily in this state
of polygamy; but even those who are en-
dowed with a higher order of intelligence,
and who would hail freedom from polygamy,
as did the Southern slave the emancipation
proclamation, dare not lift up their voices
against this incubus which is weighing
them down to servitude and degradation.
  	*	*	*	*	*	*
Let every reformer north, south, east and
west, come to the rescue of these Mormon
women, and hurl this Goliah to the earth.”
	Now, if we could only be brought to con-
sider that we are laboring under a terrible
“incubus, which is weighing, us down to
servitude and degradation;” as the strong,
heathy boy was made to see that his face
was spotted and swollen, his eyes red and
dilated with spotted fever, what a grand
terminus for the experiments of our would-
be physicians! “Ye Doctors, heal yonr-
selves!”								G.


﻿WHY WOMEN ARE WEAK.
				—
	When we consider what an intricate system
of defiance to all known physiological laws
a woman’s dress has become; the tender age
at which this defiance begins, and the re-
lentless pressure of it upon the formative
and the recuperative energies of the consti-
tution; the murderous thinness and scanti-
ness of her underdress; the effect of the ab-
sence of flannels, and the custom of baring
the neck and arms, upon the sensitive tis-
sues of the lungs and heart; the check to all
even circulation of the blood and healthy
condition of the skin inflicted by the imper-


feet and compressed covering of her feet
and hands; the unhealthy heat of the head,
consequent on the manner in which custom
requires her to collect her hair into a wad
of padding at the base of the brain; the
clasp of a rack of steel and whalebone about
all the vital organs of the body; the straight- 
jacket snap with which the seams of her
dress meet about her shoulders, arms and
chest; the results inevitable upon making
the hips the pivot upon which her heavy
clothing is hung, and the fulcrum upon
which all the motion of her body must
swing when walking—if, indeed, we apply
that term to the infantile toddle to which wo-
men are driven to get about the world;
above all, the unreasonable and cruel custom
which compels her to drop heavy skirts
about her lower limbs and feet, thus en-
dangering  her life on all occasions, her
health on any but a dry summer day, and 
her self-reliance forever; when we consider
the extent to which the common occupa-
tions of a woman deprive her of the open
air, of exercise, of change of scene, of ac-
quaintance with the world; of the extent to
which they are adapted to produce all the
varieties of sedentary disease; when we con- 
sider the unlimited influence of the mind
upon the body, of happiness upon health, and
the brooding morbidness and acute suffering
which the lives of women so largely induce
in them—shall we find it a matter of sur-
prise that women as a race are diseased and
feeble, and are bestowing upon the world 
a future legacy of diseased and feeble chil-
dren? Shall we wrap this ruined creature 
away in a shawl to die, and say, with sor-
rowful assurance: “Behold woman as God
made her?”
	Woman as God made her! As man and
the devil and her own cowardice have made
her! God never made such women as are
cradling the next generation in this land to- 
day. Side by side with “female illiteracy”
female feebleness is running a race among
us. Neglected brains and tortured bodies
are working their own way upon our actual
and possible mothers. Thoughtful physicians
are perplexed and alarmed. We are a
beautiful, useful and elevated order of ani- 
mals; the world has done the right thing by
us; it stalled and fed us; it has petted and
praised us; who can complain?—Elizabeth
Stuart Phelps.


﻿THE GARDEN FOR WOMEN.
	There is nothing better, says the Ohio 
“Farmer,” for wives and daughters phys-
ically than to have the care of a garden; a
flower-pot if nothing more. What is more
pleasant than to spend a portion of every day
in working among plants and watching the
growth of shrubs and trees and herbage, and
to observe the opening of flowers, from
week to week, as the season advances?
Then how much it adds to the enjoyment to
know that your own hands have planted and
tilled them and have pruned and trained
them. This is a pleasure that requires
neither great riches nor profound know-
ledge. The wife or daughter, who loves
home, and would seek ever to make it the
best place for husband or brother, is willing
to forego some gossiping morning calls for
the sake of having leisure for the cultiva-
tion of plants, shrubs and flowers. The ad-
vantages which women personally derive
from stirring the soil and snuffing the morn-
ing air are freshness and beauty of cheek
and brightness of eye, cheerfulness of
temper, vigor of mind, and purity of heart.
Consequently she is more cheerful and
lovely as a daughter, more dignified and
womanly as a sister, and more attractive and
confiding as a wife.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿DON’T ALWAYS FLIRT.
					—
Don't always flirt; dearest Bessie, believe me,
	You will repent it some day if you do;
Come, now, as some sage old mentor receive me,
	Take my advice, lest your conduct you rue.
Perhaps it’s good fun young men’s feelings to hurt.
Don’t always flirt, though, Bess—don’t always flirt.

True, you can count your fond slaves, dear, by
		dozens—
	All the young men you have got at your feet;
True, you are worshiped by all your male cousins,
	True, all the “belles” of the season you’ve beatl
Granted all this, Bess! you may be too curt,
Too great a flirt, Bessie—too great a flirt!

Time is going on, darling, seasons are hastening;
	Pardon me, but even you must grow old;
Think, now; if you are not good chances wasting;
	Don’t, now, I pray you, imagine me bold.
Really, though, I must my words assert,
Don’t always flirt, Bessie—don’t always flirt!

Young men who now gladly bear your caprices,
	Slaves who’re too happy to suffer your slights,
Perhaps as their knowledge of women increases,
	May not so willingly give up their rights!
Men may be ready to rudely out blurt,
You are a flirt, Bessie—you are a flirt!

Come, then, at present you have half society
	Only too glad to find grace in your eyes;
Have you not, then, had enough of variety?
	Cannot you settle to one, and be wise?
Don’t treat us always as though we were dirt.
Don’t always flirt, Bessie—don’t always flirt!

If I might venture—I know it’s audacious—
   To push my own claims, why, then, Bessie, ma
	chere,
I should be charmed, indeed, if you’d be gracious,
	And trust your dear self from henceforth to my
		care;
Promising only, all rows to avert,
That you wont flirt, Bessie—that you won’t flirt.


﻿A THUNDER STORM ON THE MISSIS-
				SIPPI.
				  —
		[FROM A LADY’S SCRAP BOOK.]
	I shall never forget that night of terror!
How the thunders crashed and the waters 
roared! How the forked lightning flashed
and gleamed, and the boat rolled and tossed
on the river, and how intensely dark the
night was! There were no empty berths
for us to lie down in, and had there been,
what sleep or rest could have been taken!
I had never before witnessed a storm in
America, and, possibly, for that reason it all
seemed more dreadful to me than it might
have done had I been accustomed to such
terrific thunderings and lightnings.
	During the storm—O, it is so horrify-
ing to think of the awful calamity which
befell my poor sister Helen! Her little
daughter followed her father on deck, but
not noticing, he returned without her. We
never saw her again. It was supposed she
walked over board into that angry river.
No trace of her could be found. Though
she could not have existed more than a
minute in the dashing waters we imagined
the little arms which could not have been
seen in the darkness, reaching out for help,
and the childish voice which could not have
been heard in the midst of the roar of the
storm and the waters, calling on us for aid,
long, long after the storm abated and the
day dawned upon us.
	The little one’s sufferings could not have
been much—but the parents—O, how heart-
rending to witness their distress!


﻿THE noblest deeds of many great men’s
lives are private benefactions performed for
humble, undistinguished individuals, of
which the world keeps no account.


IT is said that the Millerites, at Foxboro,
Massachusetts, expected the world to come
to an end July 3rd, and they put on their
robes and waited. But they soon discovered
as the world went on as usual, that they had
made a mistake in their ciphering.


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
			____
	THE French Assembly adjourned on the
4th.
	Gris, the “Fat Contributor,” has started
a weekly paper in Cincinnati.
	There are seventeen newspapers in Jap-
an, the youngest one having been started
in April.
	A skull twenty-six inches in circumfer-
ence above the ears has been dug up at Du-
buque, Iowa.
	The Irish band has split, some sailing for
home with the leader, and the others re-
maining in this country.
	General Berdan, of sharpshooting fame,
is trying to induce the Czar of Russia to buy
more of his guns.
	In Australia spring begins August 20th;
summer, November 20th; autumn, February,
20, and winter, May 20th.
	The magnificent railway station at Metz
was burned, making the fourth large fire in
that city within twelve months.
	Governor Brown still sticks to the opin-
ion that Grant will only carry three States,
all the Radical press to the contrary not-
withstanding.
	Rock Island has tested her new Holly
water works and is delighted over the re-
sults. The capacity of the works is 2,000,-
000 gallons in twenty-four hours.
	John T. Wood, Assistant Postmaster of
Indianapolis, Ind., has been discovered to
be a defaulter to the amount of $4,000 or
$5,000. He has been arrested and bailed.
	The veteran financial editor in the Uni-
ted States is Sailer, of the Philadelphia
“Ledger.” He has written the money
articles of that journal for a period of thirty-
two years and over.
	The brother of Miss Vinnie Ream, the
sculptress and pet of Congress, has been
sentenced by the United States Court at Fort
Smith, Arkansas, to serve six months in the
penitentiary at Little Rock and pay a fine of
$1,000 for selling liquor in the Indian
Nation.
	Six of the members of the New York 
Republican Commitiee have resigned and
declared for Greeley and Brown. The last
of the six is Mr. Wallace Tappan, of Syra-
cuse, who is well known to prominent Re-
publicans.
	Major General Mott, formerly of the
United States army, but now Major General
of the Egyptian army, and aid-de-camp to
the Viceroy, is on a visit to this country,
and was presented to Secretary Belknap by
Acting Secretary Richardson.
	In Gold Hill District, eight miles north-
west of Bowlder, Colorado, what is termed
a mica mine has been discovered. The vein
commences near the surface, extends down-
ward and appears to be about three feet in
thickness. Sheets of it are obtained near
half a foot square, thin as tissue and as
transparent as glass.
	Ashtabula County, the old home of Mr.
Giddings, in Ohio, is the strongest Republi-
can County in that State, but its Republican
newspaper of largest circulation supports
Greeley and Brown, and at least 20 per cent.
of the Republicans are already counted as
sure for the ticket.
	The girls of the St. Louis Normal School,
it is stated, all wear calico dresses; and if
they continue the same sensible practice
through life they will make all the better
wives, and mothers setting an excellent ex-
ample before their daughters.


﻿	PEN AND SCISSORS.
				—
	MANY errors which we see in others we
convert into virtues in ourselves.
	HATS with thermometers in the roof are
the latest invention. If the mercury gets
too high you stand on your head until your
equilibrium is restored.
	MISS Emma S. Eastman, of Worcester, a
former student of Vassar College, and Miss
Sophia B. Fleming, of Ithaca, N. Y., are the
first lady students at Cornell.
	AN intelligent writer, closes a letter to his
employer thus, “I remain very sincerely,
your friend and mule puncher, J. I.”
	“HAVE you had an invitation to the
ball?” asked a bashful gentleman of a young
lady. “Yes sir,” said the lady, to which
he replied hastily, “O! well, I did not come
to invite you, I only thought I’d like to
know if you were going to be there.”
	MR. J. J. Adams and the late Surveyor
General, and now Senator, Mr. Phineas Post
Office Hitchcock, had an interview on the
streets of Omaha.
	P. W. H.—“Did I ever pay you five cents
for a vote, Mr. Adams?”
	A.—“You never did, but I sold you two
votes for the Senate, and the bargain was
made in the presence of United States Dis-
trict Judge E. S. Dundy and Hon. John I.
Redick. Do you dare to deny it? If you
do, you and I for it.”
	The redoubtable Senator did not deny the
assertions of Adams, who makes good his
statements to Dr. Child and Judge Hyde,
and crams them down the throat of Hitch-
cock himself.—“Omaha Herald.”
	M. D. CONWAY says that Miss Kate Field’s
successful after-dinner venture in London
will probably have the effect of breaking
down prejudices against female post-pran-
dial privileges.
	MR. Proctor thinks that the great explo-
sion on the sun, witnessed by Prof. Young
in September last, indicated a force sufficient
to project matter beyond the sphere of solar
attraction. His opinion strengthens the
hypothesis that many of our meteors were
ejected by the sun.
	SAYS the “Golden Age,” The courtesies
of the White House, during the next Ad-
ministration, will be administered by Miss
Ida Greeley, the Presindnt’s handsome
daughter, a sweet little woman of the most
winsome kind.
	OF the sixty-four chemical elements
which compose our globe, nineteen have
been found in meteorites, and as no single
element has been discovered in them which
was not previously known on earth, it is
supposed that the most distant parts of the
universe are similar in composition to our
sphere.
	THE Sacramento “Daily Union” says:
“The Gentile sisters at Salt Lake City wear
silks at church-goings and flash their
diamonds in the eyes of their Mormon sis-
ters. The female adherents of the prophet
don’t like the contrast very much.”
	Dont they, indeed! If any one has
noticed an expression of sadness on the face
of a humble, plainly attired Mormon lady
while coming in contact with her gaudily
dressed “Gentile sister,” it has been elicited
by feelings of sympathy for the poor, unin-
telligent creature who can so inconsiderately
waste time and money.
	MRS. MARY F. SNOW, of the Pacific Slope
Woman Suffrage Convention, says that a
great many more men than women, propor-
tionately, seem to realize the necessity and
favor the enfranchisement of women.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


IN a little village in Vermont there lived
a farmer named Ransom. They were not
pious people—father of the reprobate order;
in fact, they never went to church. Once,
however, the family were prevailed upon to
attend preaching. When they made their
reluctant and tardy appearance the services
had begun, and they had hardly taken their
seats when the preacher gave out the first
hymn, reading it somewhat thus: “Return,
ye ransom sinners home.” “All right,” cried
the head of the Ransoms, getting in a rage
and clapping his hat on his head. “Come
along, ole woman and gals, we’ll go home
fast enough, and everybody in this ole church
knows we didn’t want to come.”
	MEDICAL societies are warned not to ask
a certain Western minister to preach for 
them. He has this text ready: “In his
disease Asa sought not to the Lord, but to
the physicians. And Asa slept with his
fathers.”
	THE ENGLISH papers have ceased deriding
the Tichborne claimant, and admit that his
his case is “rising almost to its former dig-
nity and interest.”
	“A WOMAN lives for love alone,” but love
must come in a variety of forms, such as ma-
terial for clothing, and bread and butter, &c.
Praises and kisses “alone” will not suffice.

	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

	A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL.

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.

	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"

	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.

	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing, labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.	
	
	Miss Eliza R. Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.

	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.	
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance: 		
  1Copy one year,     $2.00	1 Copy six months,    $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months, 9.00

	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents.
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

	Address all communications and  subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


Woman's Exponent . 		

VOL. I. 			SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, SEPTEMBER 1, 1872.


NEWS AND VIEWS.

	IT IS now generally conceded that the
colored vote of the country will be cast
almost solid for Grant.
	PARIS HAS nearly six thousand less of
population now than in 1866. War is a ter-
rible enemy to population.
	MAINE HOLDS its election on the 9th inst.
Both the Grant and Greeley parties are
making strenuous efforts to secure a victory.
 	THE CLERGYMEN of Wilmington, Dela-
ware, are on a "strike," demanding new
terms for attending funerals. What next!
	CAMP MEETINGS are in full blast through 
the country, yet the ranks of the uncon-
verted are but little thinned, while religious
insanity increases.
	THE GENEVA board of arbitrators, to set-
tle the difficulties between England and
America, are reported nearly through their
work. At last!
	CARLYLE SAYS he has no faith in any
man now controlling public affairs. This is
hard on Blanton Duncan of the "straight
out" Democrats.
	J. P. NEWMAN and Henry Ward Beecher
favor croquet. There is one other thing,
besides Grant, on which they are in accord.
Is this the foreshadowing of the millen-
nium?
	THE DIAMOND mania has reached Salt 
Lake, and "gem-hunting expeditions" are
moving towards the borders of New Mexico
and Arizona. Expeditions to hunt the dia-
monds of truth are still scarce.
	THE ELEMENTAL war raged furiously in
many parts of the east during last month,
and numerous deaths from lightning were
reported. Are the elements preparing to
"melt with fervent heat?"
	THEODORE TILTON says U. S. Grant and
Horace Greeley drink more whiskey than 
any other two men in the United States,
and that Greeley doesn't drink a drop. The-
odore is more pointed than polite.
	FIFTY thousand dollars, Mr. Stanley says,
his Livingstone expedition cost the New 
York "Herald," and now the question is
being asked, what is to be the paying result
of the discovery? Mercenary age!
	THE ITALIAN Republicans are split into
two factions, the Mazzinians and Interna-
tionalists, each hostile to the other, and
both hostile to the Government; and thus
the democracy of Europe emulate that of 
the United States.
	THE San Francisco "Pioneer," woman's
rights paper, repudiates Grant and Greeley,
and strongly advocates George Francis
Train for President. Unfortunately women, 
in States, are yet powerless at the polls, and
the sisterhood can do but little for the "next
President of America."
	IT IS reported that two millions of people
have already perished by the famine in Per-
sia, of whom one hundred thousand have
died in the city of Teheran alone. And yet
how little do we hear of its horrors? And 
what is being done to relieve the suffering 
and dying? Is the charity of Christian na-
tions asleep?
	THE TEMPERANCE party is troubled
whether to support General Grant, who it
is admitted uses alcoholic liquors, or Horace
Greeley, who is a total abstainer; and re-
gret that neither the Philadelphia nor Balti-
more conventions put a temperance plank 
in their platforms. To be consistent they 
should support the cold water candidate.
	A COLORED prayer meeting was "arrest-
ed" in Richmond, Virginia, on August 19;
and "parson, deacons, converts, penitents,
brethren and sisters" were rudely conveyed
to the police station on a charge of disor-
derly conduct, where the Judge discharged
them with the injunction to "go and sin no
more!" Probably they were practicing 
camp meeting religion.
	LOVE and patriotism are made to play 
severely against each other in Alsace and 
Lorraine, the departments wrested from
France during the late war. The German 
government has informed the ladies of
those departments, that the brothers and 
lovers of such of them as are most violent
in their demonstrations of hostility to Ger-
many, will be selected to serve in the Ger-
man army after the 1st of October. This is
the refinement of tyranny.
	SECRETARY Boutwell made a speech dur-
ing the North Carolina canvass, which is
likely to work serious consequences. He
endeavored to rekindle the bitter feelings of 
the war, and urged upon the negroes that
the success of the Conservative party would
result in their being placed back in slavery. 
The feelings of animosity caused by such
political speeches, are strong enough to pro-
voke, at no very distant day, a war of races
which might become even more terrible
than the war of secession.
	MR. CLAGGETT, Delegate in Congress
from Montana, who manifested such bitter-
ness during the last session against the Mor-
mons, sought re-election at the hands of his
constituents last month, and was respect-
ably shelved, Mr. Maginnis, his opponent,
being elected by a handsome majority.
After the 4th of next March Mr. Claggett
may air his anti-Mormon views anywhere
he pleases—except in Congress. He has 
been retired with Colfax, Cullom, Bingham
and others. "The mills of the Gods grind
slowly."
	IT IS reliably stated that at Niles, Michi-
gan, recently, a white girl desiring to marry
a negro, and being unable to take the oath 
that she had negro blood in her, tapped the 
arm of her sable lover with a penknife,
sopped a piece of bread in his blood, swal-
lowed it, hurried to the magistrate’s office,
swore that she had negro blood in her, and 
was married. Whatever may be thought
of her determination, her taste was ex-
ecrable; but she was bent on legal miscege-
nation, and cheated the law by an evasion 
which amounted to perjury.
	MR. GREELEY made a political speech at
Portland, Maine, on the 14th of August,
 the first he made since his dual nomination;
and of it the New York "Herald," which
has until lately been a strong administra-
tion paper, says, it "will disappoint those
who seem to believe that it is impossible
for a man of genius and talent to talk in a
practical sensible manner." And, again,
"the speech is a neat, shrewd, little affair,
and will not harm the liberal candidate."
It was a candid, manly, honest statement of
facts, and a denial of several political calum-
nies uttered against him.
	THE savants of Zurich, Switzerland, are
somewhat narrow-minded. The professors
of the University in that city, being unani-
mously opposed to the admission of further
female students, intend to apply to the fed-
eral government of the republic for a bill
restricting the rights of studentship to
males. Why do they not go a little further, 
and restrict the number of males? These
antiquated specimens of book-worms do not
seem to be aware that the world moves, and
that woman is asserting her right to the 
possession of knowledge; a right which all
progressive minds are now freely conceding.
	STRANGE, is it not, that Charles Sumner,
so long the favored son of Massachusetts,
should be berated by the press of the State
in the most bitter manner! But such is
popular favor. The idol of to-day is to-
morrow flung in the dust and trampled un-
der foot. Even the negroes, in whose behalf
the better part of Sumner's sixty-two years
has been spent, turn against him and repu-
diate him. And all this because he will
not play demagogue, but looking at the
present and future of the country as a states-
man, he sees danger in the policy of the
administration, and declares his hostility
to it.
	THE METHODIST Book Concern, of New
York, the rascality connected with which 
raised such a stench through the country a
short time ago, objects to a Greeley and
Brown banner in front of the building, and
asks for its removal. Dr. J. P. Newman,
who was so anxious to make political capi-
tal out of the discussion with Professor Or-
son Pratt in this city, and so signally failed,
is doubtless among those who are at the
bottom of this bit of spleen, as he is endeav-
oring to manipulate the Methodist church
for the re-election of President Grant. "No
union of politics and religion" has been a
favorite cry with many of the enemies of 
the "Mormon" people; yet such union is 
very distinct, and being used for personal
purposes with considerable effect, during
the present Presidential campaign.
	FEARFUL RIOTS have occurred in Belfast,
the chief city of the north of Ireland. Com-
mencing on the 15th of August they con-
tinued for a week, during which a
number of lives were lost and a vast amount 
of property was destroyed. The rioters
were Protestant and Roman Catholic mobs.
that in their zeal to prove the superiority
of their respective creeds, shed blood, pil-
laged and destroyed. And each party pro-
fesses to believe in the Prince of Peace; in
a gospel of love and salvation! And to
show that they do so, they act like fiends,
trampling every God-like principle under-
foot, and practicing a faith of hate, revenge
and murder! No wonder infidelity grows
among intelligent men and women who are
unacquainted with the gospel of truth; for
who could intelligently consider such acts,
and reflect that the teachings of priests and 
clergymen have caused them, without being
satisfied that there was something radically
wrong in the systems which produce such
results.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿TO MY PEN.
				—
And shall I take thee up again, old friend,
And in the cause of truth my weak voice blend; 
Remove from off thy point the gathering rust
And to the weary whisper hope and trust ?
I know the value of a word of cheer,
For strongest hearts grow faint; but God is near,
Guiding our frail barks through the darkest storm,
And while we trust to him we’ro safe from harm.

Methinks I hear thee say again to-day—
“Better to work than rust; this life away;
Better to plant, although it seem in vain;
The seed may reach good soil and grow again.”

The field is broad and long, the workers few
Whose hands are willing and whose hearts are
	true;
And none but such the harvest Master calls
His grain to garner ere the tempest falls—

His storm of wrath, upon the sin-stained world:
Where wrong has gained the power and truth been
	hurled
From her high throne, and crushed and trampled
	down—
Come, all ye brave and help regain her crown.

Long has the cry been sounding in my ear,
Long have I seen the pain, the bitter tear,
Wrung from the suff'ring ones on life’s hard road,
Who onward struggle ’neath sin’s heavy load.

We're weak, my pen, but then Truth's Leader’s
	strong.
And unto her must yield all powers of wrong;
If her defenders be but true and brave,
Truth will not always be a fettered slave.
										H.


﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
				  —
	The following, under date of August
18th, is from Mrs. Julia Lindsey, President
of the F. R. Society of Paris, Rich county,
Bear Lake valley:
	Money is very scarce in this part of the
country, owing to our crops having been de-
stroyed repeatedly, by frost and that terrible
scourge the grasshoppers, which has kept
the people in rather poor circumstances.
But we trust in God, and hope for better
days. We had quite severe frosts the latter
part of July, and it was at first supposed
our wheat crop was all killed; but time has
proved that it is not injured as badly as was
at first feared, which we are very thankful
for. Our society donated quite a sum to
the emigration fund, though small, I pre-
sume, in comparison with some other socie-
ties; but the fault was not in the heart but
in the purse. Our society is still alive, but
for the reasons stated above, we have not
accomplished as much as we should have
done, under more favorable circumstances.
We have our regular meetings once a fort-
night, which are not always very numer-
ously attended. But there are a number of
good, faithful sisters, who would be a bless-
ing to any society, who are determined,
with the help of God, to hold the society
together in spite of the slackness of the
few, and accomplish all the good they can.


A GOOD EXAMPLE.—A little girl, seven
years old, earned by her own labor two dol-
lars, which she has donated to the fund for
the emigration of the poor. She is the
daughter of Mrs. Cowley of the 14th ward,
of this city. This speaks well for mother
and child. Who will follow the example?
Boys and girls, remember the poor, and
God will remember you.		W. E.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
				——
	MRS. ABELL says: “A woman who has
the habit of observing closely, knows so
well what belongs to social and domestic
comfort, that she never enters a room of her
own dwelling without seeing in an instant
every trifle upon which comfort so much de-


pends. If the fire is too low or the hearth
unswept, if the chairs are not standing in
their places, or books and papers disar-
ranged, her quick and well-trained eye de-
tects in an instant what is wanting to com-
plete the pleasant air of comfort and order
which belongs to her to diffuse over her
whole household.  While, on the other
hand, if her attention has never been directed
to these matters, she may sit down without
once perceiving that the chairs are in dis-
order, drawers left open, the hearth-rug
turned up, the fire smoking, the room too
dark, or the sun pouring in its fiercest rays
to the damage of carpets. A woman want-
ing in this habit of observation, though
actuated by kindly feelings, will be guilty
of much neglect and be likely to have the
reputation of unkindness or ill-breeding.”
	LEMON DUMPLINGS.—With ten ounces of
fine bread-crumbs mix one large tablespoon-
ful of flour, half a pound of finely chopped
beef suet, the grated rinds of two small
lemons, four ounces of pounded sugar, three
large eggs, beaten and strained, and last of
all, the juice of the two lemons, also strained.
Divide the mixture into four dumplings, tie
them in well-floured cloths, and let them
boil an hour.
	LEMON TART.—Take half a pound of
lump sugar, slice three lemons, and lay them
in the sugar for the night; cover the bot-
tom of the dish with a pound of biscuits,
lay over them slices of candied orange and
lemon-peel, four ounces of pounded almonds,
and one of butter; put in the sugar and
lemons, and cross-bar the top.
	SCALLOPED MEAT.—Take any kind of
cold roast meat, cut it into exceedingly thin
slices of about an inch across, season it well,
pour over it enough wine, gravy, and melted
butter to moisten, place it in buttered scal-
lop shells, sprinkle bread-crumbs pretty
thickly on the top, and place them in a hot
oven till well browned; cold rabbit, sweet-
breads, poultry, veal, and different kinds of
fish, are specially adapted for coquilles.
Serve in the shells.
	HOUSEKEEPERS should heed the following:
When a carpet is taken up to be cleaned, the
floor beneath is generally covered with dust.
This dust is fine and dry, and poisonous to
the lungs; before removing it, sprinkle the
floor with diluted carbolic acid, to kill any
poisonous germs that may be present, and
to thoroughly disinfect the floor and make
it sweet.
	WALNUT CATSUP.—Beat about a hundred
green walnuts in a marble mortar till they
are completely broken, and then put them
into a stone jar, with half a pound of es-
chalots cut in slices, one head of garlic, half
a pound of salt, and two quarts of vinegar;
allow them to stand for ten or twelve days,
stirring them night and morning; strain off
the liquor, and boil it for half an hour, add-
ing two ounces of anchovies, two of whole
pepper, half an ounce of cloves, and a quar-
ter of an ounce of mace; skim it well, strain,
and when perfectly cold, pour it gently from
the sediment into small bottles; secure from
the air by sound corking, and store it in a
dry place. The sediment is usually reserved
for flavoring sauces.


﻿“WELL, I did not think, when I married
you, that you would grow to be such a goose!”
said a young married man to his bride, who
was pouting because she could not go to a
party in the evening. “Neither did I,”
she replied, “but your mother was telling
me, only yesterday, how much I was grow-
ing into your ways.” Both laughed, and
they concluded to make up and be good-na-
tured.


CLOUDS.
			     —
	How much the face of nature, in its vari-
ous aspects, resembles the hearts and lives
of human beings! Sometimes the sky ap-
pears so clear and beautiful one could almost
imagine that never again would storm clouds
gather to mar the loveliness and regularity
of its soft blue features; and the lives of in-
dividuals often seem the same. There is
nothing to distress or to disturb; all is sun-
shine, peace and tranquillity. But, as over
the summer sky, shadows creep, almost im-
perceptible at first, the tiny white clouds
that dot the brilliant azure in varied forms
growing larger and darker, until the rain
drops begin to descend, and thickening and
blackening into terrible tempestuous war-
riors arrayed against the humbler elements;
so also is the serenity of the soul often over-
cast with clouds of sorrow and regret; clouds
that appear too small and trifling at first to
shade the glorious sunlight of mental peace,
or tend to make earth’s garden less a para-
dise to the inexperienced and innocent;
yet, clouds that will sometimes expand and
gather blackness till they shut out all the
light, and then, triumphant, shower down
their wrath upon the poor, unsheltered hu-
man heart. But every cloud must have
spent all its fury and poured out all its sor-
rowing tears in some great hearts, that
seem to have been tried to the core, and to
have overcome temptations, until the adver-
sary has no further power with them to
tempt or to annoy. I have known a few in-
dividuals whom I believed to possess such
hearts, and to me they are the happiest and
most loveable of all earth’s inhabitants. If
a gloom would settle over those around
them, they seem to have the power to
chase it off. They are those who have
learned to acknowledge the hand of God in
all things; who have overcome selfishness.
For this one demon—selfishness—is the
foundation of evil and unhappiness. And
till the heart has overcome this fiend, clouds
of remorse and gloom will never cease to
throw their shadows o’er its otherwise
cheerful and blissful course.
								L.


CHILDREN.—“I like children," he said
to me one day at the table. “I like ’em,
and respect ’em. Pretty much all the honest
truth-telling there is in the world is done
by them. Do you know they play the part
in the household which the king’s jester,
who very often had a mighty long head under
his cap and bells, used to play for a
monarch? There’s no radical club like a
nest of little folks in a nursery. Did you
ever watch a baby's fingers? I have, often
enough, though I never knew what it was
to own one.” The master paused for half a
minute or so, sighed—perhaps at thinking
what he had missed in life—looked up at
me a little vacantly. I saw what was the
matter; he had lost the thread of his talk.
	“Baby’s fingers,” I intercalated.
	“Yes, yes. Did you ever see how they
will poke those wonderful little fingers of
theirs into every fold and crack and crevice
they can get at? That is their first educa-
tion—feeling their way into the solid facts
of the material world. When they begin
to talk, it is the same thing over again in
another shape. If there is a crack or a flaw
in your answer to the shoulder-hitting ques-
tions, they will poke and poke until they have
got it gaping just as the baby’s fingers have
made a rent out of the atom of a hole in
his pinafore that your old eyes never took
notice of. Then they make such fools of
us by copying on a small scale what we do
in a grand manner.”—“Dr. O. W. Holmes."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.				
﻿


﻿FLOWERS BY THE WAYSIDE.
			      —
	To acquire a capacity for happiness, first
rectify inward disorder.
	HE who enlists in any worthy enterprise,
should not consider an ultimate failure pos-
sible.
	Algona, Iowa, which was twenty years
ago a naked prairie, is now finishing off its
second court house.
	SOME people talk with their eyes more
eloquently than others, can with their na-
tural organ or speech.
	THE favorite method of disposing of
mysterious disappearances in Cincinnati is
to head them up in a salt barrel.
	NO position however great, no possessions
however desirable, can make us blest unless
we feel ourselves deserving of them.
	PLEASURE is perfect only when it harmo-
nizes with our permanent interests; no de-
light can be enduring which interferes with
duty.
	MORAL and religious instruction derives
its efficacy, not so much from what men are
taught to know, as from what they are made
to feel.
	OF WHAT character is your conversation?
Whatever it may be, be assured it is an un-
erring index to your heart. The tree is
known by its fruit.
	TO BE truly great, or sincerely happy, use-
fulness and beneficence, not ostentation and
vanity, must direct the train of thought and
influence words and actions.
	TEMPERANCE, by fortifying the mind and
body, leads to happiness; intemperance, by
enervating them, endangers the health and
peace of both, and ends in misery.
	A LUNATIC has turned up in Chicago in
the role of Diogenes, hunting for an honest
man with a lantern. The poor fellow might
as well make up his mind to die there.
	WHEN we observe a tendency to treat re-
ligion or morals with disrespect and levity,
we may hold it as a sure indication of a per-
verted understanding or a depraved heart.
	TRUE beauty is a soul refined, true wealth
the riches of the mind; let woman give but
little thought to loveliness of face, nor sigh
for power that in the purse alone hath dwel-
ling-place.
	EVERY degree of guilt incurred by yield-
ing to temptation, tends to debase the mind,
weaken the generous and benevolent prin-
ciples of human nature, and make cowards
of its victims.
	THERE is a reverend gentleman in Min-
nesota who spends his Sundays in prophesy-
ing that the world will not outlast the
present summer, and yet he has sown 100
acres of wheat.
	MANY people imagine themselves pat-
terns or at least as good as the average of
husbands and wives, who daily crucify the
spirit of their companion, making life al-
most loathsome.
	LUXURY, pride and vanity, have frequent-
ly as much influence in corrupting the sen-
timents of the great, as ignorance, bigotry
and prejudice have in misleading the opin-
ions of the multitude.
	IF WE desire to make others happy, we
must haste to give while our gifts can be
enjoyed, and remember that every moment
of delay takes away something from the
value of a benefaction.
	IT IS useless to endeavor to satisfy the
constant demands of the selfish and ungrate-
ful, for the more you give the more un-
grateful do they become, and the harder
will they speak against you.


﻿HEREDITARY INFLUENCES.—It is dis-
tinctly asserted by professor Lucas, and
agreed to by others, that predisposition to
any form of disease or any malformation
may become an inheritance. Thus disease
of the heart is hereditary; so are tubercles
in the lungs; so also are diseases of the
brain, of the liver, and of the kidney; so
are diseases of the eye and of the ear. Gen-
eral maladies are equally inheritable, as
gout and madness. Longevity on the one
hand and premature deaths on the other
go by descent. If we consider a class of
peculiarities more recondite in their origin
than these, we shall still find the law of in-
heritance to hold good. A morbid suscepti-
bility to contagious disease, or to the poison-
ous effects of opium or of calomel, and an
aversion to the taste of meat, are all found
to be inherited. So is craving for strong
drink or gambling, strong sexual passion, a
proclivity to pauperism, to crimes of vio-
lence, and to crimes of fraud. There are
certain marked types of character justly as-
sociated with marked types of feature and
of temperament. We hold, axiomatically,
that the latter are inherited (the case being
too notorious, and too consistent with the
analogy afforded by brute animals, to render
argument necessary), and we therefore infer
the same of the former. For instance, the
face of the combatant is square, coarse, and
heavily jawed. It differs from that of the
ascetic, the voluptuary, the dreamer and
the charlatan. Still more strongly marked
than these, are the typical features and
characters of different races of men. The
Mongolians, Jews, negroes, Gipsies, and
American Indians, severally propagate their
kinds; and each kind differs in character
and intellect, as well as in color or shape,
from the other four. They, and a vast
number of other races, form a class of in-
stances worthy of close investigation, in
which peculiarities of character are invari-
ably transmitted from the parents to the off-
spring.—“Francis Galten.”


﻿A GENTLEMAN in the cars the other day,
so far forgot himself as to place his foot,
boot and all, rather hastily upon the
shoulder of a lady who sat front of him;
and when apologizing for the overt and
somewhat ludicrous action, said he thought
he must have been partially asleep, could
hardly believe he would have done such a
thing had he been fairly awake. Gentle-
men would do well to guard against snooz-
ing in the cars, when there are ladies near,
who, unquestionably would prefer being en-
tertained colloquially, to being somnolently
kicked at. Of course the infractor was one
of our “Mormon tyrants.” But let us have
the best coloring on the outside—it was his
neighbor’s wife and not his own that he
booted.


﻿A SOMEWHAT notable tribute has just been
paid to an American artist by the lord
mayor of London. His honor having vis-
ited Munich lately, there entered the studio
of David Neal. He found him just finish-
ing a picture of Watt as a boy, studying the
power of steam. The boy is seated in the
chimney corner, watching the tea-kettle.
His mother has opened the door, and scolds
him pleasantly, as her face indicates, for ab-
senting himself from the family eating
meal, which is seen going on in another
room through the half-opened door. The
mayor invited Neal to name his own price
for the picture, and it will henceforth grace
the Mansion House in London.


A VETERAN lady, whose sons are all
grown to be big boys, either reverentially
or ironically calls her new dog “Grant.”


﻿PEN AND SCISSORS.

	A SPRING bed—a bed of radishes.

	THERE can certainly be no greater felicity
than to be able to look back on a life use-
fully and virtuously employed, and to trace
our own progress in existence by such
tokens as excite neither shame nor sorrow.
	WHAT is the ultimate scope of our present
pursuits? Are they, as they should be, such
that we can justify them to ourselves, and
likely to produce anything that will survive
the moment and bring forth some fruit for
futurity?
	LITTLE Robbie went to a show, and saw
an elephant for the first time in his life.
When he came home, his mother asked him
what he had seen. Robbie replied, “An
elephant, mama, that gobbled hay with his
front tail.”
	IT was a happy thought for Dr. Doremus
to say that if he were challenged to fight a
duel, he would suggest to his opponent that
both should take poison, and then sit down
and play poker for the exclusive use of the
stomach pump.
	A COUNSEL mimicking a witness, who as
a north countryman pronounced the word
“water” as if it had been “watter,” in-
quired of him whether in his part of the
country they spell “water with two t’s?”
“No,” said the witness, “but they spell
manners with two n’s.”
	TILL we have reflected on it, we are
scarcely aware how much the sum of hu-
man happiness in the world is indebted to
this one feeling—sympathy. We get cheer-
fulness and vigor, we scarcely know how or
when, from mere association with our fel-
low men, and from the looks reflected on us
of gladness and keen enjoyment.
	IT IS not what people eat, but what they
digest, that makes them strong. It is not
what they gain, but what they save, that
makes them rich. It is not what they read,
but what they remember, that makes them
learned. It is not what they profess, but
what they practice, that makes them right-
eous.
	THE weeping willow has a romantic his-
tory. The first scion was sent from Smyrna
in a box of figs, to Alexander Pope. Gen-
eral Clinton brought a shoot from Pope's
tree to America, in the time of the revolu-
tion, which, passing into the hands of John
Parker Curtis, was planted on his estate in
Viriginia, thus becoming the progenitor of
the weeping willow in this country.
	A CURIOUS portrait of General Washing-
ton, on earthenware, is displayed in a New
York jeweler's window. It is stated that
soon after Washington's death an American
traveling in England discovered some sets
of earthenware with these portraits en-
ameled upon them. He purchased the
whole lot, had the portraits cut out and
framed, and distributed them among his
friends.
