SPLINTERS.

	One hundred and eighty young and hardy
Scotchmen have lately landed at Quebec......
A new ocean cable has recently been laid
from the Suffolk coast in England to Hano-
ver......Gold medals have been sent to Stan-
ley, the explorer, from the St. Petersburg
and Florence geographical societies......Some
one now states that Horace Greeley, instead
of Wilkes Booth, was or should have been
Lincoln’s assassin......One hundred people
were lately thrown out of employment, at
Portland, by the burning of a rope factory...
About fifty delegates, from different parts
of the United States and Canada, were pres-
ent at the session of the Caledonia society
of North America......The Indians at Por-
tage la Prairie, Canada, refuse their annui-
ties, as last year provided by treaty, and
declare that unless better terms are granted
they will stop surveys......In the past two
years, twenty-three thousand miles of elec-
tric cables have been laid in the waters of
England, France, Spain, Holland, Turkey,
China, South America and Australia......A
late fire in the press room of the “Christian
Union,” Henry Ward Beecher’s paper, de-
stroyed about $10,000 worth of property......
Twenty-three cases of sunstroke in New
York, and seven in Brooklyn, occurred on
the 15th of August......John G. Saxe takes
the stump for Greeley and Brown......Half
the slate pencils used in the world are
claimed by Vermont to be made in the
Green Mountain State.....An English stock
purchaser has paid $13,000 for two short-
horned heifers in Kentucky......Carl Schurz
delivered his speech in Chicago in German...
It is believed that Charles Sumner and N.P.
Banks will take the stump in Maine......Gar-
rison tries to prove that Greeley was never
an abolitionist......Ludgate Hill, London’s
great thoroughfare, is to be paved with
American wood at a cost of $25,000......Kate
Field is engaged to write for the “Ameri-
can Register”......A New Hampshire paper
reports that twenty persons and a negro
were baptised on a Sunday, in its neighbor-
hood......Report says the prettiest girl about
Saratoga, is an Indian maiden, who sells
bead-work at the springs......Rev. Miss
Babcock has entered pastoral possession of the
pulpit of Warren street church, in Boston......
Last year, England bought from the United
States, $184,000,000 worth of cotton; and
manufactured from it L382,000,000 worth of
goods......Turin, Italy, boasts eighty women
over a century old, the youngest being 102
and the eldest 117......The Grant men think
to carry Maine, September 9th, by 49,000
majority; but Greeley men say they wont.
GREELEY is said to wear his trousers in-
side his boots to show he does not buy mili-
tary boots......“Colonel” Tennie C. Claflin
will take command of her regiment, she
says, as soon as it is recruited up to a thou-
sand strong.....Fanny Lewald, the German
authoress, intends to lecture in the United
States next autumn.......Mr. Boutwell is
now speaking for Grant in Maine......One
hundred and eighteen printers in Albany
have formed a Greeley and Brown club......
Chicago laid over a mile of sidewalk in
July......The female students who claimed
admission to the Scotch Universities, have
gained their point.....A Boston belle wears
the honors at Cape May......Bret Harte is
writing a melodrama......Two boats, one
containing two men, and the other a little
boy and girl, drifted over the Niagara Falls
on the 26th of July......The Emperors of
Germany and Austria met at Ischl on the
28th of August......The tomb of Abelard
and Heloise, at Pere la Chaise, is being
renovated at the expense of a noble and
[new column]
aged French lady......The Stadt Theatre,
New York, was lately sold at auction for
$12,200......The Grand Lodge of Odd Fel-
lows, of New York, reject the constitutional
amendment for admission of members ex-
pelled more than ten years......“Mark
Twain” has sailed in the “Scotia” for Eu-
rope......America is to be visited by a son of the
Khedive of Egypt......Juarez,the late Presi-
dent of Mexico, was a full-blooded Indian...
A cat was recently sold in London for
$255......The population of Russia is 81,-
500,000......Professor Agassiz is on his way
homeward......The father of the late James
Fisk, jr., has been horse-whipping general
J. W. Phelps, at Brattleboro, Vt......The
well-known and popular journalist of New
Orleans, Paul E. Roman, is dead......A pro-
ject for a tax upon domestic servants, was
recently brought forward in the French as-
sembly......A literary revolution is in pro-
gress in Poland......M. D. Conway says the
Prince of Wales possesses one good quality;
he is free from cant and hypocrisy......A
brother of Dr. Livingstone, residing at Lis-
well, Canada, has received letters from the
doctor, sent by Stanley......Miss Dickinson
is now said to be quietly pursuing her lec-
ture studies at home.


WHO IS TO BLAME?

	It has become so popular among the fashion-
able women of society, to be weak and sickly,
and under the care of a physician, that a woman
who is blest with good health and has sense
enough to appreciate it, is likely to be consid-
ered coarse, masculine and non-sensitive in her
mental as well as physical organization. If a
girl can enjoy exuberantly a bowl of hominy
and milk, and not be ready to die with dyspep-
sia in an hour afterward; if she can make her
own bed and sweep the room, or indulge in a
genuine, hearty laugh, and not complain of a
fearful pain in the side for the next forty-eight
hours; she is thought to be vulgar and unculti-
vated, and no matter how chaste and finished
her educational developments, she is an unfit
associate for those gentler, more delicate and
refined (!) creatures to whom the sight of “mo-
lasses preserves” serves as an emetic, and who
can faint away at the smell of “soap-suds.”
The reason of this is, that such a young lady
can not possess that prevalent, yet senseless
and inhuman taste, which delights in suffering
the pangs engendered by tight lacing, wearing
high heeled boots; and, in short, the whole
toilet of the fashionable lady of the day, which
makes a sacrifice of health and all real happi-
ness, and cannot fail to inflict premature death.
More than half the fault, for this fashionable
folly, may justly be charged to men, though
women are so severely blamed for it all. For
no true wife, of a kind and faithful husband, is
going to far over-reach the line in any respect,
by which he would see her guided. Were there
more true husbands, there would be more true
wives. For nearly all women can be influenced
and led by just and intelligent men, and are
always ready to unite their efforts with those


of man for the promotion of good. And if hus-
bands and wives would co-operate as they
should do upon those points, not many of their
daughters would run wild after the silly vani-
ties of the world, but would live and work for
nobler and higher aims. But men of the world
do not wish their wives and daughters to be
prudent and temperate, or they would set bet-
ter examples before them than they do. If
women were anything better than the extrava-
gant, sickly, silly, vain things that they are,
men, taking them generally, in their present
degenerate state, would sink to nothingness be-
side them.


﻿THE TERRITORIAL FAIR.

	At the meeting of the Board of Directors of
the Deseret Agricultural and Manufacturing
Society, held in the Historian’s office on Sat-
urday evening, August 24, Hon. Wilford Wood-
ruff presiding, it was decided to publish a
circular addressed to the Female Relief Socie-
ties throughout the Territory, relative to the
approaching Territorial Fair. We take great
pleasure in printing the circular, as requested
by the Board, which reads as follows:
	SALT LAKE CITY, August 17, 1872.
TO THE FEMALE RELIEF SOCIETIES
   THROUGHOUT UTAH TERRITORY:
    LADIES:—The Deseret Agricultural and Man-
ufacturing society, at a meeting of its board of
directors on the 10th day of August inst., recog-
nizing the fact that heretofore the ladies have
contributed in a liberal manner to their de-
partment, until it has become one of the lead-
ing features of our exhibitions, ordered that a
circular be written calling upon all the Relief 
Societies to unite and by a concentration of ef-
fort accomplish that which will be highly
creditable and be parallel with Utah’s advanced
growth.
	Exhibitions of domestic products and manu-
factures are considered of such utility and im-
portance that they form a prominent part of
the civil polity of the nations; and the people
who foster and excel in their products and in-
dustries lay the foundation of commercial inde-
pendence.
	A people situated as we are, so remote from
other manufacturing districts, aided by the
heavy tariffs of transportation, have every
stimulus to encourage them in this direction.
The adoption of this policy is pregnant with
benefits on every hand. While attaining the
desirable status of having “the beauty of our
apparel the workmanship of our own hands,”
the laudable condition is also reached of fur-
nishing employment for the heavy tide of im-
migrants coming westward and for the legion
of youth growing up in our midst.
	A word to the wise is sufficient: Ladies, you
are respectfully invited to contribute and to
take an active part in rendering this exhibi- 
tion a step in advance of its predecessors.
	The Fair will be held in the 13th Ward As-
sembly Rooms, in this city, on the 3d, 4th and
5th of October, so as to close before the com-
mencement of the Semi-Annual Conference. 
At this Fair the ladies of the Relief Societies
can do much to add to its interest; they can 
show how well cultivated is the taste, how skill-
ful are the hands, and how competent in work
of many kinds are those who devote a portion
of their time, intelligence, and the ability with
which they have been endowed, to alleviate
the sufferings or privations of those who need
their assistance.
For the benefit of the ladies we give the two
classes of articles in which they are more di-
rectly interested, for which prizes will be
awarded, taken from the list of premiums for
the Tenth exhibition of the Society, printed 
by order of the Board:
		CLASS T.—LADIES' WORK. 
	Supervisory Committee.—E. F. Sheets.
Awarding Committee.—ELIZA R. SNOW, MRS.
M. T. SMOOT, MRS. PHEBE WOODRUFF, MRS.
B. W. SMITH, MRS. LUCY KIMBALL, Provo,
MRS. ELIZABETH BARLOW, Bountiful.

	Applicants for premiums on butter must
accompany their samples with a written state-
ment of the time when made, number of cows
made from, quantity of salt per pound used,
how manufactured, washed or otherwise, and
how packed to entitle them to awards by the
judges.
	Applicants for premiums on cheese must ac-
company their samples with a written answer
to the following questions:
	How many cows in your dairy? Do you feed
grain or roots? If so, what kind, and when?
	What is your usual average yield per cow?
	Into what do you set your night’s milk?
	Do you take off cream in the morning?
	Do you return cream? if so, when and how?
	In what manner do you warm milk for the
reception of rennet?
	Do you use a thermometer?
	To what temperature do you heat milk for
the reception of rennet?
	How do you preserve your rennet?
	In what do you set your milk?
	How do you separate whey from curd?
	What time consumed in scalding process?
	How do you scald curd?	
	What quantity of salt do you use to ten
pounds of curd?
	Do you color your curd? if so, with what and
at what stage?	
	Do you put curd to press cold or hot?
	What amount of pressure do you apply?
	How long do you press?
	What is the average weight of your cheeses?
	Do you bandage your cheese? if so, when?
	Where are they kept?
	What do you apply to the surface in curing?
	How often do you turn them?
	What kind of a press do you use?
	What disposition do you make of your whey?
	Do you keep a dairy account?
	What was your average income per cow last
year?

﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


VERMONT ELECTION occurs on the 3rd 
inst.


﻿HOME AFFAIRS.

	THE EXPONENT continues to increase in cir-
culation, for which we thank the friends whose
exertions are directed for its success.
	THE FIRST load of granite brought by steam,
from Cottonwood, was landed at Temple Block,
in this city, on the morning of the 28th ult.
A track has been laid from the U. S. and U. C.
depot to the Temple Block, along which a loco-
motive can pass with loaded cars of rock to the
foundation of the Temple.
	A BIBLE society for Utah has been organized
in this city. While we are anxious to see the
truths contained in the sacred volume still
more widely disseminated, would it not be ad-
visable, for consistency’s sake, for the gentle-
men who have moved in the matter, either to
recognize more widely the principles taught
in it, or advocate an altered version that would
better agree with their opinions?
	TWO DAYS’ meetings were held at Logan,
Cache county, on the 17th and 18th ult., and at
Farmington, Davis county, on the 24th and
25th ult,; attended by the Presidency, some of
the Twelve and other Elders. At each place a
great deal of valuable instruction was given,
and the large numbers that attended were
much edified and strengthened in faith by the
teachings and exhortations of the speakers.
	A WOMAN ran the danger of being burned to
death in this city last Saturday night, by
placing a coal oil lamp on a hot stove. The
lamp exploded, scattering the burning fluid
over her apparel, and she was only saved by
having the blazing dress torn off her. The
recklessness with which many persons handle
inflammable fluids is surprising considering
the vast number of accidents that are continu-
ally occurring.
	ONE of the most notable events that has oc-
curred in the Territory since our last issue, was
the holding of the Indian council at Spring-
ville, Utah county. A few straight-forward
promises satisfied the Indians assembled, who
expressed themselves warmly in favor of con-
tinued peace, but believed they had not been
justly treated. If the promises again made to
them are performed, there need be little fear of
trouble from them; but if they are broken and
if the assurances given prove empty words,
more trouble may be looked for. In a long
course of experience with the Indians, Presi-
dent Young has ever retained their confidence,
simply because he always performed all that
he promised, and they never had reason to
doubt his word. It is said that much of the
late cause for uneasiness relative to the Uintah
Indians might have been avoided, had the In-
dian agent not been over anxious to show that
he swayed high authority, and, disregarding
the advice of men having experience with the
Indian character, he took a course calculated
to irritate instead of conciliate them. There
are hostile Indians still supposed to be in the
neighborhood of Sanpete and the Sevier, and
against these troops have been dispatched.


﻿WOMAN’S VOICE.

EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	Having finished my washing, got supper
over, and the children to bed, I sit down to
write to you. I notice that you seem to
avoid personalities in your publication as
much as possible. But what I have to say
is chiefly concerning myself and my private
affairs, and yet is illustrative of a principle.
Once, I considered it scandalous and out-
rageous for our family concerns to be bandied
about and ridiculed or groaned over by all
the blockheads in the Christian communi-
ties around us; but now I have become so
[new column]
used to such things that I wish to say some-
thing about it myself. I desire to tell some-
thing of what celestial marriage has done
for me, and I hope you will publish it.
	When a girl, at home with my parents
and brothers and sisters, I had a most
wretched disposition. Not that I willfully
did wrong, nor yet that I was so simple as
not to know better than to talk and do as I
often did; but somehow I seemed to be im-
pelled to make myself a miserable, ungrate-
ful, disagreeable creature.
	After marriage I kept it up. My hus-
band was always kind and affectionate, but
I would be annoyed and unhappy all the
while. When children were given me, and
the cares and duties of life were multiplied,
I grew more and more fretful and disagree-
able. I have wondered how my generous,
peace-loving husband ever stood my con-
stant irritableness, and endured to live with
me; but I suppose his love for me, ungrate-
ful as I was, and his firm faith and trust in
God, helped him to bear it all. When my
second child was two years old, and six
months before my third one was born, my
husband gently, yet earnestly, laid before
me his desire and determination to take an-
other wife. Notwithstanding the principle
was an old one to me, that my husband
should think of adopting it was new and 
surprising. I knew he believed that I would
oppose him; yet I also felt from the first
that his course was right, and that in justice
I could not raise a single objection. I de-
termined to astonish him as much as he
had done me. I therefore put my arms
about his neck and kissed him, a thing I
had rarely if ever done before, and whis-
pered, “All right; I am going to be a good
girl; see if I don’t!” This was after a little
reflection. I then asked my husband to
leave me to myself, which he did. I spent
the afternoon in prayer and contemplation,
not with tears and head and heartache, as I
am aware some of my less-reflecting sisters
do, and have done. I knew the woman
who was to become a second wife to my
husband was a true, whole-souled woman;
sweeter-tempered, more affectionate, oblig-
ing and unselfish than myself. Gossip
would soon say either that I wronged and
trampled on the poor girl, or else that she 
had fairly won my husband’s heart from
me. And I was aware that gossip could
speak truly concerning these things, unless
I altered the course I had been pursuing.
I made up my mind that if slander ever
spoke of me as she had done of others, she
should bear false witness. I would do right.
I would live nearer the Lord than I had
ever done, would seek His Spirit more dili-
gently than I had ever known how to do
before; I would try to be so good and just
and self-sacrificing that no one, however
worthy, should supercede me in any of my
lawful claims. I promised this to myself
and to the Lord. I have kept that promise
so far, and the Lord has rewarded me every
day. I have become pleasant and cheerful
in my thoughts and in my ways; a truer
and more consistent helpmate; a more de-
voted and faithful mother; a stronger, purer
and better woman; and happier in life than
I ever was before being brought into the
practice of the law of celestial or plural
marriage. Whoever may allow “polyg-
amy,” as practiced by the real Latter-day
Saints, to make them weak and miserable,
I know that it can and will bless and exalt
those who enter into and live in it wisely,
for this is what it has done for me; thanks
and glory be given to the Founder and 
Creator of the eternal law!
						COUNTRY WOMAN.
Monday Evening, Aug. 20, 1872.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


A ﻿SONG FOR THE TWENTY-FOURTH.

	All hail the glorious Twenty-Fourth !
		The birthday of the true and free; 
	The breaking light that ushers in
		Th’ eventful dawn of liberty,

	Hail to the ragged Pioneers !
		Weary and dusty though they be
	'Tis the tread of empire at its birth
		The herald footsteps of the free.

	Oh, Father rain thy blessings down!
		Thus prayeth every heart and tongue,
	On the chosen of the people’s hearts,
		Truth's noblest champion, Brigham Young.
							AGNESS S. ARMSTRONG


﻿SOMETHING NEW.—HOW IS IT?

	A writer to the “Woman’s Journal”, and
a believer in the superiority of woman over
man says:
	“It would seem that this wonderful body
of our sex, more delicate, more beautiful,
more highly organized, more sensitive, yet
with more vitality, and endowed with the
wonderful power, the divine power of giv-
ing life to other human beings, should be
treated with profound reverence at all
times.”
	When I read this sentence the question
voluntarily arose in my mind, “How has
woman been endowed with that wenderful,
that divine power!” True, it is not em-
phatically stated that she possesses this pow-
er independently; but if the expression is
not intended to converge to such a doctrine,
it does not explain itself. If such a theory
can be sustained, what a strange, new and
delightful period is dawning for woman!
She has, indeed, gained a superior and su-
perhuman basis, and need not trouble her-
self any further about good husbands, bad
husbands, or husbands of any kind. If the
time has come when she can obtain her
chief glory—“giving life to other human
beings”—independent of her inferior (!) as-
sociate—man—the world’s troubles must be
approaching a glorious “finis,” and every
one should be apprised of it. But how is
it?   Women out our way don’t understand
it.   We have not got so far along.  Would
“Shawanebeke,” who prays daily, “Thy
kingdom come,” and means “Woman Suf-
frage,” please explain? And as a compen-
-sation, I would politely and in sincerity
offer a few suggestions for her consideration, in
connection with the following true and very
sound sentiments from her own pen:
	“My daughter of fifteen asked me-to-day
why the good and sensible girls had so few
beaux, while the silly, over-dressed ones
could marry whom they pleased?  ‘Because
there are so few sensible men, my child,’
I answered. ‘Almost every superior woman
feels that in marrying she must accept her
inferior, ‘as men go’ now. Were it custom-
ary for her to select whom she would favor
with her love, she would choose more wisely;
and brainless men would not be so eligible
to matrimony as are now brainless girls.
When women assume this duty and privi-
lege, and when men are educated up to it,
there will be more and happier marriages.’
No one found it unfeminine in Victoria to
choose her own husband.”
	While it is an undeniable fact, that in
nearly every society in the known world,
“almost every superior woman feels that in
marrying she must accept her inferior 'as
men go’ now,” it is also a pleasing truth
that the great and good Creator hath pre-
pared a plan by which His intelligent and
pure and humble daughters may be more
fairly and equally provided for. A law by
which every true and noble woman may
claim the right of a virtuous and godly pro-
tector and companion for herself, and father
[new column]
for her children; making her own choice,
abiding by her own decision. That this or-
der should be misunderstood, ridiculed and
despised by the brainless and bigotted “fast
men” of the day, is not to be wondered at,
as it is calculated to deprive them of the
selfish and hateful abominations they de-
light to revel in. But that woman, with
her more chaste and elevated conceptions
of honor, sanctity and intelligence, should
proclaim against it is more surprising;
although I believe this to be merely the re-
sult of a lack of comprehension. And no
wonder women are blinded to their own in-
terests in this respect; they have been so
completely hoodwinked by the falsehoods
of corrupt and senseless braggarts.
							   NAIVETE.


﻿AMUSEMENTS FOR THE YOUNG.

	The importance of surrounding the young
with cheerful objects is seldom sufficiently
recognized. How often is the nursery the
dullest room in the house! It should be the
most cheerful, and have, both literally and
figuratively, a sunny aspect. All its con-
tents should have a lively air. The walls
should be painted of a bright color, and the
carpets be of a pattern noticeable by the dis-
tinctness of its figures or the warmth of its
tint. Colored pictures of a bright, objective
character large representations of animals—
dogs, horses and farm-yard, and groups of
ruddy boys and girls, elephants—cheerful
scenes of the field and playing and merry-
making, should be so hung on all sides as
to attract the sight and animate the spirits
of the little inhabitants of the nursery.
Their eyes and ears should be, as far as
possible, kept closed to all scenes and rela-
tions of horror and cruelty. The excite-
ment of the natural terror of the child at
darkness and solitude must be carefully
avoided, and no threats of summoning
ghosts, bogies, old witches or other mon-
sters, so fearful to the childish imagination,
should ever be permitted.
	With increasing age youth finds in the
companionship of its fellows the society
which is essential to its happiness and ac-
quires that knowledge of the traditionary
games of boyhood and girlhood which is an
endless source of gaiety and pleasure. The
top, ball, marbles, tag, leap-frog, hare and
hounds, prisoner’s base, and many other
amusements of the young, with their varied
inducements to active exercise of the body
and enlivening influences upon the animal
spirits, have a value to the child proved by
the experience of all time and all countries.
We regret, however, to learn that as they
involve a certain roughening and dirtying
of the hands, reddening of the face, and
disheveling of the hair, and unquestionable
wear and tear of the clothes, many parents
discountenance them. They are denounced
by the over-refined as the games of the rude
children of a rude age, and as not genteel
enough for the nicer offspring of modern
civilization! They undoubtedly afford the
young just that combination of mental ex-
citement and physical action in which they
delight, and from which both mind and
body receive so much benefit. We there-
fore decide, without much hesitation, in
the case of fine clothes vs. healthy and
happy children, leaving the fine clothes, as
the lawyers say, we believe, “to be cost for
damages.”
	Parents should not only encourage their
children to play these famous old games out
of doors, but make abundant provision for
the children’s amusement at home.—“Har-
per’s Bazar.”


﻿SENSIBLE RETRENCHMENT.

   Among the many rich points, finely dwelt
upon by Presidents B. Young and G. A.
Smith, in connection with the testimonies
borne by several of our prominent elders,
at the late two days’ conference held at Lo-
gan city, the importance of retrenchment
in cooking and in dress, was forcibly pre-
sented for the consideration of the sisters.
The use of unnecessary and injurious
beverages and tobacco was earnestly dis-
countenanced. Immorality in every form
was spoken against; and truth, virtue, be-
nevolence, and every attribute of a redeem-
ing and exalting nature, were strongly
vindicated. President Smith advised la-
dies, when cooking and arranging a dinner
of which friends were invited to partake,
not for each one to think she must excel
everybody else, and for this, “work herself
to death and have more dishes dirtied than
she could wash in a week.” Two or three
hours should be time enough in which to
prepare a good, respectable meal, and if her
company came filled with the Spirit of the
Lord, as they should do, it would be much
more satisfactory to them than if she
worked three days and went to unreason-
able expense to provide a sumptuous repast.
This is sound, wholesome advice, which
every woman in Utah should gratefully re-
ceive and profit by. When Abraham and
Sarah entertained Angels, instead of pud-
dings, pies, tarts and sauces, with tea or
coffee, they had bread and butter and milk,
and some veal for dinner. And when Jesus,
the Son of God, prepared a meal and invited
His disciples to dine with Him it is not re-
corded that He spread before them any
greater variety of dainties than broiled fish
and bread. Much wisdom is manifested by
a few of the Latter-day Saints in seeking
to adopt some of these simple and healthful
rules, and spending time, means and
strength, to better advantage than in, get-
ting up costly suppers. And it is to be
hoped that many more will follow the same
wise example. Much labor is performed by
the women of to-day to great disadvantage
and to no real purpose. Retrenchment in
almost every branch of industry could be
aptly applied without the least injury to art
or convenience and comfort. And thus
woman might secure much valuable time,
for gaining information herself, and impart-
ing it to her children, which now seems to
be unavoidably wasted in hard work.


﻿BOYS AT HOME.—An intelligent and
thrifty farmer says:	“But for the co-opera-
tion of boys I should have failed. The
eldest is near twenty-one, and the other
boys in the neighborhood have left their
parents—mine have stuck to me when I
most needed their services. I attribute this
result to the fact that I have tried to make
home pleasant for them. I furnished them
with attractive and useful reading; and
when night comes and the day’s work is
ended, instead of running with other boys 
to railroad stations and adjoining towns, 
they gather around the great lamp, and be-
come interested in their books and pa- 
pers.”


﻿IT IS the duty of every true woman to
look as beautiful as she can. It is her duty
to brighten and gladden the world with her
loveliness just as the flowers and trees, the
skies and waters gladden it. Her sense of
the beautiful should always and everywhere
be cultivated, for thus will her thoughts be
made pure, and her mind the home of sweet
and lovely things. Refinement comes of a
love of all things beautiful.—Ex.

﻿MISCELLANEOUS.
			   —
	TO TALK well and just enough is an ac-
complishment and wisdom possessed by
very few.
	EXTENSIVE beds of peat of the best qual-
ity have been discovered near Los Angeles,
on the line of the San Pedro railroad.
	A CONNECTICUT man rolls in wealth ac-
cumulated in the business of manufacturing
“genuine relics from the Holy Land.”
	YOU may fail to shine in the opinion of
others, both in your conversation and ac-
tions, from being superior as well as inferior
to them.
	THE London society for the prevention of
vice, has caused the suspension of the “Day’s
Doings.” A like society here could find
some, work to do.
	GRATITUDE is the great incentive to all
happiness. Without it, the wealthiest can-
not procure real enjoyment; with it, the
poorest cannot be miserable.
	WHAT we truly and earnestly aspire to
be, that in some sense we are. The mere
aspiration, by changing the frame of mind,
for the moment realizes itself.
	DURING the session of the Oregon legis-
lature two years ago, a minister preached
from the text “Thou shalt not steal.” Sev-
eral legislators, considering it a personal
insult, got up and left in high dudgeon.
	FASTIDIOUSNESS, az a general thing, iz a
holyday virtew, and i hav frequently notis-
sed that thoze individuals who are alwus
afrade they shall cum akrost sumthing hily
improper, are generally looking for it.—
 “Billings.”
	LOVE is said to be blind, but it is better
to believe that it voluntarily overlooks the
failings of its object, and, with more than
common sight, sees also the finer and more
obscure virtues which a casual observer
would never find out.
	WILLIAM LAWSON BARRY, of Nashville,
Tennessee, is the oldest printer in the Uni-
ted States, having just entered his ninety-
third year. He commenced learning his
trade in 1797, and set up Washington’s
obituary in 1799. Barry was born in Vir-
ginia, but has spent most all his life in Ten-
nessee and Kentucky, and has had a
considerable acquaintance with all of the
eminent men of those States. He is still
vigorous, and works at his trade occasion-
ally.
	KINDERHOOK, Illinois, possesses the
champion preacher. A few Sundays since
he had preached an eloquent sermon, and
at the conclusion exhorted his hearers to
embrace the opportunity presented. None
came forward, and after waiting some time,
the minister, raising his voice to the high-
est pitch, exclaimed: “Well, sit there, and
be .damned.” On the same Sunday he called
out to the janitor: “Open that window; I
don’t like to preach in a barrel with the
bung drove in.”
	AS MME. LUCCA is expected to visit us
during the coming year, the following esti-
mate of her powers and style, from an Eng-
lish journal,is of interest: “Madame Lucca’s
action is warmer and more tragic than that
of Nilsson. Her vocalization has more color,
passion and enthusiasm; is less a cold, con-
structive body of- beauty; fuller of pulse,
vividness and vitality; more sensuous and
living; less like a splendid statue of ice,
brilliant, scintillating, reflecting every
passion of lyrical drama, but possessed of
none. As a consequence, her style is more
sympathetic and exacts an equal sympathy
from the audience.”


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
				—
	STOKES is said to have spent $7,500 on his
trial.
	A seventeen-year-old rooster died lately
at Mobile, Ala.
	THE dog census of the United States is
set down at 21,000,000,000.
	THE value of the fruit product of Califor-
nia is estimated at $3,000,000.
	THE Isabel, a species of stone color, is
now the fashionable tint in London.
	A $3,000,000 hotel is to be erected in
London and run on the American plan.
	ENGLAND has 1,000,000 paupers, not one
in twenty of whom ever attended school.
	A FULL glass of water at bed time and
another just after rising, will often relieve
dyspepsia.
	A TEXAN cattle dealer is considered a
poor man if his drove does not number a
thousand head.
	AT THE old Mission of San Diego are 300
olive trees that are from eighty to one hun-
dred years old.
	SOME artesian wells 150 feet deep in Los
Angeles county are discharging live trout
two inches long.
	TWO twin boys in Warren county, Indi-
ana, aged 72 years, are tenderly cared for
by a mother of 96.
	ONE of the results of the civilization of
Japan is to leave 10,000 Buddhist priests
penniless and without employment.
	RECENT careful and scientific observa-
tions are said to show beyond a doubt that
the Colorado desert was once the bed of a
sea.
	THE dying gift of Bennett, of the New
York “Herald,” was $250,000 toward com-
pleting the Catholic cathedral in New York 
city.
	SIX thousand one hundred and seven
persons drank at the Cincinnati ice water
fountain during twenty-four hours last
week.
	THE tules or reed vegetation of the Cali-
fornia swamp land has been found to yield
fifty or sixty per cent, of paper pulp, equal
to that obtained from cotton.
	THE largest emigration to southern Min-
nesota ever known is now settling in the
frontier counties. It far exceeds the im-
mense immigration of 1866.
	DR. R. J. DODD, of the United States
navy, has given $4,000 to the Woman’s
Medical college of Pennsylvania, for the
establishment of the Hannah Matilda Dodd
Scholarship, in memory of his wife.
	CHICORY raising promises to become quite
an item in the valleys of the San Joaquin
and Calaveras rivers, Cal. The yield is said
to be fifteen to twenty tons per acre, and
the price $5 per ton. Chicory is used in
enormous quantities in adulterating coffee.
	THE Mikado of Japan can trace his pedi-
gree back for two thousand years. Queen
Victoria’s genealogy is traced back to the
foundation of the Saxon Heptarchy, A. D.
827, only a little more than one thousand
years, and the kings of France are reckoned
back about four hundred years earlier.
	AN insane man, who left his home in
Michigan about a year ago, has since been
traveling about under the impression that
he was somebody else, but retaining at the
same time a peculiar interest in his real self.
He would write home to his wife occasion-
ally, inquiring after her husband, being es-
pecially anxious to learn whether he had
“got over his crazy spell or not.”


﻿HOW IT IS DONE.
			     —
	Scene in library—gentleman busy writing
	—child enters :
	“Father, give me a penny.”
	“Haven’t got any—don’t bother me.”
	“But, father, I want it—something par-
ticular.”
	“I tell you I haven’t got one about me.”
	“I must have it! you promised me
one.”
	“I did no such thing! I won’t give you
any more pennies—you spend too many.
It’s all wrong. I won’t give it to you; so go
away!”
	Child begins to whimper.
	“I think you might give me one—it’s
real mean!”.
	“No! Go away—I won’t do it! So there’s
an end of it.”
	Child cries, teases, coaxes. Father gets
out of patience, puts his hand in his pocket,
takes out a penny and throws it at the child.
	“There! take it, and don’t come back
again to-day.”
	Child smiles, looks shy, and goes out con-
queror, determined to continue the struggle
in the afternoon, with the certainty of like
results.
	Scene in the street—two boys playing—
mother opens the door and trails one of
them, her own son.
	“Joe, come into the house right away.”
	Joe pays no attention.
	“Joe, do you hear me? If you don’t
come in right away, I’ll give you a whip-
ping!”
	Joe smiles and continues his play. His
companion is alarmed for him, and advises
him to obey.
	“You’ll catch it if you don’t go, Joe.”
“Oh! no—I won’t. She always says so—but
never does. I ain’t afraid!”
	Mother goes into the house greatly put
out, and thinks herself a martyr to bad chil-
dren.
	That’s the way, parents. Show your chil-
dren by example that you are weak, unde-
cided, and untruthful, and they learn aptly
enough to despise your authority and regard
your word as nothing. They graduate liars
and mockers, and the reaping of your sow-
ing will not fail.—Ex.


﻿IT IS not enough for a man to furnish his
family with comfortable food, clothing and
shelter. They have a claim on him above
those of physical necessities. If is not noble
in him to say, “I work hard to give you a
home, and I want you to stay there. Seek
your pleasures at home. I will not have
my family straggling here and there in the
world. Stay at home. I can see enough of
the world for all of us.” This kind of
tyranny makes a prison of home, which in-
stantly ceases to be the habitation of peace
or happiness. It would be so easy and. re-
spectable for such a man to take his family
out for a little variety and recreation, now
and then. Young people droop without a
sense of freedom within the bounds of pro-
priety and filial obedience. There are beau-
tiful influences and opportunities for improv-
ing the mind in the world, which are lost
by those who are forbidden ever to seek
them.


IT IS easy to say what ought not to be
thought of or mentioned, but when the
heart is full of long pent-up, bitter memo-
ries of abuse, neglect or tyranny, the seal
will sometimes drop from the lips : when a
sympathetic heart is near to offer a kind
word of comfort.


A “time-server”—a watch.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


TEA DRUNKARDS.
				   —
	Dr. Arlidge, one of the pottery inspectors
in Staffordshire, has put forth a very sen-
sible protest, says the “Lancet,” against a
very pernicious custom which rarely re-
ceives sufficient attention, either from the
medical profession or the public. He says
that the women of the working classes make
tea a principal article of diet instead of an
occasional beverage; they drink it several
times a day, and the result is a lamentable
amount of sickness. This is no doubt the
case, and, as Dr. Arlidge remarks, a portion
of the reforming zeal which keeps up such
a fierce and bitter agitation against intoxi-
cating drinks might advantageously be di-
verted to the repression of this very serious
evil of tea tippling among the poorer
classes. Tea, in anything beyond moderate
quantities, is as distinctly a narcotic poison
as is opium or alcohol. It is capable of ruin-
ing the digestion, of enfeebling and disor-
dering the heart’s action, and of generally
shattering the nerves. And it must be re-
membered that not merely is it a question
of narcotic excess, but the enormous quan-
tity of hot water which tea bibbers neces-
sarily take is exceedingly prejudicial both
to digestion and nutrition.—Ex.


THE authenticity of Stanley’s letters
concerning the finding of Dr. Livingstone,
is now generally conceded. Mr. Stanley is
a young man, not much over thirty, who is
well known in Colorado Territory and in
Omaha. There is a little romance connected
with his history, as it is alleged that he
fixed his affections on a young lady whose
name is not unfamiliar to the people of Salt
Lake, she having appeared on the boards of
the theatre here for a season; and that she
did not reciprocate his warmth of feeling.
It is said that as a result he became some-
what misanthropic and sought literary em-
ployment that weuld take him away from
familiar scenes and faces. He became cor-
respondent for the New York “Herald” in
Abyssinia during the British war against
the Emperor Theodore, and through his
correspondence the British government first
learned of the success of their arms and the
fall of Magdala. He was subsequently se-
lected to lead the “Herald” expedition to
search for Dr. Livingstone, and succeeded
in finding the great explorer at Ujiji in the
interior of Africa.

		WOMAN’S EXPONENT. 


		A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL. 

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.
	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!”

	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.
	
	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.

	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarte.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1  Copy one year,	     $2.00	  1 Copy six months.    $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months, 9.00

	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

  Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN’S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


Woman's Exponent.
VOL. I. 	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, SEPTEMBER 15, 1872	No. 8.﻿


NEWS AND VIEWS.
	Riots in Rhenish Prussia have followed
the expulsion of the Jesuits. More religion!
	The California State Fair commences
at Sacramento on the 19th and continues to
the 27th.
	The water supply of Albany is failing
and a milk famine threatens the city. A
steady flowing artesian well might avert
the danger.
	The Marquis of Lorne and other Eng-
lish noblemen take turns in preaching to
the London working classes; and they might
be much worse employed.
	With gold as the god of the world, aud
the “almighty dollar” so earnestly wor-
shipped, it is no wonder the question “Will
it pay?” is so often asked.
	A colored woman recently jerked her
arm out of joint in her religious fervor at a
camp meeting. Such exercises make
fashionable piety in some places.
	A new religious sect has sprung up in
Iowa, composed entirely of Germans, who
hold a community of property and live
under the prominent rules of the Paris Com-
munists.	
	Henry Wilson, Republican candidate
for Vice-President, admits he was a Know-
Nothing once, and, as a consequence, was
opposed to foreign born citizens holding
any office.
	The Geneva Board of Arbitration has
concluded-its labors, and the world may re-
pose in quietude for a season, the intermin-
able “Alabama” claims being at length
disposed of.
	Von Moltke sent to Thiers, with “his
compliments,” a copy of his history of the
Franco-German war. How the grim old
strategist must have chuckled at the sat-
urnine humor of such a present so for-
warded!
	Pere Hyacinth, the famous preacher
of Notre Dame, in Paris, has broken his
views of celibacy, as well as broken with
the Pope, and like Martin Luther has mar-
ried. Sensible Hyacinth! he may now
become a pere in reality.
	A Congress of the Internationalists sat
at the Hague early this month, and decided
that the general Council of the society
should be located in New York. The
struggle between capital and labor in this
country will ripen as a result.
	Maine election occurred on the 9th,
and the heaviest vote for years was polled.
The Republicans carried the State by a
largely reduced majority when compared
with the last vote given as heavy as the one
this year, and that was in 1868.
	The New York “Tribune” speaks of the
“Woman’s Journal” as being “conducted
by several very estimable ladies of both
sexes.” It has, therefore, the advantage
of a large number of other journals, that
are conducted by ancient grannies of the
masculine sex.
	Queen Victoria is a grandmother again.
The daughters of this august lady, like
their mother, have not learned the fashion-
able crime of murdering their unborn off-
spring. They pattern after a noble and
[next column]
virtuous mother, and make amiable and
matronly wives.
	Joseph Jefferson, so lately nearly blind
from the use of a powerful light which he
was accustomed to have play upon his face
in “Rip Van Winkle,” is again able to fol-
low his profession. A Iess brilliant light so
employed may save to the stage, for years,
one of its leading ornaments.
	An “Autobiography of Satan” has
been prepared for the press by Dr. Beard,
of England; but the learned doctor does
not claim that it is his own biography; he
only edits it. The inference may be drawn
that he was on good terms with the charac-
ter whose personal history is to be given to
the world.
	The re-election of Grant is advocated by
Wendell Phillips, who also admits that he
was in error when the mission of the Re-
publican party was said, by him, to he at
an end. The “national scold" may live to
acknowledge that he has committed other
blunders quite as serious as that one, or per-
haps more so.
	“Can Editors be manufactured?” is a
question now stirring certain journalistic
circles. Of course they can, if there is the
right material at hand to manufacture them
of. The difficulty now is that the average
material used is left in an unmanufactured
condition, and in working itself into shape
it often presents a strange mass of incon-
gruous development.
	In the potteries at Leeds, England, one
thousand women are employed, working
nine hours a day, at the rate of sixty cents
a week. Some of our charity-loving neigh-
bors, who wish to donate a portion of their
means for the relief of the “down-trodden
women of Utah,” might do good by making
up contributions for the aid and encourage-
ment of such female laborers, who are wear-
ing life away in semi-slavery, with, appar-
ently, no hope of brighter days.
	Berlin has been in a blaze of military
glory and frantic enthusiam over three Em-
perors meeting in cordial greeting and im-
perial pomp. How many of those whose
voices called forth echoes from the blue
vault above, in response to their thoughtless
and wild cheers, will find bloody graves as
a result of the crowned heads of Germany,
Russia and Austria thus meeting? Am-
bition continues its promptings, and what
diplomacy cannot reach in the way of con-
quest, the cannon and the bayonet will be
invoked to accomplish.
	“Agassiz thinks the jelly fish has an
Immortal soul” sneeringly says an exchange.
And why not? Will some sage tell where
immortality terminates in the animal crea-
tion? And why should man be supposed
to be the only being endowed with immor-
tality? What kind of a heaven would it
be where men and womon would be the
only living creatures that once inhabited
earth? And what about the horses and
other members of the brute creation seen
in vision by John the Divine as inhabiting
eternity? Modern theology is a strange
bundle of crudities.
	“The appointment by the Mormons of
missionaries to evangelize Europe and the
United States looks very much like as if
[next column]
the tail was trying to wag the dog,” says
“Hearth and Home.” Just so; and so it
was felt some eighteen hundred and odd
years ago, by the pharisaic Hebrews and
the philosophers of pagan Rome, concern-
ing a few ignorant fishermen of Galilee.
Yet now those same fishermen are canon-
ized by the most powerful nations of the
world. Time is a great revolutionizer, and
that which is most derided to-day may be-
come in a few years most popular and
sought after.
	A convention of extreme, or Bourbon,
Democrats has been held in Louisville, Ky.,
to nominate still other men as candidates to
“rule over us,” if the people would so will
it. They rejected Greeley and repudiated
the action of the Democracy at Baltimore;
and, after nominating Charles O’Conor, of
New York, and John Quincy Adams, of-
Massachusetts, for President and Vice
President, their candidates declined the
honor, while endorsing the movement; and
the convention dissolved determined to vote
for their chosen ones whether they accepted
or not. Politicians do many knavish things,
many dishonorable things, and from this
Louisville convention it is fair to infer they
are capable of many exceedingly silly
things.
	The “Golden Age” is authority for say-
ing that the Republican Executive Com-
mittee at Washington has issued a confiden-
tial circular to certain prominent members
of the Woman’s Rights party, to organize
Grant and Wilson clubs and thus work for
the re-election of General Grant. And
there are among them those who will snap
at the bait, although the party, with a large
working majority in Congress, could easily
have passed an Amendment to the Consti-
tution, any time in the last four years, to
make woman suffrage a part of the supreme
law of the land, and have sent it to the va-
rious States for ratification. Rut they did
not; and the quasi recognition of woman’s
worth, contained in the Philadelphia plat-
form, was no more than a shallow attempt
to secure the influence of the Woman’s
party in the Presidential campaign.
	The steamer “Metis” struck by a
schooner off Rhode Island,and sinking, with
a loss of life very great but its extent not
fully known; the burning of the “Bien-
ville” off Central America, between thirty
and forty more souls being hurried into
eternity; and the burning of the “Ameri-
ca” in Chinese waters, with a loss of over a
million dollars in property and eighteen
lives; are some of the marine disasters re-
corded within the past two weeks. We
boast of the advance made in science and
art, of how the elements can be controlled
or resisted, what wonderful appliances are
ready to prevent accidents, casualties and
disasters; yet the sum total of the loss of
life and property from such causes would
stagger belief if computed in the aggregate.
But mankind in their high-mindedness and
pride will not believe that the Eternal is
speaking to thorn in signs of the times, with
the elements as His agents, fulfilling the
prophecies and pointing to the near future
when He will make bare His arm in power
for the accomplishment of His fore-ordained
purposes.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿THE BLESSINGS OF TO-DAY.

Strange, we never prize the music
	Till the sweet-voiced birds are flown;
Strange that we should slight the violets,
	Till the lovely flowers are gone;
Strange that summer skies and sunshine
	Never seem one-half so fair,
As when winter’s snowy pinions,
	Shake the white down in the air.

Lips from which the seal of science,
	None but God can roll away,
Never blossomed of such beauty,
	As adorns the mouth to-day;
And sweet words that freight our memory,
	With their beautiful perfume,
Come to us in sweeter accents,
	Through the portals of the tomb.

Let us gather up the sunbeams,
	Lying all around our path;
Let us keep the wheat and roses,
	Casting out the thorns and chaff;
Let us find our sweetest comfort,
	In the blessings of the day,
With a patient hand removing
	All the briers from our way.


﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
	Mrs. Amy L.Bigler, Secretary of the F.R.
Society of Nephi, Juab county, sends us the
following, dated August 29th:
	It is now four years since the organiza-
tion of our Female Relief Society. Since
that time we have been trying to do all we
could to assist the poor, and to do our duty
in blessing those who have required a word
of counsel from us. We have one of the
best of Presidents, Mrs. Amelia Golds-
brough. We have not handled much means,
but have tried to do the best we could with
what we have had. We own one house and
lot in the north part of town, a building
spot on Main street, in the centre of town,
and a good portion of material for building
on the lot; have the foundation laid, and
expect to put up a good house this fall.
Have given $400.13 to the poor, and $30 to
the Emigration fund. Have received in all,
since the Society was organized, $836.63.

MINUTES OF A SPECIAL MEETING OF THE F. R. SOCI-
				ETIES OF PROVO.
			Meeting House, Sep. 5, 1872.
	Present, President A. O. Smoot, Bishops
Tanner, Scott, Follett and Johnson. Presi-
dent Smoot was chosen to preside.
	After the usual ceremonies of singing
and prayer, President Smoot said he met
Sister E. R. Snow in Salt Lake city, can-
vassing for students in view of opening a
school of medicine and surgery for the in-
struction of females; that those possessed
of nerve, energy and ambition for such a
laudable life, might have opportunities of
qualifying themselves. He had promised
his support by laying the matter before the
ladies of Provo, which he proposed to do
to-day. He suggested that suitable persons
be selected to attend that school. This
idea was already in practical operation in
the east, and why should not the women
of Utah take measures for gaining a knowl-
edge of the physical organization of the
human system, especially that of the fe-
males, that the ladies of our community
might receive, when necessary, medical or
surgical treatment from those of their own
sex? He said, furthermore, that the right
of franchise would prove a great blessing
in aiding the ladies to assert their right to
hold public office, and was anxious to see
them take an interest in all branches of co-
operation, that principle being one of the
stepping-stones to the Order of Enoch. It
was also their privilege to assist in emigrat-
ing the poor by enriching the P. E. Fund.
He alluded to the duties of the F. R. Socie-
ties in their administrations to the poor,
[next column]
and said theirs was a noble calling of love
and mercy, and in their appointments they
receive a portion of the priesthood of God.
He also touched on the responsibilities of
maternity in connection with the knowledge
of the gospel and its covenants. The
mother has the molding of the character of
her offspring; and mothers should realize
this in its keenest sense. The immorality
and waywardness of youth to a great ex-
tent are owing to a false sympathy or a
lack of the performance of their duty, by
parents. The Sunday school was one of the
great helps which should be encouraged as
a safe-guard by parents. The influence of
woman should be exerted for promoting the
happiness and good of the community in
which she lives. He continued his exor-
tation by urging the necessity of subscrib-
ing, “one and all,” for the EXPONENT, it
being a powerful advocate for the principles
of our religion. The “Juvenile Instructor”
was another medium through which our
youth would receive the principles of life
and salvation, and should be in every family.
He also solicited all to take an interest in
the coming Territorial Fair.
	Bishops Tanner, Johnson and Scott fol-
lowed in brief but pointed remarks on the
welfare of woman, her duties and responsi-
bities, and exhorted each of them to study
principles which would save and prepare
them for their true position, as help-mates
to man in the redemption of the human
race.
	It was moved by Mrs. R. A. Holden and
seconded by Mrs. M. J. Tanner, that Mrs.
L. W. Kimball forward a report of this
meeting to the Exponent for publication.
	Adjourned sine die.
					L. W. KIMBALL,
							Secretary.
	A meeting of the First Ward F. R. Soci-
ety was held on Thursday, September 5th;
sisters Eliza R. Snow, Zina D. Young and
others being present by invitation. After
singing and prayer, some excellent minutes
of a previous meeting were read. Mrs.
Z. D. Young first occupied a portion of time,
in encouraging the sisters of that ward, and
exhorting them to faithfulness and dili-
gence in learning and keeping the com-
mandments of God. Miss E. R. Snow then
addressed the meeting, advancing and
dwelling upon many beautiful and useful
subjects in a highly instructive and im-
pressive manner. Appropriate and inter-
esting remarks were also made by Brother
Mitchell, Bishop Joseph Warburton, Mrs.
Wheeler and Miss L.  L. Greene. The
meeting was then adjourned by singing,
“For the strength of the hills we bless thee,
Our God, our fathers’ God;” and benedic-
tion by Elder Mitchell. 
	Mrs. Mary Chase is President, and Eliza-
beth Thayne, Secretary, of the First Ward
Society. There are quite a number of poor
families belonging to the ward, who are
blessed and solaced by the energies and
good feelings and acts of the Society.
	At the meeting of the F. R. Society of
the Second Ward, in this city, held Septem-
ber 6th, Mrs. Ann McGregor presided in
the absence of the President Mrs. Necolena
Olsen. Meeting opened with singing, and
prayer by Mrs. Sarah Wheeler.  Testimo-
nies were borne to the truth of the gospel,
and business was transacted in a spirited
manner. Donations were handed in for the
purpose of assisting in finishing off the
ward school house, which, added to the col-
lection made at the previous meeting,
amounted to $34, according to the report of
the Secretary, Mrs. Jane Rickard. The So-
[next column]
ciety is united, without exception, in the
work of the advancement of the cause of
truth, and they rejoice in administering to
the wants of the needy and in comforting
the bereaved.
				OGDEN, Sept. 4, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	Since my last, of July 20th, the Relief
Society of Ogden has held two very inter-
esting meetings. For some months past
our former President, Sister Harriet C.
Brown, has desired and requested to resign
her office, which was much regretted by
all, yet it was not that she desired to cease
her labors or her interest for the Society.
By invitation President Richards, Bishop
Herrick and Counsellor Thompson attended
and assisted to complete our reorganization,
as well as to teach us. Sister Jane S.
Richards was unanimously elected Presi-
dent, and she selected Sisters H. C. Brown
and Sarah Herrick for her counsellors, who
were accepted and sustained with the great-
est unanimity of feeling. We are not
troubled with division of sentiments, al-
though there are some who do not seem to
appreciate the value of our institution, as
the Prophet Joseph and other leading
Elders in the church must have done. Still,
we hope to see the growing interest increase
and extend until all the truly worthy are
engaged with us. On the occasion of the
setting apart of our sisters to their new
callings, we were favored with much very
interesting and profitable instruction from
the presiding brethren who met with us.
President Richards after telling us much
we ought to do, exhorted the Society to be
lively and energetic,and not to be so afraid of
doing wrong as to not dare to do right.
Bishop Herrick expressed himself much
pleased with the instructions given, and
said he was only sorry all the members of
the Societies in the county were not present
to hear them, and profit by them. He felt
to bless the Society and all God’s people.
	Sister Beckington and counsellors, from
Marriott settlement, contributed to the in-
terest of the meeting by cheering remarks.
	Sister Richards expressed herself anxious
to do good in her new position, and asked
the forbearance of the sisters towards any
apparent shortcomings on her part. Sister
Brown felt better than ever in the condition
of the Society, and the work that is before
us. Our meeting, adjourned for two weeks.
			AMELIA M. FRODSHAM,
						Secretary.


YATE, the present primate of the Church
of England, and Manning, the famous Ro-
man Catholic archbishop of Westminster,
were both pupils, forty years ago, to a cler-
gyman named Cheese, who still lives in
England. It must be gratifying to the
aged tutor to now see the fruits of his by-
gone labors. A young and gifted mission-
ary belonging to the Church of Jesus Christ
of Latter-day Saints, last winter met, for
the first time in several years, the aged
elder who, he said, “taught him his letters.”
Tears came into the eyes of the gray-haired
man when he spoke of his former pupil,
that evening, in the family circle; partly
perhaps with the memory of what a little
“try patience” he once had to put up with.


THE telegraph which daily announces
enormous failures, seems to admonish com-
mercial men of an approaching crisis, which
gives apprehenshions of no very pleasing
results; for under the present maladmini-
stration of Government officials, a rather
calamitous financial storm is pending,
which threatens destruction to many.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿THE HAND OF GOD.

			TO A BEREAVED MOTHER. 
Dear sister, the evils which life’s pathway strew,
Are striving for ever the good to outdo;
They vex and torment us, afflict and bereave,
And in every manner bewilder and grieve;
They load us with burdens we scarce can sustain,
They tempt us with pleasures or torture with
	pain!
Whoever undaunted would patiently stand,
Must see and acknowledge the Great ruling Hand.

Ag’d friend, I can sympathize deeply with you :
Though young, I have had some experience too;
I have tasted, though lightly, of life’s bitter cup,
And can feel for my fellows when sadly they sup:
Your late, sudden loss is a sorrowful one—
Hereft in old age of a dutiful son;
Yet wounded and rack'd, bowing low to the sod,
'Tis good to acknowledge the kind Hand of God.

May you answer at last, “Though worn and dis-
	tressed,
With reliance unshaken still onward I’ve pressed;
Though tempted and tried, yet my heart hath not
	fail-d,
For good o’er the evil within me prevailed;
The thick c ouds of darkness were forced to de-
	part;
Warm, beautiful sunshine stole into my heart;
And there, with the balm and the chastening rod,
I saw the just Hand of a merciful God.”
									L. L.


﻿HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
	IT IS not so material what you say as how
you say it. And this rule works famously
in other respects, as well as in regard to
speaking. Particularly is it adapted to
cookery. As some people have the happy
art of saying almost anything and having
it sound well, while others, less gifted in
that direction, can hardly express any
thought in a pleasing manner; so some
cooks are extraordinarily fortunate in hav-
ing a way of fixing up good and tasty meals
out of a very little, while others cannot
make a palatable dish of any kind, though
they may have on hand every luxury that
could be desired for the purpose. The best
cooks are not those alone who can arrange
the greatest variety of dishes, but those
who can prepare their food, no matter what
it is, in the neatest, sweetest, and most
wholesome style. Good sweet bread and
butter alone make a far better repast than
many rich and dainty dishes improperly
prepared; and no woman, whatever her
other attainments may be, can be considered
really a good housewife, who cannot make
these two articles. Neatness and economy
should characterize all our domestic con-
cerns. Prudence does not show a lack of
generosity, but quite the reverse; but ex-
travagance and waste are next to dishonesty
and should, at all times, be carefully guarded
against. And for the sake of the children,
great pains should be taken by mothers, to
have such food on hand as is suited for their
age and physical development.
	A SENSIBLE mother makes the following
remarks upon the physical treatment of
children:
	“The physical treatment of children is
important, but simple, if one wishes to
make it so. The main points are, bathing,
regularity of eating and sleeping hours,
simplicity of food, and out-of-door exercise.
	The habit of daily bathing should not be
suspended when a child is a few months
old. It is very little trouble, and, done
handily, takes little time. My experience
has been that it is not best to bathe a child
in entirely cold water. When he is put for
the first time in a bath-tub, the water
should be of such a temperature that he
may not perceive the transition from the
air to the water. I have often heard mothers
say that their children could not be bathed;
that they screamed as soon as they touched
[new column]
the water. There are ordinarily, but two
causes for this: either that the child was
not introduced to the bath-tub early enough
in his existence, or that the temperature of
the water was such as to shock him. When
your baby is thoroughly accustomed to the
tub, you can gradually reduce the tempera-
ture of the water, until, after a few months,
you can wash him in almost cold water.
	A child should eat regularly and simply,
but should have some variety of food. I
have found that a child soon gets tired of
one article, if confined to it. Bread and
milk, bread and butter, different forms of
toasted bread, simple preparations of corn
flour, rice boiled in milk, and roasted pota-
toes make a sufficient variety. Give your
child plenty of exercise in the open air; if
you live in a city, all I can say is, do the
best you can; but if you live in the coun-
try, there is no excuse for the child not hav-
ing as much exercise as is needful. If you
have a garden, then, in summer, leave
him out all day, if he enjoys it; in winter,
wrap him up, and send him out in moder-
ate and pleasant weather.
	Never mind about his getting dirty at
play. His face will be kept pretty clean if
he spends enough time in the open air to
prevent his taking bad colds, and if you do
not allow him to eat between meals.
	It is somewhat trying to see your little
boy or girl reveling in the midst of a pile
of dirt, especially when you remember that
Mrs. Herbert or Mrs. Fortescue, whose chil-
dren always look as if they had just issued
from a bandbox retirement, may possibly
call, and gaze with virtuously reproving
eyes; but never mind that. You are living
for your child; you know what is best for
its health and happiness, and do not allow
yourself to be moved from the course which
you know to be right by any false fear or
pride.”


﻿BEAUTIFUL REMINISCENCE.
	The following sentiments were expressed
by a Scandinavian sister, in the 14th Ward
Assembly Rooms, on Saturday afternoon,
August 31:
	“I cannot express my feelings as I would
like to do; but when I hear you talk of the
influence which mothers have over the
minds of their children, I can hardly repress
the tears that will come into my eyes, for I
remember my mother! And her wise coun-
sel is stamped upon my heart; and has ever
been, through all the trials and temptations
of my life, a source of comfort and of re-
straint. Although I was not more than
seven years old when I had to leave my
mother, I remember when she used to say
to me, ‘Don’t talk so much, my daugther;
the Angels don’t talk so much. Don’t
laugh loud; it is not good to laugh loud.’
And many other instructions which she
gave to her children are fastened on my
memory, and have seemed to be a safe-
guard to me in many circumstances through
which I have passed. I, therefore, know
that the words and examples of mothers
have a great weight with their children, in
forming their determinations and practices
through life. I know it is from those early
impressions, formed upon my mind, that I
was prepared to receive the light of the true
gospel; and I want to live to do something
for my mother. I am thankful that I know
the truth, and I shall never cease to praise
God and my mother!”


MISS Alice Bird has twice been invited
to a professorship in the Iowa Wesleyan
University.


﻿FLOWERS BY THE WAYSIDE.

	ONE bad example may spoil many good
precepts.
	THE love of praise should ever be pre-
served under proper subordination to the
principle of duty.
	GLORY is well enough for a rich man, but
it is of very little consequence to a poor man
with a large family.
	TOO much asseveration gives a ground of
suspicion. Truth and honesty have no need
of loud protestations.
	THOUGH often blighted, true affection
never dies, but will glow brightly over the
graves of envy and rivalry.
	HE who treats men ingenuously, and con-
verses kindly with them, gains a good
esteem with very little expense.
	SOCRATES, though primarily attentive to
the culture of his great mind, was not neg-
ligent of his external appearance.
	OF ALL the unhappy deserters from the
path of science, none seem less able to re-
turn than the followers of indolence.
	TRUTH has force, reason authority, and
justice power; but they are without lustre if
the most graceful way and manner be want-
ing.
	WHEN we reflect upon the many dis-
tresses which abound in human life, where
so much is suffered in common, little room
is left for envy.
	EVERY day hath its pains and sorrows,
but if we look impartially around us, we
shall find that every day hath also its
pleasures and joys.
	THE greatest benefits we ever do our-
selves is in securing the greatest amount of
real happiness, which is only done by doing
good to our fellow creatures.
	IN judging of others we should always
think the best, employing the spirit of char-
ity and candor; but in judging of ourselves,
we ought to be exact and severe.
	THE hope of future happiness is a per-
petual source of consolation to the good;
under trouble it sooths their minds, amidst
temptations it supports their virtue.
	ART thou poor? Show thyself active and
industrious, peaceable and contented. Art
thou wealthy? Show thyself beneficent
and charitable, condescending and humane.
	WITH all the troubles and cares which
mortals have to encounter here, if we look
for good instead of ill, we shall find, in
nearly all conditions in life, there is more
cause for gratitude than for regret.
	THE pursuits of sensual pleasures are not
those which afford in remembrance the
most rational satisfaction; we recollect with
most pleasure the innocent, virtuous, and
honorable parts of our past lives.
	WHERE good is to be administered, it is
better to give than to receive; but where
wrong comes in, it is better to receive than
to inflict an injury; and if the perpetrator
has a conscience he is sure to suffer most.
	THE palace of virtue has, in all ages, been
represented as placed on the summit of a
hill, the ascent of which can only be per-
formed by labor, where difficulties are to be
surmounted, and a conductor is needed to
direct and aid our steps.
	THE cost of a change from heavy to light
clothing, made by a brilliant young lady,
when attending a ball, on a damp, cloudy
evening, was first a sore throat and head-
ache, followed by torturing rheumatism,and
in less than a week, amounting to a burial
outfit.
