SPLINTERS.

	Engine makers at Berlin are on a strike...
Ex-empress Carlotta is fast sinking at Vi-
enna, with no hopes entertained of her re-
covery......Women blacksmiths abound in
Staffordshire......A young woman in New
Orleans is hopelessly blind, from drinking
water that passed through leaden pipes......
The Spanish war schooner “Condor,” was
burned at sea on her way to Hanover; crew
and passengers were saved......A daughter
of Senator Brownlow, of Tennessee, has
been placed in a convent for attempting to
elope with a stone cutter......Horace Gree-
ley’s brother, Barnes Greeley, is said, not to
be a Greeley man......Miss Emily Faithful,
editor of the “Victoria Magazine,” Eng-
land, is coming over to America, by invita-
tion, to see and lecture us.....The Sultan has
sent to the ex-Empress Eugenie a magnifi-
cent sapphire surrounded by large brilliants,
valued at $15,000......The French Prince
Imperial has obtained permission of Queen
Victoria to become a student of the Royal
Military Academy at Woolwich......Chicago
has a Scandinavian population of 43,000......
An eccentric South Bend beauty carries her
husband’s hod when sickness disables him...
The Arch Duke Charles, brother of the Em-
peror of Austria, is soon to be married to
the Princess Maria, youngest daughter of
King Ferdinand II, who is seventeen years
old......Spurgeon is coming to America......
Hugh Stowell Brown, England’s most cele-
brated Baptist pulpit orator, is lecturing in
the east.....Two thousand one hundred and
fifty-three California subscribers to the At-
lantic and Pacific railroad stock have made
the subscription aggregate $1,528,300......
Secretary Fish is delighted at the progress
of the Geneva arbitration......Greeley ac-
cuses the administration of playing strategy
to defeat his election......Yellow fever pre-
vails as an epidemic in Carthagenia, South
America......At Albany one bushel of pota-
toes is worth as much as two of wheat......
Two Fort Stevens girls paddled a small boat
from the Fort to Astoria, a few days ago......
Cholera is raging in Laeore and other places
in British India......General bankruptcy
threatens the planters of Alabama, owing
to the ruin of their cotton crops by the boll
worm......The Illinois “Straights,” in their
Springfield convention, adopted resolutions
and uttered bitter speeches against the sale
of the party at Baltimore to Greeley......
One thousand revolutionists are reported
still in arms in Sinaloa, Mexico......At Lin-
coln, the capital of Nebraska, the Democrats
coalesced in convention and nominated a
mixed State ticket, with Lett (Dem.) for
Governor......Proofs have been laid before
the United States Commission at Browns-
ville, Texas, of the complicity of officers of
the Mexican government in murders and
robberies on the Texan border......One of
the most opulent as well as agreeable ladies
at Saratoga, this season, is the widow of
General John H. Morgan, the famous
raider......The President of the California
State University receives a salary of $10,000
in gold, a year, a house and other things
for comfort......Roasted coffee is said to be
one of the most powerful of deodorizers,
actually destroying noxious animal and
vegetable effluvia......The lumbermen of
Maine predict that the forests in that State
will not last more than five years longer.
This year, the lumber is estimated at 700,-
000,000 feet......Iron springs have been dis-
covered at Long Branch and promise to add
to the popularity of that, already, popular
summer resort......The Emperor of Abys-
sinia has presented the Jardin d’ Acclimata-
tion, Paris, with six magnificent zebras,
[new column]
valued at L120 each......Baltimore has a
water famine and is about to spend $3,000,-
000 to remedy it......The electric current
passes through the Atlantic cable at a velo-
city of from seven thousand to eight thou-
sand nine hundred miles per second......Cal-
ifornia is making her first export of leather.
......Mark Twain will travel through Eng-
land, Ireland and Scotland......U. S. Grant,
junior, has gone to study in Hanover......
Tne mayor of Yeddo is in this country......
A Jewish temple, costing $60,000, is nearly
completed in Milwaukie, Wisconsin.......
Erie, Pa., was nearly destroyed by fire re-
cently......A part of the cathedral of Canter-
bury has been burned......Arthur Helps, the
author, is to be knighted......Baron Roths-
child, of Frankfort, is going to the Holy
Land......French soldiers are to be instruct-
ed in singing......The Countess de Blau-
cherry fought as a common soldier at a
battle near Orleans, during the late French
war, and has received the cross of the Le-
gion of Honor......Americans are crowding
to Baden-Baden this season. Gambling will
be prohibited there after the close of this
year......Powers, the sculptor, has been
made a Knight of the Brazilian Order of
the Rose......The Grand Duke of Hesse
Darmstadt is to marry an opera dancer......
The Crown-Princess Victoria, of Prussia,
daughter of Queen Victoria, offers a prize
of $8,000 for the best essay on advancing
the material prosperity of workingwomen....
Carlist troubles have again broken out in
Spain......Cluseret is preparing a history of
the Paris Commune......A new process of
preserving meat and vegetables has been
communicated to the French Academy of
Sciences.


﻿THE TERRITORIAL FAIR.

	The Board of Directors of the Deseret Agri-
cultural and Manufacturing Society are busily
engaged preparing for the approaching Fair.
Hon. A.P. Rockwood has been placed in charge
of the Fair grounds; Messrs. Henry Dinwoodey
and George Naylor are superintending the erec-
tion of stalls; and the work of preparation is
being actively pushed forward. The place in
which the exhibition will be held is the 13th
Ward Assembly Rooms, a large and commodi-
ous building, and the same in which the last
Fair, that of 1869, was held. In the rear,
ground has been secured for the display of
stock, which is expected to be very large; and
altogether the prospects are that the exhibition
will be, in every sense, a credit to the Terri-
tory.
	People see here and there a specimen of su-
perior work in some branch of industry; ele-
gant Utah-made furniture, an excellent fabric
of cloth, a piece of embroidery that would
compare with the best specimens from Scot-
land or Ireland, straw-work outvieing any im-
ported; or some other article showing the
artistic skill and cultivated taste which many
Utah workmen and workwomen possess; yet
the fact is soon forgotten; add even if it were
remembered it would give but a faint idea of


what is done or can be done in the Territory.
But when we have an exhibition of the work
of skilled hands from many parts of the Terri-
tory, the eye can take in at a glance an amount,
of knowledge that a volume of descriptive
writing would scarcely convey, in regard to
the growth and development of the country,
that in less than a quarter of a century has
been wrested from a barren wilderness.
	We need not urge upon the ladies of the Ter-
ritory to exert themselves that the Fair may
be a great success so far as their special depart-
ments are concerned, satisfied that the circular
of the Board of Directors, addressed to the Fe
male Relief Societies, which was published in
the last number of the EXPONENT, will meet a
gratifying response. Yet it would have been
better if the circular could have been given to
the public a month earlier, so that ladies could
have had more time and opportunities for the
selection of materials and arrangement of dif-
ferent specimens of home manufacture. The
length of time allowed is hardly sufficient for
the production of many articles of taste and
skill, especially for women with families to
look to, which must occupy a large portion of
their time.


﻿OUR FAITH.

	It is customary for many people to speak
slightingly and contemptuously of the faith held
by the Latter-day Saints. Some are pleased
to call it absurd, others declare it is monstrous.
Are they acquainted with it? We think not.
But they seem to think it sufficient that it is a
different faith from that which they believe.
How modest mankind are, generally speaking!
They are willing to concede that every person
who thinks or believes in the same theories,
and holds the same opinions that they do, is a
sensible, intelligent individual. And, as  a
matter of course, they must be very sensible,
intelligent people; right, indeed, in all matters
of belief or opinion, and everybody that differs
with them must be wrong. This is humanity
as we generally find it. Believers in Christi-
anity, as it has been modernized, are willing
to concede intelligence and the possession of
sensible religious views to other believed in
Christianity, so long as opinions are expressed
in general terms and may, by liberal construc-
tion, harmonize broadly with their own; but
let them narrow down to details, and the be-
liever in saving election will manifest positive
hate against one that has faith in the free will
doctrine; the unitarian and trinitarian will de-
nounce each other's pet opinions in the strong-
est terms; and the Protestant and Roman
Catholic will show a detestation each of the
faith held by the other, that has in the past
made myriads of victims for the rack, the
fagot, and the fiendish instruments of torture
with which one set of “Christians” endeavored
to send other “Christians” to a hell of endless
torments, because they would not travel
heavenward in a road marked out by mortals
as ignorant as themselves.
	This is religious intolerance. Every person's
religious opinions appear sensible, rational and
intelligent to that person; but to others they
may appear absurd. We have no hesitation
in saying that the beliefs of many of those who
sneer contemptuously at our faith, appear to
us absurd, contrary to the revelations of God,
and exhibiting a woeful lack of that knowledge
which gives its possessor power with the
Heavens and over the imperfections that flesh
is heir to. Yet we do not sneer at them; why
should we? It would be better to teach such
persons correct principles if they would hearken;
though too many are lifted up in their own


imaginations, and, full of the vanity which
springs from ignorance, will not be taught.
	Our faith is dear to us. It is no blind belief,
no ignorant fanaticism. It has been intelligently
embraced as satisfactory, to our reasoning pow-
ers. It has brought with it conviction of its
truth that no barren theory could bring; and
testimonies of its divine origin that no causu-
istry nor sophistry can overturn. It has buoyed
up the Latter-day Saints to the accomplish-
ments of works that have made their name
famous in every civilized country; and the
Lord of Hosts has stamped it with the impress
of His divine seal, that those who examine it
with clear gaze, In the light of eternal intelli-
gence, can see the sign manual under which it
has been sent forth for the salvation of a world.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	THE HOME  Missionaries continue their labors
throughout the Territory with excellent re-
sults.
	ONE THOUSAND tickets of admission to the
Territorial Fair have been printed by order of
the Board of Directors of the D. A. & M. Society.
	WILL OUR friends exert themselves to bring
in increased subscription lists for the EXPO-
NENT during the approaching Fair and Semi-
Annual Conference?
	THE EXPONENT is a woman’s paper, and we
would be pleased to have still more contribu-
tions from ladies who sympathize with its ob-
jects and desire its success. We hope this
general invitation will be accepted in the place
of private and personal ones.
	ELDER BRIGHAM YOUNG, junior, of the
Twelve Apostles, has been presented to the
people, of Cache county, in conference assem-
bled, as president of Cache and Rich counties,
and the appointment has been sustained by
their unanimous vote and united prayers.
	HONS. GEORGE A. SMITH, Wilford Woodruff
and George Q. Cannon and A. M. Musser, Esq.,
have gone to California to attend the State
Fair, as a delegation from the Deseret Agricul-
tural and Manufacturing Society. Their visit
will be of value to the industries of Utah.
	THE VOTE of this Territory at the last gen-
eral election, held August 5th of the present
year, was 22,913; and yet Utah is refused a
State government! Probably the fact that Mr.
Cannon, the People’s candidate, received 20,969
against 1,942 for his opponent, may account in
part for its continued condition of tutelage.
	THE PRIMARY and intermediate department
of the University of Deseret opened on Septem-
ber 2nd, with Miss Mary E. Cook as principal.
It gives us much pleasure to speak in the
warmest commendation of this lady’s abilities
as a practical instructor and teacher of large
experience. The department has about two
hundred pupils, with room for more, in the
Social Hall in this city, where the classes
meet.
	A FEW days ago the ground was dedicated
and broken for the water-works of this city,
Alderman Groo, of the City Council, offering
the dedicatory prayer, and Mayor Wells turn-
ing the first shovelful of earth, followed by
President Young also moving part of the soil.
We chronicle the fact for several reasons.
First, these are the first city water-works com-
menced in the Territory; second, they mark the
growth, of the city and the large increase of
population; and, third, that at the inception
of all public improvements, those for what are
termed secular uses as well as those for ecclesi-
astical purposes, the Latter-day Saints always
invoke the Divine blessing upon their labors,
[new column]
and dedicate their work, and the ground that
is to be devoted to such purposes, to the Al-
mighty under whose providential care they en-
joy all the blessings which they possess.
	A TERRIBLE tragedy occurred in this city on
September 3rd. One young man named Wil-
ford Tibbits, sought and met two others named
B. Whiting and W. Wilson, accused the for-
mer of traducing him, struck them both with
a heavy club, and was shot and killed by Wil-
son, who was subsequently arrested, examined
and discharged, on the ground that the homi-
cide was justifiable, being committed in self-
defense. The deed of blood was the result of
evil passions, revenge, and, it is said, intemper-
ance. How carefully mothers should guard
and guide their children in early years, to the
repression of outbursts of vindictive passions;
how wisely should the steps of the young be
directed, that their older years may be marked
by a course honorable in itself and winning
the esteem of the good, instead of one that
might bring shame and disgrace, for evil deeds!


﻿KITES AHOY!
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	You will confer a favor on the Telegraph
Companies by asking boys not to fly kites
in proximity to telegraph wires, as they
very often “foul” the lines and give serious
trouble, especially during wet weather. If
parents will urge their sons to keep away
from the wires with kites, and not to throw
rocks at the insulators, superintendents will
feel under obligations.
		Respectfully,
			A. M. MUSSER, Superintendent.
	Salt Lake City, Sept. 7, 1872.


﻿WOMAN’S VOICE.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Had I been more literarily inclined, when
my parents and teachers used to urge upon
me the necessity of studying my books, I
should now be able to write a more interest-
ing letter to your paper than, I fear, I shall
do this time. But, however, as so few of
our efficient writers seem disposed to enun-
ciate the principles of truth in their more
elaborate and refined manner, now that
there is a chance for it, I trust that I may
not be thought out of place in offering a few
simple statements in my humble way. Es-
pecially as you have extended a general in-
vitation to all the broken-hearted, (!) down-
trodden (!!) to speak for themselves and tell
their own grievances. I have a story to
tell, I have a testimony to bear, and may
the Spirit of the Lord direct me that it
may be done in meekness and sincerity, and
be productive of good.
	It is a very extraordinary thing for an
English factory girl to mingle much in
young and gay society, and preserve her
dignity, chastity and reputation; as, doubt-
less, many of my readers can testify. Yet
such was my experience for some years,
and, thank Heaven, I escaped without a
blemish; owing perhaps to my falling des-
perately in love, at an early age, with a
young man who was the soul of honor, but
who was engaged to another lady and, con-
sequently, although he was very fond of
me, could not marry me. When he left
our place, all the young people began to
wonder on whom I would next fasten my
affections; for they did not know, and
neither did I then, that I never should care
for any one else. Two years afterwards,
the Mormon Elders came and preached the
gospel to us. My widowed mother joined


the church, and it was to please her, five
years after, that I consented to emigrate to
America, and come to the valleys of the
Saints. My associates of the factory tried
by dissuading me, to prevent my coming.
They talked of the “poor, miserable Mor-
mons,” and their horrid practices. But
when they found I was determined to fulfil
my agreements with my mother, they made
me promise to write them the truth
when I got to Utah.
	I have done so. I have told them how I
attended meetings for a long time before I
became convinced that it was the truth of
Heaven which the elders taught. How six
months after I had been baptized, I hap-
pened to meet, at a general conference,
with my old love, of whom I had not heard
a word for more than seven years, and his
wife whom I had never met before. And
that they both still declare they are happier
for having found me, and can realize and
acknowledge that the hand of God has been
over us all for good. The factory girls still
ridicule and laugh at me, but I do not mind
that, since I know that but for their pride
and ignorance they would any of them own
that they envy me my dear, honored hus-
band and my baby.
							K.G.


﻿MOTHER-IN-LAW.

	It is really lamentable to see the little
feeling which is evinced by married people
in general, and gentlemen in particular, for
the mothers of their consorts. Derisive
songs are sung, light speeches are made and
laughter provoked in connection with the
name of mother-in-law, which should be
one of the most sacred. Gentlemen do not
seem to realize, while basking in the sun-
light of youth, beauty and wit, which give
the fascinating charms to girlhood, how
much those fair young creatures are indebt-
ed to the care-worn matrons, whose wishes
are so often met with  ridicule and consid-
ered whimsical, for all that makes them
gentle and lovely and their companionship
desirable. Or, that young girls, if they
live, are bound to become aged women in
their time, and most of them, doubtless,
even such despicable creatures as—mothers-
in-law!
	Because a mother is more solicitous for
the happiness and welfare of her daughter
than any other being knows how to be, even
the daughter herself; because she realizes
more fully the fearful dangers which sur-
round, in many different forms, the state of
beautiful maidenhood, her anxiety and con-
cern are frequently misconstrued into what
unthinking and injudicious people consider
undue restriction, and even a lack of sym-
pathy on the part of the mother for her
child. Some foolhardy young men think
that when they marry their sweethearts,
the mothers have no farther claim on them,
that they have relinquished all right to direct
or advise, if they ever had any; and they
take it as an undisguised affront for their
mother-in-law to make an occasional sug-
gestion in regard to the management of the
household affairs or the care of the babies.
	Well, young men must live and learn,
like other folks; but I wish some of them
would learn a little more readily about such
things, and not take lessons, so much at the
expense of the feelings of their wives and
their wives’ mothers. Young men, please
consider that if your mothers-in-law are
women, they have lived longer in the world
than you have, and may have gained some
little information that you have never
thought of, with all your wisdom.
							FRANK.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿THE NURSERY ELF.

Dear little feet, how you wander and wander,
	Little twin truants, so fleet;
Dear little head, how you ponder and ponder
	Over the things that you meet.

Dear little tongue, how you chatter and chatter
	Over your innocent joys.
Oh! but the house is alive with your clatter—
	Shaking, indeed, with your noise.

Can't you be quiet a moment, sweet rover ?
	Is there no end to your fun ?
Soon the “old sand man” will sprinkle you over
	Then the day’s frolic is done.

Come to my arms, for the daylight is dying,
	Closer the dark shadows creep;
Come, like a bird that is weary of flying,
	Come, let me sing you to sleep.


﻿TO MAKE HOME HAPPY.

	A smile, a gentle word of kindness, a
tidy room and baby with a face clean enough
to be kissed, should always be in readiness
for the reception of the head of a family
when he comes in fatigued and careworn,
from the pressure of the busy, daily routine
of the outside world. Let him feel that if
sympathy cannot be found elsewhere, it al-
ways exists for him at home. Let him see
that his goodness in planning and laboring
for the support and comfort of his dear ones,
is appreciated and gratefully acknowledged
by the performance of every little action of
love that can be thought of for his enjoy-
ment at home. Never let him meet with a
fretful and fault-finding spirit, under the
shades of that roof which should be the
covering of his heaven on earth. If there
are losses and crosses to be met, let the dif- 
ferent members of families help to meet
them bravely, instead of trying to discour-
age. If a husband and father does not, at
all times, pay just that attention to his
family that they think he should do, let it
be remembered that he has many things to
think of, many duties to perform, that they
are not acquainted with, and could, prob-
ably, render him no assistance in, were he
to take the time to explain to them, in-
stead of making a little show of negligence,
by not endeavoring to make his family un-
derstand all his business affairs. Let heads
of families realize that to such testimonials
they owe a reciprocity, and should spend as
much time as possible at home, and always
be possessed of the same good influence
they love to meet with.
								G.


﻿USE AND ABUSE OF DRESS.
			    _____
	It was Lavater who said that women
habitually attentive to dress display the
same regularity in their domestic affairs, 
while another writer, alluding to the care-
lessness of some wives concerning their at-
tire, emphatically affirms, "It is one of the
moral duties of every married woman to
appear well-dressed in the presence of her
husband." But to effect this, expensive or
elaborate attire is by no means essential. 
The simplest robe may evince the wearer's
taste as truly as the most costly gown of
"moire antique"—in fact, the simplest
dresses are often the most graceful. Gar-
ments should follow, as far as possible, the
shape of the body as devised and found
good by the great Artificer. All that follows
those beautiful lines must be itself beauti-
ful. All that changes, deforms, or exagger-
ates those lines must be senseless, ugly, 
ludicrous and untrue. Whether a gown
swell out into the hoops of the great tun of
Heidelberg, or project backward like the re-
verse side of the Hottentot Venus, it is
alike hideous. A gown may be of many
folds, of many thicknesses, but it should
[new column]
not turn a woman into a caricature of the
form God made, and made last of all. All
women will admit the truth of this, even
while they persist in disfiguring themselves
in the very manner condemned, for, in jus-
tice to the mass of elaborately-dressed ladies
who daily walk our streets, we must admit
that were they to follow inclination alone,
their costumes would be much simpler.
There breathes not the woman who does
not secretly detest her narrow-soled boots,
with their pointed heels; the heating, brain-
depressing “chignon;” the uncomfortable
corset and unwieldy “panier.” Yet she
lacks courage to discard these instruments
of torture because fashion sanctions their
use. O fashion, fashion! Dare you number
your victims? This mania for costly “dress”
is devastating our American society, and
demoralizing American women to an alarm-
ing extent. The wives and daughters of
the rich men who lead society in this fear-
ful race of extravagance are responsible for
a great share of the prostitution which
curses the nation as-well as for thousands of
business failures, scattered families, and the
long train of miseries among us under the
cover of “keeping up appearances.” An
English tourist recently traveling through
America, complained that owing to the
prevalent custom of elaborate dressing fol-
lowed by American women, it was impos-
sible to distinguish the “shop-girl” from
the “lady of means.” And that brings us
to another part of this subject. Poor girls
dress as expensively as possible, because
they well know that if they do not, society
will punish them bitterly. To be unable to
“dress” is to be treated by men and women
both with almost disrespect in the car, the
boat, at the public gathering, the street and
the shop; to be ignored; to feel the shrug
of contempt, the sneer of levity and the
smile of scorn; to be thrust aside; to be
laughed at; to be unceremoniously dis-
placed; to be cruelly driven out of good so-
ciety; to have heart, intelligence, thought,
virtue, character, held as nothing against
silk; to be stung; to be outraged; to
be proscribed; to be insulted: all this
and much more. It matters not
whether this state of things is right or
wrong; it exists, as every woman who can-
uot follow fashion’s whims knows to her 
cost. It is time for a reform to commence
somewhere; but what woman will head it?
And “what man” will approve and help
her?—“Waverly Magazine.”


TEACH THE WOMEN TO SAVE.—There’s
the secret! A saving woman at the head of
the family is the very best saving bank es-
tablished—one receiving deposits daily and
hourly, with no costly machinery to manage
it. The idea of saving is a pleasant one,
and if the women would imbibe it at once,
they would cultivate and adhere to it, and
thus when they were not aware of it, would
be laying the foundation of a competent se-
curity in a stormy time, and shelter in a
rainy day. The woman who sees to her
own house has a large field to save in. The
best way to make her comprehend it is to
keep an account of all current expenses.
Probably not one woman in ten has an idea
how much are the expenditures of herself
and family. Where from one to two thou-
sand dollars are expended annually, there
is a chance to save something if the effort
is made. Let the housewife take the idea,
act upon it, and she will save many dollars
—perhaps hundreds—where before she had
thought it impossible. This is a duty, yet
not a prompting of avarice, but a moral
obligation, that rests upon the woman as
well as the man.—"Elko Independent.”


﻿THE SEX.

	NO WOMEN have applied for admission to
Amherst College this year.
	THE WIFE of the late General Robert An-
derson will reside in Florence hereafter.
	SIX ladies will be admitted to the
Wesleyan University, at Middletown, at
the next term.
	MISS Rye is on her way from Liverpool
with another lot of model servant girls for
the Canadians.
	IT IS an error to imagine that women talk
more than men. They’re listened to more
—that’s all.
	MRS. Barnard, a lady of some journal-
istic experience, has become managing edi-
tor of the Washington “Chronicle.”
	THREE ladies act as notaries public in
Iowa. They are Miss Zoe Taylor, Miss E.
C. Stebbins and Miss Laura A. Berry.
	“THE charming Lotta” is abroad, dili-
gently pursuing the study of French and
music in the boading school of a French
convent.
	YOUNG ladies will hereafter be admitted
to the collegiate department of Griswold
College, Iowa, on the same terms as the
young men.
	THE Academy of Sciences at Philadel-
phia has elected a woman to full fellowship,
and there is also a lady on one of its active
committees.
	AFTER a competitive examination, Miss
Carrie S. Sheads has been promoted from a
second to a first class clerkship in the Bu-
reau of Statistics.
	“THE only remarkable thing,” says Han-
nah More, “which belonged to me as an
authoress was, that I had written eleven
books after the age of 60.”
	THE Massachusetts Woman Suffrage As-
sociation has again appointed a committee
to bring the quesion of woman suffrage be-
fore the respective political parties at their
approaching State conventions.
	SIGNORA Aurelia Cinimo Falliero De
Luna, a well-known Italian authoress, has
established in Florence a semi-monthly
newspaper, entitled the "Cornelia," devoted
to the advocacy of Woman's Rights and the
promotion of the education of Italian
women.
   TENNIE Claflin and Victoria Woodhull
announce that they will sue the Springfield
(Mass.) "Republican" for libel for allowing
a correspondent to say that they had at-
tempted to blackmail Susan B. Anthony,
Lucy Stone, Elizabeth S. Phelps, and others.
They also propose to sue these ladies for
making the charge originally.
	THE expectation that Miss Nellie Grant
is to "come out" this season, fills the hearts
of the fashionable young ladies of Washing-
ton with unwonted hope. It was under-
stood before she left on the European trip
that this winter her appearance "in society"
would be the key-note of many others bud-
ding in their latest teens to enter the arena
of social fashion.
	MADEMOISELLE Celeste, the tight-rope
artist, had her leg broken by the rope giv-
ing way during one of her performances at
Shawneetown, Ky. The physician who set
the limb evidently did not know his busi-
ness, and instead of joining the bones to-
gether he lapped one over the other,
consequently the broken limb is about three
inches shorter than the other. She is now
on her way home, in Rome, N. Y., to have
the family physician break that limb again,
and reset it.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿MISCELLANEOUS.

	SEVENTY thousand dollars worth of silks,
supposed to have been smuggled, have been
seized in New York.
	A YOUNG lady who had many admirers
among the limbs of the law, on being
asked how she escaped heart-whole, sup-
posed it was owing to the fact that “in a
multitude of ‘counselors’ there is safety.”
	GOD looks not at the oratory of our pray-
ers, how eloquent they are; nor at their
geometry, how long they are; nor at their
arithmetic, how many they are; nor at their
logic, how methodical they are; but he looks
at their sincerity, how spiritual they are.
	A WOMAN lately looking at a printing-
press at work, looked up in the face of her
companion, and in a most earnest manner
inquired:
	“Well, Tim, an’ them’s the things as
writes the papers. Be’s them what they
call editors?”
	A NAUGHTY little boy blubbering because
his mother wouldn’t let him go down to
the river on the Sabbath, upon being ad-
monished, said: “I didn’t want to go a
swimmin’ with ’em ma. I only wanted to
go down and see the bad little boys drown
for going a swimmin’ on Sunday.”
	WISDOM is the associate of Justice. It
assists her to form equal laws, to pursue
right measures, to correct power, to protect
weakness, and to unite individuals in a
common interest and general welfare. He-
roes may kill tyrants, but it is wisdom and
laws that prevent tyranny and oppression.
	IT IS a sign of Christian manhood, where
one, though all the selfish instincts of his
nature impel him to do otherwise, thinks
mercifully, and waits to be gracious, and,
if he cannot form an opinion without sever-
ity, withholds his judgment till he can form
one that shall have more mercy in it. Take
care of the secret thoughts, of the inward
judgments which you form of men.
	THE character of the young men of any
community depends much on that of the
young women. If the latter are cultivated,
intelligent, accomplished, the young men
will feel the requirement that they them-
selves should be upright, and gentlemanly
and refined; but if their female friends are
frivolous and silly, the young men will be
found to be dissipated and worthless.
	DR. HOLMES says; “A woman, notwith-
standing she is the best of listeners, knows
her business, and it is a woman’s business
to please. I don’t say it is not her business
to vote, but I do say that the woman who
does not please, is a false note in the harmo-
nies of nature. She may not have youth
nor beauty, nor even manners; but she must
have something in her voice or expression,
or both, which makes you feel better dis-
posed toward your race to look at or listen
to.”
	A YOUNG mother was in the habit of air-
ing the baby’s clothes at the window. Her
husband did not like it, and believed if she
saw her practice as others saw it she would
desist. He so directed their afternoon
walk as to bring the nursery window into
full view from the central part of the town.
Stopping abruptly, he pointed to the offend-
ing linen flopping unconsciously in the
breeze, and asked sarcastically, “My dear,
what is that display in our window?”
“Why,” she replied, “that is the flag of our
union.” Conquered by this pungent retort,
he saluted the flag by a swing of his hat,
and pressing his wife’s arm closer within
his own, said, as they walked homeward,
“And long may it wave!”


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES. 
				
	OXFORD Univerity celebrates its 1000th
anniversary this year.
	THE new Freshman class of Harvard
already number 191.
	THE Free Traders of England propose an
international conference.
	THE Boston Gas Co. has a well 1,100 feet
deep without striking water.
	MARYLAND cotton factories consume
30,000 bales of cotton per week.
	THE population of Paris is said to have
decreased 500,000 in the past six years.
	THE colored voters of the country are
said to number 900,000, and the politicians
are after them.	
	MORE people have been killed by light-
ning in England this summer than has ever
been known before.
	FALL RIVER, Mass., has now in opera-
tion thirty mills, containing 1,100,000
spindles, representing $25,000,000 capital,
and employing 15,000 operatives.
	THE year 1816 was the coldest ever
known in New England. There was no
summer, and ice formed in June, July and
August.	
	THE New Haven undertaker had recently
twenty-seven dead children to care for in
one day, the deaths being caused mainly
by cholera infantum.
	THE new Catholic cathedral at Boston is
approaching completion, and, when finished,
its tower will rival the State, House as a
prominent landmark for people approach-
ing Boston.
	THE new armory, or city prison of Chi-
cago, will cost about $40,000, and will be
80x100 feet in size, two stories high. The
lockup department will contain twenty-four
cells, each 7x12.
	A MAN in Jamaica, Vt., within the last
six years has had born to him by his first
wife a pair of twin daughters, she dying,
and by a second wife one pair of twin boys
and another pair of twin daughters—being
three pair of twins by two mothers.
	THE cost of living in the larger cities in
this country is yearly increasing, but this
is also true of the cities of the Old World.
One thousand dollars in London at the pres-
ent time will purchase no more than seven
hundred dollars ten years ago.
	IT HAS been proved by repeated experi-
ments that it is possible to rule lines with a
diamond point on glass so close together
that upwards of 60,000 of them are com-
prised in the space of one inch. The appar-
ent limit of vision in the best microscope
does not, however, reach beyond lines sep-
arated one eighty-thousandth of an inch.
	CAPT. Hervey’s experiment with Chinese
workmen in his laundry at Belleville, N. J.,
is so far successful that another troop has
been sent for, and a new building for their
accommodation is in process of construction.
All animosity between the Irish girls in the
laundry and the “haythens” appears to have
subsided, and they get along very amicably
together.
	NARROW-GAUGE railroads, according to a
paper recently read before the New York
society of engineers, have an advantage
over broad-gauge roads of 25.48 per cent, in
road bed and road construction. It was also
asserted that there is a saving of 5 per cent.
in the cost of stations, of 28 per cent. in
the cost of car shops, and 21.07 per cent. on
the cost of rolling stock. The average sav-
ing on all items, it was stated, would be
24.03 per cent.


﻿HINTS FOR YOUNG MOTHERS.

	The three requisites for babies are plenty
of sleep, plenty of food, plenty of flannel.
The saying that man is a bundle of habits
is true of babies as it is of grown children.
If an infant is accustomed from its birth to
sleep from six o’clock at night till daylight,
the habit of early sleep will be formed, and
the mother may have all of her evenings to
herself. If the baby sleeps all night, a long
morning nap will naturally come about din-
ner time, after which the child, except
when very young, should be kept awake till
six o’clock. Perseverance in this routine
will soon result in securing quiet evenings
for both child and parent. Some mothers
have a long season every morning and every
night in getting their babies asleep. They
rock them and sing to them till Morpheus
enfolds them. With most children this is
entirely unnecessary. An infant can be ac-
customed by a few days’ training to go to
sleep itself for a morning nap as well as for
the longer rest at night. A mother has du-
ties to herself as well as to her offspring.
While she should exercise a most constant
care in securing its utmost physical comfort,
she should secure restand recreation for her-
self. In no other way can she keep fresh
in feeling and buoyant in spirit. Nothing
is so wearing as the unceasing tending of a
fretful baby. Every means should be em-
ployed to aid the child in taking care of it-
self and giving as little trouble as possible.
It may learn in babyhood to amuse itself
with toys or by watching movements going
on around it. Fashion as well as good sense
requires infants’ dresses to be made with
long sleeves and high in the neck. Fashion
requires children of all ages to be warmly
clad. Flannel should incase the whole body,
with the exception of the head and the
hands. The fruitful cause of colic in in-
fants is the nakedness of their necks and
arms. Regularity in feeding is as import-
ant as either of the other requisites. Ba-
bies cry as often from being over-fed or fed
too frequently as from hunger. Let the
mother obey the dictates of common sense
in this matter, and not force food into a ba-
by’s stomach for every little complaint it
makes. Children of three and four years
old need much more sleep than they usu-
ally have. For irritable and nervous chil-
dren sleep is a specific, and it can be secured
to them only by force of habit. Many light
forms of disease may be cured by keeping
a child in a uniform temperature and in
quiet. Let the young mothers who read
this column experiment on these few sug-
gestions, and we are sure they will have
many an hour in the nursery for reading
and thought.—Ex.


MISS SMYTHE, the comic singer and bal-
let dancer of London, who still follows that
calling, is Viscountess Hinton, having mar-
ried the noble “Cosman” in 1869, who per-
formed as a pantomimist in several theatres
in 1869-71; and who has lately been discov-
ered to be none other than Viscount Hinton,
heir to Earl Poulett. The couple have a
son and heir, born to them in 1870. It is
difficult to tell who persons are after they
conceive a mania for the stage. In becom-
ing so many and such varied characters, is
it a wonder they should fail sometimes to
keep track of themselves?


A MAN named William George, 103 years
of age, living at Rochester, Ill., committed
suicide by hanging himself a few days
ago.


LIVINGSTONE'S DISCOVERER is now called
Stanley- Africanus.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


POLICY VERSUS LOVE.

	The Princess Marguerite, cousin-wife of
Prince Humbert, of Italy, does not believe
in Iove for persons of her condition. She
is said to have remarked, not sentimentally
but with the common sense air of a woman
of her century, “There is no such emotion
for us.” The match between her and her
cousin-consort was merely one of State poli-
cy, and although the Prince is not an agree-
able husband, but sullen, and very often
reproachful, and the Princess sometimes re-
sents his rude snubs, she yet seems to con-
sider that it is nothing out of place. She
endeavors to be a patient, kind wife to her
arrogant liege lord, and to further the public
interest even at the cost of her own health.
But the Prince is fond of his boy, becoming
placid and amiable at his presence, no mat-
ter how inconsiderate and sullen he has
been during the day. It is a fine thing for
women so situated to believe as does Mar-
guerite of Savoy.


THE DUTIES OF A MOTHER.—By the
quiet fireside of home, the true mother, in
the midst of her children, is sowing, as in
vases of earth, the seeds of plants that some-
times give to heaven the fragrance of their
blossoms, and whose fruit shall be as a
rosary of angelic deeds, through the ever
ascending and expanding souls of her chil-
dren, to her Maker. Every word that she
utters goes from heart to heart with a power
of which she little dreams. Philosophers
tell us, in their speculations, that we cannot
lift our finger without moving the distant
spheres. Solemn is the thought, but not
more solemn to the mother than the thought
that every word that falls from her lips,
every expression of her countenance, even
in the sheltered walk and retirement of
home, may leave an indelible impression on
young souls around her, and form, as it
were, an underlying strain of that education
which peoples heaven.

			WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

			A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL.
	
	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.

	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves?  "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others !"	

	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting arid valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.

	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.

	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published, semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.	’

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year, 	$2.00	1 Copy six months	$1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months,   9.00

	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


Woman's Exponent.
﻿VOL. I.  	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, OCTOBER 1, 1872	NO. 9.


﻿NEWS AND VIEWS.

	GEORGE SAND, the celebrated French
authoress, advocates the organization of fe-
male free-masonry in France; and she is
really serious in the proposition.
	PENNSYLVANIA, INDIANA, Iowa and
Nebraska hold State elections on the 8th
inst., and that day is expected to decide the
election, in November, for President.
	A LADY refused to go onboard the steam-
er “Metis,” the night that ill-fated vessel
sailed, although her luggage was on board;
and now she thanks Providence for her es-
cape.
	THE LIBERAL Democratic ratification
meeting in New York, on the 12th ult.,
was attended by over forty thousand people.
The fact shows the interest taken in the
present campaign.
	PRUSSIA HAVING conquered France has
now commenced to wage war against Ro-
man Catholicism in Germany. This contest
is likely to be a longer one than the other,
and the result will not be so financially re-
munerative as was the war with France.
	DISCARDING THE side-saddle in riding on
horseback is gaining votaries among women
in different parts of the country, and is said
to be the fashion in Wisconsin. We are not
informed as to what is the change in the
style of dress which precedes this change
in the style of riding in that State,
	A NEWSVENDER in Berlin was fined
twenty thalers recently for selling a copy of
“Woodhull and Claflin’s Weekly.” The
Prussian authorities are not inclined to
countenance the discussion of free-love the-
ories in the style in which Mrs. Blood and
colonel Tennie C. have been accustomed to
create sensations in New York.
	THE SENSIBLE fathers are not all dead.
In Chicago recently a young lady, disregard-
ing the adventitious aids of money and posi-
tion in her lover, married her father’s
coachman. The father, instead of discard-
ing his child and condemning her to misery,
seeing that the young man “had something
in him,” quietly set him up in the livery
business, and the twain are happy.
	FIFTEEN MILLIONS and a half of dollars,
in gold, is the award made to the United
States by the Geneva board of arbitrators;
payable by Britain in Washington in one
year. The amount claimed at first, includ-
ing direct and indirect damages, was some-
where near two thousand millions. This is
considerable of a difference, and it is al-
leged the English have some claims yet to
present for adjudication.
	THE LONG roll of political conventions is
drawing to a close, but stump speaking is
being prosecuted with vigor. Bronchial
affections disappear before the power of
party and monetary inducement, and the
speakers ring out in excited tones denunci-
ations of their opponents and praises of
their party, to win votes from the multi-
tude, while in their hearts dispising and
disbelieving much that they say and sol-
emnly assert as the truth. This is manhood,
as politicians understand it! Could woman
degrade the “science” much? She certainly
might elevate and purify it.
	PERE HYACINTH has been buried. He
still lives, though—in the flesh; but his for-
mer Dominican brethren, after the custom
of their order, viewing his defection or
apostacy as a death, performed the funeral
service for him on the 5th ult. It was
rather singular that he should have married
a few days after, evidently the one event
treading upon the heels of the other.
	FROM THE promises and pledges being
made by prominent Republicans, should
President Grant be re-elected that party
will be placed under obligations to confer
the suffrage upon women. If they do so,
it will likely perpetuate the power of the
party over another Presidential election, at
least, for gratitude will secure them many
votes even from those who would not other-
wise affiliate with the party.
	WE KEEP noting the peculiar ways in
which "piety" develops itself. The latest
illustration is that of a young man in El-
dora, Iowa, who stole a horse some little
time ago, and sold the animal for two bibles
and ten dollars. Of course it was the good
book he was so anxious to obtain; and he
had probably seen some pious deacon rob
the "poor's box" of its Sunday collection,
and viewed the act as a worthy example to
be patterned after. Preaching dread of an
eternal fiery lake, instead of teaching cor-
rect principle, has a tendency to make men
infidels, dishonorable, and wicked.
	RECENTLY THE face has come to light
that the Chinese are ahead of either Eng-
lish or Americans in adulterating tea; and
it is questionable if the pure article at all
finds its way to this market. People talk
of a "cup of good tea!" But if they knew
the stuff with which it is adulterated, an-
other and very different adjective would
qualify the shrub so much in use as a bev-
erage. If people would buy their poisons
separate they could mix them to suit their
own tastes, and perhaps save some of the
money of which John Chinaman now robs
the "barbarians" who shorten their lives and
destroy their nervous systems by drinking
his adulterated tea.
	MRS. DUNIWAY, editor of the "New
North-West," Portland, Oregon, in an edi-
torial article in which she treats of Presi-
dent Grant having "reformed from a vicious
life," gets off the following: "Since the
above was written, General Grant has re-
formed from another bad habit of which he
last March was guilty, the pernicious one of
ignoring or snubbing the taxed, misrepre-
sented and protesting half of his people.
Therefore, we say all honor to him. And
upon the coarse, bigoted, narrow-minded
old dotard who cannot get the top of his
glistening pate above the filthy pool of sex-
ual politics, but who stands ready to remove
from places of emolument and trust the
hundreds of struggling women whom Gen-
eral Grant has given employment, that they
may thereby elevate themselves from the
necessity of bodily immolation upon the
altar of lust for bread, the women of Amer-
ica with one accord are crying 'anathema.'"
The language in which Greeley is here
referred to can hardly be called classical,
and the principal comment demanded by it
is, that it is at once coarse and untrue.
	MORE INDIAN delegations have been vis-
iting their "Great Father" at Washington,
making the usual demands for guns and
ammunition, provisions, and a suspension
of railway building. There is great reason
to believe that these aborigines, from con-
tact with dishonest and swindling agents,
have come to imagine that the United
States government is very rich and very
simple, allowing itself to be robbed in the
easiet manner imaginable, by the agents it
employs, and therefore fair game for the In-
dians to fleece, if the opportunity offers.
The entire Indian policy of the country
needs remodeling, by the employment of
honest men as agents and superintendents;
that is, if honest men can be found willing
to accept such offices.
	THE FIRST convention representing any
of the great political parties of the country,
to squarely and fairly take up the subject
of Woman's rights, was the Liberal Repub-
lican convention of Massachusetts, which
wound up its proceedings by explicitly de-
claring in favor of woman's right to vote,
right to fight, and right to do several other
things now denied to the sex. Charles
Sumner was the choice of the same conven-
tion for Governor of Massachusetts. The
right to vote can be exercised by all women
when they receive it; but Colonel Tennie
C. Claflin is at liberty to embody in her own
person all the right to fight which might
otherwise suffice for at least ten thousand.
The right claimed by Julia Ward Howe is
better—the right to aid in securing peace
to mankind.
	A SAD romance of real life, and accidental
death, by drowning, is related in the "Bel-
fast Morning News," as having occurred a
few weeks since, in the Thames,off Woolwich.
Eight persons had started in a lighterman's
skiff, for a run down the river, in buoyant
spirits. When near Belvidere, the river
became rough and the boat went down stern
foremost, throwing its occupants out, five
of whom were drowned, and three were
found clinging to the boat. The hero and
heroine, Mr. Whyman and Miss Hughes,
an estimable young couple, who, it appears,
were engaged to be married, were not dis-
covered until five days afterward, when
their bodies were found together. Thus,
we live to-day, but know not the hour of
death!
	WE CLIP the following from "Appleton's
Journal" for September 28: "The relatives of
the Prussian Lieutenant von Hafften, who
eloped with two young girls (sisters) from
Berlin, and who is now said to be with
them at Salt Lake city, have published a 
card, in which they assert that he is insane
on the subject of free-love, and that he has
been misled by the pernicious teachings of
a Mormon missionary named Hansen."
Now, as free-love is not taught, nor be-
lieved, in Salt Lake city, and as no von
Hafften has arrived here with or without
two sisters with whom he eloped; and as
no Mormon missionary has been teaching
or preaching in Berlin for some years, the
reliability of the foregoing extract will not
commend itself  to the reading public. In
fact it bears the appearance of a manufac-
tured canard.


66					WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿THE FEMALE RELIEF SOCIETY.
	A BRIEF SKETCH OF ITS ORGANIZATION AND WORK-
	INGS IN THE CITY OF NAUVOO, HANCOCK CO, ILLS.
			BY ELIZA. R. SNOW.
	[Reprinted from No. 1 of the EXPONENT]
	According to authentic testimony, an or-
ganization, of which the present Female
Relief Society is a fac-simile, has always ex-
isted when the Church of Jesus Christ has
been fully organized. “Elect lady,” as it
occurs in the New Testament, has direct
reference to the same—alluding to one who
presided over this Institution. See 2d
Epistle of John, 1st verse.
	The first organization of this Society, in
the present dispensation, was effected on
the 17th of March, 1842, by Joseph Smith,
President of the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints, assisted by other promi-
nent Elders. His apology for deferring the
organization till that period, was the great
pressure of duties, labors and responsibili-
ties, which devolved upon him. By his
suggestion a meeting was appointed in
the Masonic Hall, and a limited number in-
vited.
	Perhaps a few extracts from the “Minutes
of the Organization,” would be interesting,
—they are too lengthy for insertion in full:
			 "Nauvoo Lodge Room,
					March 17, 1842.
	Elder John Taylor was called to the chair,
and Elder Willard Richards appointed sec-
retary.
	Meeting commenced by singing ‘The
spirit of God like a fire is burning,’ etc.
Prayer by Elder Taylor.
	When it was moved by Pres. Smith, and
seconded by Mrs. Cleveland, that a vote be
taken to know if all are satisfied with each
female present, and are willing to acknowl-
edge them in full fellowship and admit
them to the privileges of the Institution
about to be formed. Vote unanimous.
	President Smith addressed the meeting
to illustrate the objects of the Society; that
the Society of sisters might provoke the
brethren to good works, in looking to the
wants of the poor, searching after objects
of charity, etc., to assist, by correcting the
morals and strengthening the virtues of the
female community, and save the Elders
the trouble of rebuking, etc. He proposed
that the sisters elect a presiding officer to
preside over them, and let that presiding
officer choose two counsellors; that he would
ordain them, and let them preside over the
Society, just as the Presidency preside over
the Church; and if they need his instruc-
tion—ask him—he will give it from time to
time.
	Let this Presidency serve as a Constitu-
tion. If any officers are wanted to carry
out the designs of the Institution, let them
be appointed and set apart, as Deacons,
Teachers, etc., are among us.
	The minutes of your meetings will be
precedents for you to act upon.
	He then suggested the propriety of elect-
ing a Presidency to continue in office dur-
ing good behavior, or so long as they shall
fill the office with dignity, etc.
	Motioned by Mrs. Whitney, and seconded
by Mrs. Packard, that Mrs. Emma Smith
be chosen President; passed unanimously.
	On suggestion of Prest. Smith, the Presi-
dentess Elect made choice of Mrs. Sarah
M. Cleveland and Mrs. Elizabeth Ann
Whitney for Counsellors.”
	After which the ceremony of ordination
was performed: “When Prest. Smith re-
[next column]
sumed his remarks, giving instruction how
to govern themselves in their meetings,
what  order to observe, how to regulate dis-
cussions, etc., etc., and concluded by saying,
'Do not injure the character of any one; if
members of the Society shall conduct im-
properly, deal with them, and keep all your
doings within your own bosoms, and hold
all characters sacred.’
	It was then proposed that Elder Taylor
vacate the chair. Prest. Emma Smith and
her counsellors took the chair; and
	Elder Taylor moved, seconded by Prest.
J. Smith, that we go into an investigation
respecting what this Society shall be called;
which was carried unanimously.”
	(The discussion which was lengthy and
interesting, is wholly omitted.)
	“By unanimous vote, Miss Eliza R. Snow
was appointed Secretary, and
	Miss Phebe M. Wheeler Assistant Secre-
tary, and
	Miss Elvira A. Coles, (the late Mrs.
Holmes) Treasurer.
	Prest. J. Smith said, ‘I now declare this
Society organized with President and Coun-
sellors, etc., according to Parliamentary
usages, and all who shall hereafter be ad-
mitted into this Society must be free from
censure, and received by vote.’
	Elder Taylor addressed the meeting by
saying that he is much gratified in seeing a
meeting of this kind in Nauvoo; his heart
rejoices when he sees the most distin-
guished characters stepping forth in a cause
which is calculated to bring into exercise
every virtue, and give scope to the benevo-
lent feelings of the female heart; he re-
joices to see this Institution organized ac-
cording to the law of Heaven; his prayer
is, that the blessings of God and the peace
of Heaven may rest down on this Institu-
tion, henceforth.
	After singing ‘Come let us rejoice,’ etc.,
the meeting adjourned for one week. Dis-
missed by prayer.”
	Before retiring, each gentleman present
gave a liberal donation to commence the
funds of the Institution.
	The following lines were found written
on a scrap and lying on an open Bible in the
room where, and at the time when, the So-
ciety was organized; which have been care-
fully preserved and transcribed as an appro-
priate frontis-piece to the F. R. S. Book of
Records:
	“O Lord, he[-]p our widows and fatherle[-] s chil-
dren! So mete it be Amen. With the SWORD
and the WORD of TRUTH, defend thou them.
So mete it be Amen"
	(to be continued.)


F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
St. George, Sept. 11, 1872.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:
I transmit to you a report of our organi-
zation, which was established August 24th,
1868, by President Erastus Snow, assisted
by Bishops Robert Gardner and Henry Ey-
ring, and Elders William Fawcett, William
H. Branch, Franklin B. Woolley and James
G. Bleak; and consisted of twenty-five
members, with Anna L. Ivins, as President;
Emily C. Branch and Hannah E. Crosby,
Counsellors; Augusta B. Jackson, Secretary;
and Caroline A. Jackson, Treasurer.
During the past four years the people
visited by the teachers of the Society have
contributed liberally to Its fund, and we
have always had means on hand to supply
every call for aid.
In response to the call of President Young
for help to gather the poor from foreign
lands, we collected and forwarded one hun-
[new column]
dred dollars; and are still receiving money
for the same purpose. Our disbursements
during the time since the Society was or-
ganized have been upwards of two hundred
dollars, and we have on hand six hundred
dollars in goods and capital stock in the
R. V. M. Company, and Co-operative store
of St. George. Our present roll counts one
hundred and fifty members; with Anna L.
Ivins as President; Hannah E. Crosby as
first, and Minerva Snow as second Counsel-
lor; Hannah G. Perkins, President of Teach-
ers; M. C. McArthur, Treasurer; and
			CAROLINE A. JACKSON,
						Secretary.

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We send you a brief report of the fourth
annual meeting of the F. R. Society of
Santaquin, held Sept. 5th, when we were
favored with the presence of Sisters Simons
and Weach, Presidents of Payson and New-
town Societies, whose remarks were excel-
lent and very encouraging. There was ex-
hibited upon the occasion five handsome
quilts, one of which, it was said, contained
1,932 pieces. This speaks well for the pa-
tience, Industry and economy of the sisters.
Several of the sisters spoke disapprovingly
of “slang” talk, and a happy feeling per-
vaded the meeting; many of the infirm
were administered to at the close, by the
Elders. The Society is in a prosperous con-
dition; a good feeling prevails in our
midst; and all seem willing to aid the old,
infirm and destitute. Santaquin being a
small place, our financial report is also small.
Our income since May 31, 1868, up to May
31, 1872, was $505.35; disbursements to the
infirm and needy, $288;03; our shares in
the Provo Woolen Factory and shares in the
Co-operative store are included in the prop-
erty on hand. We like your paper much,
and wish it great success.
	MRS. E. J. STICKNEY, President;
	MRS. J. D. WILSON, Secretary;
	MRS. E. F. TRITGEN, Treasurer.

	The F. R. Society of Brinton’s Ward,
Big Cottonwood, held a meeting on the af-
ternoon of Wednesday, September 18th,
which was attended by Miss E. R. Snow
and others from Salt Lake city, who took
active part in the proceedings. The Presi-
dent, Mrs. Harriet Brinton, expressed
much satisfaction at the agreeable surprise
which sister Snow’s visit occasioned, as it
was unexpected; and she earnestly thanked
the Secretary, Mrs. Elizabeth Howard, for
inviting and bringing the sisters from the
city.
	During her remarks Miss Snow said that
"It is not required of women who are seek-
ing to live their religion, gain intelligence
and assist in establishing pure and correct
principles, to neglect their personal appear-
ance; but to endeavor to elevate and beau-
tify themselves in every way, and not
disfigure their forms and make themselves
appear ridiculous by following after the
vain, senseless fashions of the world.” She
asked what was more becoming and beauti-
fying to an intellectual and refined lady
than a plain, neat dress? The tawdery friv-
olities of fashion may do for those devoid
of taste and judgment, but they ill become
women of sound thought and reason. We
should seek to increase our fund of knowl-
edge and to polish the mind. There is
more sense and consistency in that than in
covering the body with finery, and seeking
by that means to appear polished and at-
tractive. We should study our own natures,
our own organizations, and learn to under-
stand the laws of life and health. We should


be energetic and ambitious in regard to
these things, and seek to promulgate the
importance which attaches to them. She
said she desired to see a greater interest
manifested by her sisters, in the physiolo-
gical classes that were being formed; and
considered it a matter of great importance
and one that should receive much attention
from the daughters of Zion. It would be
an inestimable help to mothers in rearing
their children to have an acquaintance with
the laws which combine and govern the
different parts of the human system.
	Much credit is due to the Society of sis-
ters of this precinct, for the unwavering
exertions they make in keeping up their
regular meetings; for as the ward is scat-
tered in a great degree, many of its inhab-
itants are situated at an inconvenient dis-
tance from the meeting house; some of
them having to walk three and four miles
for the purpose of attending meeting. They
also exhibit a lively interest in the welfare
of those who are in need of assistance, and
in the cause of truth generally.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
	A conscientious woman cannot be quite
cheerful and at ease when she finds her du-
ties jarring, and much remaining undone
that ought to be done. It will throw a shade
of anxiety over her countenance, and a se-
cret depression will weigh upon her spirits,
which cannot be removed until her house-
hold matters, can be so arranged that the
duties which she feels belong to her as a
woman, in every department in life, can be
accomplished. When the mind is thus af-
fected, the face, which should be as the
sunlight, becomes clouded and careworn,
imparting by secret social sympathy, a gloom
to the whole circle of home. That the
causes may be as quickly removed as pos-
sible, great pains should be taken by hus-
bands to facilitate the performance of and
alleviate oppressive and still imperious du-
ties.
	A housekeeper will find it a great conve-
nience to have a trunk or deep drawer de-
voted to articles used in sewing and
repairing. Bags for keeping all such things
separate and in order are excellent helps,
one for new and another for old calico; one
for white and another for colored pieces;
one for stocking yarn in skeins and balls,
and one for carpet rags. A box with parti-
tions for all the implements of sewing, and
keeping each article in its own apartment,
saves much time and many steps.
	Another very important arrangement in
a family where there are children is to pro-
vide regular duties for each. One to bring
the wood, another to attend to the poultry,
cow, etc., changing round occasionally to
vary monotony; or, if girls, to dress and
undress a younger child, set the chairs in
order, and when old enough to wash and
wipe dishes. When these duties are shown
to children, it should be required of them
that they be performed in the proper
time and way, as much for the benefit of
the child as for the good it will do in help-
ing to preserve order in the domestic ar-
rangements.
	Much of the health of a family depends
upon the judgment of the mistress of the
house in varying food, and arranging the
dishes for different meals so as to be health-
ful and digestible. In some families it is fried
potatoes, meat, and hot cakes, or bread, in
the morning; soggy, boiled potatoes, and
meat at dinner; with a piece of dry, indi-
gestible pie, and bread and butter, cheese,
and “chipped” beef, with crackers, at night.
Now, nothing can be worse for women and
children, or persons of sedentary habits,
than a diet of this kind. It will do for la-
boring men, or those whose lives are spent
much in the open air, although even they
would be better for the regular introduction
into their diet of some elements which the
stomach is more capable of assimilating.
But for the children who go to school, and
women who stay at home, it is simply mur-
derous, and lays the foundation of the fu-
ture liver complaint and dyspepsia, which
have become the heritage of the American
people.
	Meat breakfasts are totally unnecessary
where dinner is taken in the middle of the
day, excepting for men employed in field or
outdoor labor. Boiled oat-meal, hominy,
or wheaten grits, with milk toast or bread
and-butter, are infinitely better. If fruit,
fresh or stewed, can be added, so much the
better. At dinner, the meat should be
fresh or exceedingly well cooked, the pota-
toes cooked dry and mealy, and some other
vegetable added, in which there is an acid,
or a good deal of juice. Grated horse-rad-
ish is invaluable in the spring, as an adjunct
to roast meat; tomatoes cooked without
thickening are an admirable regulator; beets
well cooked and sliced in vinegar are not
to be despised, and any kind of spring
greens, or salad, is a benefaction. It is a
pity that there is an odor so offensive to
some about onions, for, when young, they fill
a convenient niche before fresh fruit ap-
pears, are pungent, and occasionally very
palatable with bread and butter, and act
as one of the specifics in purifying the
blood.—Ex.


﻿FOR THE GIRLS.
			   —
	There is hardly any bodily blemish which
winning behavior and conversation will not
conceal, and, there is no external attraction
which ill nature and affectation will not de-
form. Much is indicated by voice and
manner; a subdued and gentle tone is ever
the accompaniment of refined feelings and
a cultivated mind. A loud, coarse laugh is
vulgar; but to laugh heartily when anything
is amusing, is not inconsistent with good
breeding, and may be done in a way that
accords with the nicest sense of propriety,
and is much more agreeable than a dull,
cold precision. Contempt and haughtiness
are never wise; arrogance and incivility
show anything but good breeding or gener-
ous sentiments. Giving pain to anyone
should be carefully avoided, and this may
be done by studying your own heart. Cour-
teous and affable behavior is the best to of-
fer, even to the rude and ungrateful. Flirt-
ing or coquetry, is beneath the dignity of
any true lady, and should never be indulged
in. If begun merely for pastime, in an in-
nocent, thoughtless way, it is almost sure
to be carried to excess, and is seldom
checked until feelings and words of a dis-
agreeable nature are the result; and not un-
frequently, where a conscientious and sym-
pathetic heart is concerned, but has not
been properly consulted from the first, un-
happy marriages are occasioned in conse-
quence of this unwise and ungenerous
practice. Though in some cases it is pru-
dent to conceal an opinion, when it costs no
sacrifice of principle, in all circumstances
adhere to the truth; make it a rule never
to give utterance to a falsehood. Contra-
diction and arguments are inadmissible in
social intercourse where friends meet for
pleasant enjoyment. Talk little of yourself
or your own interests, except when business
requires it. Converse of things rather than
persons in general. Always be punctual in
fulfilling engagements.


﻿TIRED MOTHERS.
			  ——
A elbow leans upon your knee,
	Your tired knee, that has so much to bear;
A child's dear eyes are looking lovingly
	From underneath a thatch of tangled hair,
Perhaps you do not heed the velvet touch
	Of warm, moist fingers, folding yours so tight;
You do not prize this blessing over much,
	You almost are too tired to pray to-night.

But it is blessedness! A year ago
	I did not see it as I do to-day.
We are so dull and thankless; and too slow
	To catch the sunshine til it slips away.
And lo! it seems surpassing strange to me,
	That while I wore the badge of motherhood,
I did not kiss more oft, and tenderly,
	The little child that brought me only good.

And if, some night when you sit down to rest,
	You miss this elbow from your tired knee;
This restless, curling head from off your breast,
	This lisping tongue that chatters constantly;
If from your own the dimpled hands have slipped,
	And ne'er will nestle in your palm again;
If the white feet into their grave have tripped,
	I could not blame you for your heart-ache then!

I wonder so that mothers ever fret,
	At little children clinging to their gown;
Or that the foot prints, when the days are wet,
	Are ever black enough to make them frown.
If I could find a little muddy boot,
	Or cap or jacket, on my chamber floor;
If I could kiss a rosy, restless foot,
	And hear its patter in my home once more;

If I could mend a broken cart to-day,
	To-morrow make a kite to reach the sky--
There is no woman in God's world could say
	She was more blissfully content than I.
But ah ! the dainty pillow next my own
	Is never rumpled by a shining head;
My singing birdling from its nest is flown :
	The little boy I used to kiss is dead !
				-- The "Aldine" for September.


THE DRESS OF CIVILIZED WOMEN.

	I do declare that I think it would be bet-
ter to die and get out of torment at once
than have to rise every morning for some
forty or fifty years and box one’s body up
in a sort of compressive armor, hang
weights to one’s hips and more weights
upon one’s head—which last are supported
by the roots of the hair; put one’s feet into 
shoes a number too small, and not of the
right shape, and with heels like stilts; and
then set about doing the whole duty of
woman with a cheerful face and a spry air, 
for from fifteen to seventeen mortal hours
out of the twenty-four! That there are so
many women who are not frightened into a
decline at such a prospect, and that they
bravely undertake to do it—nay, more, that
they even dream that under such disadvan-
tages they can work side by side with an
unshackled man, and that they die in try-
ing to do it, certainly says much for their
courage, but very little for their common
sense.
	A man’s dress to a great extent is fash-
ioned for comfort. He has contrivances for
suspending the weight of his clothing from
his shoulders. If the east wind blows he
can turn up his coat collar, button himself
up snugly, slouch his hat over his eyes,
thrust his hands into his pockets and brave
the weather. But imagine a woman re-
moving her hat or bonnet from the angle
at which fashion says she must wear it on
account of the weather, or turning any of
her “fixtures” up to protect her neck and
throat, or buttoning anything that was un-
buttoned before, or sticking her hands into
her pockets! She would be taken for an
improper character out on a mild spree, or
for an escaped inmate of a lunatic asylum,
should she endeavor by any Impromptu ar-
rangement of her habiliments to save her 
health.—“Science of Health.”
