SALT LAKE CITY, OCTOBER 1, 1872.


﻿SPLINTERS.
	The Khedive has twenty-three palaces...
Rome has 230 convents......Santa Barbara
wants a gold note bank......High heels are
going down and spines may go up again......
The Queen of the Belgians rejoices over
the birth of a daughter......The Sutro tun-
nel is in 3,050 feet......Ristori’s daughter,
M’lle. Crisi, is to be married some time in
October......Virginia city, Montana, is full
of prize ring experts......Council Bluff’s
Spiritualists are said to exclude red-haired
people from the circle......The English
Government is considering the project of
purchasing all the railroads in Ireland at
the price of $150,000,000......It is reported
that the Siamese twins are at last divided,
one being for Greeley and the other for
Grant......The San Juan mines are still the
excitement in Colorado.......The Massachu-
setts Republican State convention has given
unequivocal and complete endorsement to
Woman Suffrage.......New Albany and Jef-
fersonville political clubs marched for hours
through a drenching rain, while being
drilled a few evenings ago, without a man
flinching......A grand ball has been given
at the California State prison........The
“Christian Union” thinks the American girl
has an easy life, compared with her
brother’s......Grant papers try to prove that
the present President was not drunk at Shi-
loh; but a journal of a different nature says,
to save his military reputation, they ought
to prove that he was dead drunk the entire
week in which Shiloh was fought......Two
Greeley and Brown flags have been raised
in Shirley village, Massachusetts, the first
anti-Radical flags that ever floated there......
A swarm of bees, In Iowa, stung a horse to
death lately In half an hour........Report
says, Blaine was as poor as a church mouse
when he entered Congress, but is now a
millionaire......Princess Thyra, of Italy, is
said to be the most beautiful woman in Eu-
rope......A Nantucket octogenarian recently
spent an entire evening at the piano, play-
ing waltzes for her fantastic-toed grand-
children......Thirty-one million dollars are
invested in the wine culture of California....
A Branford, Connecticut, girl, who has been
clerk in the post office, recently confessed
the triple crime of robbing the mail, for-
gery and passing a forged check......In
Bethany, Pa., which has always given de-
cided Republican majorities, it is said, not
one vote will be cast for Grant......Five dol-
lar counterfeit gold pieces, bearing the San
Francisco mint mark of 1867, are reported
circulating in Pioche......California fruits in
great abundance, are for sale in all the east-
ern markets......Eleven prisoners are the
present occupants of the Washington
Territory penitentiary......Charlotte Cush-
man opens the Bay State course of lectures
at Boston on the 3rd inst....It is reported
that bands of Gypsies are traveling through
western Massachusetts, stealing children
and robbing orchards and cornfields......A
Coventry workman, in England, has been
sentenced to ten years penal servitude, for
starving his little daughter, four years old;
he has been allowing his family only three
shillins a week for food, while he spent
eighteen in drink........Olive Logan has writ-
ten a play called “A Business Woman,”
which will be presented during the coming
season......A Far Western lady who was not
posted in history, and had forgotten her
geography, asked a friend who was going
to Utah, to bring her a couple of Mormons
for her aquarium......A Connecticut girl
has renounced Christianity, and embraced
Judaism and a Jewish doctor to whom she
was immediately married......Jenny Lind
is reported ill......A Keokuk man took so
much stock in the comet business that he
forgave his enemies, paid his debts and pre-
pared to die......Report says, a daughter of
the late Confederate minister, John Slidell,
will shortly be married to a German Baron...
A curious disease prevails at Barton, Ver-
mont, called by the physicians there
“typhoid dysentery.”.......Charles Whee-
ler Denison, editor of the “Nation-
al Harbinger,” Washington, “regret-
fully, conscientiously and positively asserts
that the ‘Nation’s chief magistrate drinks
too much’”.....The Philadelphia “Press”
says Grant will be elected by a majority of
four electoral votes, (!) but the Chicago
“Times” admits that Greeley may be elect-
ed President......A grand dinner has been
given by the Savage Club, London, in honor
of Stanley......In an Iowa college, the Pro-
fessor of agricultural science is a woman,
Mrs. Tupper......An attempt to organize a
Greeley and Brown club at Hillsboro is re-
ported a failure, not one person putting in
an appearance.....St. Petersburg has a
prominent female lawyer, Madam Krietzoff
......The Navy Department is believed by
some to be the most corrupt branch in the
government......In Europe and the United
States there are 150 manufactories of India
rubber goods, employing five operatives
each, and consuming over ten million
pounds of gum every year......A young lady
who has been lecturing on “Thoughts and
their Chariots,” is requested to discourse
next on “Whims and their Wheelbarrows.”


﻿LADY LAWYERS.

	In the Third District Court of Utah, Chief
Justice J. B. McKean presiding, on Saturday
week, September 21, two ladies—Miss Phoebe
W. Couzins, of St. Louis, and Miss Georgie
Snow, daughter of Judge Z. Snow, Attorney
General of Utah Territory, applied for admis-
sion to the bar of Utah, were admitted, and
were duly sworn in as attorneys-at-law. While
we have no ambition, personally, to become a
practicing lawyer, nor to seek admission to the
bar even without the intention of practicing
the profession of law, we recognize in the appli- 
cation and admission of these ladies a prin-
ciple which is daily receiving wider recogni-
tion and which was, a few years ago, ridiculed
when advocated by enlarged minds that looked
beyond their time—the right of a woman to
earn her living in any honorable career for
which she has capacity. In the practical
working of this principle, as in everything
which aims at securing to woman the exercise
of her full and legitimate rights, Utah has
been markedly in advance of other parts of the
Union; and has been as far in advance of Eng-
land in this direction as England has been and
is in advance of Massachusetts, much as the
latter boasts of its philanthropic, humanitarian
and republican ideas. Among the first places
where women commenced to learn to be prac-
tical printers was here in Utah, and if the at-


tempt failed it was because those who com-
menced to learn the art believed they could
earn more with less labor at other kinds of
work. The propriety of women occupying
desks as book-keepers, and acting as clerks in
stores, so that the men thus employed might
devote their greater physical strength to
heavier labor which had to be done, was urged
years ago from the stand by President Brigham
Young and other leading men in  the commu-
nity. And to-day, on the thousand miles of
telegraph lines owned and worked in Utah, the
Deseret Telegraph Company employ more lady
operators than can be found on the same dis-
tance of wire in any other part of the world.
Ladies, too, at the present time are studying
medicine in this city, with a view to qualify 
themselves for its practice; and in every direc- 
tion in which woman’s talents or powers can 
be made available, there is every encourage- 
ment for their cultivation and development. 
All this is in Utah, and among the “down-
trodden women of Utah,” concerning whom
silly sentimentalists continue to theorize, and
waste unsought and undesired sympathy upon;
women who have every avenue open for them
to secure a living, and a competence if they
possess financial ability; and women who, if	
they become wives in families where plural
marriage exists, do so understandingly, and of
their own free will, and of their own free will
they could dissolve the bonds so accepted if
they felt disposed.
	It was telegraphed east and west that two
ladies had been admitted to the bar of Utah
as practicing lawyers, and the local press has
had considerable to say in regard to it; yet it
might with equal justness and more force have
been telegraphed that here in Utah, decried,
abused and maligned as it has been, women
enjoy more of what is contended for as woman’s
rights than they do in any State in the Federal
Union; and that they appreciate their position
and are seeking to qualify themselves for
spheres of usefulness to which their sisters in
other parts of the country can only yet look in
prospective.


﻿DON’T READ TRASH.
				——
	If there is any one thing extensively prac-
ticed that does not pay, it is the reading of silly,
trashy literature. It is a waste of time; and,
worse than that, it injures the memory, per-
verts the taste and hinders the advancement
and development of the intellectual powers of
the mind. Like vicious companions, worthless
reading steals an influence over young and un-
decided natures. For a time they realize no
evil effects from such sources, except that the
lightness and nonchalance which generally
characterize them are somewhat agreeable.
But they soon become fascinating and almost
irresistible; and engender dislike for anything
of a sounder and more reliable character.
Do not encourage the indulgence of such use-
less pastime. Get good books, and when you
read let it be for some good purpose; and al-
ways seek to become enlightened and not mis-
led by what you read.


WE ARE requested by Mr. Robert L. Camp-
bell, secretary of the Deseret Agricultural and
Manufacturing Society, to say, that where the
President of a Female Relief Society purchases
a life membership in the D. A. & M. Society,
in the name of the Relief Society over which
she presides, it will secure free admission to
the coming Fair for herself, her counselors, and
the treasurer and secretary of her Society. So
also when the head of a family has a life mem-
bership it secures free admission to his family
as well as himself.

HOME AFFAIRS.
				——
	THE APPROACHING general Conference
promises to be largely attended and more than
ordinarily interesting.
	A BOUNTIFUL harvest has been gathered
throughout the Territory, and the people
should feel proportionately thankful to a kind
Providence.
	THE OFFICE of the EXPONENT will, after the
5th inst., be found on West Temple street, op-
posite the City Market, upstairs in the build-
ing now being erected for the purpose.
	DURING THE past week lists of clubs of sub-
scribers have reached us from different parts of
the Territory, the money accompanying each.
Our friends who continue to work for the in-
creased circulation of the EXPONENT, have our
warmest thanks for their exertions in its be-
half.
	ON THE 3rd inst. the Territorial Fair com-
mences, and on the 6th inst. the Semi-Annual
Conference begins. These will draw a large
number of our friends to the city, any of whom
we shall be pleased to see at the office of the
EXPONENT, on West Temple street, opposite
the City Market.
	THE PHYSIOLOGICAL Class, organized some
weeks ago in the 15th Ward F. R. Society Hall,
meets now at the Social Hall, twice a week; at
four o’clock p.m., on Tuesdays and Thursdays.
Ladies interested In the study of physiology
will find it very advantageous, to connect
themselves with this class.
	ROBBERIES ARE on the increase in Salt Lake
city, with as much boldness as if the villains
were operating in the dead of winter. But
why need they be afraid? The third District
Court is in session, yet the presiding Judge, J.
B. McKean, has failed to grant the grand jury
which the Attorney General of the Territory
moved for, and rogues and criminals going un-
whipt of justice become bold In their villainies.
	IN THE present number of the EXPONENT
we reprint the first part of Miss Eliza R. Snow’s
brief history of the first organization of the
Female Relief Society in Nauvoo. The de-
mand for this sketch, after the first and second
numbers which contained it were exhausted,
has induced us to print a heavy edition of the
present number, with which to supply those
who desire it. Copies of the first eight num-
bers are not on sale at this office, but new sub-
scribers from this date can receive the EXPO-
NENT from the present number until the edition
now printed is all sent out, as we shall print an
equal quantity of copies of each subsequent
issue.


EDUCATE YOURSELF.
				——
	Woman was designed to be something
more than a domestic drudge; and it is not
right for her to confine herself exclusively,
to that monotonous calling, having no
thoughts, no interests, hopes or prospects
above and beyond so humble a sphere. For,
worthy and honorable as are all common
branches of labor, the hands should not alone
be industrious; but the brain should also be
instructed how to work, and allowed to ex-
pand and improve. And that not only in
things connected with the daily routine of
household duties, but in matters of greater,
and even less importance as well. Sensible
people say, that a girl should not be consid-
ered marriageable until she understands
how to make good bread and wash her own
clothes. Exactly. And we would add,
neither should she be imagined capable of
filling the position of a true wife and mother
unless she can, at least read and write un-
[new column]
derstandingly and converse intelligently
upon other topics than those concerning the
barn-yard and kitchen. And if, after mar-
riage, greater pains should be taken to in-
crease her fund of knowledge pertaining to
the proper care and management of a house
and family’s domestic interests, so also
should more attention and time be devoted
to her own moral and intellectual culture.
When a man has finished his labors for the
day, he usually has a little time for reading
or quiet contemplation and rest. It would
be justly thought absurd and cruel to require
of a child or servant, all the time to be
spent in work, with no chance for mental
improvement. But working women have,
somehow, so arranged, or disarranged, their
work that, generally speaking, it is never
done. And if they ever do sit down for a
moment’s rest, or take up a book or paper,
to use a familiar phrase, “there are a dozen
things staring at them, that want to be
done,” and they cannot enjoy the little
time which they feel as though they were
stealing. And thus a wall is often built,
confining woman to the narrow sphere of
domestic duties, in which she becomes self-
ishly wrapped up in her own interests, and
devoid of those tastes and sentiments
which might otherwise aid her in becoming
an agreeable and useful member of society.
Ill health or extreme poverty, may excuse
a woman for being kept much away from
society, and having little chance for mental
improvement. But even these disadvan-
tages would soon disappear, in most cases,
if a proper and wise reformation could be
called into requisition.
	It often occurs that as soon as a young
lady is married, she seems to think there is
no need for her to pursue her studies any
farther. She may have spent years of time
and cost her parents much expense, to ac-
quire an education, which, when it has as-
sisted in gaining her a husband, she has no
further use for. If that was her only motive
in pursuing her youthful studies, the object
was unworthy the labor and means expend-
ed. Rut woman should consider that for
her as well as for man, there is no end, no
stopping point to be found in the acquire-
ment of knowledge. And she should be
industrious and energetic in the pursuit of
useful information of every kind. It is an
excellent thing to know the easiest and
best methods to keep a house in perfect or-
der; how to prepare a meal of victuals
quickly and tastily; take the advantages of
a heavy washing and ironing, or spin your
own stocking yarn and knit it. But all this
does uot make a woman an interesting and
amiable companion, which it is her right
and duty to be, if her ways are awkward,
her language coarse and vulgar; in short,
her mind uncultivated. Yet, to be edu-
cated in these things will make her of
greater use to the world, than merely what
is now generally considered an accomplished
lady: that is, to have a knowledge of music,
be able to speak French or Latin, and dress
fashionably. Combine a consistent portion
of the two, and mix in a little of common
arithmetic and geography, morality, philo-
sophy and sense, and then go on securing
little gems of wisdom here and there,
wherever they are to be found. They will
never cease to exist, and the best educated
persons can do no better than to keep pick-
ing them up and adding to their store.
									G.


VERY large fires are reported to be again
raging in some of the Michigan woods, and
apprehensions are entertained in some quar-
ters of a repetition of the fearful scenes of
last fall.


FACTS AND FIGURES. 
Olympia has 312 school children.
The Memphis Industrial Exposition will
cost $30,000.
Slavery is to be gradually abolished in
Cuba and Porto Rico.
A petrified buffalo, full size, standing
and in the act of eating, has been found
near Spinneyville, Iowa.
California has sent by steamer her first-
cargo of wheat, wine and brandy directly to
England.
The largest water tank on the railroad
has just been completed at Albany; Its ca-
pacity is 75,000 gallons.
A Wayne county dog has this season
“chawed up” seventeen rattlesnakes. “Our”
dog beats that. It has killed five skunks in
as many weeks.
According to the State Geologist of Cali-
fornia, the view from the top of Mt. Diablo
is the finest in the world, not excepting any
of the Alps.
The Italian journals speak of a curious
discovery made at Pompeii—namely, a
glass bottle still full of oil. The liquid is
to be analyzed to ascertain its greater or less
degree of preservation.
Senator Trumbull stated in his speech
at Indianapolis, that there was “money
enough squandered by office-holders under
the Federal Government every year to buy
a house and farm and give $1,000 to every
man in the State of Indiana—and that there
are 400,000 men in that State.”
Italian papers report an increasing agi-
tation among the working classes in the
principal Italian towns. “The condition of
the working class,” says the “Persever-
anza,” “has in the last few years completely
altered. In the first place, the number of
workingmen has increased in an extraordi-
nary degree. The progress of industry, on
which we rightly congratulate ourselves,
has led to the establishment of centres of
labor which formerly had no existence.
Great masses are more easily agitated than
individuals, and it is evident that a strike
can be organized more effectually in a town
where there are a thousand workmen than
where there are a hundred. Wages have
not increased in the same proportion ns the
prices of the necessaries of life, besides
which the demand for labor is in this coun-
try greater than the supply.”
Professor Jurgeson, of Berlin, gives
an interesting account of the transfusion of
blood from persons in good health to one
who had been poisoned, with the most happy
results. He states that on the 9th of De-
cember, a young man was poisoned by tak-
ing a solution of the ignition mass of eight
bundles of matches. On the 11th of Febru-
ary, after causing the excitement of the
activity of the heart by champagne, the
transfusion of 580 cubic centimetres of
wholesome blood, which had just been taken
from three healthy persons, was effected
into the vein of the arm, 500 cubic centi-
metres being taken, of the patient’s own
blood, from an artery, at the same time-
Immediately an improvement in the condi-
tion of the patient took place, and in March
he was able to quit his bed. What a splen-
did thing it would be, were the doctors only
capable of ejecting all the diseased and cor-
rupt blood of individuals and transmitting
that which is healthy and pure; thus mak-
ing sound men and women of all the un-
sound ones. But the query is natural:
Where could they obtain sufficient pure
blood for the purpose?

﻿PEN AND SCISSORS.
Spiritualism is on the increase at Nash-
ville.
	Stanley-Africanus, though not thirty,
is gray haired.
	A Rattlesnake bite was cured lately at
Fayette, Ill., by liberal applications of
mud!
	A Peoria dentist announces that he ex-
tracts teeth with great pains. Most dentists
do.
	Leisure is sweet to those who have
earned it, but burdensome to those who get
it for nothing.
	The Edgefield and Kentucky road will
hereafter be known as the Nashville, Chi-
cago and St. Louis railroad.
	Josh Billings says very truly, “You’d bet-
ter not know so much than to know so many
things that ain’t so.”
	A stylish bonnet can be obtained from
Paris for $126. Indulgent husbands will
cut this out to show their wives.
	A little boy in Buffalo bled to death
from the effects of a wound in the leg,
caused by a glass bottle which was thrown
at him by a playmate.
	The peace league of the Hague has ad-
dressed a communication to the Emperors
of Russia, Germany and Austria in favor of
peace in Europe.
	In Switzerland a milkmaid who is a good
singer gets more salary than others, be-
cause under the influence of music cows
“give down” better and give more milk.
	What a glorious world this would be if
all its inhabitants could say, with Shake-
speare’s shepherd: “Sir, l am a true laborer;
earn what I wear; owe no man hate; envy
no man’s happiness; glad of other men’s
good; content with my farm.”
	A fellow at Norwich was bitten by a
dog. As soon as he recovered from his
fright he declared he would kill the animal.
“But the dog isn’t mad,” said the owner.
“Mad!” shouted the victim, exasperatingly,
“what in thunder has he got to be mad
about?”
	The band of an American circus travel-
ing in France was stupid enough to go out
into the streets in the Prussian military uni-
form they had borrowed or stolen in Ger-
many. Although they played the Marseil-
laise, they were compelled to fold their tents
like the Arab and steal away in front of a
whole infuriated town.
	Orange is said to be the proper color for
spectacles, instead of green or blue, for per-
sons of weak eyes. At the Milwaukee Sol-
diers’ Home a room in the hospital is lighted
through orange colored windows, and is
used, with very satisfactory results, for pa-
tients who have certain diseases of the eyes
requiring the exclusion of the actinic rays 
of light.
	Somebody wrote to the editor of a vil-
lage paper to ask how he would “break an
ox?” The editor replied as follows: “If
only one ox, a good way would be to hoist
him, by means of a long chain attached to
his tail, to the top of a pole forty feet from
the ground. Then hoist him by a rope tied
to his horns to another pole. Then descend
on his back a five-ton pile-driver, and if
that don’t break him, let him start a coun-
try newspaper and trust people for sub-
scription. One of these two ways will do it
sure.”


THE MOORISH GIRLS.
	The young Moorish girl of Algeria, is one
of the prettiest and most fascinating crea-
tures in existence. Her features are oval,
her eyes are wonderfully expressive; but
she is as deceitful as the most artful Euro-
pean coquette. The gracefulness of her car-
riage is exceedingly striking. Her hair,
which is not unfrequently straight, though
sometimes curly, is as dark as the raven’s
plumage. It Is generally arranged in bands,
and is passed round the head and fastened
in a simple or double tail, which is tied
with ribbons, and almost sweeps the ground.
The “coiffure” Is covered with a velvet
“chachia,” which is fastened under the
chin with two pieces of ribbon.
	The Moorish maiden has very small hands
and tiny feet. Her stature is about the
middle height, and her carriage graceful.
Her costume is generally very elegant. The
plainest toilette consists of a gauze chemise,
with short sleeves and a “serroual” (pair of
drawers), made of white calico or muslin,
very wide, and reaching down to the knee.
It not unfrequently happens that the Moor-
ish maiden wears, in addition to the above
toilette, the “djabadoli,” a kind of richly
embroidered vest, which seldom or never
reaches above the shoulders, and which
tends to contract the chest.
	At other times she wears the “rlila,” a
kind of jacket made of silk, and richly em-
broidered in gold. She wears over her
hips, not a crinoline, but a large piece of
striped silk called “fouta,” which reaches
down to the ground; and over that a silken
sash, the gold-embroidered ends of which
hang down in front. When the “rlila” is
worn, the “coiffure” is changed; and the
“chachia” is replaced by a silk handkerchief
of bright colors, or else by the “sarma,”
which is nothing else than the “hemine,”
which the French women wore in the four-
teenth century. The “sarma,” which is
made of gold or silver thread, is generally
a masterpiece of embroidery. We must
not omit to mention the “babouches,” which
are made of green velvet, and which almost
cripple the feet as much as the slippers
worn by the Chinese ladies.
	The Moorish women never move abroad
without being closely veiled. They en-
velop themselves so in a white “kaik” in
Algiers, and in a blue one at Constantine,
that it is utterly impossible to catch a
glimpse of any other part of their features
than their eyes.
	Such is the physical portrait of a Moorish
woman. Her moral one has nothing very
attractive in it, which arises from the want
of education. If of poor parents, she is
beaten frequently, and sadly cuffed about
by her parents. Her only wish is to escape
from their tyranny.
	If her parents are rich, even then she is a
stranger to domestic comforts. She is gen-
erally secluded in a corner of the house, and
left entirely to the care of a negress; and
as she grows up her sole aim and delight
is to become one of the most arrant co-
quettes, and to plunge into all kinds of in-
trigue. A woman, according to the ideas
of all Mussulmans, is nothing more nor less
than a mere toy—a plaything, a piece of
funiture, which every man ought to posses,
but who has no right to think or to act.
Hence the cause of her profound ignorance
and semi-barborous state; and yet the Moor-
ish women possess wonderful talent for
learning. There is a French institution at
Algiers to which young Moorish girls are
admitted.
	Those children who belong to the poorer
class, and who are sent there by their pa-
rents for the sake of the small sum that is
given to all who attend, and because they
get rid of them during the day, are remark-
ably intelligent and quick at learning. The
Moorish girl is not named until eight days
after her birth; but until that ceremony
takes place she is always called “Fatima,”
as the mother of the Prophet. Their prin-
cipal names are Alcha, Bedra, Djohar, Fati-
ma, Halima, Haouria, Khredoubja, Khreira,
Meriem, Mimi, Mina, Mouni, Orgoya, Rosa,
Safia, Sbida, Zeina, Zohra. Not a few of
these names have very flattering significa-
tions; for instance, “Zeina” signifies pretty,
and “Sbida” orange flower. 
	The Moorish maiden marries when very
young; but scarcely any change takes place
in her social position after she quits her
paternal roof to abide under that of her hus-
band. If she moves in good Arab society,
she passes most of her time at the baths
(the gossiping shops of the East and of Al-
geria) and at her toilette; the rest in eating
all kinds of sweetmeats, disputing with her
husband’s other wives, or making appoint-
ments with her friends to visit the mosques,
where she goes to offer up prayers to the
holy marabouts that she may be the mother
of many sons. Thus does she pass her days
in trifles light as air until a certain period,
which, alas! comes but too soon—that is,
when her youth and beauty vanish like a
dream—and then she foils into a lethargy
from which the hand of death alone relieves
her.
	Should she belong to the poorer class,
then her condition is much more to be la-
mented. She is trained up from youth to
perform the most laborious work, and for
which, like the donkey, “she receives more
kicks than presents.” Her husband beats
her and her children, and her condition is
neither more nor less than that of a domes-
tic animal.


An Old-Fasioned  Mother.—Thank
Fortune, some of us have an old-fashioned
mother—not a woman of the period, en-
amelled and painted, with her great chig-
non, her curls and bottines, whose white,
jewelled hands have never felt the clasp of
baby fingers, but a dear,old-fashioned, sweet-
voiced mother, with eyes in whose depths
the love-light shown, and brown hair
threaded with silver, lying smoothly upon
her faded cheek. Those dear hands, worn
with toil, which guided our tottering steps
in childhood and smoothed our pillow in
sickness. Blessed is the memory of an old-
fashioned mother! It floats to us now like 
the beautiful perfume of some woodland
blossom. The music of other voices may be
lost, but the entrancing memory of her
voice will echo in our souls forever. Other
faces will fade away and be forgotten, but
her's will shine on until the light from
heaven’s portals shall glorify our own.
	When in the fitful phases of busy life our
feet wander back to the old homestead, and,
crossing the well-worn threshold, stand
once more in the low, quiet room, so hal-
lowed by her presence, how the feeling of
childish innocence and dependence comes 
over us! How many times, when the
tempter lures us on, has the memory of
those sacred hours, that mother’s word, her
advice and counsel, saved us from plunging
into the deep abyss of sin! Years have
filled great drifts between her and us, but
they have not hidden from our sight the 
glory of her pure, unselfish love.


A chief of the Chippewas drives a dray
in Detroit.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿ LINES.
		[Written for the EXPONENT.]
Awake, my muse, so long thou’st slumbered,
	All thy laurel wreaths will fade;
Days have passed away unnumbered
	Since I last invoked thy aid.

I would have thee, while awaking,
	With bright laurel twine my brow,
For ambition’s morn is breaking
	Brightly o'er my pathway now.

Highest thoughts, bright aspirations,
	Oft my beating bosom swell;
Shall I banish such reflections !
	Shall I bid such thoughts farewell !

No, but rather let me cherish
	Thoughts so bright, so pure, and deep;
Every idle dream shall perish,
	Every lighter care shall sleep.

Life’s bread pathway lies before me—
	Shall I travel without aim ?
Guardian spirits hover o’er me !
	Help me strive to gain a name !

Hope, bright star, shall onward guide me
	Through night’s deepest, darkest gloom;
Shed its radiance o’er my pathway, 
	Light my passage to the tomb.
  Provo.								M. J. T.


﻿BE KIND TO THE OLD.
Be kind to the old, back in the spring of life,
They may have been o’ertaken by the storms of
	strife,
And amid summer’s sweet buds, fruit and flowers,
They may have seen but few of pleasant, happy
	hours.

Be kind, then to the old, for in the scenes gone by.
Dark, foggy clouds have dimmed the once clear
	eye,
When sorrow’s trying flood did nigh o’erflow,
The hopeful promise of long, long ago.

Be kind to the old, for autumn cool has come;
The Reaper soon will gather the ripe harvest home;
Life’s winter neareth—Ah! how cold and drear!
With no warm, loving smiles the aged heart to
	cheer.

Be kind unto the old as you would blessing seek—
God loves the loving, the filial and the meek;
Parents will cherish, children praises sing.
And an approving conscience choicest blessings
	bring.
San Bernardino.						MINNIE.


THINGS WORTH FORGETTING.
	How much wiser we should be if we could
remember all the things worth remember-
ing that occur day by day all around us; and
how much better we should be if we could
forget all that is worth forgetting! It is
almost frightful, and altogether humili-
ating, to think how much there is in the
common on-going of domestic and social life
which deserves nothing but to be instantly
and for ever forgotten. Yet it is equally amaz-
ing how large a class seem to have no other
business but to repeat and perpetuate these
very things. That is the vocation of gos-
sips—an order of society that perpetuates
more mischief than all the combined plagues
of Egypt put together. Blessed is that man
or woman who can let drop all the burs and
thistles, instead of picking them up and
fastening them on to the passenger. Would
we only let the vexing and malicious say-
ings die, how fast the lacerated and scandal-
ridden world would get healed and tran-
quillized! Forget the gossipings and
bickerings and backbitings, and remember
only the little gleams of sunshine and po-
etry that can illuminate the humblest life
if we will only drive away and forget the
clouds engendered by things that should
never be remembered.


﻿MISCELLANEOUS.
	A little girl of eight or ten summers
being asked what dust was, replied that it
was “mud with the juice squeezed out.”
	The public should remember that the rate
of postage upon transient newspapers to
any port in the United States is now only
one cent.
	Wise anger is like fire from the flint;
there is a great ado to bring it out; and
when it does come it is out again imme-
diately.—“Matthew Henry.”
	A slight answer to an intricate and use-
less question is a fit cover to such a dish—a
cabbage-leaf is good enough to cover a dish
of mushrooms.—“Jeremy Taylor.”
	Papa.—“Bless me! what a glare! Where’s
your window-curtains, Maria?”
	Mamma.—“Now, don’t be angry, George!
You refused the girls their Dolly Vardens,
and as I would not let them cut up your
dressing-gown, the poor things were forced
to take the curtains.”
	The Republican women of Massachu-
setts have pronounced on the political situa-
tion, at a meeting held in Boston on the
25th ult., and in an address to the women
of the United States. They congratulate
the women of the country on the progress
which the Woman’s rights movement has
made; and look upon Charles Sumner as a
political apostate. Alas, for Sumner, he
has fallen from grace, of the kind Massa-
chusetts appreciates.
	Expectations.—It is reported as a re-
cent occurrence, that a poor man fell in love
with an heiress, and the passion being re-
turned it only needed the consent of the
parents to make them happy. At length,
meeting the father, he asked for the daugh-
ter’s hand.
	“How much money can you command?”
asked the millionaire, gruffly.
	“Not much,” was the reply.
	“What are your expectations?”
	“Well, to tell the truth, I ‘expect,’ if you
refuse your consent, to run away with your
daughter and marry her without it.”
	The Future of Timber.—That the
timber interest of the United States is be-
coming a very important one is not a mat-
ter of surprise, when it is known that
seventy-five million dollars’ worth of fuel
is burned every year in the United States.
Locomotives consume over eight million
cords of wood annually, and over a hundred
million dollars’ worth of sawed lumber is
yearly employed in building and in manu-
factures. Four million acres of forest dis-
appear every year before the axe, to supply
all these demands. No wonder that timber
planting is occupying so much attention in
the west. It is said that California has cre-
ated an officer for the State, of Superintend-
ent of Forest Culture, at a salary of fifteen
thousand dollars a year. This is not prob-
ably wholly accurate, but it shows the di-
rection of public thought. Wood is being
continually put to new uses; one of these,
wooden pavements, will take an immense
quantity in all large cities. there is some
opposition at present to their general intro-
duction on account of the early decay—
about ten years being the limit to this kind
of causeway. But it has been found after
all cheaper than stone blocks lasting fifty
years, because the injury to horses is less,
the wagon tear is less, and the time saved
in going over them enormous. This saving
of time will alone pay for the whole cost of
the road. It is very probable, therefore,
that the consumption of timber will go on
much faster in the future than in the past,
as its uses are steadily increasing.


BUSINESS.
	Many of the business men in the differ-
ent parts of the country, seem to consider
that if they do not murder a man for his
money, nor actually steal it from him, their
dealings must be honest and honorable. No
difference how great the advantage they
take of the necessitous circumstances in
which another is placed, or of his mistakes
or ignorance; it is the “Almighty dollar”
that they are after, and with them it is no
matter how it comes, so they get to clutch
it. But they would not like to be thought
dishonest. Oh, no; they are “our honor-
able business men!”
	And some women seem to think that it is
none of their business where the “money
comes from,” if they can only get to handle
a good share of it. They never question
their husbands as to the prospects ahead,
debentures or anything of the kind; such
affairs are no business of theirs, and they
do not trouble themselves about other peo-
ple’s concerns.
	The business of every just and upright
man, should be in all his dealings with his
fellow men, to see that they, as well as
himself, are benefitted and blessed. And
no true woman will allow her selfish propen-
sities to so far overpower her consciousness
of right, as to require means for her per-
sonal gratification which could be more
wisely spent, or which have not been ob-
tained in a legitimate way.
	It is everybody’s business to do as much
good and as little harm in the world as pos-
sible, and no one has a right to do anything
contrary to this rule.					L.


﻿RIGHTS AND WRONGS.
				—
	The cogency with which “rights” and
“wrongs” have long been bandied about, by
different parties and denominations, and
the impromptu manner in which these sub-
jects are being handled now, show a spirit
of unjust and relentless constraint on the
part of those in power, while to those who
clamor for privileges with which they are
not, but should be, invested, in many in-
stances, it gives an air of unwarrantable de-
fiance.
	How these things will terminate remains
with the near future to which we are fast
approaching. But one of two things must
inevitably occur: Either the two bodies
will collide, or a grand union of interests
must take place. Why not the latter?
Why should not hirelings receive just com-
pensation for their labors, and where their
exertions serve to enrich their masters, why
should not they likewise be rewarded by a
generous share in the profit made? Why
should not woman stand side by side with
man, and feel that her interests are his, and
that apart from the general welfare and
happiness of the human race, her aspirations
and anticipations are nothing? True woman
has no desire to step ahead of man, or to be
considered superior to him. Her present
aim is but to walk with dignity, keeping
pace with the real whole-souled man, and
not being thrust back to grovel in ignorance
and vice with the lawless and unprincipled
libertines, who in the forms of men infest
the country to an alarming extent. Men
who are just and wise, do not seek to pre-
vent woman’s coming up even with them;
nor will they refuse her their protection
against the persecutions of villainous ma-
rauders, who would rob her of justice and
peace, who seek to keep her out of her true
position by the side of man, which she will
ultimately hold—not by force but by mutual
agreement.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿WHY WOMEN ARE EXTRAVAGANT. 
			   —
Somebody once said that the women of
to-day are so extravagant in dress, and so
helpless in other respects, that none, but
rich men can afford to marry, and foolish
people have been saying the same thing, or
something very like it, ever since. Every
time a man fails in business, people take a
mental inventory of his wife’s wardrobe and
cry out, “Poor fellow! he was ruined by her
extravagance!”
No account is taken of his club expenses,
or his unnecessary restaurant bills, or his
fast horses, or the vanity which prompted
him to buy a bigger or a finer house than
he needed. Nothing is said of his dress
coats made by some Monsieur Snip, who
charges extra price because he calls himself
an “artist tailor.” The man may have
gambled his money away, or have lost in
reckless stock speculation, for all anybody
knows to the contrary, while his wife, be-
lieving him to be rich, has dressed and
lived only as his circumstances warranted,
doing it, too, only for his sake, that he
might not be ashamed to introduce her as
his wife. Or, if she had been as extrava-
gant as he, the fault is usually his so long as
the fact remains that any proper man can
lovingly control the course of any proper
wife. Women are often extravagant. The
fact cannot be denied. But that they are
more so than men is by no means true. As
a rule, every woman wishes to live within
her husband’s income, and in nine families
out of ton all the economizing is done by
the wife. This constant iteration of the
charge against women, however, has se-
cured a kind of passive acceptance for the
theory, and nothing is more common now
than for the young men with salaries of two
or three thousand a year to lament their in-
ability to marry, because women are so ex-
travagant. One of these has recently told
the public through the newspapers how it
takes all his salary of two thousand dollars
to support a single man, and looking over
his bills of items and finding that it takes
fifty dollars worth of perfumery every year
to keep him sweet, we quite agree with
him that he should not marry.—“Hearth
and Home.”

			WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

			A UTAH LADIES JOURNAL.

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply

	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves ? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"

	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.

	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.

	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:	
1 Copy one year, $2.00 	1 Copy,six months, $1.00 
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months, 9.00
	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents 
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

	Address all communications and subscriptions, 
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City, 
Utah.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. I. 	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, OCTOBER 15, 1872.	NO. 10.


﻿		EDITORIAL NOTES.

	TURKISH LADIES are discarding thick
veils. The world moves!
	BRAVE WOMEN of the period—Those who
dare to dress comfortably.
	 NOVEMBER 5TH is the day on which the
Presidential election will be held.
	FALLEN GREATNESS—The “Cardiff Giant"
bought to be set up by an Indiana farmer as
a gatepost.
	DACOTAH HAS had an earthquake.  Are
they traveling east from California without
giving Utah a call? They can be spared
here.
	MRS. FAIR was acquitted on her second
trial in San Francisco, and the result called
forth an abundance of wrathy newspaper
criticisms.
	NO RACE nor clime can claim lengthened
years as a special heritage. A woman of color
died in Philadelphia a few days ago aged
a hundred and five years.
	GEORGE FRANCIS TRAIN has again gone
to Europe, but will return this month. He
has still inscribed on his banner “N. P. A.”
—Next President of America — Next?
When?
	PRUSSIA MAINTAINS Protestantism by re-
fusing to permit Mormon Elders to preach
in its dominions, and by expelling Jesuits.
Prussian Protestantism must be weak-kneed
and delicate to be so afraid of contact.
	CINCINNATI HAD an inaugural attempt at
a war of races, on the night of the 7th inst.,
when an Irish procession and a colored mob
came into collision. A general war of races
would be more terrible than a war of sec-
tions, of countries, or even of kindreds.
	THE MICHIGAN University has received
nineteen young ladies in the “freshman”
class, and Michigan University is honored
in so doing. The silly prejudices against
according woman the educational rights en-
joyed by men are disappearing
	PENNSYLVANIA, OHIO and Nebraska held
elections on the 8th inst., and gave majori-
ties for the Republican candidates. Indiana,
on the same day, gave a majority for the
Democratic candidate. The result is not
favorable to Mr. Greeley’s election in No-
vember
	WORD COMES from the Pacific Coast that
a woman suffrage bill has been introduced
into the Oregon legislature. Oregon may be
just enough to have the bill passed and be-
come law,and teach Massachsetts that justice,
truth and light are to come from the
west to the east, reversing the old order of
things.
	A BEQUEST of $350,000 has been made by
Miss Sophia Smith, of Massachusetts, to en-
dow a new college for women at Northamp-
ton in that State, where young women may
have means and models of instruction equal
to those now enjoyed by young men. Telling
the simple fact is ample eulogy on Miss
Smith, all honor to her!
	SCOTT'S LINES commencing “Love rules
the camp, the court the grove” might be
altered in this mercenary age to “Mammon”
exercising the ruling power. It is now said
Pere Hyacinth made $75,000 by his mar-
riage. Of course money was no induce-
ment, but it was convenient to find it a
portion of the matrimonial bargain.
	PRINCE NAPOLEON endeavored to take
up his residence in Paris last week, his
wife, the Princess Clothilde, accompanying
him. But the government of Thiers per-
emptorily ordered them to leave,
and the princely pair had to travel.
The name of Napoleon is not as popular in
France as it was before the surrender at
Sedan.
	WASHINGTON D. C., had the strange
sight a few days ago of six persons being ar-
raigned in one of its courts, charged with
murder; Washington is moral, and temper-
ate, and virtuous, and free from crime; and
that is the reason probably why it becomes
so potently stirred up against Utah occasion-
ally. Were these six alleged murderers an
accident?
	MORE TROUBLE in Spain. This time it is
a revolt of the troops at the town of Ferrol,
a strongly fortified sea-port with some 17,-
000 inhabitants, and some of the citizens
have joined the insurrectionists. King
Amadeus is verifying the proverb:	“Un-
easy lies the head that wears a crown;” and
the prospects of a peaceful reign over the
revolution-loving Spaniards are not very
bright for him.
	WHEN THE FALL and Winter fashions be-
come fairly matured they promise to pre-
sent as much hideousness as any late ones
have manifested; and people will admire
themselves imitating a style of hair-dressing
that has been fashionable in central Africa
for generations; while clothing will be
modeled for every purpose but the comfort
and convenience of the wearer.
	A PROPOSITION to license and “ticket”
the inmates of houses of ill-repute in New
York is being discussed, and the “Star” of
that city sensibly calls for the same rules be-
ing applied to the male visitors at the
places. It adds: “This theory that the men
have ‘rights’ which women are not to share
in that direction, is not only unchristian but
indecent.” A sentiment with which every
right-minded person will agree.
	A HOUSE fell in Louisville, Kentucky,
the other day, killing a family inside, and
warrants were promptly issued against the
architect, the builder, and the foreman en-
gaged in the erection of it, charging them
with the murder of the persons killed. If
men were more generally held responsible
for the fatal results of their carelessness or
wilful neglect to take necessary precautions
where human life is involved, there would
be fewer accidents by railroads, on steam-
ships, by explosions, and other ways by
which life is recklessly slaughtered. It is
time this much-needed reformation was be-
gun, and the action of the Louisville City
courts is a healthy sign.
	THE ANNIVERSARY of the birth day of
Father Matthews, the once famous Irish
temperance preacher, was widely celebrated
by the temperance men of the United States,
on Thursday last, the 10th inst. One good
thing Father Matthews did for Ireland. Be-
fore he traveled through the country stir-
ring up her children to enthusiasm by his
fervid eloquence, and administering the
pledge of total abstinence to thousands, It
was not considered a disgrace to be intoxi-
cated, only so far as it showed a limited ca-
pacity to drink fiery whisky. But since his
day, wide-spread as intemperance still is, it
is viewed as disgraceful and degrading to
be seen drunk. When some reformer can
accomplish as much for these United States,
he will be worthy of a statue of California
gold, on a pedestal of Nevada and Utah
silver, and gemmed with Alaska and Arizo-
na diamonds.
	“FATHERLESS, MOTHERLESS, no friends,
no home!” were words contained in a letter
which helped to show the cause of a suicide,
lately committed in London by an unfortu-
nate girl about twenty years of age, who
preferred death by her own agency, rather
than to live and follow the paths of vice.
She asked forgiveness of all who might
hear of her, for the crime she was about to
commit. It is easier to forgive such as her,
than the enlightened Christian age whose
follies drive them to deeds of desperation.
While President Brigham Young sets an ex-
ample before the people of the Church of
Jesus Christ, and the whole world, by feed-
ing, clothing, sheltering and schooling a
great many young people who would other-
wise be homeless and friendless; giving
them places with his own children, becom-
ing indeed a father to the fatherless; the
world derides him and his ways, of which
it understands nothing, and would gladly
do with its benefactor to-day, as the Jews
with its Redeemer less than nineteen centu-
ries ago. Yet innocence, driven to desper-
ation, rushes headlong into eternity, and
the Christian age hardly gives a glance to
the miserable unfortunate, but hurries on
preparing others for the same horrid future.
	THE "MISSOURI REPUBLICAN,” under
the heading “The Oppressed women of
Utah,” has the following sensible remarks,
which are presented for general considera-
tion to pusedo philanthropists, silly senti-
mentalists, and anti-Mormon lecturers:
“We have heard a good deal about the op-
pressed and degraded women of Utah, and
the unclean and tyrannical vassalage which
the Mormon social system imposes upon the
female sex. This string has been tuned
and played upon until it appears to have
snapped. It seems now that the women are
almost every body in Utah. They have
more power and more rights than they have
anywhere else on this green earth, and the
boasted lord of creation is entirely a second-
ary personage in the land of saints. This
may look like a strange condition for Utah,
of all the world, but it is true. That Terri-
tory and its social system seems to have
been the lock and key by which women
have at last entered into the wider, nobler
sphere for which they have prayed and
worked. An incident which calls up the
privileges which the women of Utah enjoy,
lately occurred in Salt Lake City. Miss
Phoebe Couzens, of St. Louis, and Miss
Georgie Snow, daughter of the Attorney-
General of the Territory, were admitted to
the bar in the District Court, and the cere-
monies were imposing. The brace of pretty
girls besieged the august Court of Chief
Justice McKean, and the Court capitulated.”


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿MORTALITY.

Ye dainty mosses, lichens gray,
	Pressed each to each in tender fold,
And peacefully thus, day by day,
	Returning to your mould.

Brown leaves, that with aerial grace
	Slip from your branch like birds a-wing,
Each leaving in the appointed place
	Its buds of future spring.

If we, God’s conscious creatures knew
	But half your faith in our decay,		
We should not tremble as we do
	When summoned clay by clay.

But with an equal patience sweet
	We should put off this mortal gear,
In whatsoe’er new form is meet
	Content to re-appear;—

Knowing each germ of life He gives
	Must have in Him its source and rise,
Being that of His being lives,
	May change but never dies.

Ye dead leaves, dropping soft and low,
	Ye mosses green and lichens fair,
Go to your graves, as I will go,
	For God is also there.
						MRS. MULOCK CRAIK.


﻿THE FEMALE RELIEF SOCIETY.

A BRIEF SKETCH OF ITS ORGANIZATION AND WORK-
	INGS IN THE CITY OF NAUVOO,HANCOCK CO., ILLS.

			BY ELIZA. R. SNOW.

	[Reprinted from No. 2 of the EXPONENT.]

			(CONCLUDED.)
	When organized, the Society consisted of
nineteen members, exclusive of its officers.
This number augmented from time to time,
until at the close of the fiscal year it
amounted to 1,158. The meetings were so
large that the largest hall in the city was
densely crowded and it became necessary to
adjourn to the Grove, the usual place in
the Summer for holding Sabbath meetings,
which was provided with a suitable stand
and a supply of seats for large assemblies.
	The Society soon became so popular that
even those of doubtful character in several
instances applied for admissiou, and to pre-
vent imposition by extending membership
to such ones inadvertently, stricter rules
were adopted than seemed requsite at first.
Each one wishing to join the Society was re-
quired to present a certificate of her good
moral character, signed by two or more re-
sponsible persons.
	The meetings were opened and closed
by singing and prayer, and systematic order
was observed throughout. In each meeting
reports were given by those whose duty it
was to visit from house to house and inquire
into the circumstances of the sick and desti-
tute—donations were received and those
subjects discussed which appertain to wo-
man’s duties, influence, responsibilities,
etc., etc.—whatever has a tendency to bene-
fit and elevate society at home and abroad.
	Frequently President Joseph Smith, Bish-
op N. K. Whitney and brethren of the
Twelve met with the sisters, and through
the inspiration of the Spirit of God, imparted
rich counsel and intelligence; instructing,
not only in duties concerning the poor, but
also in all the relations of life, as wives, mo-
thers, daughters, sisters, and, as saints of
the Most High, our duties to one another
and our responsibilities as examples to the
world, etc.
	The first Winter after the Society was or-
ganized, was exceedingly cold and severe.
Many, in consequence of exposure and hard-
ship in their expulsion from the State of
Missouri, and the unhealthiness of the cli-
mate of Nauvoo, had been reduced by sick-
ness to destitution, and had it not been for
[new column]
the timely aid of the F. R. Society would
have suffered very much, and probably some
would have perished.
	About the last of July, 1842, when some
of the officials of Missouri were taking meas-
ures to drag President Joseph Smith from
Illinois by an illegal process, the F. R. Soci-
ety sent a petition to Thomas Carlin, Gov-
ernor of Illinois, in behalf of Pres. Smith,
claiming his protection as chief magistrate.
The petiton was signed by every member of
the Society, and presented by its President,
Mrs. Emma Smith, accompanied by Mrs.
Amanda Smith and Miss E. R. Snow. The
Governor’s residence was in Quincy, fifty
miles south from Nauvoo.
	The following is transcribed from my
journal, written after my return: “The
Governor received us with as much cordi-
ality and politeness as His Excellency was
master of, assuring us of his protection by
saying that the laws and Constitution of our
country should be his polar star in case of any
difficulty. He manifested much friendship,
and it remains for time and circumstances
to prove the sincerity of his professions.”
	We subsequently ascertained that at the
very time that the ladies of Nauvoo were
visiting him, presenting the petition and
listening to his protestations of friendship
and love for legal right and justice, his em-
issaries were engaged in secret plotting in
his behalf, against the rights and safety of
President Smith, and aiding the unprinci-
pled bloodhounds of Missouri. But what-
ever the result, the F. R. Society had made
a noble effort, and it was compensated by
knowing that the great, good man, Joseph
Smith, appreciated it.
	The city of Nauvoo was divided into four
wards, and after the Society became too nu-
merous to convene in one assembly, it was
decided to meet alternately in the different
wards from week to week so as to give equal
opportunities to all.
	Pres. Joseph Smith donated a city lot to
the Society for the purpose of making homes
for the homeless, and also to furnish work
to those who were able to work and were
out of employment, which he proposed
deeding to the Treasurer and her successors
in office, for the use of the Society. He
also donated the frame of a house, and made
arrangements for it to be moved on the
aforesaid lot, for a commencement of the es-
tablishment, But this benevolent and judi-
cious project, with many others which were
designed for the amelioration of the condi-
tion of suffering humanity, was blasted in
the bud by the blighting hand of religious
persecution, and through which the once
beautiful city of Nauvoo became a desola-
tion, and its inhabitants exiled to Mexico.
	God, who makes the wrath of man praise
Him, has overruled, as He ever does in all
things, for the good of them who put their
trust in Him. In having been driven from
a city, we have become a Territory—should
have been a State—and here, the Female
Relief Society, more amply developed than
in Nauvoo, has extended its branches to
every ward, and settlement from Bear Lake
in the North to Santa Clara in the South,
and yet, as seen in prospective, it has but
little more than emerged from its embryo
state in comparison to its great future.


﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
				—
	Miss Eliza R. Snow, President of the Fe-
male Relief Societies, accompanied by Mrs.
Elizabeth Howard and the editor of the
EXPONENT, paid a visit recently to Payson,
Utah County, and held a meeting of the
F. R. Society of that city, attended by many
of the adjacent settlements, in the
[new column]
Payson New Tabernacle on September 26th,
at 2 o’clock, p.m.
	Bishop J. S. Tanner and other Elders, pres-
ent by invitation, occupied seats in the
stand.
	The meeting was called to order by Mrs.
B. Jane Simons, President of the Payson
Society, and was opened by singing and
prayer; after which President Miss E. R.
Snow addressed the assembly.
	She said the name, “Female Relief So-
ciety” was suggested and established by
vote when the first organization took place.
The question had been agitated for some
time, whether the name would not be
rendered more appropriate by the omission
of the word “Female,” and the calling of
these associations merely “Relief Societies.”
She thought that this modification would
improve the application; and others with
whom she had communicated on the subject
held the same opinion. But as the name
was established by vote, it must also be
changed by an expression from a majority
of those interested, if at all, because every
thing we do should be done legally. She
then called for an expression of the feelings
of those present and all voted for the modi-
fication of the name. The speaker contin-
ued, “The sisters of Payson hold a very
warm place in my heart; they have proved
themselves ready to make sacrifices of their
own feelings for the general good. Those
who are the most noble, cultivated and re-
fined, are ever the first to sacrifice their
feelings for the good of others. Woman fills
an important sphere; her duties are most
significant; and for this reason she should be
educated. It is admitted that woman forms
the character of society; that she gives char-
acter to her children, and that the most
minute parts of their education depend up-
on her. It is quite as necessary that we
study the superstructure of the human body,
as many other things upon which much
time is spent. We are calling on those sis-
ters who are sufficiently ambitious and per-
severing, to come forward and aid in organ-
izing and supporting classes for the purpose
of gaining knowledge pertaining to the laws
of life and health. We want a union of faith
and prayers upon this subject from the sister-
hoods throughout the Territory; and do not
expect to accomplish much without the co-
operation and assistance of the brethren,
but this we shall have. Our girls should be
educated in order that they may become
wise, good mothers, and plant the seeds pf
true, rich intelligence in the future genera-
tion. Many of the young men of our com-
munities would be less intemperate find un-
stable, had their mothers better understood
the laws which govern human nature.”
	The meeting was also addressed by Mrs.
Elizabeth Howard, Miss L. L. Greene,
Elders Isaiah Coombs, John Loveless, Robt.
Wimmer, Gustave Simons, Orrawell Sim-
ons, President Mrs. B. J. Simons—who
suggested that another meeting be held in
the evening which was voted for unani-
mously—Counsellor Mrs. Agnes Douglas
and Bishop J. S. Tanner, who also dismissed
the meeting by prayer.
	At 7 o’clock, a general invitation to all
who were desirous of attending having been
extended, a very respectable congregation
of gentlemen and ladies gathered and
listened to short but interesting and edify-
ing remarks, from Elders George Rust,
D. Lant and Miss E. R. Snow. A welcome to
Sister Snow was also read.
	In her remarks, Miss Snow said to the
sisters, “Our aims have been too low; we
must improve them. We have been in the
habit of thinking we could do so little.”
She recommended the organization of a


Young Ladies’ Society, and, if she might be
permitted, would recommend the brethren
to organize Young Gentlemen’s Societies
also.
	The kind hospitality with which Miss
Snow and her companions were received
and entertained during their visit to Pay-
son, by all, and particularly by Mrs Agnes
Douglas and family, deserves honorable
mention. They were called upon by a
number of the sisters, among whom
were Counsellor Mrs. Moore and Mrs. Mary
A. Hardy, Secretary of the Payson Relief
Society.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	MOTHERS in general, remarks Miss S., do
not realize how early the education of their
children commences. Children are born
with intelligence undeveloped; and as it is
manifested at times in the expressions and
actions of the tiny infant, it understands
more, and has really more knowledge of
what we say to it, than people gener-
ally give it credit for. That child upon its
mother’s lap—a baby six or eight months
old—knows from her kisses and caresses
that she loves it; that when it jumps and
coos she is pleased with its brightness and
good nature; and if it ever cries and is dis-
tressed, it realized from the soothing, pity-
ing tone and the tender expression of its
mother’s face, that she sympathizes and suf-
fers with it. It knows, too, if she is truth-
ful and honest with it. Its education is al-
ready begun. Mothers, if you would have
your children love and speak the truth,
never let them hear you speak the least un-
truth. If you would have them virtuous,
charitable and generous, cultivate the same
qualities in yourselves. And if you want
them to be reflective and studious, prayer-
ful and diligent in doing good, let them
see you the same in your every day course;
and even before the little ones make their
appearance upon the grand stage of life, let
these principles be implanted in their or-
ganizations. We cannot plant thistle seeds
and expect fig trees to grow from them. If
you desire your offspring to be healthy
and live long, take care that you do not en-
gender in their physical natures the seeds
of disease and premature decay. First learn
wisdom yourselves, and then you will be
potent to impart the same to future genera-
tions. In giving instructions of any kind,
or corrections, light or heavy, it is a thing
worth remembering, that “All wise govern-
ment begins with self-government.”
	BATHING.—The first thing to be done
when a bath is to be given is to prepare the
room, making it of a comfortable tempera-
ture. The second is to prepare the bath,
using soft water, and making it of the right
temperature, as indicated by a thermome-
ter. Persons sometimes ask us to explain
what is meant by certain temperatures, so
that they can get along without a thermome-
ter. This is impossible. The terms, hot,
cold, warm, tepid, are so indefinite and con-
vey such different impressions to different
persons, as to be entirely unreliable in giv-
ing directions. If a person in fever is to be
packed, his conditions are much more readi-
ly controlled by wetting two sheets in
water at 90°, wringing them but slightly,
and packing him in them, or even puting
him into a fresh pack when the one becomes
heated, than by putting him into a cold
sheet, The idea that the hotter a person is
the colder should be his bath, is productive
of great mischief. The rule is exactly the
reverse of this; that is: a person in a high
fever should have his bath at a higher tem-
perature than if he had no fever; for what,
[new column]
in the latter case, would be a pleasant tem-
perature to him, would be shockingly cold
in the former.
	BLEEDING AT THE LUNGS.—Put cold
cloths on the head, a hot brick or hot bath
for the feet, and on the throat and chest ap-
play cloths wrung out of warm water. Keep
the patient quiet.
	IN all diseases we cannot too strongly urge
the necessity of good nursing. Absolute
cleanliness of person, clothing, room and air
must be had at all times. Quietness, hope-
fulness and cheerfulness are of more value
to the sick than gold, silver and diamonds.
—Ex.


﻿THE SEX.
				      —
	MRS. D. P. Bowers, Philadelphia’s favor-
ite comedienne, has bought a country seat
near Manchester, Mass.
	MRS. Partington does not believe in shak-
ing hands across the bloody sarcasm. Most of
the women agree with her.
	ADELINA Patti has been invited by Mme.
Thiers to give a beneft in Paris to the suf-
ferers by the war, and will do it.
	MRS. Tator, the first woman lawyer on
the Pacific Coast, has been refused admis.
sion to the bar of Santa Cruz, California.
	ROSA Bonheur is said to have expressed
recently a desire to visit the United States,
and, if possible, to do so within the next
twelve months.
	MISS Austine Sneed, “Mrs. Grundy,” of
the “World,” has been engaged to “do”
Washington next winter for a London week-
ly, writing at odd intervals whenever there’s
need.
	THE Princess Salm-Salm having backed
out of the gayeties of this life Into the aus-
terities of a convent, the Queen of Prussia
has sent her a thousand-dollar testament.
	TURKISH ladies are asserting their right
—to be seen. High-born Osman damsels
now dress in Parisian style, wearing veils
of the thinest possible gauze, which soften
without concealing the features.
	COUNTESS Beauchamp has decided talent
as a sculptress. At the Royal Academy her
ladyship is an exhibitor of the figure of her
youngest child. It is said to be excellent
both as a likeness and work of art.
	MADAME de Stael said: “If I were mis-
tress of twenty languages, I would think in
the deep German, converse in the gay
French, write in copious English, deliver
in noble Greek, and make love in the soft
Italian.
	IN Dover, New Hampshire, this Summer,
four young ladies, Adelaide, Jenny, Emma
and Alta Hanson, with the assistance of one
man, their brother, made and gathered sixty
tons of hay. One of them, Miss Emma, her-
self mowed the whole of it, with a two-horse
machine. This promising young farmer is
but seventeen years of age.
	MISS Emily Faithful, who will shortly ar-
rive here to discuss the woman question, is
a lady of wealth and high social position,
and an intimate friend of Queen Victoria.
She devotes her life and money to the ele-
vation of her sex in remunerative employ-
ments.
	IT is well for the girls to know the risk
they run in marrying brakemen. An en-
thusiastic member of the guild In St. Louis
the other night was awakened from a dream
of an Impending mishap by a cry of pain,
and found himself sitting up in bed holding
his wife by the ears, having almost twisted
her head off.


﻿ON THOUGHT BEING EDUCATION.

	A remark made by one of the Sisters,
while speaking in the 14th Ward at a late
meeting, struck me very forcibly as being
true. She said good thoughts showed good
education. This is something that we should
consider more than we do, for upon it de-
pends our happiness more than we are aware.
Thought is truly “the spring of human con-
duct;” and how careful we should be to not
allow an evil or impure thought to have
place in our breast.
	How often we find ourselves giving way
to thoughts that we would be ashamed to
have our most intimate friend know; and
then, to think, that the Lord can discern our
most secret thoughts, should be enough to
cause us to check anything that tends to
evil. Let us consider that we are being
educated by our every thought. When we
feel in the least unhappy or unpleasant, we
may know that our thoughts are not run-
ning in the right direction, and we should
banish them in a moment, and cultivate.
those that will bring peace and happiness,
all the day long. When we can control our
thoughts as we should, we will be near per-
fection. It should be our constant prayer to
the Lord to give us power to govern our
thoughts, for when we can do this we will
have gained a great victory, and will be
tolerably thoroughly educated.
							M. A. F.


﻿THE BENEFIT OF WOMAN TO MAN.
			    —
	Did it ever occur to any of our readers
what a refining and polishing influence the
society of woman gives to man? The asso-
ciation with intelligent and educated women
is ever observable in the man. The com-
mon coarseness of many; the bashful awk-
wardness of others; the general tendency of
man to satisfy his passions—are all toned
down and obliterated, when they are con-
tinually brought into the society of women. 
The gentle, kind and insinuating way a
good woman’s influence is thrown about the
rude and reckless man, is always observa-
ble. She seems to reach into his heart with
fingers that very soon weed out every un-
couth and unseemly plant. She does this,
apparently, without an effort; she gives to a
man grace, fluency in conversation, gentle-
ness of manner, complete ease in society and
a love of the arts, sciences and beauties of
nature; she throws about his experience an
atmosphere of love, confidence, hope, trust,
honor, fidelity and virtue! The rough ob-
structions to be met with upon the exterior
of his character are very soon cut away and
the scars healed up from sight. The pro-
cess of change from a vicious, lonly life, to
one of female association and refinement, is
often slow and tedious; but the metamorphic
change is sure to take place under the love, 
judgment and guidance of a true woman. If
there is anything in man that may be called
“metal,” the beneficial influence of woman’s
association will burnish it to such a bright-
ness that its kind and quality can soon be
determined.
	Some coarse and brutish men scoff at the
beneficial influence of woman, but most all
sooner or later are led to feel its power. The
man who can never feel this reclaiming,
purifying power, and has no faith in woman’s
ennobling, elevating influence, is possessed
of a heart poor indeed. We do not wish his
society—we know our business relations
with him cannot be pleasant and compan-
ionable. The low, vicious habits of life are
his, as he has no incentive to be otherwise,
or even wish to elevate his character.—
“Willamette Farmer.”
