﻿SPLINTERS.
	The Pope has appointed the reverend Mr.
Vaughan Bishop of Salford,England......The
rinderpest has appeared in the country
around Mons, Belgium.......The desperado
Saunders, alias “Red Bill,” was recently
shot at Osceola, Missouri, by the city Mar-
shal......Dr. J. Brinton Smith, Principal of
the St. Augustine colored school of Raleigh,
North Carolina, is dead......Iowa supports
thirteen camp meetings at once......Feldspar,
hitherto imported from Spain, has been dis-
covered in Connecticut......Georgia will not
tax cotton or woolen mills erected there for
two years......The Pope was considerably
indisposed at Rome the other day......San
Antonio, Texas, offeres $500,000 to expedite
the railway communication with Austin......
It is rumored in France that Gambetta will be
a candidate for the Vice-Presidency of the
Republic......E. R. Hoar has been nominated
for Congress in the Seventh District of Mas-
sachusetts......The total value of all known
diamonds, exclusive of Arizona and Alaska,
is $1,000,000,000......Governor Scott, of
South Carolinn, has commenced suit against
the New York “Tribune” for libel......San
Francisco had an earthquake on the 1st inst.
......Late reports from Japan say the party
of progress are losing ground......The death
penalty has been abolished in Peru, and
fifteen years imprisonment is to be the
maximum of all punishment in that Repub-
lic......The best portrait of Bismarck is said
to be engraved as a frontispiece to his life,
published two years ago by Harper & Bro’s.
......Sumner demands peace and reconcilia-
tion on all sides......Queen Victoria has pre-
sented Nellie Grant with her portrait set in
precious stones......In some of the towns in
New England, apples are so plentiful that
farmers are offering cider at $1.50 per barrel.
......A New Orleans lady, the champion
swimer at Mississippi City, swam 1,600 feet
without stopping, a few days since, and.
thinks she could have made 2,000 by a little
more exertion.......Bierstadt is in Inyo
County, California, taking, sketches in the
Sierras for future paintings......English is
the vernacular of seventy millions of people.
......During the year past, the tax collector
of San Francisco has paid into the treasury
over $2,800,000......Miss Grace Anna Lewis,
of Pennsylvania, has achieved a large suc-
cess in the department of ornithology, and
has given public lectures on birds.......The
United States has 65,000 miles of complete
railroads......A swimmer at the steamboat
wharf in Fall River, recently, dispersed a
whole prayer meeting to rescue him, by pre-
tending that he was drowning..........Blue
overcoats and gilt buttons, like our grand-
fathers wore, will be the style for the com-
ing winter....... A Paris gymnast, known as
Sampson Iron Jaw, whose specialty was pick-
ing up weights with his teeth and throwing
them over his head, raised a barrel with his
teeth and in attempting to dispose of it, fell
dead with a broken spine......The Iowa girls
turn out in torchlight processions and fill
the air with shrieks for their favorite candi-
dates......An Indiana woman, lately di-
vorced, writes a letter of advice to the girls
in which she says, “I would not marry
young, and when I was married would live
at least fifty miles from my husband’s rela-
tives”.....Apple trees are blooming for the
second time in the neighborhood of Gilroy.
......The railroad companies own one-fifth of
Kansas......A late heavy storm did much
damage to the shipping on the great lakes,
and many lives were lost......Athens, Geor-
gia, has a paper called the “Cat” with the
motto “I can scratch”......Mobile has sus-
pended her dog law for want of dogs......A
[new column]
Detroit ferry-boat draws custom with a brass
band......A Virginia letter-carrier speaks
eight languages besides the profane......A
considerate man in Illinois paid all his debts
before committing suicide......Trouble is
threatening between Chili and Bolivia, and
somebody may get killed.......A Michigan
lad kicked a can of nitro-glycerine. He did
not need a coffin, but will require an artifi-
cial leg......A London law-suit, a hundred
and forty-seven years old, has just termina-
ted......South Carolina holds an election to-
morrow.......“Inflatable rubber bustles” are
announced, and they are said to impart
great bouyancy to the movements. Some
ladies could do with less buoyancy......St.
Louis has invented moonlight funerals......
Omaha has grown to 20,000 inhabitants
from none in eighteen years.......Seventy
students can be admitted to the Russian
special courses, of medicine and surgery for
women. Three hundred have applied......A
ten foot alligator is boarding at the St.
Louis dog pound......More news and less
politics will characterize the newspapers in
another month......A western paper calls
Laura D. Fair “the pretty bully in bomba-
zine.”


NOTICE.
			    ——
	As the word “Female” is useless in "Female
Relief Society,” we wish to drop it, which can
legally be done with the concurrence of a
majority of ladies composing the various branch-
es of the Society throughout the Territory.
It must be done by vote. We therefore request
all of the Presidents to take an action on the
subject, at their first meeting, and report the
result to the Editor of WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
							ELIZA R. SNOW.
    SALT LAKE CITY, Oct., 8th, 1872.


﻿OUR JOURNAL.
	With the present number of the Exponent
five months of its existence as a journal will
have passed, and it is time that we asked those
clubs that subscribed for six months to renew
their subscriptions. We would request them to
take this matter into consideration now, that
by the time the thirteenth number goes to
press we may know how many copies to print.
Although the circulation of the Exponent has
increased rapidly since it was started, the paper
is not yet paying expenses. To do so will re-
quire a still larger circulation, for the desire is
not to make of it an advertising sheet, which
would pay, but a journal of value to its readers
and one worthy to be preserved. We hope with
increasing patronage to make it more valua-
ble than it is, and a means of acomplishing
good that can be pointed to with pleasure by
everyone interested in its success.
	But to do this the brains of two or three per-
sons are not enough. There are numbers of
ladies throughout the Territory who could


write and yet do not, and could clothe in point-
ed, touching words,ideas that would be of great
value to their sisters everywhere who read the
Exponent. There is more original matter
published in this journal than some of its
readers imagine. The first page of every num-
ber is editorial though it does not so appear;
and many contributed articles are published
with nothing but an initial letter to indicate
who is the author.  Still, it is desirable that
more original matter should appear. We would
also request of ladies to overcome the diffidence
which many of them have to their names be-
ing published, and let them appear attached to
their articles. It will not only show that the
contributions are original, but it will also show
that you entertain honest opinions which you
care not how openly you express.
	If the sisters throughout the Territory, and
especially those who occupy positions as officers
of the Relief Societies, will aid us by their
influence, by forwarding contributed articles,
short and suitable for these columns, and by
increasing our subscription list, we will en-
deavor to labor with all diligence and faithful-
ness to meet their desires in making such a
journal as will best represent the women of
Utah, their wants and wishes.


﻿AT THE FAIR.
	The Relief Societies, from different parts,
manifested their interest in the progress of
home science, by contributing handsomely to
the many attractions, useful and ornamental,
exibited at the Territorial Fair, considering
the time allowed them after being notified that
they were solicited and expected to take part in
it. The Societies of the Third, Seventh,
Eighth, Ninth, Twelfth, Fourteenth and
Twentieth Wards of this city were represented;
also those of Ogden, Bountiful, and Lehi.
Articles belonging to individual members of
other Societies were presented, but so far as we
have been able to ascertain, the above named
were the only Societies specified. Premiums
were awarded to those Societies as follows:
	For the best collection of articles, useful and
ornamental, 14th Ward Relief Society, silver
medal; 2nd best, 9th Ward Relief Society,
$2 00; third best, 12th Ward, $1 00.
	Ornamental needle work, 9th Ward R. S.,
$2 00.
	Ottoman cover, 2nd best, 7th Ward R. S.,
$1 00.
	Netted collar, 9th Ward R. S., $2 00.
	Worked collar, Lehi R. S., $2 00.
	Crochet work, 2nd best, 12th Ward R. S.,
$1 00. 	
	Patchwork quilt, 2nd best, 14th Ward R. S.,
$1 00. 
	Album quilt, 20th Ward R. S., $2 00.
	Knitted work, Bountiful R. S., $2 00.
	Knitted spread, 12 Ward R. S. $2 00; 2nd
best, 3rd Ward R. S., $2 00.
	Straw hat, 14th Ward, R. S., $2 00; 2nd best,
20th Ward R. S., $1 00.
	Straw bonnet, Lehi R. S., $2 00; 2nd best,
Bountiful R. S., $I 00.
	Silk embroidery, 9th Ward R. S., $2 00.
	Best spread of home raised cotton, Lehi R.
S. $2 00.
	Feather plumes, (white and colored) Lehi R.
S.,	$2 00.
	Worsted embroidered slippers, 7th Ward R.
S., $2 00.		’
	Braided watch case, 2nd best, 12th Ward R.
S.,	$I 00.
	Best rag carpet, Ogden R. S., $2 00.
	Several of the articles which drew prizes were
got up in very little time, and had the invita-
tion to the Societies been issued a month earlier


the exhibition of articles contributed by them
would have been much better.
	Taken altogether the Fair was quite success-
ful, which must be a cause of gratification to
the Board of Directors of the D. A. & M. Soci-
ety; and the ladies have reason to feel satisfied
with their share in its attractiveness. In the
department devoted to art were some very
beautiful specimens of ladies’ work in wax,
which drew prizes and were greatly admired by
all who saw them; and the specimens of differ-
ent kinds of work exhibited by individual
ladies, as well as by the Relief Societies,showed
highly cultivated taste and excellence of work-
manship in a very superior degree.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿CONFERENCE.
	The Forty-Second Semi-Annual Conference
of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day
Saints, which commenced on Sunday morning,
October 6th, the usual time for the beginning
of the Fall Conference, was a period of true re-
joicing to the honest Saints who had the privi-
lege of being present and hearing the instruc-
tions given from the stand. The same peaceful,
charitable and intelligent spirit which ever
characterizes these Conferences prevailed at
the late one, and the counsel and admonitions
offered for the consideration of the people should
be long and well remembered. And not only
kept in rememberance, but put into daily prac-
tice by all who have the testimony of the Holy
Spirit that the principles taught them are pure,
and essential to the promotion of peace, happi-
ness and prosperity in this and in a future state
of existence. During the forenoon of the first
day of Conference, President Young remarked
to the Saints that when left to themselves for a
short time they were apt to begin to look at the
failings of others, forgetful of their own, and
thus lose the Spirit of light and deceive them-
selves. He said that prosperity would be given
to a portion of the Saints, while others could
not bear it but would be overthrown thereby.
	Who would not like to be prosperous in their
efforts to accomplish what they believe to be
right? Would it not “pay” any believer in the
Scriptures to “Seek the Kingdom of Heaven
and its righteousuess,” thus laying a sure foun-
dation upon which to build those things for
which the whole human family are striving-
riches, power, glory? There would certainly be
more wisdom in looking to ourselves and seeing
that we are prepared to receive blessings as they
are handed out to us, that we may not be over-
thrown by any of them, than in running wild
after the follies and vanities of the world, as
some of the brethren and sisters seem to be do-
ing. For as fast as we humble ourselves and
make ready, all the riches and glory that we
can take care of will be given to us.
	The President also remarked that “If the
Lord designs to try His people in all things as
has been spoken, we should see to it and be
prepared for each event as it transpires. For we
live in the fulness of times, when the Lord will
come to reign as King of Kings, and He should
be our law giver.”


﻿HOME AFFAIRS.
The Report of the Hyrum Society was re-
ceived too late for publication this number, but
will appear in our next.
Provo and Logan are talking of papers, and
each will probably have one before a great
while. Southern Utah, too, should support a
larger sheet than the “Pomologist,” while in
Mr. J. E. Johnson it has a veteran editor of ex-
perience and ability.
"Will correspondents have the kindness,
in every instance, to send their names with
[new column]
their contributions? This is not that they may
be published, but that we may know who the
writers are. We have contributions now which
we hesitate to use, solely because the names of
the writers did not accompany the manuscripts.
We are pleased to note the prosperity of the
“Ogden Junction," which has changed its
semi-weekly form to a quarto shape, with a
new and hansome heading; and is published as
a daily as well. The “Junction” is a lively,
wide-awake sheet, which continues to grow in
public favor; and under the editorial and busi-
ness management of Mr. Penrose has been
made a financial success. It has our best
wishes for its continued prosperity.
The mild, beautiful weather, which prevailed
during the session of the Territorial Fair and
Semi-Annual Conference, gave additional cause
for enjoyment and gratitude to the people who
attended both with a desire to be pleased and
benefitted, in connection with the excellent
music afforded by the bands and the united
choirs, and the many splendid ideas given to
the Saints for reflection and general use.
President George A. Smith leaves this
morning for the East, accompanied by Elder
Feramorz Little. President Smith designs to
visit several parts of the United States, some
countries of Europe, and the Holy Land during
his absence, which he expects will extend to
about a year. The faith and prayers of the
Saints will be with him and the company that
will travel with him, who leave about the 26th
instant, during their absence.
The many friends of Sister Eliza R. Snow
will be pleased to learn that she is to make one
of the company that is to start about the 26th
of this month for a trip through a portion of the
United States and a visit to some other countries,
including the Holy Land. That she will go
with the faith and blessings of the Sisters who
have known and appreciated her good works in
Zion, is fully evinced by the many tokens for-
warded to that effect. Prayers will be offered
up on every hand, for her good health and
pleasure while she will be absent, and for her
early and safe return to her mountain home.
A Teachers’ Association was organized in
this City during the recent Conference, the
Convention of which it was the outgrowth be-
ing well attended by teachers from different
parts of the Territory. The Association is a
good one; its objects should command the sup-
port of every person in the Territory who has
the cause of education at heart, and its efforts
cannot result other than in good. Still, we
were disappointed in one thing connected with
it. There are many ladies engaged in the pro-
fession of teaching in the Territory, and who
are doing good work; yet on looking over the
names of the officers we do not find a lady’s
among them.
At a meeting of the Superintendents and
Teachers of Sabbath Schools, held on the even-
ing of the 7th inst., at the City Hall of this
City, nearly all the Counties in the Territory
and Wards of this City were represented. Many
Interesting topics pertaining to the best meth-
ods of conducting Sabbath Schools were ad-
vanced and dwelt upon, by several members;
and much encouragement and credit was given
to schools in general. Sabbath School Jubilees,
it is contemplated, will be held semi-annually
In Ogden, hereafter. So much good is believed
to have resulted from the one lately held there,
that all are anxious to have an improved repe-
tition, and an expansion of the blessings real-
ized, about the 1st of May next. Other organi-
zations of the same kind will, doubtless, adopt
similar proceedings for the gratification of
Sabbath School attendants, and the prosperity
of the Schools,


﻿WOMAN’S VOICE.
				   ——
			KAYSVILLE, Sept., 26th; 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	In looking over the last number of the
EXPONENT, I felt thankful that our Father
in Heaven had given us an opportunity of
expressing our feelings with regard to 
"polygamy," as it is called. I have had a 
practice of twenty years in it, and I know
and can testify to the world that it is a pure
and holy principle; and if it  practiced ac-
cording to the will and design of our Father
in Heaven, it cannot fail to ennoble and ex-
alt the sons and daughters of Adam. I know
that no true Latter-day Saint can honestly
say that "polygamy" is not true. We are 
not as foolish as many suppose us to be. We
know in whom we have trusted. We do
not count the trials of this present life
worthy to be compared with the glory which
will be given unto us, if we will faithfully
discharge our duties. We did not come on
this earth like butterflies for gaudy show,
but we came here to help  accomplish the
will of God. We do not care what the 
world may think, or say, about us; it has no
effect upon a true Latter-day Saint; and they
who profess to be Saints who do not see
that plurality is right, should never raise
their hands or voices against it, for if they
do they will instead of injuring us bring
down the displeasure of an angry God upon
themselves. There is one thing the world
may rest assured of; that as long as the
Kingdom of God remains upon the earth, so 
long "polygamy" will remain. The wicked
may threaten and lay secret plans to try to
destroy us; but that God who has thus far
brought us safely through all the changing
scenes which we have been subject to, will
continue to overrule all things for the good
of His children, and for the building up of 
His Kingdom.								S.


﻿A MUSICAL WAIF.
				      —
	One of the finest specimens of home pro-
duction that we found, while attending the
Territorial Fair, was a beautiful little “music
box,” which not only performed nicely with-
out the trouble of winding up, but had also
the remarkable power of self locomotion.
The only unpleasantness connected with its
intonations, was while we listened it played
but one tune and that in rather a high key.
Our attention was drawn to the little “in-
strument,” standing by its self in the hall,
by its mournful yet lusty rendering of “I
can’t find my mama,” wbich it continued to
play while we stood by for a minute or so.
Our sympathies were aroused, and bending
down we endeavored by gently patting, and
saying something of a soothing and hopeful
nature, to change the tune or stop the music;
but we started up in dismay upon finding
that this only made it strike an octave high-
er and continue the same tune with double
the force. We felt like taking that strangely
attractive yet neglected bit of household
furniture in our arms, and lulling it into
quietness; but we had read upon so many
other articles, “Please don’t touch,” that al-
though this precious little token did not
bear the signal, we thought perhaps we had
better not interfere with it farther, and so
passed on. After a little while, we made it
in our way to pass the spot again, but the
music had ceased and the instrument was
gone. Likely the owner had been around
and concluded, as it was Saturday in the
afternoon, it might as well go home. We
made several enquiries concerning it, but
did not ascertain to whom it belonged.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.﻿


﻿ LINES.
WRITTEN FOR ISABEL HAMILTON.

	[PUBLISHED BY REQUEST.]
Sister give thy baby up—
	It is thy Father's will;
The little time he stayed with you
	A mission 'twas to fill.

He was a little angel sent,
	From fairer worlds on high,
To take a body here on earth
	The laws of life to try.

But now it is the Savior calls
	The little sufferer home;
Why should we mourn to lay him down
	When he his work hath done?

Our Father we now dedicate
	This baby unto Thee:
May we be worthy still to claim
	Him in eternity.
					MARGRET A. WHITE.


﻿BEECHER ON WOMAN SUFFRAGE.
				  —
	We can see no necessity for going back
to “an ancient Semitic nation and a king
who had seven hundred wives,” to deter-
mine the question of woman’s rights. Ask
some men of the present age, good, noble,
honest, temperate and intelligent men,
with two, five or ten wives each, and they
will answer that woman should not only be
granted suffrage but should be free to avail
herself of every advantage which men have
for gaining useful knowledge. One of their
mottoes is “educate the mothers, elevate her
children.” The following ideas are sound
and correct, and it is a pity that all men do
not think with Beecher on the subject in
question:
	People fear the effect upon the family if
women should vote and think. They think
that the tenderness and sweetness of the
family relation has something to do with
weakness. It has much to do with fineness,
but not with weakness. It is not the fact of
woman’s looking up to man that pleases
him—it is that in her that looks up. We
know what Solomon said of woman without
thought and without judgment, and he
surely ought to know—he had seven hun-
dred wives. If the question of Woman’s
rights is to be determined by quoting the
Old Testament, I have but a word to say: It
is the last question which ought to be de-
cided for us by an ancient Semitic nation
and a king who had seven hundred wives.
A greater freedom for woman will not unsex
her. That is the very fear that the Orient-
als everywhere have to-day, and you laugh
at them. Once, in Athens, no woman could
learn literature, eloquence or philosophy
unless she were a courtesan. The virtuous
mother and wife were denied these refine-
ments, this knowledge, which were thus
made the insignia of unvirtue. Since then,
woman has steadily progressed through the
centuries, until now she is a leader in litera-
ture, foremost among artists. Has litera-
ture, art and philosophy made her less fit
for a wife and mother? Does it take away
taste and destroy refinement to ponder
deeply? Make mothers more and you make
their children more. You will not make
them coarse by giving them power. Is God
coarse because he is infinite? That which
the family needs more than anything else Is
a higher state of womanly development.


A LADY of San Francisco, named Mrs.
Chloe Shorey, is making a silk quilt, which
is to contain upwards of 17,000 pieces. The
same lady won a prize at a State Fair in
Vermont for the superior beauty and artistic
skill displayed in a quilt which contained up-
wards of 20,000 pieces, some years ago when
a resident of that State.


﻿GRAINS OF GOLD.
			      —
	LOVE is to the moral nature what the sun
is to the earth.—Balzac.
	ONE of the best remedies for the ills of
life is to have so much noble occupation as
to forget them.
	ONE may live as a conquerer, a king, or a
magistrate; but he must die as a man—no
discount on that.
	BASE all your actions upon a principle of
right; preserve your integrity of character,
and in doing this never reckon the cost.
	THE nerve which never relaxes, the eye
which never blanches, the thought which
never wanders—these are the masters of
victory.
	MEN are often capable of greater things
than they perform; they are sent into the
world with bills of credit, and seldom draw
to their full extent.
	THE following sentiment, given at a rail-
way festival, shows the heart of a true man:
“Our mothers—the only faithful tenders
who never misplace a switch.”
	COURAGE and modesty are virtues which
every sort of society reveres, because they
are virtues which cannot be counterfeited;
also they are known by the same hue.
	TRUE courage is cool and calm. The
bravest of men have the least of brutal,
bullying insolence, and in the very time of
danger are found the most serene and free.
	THE hardest thing in the world for a little
man to learn is that people don’t give them-
selves the trouble to think abou this littleness.
This is equally true in its moral application, 
	I FEEL that I am growing old for want of
somebody to tell me that I am looking as
young as ever. Charming falsehood! There
is a vast deal of vital air In loving words.—
Lander.
	THOSE who retire from the world on ac-
count of its sins and peskiness, must not
forget that they have yet to keep company
with a person who wants just as much
watching as anybody else. 
	WHEN a man thinks that nobody cares
for him, and that he is alone in a cold and
selfish world, he would do well to ask him-
self what he has done to make anybody care
for and to love him. And a woman has a
perfect right to do the same.
	FOR your own, as well as for your child-
ren’s sake, learn to speak low. They will
remember that tone when your head is un-
der the willows. So, too, will they remem-
ber a harsh and angry tone. Which legacy
will you leave your children?
	IT is noble and a great thing to cover the
blemishes and to excuse the faults and fail-
ings of a friend; to draw a curtain before
his stains, and to display his perfections; to
bury his weakness in silence, but to pro-
claim his virtues on the housetop.
	IT is not work that kills men; it is worry.
Work is healthy: you can hardly put more
upon a man than he can bear. Worry is
rust upon the blade. It is not the revolu-
tion that destroys the machinery, but the
friction. Fear secretes acids; but love and
trust are sweet juices.—Beechea.
	IF we work upon marble, it will perish;
if we work upon brass, time will effect it; if
we rear temples, they will crumble into dust;
but if we work upon the immortal minds—if
we imbue them with principles, with the
just fear of God and our fellow men—we en-
grave on those tabletssomething which will
brighten to all eternity.

﻿LOVE.
				   —
	I wish that dear word, so expressive and
so real in its beautiful significance, was not 
so much abused by praters who do not un-
derstand, or else do not stop to consider its
true meaning and worth. It is always an-
noying to me to hear love spoken of in a
trifling and heartless manner by the un-
thinking or vicious. Why should they gos- 
sip and laugh over a theme so holy! A great, 
fine, noble looking man—a bachelor—whose
very smile bore indications of disappointed
hopes, decided once that love, like wine,
had become so much adulterated that there
remained nothing genuine about it. It
would have sounded more philosophical, I 
thought, had he said that love, like woman,
had been underestimated so much and so
long that its real value was now rarely, if
ever ascertained and appreciated. The fact
that many people who make and sell wines 
for the sake of the profits they realize from 
so doing, are not honest enough to care any- 
thing abut the quality of the liquor pro- 
duced, only as its price is affected, is no 
proof that if a man grows grapes and makes 
his own wine, he cannot have it as pure 
and sweet as if there were no such thing In
existence as adulterated wine. And because
we do not find the world full of love like
that of home, is no sign that it has ceased to
exist in its true, native,guileless state. “True
love seeks the happiness and welfare of its
object.” It is a pitiable heart that has only 
room in it for one individual, or even a
small circle of near friends and relatives.
The lover of mankind labors for the good of
the human family, and he will never be left
without love, the purest and best, for it 
reigns predominant in his own breast.
									L.


﻿THE PHILOSOPHY Of RAIN.—To under- 
stand the philosophy of this beautiful and
often sublime phenomenon, so often wit-
nessed since the creation of the world, and
so essential to the very existence of plants
and animals, a few facts derived from ob-
servation and a long train of experiments 
must be remembered:
	1.	Were the atmosphere every where at
times of a uniform temperature, we should 
never have rain, or hail, or snow. The wa- 
ter absorbed by it in evaporation from the
sea and earth’s surface, would descend in
an imperceptible vapor, or cease to be ab-
sorbed by the air when it was once fully
saturated.
	2.	The absorbing power of the atmos-
phere, and consequently its capacity to re- 
tain humidity, is proportionately greater in
warm than cold air.
	3.	The air near the surface of the earth
is warmer than it is in the region of the
clouds. The higher we ascend from the 
earth the colder do we find the atmosphere. 
Hence the perpetual snow on very high  
mountains in the hottest climate.
	Now, when, from continued evaporation, 
the air is highly saturated with vapor, 
though it be invisible and the sky cloudless,
f its temperature is suddenly reduced by 
cold currents descending from above, or
rushing from a higher to a lower latitude,
or by the motion of saturated air to a cooler 
latitude, its capacity to retain moisture is 
diminished, clouds are formed, and the re- 
suit is rain. Air condenses as it cools, and, 
like a sponge filled with water and com- 
pressed, pours out the water which its di- 
minished capacity cannot hold. How sing- 
ular, yet how simple, the philosophy of rain! 
What Omniscience could have devised such
an admirable arrangement for watering the 
earth?


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


TRIP LIGHTLY.
		  —
Trip lightly over trouble,
	Trip lightly over wrong.
We only make grief double
	By dwelling on it long.
Why clasp woe’s hand so tightly?
	Why sigh o’er blossoms dead?
Why cling to forms unsightly?
	Why not seek joy instead?

Trip lightly over sorrow,
	Though all the day be dark,
The sun will shine to-morrow,
	And gaily sing the lark;
For hopes have not departed,
	Though roses may have fled;
Then never be down-hearted,
	But look for joy instead.

Trip lightly over sadness,
	Stand not to rail at doom;
We’ve pearls to string of gladness,
	On this side of the tomb;
While stars are nightly shining,
	And the Heaven is overhead,
Encourage not repining,
	But look for joy instead.


﻿JAMES FISK AND THE NEWSBOY.
				——
	 Stories of Col. Fisk are constantly in circu-
lation—some true, others not; but a little
incident which occurred not many months
before his death is perhaps one of the most
I touching of any that have been given to the
public. This one Mrs. Fisk takes especial
pleasure in recalling, illustrating, as it does,
her husband’s kindness of heart and readi-
ness of sympathy and help to those who
I needed aid. Col. Fisk had often noticed in
Twenty-third street a little crippled news-
boy, and one day he spoke to him in his
bluff, off-hand manner:
	“Well, my boy, how’s business?”
	“Not very good, sir,” was the reply.
	“What’s the matter?”
	“Why, you see I’m lame and can’t sell 
my papers very well.”
	“Not a very good lookout for you, is it? I
say, my boy, how would you like to go into
business with me?”
	The boy looked perplexed and eyed the
Colonel curiously.
	“I think we might strike a bargain. You
come to the Opera House at 11 this morn-
ing. I’ve got a plan for you. Now be on
time.”
	At 11 o’clock the boy was there, quite
curious to know what the Colonel wanted.
	“Hulloa, boy, you’re a good one. Now
see here, do you know a place for a paper
stand?”
	“Yes, sir, tip-top.”
	“Where is it?”
	“Down here at the corner.”
	“How much will a stand cost?”
	“Lots of money; much as fifty or a hun-
dred dollars.”
	“You don’t say so; why that’s a fortune.
Do you think there’s monoy to be made
there?”
	“Yes I do. It’s a first-rate place.”
	“Wel, I’ll get a carpenter to make the
stand and I’ll stock it; then you shall take
care of it and we’ll be partners; you and I
will go into the paper business.” 
	Col. Fisk then arranged with the boy
what part of the profits he should receive,
told him when the stand should be ready
and sent him on his way rejoicing. The plan
was very successful. Trade was good, and
evey week the boy carried his share of the
money to his partner. Seeing the boy’s
determination. Col. Fisk quietly put the
money aside, and one day gave it all to the
boy, releasing him from his contract to pay
any part of the money to himself. When
Col. Fisk died there was nowhere a sincerer
mourner than this little newsboy, and the
little stand on the corner was heavily draped
on the day of the burial.


﻿MISCELLANEOUS.
				     —
The sum of $836 was realized by the read-
ings of Miss Charlotte Cushman at Newport,
given in aid of the Newport hospital.
	THE LOUISVILLE “Courier-Journal” says
that Judge Davis is the proprietor of a smile
that would make Colfax turn green with
jealousy. It “commences in the center and
spreads all over his face like a batter-cake”
	A GOOD WOMAN never grows old. Years
may pass over her head, but if benevolence
and virtue dwell in her heart she is as
cheerful as when the spring of life first
opened to her view. When we look upon a
good woman we never think of her age; she
looks as charming as when the rose first
bloomed on her cheek. That rose has not
faded yet; it will never fade. In her neigh-
borhood she is a friend and benefactor; in
the church the devout worshipper and ex-
emplary Christian. Who does not love and
respect the woman who has passed her days
in acts of kindness and mercy; who has been
the friend of man and God, her life has been
a scene of kindness and love, devotion to
truth and religion? We repeat, such a wo-
man cannot grow old. She will always be
fresh and buoyant in her spirit and active in
her humble deeds of mercy and benevolence.
If the young lady desires to retain the bloom
and beauty of youth, let her not yield to the
sway of fashion and folly; let her love truth
and virtue, and to the close of life she will
retain those feelings which now make life
appear a garden of sweets, ever fresh and
green.—Ex.
	PRINCESS ALEXDROWA Troubescheneff,
nee Stratoneroskoff, (or words to that effect,)
sailed from Bremen recently on the New
York steamer. She comes to the United
States to join the “Oneida Society,” New
York. The Princess belongs to one of the
families of the old feudal nobility, is im-
mensely wealthy, and enjoys a certain popu-
larity as a graceful writer of local plays.
She also published a few years since a small
volume of Russian poetry, in the Deshoul-
lieries style. She is a Princess of no small
talent, but her “forte” is the languages; she
speaks no less than seven of them. Is it
possible that a person so richly gifted shall
soon be seen among that sisterhood of the
Bloomer costume, in those odious brown
frocks and drawers to match; her beautiful
tresses cut to her ears, and those lovely
white hands busily engaged manufacturing
apple sauce? The New York “Herald”
may spare Mr. Stanley this time. She has
declared, on leaving, she would refuse to be
interviewed by American newspaper re-
porters.—“New York Herald.”
	“OYEZ, OYEZ! Advance to the bar Miss
Phoebe Couzens, and receives an address of
welcome from Judge McKean and the hearty
congratulations of her brethren in the law.
All this in Salt Lake City, on motion of
Governor Woods. At the same time Miss
Snow, daughter of the Territory Attorney
General, was also admitted on motion of ex-
United States Attorney Hempstead. What
with the progress of the gentler sex in
Wyoming politics and Utah law, that part
of America promises to be ‘the woman’s
kingdom’. Now let us see if the new law-
yeresses, bretherin’ and sisterin’ of the bar,
do themselves proud.”
	We know that fair Phoebe will. She was
exceedingly fair to look upon, and had
sweet, winning ways and a musical voice.
But why, oh! Phoebe, goest thou to Salt
Lake? Was it in search of a part of a man
in that polygamous region, when thou
couldst have had that here?—“Washington
Capital.”


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
				—
	THREE-FOURTHS of the pleasures of a holi-
day lie in the planning of it.
	HENRY W. LONGFELLOW pays a tax of
$2,666, and James Russell Lowell $753.
	DUVALL county, Fla., with a population
of less than 12,000, has, during the last
twenty-four months, had 52 homicides and
suicidal deaths, and 32 fatal accidents. Only
one murderer has been hung.
	REV. DeWitt Talmadge’s Tabernacle in
Brooklyn started three years ago with 19
members. To the 19 have been added 500
by letter or profession, and the congrega-
tion has given for all objects $98,800.
	JUSTICE is impartial. P. D. Banks, an
Iowa man, has no money to speak of and
goes to the penitentiary for ten years for
robbing the mails. Simon Smith, of the
same State, equally guilty, and a counter-
feiter besides, pays $2,400 and escapes.
	A NIAGARA Falls correspondent has spent
some time in classifying the arrivals for last
season, which numbered 41,975; New York
heads the list with 14,978; Illinois shows 1,-
490; the British Provinces, 1,565; Europe,
1,100; the Orient, 105, and Africa, 4.
	THE collar of the Order of the Golden
Fleece, which was sent by King Amadeus
of Spain to the Comte de Flanders, is the
identical collar which was worn by Christo-
pher Columbus, and with which he was in-
vested with the Order by Ferdinand and
Isabella, in honor of the discovery of America.
	THE eleven Territories, as accurately as
can at present be ascertained, contain over
one billion and a quarter acres, exceeding
by nearly two hundred thousand square
miles the aggregate territory of all the
present admitted States of the Union. The
Territory of Alaska, containing 369,529,600
acres, is included in the number stated.
	GARDEN Islahd is the name of a little is-
land in Lake Ontario, near the city of King-
ston. The population is about 1,000. Twenty
years ago a law was passed that no liquor
should on any pretense be brought on the
island. There is not a pauper nor a police-
man among the inhabitants, and not a case
has come before the magistrate for fifteen
years.
	A DETROIT man recently forswore tobac-
co. He eats a few things to get through
each day. The following is a list of a few
little things required the first day:	Six
oranges, fourteen apples, ten cents worth of
peanuts, two sticks of spruce gum, three
large sticks of candy, a pint of roasted chest-
nuts, one cake of maple sugar, three cents
worth of liquorice root, and half an ounce of
camomile blows.
	A COLORED member of the Texas Legis-
lature was recently seen with a roll of green-
backs in his hand, over which he chuckled
so loudly as to attract the attention of a by-
stander, who said to him: “What are you
laughing at, Jim?” Jim replied, “You see
the money?” “Yes.” “Well, boss, I just
got that for my vote; I’se been bought four
or five times in my life, and dis is de fust
time I ever got the cash myself.”
	A WONDERFUL cave has been discovered
in Center county, Pennsylvania, said to be
equal, if not superior, to the Mammoth
Cave, of Kentucky. When once inside, the
most magnificent and delightfully beautiful
sights are presented to the gaze. The rooms
are numerous and large, but have not yet
been fully explored. Directly under the
cave is another, supposed, to be much larger
and richer in its queer and Indian-like
specimens.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN’S AIMS.
				     —
	“Our aims have' been too low we; must
improve them.”
	It is argued by some that we need incen-
tives. We need our eyes opened and our
understandings quickened to make us com-
prehend the incentives which we have al-
ways had, to induce us to educate ourselves.
Should not the knowledge that by so doing
we not only benefit ourselves, but improve
society and expand and elevate the nature
of future generations, be a sufficient incite-
ment to energy and perseverance?
	Too many gay, thoughtless young women,
who have abundance of time and talent,
were they but properly applied, to make of
themselves some of the brightest ornaments
of society, give their attention to nothing
but fashionable nonsense and vanity. Many
a superb form and lovely face seem to be
considered by their possessor to have been
bestowed upon her for no other purpose than
for making conquests and gaining a good
match. Many a fine, sweet voice exerts its
power only for the attraction of a set of
trifling, worthless admirers and flatterers.
What have our mothers been about! Why
have we not been taught to couple goodness
and intelligence with all these beautiful
gifts, these priceless endowments with which
God has blessed us, that the influence and
power we hold might be something worth!
Why do fathers, brothers, husbands and
lovers indulge and encourage inconsisten-
cies in dress and indolence, and yet exclaim
against them and against women as their
inventors! Why do they not lift us up, or
rather allow us to climb for ourselves? for it
is quite impossible for them to teach us
what we are not inclined to learn, but they
might assist and cheer us on when they see
us trying to improve our aims, instead of
seeking to decoy us back into the old chan-
nel of idleness and ignorance, by flattering
vanity and encouraging useless pastime.
Were there no future upon which to base
our aims, the satisfaction there is to be found
in deep thought and earnest study, in gain-
ing wisdom and cultivating intelligence, is
far greater than any enjoyment realized in
air castle-building.


﻿DANIEL BOON's sister Hannah is living in
Caldwell County, North Carolina. She is 85
years old.

[new column]


		WOMAN’ S EXPONENT.
		
		A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL.

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women every where. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.

	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves?  “It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to he misrepresented
by others!"

	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.

	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound 
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but 
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R. Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from 
her numerous duties.

	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will he published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the  
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so 
valuable that it will be found in every family in 
Utah.

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:	
1Copy one year, $2.00			I Copy six months,    $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00		10 Copies six months, 9.00

	No subscription received for less than six months. 
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents 
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a 
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

	Address all communications and subscriptions
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,	
Utah.


Woman's Exponent.
﻿VOL. I.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, NOVEMBER 1, 1872. 		
.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	THE EASIEST thing In the world to make
—a mistake.
	JUDGE CURTIS got eight thousand dollars
for defending Mrs. Fair, and securing her
acquittal. Crittenden only got a bullet.
	ON TUESDAY next, the 5th inst., the Uni-
ted States will elect the President for the
four years commencing March 4th, 1873.
	ONE'S TEETH chatters to think that while
Utah has been having a delightful Indian
Summer the crowning enjoyment of Michi-
ganders was sleigh-riding.
	STANLEY-AFRICANUS, the discoverer of
Livingstone, will lecture on his African ex-
perience, and may “discover” Salt Lake on
his way to the Pacific Coast.
	AGASSIZ "FISHED" in the Sacramento
river, and by exploding torpedoes caught
about a thousand. But it was science that
authorized the finny butchery.
	THE GREAT question of the coming Presi-
dential electian still remains undecided; and
radicals of the two contending parties con-
tinue slashing at each other with increas-
ing violence.
	“POMEROY'S DEMOCRAT” says Brigham
Young’s smallest child has forty-nine moth-
ers. If he appreciates the worth of one
mother as he should do, he must consider
that child blessed indeed.
	PAYMENT OF workmen on Mondays, in-
stead of Saturdays, is being agitated in the
east. A sensible movement, and one that
may prevent much intemperance, if it does
no further good, should it prevail.
	ONE OF the most shocking and terrible in-
cidents we find recorded in late news items,
is that of a little boy, in Lexington, only
seven years of age, purposely and deliber-
ately shooting and killing a little girl five
years of age.
	GREELEY SAYS “strategy in politics is the
tribute which craft pays to strength: The
wolf-skin proving too short, the fox’s tail is
pressed into service to piece it out.” That’s
quoting Bulwer’s “Richelieu” with a pres-
ant application.
	A GENTLEMAN in Paris had his false
teeth seized upon for debt; he claimed that
they were sacred, as a part of his person,
and that the officer had no right to touch
them, but the officer contended that he had.
Are teeth personal property? It is a tooth-
some query.
	AN IOWA patriarch is too old to be fash-
ionable. Over eighty, he is yet the father
of a three-year old baby, the last of thirty,
and is proud of his youngest. Here in Utah
such a man with his wife would command
respect, as an honor to the commonwealth.
How is it in Iowa?
	VIRGINIA, THE “Old Dominion,” still en-
joys horse racing with ladies for riders; and
at a recent fair in Culpepper, three fair
equestrians rode so well that the judges had
to decide by lot which should have the
prize. There is more vigor of body and
manly honesty to the next generation in
such exercises being enjoyed by the coming
mothers, than lolling in cushioned chairs
devouring trashy sentimental novels can
give.
[new column]
	INDIAN TROUBLES in Texas, in Dacotah,
on the line of the Northern Pacific, in Idaho,
Arizona, and in other parts of the great
sparcely settled portion of the United States,
while delegations of chiefs are visiting
Washington, and being feasted and petted
there. However wise may be the general
policy of Government towards the Indians,
it will be unsuccessful until honest men can
be found to act between the Government
and the Indians; and honest men seem a
scarce article in such positions.
	GROSS FRAUDS are charged in the late
Pennsylvania election; and what is worse,
in several instances glaring ones have been
proved. Just when money can buy the
requisite number of votes, or fraud secure
them, or both combined,to carry an election,
at that time republican liberty has received
its death blow in the country, and central-
ized tyranny of any pre-determined kind
need only wait the will of capitalists and
clever scoundrels who may be placed in con-
trol of election matters.
	A TERRIBLE horse disease is spreading
in epidemic form through the Eastern States.
Having made its appearance in Canada
about a month ago, it has spread into the
United States, and has attacked thousands
on thousands of animals. Science cannot
account for it, and the knowing ones are be-
wildered. It is one of the signs of the times
which the wise understand, but which those
who disregard the workings of a higher
Power among the nations vainly seek to
comprehend.
	SARAH M. HUNTINGDON, of Norwalk,
Connecticut, is likely to be the first woman
that will cast a vote for President of the
United States. On Saturday last, the 26th
ult., she was duly recognized by the select-
men of that town, and on next Tuesday can
cast her ballot for Grant or Greeley as potent-
ly as any “independent sovereign” in the
country. All honor to Norwalk, which has
set the example to all other towns in the
States, by following the lead of Utah and
Wyoming, in giving to woman representa-
tion with taxation.
	THE TRIAL of the New York official
thieves may be said to have commenced
with that of Mayor Hall, and so far the dis-
closures show how the tax payers of that
city were robbed. When New York, Phila-
delphia, Washington, Boston, Chicago, San
Francisco and a few other large cities weed
out their official rogues, their masculine de-
stroyers of virtue, and their dens of infamy
and vice, we will be content at their paying
a little attention to Utah. But their work
of justice and labor of charity should begin
at home.
	IF THE following could be adhered to and
its regulation respected the complaints
about tyrant officials and misconstructions of
law, order and justice, would cease to vex
the ears of the Government: “Complete
liberty and exact equality in the enjoyment
of all civil, political, public and private rights
should be established and effectually main-
tained throughout the Union by efficient
and appropriate State and Federal legisla-
tion. Neither law nor its administration
should admit of any discrimination in re-
[new column]
spect to citizens by reason of race, creed,
color, or previous condition of servitude.”
	AMONG THE most important events of the
current year will be counted the opening of
the telegraph lines across the continent of
South America, connecting Valparaiso with
Buenos Ayres, and to Australia, connecting
Melbourne and Singapore. When connec-
tions between Panama and Valparaiso, Egypt
and the Cape of Good Hope, Panama and
San Diego shall be made, telegraphic bonds
will then bind together the whole civilized
world. And railways and steamships will
hasten on, making the inhabitants of the
earth as near one family as their varied
customs and notions will admit of their be-
coming.
	J. A. FROUDE, the eminent English histo-
rian, has been telling New Yorkers what he
knows about Ireland’s dissatisfaction under
its subjugation to England; and frankly con-
fessed that, with other intelligent English-
men, he was at a loss to understand the
cause of that dissatisfaction. When such
men honestly admit their ignorance on so
important a matter, there is reason to hope
that it may be intelligently enquired into;
but in the past, the great blunder has been
one that is too common in many things and
with many persons, of imagining that every-
thing was understood, and proceeding to re-
move causes of evil which did not exist,
while the actual causes were left untouched
and undiscovered.
	“THERE IS no longer any temptation to
indulge in tight lacing for a small waist is
not now considered fashionable; that is, a
waist out of proportion to the height of the
figure and the breadth of the shoulders.
Fashion and comfort are, therefore, happily
in accordance with each other. Another re-
form is that the waist is now displayed the
natural length, which, in a properly pro-
portioned figure, is in a line midway between
the hip and the lowest rib.” We clip the
forgoing from a lady’s magazine. It is to
be hoped that news of this common-sense re-
form is being rapidly and widely spread,
and the change adopted. If fashion could
dispense with the temptation to indulge
in the use of high heels and hunch-backs,
and some other ridiculous, unnatural and
cruel customs, she would be a much more
pleasing and loveable tyrant than she now
appears.
	TWO EMINENT Americans have passed
from, earth since the last issue of the Ex-
ponent; one—Wm. H. Seward—eminent
as a statesman; the other—“Fanny Fern”—
famed in the world of letters. The nation
mourned the honored Seward, and unaffect-
ed sorrow was felt when his death was an-
nounced. The authoress, too, had a wide
circle of mourners. But death strikes the
lofty and the prominent with as little hesi-
tation as the lowest and most obscure. It is
the common lot, and they meet it best who
meet it bravely after well spent lives. The
people of Utah should preserve green the
memory of Mr. Seward, who, animated by
liberal views and possessing a statesman’s
enlightened and far-seeing mind, was over
opposed to the persecution and proscription,
sought to be directed against them, and en-
deavored to have their rights as Ameri-
can citizens justly observed.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿THOU ART GOING.
		     —
RESPECTFULLY INSCRIBED TO MISS ELIZA R.
	SNOW ON HER APPROACHING JOURNEY TO
	JERUSALEM.
					—
Thou art going dear sister, to th’ land long forsaken,
	Whose people, a hiss and a by-word, have been;
Who rejected the Savior, the way of salvation,
	And for centuries have dwindled in darkness and
	sin.

But the Spirit of God is now moving to gather,
	A people once favored, as scriptures record;.
Their cities and temples again there will flourish,
	And themselves live in peace, for a time, saith the
		Lord.

And thou, when thy feet tread the paths once familiar
	And dear to the Savior while dwelling below.
Thy soul will go forth in communion with Heaven,
	And glad strains of praise from thy full heart will
		flow.

Oh! what a rich feast, to a nature like thine,
	To view the paths trod, by that being divine,
And to feel that ere long He will tread them again,
	And be known to the Jews, as the Savior of men!

Thou art going, and with thee our blessings will go,
	And we’ll pray for thy safety thy long journey
	through;
We will not forget the rich counsels you’ve given,
	For they will refine and prepare us for Heaven.

May Heaven’s rich blessings rest on your companions,
	And Israel’s God be your guide and your friend;
May angels protect you, while absent from Zion,
	And guard you safe home, when your journeyings
	end.
						MRS. ANNE RANDALL.
	SPANISH FORK, Sept. 30th 1872.

And let us add, in friendship’s warmest tone,
	Servant of Zion’s daughters, not alone
We crave for thee the wage of peace and health,
	But new, bright stores of intellectual wealth;
Rich gems of thought, sweet words of solace, too,
	Thou’ll glean and bring to us, beloved and true.
										LULA.


F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.

		 HYRUM, Cache County, Utah,
					October 9th, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	The Female Relief Society of this Ward
was organized nearly four years ago, and 
since that time we have accomplished much 
good. Our Society is in a healthy condi-
tion; we have endeavored to do all in our
power to comfort and bless the poor, the
widow, the fatherless and the sick; thus as-
sisting our Bishop and Teachers in some of
their labors. In addition to this we have 
sent ninety dollars to assist in gathering the
poor, and have given forty dollars to aid our
Sunday School in purchasing a library and
rewards. In connection with this Society
there is a Young Ladies' Retrenchment As-
sociation. We have a braiding school and
make straw hats in Summer.
	Although the outside world may number
us with the "poor, oppresse women of
Utah," we experience great pleasure in say-
ing, we have lived in the States and in the
old world, but prefer living in, and enjoy-
ing the glorious liberties of Utah and her
"Mormon" institutions, and in seeking to 
fill woman's mission as wives, mothers and
daughters, aiding suffering humanity
wherever found.
	"The widow's heart shall share our joy,
		The orphan and oppressed
	Shall see we love the sweet employ
		To succor the distressed,"
And thus maintain woman's legitimate
rights.
		CHRISTINA LILJENQUIST, President,
		MARTHA WARD,
		LUCY WILSON;	Counselors.
			HARRISVILLE, Oct. 12th, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	We send you a brief notice relative to the
advancement and progress of the F.R.
[new column]
Society of Harrisville, Weber County, which
was re-organized the 31st of March last, as
follows: Melissa A. Shurtliff, President;
Sarah J. Taylor and Nancy Rawson, Coun-
selors; Mary E. Taylor, Secretary, and
Louise Harris, Treasurer. We meet semi-
monthly, and our meetings are well attended.
Peace and good order prevail.  All seem
willing and anxious to do all they can to ad-
vance the welfare and objects of the Society,
which is small; but we have added $54 75
to its funds since the re-organization. We
receive and read the Exponent, and feel
much interested in the matter therein con-
tained.
			MELISSA A. SHURTLIFF, President.
			MARY E. TAYLOR, Secretary.
					—
				SPRINGVILLE, Oct. 12th, 1872.
	A celebration in honor of the organization
of the F. R. Society of Springville, which
was organized June 7th, 1868, was held in
the meeting House of this place, September
2th, 1872, the President of the Society,
Mrs. Cynthia Clyde, presiding.
	The choir sang pieces selected for the
occasion. The Financial report read by Mrs.
Hannah Carter, Secretary, showed that since
the organization $1,387 20 had been re-
ceived in donations, profits on labor, &c.
Disbursed to the poor, $672 68; paid to the
Provo Factory, on shares for the benefit of
the Society, $188 43; paid to the P. E. Fund,
$30; donated to the building of the Temple,
$305 70; donated for public purposes, $174.-
50.
	Addresses made by Bishop Bringhurst
and some of the Sisters, were instructing
and encouraging; and were followed by a
dialogue entitled “A Sewing Circle,”, by
twelve young ladies and one gentleman.
Songs, Recitations, Toasts, &c., followed.
			HANNAH CARTER, Secretary.


﻿HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
				—
	IF a woman aspires to be a proficient in
music, dancing, dressmaking, millinery, or
any other pleasant and useful forte, she ap-
plies her attention to it and takes pains to
acquaint herself with the various require-
ments which are likely to meet her in her
chosen pursuit. But the one great object
for which woman was designed, is too gener-
ally totally neglected, until she crosses the
threshold, enters a new world, and has ac-
tually taken upon her, by vow, the greatest
responsibilities which ever are or can be
placed upon her. She has promised by sacred
covenant to be a true, a faithful wife. How
little must girls realize of the vast import-
ance attached to that promise, which to ful-
fill with dignity and wisdom, requires the
choicest learning and discretion. True it is
that a good, kind, daughter and sister can
hardly fail to make a tender, patient, wife
and mother; but frequently much more is
required of the wife than the daughter ever
thinks of. Husbands are not likely to be as
charitable and considerate with their young
wives as parents with their daughters. And
it should be remembered that perfection in
any one branch of education, or even in
many of the most noble character, does .not
constitute a true wife. Whatever her other
attainments may be, if a woman does not
possess an even, well-governed temper, and
agreeable disposition, she will not make a
happy wife; for all other powers of mind and
body, it matters not how vivacious and bril-
liant they may appear at times, would fail
to create the requisite sunshine for the
rendering of a home cheerful and pleasant.
And in order to keep good natured, if a wo-
man is as sensitive and high-minded as any
man of common understanding would like 
[new colmn]
his wife to be, she must know how to con-
trive and manage her household affairs so as
to have every thing work smoothly, in
proper time and order, and have no jars to
interfere with the quiet concerns of every-
day life. Almost above every thing else,
she should be a first-class cook—i. e., she
should cultivate reason in this as well as
other things, and cook sensibly. In “John
Halifax” we find the following which recom-
mends itself:
	“Good temper, with many people, is
dependent upon good health; good health
upon good digestion; good digestion upon
wholesome, well-prepared food, eaten in
peace and pleasantness. Ill-cooked, untidy
meals are as great a cause for bad temper as
many a moral wrong; and a person of sensi-
tive physique may be nursed into a settled
hypochondria by living in close rooms,
where the sweet fresh air and sunshine are
determinedly shut out.”
	Potatoes.—No amount of fancy French
dishes can atone, at table, for want of skill
or care in cooking potatoes. Yet cooks often
fail in this simple and common dish. They
will put potatoes into cold water and bring
them slowly to a boil; or boil or bake them
too long. They should be removed from
the fire as soon as done. If boiled the water
should be drained off immediately.
	Potato Pudding.—Four ounces of pota-
toes, three ounces of white sugar, the same
of butter, two lemons, four eggs. Rub the
boiled potatoes through a sieve, add the
sugar pounded, and the butter; mix well;
then add the eggs well beaten; and last of
all the juice and grated rind of the lemons.
Line your dish with thin puff paste, making
it thicker at the edges; bake in a moderate
oven, not letting the pudding stand long
before baking; and it must be well stirred
just before putting it into the oven.
	Extraordinary stories are told of the
healing properties of a new oil, which is
easily made from the yolks of hen’s eggs.
The eggs are first boiled hard and the yolks
are then removed, crushed, and placed over
a fire where they are carefully stirred until
the whole substance is on the point of
catching fire, when the oil separates and
may be tured off. One yolk will yield nearly
two teaspoonfuls of oil. It is in general use
among the colonists of South Russia, as a
means of curing cuts, bruises and scratches.
	To Cure Pimples.—Abstain from every-
thing containing melted butter and lard.
Take a thorough daily scrubbing from head
to foot. Sleep cool, drink pure water, think
pure thoughts, and live a pure life.


"WE HAVE,” said Mr. Cox, “fallen on an
evil time. The olden day, when austerity
and simplicity gave rule to manners and
men, when a sense of public duty was the
brightest virtue of the statesman, has be-
come a nebulous, dim and hazy; recollection.
Can we not redeem the time? Is there no
man who has the plainness and frugality,
the benevolence and justice, to meet the
crisis?” There are men, and women too,
who have still enough of elevated and un-
selfish principle about them to be willing to
undertake the redemption of the good times
past; but where there are so many slothful
and dishonest harpies seeking and obtaining
offices, as judges and rulers, all over the
country, in order that they may live from
the earnings of the people, and not have to
exert themselves, as honest men do, to ob-
tain a livelihood, Mr. Cox, or any one else
need not hope for a speedy reformation; for
no man good and great enough to create
one, is likely to be vested with the authori-
ty.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿ RESPONSIVE.

	TO MY MAGNANIMOUS FRIENDS.
	
	Impress’d with, kindness which exhales
		Beneficence unsought;		
	I fain would speak, but language fails
		To grasp the swelling thought.

	E’en gratitude, deep cherish’d word,
		Unable to express
	The feelings in my bosom stirr'd,
		Dissolves in thankfulness.

	I go with humble, grateful heart
		For every kindness shown:
	May Heav'n its richer gifts impart
		To each and every one.

	I go depending on the care
		Of God, our Father God;
	For which, I ask your faith and prayers,
		While I remain abroad.
	
	The warm emotions of the heart
		Too full for utterance,	
	Must bask in silence—must resort
		T' unspoken eloquence.
									ELIZA R. SNOW.
Salt Lake City, Oct. 26th, 1872.


﻿DIVERSIONS. 
				—
	MANY of the rich planters of Jamaica live
on coffee grounds.
	“DAUGHTERCULTURAL shows” is the
latest name for a family party.
	IF Adam is accountable for “consequen-
tial damages,” he will have a rough time of
it. 
	THE Jewish “Messenger” wishes Stanley
to try his skill on the ten lost tribes of
Israel. 
	BILLINGS says, “There ain’t ennything
that will kompletely kure lazyness, but i
have known a second wife to hurry it sum.”
	“DO YOU think,” asked Mrs. Pepper,
“that a little temper is a bad thing in a
woman?”
	“Certainly not, ma’am,” replied the gal-
lant phiolsopher; “it is a good thing, and she
ought never to lose it.”
	A MUSIC dealer has in his window a sen-
timental song thus marked: “Thou hast
loved me and left me, for twenty-five
cents.”
	A little girl was tenderly nursing her
sick doll the other day, and on her mother
asking what ailed it, she replied, “It’s got
the ‘Alabama claims very bad.”
	The Earl of Shaftesbury, noted for his or-
thodoxy, said lately that if the Pope had a
wife, she would not allow him for an hour to
remain in the belief that he was infallable.
	An old maid suggests that when men
break their hearts, it is all the same as
when a lobster breaks one of his claws—an-
other sprouts immediately, and grows in its
place.
	An Illinois lover closed a letter to his lady
sentimentally as follows; “My best loved
one, I chawed the postage stamp on your
last letter all to thunder, because I knew
you licked it on.”
	A clergyman addressed his female audit-
ors as follows: “Be not proud that our Lord
paid your sex the distinguished honor of ap-
pearing first to a female after resurrection,
for it was only that the glad tidings might
spread the sooner.”
	THE characteristic of the umbrella is its
power of changing shapes. You may leave
a bran new silk with an ivory and rosewood
handle at any public gathering, and within
three hours it will transform itself into light
blue or faded brown cotton, somewhat less
in size than a circus tent, with a handle like
a telegraph pole and five fractured ribs.


﻿THE SEX.
			      —
	THE Queen of Sweden is a novelist. She
writes for the press over the nom de plume
of Annie Arden.
	ABBY Sage Richardson has a now lecture
for this season, on Sir Walter Raleigh and
his American voyages.
	A young lady in Georgia has petitioned
the Legislature to pass an act prohibiting
flirtation on the part of young men.
	A YOUNG lady in Harrisburg has been
made blind by the poisonous dyes in some
goods she has been sewing in a fashionable
dry goods house.
	CHICAGO has a shooting gallery for the ex-
clusive use of ladies, among whom there is
a growing restlessness to become proficient
with rifle and pistol.
	HARRIET Martineau and five hundred
other English women have petitioned the
House of Commons to dispense with the
“cat” as a means of punishment.
	A LONG Branch beauty was recently
weighed in her promenade costume and
turned the scales at 165 pounds. In her
bathing dress she weighs 106 pounds.
	In a Florida young ladies’ seminary the
thoughts of the inmates are taken from
dress, by putting the girls in uniform-
dark green for Winter and pink for Sum-
mer.
	“Dear me, how fluidly he talks,” said
Mrs. Partington recently at a temperance
meeting. “I am always rejoiced when he
mounts the nostral, for his eloquence warms
every catridge in my body.”
	Mrs. Richardson, of Cheshire, a relative
of Susan B. Anthony, who has carried on a
large farm for twelve years since her hus-
band’s death, thinks, now she is seventy-
seven years old, it is about time to retire
from business, and so is building a cottage
at the center of the town.
	Lilian Edgarton was lately compelled to
appear before a Memphis audience without
her lecture manuscript and her pretty dress,
both of the articles being in her trunk. She
compromised, however, by reading a sketch
of another lecture, and reciting “The
Launching of the Ship,” and the next even-
ing gave her lecture in full costume.
	Miss Edith Challis denies the truth of the
report of having been made the heiress of
an opulent old lady to whom she was kind
during her illness. Miss C. also says that
several pious English relatives, who had
ceased to recognize her since her connection
with the stage, became suddenly sociable,
and invited her to stay at their houses, on
learning that she had become a capitalist.
	The celerated lady dentist of Berlin, Mrs.
Henrietta Herschfeld, is described as a re-
fined and beautiful woman, with wonderful
strength in her small hand. She extracts
teeth with unsurpassed dexterity and pre-
cision. Moreover, she prepares well-written
articles for magazines, in which she instructs
mothers in regard to the care of children’s
teeth—a matter which seems not generally
understood in Germany.
	A SPINSTER who can stand the derogatory
remarks concerning old maids no longer,
writes to the Cincinnati “Times:” “I am
happy to say that I have for many years be-
longed to that class of much reviled women;
but many of my intimate friends have been
married women,and from them I have heard
more scandal and detraction, more jealousy
and rivalry, more of the spirit of ‘envy,
hatred, and malice and uncharitableness,’
than from any old maid I ever knew or heard
of.”


EDUCATION AND EMPLOYMENT OF
			GIRLS.
			  ——
	Nothing so tends to the degeneracy of
womanhood as a life of laziness and indulg-
ence—an aim for a wealthy husband, and
then life with no object nor aim beyond
raising a family, whether taste lies in that
direction or not. It is not only bad for
physical health, but is bad for moral also.
	A very serious and common mistake in
the training of our girls is the neglect defi-
nitely to provide against the vicissitudes of
life with the faculty of noble self support.
Just think of your daughter, well born and
rocked in the lap of luxury, coming, one of
these days, to sewing or keeping boarders
for a livelihood. Yet thousands of girls,
upon whose girlhood fortune lavished her
gifts, have come to that. It seems to us
that a very rich man who has daughters
should provide for them with visions of the
almshouse continually floating before his
eyes. No amount of pecuniary endowment
will be a sure defence against the demons of
pauperism. The fires may burn it up, the
waves may engulf it, prodigal scoundrels of
husbands may waste it, rascally trustees and
executors may pillage it. These and a
thousand kindred contingencies considered,
besides the proverbial helplessness of wo-
man, are almost enough to make a thought-
ful and cautious man pray that daughters
may not be born unto him.
	So, then, we say, let the education of our
girls provide for the possible problems of
self-help in the noblest possible way. The
daughters of millionairs and mechanics alike
should be made distinctly to understand
that all the love romances lie, and that there
is no such thing as making a compact with
fortune to avert the necessity of honest toil.
Nay, more, we would have the girls taught
that labor, especially brain labor, for the
benefit of the world, is too noble to be un-
dertaken for mere mercenary ends, albeit
the world must and will pay for it.
	Our American girls need a great deal of
discipline in this respect, and their fathers
and mothers need to have a great many of
their foolish notions of propriety about toil,
and the wicked caste idea it encourages,
killed as the rank social weed it is. You
will hardly find a girl who will teach
school, or a parent who will allow it, except
under the compulsion of a scanty purse.
What a shame to our Christianity is this!
A fine lady, rustling in her silks, boasts that
she has had all masters in literature and art,
has seen all the galleries of Europe, speaks
three languages, can draw from nature, and
we know not what else; and yet she would
not soil her respectibility by teaching a child
of ignorance the alphabet or hammering the
multiplication table into a class of little
know-nothings in calico! Sure enough, the
millennium has not come yet.—Balance.


﻿A LITTLE girl, not far from Schenectady,
after noticing for some time the glittering
gold-filling in her aunt’s front teeth, ex-
claimed: “Aunt, I wish I had copper-toed
teeth like yours.”
	A PAPER recently alluded to a man as a
“battle-scared veteran.” The compositor
was so agitated when the editor made him
correct it, that he changed it to “battle-
scarred” veteran. And still the veteran in
question was not satisfied.
	THERE have been several instances lately
out West of girls who have run away to join
the circus. What a joy it must be to a
gentle maiden to array herself in gauze and
spangles, and dash through paper covered
hoops, and associate with teamsters and
rowdyish circus men.
