﻿FOR PALESTINE.
				    —
	On the morning of the 26th ult. Sister Eliza
R. Snow, President of all the Female Relief
Societies, left this city by the early train, on
her way to Palestine, or the Holy Land. Her
brother, President Lorenzo Snow, of Brigham
City, Elder George Dunford and Bishop Jacob
Weiler, of this city, and Elder Anson Call, of
Bountiful, Davis County, formed the party
with which she traveled east, they all designing
to unite with President George A. Smith before
he leaves for Europe. Bishop Thomas Taylor
of this city, and daughter, left a few days be-
fore those who started on the 26th, and Elders
John Taylor and Paul A. Schettler still expect
to go. The entire party, when united, will
number some twelve or thirteen persons, and
may be absent for nearly a year. They will
bear with them the earnest prayers and best
wishes of the thousands of Latter-day Saints
in these valleys, for pleasant voyaging while
absent, and their safe return.
	We hope to be able to present the readers
of the Exponent with communications from
Sister Snow, as she can find time to write in-
cidents of the journey; and we need scarcely
say how deeply interesting such letters will
be, however brief.


﻿FALSE LOGIC.
			 	——
	On the 18th ult. a woman in this city took
poison and died. Her domestic life had been
unhappy, and from the indifference manifested
by her husband under the terrible circum-
stances, according to the evidence given at the
inquest, there is reason to believe that he
viewed the self-inflicted destruction of his wife
as more of a relief than a calamity. How far


h e was responsible for the fearful act, by his
treatment of his wife, will probably never be
known in time; but the unhappy woman fled
from a life of misery to a sudden and self-pro-
cured death. Her husband was not a polyga-
mist, nor were either of them “Mormons;” facts
which there seems little need for our stating,
for had either been different the tragic incident
would have been heralded to the world as a
natural result of “Mormon polygamy.” Sup-
pose this family to have been Methodists,
or Roman Catholics: did their domestic in-
felicity destroy any truth which either of
these religious systems possess? Did it
prove either to be a false faith? And is
their unhappiness and the suicide resulting
from it, any argument against marriage, or any
reason why people should not marry but re-
main single?
	Suicides are so common in Christian countries
that they form a prominent item in social and
criminal statistics. What would be thought of
the person who would reason, because married
persons lived unhappy and one or other of a
couple sought this terrible means of breaking
the marriage tie, that therefore marriage was a
curse to the human family? Yet reasoning of
this absurd kind is freely used relative to “Mor-
monism,” and the system of marriage which is
held sacred by the Latter-day Saints. Men and
women have their irritable moments, their
hours following rash words hastily spoken,
when regret, pain, and even anger, hold sway.
This is the result of their inability to exercise
perfect control over themselves; one of the evi-
dences that humanity is imperfect. Yet who
shall say that any ordained condition of society
is responsible for this? It only proves, as the
poet has beautifully expressed it, that “The
trail of the serpent is over us all.”
	The case which we have referred to in this
city furnishes no text, only that man can be
unkind and brutal, and woman, maddened, can
do desperate things. And had the pair been
professedly “Mormons” this would have been
the only text derivable from it. "While the
conclusion would have been certain that neither
lived according to the Gospel of the Son of
God. But one thing must be noted in connec-
tion with it. The very silence of those papers
and parties that would have seized the circum-
stance most eagerly as a powerful argument
against polygamy, had the persons been “Mor-
mons,” is an acknowledgement of the superi-
ority of “Mormon” principles, by admitting
that it would have been much more noteworthy
had it occurred in a polygamist family. While
the truth remains the same, that neither mon-
ogamy nor polygamy is free from misery where
ungoverned passion rules; while they who are
governed by correct principle, animated by
pure desires, and understand aright the relation-
ship which the sexes bear to each other and man-
kind to the Creator, will live in love, peace
and happiness, though the famly circle should
embrace one husband and fifty wives.


ENJOYMENT FOR THE YOUNG.

	One of the most pleasant and interesting 
sights we have witnessed in a long time was the
Sunday School excursion from the north part
of Utah County, to this city, on Monday, the
21st ult. The Sunday Schools of Lehi, Ameri-
can Fork, Pleasant Grove and Alpine, to the
number of over thirteen hundred, accompanied
by their Superintendents and Teachers, and by
Bishop Harrington, of American Fork, Bishop
Brown, of Pleasant Grove, and Bishop Mc-
Cullough, of Alpine, and having along the
brass bands of Lehi and American Fork,reached
this city about noon. They visited the New
Tabernacle, heard the grand organ play, looked


through the building; and then marched to the
Theatre where they enjoyed an afternoon per-
formance. At about half past four o’clock in
the afternoon they left by the cars for Lehi, a
happy-looking, satisfied, intelligent company,
having evidently much enjoyed the excur-
sion.
	This thing of providing pleasure for a large
number, mainly of young and growing persons,
is worthy of all commendation. Youth seeks
amusement as naturally as the flowers seek
sunshine, and if denied it or deprived of it, will
droop and wither as a tender plant would be-
fore a keen frosty blast. But the amusement
should be carefully planned and arranged by
more mature minds, that it may be at once a
means of instruction and enjoyment; a medium
by which intelligence can be increased and the
desire for amusement be gratified. Because
this Sunday School excursion met these objects,
feasted the young eyes and gladdened the young
hearts, while giving them a more extended idea
of what is to be seen in places away from home,
we viewed it with so much favor; and trust the
mothers of the future hope of Israel will lend
every aid in their power to the repetition of such
seasons of enjoyment and pleasure for the
growing “olive branches.”


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿PREPAYING NEWSPAPER POSTAGE.
			     —
Complaint has been made that subscribers to
the EXPONENT in Ogden have been required
to pay three months’ postage when commenc-
ing to receive their papers about a month be-
fore the postal quarter expired; and another
three months’ postage at the beginning of the
next quarter; thus making the payment of six
months’ postage for four months. We have
examined into the law on the subject, and find
that the postmaster is required to demand three
months’ postage in advance; but a subsequent
ruling on the same law, by the postal authori-
ties, decides that a person may pay for a fraction
of the quarter with the full quarter; or, in other
words, a subscriber to the EXPONENT who com-
mences to receive it a month before the postal
quarter terminates, can pay the postage in ad-
vance for four months, being the one month
and the ensuing quarter, while payment for the
one month alone could not be taken at the post
office. It may be that the postmaster at Ogden
is not aware of this ruling, but if he searches
the “Postal Record" he will find it, and all
postmasters are required to be governed by
it.


﻿HOME AFFAIRS.
					   —
	THE SAD occurrence of the drowning of the
little son of Mr. L. P. Borg, in the 3rd Ward of
this city, on the 21st ult., should be another
forcible warning against carelessness, and neg-
ligence in leaving wells or other places with
the possibilities of accidents to children.
	ON FRIDAY, the 18th ult., Mrs. Bower, a
milliner of this city, committed suicide by tak-
ing laudanum, the terrible act being prompted,
it is said, by domestic unhappiness. The very
worst way to meet an existing evil, which may
be a cause of sorrow, is to rush unbidden into
eternity.
	ANY ONE who visited the Matinee on the af-
ternoon of the 21st ult., when the excursionists
from Utah county were present, could not fail
of being impressed with a sense of what virtue
and temperance can and do produce. It was a
fair representation of a little portion of the
growing strength of Israel.
	PROFESSOR DUE'S brilliant display of fire
works, given on the evening of the 24th ult., on
the Museum grounds, was a source of general


satisfaction and gratification to every one who
witnessed it. The music was good, and the
splendor of the different colored, spangling,
sporting flames, as they darted heavenward, or
whirled in rapid evolutions, was delightfully
entertaining.
	THE EFFORTS which are being made by the
Superintendents and Teachers of Sabbath and
day schools to enhance the interest of the
young of our Territory in obtaining useful
knowledge, in order that they may themselves
become useful in society, is praiseworthy and
should be continued and increased as rapidly as
wisdom and opportunities will admit; and be
aided by parents and all who are interested in
the welfare and cultivation of the growing
generation.	
	ON WEDNESDAY afternoon, 23rd ult., as Seth
Loveland, son of Colonel Chester Loveland, of
Brigham City, was returning home from Cor-
inne in a wagon, with two young ladies, one of
the ladies, who was driving the team, dropped the
reins, which Mr. Loveland attempted to regain
by reaching over the front of the wagon. He
fell, and the wheels passed over his body in-
flicting injuries so severe that he died the next
morning. The sad event has called forth many
sincere expressions of sympathy for the family,
and of regret that a fine young man should be
thus suddenly and terribly deprived of life.
	ANOTHER RAILROAD has been commenced in
Utah. The Utah Central has been a great suc-
cess, fully verifying the expectations of its
projector, President B. Young. The Utah
Southern and Utah Northern are both making
progress towards bringing the remote parts of
the Territory into railroad communication. The
American Fork line, as a feeder to the Utah
Southern, is now in operation up the canyon
nearly to Deer Creek, with its terminus at
Lehi; and a week ago ground was broken for
another branch road from the Utah Southern
to Bingham Canyon, connecting with the main
line at Sandy Station. No portion of the West
is making greater strides in desirable progress
than Utah; and yet,where would all the wonder-
ful development of the great West have been,
had it not been for the handful of Pioneers who,
a quarter of a century ago, traveled in faith,
and forced their way to the successful coloniza-
tion of these valleys?


﻿WOMAN’S VOICE.
			    ——
	BIG COTTONWOOD, Oct. 10th, 1872.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I highly appreciate your excellent paper,
which must contribute greatly to the inter-
ests of our sex here, as through it we have
the privilege of communicating our views
and aspirations and strengthening each
other in our most holy faith.
	I have been a member of the Relief Society
from its first organization here. I have been
for a long time unable to meet with the
Society, but my heart is with the Sisters
and I love them with that tender affection
which I bore for my own mother and sisters
according to the flesh. I left houses and
lands, father, mother, sisters and brothers
for the Gospel’s sake, and expect here to re-
ceive the reward promised by the Savior to
those who forsake all else for the purpose of
serving Him. I desire to be useful in His
Kingdom, all that I am and have are de-
voted to the blilding up thereof. The sub-
ject of co-operation is one of the deepest in-
terst to us. We have learned already that
union is strength, and as the “downtrodden
women of Utah” have access to every hon-
orable avocation for the purpose of enrich-
ing and enobling themselves, I see no reason
why co-operation should not be introduced
[new column]
and carried on very extensively, among
them. By means of co-operation in our
Societies we are enabled to assist in gather-
ing the poor, and sustaining them in our
midst until they can find ways of sustainiug
themselves. But great improvements can
be made by us if we are willing to be taught
and will be obedient to the requirements of
Heaven. There are many children among
us that are learning mischef for want of
proper employment. Might not schools of
a co-operative nature be instituted in con-
nection with establishments for different 
kinds of labor, silk culture, the manufactur-
ing of silk goods, and other productions, in
which children could be taught, even at a
very early age, to be useful, and still have
their regular hours for study and also for re-
creation? If you fill a measure full of wheat
of a choice kind, there is no room for seeds
of obnoxious weeds therein; and likewise,
if we see that the minds of our children are
stored with precious grains, there will be no
place in them for erroneous doctrins to
enter. I know a sister who has about
twenty acres of land which is enclosed and
as productive as any land in the Territory,
which she would gladly put into any co-
operative company of the kind, to do good;
and another who is a silk dyer, and who is
waiting for an opportunity to set up her
business. These sisters have the spirit of
co-operation. Many such, by waiting, might
accomplish a great deal of good. Father is
rich. The earth is His, the cattle upon a
thousand hills, the gold and the silver are
all His; and He would let us use His means
for the building up of His Kingdom, if we
would trust him and exercise and exhibit
good judgment. I pray for the success of
every wise and laudible undertaking.
				——		  EXCELLETE.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT :
	Why does not some one take it in hand to
have lawful marriage proven a crime and
prohibited? I should think that some of our
loving, influential, and earnest sympathizers
—particularly women who have to listen
day after day to such heartrending and
astonishing questions as “Will polygamy
ever be a crime in Utah !” might see it. If
the Lord ever commanded Adam and Eve
to multiply and replenish the earth, He
also commanded Abraham to take Hager
and by her raise up a son of whom he would
make a great nation. If the latter instance
may be proven a crime, so also may the for-
mer, for both were commanded by the same
great Lawgiver. Who can doubt that the
Alwise and Merciful God had respect unto
His daughter Hager, her prayers and de-
sires, as well as to Abraham and Sarah and
their supplications? She was one of His
children, as they were, and for her benefit
as well as theirs was the commandment
given, that she might fulfill the designs of
God concerning her, and assist in glorifying
His name. True, the Lord suffered her to
be cast out from her husband’s house, but
He did not forsake her. Neither will he
forsake women now, if they will only trust
Him, and acknowledge His dealings among
the children of men. “Will polygamy ever
be a crime In Utah !” is not half so sound a
question as one I heard asked, not long ago,
by a lady who was tired and fretted in her
domestic relations,and who was sane enough
to ask advice of one who was able to give it,
in a kind, sisterly and reasonable way. The
question was: “How long will we have to be
tried in these things?” And the answer
came pleasant and cheery, from one who
had passed the ordeal and triumphed nobly,
“Until they no longer try us.” I have more
to say on this subject, but will intrude no
further upon your space at present. 	G.


﻿				WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿MISCELLANEOUS. 
			     —
	DOCTOR Franklin said that every little
fragment of the day should be saved.
	A ST. LOUIS paper tells its readers that
“Louisville mules, after kicking the counten-
ance off their drivers, calmly haul the corpse
to an undertaker’s shop, where it can be at-
tended to.”
	THE EMPEROR of Austria has established
a Jewish theological faculty in the Imperial
University, to be equal in its rights and
privileges to the Roman Catholic and Prot-
estant faculties.
	CHILD-SELLING in Japan is carried on to
such an extent that the Government is try-
ing to suppress it. The purchase money of
a little child is only three dollars, and the
girls of fifteen years of age can be bought
for twenty.
	THE DIFFERENCE in nature was well il-
lustrated the other day at the depot. Two
sisters, met:	“O, my dear sister,” said one,
exhaustedly, as they embraced. “You’ve
been eating onions,” said the other, calmly
and fearlessly.
	A LOVING heart and pleasant countenance 
are commodities which a man should never
fail to take home with him. They will best
season his food and soften his pillow. It
were a great thing for a man that his wife
and children could truly say of him:	“He
never brought a frown or unhappiness across
his threshold.”
	FRET NOT.—“The horse that frets is the
horse that sweats,” is an old saying of
horsemen, and it is just as true of men as
horses. The man that allows himself to
get irritated at every little thing that goes
amiss in his business, or in the ordinary af-
fairs of life, is a man that, as a rule, will
accomplish little and wear out early. He
is a man for whom bile and dyspepsia have
a particular fondness, and for whom chil-
dren have a particular aversion. He is a
man with a perpetual thorn in the flesh,
which pricks and wounds at the slightest
movement; a man for whom life has little
pleasure and the future small hope.
	CHINESE ETTIQUETTE.—The Chinese “carte
de visite” is a curiosity. It consists of a
bright scarlet paper, with the owner’s name
inscribed in large letters—the bigger the
more exquisite. For extra grand occasions
the card is folded ten times, the name is
written on the right hand lower corner,with
a humiliating prefix, like, “your very stupid
brother,” “your unworthy friend who bows
his head and pays his respects,” etc., the
words “your stupid” taking the place of
“yours respectfully.” It is etiquette to re-
turn these cards to the visitors, it being
presumable that the expense is too great for
general distribution.
	A YOUNG woman recently appeared before
the Court at Liege with a demand for a sep-
aration or divorce from her husband, on the
ground that he had hired, without her con-
sent, a suit of apartments which she could
not occupy. She was informed that this
simple disregard of her wishes was not suf-
ficient ground for a divorce. The law de-
manded that she should follow her husband.
“What! the law compels me to follow my
husband?” “Certainly.” “We shall see,
then.” The lady disappeared and at once
attached herself to her husband and followed
him every step that he took for two days.
This was sufficient to attract the attention
of the whole city, for the poor husband could
no more escape her than he could his
shadow. Finally the police interfered, and
she was arrested for causing the gathering
of a crowd on the streets.


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
				—
	The first railway in Tunis has been
opened.
	In New York, 77,790 pupils attend the
public schools. 
	There are 285 iron-clad vessels in the
twelve navies of the world.
	There are 573 lighthouses on the coast
of the United States, and 22 light ships.
	Parepa Rosa has received a golden med-
al from the London Philharmonic Society.
	Since the abolition of the gallows in Iowa
there has not been a murder in that State.
	An iron church, costing $160,000, for the
town of Amron, Peru, was shipped from
New York.
	Street cars are said to run to Mount
Zion, Jerusalem, the street lamps being
lighted with gas.
	The citizens of Ohio, it is estimated, have
their lives insured to the aggregate amount
of $130,000,000.
	In Perry city, Georgia, one dollar tax is
abated for every shade tree planted by the
citizens along the sidewalks.
	An English church has been erected at
Cairo, and the Khedive of Egypt has of-
fered a site for the purpose.
	In a stone quarry at Anamoso, Iowa, I
solid block of stone,on being sawed through,
was found to contain a petrified human foot.
	SPEAKING of the climate of the Argen-
tine Republic, Professor Gould says: “A
bowl of water left uncovered in the morning
is dry at night; ink vanishes from the ink-
stand as if by magic; the bodies of dead
animals dry up instead of decomposing, and
neither exercise nor exposure to the sun’s
rays produce perspiration.”
	Shakespeare was married at 18; Dante,
Franklin and Bulwer at 24; Kepler, Mozart
and Walter Scott at 26; Washington, Napo-
leon I. and Byron at 27; Rossini, the first
time at 30 and the second time at 54; Schil-
ler and Weber at 31; Aristophanes at 36;
Wellington at 37; Talma at 39; Luther at
42; Addison at 44; Young at 47; Swift at
49; Buffon at 53, and Goethe at 57.
	A wonderful cave has just been dis-
covered in Kansas, which has a lake of pure,
clear water extending almost from the en-
trance, in a northeast direction, as far as the
sight of the discoverer could reach. He
threw stones as far as possible, and could
hear nothing but the splashing sound that
water gives echoed back to him. The
height of the roof above water is seven of
eight feet.
	West of the Mississippi river the United
States still owns 973,482,593 acres, dis-
tributed as follows: Missouri, Iowa and
Arkansas, 16,000,000; Dakota and Wyom-
ing, 145,295,284; Montana, 86,604,605; Kan-
sas, 43,148,076; Nebraska, 55,223,637; Color-
ado and Idaho, 117,800,000; New Mexico
and Utah, 224,140,000; Nevada and Arizona,
136,000,000; Minnesota, 36,876,170; Indian
Territory, 154,000,
	The total reduction of customs duties un-
der Act of Congress, June 6th, 1872, and on
tea and coffee under the Act of May 1st,
1872, on the principal dutiable articles and
manufactures, in addition to the free list, as
estimated at the Treasury Department,
amounts to $31,172,761 38. Of this sum the
reduction on tea and coffee alone amounts to
$15,893,846 67. In addition to this there is
a total reduction of interest and taxes of
$20,651,000—making a grand total of $51,-
923,761.


﻿[Written for the Exponent.]
		A PLEA FOR THE BOYS.	
	Oh, yes, I know the boys are wild,
that they use bad language and cultivate
bad habits, and, as you say, “make them-
selves a great nuisance;” but, my friend, I
don’t think the boys deserve quite so much
blame as they get. When a little fellow 
goes with his father to church, if he is well
taught, he will listen to what the speaker
says; and especially will he notice every
word which comes from the lips of his own
father. Well, if father testifies that it is
the duty of all who would wear the name of
Saints, to keep the Word of Wisdom and
follow the strict rules of temperance in all
things, and especially to abstain from hot
drinks and intoxicating beverages of all
kinds, if the boy knows nothing to the con-
trary, he is apt to think his father is a
pretty good Saint. But if father goes home
from church and sits down to his dainty cup
of tea, and after dinner lights his pipe or
cigar and lazily puffs its filthy fumes with
the air of perfect contentment which smok-
ers are apt to assume, the young philosopher
will draw sharp comparisons between pre-
cept and practice, and if he make a poor
choice, remember it is no more than his
father did. If some quiet neighborhood is
in an uproar on a fine morning through find-
ing woodracks and wagons cutting gambols
on the tops of fences and barns, and pendant
cats making hideous music, and dogs and
yearlings running wild with kettles tied to
their tails, don’t suppose that “those boys”
invented all the mischief themselves; but
rest assured that some father, uncle or other
“jolly good fellow” has been regaling the
“young imps” with spicy reminiscences of
his boyhood, and this is only the legitimate
fruit of the seed thus sown. If boys waste,
their fine powers of mind in studying how
to evade the drudgery of learning lessons,
and play tricks to puzzle and annoy the
schoolma’am, don’t forget how the old boys
boast of their proficiency in that line. Don’t 
set them down as unmitigated annoyances
unless you wish them to prove your opinion
correct. Don’t always beg them to spare
your nerves when they want a little harm-
less play at home. When I heard a father
complain that “outside influences” was too
strong for him, and it was almost more than
he could do to keep his boys under any re-
straint whatever, I could not help wonder-
ing if “inside influence” were what they
should be. Did his son ever feel at liberty
to bring a comrade home with him for din-
ner, without fear that mother’s brow would
cloud and mother’s lips be dumb while the
youthful guest remained? Or that father,
ignoring the visitor’s presence, would im-
prove the occasion to rebuke him sternly for
some boyish negligence and wound his self-
respect in the presence of his friend? Did
father ever ask his opinion or advice con-
cerning even the smallest of his affairs, to
show that he thought his son a reasonable
being and possessed of at least ordinary un-
derstanding? Did father ever make the
slightest pretense of leaning upon him as
upon his best friend, or treat him more like
a peer than a servant? Parents, I fear we
are too jealous of our authority and exercise
too much control over our boys. Would it
not be better to lay by some of this authority
and show a little more of the friend and
companion? Show a little more interest in
their personal affairs, and cultivate in them
a little more interest in ours? Do not com-
pel them to this or that course because it is
their duty to obey, but paint for them the
results of righteousness in such charming


colors that they cannot fail to choose the
right way. And above all, let our teach-
ings be more by example than by precept.
Let us follow the principles which we teach;
let our actions correspond with our words,
and then the boys cannot fail to believe us.
But so long as we recommend one course
and follow another, we cannot expect their
moral basis to be quite settled. So long as
we deny them any recreation at home, we
need not be surprised if they seek it abroad.
So long as we do not lead them into proper
courses, we cannot wonder that they fall into
improper ones. So long as we are too stern
and austere, we cannot wonder that, in their
disgust, they fly to the opposite extreme.
Admit that they have some rights, as em-
bryo citizens, which you are bound to re-
spect; and then go before to lead them into
straight paths, rather than behind, to drive.
Say very little about “authority,” and
"obedience,” but contrive so that the boys
do just as you desire, while thinking they
are having exactly their own way.
	BEAVER, October, 1872.	L. L.	D.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿A CHEERFUL HOME.
	A single word may disquiet an entire
family for a whole day. One surly glance
casts a gloom over the household; while a
smile, like a gleam of sunshine, may light
up the darkest and weariest hours. Like
unexpected flowers which spring up along
our path, full of freshness, fragrance and
beauty, so kind words, and gentle acts,
and sweet dispositions, make glad the home
where peace and blessings dwell. No matter
how humble the abode, if it be thus gar-
nished with grace and sweetness, with kind-
ness and smiles, the heart will turn laugh-
ingly toward it from all the tumults of the
world, and home, if it be so homely, will be
the dearest spot beneath the circuit of the
sun.
	And the influence of home perpetuate
themselves. The gentle grace of the mother
lives in her daughters long after her head
is pillowed in the dust of earth; and fatherly
kindness finds its echo in the nobility and
courtesy of some who come to wear his
mantle, and to fill his place; while on the
other hand, from an unhappy, misgoverned
and disordered home, go forth persons who
shall make other homes miserable, and per-
petuate the sourness and sadness, the con-
tentions, and strifes, and railings, which
have made their own early lives so wretch-
ed and disordered.
	Toward the cheerful home the children
gather “as clouds, and as doves to their
windows;” while from the home which is
the abode of discontent, and strife, and
trouble, they fly forth as vultures to rend
their prey.
	The class of men that disturb and disorder
and distress the world are not those born
and matured amid the hallowed influences of
Christian homes; but rather those whose
early life has been a scene of trouble and
vexation, who have started wrong in the
pilgrimage, and whose course is one of dis-
aster to themselves and trouble to those
around them.


﻿				[Written for the Exponent.]
		HEALTH AND HAPPINESS.
				   —
	It is generally admitted that cheerfulness
is essential to health. Many women pine
under gloomy and despondent spirits, and
thus work themselves into maladies and
nervous affections, who might otherwise be
strong, whole-souled and energetic. Under
some circumstances, it almost seems that
this cannot be avoided; yet, by taking a
proper course, it can be overcome. It may


require a severe struggle and many an ex-
traordinary effort, but an average power of
mind and tolerable strength of character,
which can be increased by earnest prayer,
will conquer; and free a person from depres-
sion of spirits and languishing debility of
body, when all the doctors and medicines
that could be procured for the purpose would
utterly fail.
	To tell of the trials and hardships through
which we are passing, and talk over the
grievances we are brought to encounter, is
not the safest remedy or surest cure for such
ailments, but rather incentives to heart dis-
ease and irratibilities of different kinds.
Neither is it wise to sit mopishly down and
brood over discontent and dismal occur-
rences in the way of life, forgetful of the
blessings all around us, which we may pos-
sess if we will only earn and accept
them when they are offered to us. But
when bitter thoughts would force themselves
upon the mind, unthinking or unkind as-
sociates introduce subjects of a disagreeable
nature, the best way to do is to dismiss all
such things in short order, without stopping
to think it impossible to help dwelling upon
them. The very excellent means for do-
ing this are so numerous and varied, and so
much at the disposal of every one, that it is
hardly worth while to mention any of them
here. But the necessity of woman’s em-
ploying these means, understanding them
and taking time, aside from other duties,for
exercising them assiduously, is a subject of
importance to many. With some, just so
much work must be accomplished in a day,
whether they feel at every step as though it
must be the last,or otherwise. While others,
feeling dull and heartsick, often with just
cause, mope around and accomplish nothing,
when with their ambition slightly aroused,
they would be abundantly able to do a large
amount of labor,benefitting themselves there-
by in many ways. Either method for get-
ting along is a mistake. Extremes are un-
warrantable and should be avoided. To
work all the time or not at all is injurious to
health of mind and body. A walk in the
fresh air, a chapter from a good book,a romp
with the babies or a sweet little song, even
if you have to sing it yourself, often in- 
creases health and happiness.			L.


REFLECTIONS.
Editor Woman's Exponent:
	I have enjoyed reading your valuable
paper and would prefer to read rather than
to write. But I sometimes have reflections
which prove profitable to myself and may
interest others. I naturally love children
and always enjoy Sabbath Schools, when my
health will admit of my attendance. I left
home in May for a visit to one of the adja-
cent settlements. The change of air seemed
beneficial to my health, and I was invited
to spend the Summer, and when I found I
could make myself useful by instructing
children I gladly accepted the invitation. I
visited home too often to take a class in
Sabbath School, but went, however, as a
visitor; and on one occasion they opened the
School by singing “We are the true born
sons of Zion.” The children sang it well,
which caused my heart to rejoice in the
Spirit of the work, anticipating their future
capabilities if they continue to apply their
hearts unto wisdom, that is to love and fear
God; for we are told that “The fear of the
Lord is the beginning of wisdom.” Never
before did I feel the full weight of the re-
sponsibilities of a Sabbath School teacher.
The thought arose in my mind, would it be
of any value to be one of the true born child-
ren of Zion if not awake to one’s duty?
Were not the Jews the chosen people of
God? And yet how few acknowledged Jesus
Christ to be the Son of God! Were not the
Nephites blessed when humble and faithful
and filled with the Spirit of the Lord? But
look at the contrast when the cares of the
world engrossed their attention and caused
them to relax their hold of divine intelli-
gence from time to time, until the Lord
withdrew His Spirit and gave it to the
Lamanites, so that Samuel the Lamanite
foretold the coming of the Savior and also
concerning His death. The Book of Mormon
cites us to a parable of a vineyard, the fruit
of which became bitter and unfit for use, so
that it was necessary to hew it down, that
better might be placed in its stead. Is it
well, then, for us to spend our time in look-
ing at the faults of others except that we
may know how to shun evil and cleave unto
that which is good? Better would it
be for us to measure ourselves by the pattern
our Savior left us, that we may know how
near we come to it. This would teach us
humility and save ns from becoming bitter
in our minds, and prove a good example be-
fore our children who are in danger of being
embittered also. For the Apostle says, “If
God spare not the natural branches take
heed least He spare not thee.”
	I noticed also in the lesson, Math. 8th
chap. 11 and 12 verses, that it reads, "And
I say unto you, that many shall come from
the East and the West and sit down with
Abraham and Isaac and Jacob in the Kingdom
of Heaven; but the children of the Kingdom
shall be cast out into outer darkness; there
shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth.”
This is a mournful picture to look at; but is
it not profitable to see things as they are,
that we may love our children with the
right kind of love and not indulge them to
their own distruction? In all my acquaint-
ance with children I have never known one
whose attention could not be gained by read-
ing or rehearsing Bible stories; especially
when I felt to enjoy the Spirit of the Gospel
myself; and this we need in teaching the
young at home,as much as any missionary who
is sent abroad to preach the Gospel of Jesus
Christ; and with it we benefit ourselves as
well as those we instruct; and when their
attention is directed to the teachings of the
Savior, they are most easily governed. I
have sometimes said to children, “I am
sorry you did that, you should not do so,”
when they sorrowed as much as I did for
the wrong action, and this seemed to be all
the chastisement necessary.
							M. E. K.


A GOOD word is an easy obligation; not to
speak ill, requires only our silence, which
costs us nothing.	
	YOUNG persons should, above all things,
beware of beginnings, and by no means par-
ley with temptations.
	A WISE man is humble, because he thinks
of what he has yet to learn instead of
what he already knows.
	IT is one thing to wish to have truth on
our side, and another to wish to be on the
side of truth.—Wheately.
	WIT loses its respect with the good when
seen in company with malice; and to smile
at the jest which plants a thorn in another’s
breast is to become a principal in the mis-
chief.
	THERE is nothing more pitiable in the
world than an irresolute man, oscillating be-
tween two feelings, who would willingly
unite the two, and who does not perceive
that nothing can unite them.


NOTHING sets so wide a mark between a
vulgar and noble soul, as the respect and
reverential love of woman. A man who is
always sneering at women is generally a
coarse profligate, or a coarser bigot.
	LORD Bacon says, “But little do men per-
ceive what solitude is and how far it ex-
tendeth; for a crowd is not company, and
faces are but a gallery of pictures, and talk
but a tinkling cymbal, where there is no
love.”
	CARLYLE, after empting his quiver of
more satirical arrows than any brother es-
sayist, coolly says: “Sarcasm I now see to
be, in general, the language of the devil; for
which reason I have long since as good as
renounced it.”

								 St. George, Utah.
[next column]
	 
				WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

				A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL.

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.

	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"

	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of Woman’s Exponent, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on  leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading. 

	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In Woman’s Exponent a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.

	Woman’s Exponent will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year, $2.00	1 Copy six months, $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months, 9.00

	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers Woman’s Exponent, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
Vol. I.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, NOVEMBER 15, 1872.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	A wide room—room for improvement.
	Miss Loud is giving loud lectures on
“Woman Suffrage” in Massachusetts.
	A dangerous malady among horses has
spread with dreadful rapidity and melan-
choly results throughout the country.
	The Relief Society in Portland, Oregon,
has erected a “House” for homeless unfortu-
nates; an example worthy of notice and
imitation.
	Another terrible disaster, the burning
of the “Missouri” at sea, is recorded with
fearful pictures of suffering and death. The
accident occurred twenty-five miles from
Abaco, on the 22nd of October.
	It is estimated that about nine-tenths of
the people of Vienna live altogether in cafes
and restaurants. How will children thus
reared ever know the blessings which rests
in the memory of a “Sweet Home!”
	For the first time in the history of
American politics, women voted for Presi-
dent at the late Presidential election, and in
two or three places their votes were received,
while in others they were rejected. The
world moves.
	The next Congress will be more com-
pletely Republican in its character than any
that has met in Washington for some years.
Will it verify the hopes of Woman Suf-
ragists, and give woman suffrage to the
country? Time will tell.
	An effort is being made in France to
take the public schools out of clerical hands,
on account of the atrocious scandals concern-
ing the behavior of certain Fathers. The
marriage of Pere Hyacinthe seems to have
augmented the cause of discussion.
	James C. Crittenden, son of the late A.
P. Crittenden, is reported to have deter-
mined that Mrs. Fair should not remain in
San Francisco, after the result of the trial;
and to have been prevented from shooting
her by Mr. Tyler. He was doubtless,
“insane."
	An appropriation of $50,000 worth of
land has been made, for the establishment
of a Professorship, to be known as the
“Agassiz Professorship of Oriental Lan-
guages and Literature,” and to belong to
the University of California. The gift is
from Senator Tomkins, and is well applied.
	Miss Stride of London, has been striding
through the byways of that great City, in
the noble and highly laudable pursuit of
picking up and careing for, thus bringing
back to light and hope, poor, sorrow-stricken,
castaways, three hundred and seventy-five
of whom she has been the means of having
housed, clothed and fed, nursed and won
back to new, bright life and faith. This,
indeed, is true philanthropy.
	A man and woman in Scotland wishing
to be married and not having the requisite
funds to pay a minister, took each a handful
of meal, knelt down before a basin of water,
put the meal therein, mixing it in token that
they “would not sever till death did them
part,” took oath to this effect on a Bible, and
rising declared themselves man and wife.
A modern and economical method of getting
the matrimonial knot tied.
[new column]
	Mrs. Dr. Mary P. Sawtelle thinks that
every unmarried woman should have one
quarter section of land granted to her. She
has been successful in getting a small appro-
priation from the State Legislature of
Oregon for the encouragement and benefit
of immigrants; and has been moved to
engage in this good work by seeing thou-
sands of women whose protectors and
supporters have fallen in battle, toiling and
suffering in great cities. May her humane
exertions be well rewarded!
	On Saturday evening, 9th inst., at half-
past seven o’clock, a fire broke out in Boston,
which burned for about forty hours with
great violence, destroying over a hundred
million dollars worth of building, besides
the property in them. Coming a year and
a month after the great Chicago fire, which
it appears to have exceeded in the value
destroyed, it forces an inquiry as to the
cause not merely of the fire but of its fearfnl
spreading, setting at defiance all the means
known of for the suppression of conflagra-
tions in large cities.
	A highly interesting work is announced
as soon to be given to the public, bearing
the title of “Travels Around the World,” by
the late Hon. William H. Seward.   Olive
Risely Seward, the adopted daughter of Mr.
Seward, who accompanied him on his
journey, edits the work and gives accounts
of travels, observations and philosophical
reflections in the great Statesman’s own
words in connection with his conversations
with Presidents, crowned heads, East Indian
potentates and the Pope; of the crossing of
nearly all the mountains, oceans and rivers
in the world and the courtesies extended to
him in the countries which he visited.
	On Tuesday the 5th inst., Presidant U.
S. Grant, was re-elected to preside for
another term of four years over the United
States. The position taken by the woman
suffragists in the election campaign, the
declaration contained in the platform of the
Philadelphia Convention, and the subse-
quent action of the Massachusetts Repub-
licans, ought, when combined, to give some
assurance that woman suffrage would become
a portion of the supreme law of the land in
the next two years.  For the Republican
party, in the next Congress, can pass any
measure it pleases, and it can secure the
ratification by the requisite two-thirds of
the State legislatures of any amendment it
may propose to the Constitution.
	We clip the following from the “Cincin-
nati Commercial” of October 29th, and insert
it among Editorial Notes without further
comment: “A letter from Connecticut says:
Old Norwalk, long the Gibraltar Of Republic-
anism in Connecticut, but which was carried
by the Greeley party at the recent town
election, has still further advanced, and to-
day admitted to all the rights of an elector
Mrs. Sarah M. L. Huntington. The lady
appeared before the Board of Selectmen—
Messrs. Joseph P. Hanford, Andrew Selleck,
William H. Bauton and Henry K. Selleck,
Town Clerk, officers by law required to ex-
amine and admit all persons legally entitled
to suffrage, and demanded her rights by
virtue of the Constitution of the United
States. Her argument was clear and logical,
and could only be answered by the plea that
[new column]
in the opinion of most persons the law did
not contemplate women’s voting. Mrs.
Huntington so ably and dexterously knocked
aside this argument that the Board were
forced to the unanimous judgment that she
had proved herself equally entitled with
negroes to vote under the Fifteenth Amend-
ment, and Selectman Selleck administered
to her the customary freedman’s oath,
Her name was placed by the town clerk on
the voting list as a legally registered voter,
and nothing now can prevent her voting at
the coming election, as she is resolutely
determined to do. Thus Norwalk will be
entitled to the distinction of being the first
place in any State of the Union where a
female has been permitted to vote and
advanced to all the rights and privileges of
the American citizen. Two years ago last
April our State election occurred just as the
passage of the Fifteenth Amendment was
promulgated, and our Board admitted
negroes to vote, although they were denied
the privilege in every other town in the
State. Subsequent decisions of our ablest
lawyers confirmed the legality of that action.


﻿SABBATH SCHOOLS.
				—
Editor Woman’s Exponent:
	A great deal has been said and done of 
late by the First Presidency and others in 
behalf of Sabbath Schools. If the majority 
of parents would take greater interest in the
matter, and visit the Sabbath Schools
occasionally, it would be encouraging to
both teachers and scholars. Some may
think it onerous to go Sunday after Sunday
as a teacher to a class. But, speaking from
experience, I can say that to go with the
spirit of the Gospel is a delightful pleasure.
I have been a teacher some years, and have
never found it a loss but have been amply
rewarded. For while we thus impart the
blessings of knowledge to others we are
ourselves recipients of like blessings. If we
desire our sons and daughters to fill honor-
able positions in society, we must lay correct
foundations for them in their childhood.
Faith in God should be early and firmly im-
pressed upon the minds of our children,
that it may grow with their growth, and be
to them, in future time, a consolatory guide
to smooth life’s rugged pathway. The people
of the world seem to have lost the gift, so 
that when troubles overtake them they sink
in despair. Not so with the people of God.
Their tribulations serve only to consume
the dross, and elevate and refine the
character.
	The would-be sympathizers who draw
long faces and groan over the condition of
women in Utah, should reserve their 
sympathy for themselves and their associates.
We, as wives and mothers, honored and
blessed, seeking to ameliorate the condition
of all within the reach of their influence,
have no need of it.			   J. S. C.
	SMITHFIELD.


MISS LIVINGSTONE, daughter of the great
African explorer, has received a letter from
her father, who acknowledges having been
found by Stanley. Will the doubts in Stan-
ley-Africanus be silenced at last?


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿[FOR the Exponent.
		A MOTHER’S RESIGNATION.

Oh, rare, pale image! wrought in priceless clay;
Of faultless outline, wearing matchless grace;
Whose broad, fair brow where mind held kingly sway,
Well crowned the beauty of the tender face,
Small fingers, clasping flowers pale and sweet—
Fair emblems of the dear one’s brief, bright stay !
All, all, from gold-crowned head to slender feet.
More precious are than pearls, though only clay.

For oh, this empty casket held a soul
Most grand, yet gentle, noble, good and brave;
And that shall live while endless ages roll,
Though this dear clay must moulder in the grave.
The glad, young soul, buoyant, pure and bright,
With all the witching ways to childhood known,
Those clear, deep eyes, truth’s crystal mirrors, light
No more this world, with clouds now overblown.

The world rolls on with all its devious ways,
Its cruel pitfalls, tempests, snares and thorns,
And struggling souls who fix their fainting gaze
Upon the goal, beyond the strife and storms.
Temptations still entrap unwary feet,
And rushing floods engulf, and lions roar,
And treachery stings, still yawns the gulf deceit,
And chasms the bridge that faith can scarce span o’er,

Oh favored son! whose white and tender feet
Were not fore-doomed the flinty paths to tread,
And bleed and blister ’mid the dust and heat,
While thorny chaplet crowned the martyr head;
Thou wert admitted through a pearly door
Where dewy flowers instead of serpents twine;
Temptation lurks in wait for thee no more,
For now, beloved one, thou art God’s—and mine.

Then go, my saint, and for thy mother pray,
Whose torn and bleeding heart beats faintly on,
And aches so yearningly thy head to lay
Upon her breast, thy heart against her own—
For flesh is weak; but mother-love is strong
And deep laid as the everlasting hills;
I’d not recall thee, lest I’d do thee wrong
To wish thee subject to life’s fearful ills.

While here, I longed to see thee conquer wrong
And champion virtue, strong in honor’s might;
But oh! how many a dear and gifted son
Has failed to win the battle all must fight
Thou might’s’ have won the victor’s deathless crown
And gained thy God’s serene, approving smile.
But, fearful thought! thou might’st have faltered
	down
The depths of sin, ensnared by cunning guile.

I fear not worldy pride may win thee now,
Nor guile betray, nor flames of guilt devour;
No passions vile e’er bend thy sinless brow,
Nor deadly foes assail thee every hour.
God’s will is wiser than our frail desires,
His mercy tenderer than our purest love;
And I can yield to this since He requires, 
And name thee now, “My waiting saint above.”
									Lu Dalton.


F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
					—
			SMITHFIELD, CACHE CO., 
						Nov. 8th, 1872. 

Editor Exponent:-
	We held our Monthly meeting on the 4th
inst., of which I send you a very brief
synopsis: Meeting was opened by singing
"O God the spring of all my joys:" prayer
offered by Counselor Mrs. Mary Ainscough:
minutes of previous meeting were read and 
accepted. President E. T. Morehead then 
addressed the meeting, encouraging the
sisters to be zealous in good works. Spoke
at some length upon Sabbath schools, and
the great blessings accruing therefrom to the
rising generation. felt deeply interested in
that laudable work, exhorted mothers to
have their children in attendance at proper
time, made reference to what the Hyrum
F. R. Society had done in behalf of the
Sabbath School in that settlement, which
was highly commendable. Counselor Mary
Ainscough spoke upon the same subject;
and upon the training of our children, 
urging the necessity of setting an example
before them worthy of imitation, etc. Mrs. 
J. Grant spoke on the principle of the 
Gospel and the blessing received in yielding
obedience thereto. 
	Some articles were read from the WO-
MAN'S EXPONENT which we prize very
highly. A vote was taken that we drop the 
[new column]
word Female from the name of the Society
as suggested by Sister E. R. Snow, which
was unanimous. Monthly meeting  adjourned
till the first Monday in December.
	Benediction by Mrs. N. A. Noble.
		Mrs. Jane S. Coleman, Secretary.
				_____
			Farmington, Oct. 25th, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	In Compliance with the request from
Sister Snow, to forward the proceedings of
our first meeting after seeing her notice, I
send the following. At our last meeting we
all felt to coincide with Sister Snow, and
the vote was unanimous to drop the word
“Female” from the name of our Society.
	We had a very good and interesting
meeting, the subject under consideration
being fashion and dress, to impress upon
the mothers the necessity of trying to cure
that spirit of fashion which exists among
the young daughters of Zion, at the present
time.
	I am a reader of the Exponent, anx-
iously watch for its coming, and am glad to
see it improving.
			S. J. Holmes, President.
			   _____
	From Mrs. Emily L. Hepworth, Secretary
of the F. R. Society of Oxford, Cache County,
we receive a lengthy report of a meeting held
at that place on the 22nd of October. Mrs.
M. A. Boice, who has been the President of
the Society for three years—since its organi-
zation—on account of her age and her many
responsibilities aside from those entailed
through her position in the Society, in
accordance with the advice of her husband,
presented her resignation to the sisters. A
very humble, pleasant spirit characterized
her resignation, and she blessed the sisters,
desiring to continue one with them and to
do all in her power to assist in sustaining and
strengthening the Latter-day-work. Kindly
feelings were expressed and thanks offered
to Sister Boice, for the good she had done in
the Society. Her resignation was accepted,
and on motion of Bishop George Lake, Mrs.
Sarah Cordon was chosen and sustained, by
a unanimous vote, to fill the vacancy thus
made. Sister Boice expressed warm wishes
for the success of the EXPONENT. 
				_____
	Reports of two meetings held in Ogden,
one on the 17th, the other on the 22nd of
Oct. The first was the regular semi-monthly
meeting of the Relief Society. Called to
order by President Mrs. J. S. Richards;
prayer by Mrs. Martha Bingham. The
minutes of the two preceding meetings
were read by the Secretary and acccepted. 
Sister Richards made remarks. She was
pleased with the carpet which had been
made by the Society, it had received much
praise both here and at Salt Lake City: She
had enjoyed herself much at Conference, had
visited and been instructed by Sister Snow. 
The motion was made and seconded that we 
leave off the prefixture of Female from the
name of the Society. Carried unanimously. 
An article concerning the name, was read
by sister H. C. Brown from the EXPONENT. 
Remarks were made by Martha Bingham,
Sarah Herrick and A. M. Frodsham. An
especial meeting was appointed for the fol-
lowing Tuesday, at, which, Sister E. R. 
Snow, her example as a faithful and energetic
Saint and her approaching jonrney to Jeru-
salem were to be topics for consideration.
Secretary A. M. Frodsham requested the
sisters to subscribe for the EXPONENT, and
meeting was dismissed by Sister Richards. 
							Oct. 22. 
	The sisters met according to adjournment, 
sister J. Richards presiding. Several sisters
[new column]
from Marriott and Lynn settlements were
present. Prayer offered by Sister Bicking-
ton. Singing “We thank thee, O God, for
a Prophet.” Sisters Bickington, Baird and
Tracy addressed the meeting. President J.
S. Richards spoke of the faithfulness and
untiring diligence of Sister Snow. Considered
her pattern for us all. She was pleased with
the interest and anxiety manifested by the
sisters to do good. Sister Mitchell from S.
L. City said she appreciated Sister Snow,
had often had the privilege of listening to
her wise counsel and been thereby. blessed
and edified. Further remarks were made
by J. S. Richards and Counselor H. C.
Brown. Sincere feelings of love and respect
for Sister Snow, and blessings on her, were
warmly and generally expressed. Meeting
adjourned for two weeks. Prayer by A. M.
Frodsham.
			____
		WILLARD, Nov. 3, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT.—
	Our Society was organized the 25th of
April 1868, and since that time has been the
means of doing much good, in feeding the
hungry, clothing the poor, and administer-
ing to the sick. Our President, Sister Mary
Ann Hubbard, is a woman full of faith and
good works, and is ably assisted by her
Counselors, Sisters Hellen C. Wood and Mary
Harding. The sisters are united and feel
well, always willing to assist in anything
that is required of them. At a Sewing
Meeting, held on Thursday, Oct. 31st, the
motion was carried to drop the word Female
from the name of the Relief Society. I
pray that the Lord will bless the endeavors
of all in their desire to do good.
			Hannah Cook, Secretary.
				____
	A very brief report from Mrs. Sarah H.
Crosbey, Hebron, Washington Co., shows
that a re-organization of the Relief Society
in that settlement, took place on the 19th
ult., that a working meeting was held on the
2nd inst., and that the Society is a small but
prosperous one.


﻿A Wisconsin man lent his cook a $150
watch to know when to get his dinner, and
hasn’t seen the cook or time-piece since.
	The Washington clergymen are protest-
ing against Sunday funerals, and doctors are
requested to regulate demises accordingly.
	While the population of Russia has in-
creased five fold in the last century and a
half, that of the United States has increased
forty fold.
	A California paper says that during a
recent earthquake in that State a man made
an involuntary exchange of his farm for a
fish pond.
	There are four lady students in Middle-
town College, members of the freshman
class. Three of them had previously been
repulsed at Amherst.
	Mrs. Scott-Siddons, the well known
reader, is thought by some connoisseurs
quite the best living interpreter of charac-
ters in which the elements of tenderness,
pity and woe are conspicuous. She was born
in India, in 1844. Her father died when
she was quite young; then her mother
returned to England, and afterward went to
Germany to educate her two precious
daughters. She first appeared as an ama-
teur actor in a private dramatic entertain-
ment, when only eleven years old, and filled
all who saw her performance with wonder at
her talent and promise. She was trained for
the stage, made her debut in London, in
1867, and gave her first reading in this
country at Newport, in 1868. Since her first
appearance before the public, she has filled
a leading place in the histrionic profession.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
				—
	THE SIMPLE truth is, that, unless women
have a routine of duty that diverts their
thoughts from themselves, and gives them
something to think of besides dress and the
exhibition of it, they degenerate. The only
cure for this that we know of, is universal
housekeeping. There is no man who can
afford to pay a fair price for board, who can-
not afford to keep house; and housekeeping,
though it be ever so humble, is the most
natural, and the healthiest office to which
woman is ever called.
	The devil always finds work for idle hands
to do, whether the hands belong to men or
women; but American men are not apt to
be idle. They are absorbed in work from
early until late, and leave their idle wives,
cooped up in rooms that cost them no care,
to get rid of the lingering time as they can.
Is it kind to do this, or is it cruel? If it is
kind in its motives, it is cruel in its results.
The whole system of boarding-house and
hotel life is vicious. To live in public, to be
on dress parade every day, to be always part
and parcel of a gossiping multitude, to live
aimlessly year after year, with thoughts
concentrated on one’s person and one’s
selfish delights, to be perpetually without
a routine of healthy duty, is to take the
broadest and briefest road to the degrada-
tion of all that is admirable and loveable
in womanhood. It is to make, by the most
natural process, that gay, gaudy, loud, frivo-
lous, pretentious, vain, intriguing, unsatis-
fied, and unhappy creature which the
Englishman knows as the “New York
woman.”—“Scribner’s Monthly.”
	The above is certainly well adapted to the
position of ladies belonging to fashionable
society. Our country girls and farmers’
wives would be likely to say, that Ameri-
can women are no more apt to be idle than
American men; but such ones see and know
but little of the vanity and worthlessness
which constitute the lives of fashionable
women, and which is being over fed and
continually encouraged by fashionable men,
even while they cry out against it.
	IRON RUST may be removed by salt mixed
with a little lemon-juice.
	REMOVE MILDEW dipping in sour but-
termilk, and laying in the sun.
	A LITTLE oil of cloves poured into a bottle
containing gum mucilage prevents it from
souring. 
	A SOLDER for uniting steel to brass, and 
one which adheres perfectly to both and is
not affected by the unequal dilution of the
two metals, is composed of three parts of
tin, thirty-nine and a half parts of copper,
and ten parts of zinc. 
	PEAS A LA BOURGOISE.-Put your peas, 
which should not be old, into a clean stew-
pan with butter, in the proportion of half
a pound to a quart of peas; add the heart
of a good, large lettuce, a bunch of parsely, 
a few small onions, and a pinch of salt; let
them stew together slowly till done; thicken
before serving with a little butter and flour;
add a little white sugar in powder, and
serve. Some cooks thicken with the yolks
of two eggs instead of flour. 
	A NICE way to bake sour apples is to dig
out the cores, place the apples in a deep tin, 
fill the cavities where the cores come out
with sugar, pour a cup of hot water in the
tin and bake in a quick oven; you will have
a healthful and palatable dish. 
	HOUSEHOLD BREAD, for large families.-
Mix fourteen pounds of flour with six
pounds of mealy potatoes, previously well
mashed; add three teaspoonfuls of salt and 
[new column]
make a dough with water; then work
it well with four teaspoonfuls of yeast, and
after three or four hours bake it.
	TRANSPARENT POMADE.—Take sperma-
ceti, two ounces; castor oil, five ounces;
alcohol, three ounces; oil of bergamot, half a
drachm; oil of Portugal, half a drachm. Melt
together the spermaceti and castor oil, pour
in the alcohol by degrees, stop the heat and
add the oils; stir well to incorporate, and
pour into glass jars. Flavor to suit.


﻿[Written for the Exponent.
WHERE SOCIAL REFORM MUST ORI-
			GINATE.
			    —
	In the present age of the world we enjoy
countless advantages over all that have gone
before us, in the comforts and conveniences
which the grasping, comprehensive mind of
man, with the blessing of God, continually
brings to light, but with the multiplied
wants, and puny strength of the present
day, each new invention to economize time
and toil seems only just sufficient to enable
man to exist until something more can be done
to lighten his grievous burdens. His com-
plicated and numberless bodily ills drag
him down like an incubus, and chain his
mind to the dust when it should surmount
the clouds. Men of intellect and learning
spend their lives concocting drugs with
which to combat the multiplicity of diseases
which prey upon humanity, and humanity
blindly, recklessly, swallows all, patent pills,
quack nostrums, and, above all, ardent
spirits in the form of bitters and tonics,
which last still further inflame those gross
animal passions which, all admit, are, in
their unchecked career the root of nine-
tenths of these debilities. It is acknow-
ledged that excessive indulgence has robbed
man of his primitive vigor and given rise to
a long train of frightful diseases which will
continue to afflict the race until means can
be used to end them. The sooner this is
done the better, and the trouble must be
met boldly.
	It is useless to cry out against social de-
pravity and deplore its evil work and
never lift a weapon against it. It is
equally useless to fight against remote
conseqlittlees and have the ruling cause
untouched. Why cannot men learn the folly
of battling against effects, while nurturing
aud fostering causes? Why bewail the
work of the destroyer in desolated homes
and the blighted promises of many a fair
young life, and call on the strong arm of
vengeance on the head of the evil doer; and
still purge not the hidden springs of iniquity,
and ignore the fact that those same mourn-
ing households have contributed, and still
contribute their mite, to the deep fountain
of that deadly poison which creeps insidiously
through society, the highest and the lowest,
over on the alert for its prey, here and there
fastening its fangs upon the most promising
in the land, never relaxing its hold until it
has devoured, not the body only, but the
usefulness, the peace, the soul of its victims?
And why be slaves to physic, seeking by its
unaided strength to overcome disease of
body and mind, while moral or rather im-
moral causes are daily originating new
phases of suffering? The source of disease
being sin, it must receive mental and moral
treatment. It is not enough that the vigi-
lant eye of the law is ever watchful to detect,
and its strong right hand ever uplifted
to punish crime; these measures deal only
with effects, cropping here and there a twig
from the great tree of immorality. But its
weapons are too short to reach the great,
strong trunk which is no more disturbed by
[new column]
the clippings than a strong oak by the
pruning knife. No! the strength of social
crime lies far beyond the verge of legal
jurisdiction, and at its safe distance, mocks
its futile endeavors. Then where shall we
look for its antidote, and whence is our
deliverance to come? It is plain that the
fountain of sin must be cleansed before the
stream will run clear. But where shall we
look for this fountain? Social evil does not
derive its strength and perpetuity from those
miserable libels on “the Image of God” who
unblushingly avow their infamy, and are
shunned as lepers by respectable citizens,
for they, poor, miserable, comfortless
wretches—as we know them—create far less
evil in the world than is laid to their charge.
It is where they cannot enter; where peace-
ful, law-abiding citizens dwell, that the
seeds of crime are planted and nourished
until they “can be no longer hidden,” and
then spewed forth to go abroad fulfilling
their dreadful mission. Look to it, you
fathers who “live above the law,” and
piously bewail the depravity of the world,
that you are not secretly giving aid and
sustenance to that very cause by giving full
license to those appetites which cannot be
said to degrade man “to the level of brutes,”
since brutes, without the aid of Reason and
Religion, do strictly observe the laws of
Nature. Oh, you honest citizens! do you
degrade yourselves “below” the brutes by
disregarding Nature’s laws in your own
homes, and seeking only your own sensual
gratification, persistently closing your eyes
to the fact that thus you perpetuate and foster
a low tone of morals, and send abroad into
the world, exaggerated types of your own
depravity, to carry their pestilential presence
and shameful deeds everywhere? Do you
honestly acknowledge to your accusing con-
sciences when your sons give themselves
up to evil excesses that your own ways of
life have been their ruin? Do you ever repent
in secret anguish that you did not exercise
your divine right of sovereignty over your
own inferior passions and lead instead of
following them, exercising them only with
a fixed and holy purpose, as a sower scatters
grain in the spring? This is where social
reform must originate. When you do this,
oh, fathers and mothers, and forsake the use
of stimulants, you shall have the proud
assurance that your sons and daughters will
never be unresisting victims to those detest-
able passions which are the prolific source
Of bodily suffering and mental anguish in
this sin-weltering world. This course will
restore man’s primitive vigor, and class as
entire gain instead of mere necessities, the
wonderful inventions of genius. This will
strike the heart of social evil and give law
no subjects on which to waste its idle
strength, and rob the countless swarms of
quacks of their victims whose own sins
place them helpless in their power. And
fail not to remember, oh, parents! that as
you are responsible for the existence of
deathless beings, so you are responsible for
their dispositions; and that through them,
your secret thoughts are brought to light;
and do not dare ignore your responsibility,
nor use irreverently your fearful, your
wonderful powers.			L. L.	D.


There are two things in this life for
which people are never prepared, and they
are twins.
	A prosperous merchant has for his motto:
“Early to bed, and early to rise; never get
tight and advertise.”
	For the Centennial of American inde-
pendance, Philadelphia will erect a building
that will cover fifty acres of ground.

flicted on criminals.......One of the late

fifty new novels this fall......Some of the
