arrangements to let notorious criminals
escape prosecution, on their consenting to
pay money towards one of the candidates
in the presidential contest.


﻿PHILOSOPY AND PROPHECY

	When the great Chicago conflagration was
held to be a judgement—one of those which
the prophecies have said should be poured out
upon the nations for their wickedness—the be-
lief so expressed was met with sneers of the
most violent kind, and the idea was denounced
as puritanical and fanatical in the extremest-
degree. In fact so wide-spread is the infidelity
of the present day, that to recognize the hand
of the Almighty in anything is held to be
fanatical and puritanical. The world of Chris-
tians have turned philosophers, and their
philosophy does not recognize God. It finds a
cause for everything without the intervention
of Deity; and, when narrowed down to a point,
popular religion now is a faith that does not in-
clude a belief in the existence of an Omnipotent
power at all.
	Last Saturday night another calamity like
the Chicago one burst upon the City of Boston.
For over forty hours the flames raged beyond
control, sweeping awy building after building
constructed of materials usually held to be fire-
proof. An exchange says:
	The same astounding results of intense heat
witnessed in the Chicago conflagration were
demonstrated in this. Granite buildings per-
ished almost instantaneously, crumbling to
powder as though they had been simply consol-
idated blocks of ashes or sand. Great masses
of stone burst into fragments, as if split by
blasts of nitro-glycerine, and the air seemed
full of deadly missiles, threatening destruction
to any one who attempted to stay the riotous
progress of the destroying flames.
	And of the Chicago fire a gentleman who
witnessed the awful conflagration says that
blazing beams of wood were carried long dis-
tances, as if invisible bands were at work to
so spread the agent of destruction. Forty years
ago the people of New York, Albany and
Boston were warned by the Lord through His
servants that His wrath would be poured out
upon them unless they repented. But who
would expect New York with its wealth, its
power, and its pride, to repent? And who
would look for the intellectual and haughty
Boston to think that it needed repentance? It
may seem harsh and almost inhuman to speak
thus of a terrible calamity while the sympathies
of the nation are turned towards the city that
has suffered so much. But would the surgeon
be humane whose sympathies prevented his
advising cauterization where a rabid dog had
bitten a human being? It will be said, and
justly, that this visitation, like all others of the
same kind, has swept away the last stay in life
of the good as well as of the bad. Probably
as in the Chicago fire the dwellings of the worst
women who live in Boston have not suffered.
But were they the wickedness of that city?
Who made them things of crime and shame?
Who drove them to degradation and ruin?
Let the men who occupy palatial buildings an-
swer.
	Philosophy seizes minute details and hypo-
critically asks explanation; while prophecy
deals with generalities, and warns a people
beforehand. Philosophy calls for an analitical
investigation of the causes which produce such
a calamity; prophecy foretells the evil and
warns a people against it. For years the
Almighty has warned this and other nations,
but His warnings have been laughed at and
disregarded. He has said that after the warn-
ing of His servants He would send earthquakes,
the sea breaking beyond its bounds, the voice
of the tempest, and the power of Jehovah
made manifest in great storms and tribulations
coming upon the earth. All these things are
of daily occurrence.  Weekly the inhabitants
of the earth are startled with accounts of
calamities which frighten and appal; yet
philosophy accounts for them while prophecy
is never considered. And apparently thus it
will be until the great consumation shall have
been effected, and the few having faith in God
shall see and experience the power of His
salvation.


﻿A FIXED AIM.
				—
	PEOPLE who have fixed aims in life, work
more persistently and, with better heart than
those who have nothing to look forward to, and
no distinguishing feature in their daily avoca-
tions. An ordinarily industrious person will
become so engrossed In a pursuit that little
disappointments will be heedlessly rushed by in
the determination to accomplish his or her ob-
ject, where a certain design is in view. The
same organization of humanity, having con-
ceived no bright hopes of a glorious success and
a brilliant future, will be overcome by the
merest  trifles in the way of discouragement,
and often relinquish a point when it has been
almost obtained with scarce an effort, becaus
some little obstacle appears in the way. It i
well to set out early in life, with a fixed pur-
pose. Better to strive for something, even if
disappointed in reaching the mark set up as a
guiding star, at the point first anticipated, than
to be an aimless sluggard, passing through the
world with nothing to strive for, nothing to
hope for, nothing for which to live.


﻿HOME AFFAIRS.
				     —
	PRESENT INDICATIONS are that more benefit
is likely to accrue from the Sabbath Schoois,
taught in the various Wards in this City, under
the new system of holding Sabbath Schools
and meetings, than has been realized heretofore.


As no Sabbath meetings are to be held in the
Tabernacle during the Winter, and the fore-
noon is to be devoted to Sunday Schools in
each Ward, opportunities are offered for a
much larger attendance at these schools.
Superintendents and teachers will seek the as-
sistance of elders who have the faculty of
imparting intelligence, to form and instruct
classes of youths in any or all useful branches
of education, but particularly in the principles
of the Gospel. Sacrament and evening meet-
ings will also be conducted in the meeting
houses of the different Wards.
	THE PHYSIOLOGICAL Class, under the super-
vision of Mrs. Sarah E. Kimball, is progressing
finely. Books appropriate for students com-
mencing the study of Physiology have been
ordered and received, and may be obtained by
application to Miss Mary E. Cook at the school-
room, the Social Hall.
	AMONG THE recent deaths which have occur-
red in this City, we note that of Sister James
S. Brown as one of the most affecting. Her
example as a true daughter, sister, wife and
mother, and a faithful Latter-day Saint, her
acquaintance testify.
					—
	WE WILLINGLY give place to the following
obituary notice which has been forwarded to us:
	Father Daniel Stanton was a soldier, in the
war of 1812. He has filled many responsible
positions in the Church and for his country
honorably. He leaves a numerous family to
mourn their bereavement. His loss is felt by
the community in which, for some time, he
filled the office of Judge and Justice of the
Peace, until last August. He was born May
28th, 1795, in Onondago, New York. Father
Morley, Daniel Stanton, etc., etc. and their
families were living as one; or in the Apostolic
Order. Joseph Smith’s first Elders said they
were sent there. Father Stanton was the first
to believe and confess it. Parley P. Pratt bap-
tized him. He had lived 77 years, 9 months and
29 days.
	ON FRIDAY, the 9th inst., a company of ladies
and gentlemen, by invitation of President B.
Young, enjoyed a ride to the Promontory and
back again, a distance of 180 miles, behind the
new locomotive which has been built for the
Utah Central Railroad. The weather was fine,
much of the scenery and road, including where,
for some distance, the latter is cut through
solid rock, was new to many of the excursion-
ists; and, altogether, the undertaking to have
a little season of pleasurable entertainment was
a quiet success, as all such excursions are when
properly arranged.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


CORRESPONDENCE OF MISS SNOW.

	Miss E. R. Snow writes from Chicago, under
date Oct. 30, 1872:
	My trip thus far has been very satisfactory.
In crossing the plains, I frequently drew the
contrast between the present and the past, and
could hardly realize the present to be a living
reality. To travel with ease, devoid of fatigue,
in three days, a distance which a few years ago
required more than three months of weariness
and privations to accomplish, is certainly a
very great change. 	*	*	*	I took an
earnest glimpse of the beautiful, stupendous
bridge as we crossed the Missouri river. It is a
piece of workmanship worthy of a critical day-
light observation.
	We left Council Bluffs in the midst of one of
those thunder-storms so common to that local-
ity, beyond the necessity of artificial irrigation.
It was 9 o’clock at night, but what were the
night and the storm to us! Instead of pitching
tents and circling beneath a dripping roof, we
were comfortably seated in a palace car and
traveling at an almost incredible speed. So
much for the blessings of God on the march of
improvement. These night travels cheat us of
many pleasurable scenic views, a tax which
time-saving naturally brings.
	At the Bluffs we took the Rock Island route
to Chicago, and arrived at R. I. City where we
breakfasted, and took a hasty view of the stir-


ring, beautiful place, with the railroad bridge—
one in progress of erection—all curiosities to
one as little acquainted with railroads as my-
self. The scenery from Rock Island to Chicago
is varigated, and as seen while passing, as we
did, at times at the rate of nearly a mile per
minute, is very delightful; one characteristic
worthy of notice, being the freshness of verdure
in comparison to what we had previously
passed.
	We arrived here last evening at 4 o’clock, and
the missionaries to Europe left on the 5 o’clock
train for New York.	*	*	* 	My health
is good, and my heart full of gratitued. Sister
Mercy R. Thompson joins in love to all our
good sisters.	
						ELIZA R. SNOW


[Written for the Exponent.]
		A WORD ON OBEDIENCE.

	Children of the Saints, what are your as-
pirations? Would you like to be great and
honorable and distinguished men and
women? Let me tell you how to become so.
It all depends on your obedience and faith-
fulness. Be obedient to your parents,
honor them and learn to be punctual in
every thing. These are the foundations to
build upon, then it will not be hard for you,
as you grow older, to obey the counsel of God
which is given to man. When you see a
disobedient child you will see an unhappy
one. If a child has a rebellious spirit and
acts contrary to his parents’ wishes, he feels
ashamed to meet those parents, and if he is
made to comply he is angry and sullen and
you will often hear such ones say, “I wish I
was dead for I am never happy,” On the
other hand, you see one who is willing to be
lead by his father or mother, and you find
one who is contented and happy and joyful.
Such ones grow up a joy and comfort to their
parents, an ornament to society, and a bless-
ing to the world. And now, my sisters, if
we have not learned this great lesson, it is
high time, that we began to school our minds
in obedience to the commands our Father
has given us, for our exaltation and happi-
ness. Often when we feel as though
we could not endure what we are called to
pass through, it is partly because of a diso-
bedient spirit and our self-will, and we feel
like the disobedient child, we wish we were
dead, we are so unhappy. When if we will
bring ourselves to feel—“It is the will of
my Father; He has commanded; it is His to
command, mine to obey;” joy will spring up
in our hearts, and we will be cheerful and
happy. This is my experience.
						 A MOTHER.


[For the EXPONENT.
	CO-OPERATIVE AMENDMENTS.
				 —
	How absurd and ridiculous the ideas
advanced by some of our Woman Suffragists
are! For instance, says one "woman’s-
rights”-man, speaking of woman: "She has
been continually anathematized and tor-
mented with the idea that she is the ‘weaker
vessel.’ Her father, brother and husband
have always told her that her mind was
weak and small, and that she could not com-
prehend great things, nor do great works.
Sometimes, her mother and sister are joined
in this wholesale slander of the female mind.
When a little girl she has been paralyzed
with the thought of her inferiority. *  *  * 
How could woman be anything with the
whole world against her?” As though
woman has never been anything! Woman
is to a large extent responsible for this “per-
secution" and “oppression" to which she
has been subjected by men. She has been
too willing to have something to lean on,
some one to look up to, and to do the conse-
quential parts in providing for herself and
her offspring. But has she been nothing


and done nothing for the world? Is it
nothing to have been the-mother of all the
great men and women? I think it is con-
siderable in woman’s favor. And I also
think that it is right for her to improve and
elevate her condition in every legitimate
way, and thus become a nobler and wiser
mother of purer and better beings. In
making these amendments, every sensible
and good man will, I am convinced, assist
and encourage her, and glory in her earnest
and spirited resolutions. There is no true
man but what has the highest appreciation
of her merits or .that ..would not sacrifice
more for her welfare and happiness than he
would for his own.
								INEZ.


﻿For the EXPONENT
	WHY BLAME THE GIRLS?
			  —
	Is it fair to accuse all young ladies with
the follies and nonsense of indulging in the
false pride and vain imaginings which
characterize the great portion of society at
the present day, and still hourly increase?
A short time since, a worthy gentleman
remarked something like the following:—
“If a young man would like to marry a girl,
he dare not tell her of it; for when a girl
gets married now-a-days, she wants a piano,
a Brussels carpet and furniture to match, a
horse and buggy, thinks she must dress in
the nicest style, and have two hired girls to
wait on her." These suggestions are closely
applicable to a large majority of our girls, it
is true, but there are exceptions; and what
is more, the other side of the picture was
not drawn. Yet no one who is at all
acquainted with the present condition of
society, could justly condemn the following
portrait as being incorrect, so far as it ex-
tends:  If a young lady is asked to marry,
she dare not say “yes,” for a young man
now-a-days can scarcely be met in any-
position in society but he has a cigar in his
mouth, his face is flushed with wine, and to
judge from his manner of deportment, he
considers himself superior in every sense of
the word to most people, and would not
marry a girl whom he thought inferior in
the least respect to any one but himself.
What reasonable girl feels herself capable of
filling the position of wife satisfactorily to
such a being, even if she did not shrink
from his habits of intemperance? Let the
young men reform and see if the girls will
not be on hand to assist them.
							A GIRL.


It doesn’t matter how watchful and vigi-
lant a girl is; if a rude fellow kisses her, it
is ten to one he will do it right under her
nose.
	To accept the statements of the partisan
press on both sides, the two very worst men
in the United States are the candidates for
the Presidency. Such is partisan politics.
	Some people are as careful of their troubles
as mothers are of their babes; they cuddle
them, and hug them, and cry over them,
and fly into a passion with you if you go
to take them away from them; they want
you to fret with them, to help them to be-	
lieve that they have been worse treated
than anybody else. If they could, they
would have a picture of their grief in a
gold frame hung over the mantle-shelf for 
everybody to look at. And their grief makes
them ordinarily selfish—they think more of
their little grief in the basket and in the
cradle than they do of all the world besides
and they say you are hard-hearted if you
“dont fret.” “Ah! you don’t understand
me—you can’t enter into my trial!”


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿TO STRANGER FRIENDS. 
			    —
Stranger Sisters, kindly caring,
For a lone one in distress,
Neither time nor int’rest sparing
that would serve to soothe and bless;
May the light of Heaven never
Cease across your path to shine;
But the grace of God, forever,
Yield you strength and power divine.

Have ye wand’ring sons or brothers?
May they meet with friends as true,
Finding at the hands of others
That which ours receive from you. 
While this prayer is but a token
Relevant, deep thanks accord,
Other thoughts must rest unspoken
With the future and the Lord.
							L. L,


THE SEX.
			      —
	The “World” speaks of that “maudlin and
murderous hag, Mrs. Fair.”
	Rosa Bonheur’s last painting is a tiger
fighting a hyena. It is said to be her master-
piece.
	At Mankato, Minn. a woman was accused
of throwing a baby into the river. The
river was raked by an incensed community
and a dead cat brought to light.
	A woman in Fond du Lac, Wisconsin, has
sent the saloon keepers there, a bill of $200,
being the amount legally due her for a
pounding inflicted by her husband.
	An exchange says that Miss Sabone
Kornell, of Little Camplon, R. I., is en route
to Omaha on foot. She had arrived at Terre
Haute, Ind., at last accounts, and was then
healthy and hopeful.
	A Boston lady, but eighteen years old,
named Avonia Bonney, made a highly suc-
cessful debut at the Balbo Theatre, Turin,
a few weeks ago. She appeared in a new
opera by Joseph Boszelli.
	The New York “Tribune” is very ungal-
lant and alludes exultingly to the discomfiture
of mothers and daughters who have been
“angling” this Summer at the various water-
ing-places with such poor success. Their
failure is accounted for by the somewhat
worn theory that the poor but honest young
men are frightened away by the display of
extravagance on the part of the frivolous
young ladies.
	A little girl in a New York Orphan
Asylum quarreled with another girl and
scratched her face. For this she was
punished and required to learn and repeat a
verse from the Bible, being allowed her own
selection. She chose the first verse of Psalm
44, which runs as follows: “Blessed be the
Lord my strength which teacheth my hands
to war.and my fingers to fight.
	Molly Strong, of Newton, Michigan,
dug a well forty feet deep last summer, and
received one hundred and thirty dollars
from her father therefor. Then a lot of fel-
lows went fooling around after Molly, and
wanted to marry her; but she pensively
kicked them outdoors, with the remark
that she “wasn’t going to take fellers who
wanted her just because they found out she
could work like a horse.”
	The Princess Salm-Salm has entered a
Catholic convent at Innspruck in the Tyrol.
The Princess is an American by birth, a
Miss Agnes Leclerq, of Baltimore, and her
romantic adventures in Mexico, six years
ago, filled the newspapers of the time. She
saved her husband’s life in Mexico, and got
him restored to his military rank in Ger-
many. He was killed at Gravelotte, at the
head of his regiment, She was at one time
a circus rider, and her career altogether has
been filled with adventure.


HUMOR.

	One thing that can always be found—
fault.
	Something you can always borrow—
trouble.
	Vested interests—Money in the waist-
coat pocket.
	What is taken from you before you get
it? Your photograph.
	Who pays the highest price for a home?
The woman who marries for one.
	If you court a young woman, and you are
won and she is won, you will both be one.
	Why is the alphabet like cutting the first
teeth?—because it is taught yer (torture)
when young.
	“You seem to walk more erect than usual,
my friend.” “Yes, I have been straightened
by circumstances.”
	The Freeport "Journal" says: Pairs are
abundant in this place and front gates are
loaded down with them on fine evenings.
	The man whose hair turned white in a
single night is surpassed by the New York
girl who lost hers completely off in a single
dance.
	“Mamma, I don’t see how Satan turned
out to be such a bad fellow; there wasn’t
any devil to put him up to it.”
	Lord Brougham once said that no man
had any right to a nervous system who does
not possess two thousand a year.
	A poor young man remarks that the only
advice he gets from capitalists is to “live
within his income,” whereas the difficulty
he experiences is to live without an income.
	It is stated that a boy was lynched while
endeavoring to sell the passengers on one of
the blockaded Pacific Railroad trains, copies
of the ballad of “Beautiful Snow.”
	“Ah, parson, I wish I could take my gold
with me,” said rich old Deacon Swipes. “It
might melt if you did,” quietly replied the
parson.
	Epitaph on Mrs. Jones:—“She was first
cousin to the Earl of Cork; she was bland,
affable, religious; she painted in water-
colors; and of such is the kingdom of
“Heaven.”
	An inquiring man thrust his fingers into
a horse’s mouth to see how many teeth he
had. The horse closed his mouth to see how
many fingers the man had. The curiosity
of each was fully satisfied.
	What is fashion, Annie? Fashion is
something that causes Betsy, who goes bare-
headed all the week, when the sun is
shining, to wear gloves and carry a parasol
on Sunday, when it is cloudy.
	A little boy was sent to a store for some
eggs. Before reaching home he dropped
them. In answer to his mother, who asked,
“Did you break any?” he replied, “No, I
didn’t break any, but the shells came off
some of them.”
	A Sunday-school teacher who had almost 
become discouraged over the listlessness of
her class, at last felt rewarded by an inter-
esting look from a little girl. The reward
was lost when the little creature touched a
bracelet on her arm and asked, “Teacher,
are them threaded on ’lastic?”
	There is a man in Portland, Maine, who
supports his family in a handsome style by
simply tying an able-bodied cat by the tail
to a clothes-line every night, and then going
out in the morning to collect the soap, shav.
ing-cups, brushes, etc., thrown into the
yard by angry boarders in adjoining houses.


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
				 —
	Ohio produced 9,072,063 pounds of cheese
last year.
	Miss Gilbert is trying to put a library in
the St. Louis jail.
	A Church organ atSchuylerville, New
York, is run by water.
	Women fill the clerkships in the Stock-
holm banks. They are not given to embez-
zlements.
	Mrs. Louisa W. Foss is a new candidate
for the honors of the elocutionary rostrum.
	Metz has lost a third of its population by
emigration since the German occupation.
	Thomas Ansbro has been convicted of
libel against Edith O’Gorman “the escaped
nun.”
	The Marquis of Bute has sent a collection
of minerals from Wales for the American
Exhibition in 1876.
	The widow of Mark Lemon, late editor of
“Punch,” has received a pension of L100
from the British Government.
	The Duke of Genoa, nephew of the King
of Italy, is about to undertake a voyage
around the world.
	San Francisco rejoices in a railroad ring,
a mining ring, a wheat ring, a whisky ring
and a real estate ring.
	A ring of land grabbers at East Saginaw
expects to make $2,000,000 in stealing the
lands of tbe Chippewa Indians.
	Anna Dickinson has two new lectures
ready, one on the woman question, the other
on the labor question.
	Hanna Matilda Dodd, deceased, gives
$1,000 to be expended in medals for the
best girl graduates of the Boston High
School.
	There is another case for Darwin near
Vancouver. A man residing near there
recently broke a fresh egg, and found there-
in a snake three inches long.
	Since the great fire six hundred and
thirty-seven buildings have been erected in
Chicago, at an estimated cost of $40,000,000.
One looking upon the re-built city can
scarcely trace the effects of the fire.
	The divorces granted last year in Con-
necticut numbered 409, being one more
than the previous year; while the marriages
numbered 26 less than the year previous
In more than two-thirds of the cases the
wife was the petitioner.
	An immense Bunsen battery is being
constructed at the Holy Cross College, Wor-
cester, Mass., which when complete, will
be powerful enough to illuminate the whole
with the electric light.
	Some figures given by the Boston “Adver-
tiser” show the profits of the manufacturers of
that State to be $102,000,000 in 1870, or
about $68 per capita to the population. The
wages paid amounted to $18,000,000 which
to those employed in the factories, was equiv-
alent to $421 each.


﻿A ROMANTIC story is told of how, during
the bombardment of Paris, a gallant French
captain, Legrand by name, undertook to bear
dispatches into the besieged city, by swim-
ming across the Seine; how he failed in his
mission, his fate remaining a mystery for
many months; and how, very recently,
there was fished up from the bottom of the
river, a skeleton, accoutred in uniform, and
clutching a water-proof satchel containing
the missing dispatches. A funeral with
military honors, and an imposing monument
in Pere-la-Chaise follow as things of course.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿WOMAN IN ART.
			   ——
	Harriet Hosmer, of Watertown, Mass., is
the most widely known among the women
sculptors at Rome. An ideal head called
“Hesper,” daguerreotypes of which were
taken to Gibson, the English sculptor at
Rome, obtained that artist’s consent for the
lady to become his pupil. Among her art-
studies were copies of the “Venus of Milo,”
the “Cupid of Praxiteles,” and the “Tasso”
of the British Museum. Her first original
attempt was a head of “Daphne,” then of
“Medusa.” Two replicas to the “Daphne”
were subsequently ordered. Her next de-
sign was the shepherd wife whom Paris
deserted for Helen. In 1856 she executed
a statue of “Puck,” three copies of which
are in noble collections in England. The
following year she made a statue of
“Beatrice Cenci Asleep in her Cell,” and the
design of a monument erected in one of the
churches in memory of a beautiful daughter
of Madame Falconet, an English Catholic
lady resident of Rome. “Zenobia,” a colossal
work, architectural in style, with massive
and highly finished drapery, was sent to the
United States for exhibition, and received
the praise of art connoisseurs. In the Paris
Exposition of 1867, she exhibited the
“Sleeping Faun,” and the Legislature of
Missouri gave her a commission to execute
a statue of Thomas H. Benton.
	Miss Emma Stebbins, of New York, like
Miss Hosmer, has long been a resident at
Rome, devoted to the plastic art. She ex-
ecuted a statue of Columbus, which has
found many admirers. One of the most
pleasing and originial specimens of her skill
is a figure of “Joseph,” represented in boy-
hood. A statuesque adornment for a
fountain commissioned for the Central Park
is a representation of the “Angel of the
Waters”—the miracle beside the pool of
Bethesda.
	Miss Foley, of Vermont, has achieved an
enviable reputation by success in relievos.
Among her celebrated works is a bas relief
of Longfellow, a head of William Cullen
Bryant, and one of Charles Sumner.
	Edmonia Lewis, a dusky maiden of negro
and Indian parentage, while in her teens
abandoned herself to sculpture. During the
Union war she exhibited a bust of Colonel
Shaw, the fair-haired hero and martyr to
the cause of her race, at the Soldiers’ Relief
Fair in Boston. Since then she has modeled
the “Freedwoman on First Hearing of her
Liberty” and “Hager in the Wilderness,”
and has excited much interest among the
Roman studios during her residence in
Italy.
	 Mrs. Ames, wife of the eminent portrait
painter, has executed from memory a suc-
cessful portrait of Lincoln, in marble.
	Miss Anne Whitney, of Boston, has been
brought into favorable notice by her original
statues of “Godiva” and “Africa.”
	Mrs. Freedman, wife of the well known
American painter, J. E. Freedman, during
a long residence in Italy achieved great
fame by the delicacy and finished modeling
of several marble portraits and an elaborately
sculptured vase, carved in alto-relievo.—
N. Y. “Star.”


﻿LADIES OF OLDEN TIME.

	History and tradition offer many types of
beautiful womanhood. The grace and love-
liness of Greece, the more heroic grandeur
of old Rome, the social and personal equality,
so to speak, of the white-armed Scandin-
avian women, the domestic activities and
courtly ladyhood of the medieval “chate-
laine,” all these, and many more, are as


pictures, wherein we see represented the
ideal woman, each perfect after its kind,
and all beautiful. In saying this, we must
of course, allow for the difference of custom
between then and now, and not judge accord-
ing to the religion of our nineteenth century
drawing room. Now the rule is for men to
pay attention to women, and to prevent their
doing the most trifling act for themselves,
and “serving” is the last thing our ladies
think of; then—though this “then” is very
wide, both in race and chronology—the
distinctive quality of womanhood was her
service, and the more lovely and perfect the
lady, no matter what her nation or era, the
more thoroughly she performed the assigned
functions of her state, and the more inex-
tricable her ladyhood was connected with
such performance. Queens and princesses
bound up the wounds of, and poured out
wine for their lords and knights; and in
that pretty story of “Walthere and the
Lady Hildegund,” Walthere is represented
as falling in love with the lady while she is
serving him with wine. Also, in the story
of “Beowulf,” Hrthogar’s queen, the noble
Wealtheow, advanced from her seat to bear
the cup to the warriors. First she offered it
to the king, her husband, and greetingly
“bade him to be blithe at the bear-drink-
ing, he who was dear to his people.” Then
she went round the hall, offering the cup,
and distributing gifts to each. At length
the “ring-adorned queen, exalted in mind,”
bore the mead-cup to Beowulf himself, and
“sagacious in words,” addressed the hero
in flattering terms. The same kind of
thing prevails in some of the more northern
parts of Europe to this day; and to our mind
the picture of our young daughters of the
house themselves serving the guests—not
as inferiors, be it understood, but as the dis-
pensers of hospitality and welcome—is a far
more lovely manifestation than the silent
automatic attendance of half a dozen men
in livery, who have reduced “waiting at
table” to a science, wherein he is the
greatest proficient who is the most mechani-
cal and the least human. — “Chimney
Corner.”


﻿A YOUNG LADY’S THOUGHTS.
					—
	The following is an extract from a private
letter, written by one young lady to another,
and courteously handed to us for publication:
							Nov. 1, 1872.
	You have been in my thoughts all the
morning, and I have been wondering
whether you would think of a year ago to-
day, or not. It was just another such a
morning as this—bright and clear and sunny
and beautiful. I called on you at an early
hour that morning—I recollect the date
because I have rarely in my life enjoyed
any little circumstance as I did that health-
ful, happy walk. I wanted you to go for a
run on the hill with me, but—let me tell
you how you were employed when I entered
the house:	Mixing bread and	making
preparations to wash. You couldn’t go, so
excused yourself. You know I was just
home after a month’s visit to——, and I
could not help contrasting your position
with that of some of our pretty belle butter-
tlies, who, I knew, were not out of bed by
that hour, and who, when they did rise
would spend all that delightful morning
shut in from the beautiful, glorious sunshine,
dressing and powdering, preparing to receive
or make fashionable calls. How I appre-
ciated you and your real worth, then! There,
too, was your big brother in his little shop,
measuring and planing and hammering
away, and looking so good natured and con-
tented, that I thought, at the moment, any


one who would not put up such an honest
tradesman, in any case of importance,
against a dozen of worthless, polished city
puffs, who get their living—no matter how,
would be badly cheated.
	How thankful we should be, my dear
friend, that our mothers have been wise
enough to show us the importance and the
dignity of labor! They have not taught us,
either, that physical strength, its culture
and development, is all that is worthy of
attention, as some coarse-minded people
seem to believe. But that the powers of
thought and reason, the immortal mind,
must also be carefully regarded; to cultivate
order, polite manners without affectation,
neatness, sweet temper, a love for the
beautiful in nature and in art, and respect
and reverence for all things pure and good.
Your mother and my mother have taught
us these principles, and if we do not employ
and enjoy them, the fault is ours and not
theirs.
	There are few mothers like ours. God
bless them and bless the poor, old world with
many such in future. 	  *	  *
								JULIA.


﻿Jumping from trains when in motion has
been made a penal offence in England.
	A remarkable old English gentleman
is missing, and is so particularly described
in an advertisement in the London Times
that, if he be found, the finders will be at
no loss to recognize him. He wears “a
brown wig and a green shade over one eye.”
He has no hair on his face, but he carries a
deep red scar over his left eyebrow, has
“large yellow teeth dressed in black with a
grey overcoat.” Now a man with a wig
over his eye and an overcoat on his teeth
ought to be identified with a positive assur-
ance wherever he may turn up, especially if
he spends freely and gives checks on Lon-
don banks, as this old-gentleman-errant is
said to do.—New York Tribune.

	An English journal announces an im-
provement in railway signalling, put into
operation at Birmingham, which is on the
London and Northwestern Railroad. It says
that “by a new arrangement of signalling
there will be no more screeching from en-
gines within the station, except for shunt-
ing or pilot engines. From all the departure
platforms telegraph wires have been laid
down communicating with the pointsmen’s
signal boxes at each end of the station.
When a train is ready to start the superin-
tendent unlocks the signalling-box, and sig-
nals in what direction the train is going.
On receiving the signal that a train is ready
to start, the pointsman shows the signal
“line clear,” and the driver starts his train
without any preparatory whistling.”
	An Indiana editor lays down his shears
for a few minutes to write a double-leaded
editorial, in which he plaintively remarks:
“We are the recipient of half a peck of nice
onions, two watermelons and a bottle of
ginger-beer from one of our subscribers.
The gifts were like the shadow of a rock in
a weary land. We are glad some one re-
membered us in the midst of our labors and
cares, and evinced that remembrance in so
delicate a manner. We dote on onions and
love melons dearly; and so long as the fra-
grance of the former and the gripes of the
latter linger about us, we shall hold the kind
donor in affectionate remembrance. Of gin-
ger-beer we have never been able to speak
enthusiastically, but may say that our chil-
dren enjoyed it greatly; while the empty
bottle added not a little to the effective
force of our office armory.”

			WOMAN’S EXPONENT.
			
		       A UTAH LADIES' JOURNAL.

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.

	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!”

	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of Woman’s Exponent, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leadings topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.

	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In Woman’s Exponent a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.

	Woman’s Exponent will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year, $2.00	1 Copy six months, $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months, 9.00

    No subscription received for less than six months.
    It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

    Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers Woman’s Exponent, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


Woman's Exponent.
Vol. I.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, DECEMBER 1, 1872.


EDITORIAL NOTES.
				—
	ln Burlington, Iowa, the street lights
are extinguished at 10 o'clock. It is pre-
sumable that the people there are temperate
and moral.
	After the horses, first came the
chickens and then the hogs, as sufferers by
the epidemic among the animals first named.
Will man come next?
	The fair famed English poetess, Eliza
Cook, is reported ill beyond recovery. Does
she still cling to the “Old Armed Chair” of
which she has so sweetly sung?
	From present indications the coming
winter will be a hard one. Some time ago
work on the Nothern Pacific R. R,. had to be
suspended in consequnce of the severity of
the weather.
	Pioche is anxious for the railroad track
to be extended form the terminus of the
Utah Southern to Bullionville, that the
coal mines of Kanarra may be bound to the
silver mines of Pioche
	A Pennsylvania woman lately sold her
two weeks’ old baby for $10, but wanted it
back soon after, because another party had
offered $25 for it. Some people have
curious ways of making money.
	The last present ever received by Abra-
ham Lincoln, an elegant family Bible given
him by the colored people of the South, has
just been presented by Mrs. Lincoln to Rev.
Dr. Miner of Belvedere, Illinois.
	Fires and storms on land and sea, inun-
dations and general devastation, reports
of which daily crowd the telepraphic columns
of the newspapers, seem indicative of a
universal crisis in some direction, at no
distant period.
	A friend announces that the whole world
is apparently on the point of committing
suicide, but what with its present intemper-
ance, insubordination, insolvency and gen-
eral inconsistencies it might philosophically
be considered as being already in the very
act.
	In Marseilles, France, a jury recently
acquitted a beautiful young brunette for the
crime of assassinating her lover for refusing
to purchase a gold watch to which she took
a fancy. Another decided case ol insanity;
for if the murderess was “compos mentis”
the jury certainly not.
	Boston’s relief fund, for the sufferers
by its great fire, has reached about two
hundred thousand dollars. Wealth has not
made the Bostonian heart altogether hard,
nor frozen up its fountains of charity; or
else the great conflagration has softened
the one and thawed out the other.
	Scotland seems to be highly favored
with visits from distinguished people this
year. The Queen and the Prince and
Princess of Wales are there; The French
Empress and the Prince Imperial but lately
left; Gladstone and John Bright have gone
there; Stanley has been there; and Lowell
is somewhere in that part of the Globe.
	Cooper told of the “last of the Mohicans”
years ago, but it appears that about 100 of
the tribe still survive and have been in-
vested with the rights of citizenship by the
[new column]
Legislature of Connecticut; and that they
are as industrious and well informed as any
of their white neighbors. America may
yet be proud of her aborigines.
	Senator Sumner has returned from
Europe, and on Wednesday last he met
Senator Henry Wilson, the newly elected
Vice-President, in the office of the New
York “Tribune,” the two greeting each
other cordially. Thus the political hatchet,
it appears, is being buried by the “Tri-
bune,” and the rival factions of Republicans.
	Mrs. Greeley, formerly Miss Mary G.
Cheney, whose death lately occurred at the
residence of Alvin Johnson, in New York,
is said to have been a lady of highly culti-
vated intellect, taste and social attainments.
True sympathizers will condole more with
the old Chappaqua Farmer on her loss than
upon any political disappointment which he
has lately sustained.
	Diamonds and other precious stones are
becoming so plenty, or so plentifully talked
of, in this western region, that we do not
wonder at mothers when referring to their
babies, speaking of eyes like diamonds, and
lips like rubies. It is to be feared that some
of the investors in diamond expeditions,
may have reason to look blue as sapphires
before their experience is ended.
	A political crisis in France, more in-
surrection in Spain, Rome threatened with
radical uprisings, trouble in Prussia between
the Emperor and the national Diet; with
every leading nation, including our own,
talking of strengthening its powers of offense
and defense. These are the evidences of
“peace” which the civilized world presents,
and of which there has lately been so much
vaunting.
	On Wednesday, Nov. 20, Boston sufferd
loss by another large fire, although it was
small compared with the great conflagration
which so short a time before had preceded
it. The total loss reached nearly $350,000;
and the large printing establishment of
Rand & Avery was destroyed, with the
December number of “Old and New,” one
issue of “Littell’s Living Age” and other
periodicals.
	A Sensational New York paper reported
recently that Greeley was insane, and the
story was telegraphed over the country. If
Greeley is crazy, who in New York is wise?
He suffered deeply in feelings and endured
much loss of sleep during the last illness
and death of his beloved wife—prostrating
him for a time—but he is likely to live and
prove himself much more sane than those
who gave currency to such a tale.
	The Woodhull and Claflin sisters have
been saying and publishing fearful things
about Henry Ward Beecher, Mrs. Theodore
Tilton, and New York “society” generally.
They were arrested, lodged in jail on the
charge of publishing “obscene literature,”
and also held on a charge of libel against one
Challis, a New York banker or broker. If
one tenth part of what they charge—and
they defend much of it—be true, then the
corruption of “Gotham” is something terri-
ble to think of.
	Miss Kate Janaway is the daughter of
an ex-Mayor of Memphis, Tennessee, but
she has learned that woman’s accomplish-
[new column]
ments should include more than a little
piano and a large amount of fashion and
novel reading. She caried off premiums at
the late Agricultural Fair for roast beef,
plum pudding, and her style of preparing a
variety of vegetables; enough to give her
the claim to be considered the best cook in
Tennessee. And she received twenty-five
offers of marriage on the spot! How much of
romance, and how much of prosaic Common
Sense were in those offers?
	Henry Berge, “the guardian angel and
the patron saint of the bestial race,” stand-
ing at the junction of Third and Fourth
avenues, at the Cooper Institute, New York,
during the horse distemper, displayed his
professional shield, and in the name of the
august Society for the Prevention of Cruelty
to Animals, ordered men to cease driving
their gasping horses. And being reinforced
by the police he carried the day. Good for
Henry. But what kind of beings must
they have been who had to be compelled to
unhiteh their sick and panting beasts?
	We clip the following from the Wash-
ington “Star” of November 20, where it
appeared under the heading “Hero and
Heroine,” without further comment: “Syl-
vester Frambes, of Tippecanoe, Spencer
county, Ind., was drawn into a sugar crusher
and had his arm crushed to tbe shoulder one
day last week. His wife came to his assist-
ance, and when she had released him by
backing the horse she fainted away. With
great nerve and endurance he carried her to
the house on his uninjured arm, and re-
stored her to consciousness, when she went
for a surgeon, who disjointed the arm at
the shoulder. He will probably recover.”
	Vermont does not believe that capital
punishment is “legal murder,” as some “ad-
vanced minds” term it. The Legislature
of that State, by a large majority, has re-
fused to abolish the death penality. That
whiich is needed to repress alarming and
heinous crimes throughout the country, is
not abolishing of capital punishment, nor
the passage of severer statutes; but the due
honoring of wise and just statutes and their
rigid enforcement. But while men can kill
with impunity, and gloat over their deeds
of blood, and can be rescued from the
clutches of the law,unharmed and un-
punished, by influence or money, what will
bad men care whether such a penalty
remains upon the statute books of States or
is formally abolished.
	Another paragraph on this page refers
to the story that Horace Greeley had gone
insane, refusing to credit it, and looking
forward to a length of life for him. Later
reports confirm the belief in his sanity, but
destroy the hope of his prolonged life. The
great editor is gone. He died on Friday
evening, 29th ult., at ten minutes to seven
o’clock, nearly sixty-two years of age. The
cause of his death was virtually sleepless-
ness. He could not sleep, after the exertion
and exhaustion consequent upon the death
of his wife, and the strain upon him during
the Presidential campaign; and in about four
weeks after she was carried to the grave,
his spirit passed from earth, the sleepless-
ness having turned to inflamation of the
brain. And so another great and wise man
has fallen!


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿	Written for the Exponent.
			LONGINGS.

		All night! All dark!
		So drear and sad!
		No light! No little spark
		To make me glad;
  No loving arms to twine around me now;
  No tender kiss to soothe my aching brow.

		Weary! Weary!
		Heaven is full of rest.
		Earth seem so dreary,
		Life so poor at best—
  And yet we all must take an active part,
  Nor sink beneath the burdens of the heart.

		Hopeless and sad
		Our way may be—
		Lord make us glad
		To worship Thee.
  Oh! lead our footsteps in some brighter path;
  Teach man to him Thou givest all he hath.
		
		Hope sends her light,
		Like some bright star;
		And lends us sight
		To joys afar;
  That we may see wisdom in Thy ways,
  Nor spend, in vain repining, all our days.
PROVO.							M. J. T.


﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.

	Sister Amelia M. Frodsham writes from
Ogden under date Nov. 22:
	We have been having severely cold
weather, still a few of the sisters met in the
capacity of a meeting on the coldest day we
have had There is a lively interest taken
in the Society, and great desire to do good
is manifested in caring for the poor. This
work is one of grave interest to me, and I
feel that much good may be accomplished
through the Exponent. The sisters need
not be idle, and think they have no mission
to perform, for even that of wife and mother
is, to me, a vast and noble one. Yet in
connection with these cares, we can find
time to speak words of kindness and com-
fort to those who are cast down, administer
to the wants of the poor, and visit the sick.
My prayer is that God will open the hearts
of those who have means to give to those who
have not, and both will receive a blessing.
I believe when people do good deeds their
own consciences reward them for it.

	On Saturday, Nov. 23, at the general Re-
trenchment Meeting, which is held Semi-
monthly in the 14th Ward Assembly
Rooms, President Mrs. Isabella Horn ex-
pressed a great desire to witness a revival
among the sisters; that they might become
more united in faith, and also in the extend-
ing and promulgating of every wise and
beneficial principle. And for this cause, she
hoped that the meetings would be well
attended during the coming winter. Com-
ing together and holding converse in an
intelligent manner, upon topics of interest,
serves to enlighten, refine and elevate the
mental faculties; and the more there are
who associate thus together, the broader will
be the current of intellectual improvement.
The house could be made warm and com-
fortable, would accommodate a goodly
number, and she desired to see it well filled
every meeting day. A number of the sisters
made short but pithy remarks.
	Physiology was chosen for the subject to
be considered at the next meeting.

				Brigham City, Nov. 20. 1872,
Editor Woman's Exponent.—
	At a recent meeting of our society a
motion to have the word “Female” dropped
from the name, according to sister Eliza R.
Snow’s suggestion, was carred by a unani-
mous vote.	
				Harriet A. Snow. President.
[next column]
				Kamas Prairie, Nov. 24, 1872.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	At a meetiug held iu this place, Nov.
15th, Sister Ann Harder presiding, it was
moved, seconded and carried unanimously
that the word “Female” be dropped from
the name of the Society, in accordance with
the suggestion of Sister Eliza R. Snow and
others of our faithful sisters in the Relief
Societies. Some satisfaction was expressed
at the change, for we had long considered
the canceled word unnecessary to the signi-
fication of the appellation. The Exponent
was warmly advocated by the President,
and several of the sisters. We felt to
endorse the sentiment so ably expressed on
the last page of your valuable publication:
“It is better to represent ourselves than to
be misrepresented by others.” We have
been misrepresented for so long a time that
some may consider it not worth our while to
speak for ourselves, and in defense of the
truth at this late hour. But we may at least
assert our right to utter our own opinions,
belief and knowledge. For since the
glorious Gospel of the Son of God has made
us free, we are free indeed, and seek to
honor the God whom we revere, and trust
in Him who is the Rock of our Salvation.
				Mrs. Phebe Young,
						Ass’t Secretary.

			Alpine City, Nov. 21st, 1872.
Editor Exponent:—At our last meeting a
vote was carried unanimously in favor of
dispensing with the word “Female” as an
unnecessary prefix to the name of the
Society. The sisters feel well and are on
hand to do their duty as Saints.
	We have a hundred-dollar share in the
woolen factory at Provo, a twenty-five
dollar share in our Co-operative store, have
donated twenty-five dollars to help gather
the Saints, and made other collections to be
used for the benefit of the poor, to the
amount of sixty dollars. We try to give,
and take, lessons of economy.
	I have been a member of this church for
thirty years, and have never seen a time
when the truths of the Gospel were so en-
couraging as at present. The Lord said he
would choose the weak things of this world
to confound the wise. This is being fulfilled;
and the wisdom of God is greater than that
of man.
					Margaret McCullough.

				Santaquin, Nov. 19th, 1872.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	I forward a brief report to you, of our last
monthly meeting, held on the 7th inst., at
which a vote was taken to omit the word
“Female” from the name of the Society, as
suggested, which was unanimous. Presi-
dent Mrs. E. J. Stickney, who had just
returned from a visit to Salt Lake City,
made some excellent and very cheering
remarks. Several highly commendable
articles were read from the Exponent,
which we value very much; and meeting
adjourned until the first Monday in Decem-
ber. Benediction by Counselor Mrs. Green-
halch,
				Julia Wilson, Secretary.


﻿The will of the late Mrs. Arabella Rice,
of Portsmouth, N. H., who is said to have
been the wealthiest lady in the State, dis-
poses of about $200,000 in legacies; among
those of a public nature being $30,000 to the
town of Kittery for a public library, $20,000
to the State Asylum for the insane, and
$3,000 each, to the Portsmouth Atheneum
and Unitarian Church.
	Seventy thousand Angora goats leap
from precipice to precipice in California.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
		
	One good strong brain at the helm of the
family craft is worth several pairs of stout
hands. As everything aboard ship depends
upon the skill with which the rudder hands
are made to do their duty, so everything in
the household depends on the outlining of
the year’s, the week’s, or the day’s work.
If the mistress leaves matters to drift as it
happens, or trusts the management of her
affairs to unskilled or unfaithful hands, she
will soon find herself in the midst of
inextricable confusion. We cannot but
think that more than half the blame mis-
tresses lay upon their servants for work ill
done or undone belongs at their own doors.
The person who voluntarily takes and holds
a subordinate position virtually acknowl-
edges her inability to setup for herself; she
confesses by so doing that she needs a
mistress, and that her duties may be pre-
scribed by another. When fully instructed
in the routine she is to follow, she may be
left to herself, in a measure; but rare is that
servant who does not need at times the
sympathy, the thoughtfulness, the aid of
her mistress in accomplishing the work
expected of her. Our great merchants, our
leading bankers, our prominent railroad
men do not trust the management of their
business to subordinates. They visit every
important post in person, and not by proxy,
and know by ocular inspection that every-
thing is right. If one needs to engineer her
way in household matters when she can
hire muscle to carry out her plans, much
more is brain-work necessary when she has
nothing but her own hands to depend upon.
It is worth while in such a case to study out
the best plan for the week’s campaign, to
decide upon what first shall be done and
what next, to arrange the points of attack
in the most skillful and felicitous manner,
and with reference to the time and the
power that may be devoted to accomplish
the desired ends. By this means the house-
keeper will know exactly what she is doing,
how her work progresses, and be able to
take advantage of whatever may turn up
favorable to her wishes. Most of us can call
to mind women of feeble health who accom-
plish marvels in the way of work, simply by
their skill in planning. In this men may help
their wives very much if they will. The
unbiased judgment of a friend is often of the
greatest value to one fretted and wearied by
complications and perplexities in his affairs.
The man coming in from the great world
into the little world of domestic life can
often tell at a glance at what point friction
is greatest, exactly where the fulcrum shall
be placed so that with the lever in hand the
weight to be moved shall be lifted. Where
the wife is wanting in the ability to map
out her line of operations, and the husband
possesses it, he should use this talent for
the best good of both parties. It matters
not which half of the dual unit stands at the
wheel, if the course of the ship is ever
toward the desired haven, and away from
rocks and shoals.—“New York Tribune.”
	White Cake.—One cup of butter, two of
sugar, one of milk, four and a half of flour,
four eggs, one and a half teaspoonful of
cream of tartar, half do. of soda.
	Soft Gingerbread.—One cup of me-
lasses, one of sour cream, two eggs, salt,
ginger, one teaspoonful of soda; make it
into a thick batter with flour.
	Potatoes.—Always add salt to the water
while potatoes are boiling; boil moderately,
not violently, and let them be only well
covered with water.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿TWO SIDES OF LIFE.
					—
		There is a shady side of life,
		   And a sunny side as well,
		And 'tis for every one to say
		   On which he’d choose to dwell;
		For every one unto himself
		   Commits a grievous sin,
		Who bars the blessed sunshine out,
		   And shuts the shadows in.
		
		The clouds may wear their saddest robes,
		   The sun refuse to smile,
		And sorrow, with her troop of ills,
		   May threaten us the while;
		But still the cheerful herat has power
		   A sunbeam to provide;
		And only those whose souls are dark,
		   Dwell on life’s shady side.


﻿FLOWERS.
				—
	Nothing is so reasonable and cheap as
good manners.
	There is no grace in a benefit that sticks
to the fingers.
	How few the men who dare to be just
what they are.
	No person knows what he would do till
comes the time to decide.
	If you would add to the happiness of
yourself and others, work without fretting.
	Mrs. Partington says she gets up every
morning at the chill carrion of the chande-
lier.
	He who does evil that good may come
pays a toll to the devil to let him into
Heaven.
	Of all earthly music, that which reaches
farthest into heaven is the beating of a
loving heart.
	If men strove as hard for happiness as
for money, manhood would be more glorious
than now.
	Neither death nor life is as serious as
maarriage. Yet nothing is entered into
more thoughtlessly.
	A man that hoards riches and enjoys
them not, is like an ass that carries gold
and eats thistles.
	Good epigrams, like good jokes, never
lose their youth; but when and where they
commence their wanderings, “you nor I nor
no one knows.
	One should not be downcast at failures.
They are often far better for the student
than success. He who goes to school to his
mistakes will always have a good school-
master, and will not be likely to become idle
or conceited.
	“When speaking of the dead,” says Sir
James Puckle, in his “Gray Cap for a Green
Head,” a volume abounding in quaint
similies folded about kernels of good sense
—“When speaking of the dead, so fold up
your discourse that their virtues may be
outwardly shown, while their vices are
wrapped up in silence.” A good way in
which to speak of the living, too.
	“Who says angels must be all young and
splendid? Are there not some comforting
ones, shabby and tender, whose radiance
does not dazzle nor bewilder; whose faces
are worn, perhaps, while their stars shine
with a tender, tremulous light, more sooth-
ing to our aching earth-bound hearts than
the glorious radiance of brighter spirits?”
	The habit of fretting becomes so settled
that it is very difficult to overcome, in every-
thing in life, whether of great or small
importance. It is well worth the effort to
look upon things in a cheerful light for
personal comfort and the happiness of those
around us. A hornet’s nest is a dangerous
and disagreeable thing to stir up, but not
worse than the disposition of some fretting
men and women.—“Elm Orlou,”


[For the Exponent.
			STANDING ALONE.

	Down he goes! And, Oh! what a thump
on that precious head! It must be bathed
in cold water and camphor. And tender,
pitying expressions must be uttered to sooth
the little wounded heart—which is as nearly
broken by having made this first grand
attempt to “do something,” and met with
so sudden and unexpected a failure, in the
way of a fall, fright and injury, as it ever
will be by heavier falls in future life. All
the laughter and innocent joyousness which
illumed the baby face a moment before,
have disappeared, and tears of anguish and
cries of dispondency and pain, tell how
accutely the disappointment is felt by the
enterprising young genius, just beginning
to learn to walk. His hopes are blighted,
his prospects so bedimmed and clouded that
he feels he never can be anything or any-
body in the world, and overwhelmed with
grief and discouragements, he allows his
little head to droop upon a loving neck, and
there wails himself to sleep under the in-
fluence of the sweet lullaby of a sympathiz-
ing voice. After a brief rest, however, he
will awake again, and with renewed anima-
tion and vigor, stand bravely forth to make
another effort with, perhaps, a little more
caution than before, and, very possibly
better success.
	But other failures lie in wait for him.
Other disappointments will have to be
encountered. The path of life he will find,
is thronged with them. Every little way
there is a hill to be surmounted, a summit
to be gained. And untaught feet are ever
apt to choose the slippery paths, because
they look most smooth and inviting. And
with a fall as sudden as the infant’s first,
are fair, bright prospects often stricken
down, and high hopes blasted by a single
shock, and all the sunshine of a young, glad
life crushed and lost apparently for ever.
Then; should there be no sympathizer near,
no voice to cheer, no practiced hand to
guide, and to lift up and rest the weary head,
and bring new life and animation to the
over burdened, bruised and breaking heart
—how terrible to stand thus all alone. But
it is not, nor never can be so to one who lives
for pure and noble aims. The happiest
heart is heavy in its turn; the strongest,
weak at times, and calls for aid; but there
is a Source of strength for the fainting soul
which never fails, and sunshine for the
gloomiest heart, brighter and more glorious
than the mother’s smile upon her loving,
trusting dear one.				G.


﻿EASE IN SOCIETY.

	“I’d rather thrash in the barn all day,” said
Reuben Riley to his sister, as he adjusted an
uncomfortable collar about his neck, “than
go to the pesky party. I never know what
to do with myself, stuck up in the parlor all
the evening. If the fellows would pull
their coats off and go out and chop wood on
a match, there’d be some fun in that.”
	“Well, I hate it as bad as you do, Reu-
ben,” said sister Lucy. “The fact is we
never go nowhere, to see anybody, and no
wonder we feel so awkward when we happen
to stir out.” 
	The remarks of this brother and sister
were but the echoes of the sentiment of
many other farmers’ boys and girls when
invited out to spend a social evening. But
poor Lucy had not hit the true cause of the
difficulty; it was not because they seldom
went any where, but because there was such
a wide difference between their home and
the company’s manners. The true way to


feel at ease in any garb is to wear it often.
If the pleasing garb of good manners is only
put on upon rare occasions, it will never fit
well and seem comfortable.
	Learn to behave properly at home, to
cultivate yourself. Do not sit or stand or
lounge about in an ungainly attitude, but
acquire a manly, erect bearing. I have
never seen such vigorous, hardy manhood
in any class as among cultivated farmers
sons. Let table manners be especially
looked after.
	If you are so unfortunate as to have a
mother who is careless in this regard, you
must do the best you can to remedy the
early defects in your home training. Note
carefully how well-bred people behave, and
do your best to imitate them. It is noble to
be an imitator of all that is good and beauti-
ful. Above all, if you wish to be at home
in society, fill your brain with ideas. Set
your mind at work. Wake it out of the
sluggishness it would naturally sink into if
you were only a plodder and nothing more,
by good stirring thought. Take the news-
papers and read them thoroughly. Know-
ledge is a great power in many more senses
than one. If you go into society with
something in your mind worth talking about,
you will not fail to find listeners who will
treat you with respect; and where you are
well received, you will not fail very soon to
find yourself at ease.—“Country Gentle-
man."


﻿[Written for the Exponent.]
		WHEN TO SPEAK.
			     —
	There is often virtue in remaining silent
when malevolence, jealousy, contempt or
ridicule excites the colloquial powers to
scorn, inconsistency, harshness or derision.
But there are also times when the tame,
taciturnity of which some pseudo-models 
of propriety are so anxious to present a
formula, reaches culpability. And these 
instances are of more frequent occurrence
than is generally admitted or realized.
When we hear the truth assailed falsely, it
is a duty for us to open our mouths and
endeavor to correct the heresy if possible,
by sound, philosophic reasoning. And this
may be done in a frank, easy manner, with-
out extravagance or cause of offence. When
a cheerful and clear mind may infuse a ray
of light into a burdened and beclouded one,
by words of counsel and comfort, it is un-
charitable and ungenerous to preserve
silence. And some gifted, yet over-careful 
people, may have accounts to settle hereafter 
for bridling the tongue too closely; if they
do not allow it to be of use where it might,
but for the mock-modesty of an affectedly
delicate instinct. Meeting a person in the
street, with whom you have a slight acquaint- 
ance, and who is in the least degree worthy
of recognition, and passing without notice,	
shows a lack of sociability which is often
mistaken for superciliousness. It is better
to speak, even should the reflection present
itself that others imagine themselves bet-
ter or wiser than we are. For if we believe
that other people entertain such feelings,
they may be led to credit the same opinion
of us, by our lack of sociability. But should
it be the case that they look upon us as
courtiers because we do not shun them, we
will, at least have the appearance of being
letter bred than they, if for this reason
they always allow us to speak first.


﻿TO ARRIVE at perfection, a man must have
very sincere friends or inveterate enemies;
because he would be made sensible of his
good or ill conduct either by the censures
of the one or the admonitions of the other.

﻿SPLINTERS.
				—
	Flogging has been abolished in the public
schools in Chicago......Nellie Grant is at
home......A man in Petaluma, California,
has made a discovery of means by which
perpetual motion may be sustained, and
values the discovery at $20,000,000; of
course......The Supreme Court of Maine has
admitted Mrs. C. H. Nash to practice as an
attorney and counselor at-law......Rome is
to have a daily newspaper edited by an
American......A plague is carrying off the
poultry on the banks of the Hudson and
every three hundred inhabitants......The
known coal deposits of the Rocky Mountain
region extend over an area of upwareds of
250,000 square miles......Tennessee has a
colored female preacher......Rosa Bonheur
will visit the United States next year......
Boston public Schools have abolished the
system of offering prize medals......The
Oneida Community abolish marriage alto-
gether and the women of the Community
are said to look listless and spiritless......At
Oatka Falls, Kansas, they have a farmers’
wives’ club......A recent diamond robbery at
the Cape of Good Hope amounted to the
value of L30,000......Mrs, Yelverton is in
Africa with the intention, it is reported, of
joining Dr. Livingstone......The public debt
of Texas is estimated at $23,361,000......A
couple of young ladies at Monticello Avenue,
Jersey City, “laid out” a drunken sailor in
a ditch, the other day, for standing in their
way on the crossing and demanding a kiss
for toll......The “Freeman’s” oath was duly
administered to Mrs. Huntington of Norwalk
Conn., by the Board of Selectmen, but the
registers refused to put her name in the
voting list......The “Young Woman’s Chris-
tian Association,” of Boston, is soliciting
contributions, to aid young women who
have been thrown out of employment by
the great fire......Specie in the Bank of
France increased one million francs in one
week lately......J. Richie, of Vallejo, Cali-
fornia, has this season captured fifteen
sharks......Earthquake shocks in the Owens
river and Haiovi Meadows have been quite
frequent of late......Rich tin deposits are
reported to have been discovered within
twenty miles of San Francisco......The resi-
dences of the late James Gordon Bennett, at
Washington Heights and Fifth Avenue, New
York, are to let for five years......The St,
Louis “Evening Dispatch” has been pur-
chased by Robert Barclay for $120,000......
The Secretary of War compliments Congress
for completeness in last year’s legislation......
Susquehanna rivers......The laws of Kansas
give mothers the management of their
children......Judge Mayer refused to record
the votes of women in Oregon, on election
day......Cinnabar is said to abound in the
vicinity of Harrisburg, Linn Co., Oregon.
......A German woman refused to acknow-
ledge her three children to avoid paying for
their ride on the cars......California has a
hotel composed of ten immense hollow trees;
they are Calaveras Big trees...The figures
of the entire standing army of Europe are
put at 5,164,300......On the splendid marble
monument and tomb of Ferdinand and
Isabella, in the Cathedral at Grenada, the
king and queen are represented as lying
side by side......A new religious sect has
been started in Iowa, the members of which
call themselves Americans......An Italian
journal announces the death, at Placenza,
her native city, of Benedotta Pisaroni, aged
79, a celebrated soprano and contralto
singer of former days......Old woodsmen
mention as a curious fact, that beech and
sycamore trees are never struck by lightning,
though found in close proximity to oak,
[new column]
hickery and other species of trees that are 
often found torn to pieces by the subtle
fluid......A recent female collector who had
been round gathering up contributions for
“Widow Smith,” in San Francisco, was
discovered drunk in a saloon frequented
by thieves, and taken to the “calaboose.”......
San Francisco, it is said, has one widow to
Memories of Dean Alford are being pub-
lished...The great lion tamer, Herr Dries-
bach, is a farmer now, in Iowa......The
Grand Duke Nicholas, of Russia, is on a
pleasure trip to Greece......The Pope has
nominated an editor to the bishopric.......
The monument, of General Lee, in Rich-
mond, Virginia, will soon be completed......
The royal physician, Sir William Gull, lately
received $4,000 for a single visit......The
house of Dante, in Florence, is being sadly
neglected......The grandfather of the late
William H. Seward was a Colonel in the
Revolutionary war......Female voices are
prohibited in the choir under the jurisdic-
tion of Archbishop Manning......St. Louis
is going to erect a statue to the gallant Gen-
eral Nathaniel Lyon.....Mrs. Dr. Groasse,
of Milwaukee, has been sentenced to five
years imprisonment for passing counterfeit
money.
