	Subscribers will please remember that the
present number commences the second half
year of the Exponent, and they who subscribed
for six months will understand that their sub-
scriptions have expired. Ladies in this city,
who have paid for having their papers carried,
and do uot receive them, will please notify the
editor and the matter will be rectified. City
subscribers who have not paid the carrier, can
obtain their papers at Campbell & Patter-
son’s.


﻿WHAT WE MEAN.
				    ——
	Queries are still frequently made concerning
the object of the EXPONENT; whether it is de-
signed to merely vindicate the cause of woman,
her aspirations and prerogatives; or whether its
contents are calculated to be strictly religious,
with no diversity of sentiments and principles;
if it sustains all theories advanced by the Pro-
phets of God in these and former days, or if it
has no other aim than the advocacy of “Plu-
rality of wives.” Notwithstanding the concise
yet full explanation given on these subjects in
the Prospectus to the EXPONENT, which so far
has been published on the last page of each
number of the journal, we willingly comply
with the request to endeavor once more to ex-
plain our intentions definitely and comprehen-
sively.
	The EXPONENT does not claim to support
any one particular cause, practice or opinion.
But being an advocate of everything which is
calculated to interest, instruct, or in any way
benefit woman, its columns are open for the re-
ception of all communications of a suitable
nature, from the pen of any of the ladies of
Utah, whose views and practices it is chiefly
designed to represent.


﻿HOLIDAYS.
					—
	Thursday, the 28th ult., was by Presidential
proclamation set apart as a day of thanksgiving
for the United States. While not designated
as a day for recreation and enjoyment it was
generally so observed, and looked upon as a
“holiday,” in the ordinary acceptance of the
word, which is simply a corruption of “holyday,”
or a day set apart for pious observances. So
such seasons were at first considered in Europe,
and were usually dedicated to some “patron
saint,” some man or some woman whose
accorded piety was supposed to have endowed
them with a special power of blessing, and a
country or a place was dedicated to, and a day set
apart out of respect for each, that their patron-
age might be secured. These were called, “holy
days,” and are now called “holidays,” being
at first a mixture of worship and amusement;
of prayers offered up to the Saint whose day
was being celebrated, and of joyous exercises,
which formed a healthful relaxation from the
cares, and toils of every day life. In some
Roman Catholic countries these days were
multiplied beyond all bounds, encouraging
laziness, intemperance and their kindred vices;
while in these United States, where no religion
is specially recognized, there is a scarcity of
such seasons of relaxation. And while Thanks-
giving Day is now accepted as a time for
general enjoyment and amusement, there is
little doubt that our forefathers, in first deciding
upon such an occasion for a general union of
the whole people in giving thanks for mercies
received, were solemnly sincere, and really did
dedicate the day to returning thanks to the
Almighty, in prayer and praise, for His protect-
ing care and vouchsafed blessings.
	But in this regard, “the spirit of the age,” as
exemplified in the United States, is widely
wrong. It drives hard on the road to money-
making, looks only to present or future fortune,
and disregards the innocent, recreative relaxa-
tion which days devoted to amusement and
enjoyment afford. Various parts of different
countries observe days as fairs, feasts, wakes and
the like, the aim being to bring people together
for general enjoyment, with a suspension of
work and buisness, all of which is conducive to
the general welfare; for the mind needs change
and the body rest, other and more than are ob-
tained by a simple and proper observance of the
Sabbath.
	For these reasons Sunday School festivals
social parties, pic nic parties, theatrical per-
formances, and similar assemblages for enjoy-
ment, have been encouraged in this Territory
by President Young and other leading men, and
for these reasons, we believe a wise and proper
increase of “holidays,” or days expressly
devoted to relaxation and enjoyment, would be
conducive of good to all. Hence we urge upon
parents the wisdom of affording to children a
judicious amount of amusement, and upon all,
the requisite change, variety and recreation
necessary to highten the enjoyment of life and
prolong its existence for good upon the earth.


DEMORALIZING INFLUENCES.
					  —
	Atrocities of late occurrence, in this city,
serve to render the aspect for the return of the
“happy days gone by,” when purity and inno-
cence were in no danger of being contaminated,
or virtue outraged, in no wise auspicious.
There has been a time, since the settlement of
Utah by the “Mormons,” in which the modesty
and chastity of woman were here regardrd as
her chief adornments; and held more sacred by
her honorable and respectful protectors than
every other treasure, even life itself. But base,


villainous men have intruded their persons
and fiendish practices even here, as emissaries
of the evil one, to tempt, decoy, entrap, and, if
possible, to destroy the virtue of the young and
thus prevent the future progress and prosperity
of the pure minded and God-fearing among the
people. And this fact, standing prominently
forward, as it does, should be a powerful admon-
ition to the inexperienced, the young and the
virtuous to be circumspect in their associations,
their coversation and deportment. Not only
should woman seek to maintain her own
crowning glory, purity and integrity, but
mothers and sisters will also do well to wield
the benign influence of which pure and noble
womanhood is ever possessed, energetically and
ceaselessly over the minds of the young of the
opposite sex. There is danger for them as well
as for the beauty and simplicity of maidenhood,
and they cannot be too prudent in respect to
the company they keep and the inflnences
which they allow themselves to be made subject
to.


WOMAN’S' EXPONENT.


﻿HOME AFFAIRS.
				    —
	Work on the Temple is progressing finely
this season. It is really gratifying to take an
occasional close survey of the structure and
workmanship of such a splendid edifice.
	Will the Presidents of the Relief Societies
please notice Sister E. R. Snow’s note at the
head of the editorial in number 18, Oct. 10th, of
the Exponent? Some of the Secretaries are
sending us reports in which no reference is
made to it, or the proposed changing of the
name of the Society.
	Hons. George Q. Cannon and Wm. H.
Hooper left this City for Washington, to repre- 
sent Utah in Congress, on Wednesday, 27th ult.
The latter gentleman is the Territorial Dele-
gate for the present session; and the former is
Delegate elect, to take his sea on the 4th of
March next with the assembling of the lately
elected Congress.
	A brief synopsis of the doings of the general
retrenchment meetings conducted in this city,
will hereafter occupy a space in our Relief
Society Reports, for the benefit of ladies resid-
ing in different parts who have not the privilege
of personal attendance. At the same time, all
who have opportunities, should not fail of being
present at these meetings.
	General Thos. L. Kane, whose eloquent
account of the “Expulsion of the Mormons from
Nauvoo” will be familiar to most of our readers,
has been paying our city a visit with his wife
and two children. The heartfelt prayers of
thousands have often been offered up for him,
for the truthful and noble manner in which he
recorded the acts and sufferings of a devoted
people in their struggles to preserve their
religious liberty. He is on his way to Cali-
fornia, seeking health and recuperation, by
direction of his physician. We regret to learn,
though, that Mrs. Kane contracted a severe
cold on their way this far westward.
	Hon. Jas. G. Blair, of Missouri, Member of
Congress, whose eloquent speech in the Hall of
Representatives, last session, in behalf of the
people, and especially of the women, of Utah,
called forth a letter of acknowledgements from
some of our leading ladies, and a courteous
response from the gentleman, paid a brief visit
to this City lately. If men with such broad,
generous, and liberal views were elected by
every constituency throughout the country,
to represent them in Congress, there would be
no fear of Constitutional provisions being tran-
scended to work persecution, through form of
law, against a people because of their honest
faith and worship.


﻿[Written for the Exponent.
LOVE THY NEIGHBOR AS THYSELF.
			  ——
	Most Christians set this commandment
down as being too difficult to be reduced to
practice—too utterly fanatic for serious con-
sideration; but, if properly understood, in
its true meaning and intent, they would
recognize one of the most beautiful and
heavenly lessons to mankind.
	It does not enjoin that we “prefer” our
neighbor’s interests to our own, nor even
that we should not help ourselves “first”; but
that we should do all for our neighbors that
we could desire to have done for us under
corresponding circumstances; that we should
be as willing to see them prosper as to pros-
per ourselves; as glad to have them take
pleasure as to be happy ourselves; as well
pleased to hear them well spoken of as to
receive praise for our own merits. This
will not be hard to do when we look at it in
a clear light.
	It is an admitted fact, that a nation’s
honor reflects great lustre on the character
of its humblest citizen; and as a nation’s
honor is individual excellence often repeated,
can it be denied that the man or woman
whose walk in life is patriotic, brave, high-
minded or in any other way noble, so far,
loves his neighbor as himself? How beauti-
ful the arrangement, that to serve one’s self
conscientiously and faithfully, is the very
best way fo serve the public! By the person
who lives a pure life, are neighbors
served beyond compute; for in the sight of
the young, the example is more valuable
than untold gold. The one who originates
any wise, order-producing regulation in
one’s own business or household, has con-
ferred a blessing on the race. Those who
build dwellings which are models of beauty,
economy or convenience, confer benefits on
the public. Those who introduce improve-
ments on the farm, in the dairy or any other
place, have truly loved their neighbors.
And those who most conscientiously and
truly serve the public, do most adequately
and effectually serve themselves! One who
zealously guards the welfare of a country
or community builds the strongest defense
around one’s individual welfare. One who
makes great improvements in public works,
greatly increases the value aud security of
private property. Those who most truly
consider the happiness of their fellow
creatures are most certain to win it for
themselves.
	What better legacy can be left to the
world, than highly educated and purely
moral descendants? And how is it possible
to secure, without danger of failure, a good
education to one’s own descendants, save by
founding institutions and systems for the
broad diffu sion of knowledge, and in-
cluding one’s own in the general blessing?
If you create a deep and wide-spread interest
in the cause of mental cultivation, you erect
a barrier between the public and many an
evil; which evils, had they been allowed to
gain influence might have created an
opposing current strong enough to counter-
act all your labors in behalf of your own, if
confined to them.
	It is almost impossible to keep your
children untainted while those of your
neighbors are not circumspect; for if you
undertake to keep them entirely apart, the
chances are that you will fail miserably In
your design. If you should succeed, it would
be a doubtful gain; because children are
reasonable beings and must know a cause for
any admonition, and to be told that others
were not fit to associate with them, would
engender vanity and superciliousness, two
[new column]
evils quite as hurtful and more lasting than
childish rudeness, and ill-breeding.
	Now what is to be done? “Love thy
neighbor as thyself!” Bear with thy neigh-
bors’ children. Not exactly by making
them bosom friends, but by pitying their un-
tutored, uncherished condition. Pefrain
from speaking of them with unmitigated
disgust and scorn. When you can, without
injury to any one else, throw into their way
some little pleasure coupled with a kind
word, which will be as a beam of light in
their clouded lives. Rest assured they will
be a little better for it. And this is all you
could ask for yonr own in like circum-
stances.
	If you are anxious to have your dear
ones adhere strictly to the rules of temper-
ance, that their nerves may be unshaken,
their intellects untrammeled and all their
faculties fully and harmoniously developed,
be sure there is no more certain way than to
inaugurate a general movement in that
direction; endeavor to make it “the fashion”
to abstain from every kind of stimulant and
every kind of excess. Organize abstemious
associations—with watchwords of modera-
tion.
	The more means and influence a person
possesses, and the more prominent the
position he or she occupies, the more bind-
ing upon that individual is the duty. “Love
thy neighbor as thyself;” because the sphere
is wide and the power to do good, greater;
and just in accordance with the use made of
opportunities offered, so shall judgment be
awarded. It has been said and truly, that
character is no more the outgrowth of pri-
vate teaching, than of the spirit of the age
in which it is nurtured. How important,
then, to lend every energy toward shaping
the “spirit of the age” in a healthy
direction! How unselfishly should all pa-
triots labor to leave behind them on the
pages of history, bright precedents for future
statesmen to copy! How earnestly should
parents desire to improve the moral tone of
the society into which their children are to
enter.
	As persuasion is more powerful than
coercion, let the sinner be pointed to the
better way, and taught the beauty of holi-
ness, rather than upbraided and denounced
for his or her short comings; for as the
meanest beggar will not part with his rags
on being told they are foul unless he can
obtain something better, so will not the
erring forsake their errors until convinced
of a better way.
							L. L. D.


TO CLEAN SMOKY PAPER-HANGINGS.--
Take a piece of wood of the shape of a
scrubbing-brush, nail a handle on the back,
then upon the face nail a piece of dried
sheepskin with the wool upon it, or flax or
tow will do, or cotton-flannel of several
thicknesses will answer very well. Dip this
brush into dry whiting, and rub the smoke
lightly with the brush, on the upper parts
of the room first, protecting the carpet with
matting of newspapers, as the whiting-dust
is hard to sweep off a carpet. The whiting
that remains on the wall is easily brushed
off with a soft cloth attached to a stick. It
is very effectual if the room is not damp and
the whiting is dry.

are dead to yesterday, and are not born to
the morrow.

WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿A WORD FROM ST. GEORGE.
				—
	ST. GGORGE, UTAH, NOV. 15, 1872.
DEAR EXPONENT:
	Permit me to have a few words with your
numerous and intelligent readers, upon a
topic as old as Eden, and as lovely, fragrant
and interesting as a full blown rose—namely--
	HOME AND FLOWERS.	
I place those words in connection as befng
the dearest among the 40,000 contained in
our natal language. I love flowers, and often
ask myself what is a home “without flow-
ers?” And ask your readers—what sort of
children would those be reared in a home
destitute of flowers? From my stand-point
experience and observation, I will say, they
will lack that chastity of ideas, and that
pleasing refinement of feeling, that gentle-
ness of demeanor, that tenderness and love,
which so much endears the child to its
mother and friends. Children naturally
love flowers, and a little encouragement in
assisting them to plants, seeds, a place to
use them, together with a little advice, and
most children readily become florists.
Flowers yield a fragrance only equalled by
their beauty. Are you sad? go among your
flowers and you at once feel comforted. Are
you excited or angry? go at once amid the
bloom of your garden, gladden your eyes
with the beauties around you, and inhale
the soothing perfumes, and your nerves will
soon become wonderfully calm. Do the
toils, cares, and trials of life bow you down
and discourage? go and commune with
nature’s floral gems, and you are soothed,
comforted and strengthened to go on. Are
you skeptical in regard to divinity and its
attributes? spend a day with the plants and
flowers; with a microscope examine the
beauties of form and color, of the minute
particles that compose them; then ask your-
selves, whence came they? Did you ever
hear profanity uttered by any one admiring
flowers? If so, it has been seldom, for
flowers influence to gentleness, to refine-
ment, to morality, to religion. Flowers are
easily grown. They are but weeds taken
from uncultivated nature in various parts of
the earth. Plant them in your gardens,
along the walks, in your front yards, around
your houses, on porch and veranda, in the
windows of your parlors, kitchens—every-
where that light and heat may reach; let a
pot with some flowering, fragrant, or orna-
mental, plant be placed. The care of these
will assist any lady to while away pleasur-
ably many an idle or spare moment. If
you would rear your children uncontamin-
ated with the vices and crimes learned in
the streets, rear flowers and fruit for them,
and teach them to feel an interest in these;
and provide books and other allurements in
abundance, and your children will stay at
home, and seldom fail to be intelligent and
a comfort. Home and flowers—ever one
and inseperable.					FLORA.


﻿Written for the EXPONENT.
	WOMAN'S SPHERE.
			—
	Any pursuit which is calculated to draw 
the thoughts and interests of women from
the charms of the kitchen, the nursery, the
flower garden, home, altogether, is a falla- 
cious one. The chief object which actuates
the mind to exert itself in preparing paths,
delightful for the feet to walk in, labors
pleasurable for the hands to perform, struc-
tures of whatever nature beautiful for the
eyes to behold or the senses to contemplate,
is that happiness may be secured. Many
of the “strong minded” women of the
present period have wrought within them-
selves the strange belief that the magnifi-
cent pedestal around which are hung the 
pure and lovely adornments of virtuous, in-
nocent, yet intelligent enjoyment, cannot
be erected within the circumscribed cofines
of domestic felicity, But when a home is
what it should be, it possesses all the
elements necessary for providing each of its
inmates with suitable occupations for head
and hands, at least, a portion of the time.
And no woman will ever be more abun-
dantly blessed or the recipient of more real
enjoyment, than she finds herself in filling
faithfully the sphere most wisely and
graciously alloted to her, that of a true wife
and mother; which is at once the most noble,
dignified and graceful position she can
occupy. Ambition may induce her to under-
value that sphere, which she considers un-
worthy her gifted intellectual powers; or
she may be prompted by holier motives, for
regeneration and reform, to leave her bright-
est and best prospects for tranquil happiness
to the care of servants, while she gives her
attention to public concerns. In either case
she will meet with disappointment, and
sooner or later find out her mistake. To
labor publicly under the selfish incitement
of gaining individual popularity, is an un-
wise and undignified course; and woman
is not likely to introduce any great reform-
ation by following man in this respect.
Necessity may some time compel women to
quit their homes and accept situations in
various walks of life mostly filled by men;
but while every position in which a liveli-
hood may be gained by honest industry, is 
ever honorable if honorably filled, no woman’s
time should be so fully occupied, could it
be avoided, in any employment that she
cannot supervise, in some degree, her own
domestic concerns. Many a woman saves
more by her frugal management, in a week,
than several might earn at hard labor in a
month. One bright, cheerful home, with its
healthy, happy inmates will create more
real sun-shine than a dozen hotels and
boarding houses with all their idle, gossip-
ing occupants. Why should woman seek to
become assistant ruler of the nations in any
other sphere, than that of the regulation
and government of the dearest and most
pleasant spot on earth—her home? INEZ.


﻿TO BE free from desire, is money; to be
free from the rage of perpetually buying
something new is a certain revenue; to be
content with what we possess, constitutes
the greatest and most certain of riches.


﻿A REMARKABLE  case of catalepsy oc-
curred last week at Fort Wayne, Indiana.
A little girl, three years old, was supposed
to have died, and every preparation for the
funeral was made two days thereafter.
While the funeral services were in progress,
and the body of the child was about to be
conveyed to the tomb, the supposed corpse
revived, and to the great surprise and joy of
mourning friends the child was again re-
stored to life, and has now fully recovered.


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
				—
	The first boy mentioned in the Bible—
Chap. I.
	Kittens are superseding lap-dogs for
street-wear.
	Lady ushers are the latest wrinkle for
fashionable weddings in New York.
	The latest medical mania in France is an
almost exclusive diet of water cresses.
	Kellogg’s dresses in “La Traviata” are
magnificent, and were made by Worth.
	Bret Harte’s father was formerly Princi-
pal of the Hudson (New York) Academy.
	Little Raven, the Arapahoe, has a head
ust like Beecher’s and is an eloquent orator.
	The present Czar of Russia is so in-
temperate that his physicians say he cannot
live many months.
	Prince Leopold, Queen Victoria’s young-
est son, will commence a course of study at
Oxford this October term.
	In the long course of his legal practice
Mr. Seward was never known to act for
a man against a woman.
	Froude safely passed those two dreaded
bugaboos of the ocean voyage—Hell’s Gate
and the newspaper interviewer.
	John Parker, who at one time had the
reputation of being the fastest type setter
in Texas, died lately in Huntsville.
	The late Benjamin Abbott of Boston, left;
bequests amounting to over $80,000, of
which $10,000 went to the Universalists.
	Mrs. Joseph Spinney has given $20,000
for the construction of a Methodist chapel
at Great Neck, L. I., and $10,000 for its
maintenance.
	Ex-Governor Magoffin, of Kentucky, has
discovered and entered as Government land
an island in Lake Superior, containing about
1,200 acres of good land.
	Mrs. Partington has been reading the
health officers’ weekly reports, and thinks
“total” must be an awful malignant disease,
since as many die of it as of all the rest put
together.
	Horace B. Claflin, the New York dry
goods prince, once insisted on paying $50,000
revenue tax in addition to the regular
assessment, believing that amount was
honestly due the Government.
	Mrs. Chapman and Mrs. Chassellette,
widows of the engineer and fireman who
were killed on the Erie Railway by the
negligence of the train dispatcher in tele-
graphing, have each recovered over $5,000
damages against the Erie Railway.
	Thiers ordinarily dresses in a suit of
black broadcloth, frock coat and decidedly
ancient trousers. He wears an old-fash-
ioned New England high shirt collar, and
invariably carries an umbrella or cane, upon
which he leans while shuffling along the
boulevard.	
	At a trades’ meeting the following toast
was given: “The printer—he beats the
farmer with his hoe, the carpenter with his
rule, the mason in setting up columns; he
surpasses the lawyer and doctor in attend-
ing cases, and beats the parson in the
management of the devil.”
	Cleopatra’s Needle is to be removed from
Alexandria to London. This obelisk origin-
ally stood at Heliopolis, and was brought
down to Alexandria by one of the Caesars
and was, in 1852, presented by Mohammed
Ali to the British Government. It is seventy
feet high, and at the base is seven feet
seven inches in diameter.


﻿KING SMITH.—A notable personage has just 
died—Mr. Augustus Smith, King of the Scilly
Islands, of which there are 140, although
only six are inhabited. Mr. Smith was a 
wealthy and crotchety Englishman, who 
in regard to government had notions of his
own which he wished to carry into practice, 
and so he became lessee of the Scilly Islands, 
under the County of Cornwall. He thus be- 
came king of about two thousand subjects, 
most of whom were a wretched lot of
peasants, for the islands are poor and unfruit-
ful. With a court of twelve men he ruled
the land, readjusted the relations of land and
people, and attended generally to their moral,
social, and religious status, although in the 
latter item he was not very strict.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿ADVICE TO A YOUNG BROTHER.
				——
	Do not be anxious to leave the dear ones
to whom you are indebted for your mortal
existence, as long as you can earn and ob-
tain a livelihood by clinging to them. There
will be time enough in which you may
learn to do without them, when you can no
longer avoid being thus thrust upon your
own resources. Do not imagine that because
your father is not a rich man and cannot
afford to support you without work, and
send you to the high school, you cannot be-
come educated and wise and great. The
worfd is full of knowledge, and there is no
lack of books and periodicals, which treat on
all the arts and sciences. The gaining of
wisdom is within the reach of all. For God
did not create His children to be drones,
and yet He requires nothing of them that
He does not find means for their obtaining.
Application is all that is necessary to make
a mind bright, reflective and intellectual.
Do not think too much of amassing wealth
and makinga “big show” in the world. Riches
cannot purchase happiness. They may help
to augment it, but are frail supports, liable
to slip away at any moment, and hopes and
aspirations which are built upon them are
in constant danger of being dashed to the
ground. Your safest, surest way of erecting
a defense against misfortune and want is in
learning a useful trade. Do not imagine
that because the hands of a clerk are soft
and white, while yours are hardened with
labor, and sun-burnt, that people of sense or
even good taste admire him and scorn you.
Remember that but for the energies of the
tradesmen, there would be no store-rooms
built, and no goods to store. Honor your
position in society and it will be honorable.
Always treat others with proper respect, and
you will be respectable and respected. Be
temperate in all your habits, and you will
never need to be troubled with doctor’s bills.
Be strictly honest in all you dealings and you
will always find better use for you money
than paying lawyers’ fees. Be virtuous and
industrious, and you will become wealthy
and great. Love God and serve Him, and
you will always be happy.
									L.


﻿WOMEN’S FOREHEADS.
				  ——
	When phrenology first began to attract
attention as a science, high foreheads of
women, as well as of men, were associated
with intellect.
	Every member of the opposite sex, how-
ever dull or uncultivated she may be,
admires mental gifts, and has no objection
to the reputation of possessing them herself;
consequently she determined to have the
seeming, if not the reality, and stripped her
forehead of the clustering tresses, and even
removed the hair by artificial means, that
she might present a front which would
awake the enthusiasm of Gall or Spurzheim.
	For a number of years this mania for
high foreheads raged in spite of the patent
fact that they detracted from their feminine
loveliness, giving it a hard, bold, masculine
expression that should be sedulously avoided.
All the classic models of beauty, whether in
marble or on canvas, from the Venus and
Phyrne down to the Marys of Raphael and
the Magdaleus of Murillo, the picturesque
damsels of the Campagna, and the classic
Suliote maidens, instead of high have quite
low foreheads—something our own women
would seem at last to have discovered.
	Horace, and Catullus, and Ovid all sang of
the fair, fond creatures whose white fore-
heads gleamed like the crescent moon be-
neath the dark cloud of silken hair.


﻿Artists have so painted feminine beauty.
Men of taste and gallantry have admitted
such. Phrenology has ceased to be connected
with aesthestic subjects, and therefore we
have returned to nature and art. Indeed,
the passion now is rather for low foreheads,
for hair over the temples, and lovelocks
that shade the lustre of deep eyes.
	This is rather overdone—but it is prefer-
able to lofty foreheads and stripped brows,
that make the face more fitting for a Roman
senator than a genteel, tender, womanly
woman. Every man of taste must rejoice
that something like an approximation to
the old models and correct standard of femi-
nine loveliness has been established, and
they are no longer painted with
	“High white fronts that tell of power
	Which ne’er is fashioned by the gentle heart."


﻿Aged beauty.—Some one writes of the
beauty of old people. Who of us but have
seen this beauty, perchance upon an aged
grandmother, perhaps upon a mother ripen-
ing for Heaven, catching the radiance of
celestial glory ere they departed. He says:
“Lord Lytton speaks in one of his novels of
a man who was uglier than he had any
business to be; and if he could but read it,
every human being carries his life in his face,
and is good looking or the reverse as that
life has been good or evil. On our features
the fine chisel of thought and emotion are
eternally at work. Beauty is not the mon-
opoly of blooming young men and of pink
and white maidens. There is a slow-grow-
ing beauty which only comes to perfection
in old age. Grace belongs to no period of
life and goodness improves the longer it
exists. I have seen sweeter smiles from a lip
of seventy than upon a lip of seventeen.
There is the beauty of youth and the beauty 
of holiness—a beauty much more seldom
met and more frequently found in the arm-
chair by the fire, with grandchildren around
its knee. Husband and wife, who have
fought the world side by side, who have made
common stock of joy and sorrow and grown
aged together, are not unfrequently found
curiously alike in pitch and tone of voice—
just as twin Pebbles on the beach, exposed
to the same tidal influences, are each other’s
second self. he has gained a feminine some-
thing, which brings his manhood into full
relief. She has gained a masculine some-
thing which acts as a foil to her woman-
hood.”—Ex.


﻿The evil done by the first utterer of a
slander is small compared with that which
is spread through a community from the re-
petition of the false tale by idle babblers.
These persons would fain excuse themselves
by alleging that they had heard it from Hr.
So and-so, a Mrs. Such-a-one, or they shelter
themselves under the common generalities
of “people say,” or “they say.” Counter-
feited coins and bank-notes, however ingen-
iously executed, do no harm if they remain
in the hands of the original forger. It is by
their circulation that the people suffer.
Somebody once said to a sage: “A man
slandered you in my presence.” “If,”
replied the wise man, “you had not listened
with pleasure, he would not have defamed
me.” The remark was a just one.


﻿This is the way St. Louis ’squires aid suf-
fering young people, according to the
“Democrat:” “A loving couple called on
Justice Jecko to get married. On examina-
he learned the girl was not yet 18, and he
told her to go up street to another justice,
and by the time she reached there she would
probably be old enough. Upon this hint the
lovers brightened up, and on the next trial
accomplished their purpose.”


﻿THE SEX.
			      —
	One of Fanny Fern's daughters lives at
St. Paul.
	Three English ladies recently achieved
the ascent of Mont Blanc, and had a trium-
phant recption on their return.
	Laura Sainz, Marchioness of Patayama,
late lady-in-waiting to Queen Isabella, is
making a fortune on the lyric stage in Italy.
	For domestic reasons, Kate Fields will be
unable to lecture in the West at all this sea-
son, and will be obliged to curtail the
number of her engagements in the East.
	“I'm not used to begging,” said a little
girl to a lady of whom she had asked alms,
“ ’cause only two weeks ago my father was
a merchant!”
	"Why, child, how could you be reduced
to poverty so soon?”
	“My father took a bad two-dollar bill at
his peanut-stand, and it ruined him,” sob-
bed the child.
	When a child, Pauline Lucea manifested
such a decided taste for drawing as well as
for music, that for a while her mother was
disposed to let the former inclination prevail.
She took daily lessons in drawing, but was
also allowed to go on with her vocal studies.
She made wonderful progress in the use of
her pencil, yet such was her proficiency in
singing that very soon there was no question
that her true vocation was music.
	“It is a fact,” says Mr. Scudamore, “that
a telegraph clerk in London, who was
engaged on a wire to Berlin, formed an
acquaintance with, and an attachment to a
female clerk who worked on the same wire
in Berlin, that he made a proposal to her
and that she accepted him without having
seen him. They were married, and the
marriage resulting from their electric affini-
ties is supposed to have turned out as well
as those in which the senses are apparently
concerned.”
	There was a social meeting, of five elderly
ladies, sisters, at Concord, at the residence
of R. N. Rice, son of one of them, the
“youngest” of the five being a “greatgrand-
mother,” and their combined ages reaching
three hundred and ninety years, averaging
about eighty years. By arrangement, they
were got together from different parts of
the State, and their re-union under one roof
was as happy an event as it was unexpected
to all of them, they not having been together
for about twenty years.
	Mrs. Florida White, well known in the
fashionable world forty years ago, was one of
the most beautiful, accomplished, elegant
and attractive women of her day. She was
the daughter of Gen. Adair, of Kentucky,
and her first husband was Joseph M. White,
the second delegate sent to Congress from
the Territory of Florida. Highly educated,
full of genius, her society was courted by
the most elevated circles in Washington,
New Orleans, New York and Boston. She
was a brilliant conversationalist, ready and 
effective at repartee, and a sincere, warm-
hearted gentlewoman of the most gracious
and generous impulses. On one of her visits
to Rome she was presented to the Pope.
	“Kneel, my daughter,” said he, as she
stood erect in her imperial grace before
him. All kneel to me except the daughters
of sovereigns.”
	“I am a princess in my own right, Your
Holiness,” she replied.
	“How can that be, when you are an
American born?” “In my country the people
are sovereign, and I am a daughter of the 
people.”
	The Pope smiled a gracious assent, rejoin- 
ing, “Then receive an old man’s blessing.”

			WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

		A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL.	
	
	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.

	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.

	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves ? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"

	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of Woman’s Exponent, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.

	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.

	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.

	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In Woman’s Exponent a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.

	Woman’s Exponent will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year, $2.00	1 Copy six months, $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months, 9.00

	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers Woman’s Exponent, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


Woman's Exponent.
﻿VOL. I.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, DECEMBER 15, 1872. No. 14.


﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.

	Horace Greeley was born on the 3rd of
February, 1811, and died on the 23rd of
November 1872.
	The case of Stokes Is not yet decided.
And Jim Fisk is said to have a successor in
Nooman Henry Smith of Wall street.
	The wife of Father Hyancinth is re-
ported a very amiable, intelligent and at-
tractive lady, and devotedly attached to her
husband.
	In his late message to the Senate and
House of Representatives President Grant
does not forget Utah, but he fails to mention
Woman Suffrage.
	“A Crowning domestic disappointment,”
is what a San Francisco paper styles a Chi-
naman, If John could read that wouldn’t
he feel complimented?
	The gas stokers' strike, in London, is
likely to terminate in their being compelled
to “strike alight” once more at the old price,
though the new “strike” has not the appear-
ance of being “all gas.”
	An Exchange says on the question
“Shall ladies ride astride?” “It does not
seem half so dangerous for a lady as for a
politition to attempt it.” To us, it would
seem very unbecoming for either.
	Two 180 pound balls suspended by a cord
from the teeth, while holding a 100-pound
ball in each hand; and the shouldering of a
500-pound cannon, is a story of rather large
dimensions to be told of a woman on the
London stage, but it is said to be true.
	General O. C. Maxwell, a gallant officer,
who was wounded five times in war, while
fighting for his country, shot and killed
himself at Dayton, Ohio, recently, as if de-
termined to die by violence at last, though
tempted Providence had so long and kindly
guarded him.
	In Tofsfield, Massachusetts, an aged
couple have lately celebrated the 60th
anniversary of their wedding day; and the
kind old gentleman had the candor and good-
ness of heart to acknowledge, as many a one
might who never does, that “under God he
owed his success in life, mainly to the
untiring devotion of the good woman at his
side.”
	The Distinguished Swiss scientist, Pro-
fessor Carl Vogt, is shortly to visit America.
Excursionists, journalists, lecturers, and
travelers of every profession and of every
nation are, without pausing to realize the
fact making it plainly manifest that this is
the day of which it is spoken, “Many shall
run to and fro and knowledge shall be in-
creased.”
	Indulgence in the “social glass” and
then in the contemptable jug is daily prov-
ing the destruction of thousands who, in
other respects have every chance for becom-
ing distinguished as men of honor and
superiority. Will the time ever come when
such individuals will listen to that impor-
tuning in which exists their only safeguard,
“Touch not, O taste not the wine!”
	Reports of the progress of the opening
band of subscriptions for providing for the
celebration of the 100th anniversary of
American Independence—the Centennial, to
be held at Philadelphia, in the year 1876—
are unfavorable. The people “don’t see it.”
They have, many of them, lost sight of the
old Constitution; and no wonder. Their
teachers and rulers have laid it aside.
	In some parts of Japan, married women
have a strange method for securing their
loyalty to their husbands. That no one may
be tempted by their beauty, they disfigure
themselves by shaving their eyebrows and
blacking their teeth with fine betal nut; a
precaution which is said, however, to be
unnecessary, as beauty among them is very
rare, and after marriage they fail rapidly.
	To prevent her money being taken by
burglers, should any happen to call in her
absence, on leaving home, a New York lady
concealed $450 in the ashpan. Contrary to
her expectations, her husband went home
before she did and in starting a fire, inno-
cently made a burnt offering of the green-
backs. That lady’s precaution against
thieves is rather too flimsy to recommend
itself.
	An exchange avers that in Yorkville,
Tennessee, they are determined to have the
gospel in peace, if they are compelled to
fight for it, as proven by a man named
Sharkey being promptly shot on the spot for
causing disturbance in a meeting there.
But it is bettr to offer timely warning before
shooting, and possibey preserve order by
threats, as some of the gentle worthies out
West imagine they do some times, than to
really, positively “shoot!’
	New York papers tell of a fearful
explosion of 500 pounds of nitro-glycerine
in Yonkers, N. Y., by which two young
men were blown to atoms, another mortally
wounded and a fourth, severely but not
fatally injured; and general havoc produced
for miles around, in so much that many
believed that an earthquake had taken place,
and very great consternation prevailed.
Thus daily disasters are occurring on every
hand and in every manner.
	A young artist in England has the
wonderful capacity of seeing with the naked
eye the most minute objects, which ordin-
ary eyesight can only distinguish when
highly magnified; such as the infusoria
contained in water. Scientists who have
examined into the case pronounce the mag-
nifying powers of his vision about five
hundred diameters. This singularity of
sight was occasioned by a transformation
of the eye caused by a peculiar disease when
quite young.
	Queen Victoria is living in the plainest
possible style, and is said to spend much of
her time in the open air; walking and riding
often crossing the Dee and always using in
her rides, an uncovered carriage, a phaeton
or a wagonnette. No ceremonious a-do at-
tends her, but the royal carriage is preceded
by an outrider, who sees that the way is
clear, and the Queen goes quietly on with a
smile and a bow for every one who chances
to meet her. A “picnic” in the woods is
frequently enjoyed by the Queen and her
ladies, and at such times no chair of honor
is provided for her, but she finds a seat on
the clump of a tree or any other suitable
place. This lady has never given to her
subjects a more befitting lesson than the life
of humility, virtue and sense which she
lives may afford as an example.
	The educational advantages of the
country are progressing so rapidly, and
have, indeed, already gained so great an
eminence that there is no longer any excuse
for men or women to cast reflections on cir-
cumstances or waste time in vain regret
that they have not opportunities of basking
in the hallowed sunbeams of intelligence.
Time and application with present and
coming capacity, will make philosophers
and scientists for the people. But will the
light obtained be used to elevate and enoble
the nations yet to be, or will degeneration
still continue to control the paths in
which we tread, mocking reason and
bidding defiance to the laws of nature and of
nature’s God! We can only prognosticate
what will be the result of certain courses.
	Affairs In France assume a serious
character. Late dispatches say that trouble
is apprehended, and present indications
justify such apprehensions. The monarch-
ists, it is stated, have endeavored to force a
vote, without delay, on a proposition to
censure the Government in a rigid manner,
and repudiate the republic formally. A
proposition in an other form, making con-
cessions which were then thought satisfac-
tory, and a motion to postpone the vote on
the main question for a day, prevailed. A
vote was afterwards passed on a question
censuring a minister and the Government 
for courting popularity with the municipali- 
ties. It is reported that MacMahon, who
is commander-in-chief of the French army,
and Ducrot, who holds an important com-
mand, refuse to obey orders of others than
the monarchists of the Assembly. A civil
war in France is almost threatening to
follow shortly, as the people are opposed to
a monarchy; but might accept the Napoleonic
Empire.
	Dr. Chapin, who delivered the funeral
address of Horace Greeley, stated that his
last words were “I know that my Redeemer
liveth. It is done.” The funeral of this
great man, which took place at New York
on the 3rd inst., as reported, was the grand-
est and most imposing ever witnessed
on this continent. The corpse was viewed 
by 50,000 people, and at the funeral were 
present 100,000 strangers. The working
people from all parts crowded in to take
a farewell glance at the face of their friend
and benefactor, and feelings of regret and
signs of emotion were demonstrated both by
acquaintances and strangers. Thurlow
Weed said of him among other eulogiums, 
“Looking back over Greeley’s life I think
he showed as much unselfish ambition as
any man that ever lived. His whole life
was given to opposing injustice and oppres-
sion. His character was perfectly pure. He
had no vices that I know of, and I was most
intimate with him in all matters. He was
fearless in the expression of his belief in
what was right. His whole aim was to do 
good.”


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿BIRTHDAY MUSINGS.
 		  —
	TO A FRIEND.
		  —
	We are falling,
	We are falling,
In the sear and yellow leaf,
	Time is calling,
	Time is calling,
Telling us that he's the thief
	That is stealing,
	That is stealing,
All our youthful form away;
All the time he keeps revealing,
He is making us his prey.

	Hair is shewing,
	Hair is shewing,
Silver threads within each braid;
	Eyes are growing,
	Eyes are growing,
Dimmer in our later grade.

	Steps are slower,
	Steps are slower,
As the downward path we tread
	Voice is lower,
	Voice is lower,
Bow’d is oft the weary head.

	Shall we sorrow,
	Shall we sorrow,
That our life is in decline?
	There’s a morrow
	There’s a morrow
"Where eternal suns will shine!

	My Mother Earth
	My Mother Earth!
In thy bosom we shall sleep;
Thou alone and not another,
Shall those sacred vigils keepl
	Till the morning,
	Till the morning,
When the resurection, comes—
Then with all a Brides’ adorning
Christ will take us to our Homes—

	Ready waiting,
	Ready waiting,
All prepar’d by Beauty’s hand,
	All dilating,
	All dilating,
On the scene I now command.

	God of Heaven!
	God of Heaven!
Hold us that we never fall!
	All forgiven,
	All forgiven,
God and we be All in All
G. S. L. City.	 	  HANNAH T. KING.


F.R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
				—
			Salem, Utah Co., Utah.
					Nov. 20th 1872.
Editor Exponent:—
	We held a meeting ou the 21st inst., of
which I send a short report. Meeting was
opened by singing “How beautious are their
feet,” prayer by Counselor Mrs. Lucinda
Jackman; minutes of previous meeting
were read and accepted. Counselor Mrs. L.
Jackman then addressed the meeting, spoke
upon the training of our children, the duty
of mothers towards their daughters, urging
them to set patterns worthy of imitation,
look after the sick and needy; said she
was pleased with the spirit of interest
which the sisters manifested in all that per-
tains to the Society. In compliance with
the suggestion of Sister Snow a motion was
made and seconded that we drop the word
“Female” from the name of the Society;
carried unanimously, a report of the same
to be forwarded to the Exponent. Meeting
adjourned until first Thursday in Decem-
ber. Benediction; by Mrs. Elizabeth B.
Davis.
			M. P. Killian, Secretary.
				—
				Nephi, Dec. 1st 1872.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
According to the suggestions  Sister Eliza
R. Snow, at a meeting of the Relief Socitey.
held at this place on the 17th of October, the
word “Female” was dropped from the name
of our Society, by a unanimous vote. You
[new column]
will please excuse me for this delay in report-
ing, as I have a large family to attend to
and it seems almost impossible for me to get
time to write more than I am obliged to for
the Society. And another tning, I feel my
incompetency to write any thing worthy the
scrutiny of the public eye. I do not feel
proud to see my name in your paper; still, I
like to do my duty if I know what it is. I
have set out for salvation in the Kingdom
of our God, and am determined with the
help of my good husband and the watch-care
of my Heavenly Father to win. Our Society
is not as flourishing as we could wish, some
of the members live quite a distance from
our place of meeting, and some are careless,
I think, about attending our meetings, but
there are the faithful few that feel it aduty
as well as a pleasure to go to meeting, and
do all they can to comfort and bless the
poor.
				M. L. Bigler, Secretary.
				  —
				Big Cottonwood,
					December 2nd 1872.
Editor Exponent:--
	At a meeting of the Relief Society of this
Ward, held 27th November, the word
"Female" in connection with the name of
the Society was dropped.
	We meet semi-monthly, and are making
the best of our time at improvement. We
are trying to gain the victory over diffidence
and backwardness in speaking, and we feel
thankful that our Record Book abounds in
good instruction given from each to each.
	After singing and prayer our meetings
are devoted to teaching and telling our expe-
rience in spiritual and temporal knowledge,
which we consider a very good plan for
mutual improvement. When we deem it
necessary we have sewing meetings, which
are pretty well attended, still, not so well as
they should be, but as a general thing it is
the few who accomplish the greatest amount
of good.
	Our financial condition is good; we intend
very soon commencing to build a hall for
our own use, as we have over 10 shares in
the Co-operative Store and some means on
hand. This, with our industry the cming-
winter enables us to look forward to beocom
fortably situated before this time next year
	We intend giving the very young ladies
some socks to knit, for the benefit of our
building, so that each, no matter how young,
may have an opportunity of lending a help-
ing hand, besides stimulating them to be
liberal and industrious.
	We also look to the necessities of our poor
so that they are not in want.
	Your very valuable and interesting paper
is increasing in esteem and popularity, the
general feeling with us being a desire that
it may meet with every success.
		HARRIET W. BRINTON,

	LAVINA HARPER,

	PROVO CITY, Dec. 5th, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	A meeting of the 1st Ward Relief Society
was held at the School House on the 5th inst.,
Counselor Mrs. Ann Jones Presiding, Presi-
dent Mrs. Caroline M. Johnson being absent
on account of sickness. After singing, and
prayer by Mrs. Ann Needham, minutes of
the preceding meeting were read and ac-
cepted; Counselors Mrs. Ann Jones and Mrs,
June Allman gave some good instructions
and comforting words. Several of the sisters
[new column]
bore testimony to the truth and expressed 
their desires to do right. The Secretary 
read from the EXPONENT a letter from Miss 
Eliza R. Snow. A vote to drop the word
“Female” from the name of the Relief
Society as suggested by sister E. R. Snow
was carried unanimously.
	Our Society was organized the 14th of 
May 1868; since that time up to May 14th 
1872, we have disbursed to the poor $309;
paid for public expenses $130; we own 
shares in the Provo woolen factory to the 
amount of $200, and in the Co-oporative
Store to the amount of $75, and have in the 
Treasury $96.56. Besides the above dona- 
tions, there have been 297 days work done
by the Society.
	MRS. SOPHY MECHAM Secretary.
	MRS. SARAH H. SANDERSON Tres.	

	Bountiful, Dec. 9th 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	Directly after Conference, at our usual
meeting a unanimous vote was taken to
drop the word “Female” from the name of
our Society, according to Sister Eliza R.
Snow’s suggestion. There is a good feeling
in our Society and a desire with the sisters
to do all they can to help the poor. We
meet twice a month for labor and many of
the destitute and aged have been made to re-
joice in the faithfulness of our exertions. Not-
withstanding many of the sisters live quite
a distance from the place of meeting, they
remember the time appointed and a portion
of them assemble together. THE EXPONENT
is hailed with pleasure. 	E. H. Barlow.
					—
FOURTEENTH WARD ASSEMBLY ROOMS,
	Salt Lake City, Dec. 7th, 1872.
	General Retrenchment Meeting of the
ladies of the several Wards. Singing: Prayer
by Counselor Mrs. E. Howard; Singing:
Minutes of last meeting read by assistant
Secretary Miss Maggie Mair, and accepted.
Opportunity offered for the reading of min-
utes of meetings held by different branches
of the Society. President Mrs. Mary A.
Freeze read the minutes of the last meeting
of the Young Ladies’ Retrenchment Associ-
ation in the 11th Ward; Secretary Miss Abby
Piggot read minutes of two meetings of the
Young Ladies’ Retrenchment Association
of the 8th Ward. Counselor Mrs. Sarah M.
Kimball, (President of the Physiological
class,) said she had no apology to make but
regretted that she was not as well qualified as
she would like to be to treat upon the subject
before the meeting, namely, Physiology. It
is a great and important study for every
woman who is or ever expects to become a
mother. We may, by not understading the
was of health, inflict grievious wrong upon
ourselves and those who will come after us.
“True Science and true Religion walk hand
in hand;” the Science of the formation of
the human body is a most wonderful and
beautiful study. Counselor Mrs. Woodruff
said she was pleased with the subject before
us; the longer she lived the more necessity
she saw for the daughters of Zion to inform
themselves upon these matters, which apper-
tain to our present and future salvation.
Mrs. Elenor Pratt did not feel prepared to
treat upon the Science of Physiology but
felt it a very important text. We all can
gain practical knowledge on this subject by
observation. Mrs. W. East felt that the
laws of life, the book of nature were among
the greatest objects of interest to us all. A
temperate life and a prayerful heart pro-
duce a fruitful mind. Further remarks
were made by Z. D. Young and Mrs. Wat-
mouth, and meeting adjourned for two
weeks. Singing; benediction by Counselor 
Mrs. Sarah M. Kilmball.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	A mother who thinks more of the beau-
tiful face and elegant form of her daughter
than of her health and education, is likely,
by neglect of the latter to injure and perhaps
destroy all traces of the former. Healthy
employment for mind and body, with
suitable relaxation and recreation, is the
surest incentive to happiness that can be
found; and without health and happiness,
beauty cannot exist. There is no exercise
better calculated to assist in finely develop-
ing the female form than the various duties
of housekeeping moderately and judiciously
engaged in. The varied motions required
in sweeping floors and making beds, reach-
ing, lifting and swaying are excellent
methods of expanding the chest and giving
proper growth and elasticity to the body
and limbs, besides engendering the spirit of
industry and a desire to be useful in the
young. Carding, spinning and winding
yarn are also healthful employments, but
should not be too rigorously performed at
an early age.
	A tea made of peach leaves is a sure cure
for a kidney difficulty.
	A tea made of chestnut leaves, and
drank in the place of water, is said to cure
the most obstinate case of dropsy.
	A tea made of ripe or dried whortle-
berreies, and drank in the place of water, is
a sure and speedy cure for a scrofulous diffi-
culty, however bad.
	Cruellers.—Two eggs, two tablespoon-
fuls of melted butter, two do of new milk,
four heaping tablespoonfuls of sugar, one
teaspoonful of soda, salt, and flour enough to
make it easy to roll. Cook in hot lard.
	Sponge Roll.—Three eggs, one cup of
sugar, one of flour, one teaspoonful of
cream of tarter, half a spoonful of soda,
rubbed carefully through a sieve. Bake in
a dripping pan, spread with jelly when
warm, and roll.
	Stewed Macaroni.—Boil two ounces of
macaroni in water, drain it well; put into a
saucepan one ounce of butter, mix it well
with one tablespoonful of flour, moisten
with four tablespoonfuls of veal stock, and a
gill of cream, add two ounces of grated par-
mesan, one tablespoonful of mustard, salt
and cayenne to taste; put in the macaroni,
and serve as soon as it is well mixed with
the sauce and quite hot.


SEVENTY YEARS AGO.

	A dear aunt, who is now more than
eighty years old, and still on the sunny side
of life, furnishes us many interesting and
delightful stories which are all the more
beautiful because we can rely on their truth-
fulness; all the more fascinating, because of
the little tinge of romance which her child-
like innocence unwittingly, yet artistically,
draws occasionally, and all the more vener-
ated because they are forty, fifty and seventy
years old and have never been published.
We select the following:
	“It always did me good to wash. Many a time
when a girl at home, I would get up in a morn-
ing feeling half-sick and very miserable. Af-
ter breakfast I would say to mother, ‘I think
I will wash to-day.’ ‘Well, mother would
say, ‘now I shall look out for a good day’s
work, and am sure it will be done.’ Mother
never said to me ‘go to work;' never. But
she often tried to keep me from doing as
much as I wished. Once, I remember,
when I was but ten years old, my sister,
who was two years younger, and I came home
from school at night, mother was weaving


and had used up all the filling that was
spun. ‘Now girls, she said to us, ‘to-mor-
row you will have to stay at home and card
and spin so the loom need not stand idle.'
I felt bad about that, for I disliked to miss
my lessons. Father and mother were in-
vited out for the evening, and would not
return before twelve at night. As soon as
they were gone, I said to my little sister,
'Susan let us go to work and card, and spin
the yarn to-night while mother is gone, so
we can go to school to-morrow.’ We there-
fore set to and I had to contrive to keep
Susan awake; but when father and mother
came home at 12 o’clock I was just finishing
off the last threads. ‘Girls! What are you
about! Put that wheel away this minute!’
said mother. And I did, but the yarn was
spun and sister and I went to school next
day as usual. O! how I used to carry on,
when a girl! Always up to some such
caper to surprise and disappoint, yet please
my mother.”
	The fine dark eyes of the aged narrator
still twinkle and dance with the love of fun
and mischief, and her voice is still musical
and sweet as it trills a little, low laugh, after
telling a story of the long past. And the
listener can only contemplate that such were
America’s daughters seventy years ago.
Where could such a girl be found to-day?
What does the fashionable education which
our young ladies must now acquire to be
considered accomplished and refined, fit
them for? Certainly not for the life of
practical usefulness and sound judgment
which the well earned and substantial
education that venerable lady received, has
made her the happy delineator of.


﻿DECOROUS.
		—
	A Tennessee writer gives the following
telling description of the ostensible prodigy:
“Those who have not seen him ask what
he is like. He is like everything. He’s a
simoon trom India—a gentle zephyr from
the downs of Devon; a whirlpool off the
coast of Norway—an eddy in a New England
trout brook; a tornado from the plains of
Texas—a soothing, summer breeze from the
hop yards of Minesota; an incarnate denun-
ciation—a merciless sweeping Iconoclast; a
miser in praise—a prodigal in abuse; a
modern reversed atlas, who stands on, in-
stead of carrying the world; an over-power-
ing egotist, without vanity; the very light-
ning-bug of possible reformation; the modern
Hercules, who undertakes the job of
cleaning the Augean stables of society,
government, religion, morals, laws, physic,
education—everything.	He’s the credit
Mobelier; the credit financier; the prospec-
tive sextillionaire—the next President of
the United States; the embodiment of
international; a huge syndicate; a “blase”
traveler, who has seen this little world, and
who aspires to a jaunt over Saturn and
adjacent planets. He’s the chain-lightning
orator of the new dispensation; he’s the
reckless bull in the China shop of the world;
he’s a Rochefort on a large scale; a little
Commune all by himself; he’s a self-wind-
ing machine, eontinually throwing off
intellectual hypothesis, and scattering them
broadcast over the world; he’s a human
mower and reaper—mowing down (in his
Imagination) follies and errors, blunders and
iniquities, and reaping all the fame that
comes from improvements time works in its
natural course, he is in fact—Train, who
when dead, can never have an imitator.”


﻿A WRITER says: “It is not the drinking,
but getting sober, that is so terrible in a
drunkard’s life.” Why get sober at all,
then, says old Smiggles.


﻿FLOWERS.
			     —
	Good temper is like a sunny day, shed-
ding brightness on everything.
	John Wesley, at the age of seventeen,
wrote: “I do not remember to have felt low-
ness of spirits for one-quarter of an hour
since I was born.”
	It is worthy of notice that, while second
thoughts are best in matters of judgment,
first thoughts are always to be preferred in
matters that relate to morality.
	Depend upon yourself; riding upon the
shoulders of another is dangerous and foolish.
If you are not cast off into a disagreeable
place, you might be let down in a very
ugly manner, when you least expect it.
	Esteem an act of benignity greatly prefer-
able to munificence; the former is peculiar to
great and distinguished persons; the latter
belongs to flatters of the people who court
the applause of the inconstant vulgar.
	There may exist a sort of love where
the beautiful beacon of untarnished trust
has ceased to shine; but such is not the
love which lights the heart with hope; the
eyes with sympathy and the soul with
gratitude. Such love is only found within
the temple of unbounded, fairly-tried, and
unshaken confidence.
	Life’s Voyage.—In the voyage of life a
man may be wrecked as is a ship. Con-
science, however, is an anchor that will in
most circumstances insure him safety. It
is to be remembered, nevertheless, that like
the anchor, conscience may be carried away,
and so ensure ruin and wreck.
	Revenge is a momentary triumph which
is almost immediately succeeded by remorse;
while forgiveness, which is the noblest of all
revenge, entails a perpetual pleasure! It
was well said by a Roman emperor that he
wished to put an end to all his enemies by
converting them into friends.
	Where education has been entirely neg-
lected or improperly managed, we see the
worst of passions ruling with uncontrolled
and incessant sway. Good sense degener-
ates into craft, and anger rankles into malig-
nity. Restraint, which is thought most
salutary, comes too late, and the most
judicious admonitions are urged in vain.
	The following is an old Jewish story: “A
good man had a good wife; but because
they had no children, they mutually put
away each other. The good man married a
bad (a heathen) wife, and she made him
bad (a heathen;) the good woman married a
bad (a heathen) husband, and she made
him good.”
	The fruits of the earth do not more
obviously require labor and cultivation to
prepare them for our use and subsistance
than our faculties demand instruction and
regulation, in order to become upright and
valuable members of society, useful to
others, or happy and ourselves.—“Barrow.”
	The house will be kept in continual tur-
moil when there is no toleration of each
other’s feelings, no meek submission to
injuries, and no soft answer to turn away
wrath. If you lay a single stick of wood in
the grate, and apply fire to it, it will go out;
put on another stick, and they will burn;
and a half a dozen and you will have an
effective blaze. There are other fires subject
to the same condition.  If one member of a
family gets into a passion, and is left alone,
he will cool down, and possibly be ashamed
and repent. But oppose temper to temper,
let one harsh answer be followed by another,
and there will soon be a blaze which will
enwrap them in its burning heat.

﻿SPLINTERS.
				—
	Froude is ill and his lectures have been
postponed......The will of Mr. and Mrs.
Greeley leaves the property to the two
daughters......A bill has been introduced at
Washington asking the grant of a pension of
$2,000 yearly to the widow of the late Gen-
eral Meade......The public debt statement
shows a decrease of the debt, during the
month of December of $1,198,229......Thiers
consents to remain President if the minis-
ters will withdraw their resignations......In
Lake county, Oregon, the Indians are caus-
ing considerable trouble; all the settlers on
Link river are reported murdered......A
crop of cotton raised this season on the
Pacific coast, turns off over four hundred
ponnds to the acre......Cedarville, Surprise
Valley has had a conflagration......Somebody
has discovered a splendid ledge of stone
coal near the Central Pacific Railroad,
twenty miles from Virginia City......Chris-
tina Rosetti is in a decline......In the Senate,
on the 2nd inst., the Chaplain in his opening
prayer referred to Horace Greeley as one
whose pen had been a benefactor to man-
kind......Prior to the failure of the banking
house of Bowles Bros., New York, Josie
Mansfield is reported to have been a deposi-
tor in that establishment to the amount of
$37,000......The Secretary of the Treasury
reports excess of imports over exports for
the United States in trade with foreign
countries, during the fiscal year, ending
June, 1872, $182,548, 991......Sumner would
have the names of battles with fellow citi-
zens discontinued on the army register, and
never placed on the regimental colors of the
United States......Seamen pronounce late
storms on the lakes, the heaviest ever
known......Brooklyn’s spice mills have re-
cently been consumed by fire......Ball play-
ing, military drill, rowing and archery are
among late recommendations of healthful
exercise for women......Susan B. Anthony
and fourteen other women, charged with
illegal voting, have been arraigned before
United States Commissioner Storrs......A
small muff of black fox fur, in some places,
costs $300......Hereafter the Prussian Gov-
ernment will use only American paper for
its bank notes......A recently deceased
French gentleman bequeathed to the
widows of Paris an annuity of $6,000......
While E. Cady Stanton and Joslyn Gage are
both for Grant, the husbands of both these
Iadies voted for Greeley, it is said......Edin-
burgh has decided that the lady medical
students in the University are entitled to
be educated and take degrees......It is pro-
posed to erect a printers’ monument to the
memory of Greeley.......Two high stepping
colored boys supply the place of a Baltimore
doctor’s horse......Thieves lately entered the
Allegany Hospital at Pittsburgh, Pennsylva-
nia, and carried off the bedclothing and
furniture......The Vienna Exposition is to
have a 200,000 gallon saltwater acquarium.....
A free use of lemon juice for rheumatism is
prescribed by English and French physi-
cians......A cave, of several hundred tons,
occurred in the Eureka Consolidated mine
a short time ago, but no one was injured......
Sumner tells a touching story of the wreck
of the “Assyria,” an English freight vessel.
......A locomotive on the San Jose Railroad
was recently thrown from the track by a
cow, locomotive turned over and four cars
badly smashed. How’s that, will the owner
of the cow be required to pay damages?......
The Greeley electors of Arkansas, it is said,
have assembled and cast the vote of the
State for B. Gratz Brown for President, and
N. P. Banks of Massachusetts for Vice Presi-
[new column]
dent, charging fraud upon Grant’s victory
......A lady in Louisville has discovered
diamonds—her diamond rings which were
carried away and hidden by rats......A re-
ward is offered of $1,000 by the San Fran-
cisco police, for the arrest and conviction
of an unknown desperado.


﻿PRESERVE HEALTH.
					—
	The worst spendthrifts in the world are those
who squander their health. For, without
health, success in any profession is uncertain
if not positively unattainable. But even should
victory crown the ambitious hopes and unre-
mitting labors of one who has outraged the
laws of nature, there is little enjoyment to be
realized when deprived of health, compared
with what might have been its accompaniment.
A great man once remarked to a young pro-
fessional, “I am certain I could have performed
twice the labor, both better and with greater ease
to myself, if I had known as much of the laws of
health and life at twenty-one as I do now.”
	No study can be of more importance to the
human family than that of the human physique
its self. For only with proper understanding
of the corporeal structure of a system, can the
promotion of its usefulness and longevity be
safely attempted or permanently secured. And
one of the most reprehensible features in modern
prodigality is the little attention paid to the
divine regulations of economy in regard to
health.


﻿SELF SUPPORT.
					—
	THE SUBJECT before us is an important one—
if clearly considered, the one most important
of all subjects, to every reasoning being. One
can do for one’s self that which cannot be done
by another, either for good or bad; and also
that which can not be done for another, be that
other never so near and dear. Self reliance and
self labor are the only true methods by which
one can become intelligent or rich in any sense.
Men and women who have distinguished them-
selves as persons of honor and usefulness, have
carved their own roads up the rugged hill of
science and philosophy. Those who are lifted
from one notch to another and pushed along
continually by the strength and influence of
friends, unless they have used all the power and
force they are themselves possessed of, have no
just claims to wealth and reputation which the
exertions of others may sometimes enable them
to reach.   It is not safe to trust to such posses-
sions, for they will slip away, when the friends
who founded them, weary of proping them up,
or are themselves taken hence.
	No amount of gifts can aford the satisfaction
that well-earned goods will bring. It is better
to obtain less of wealth, or honor, and feel the
gratifying assurance, safety, and that self con-
fidence which inspires to renewed efforts, than
to live in dread of being cast upon one’s own
resources with no knowledge of what may
follow, no self supporting aim to pursue and no
courage to invent means of rising step by step
to higher and more worthy positions. The best
and only safe course to be taken by persons in


such conditions, is to go to work earnestly and
in as short a period as possible, in reason and
honor, work themselves into more reliable and
snbstantial conditions, where self support will
be at once necessary and easy, and suitable.


﻿HOME AFFAIRS.
				    —
	A VERY enjoyable season was spent at the
Ward party in the Thirteenth Ward Assembly
Rooms, on the evening of the 12th inst., young
and old mingling sociably together, and all
participants appearing to meet with pleasure
and satisfaction.
	THE LADIES of Utah will all be gratified to
learn from her own pen of the good health
of Miss E. R. Snow, and the satisfaction she
had realized in journeying up to the 17th ult.,
the latest date contained in her communica-
tion, which appears in this number of the
EXPONENT.
	IT IS	 worthy of note that in the splendid new
meeting house of the Sixteenth Ward in this
city, which is building under the jurisdiction
of Bishop Kesler, a room is set apart and is to
be dedicated for the express use of the Relief
Society. And the Society deserves thecompli-
ment, for their words and works show that they
are an energetic and faithful association.
	THE INTERIOR of the First Ward School
Room, to which our attention was lately
drawn, and the merits of which, with well in-
formed friends, we had the pleasure of dialogiz-
ing, is certainly worthy of recommendatory
notice. The extraordinary deftness with which
all its conveniences are arranged for the accom-
modation and satisfaction of the friends of
education who may enter it, is truly gratifying
even to casual visitors.	
	THE TERRIBLE results attendant on dissipa-
tion and a disregard for morality and temper-
ance has again been woefully depicted in this
city, in the case of Mr. and Mrs. Griffiths. In
the forenoon of Friday, the 5th inst., Griffiths,
insane from the inordinate use of spirtuous
liquors, shot and severely wounded his wife,
with the intention of killing her; afterwards
shooting himself in such a manner as to pro-
duce the desired effect. Yet, we are informed
that Mr. G. was, at one time, a steady, respected
and very reliable man.
	“THE FIRST Ward Free Library',” a hand-
some assortment of valuable works, the titles
of which, with the names of their donors are
cleverly written on neat cards which, in con-
nection with maps and charts, grace the
carefully finished walls of the room, is deserving
of special observation. And much credit is due
to the Bishop of the Ward, brother Joseph
Warburton, who, we understand, is himself
the skilfull manager of these concerns. A fine
brick building, the completion of which will
very shortly be attained by the industrious and
persevering people of the First Ward, is soon
to take the place of the small adobie one.
	JULIA HORN Burton, whose funeral took
place in this city on the 29th ult., still living in
the hearts of numberless sincere and devoted
friends, will ever be remembered as one whose
pure and lovely life was an example worthy of
imitation by the most reflective and assiduous
reformers. "As a teacher in Sabbath School,"
remarks Bishop Thomas Taylor, offering her
works as a pattern for others, "she could not be
surpassed. She so won the affections of the
children with whom she was associated that
her class often became so large that it was
necessary to divide it, while the beautiful
morals she taught both by word and actions
will never be forgotten by those Sabbath
School schollars who so loved her." It is


not difficult to see that such a teacher
of youth should be a most exemplary daughter,
sister and wife. She often said during life,
that she had not the slightest dread of death
but hoped, when she did die she might have a
nice funeral. And that desire was certainly
gratified. For on the occasion of her funeral a
very large and attentive concourse of friends
listened to most excellent and instructive
discourses from Elder John Taylor and Presi-
dent D. H. Wells, and a very impressive prayer
by Elder Joseph F. Smith.
