﻿PLEASANT COMMUNICATIONS.
					—
					FOUNTAIN GREEN,
							Nov. 26th 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	I am always pleased to welcome your
paper, and truly glad that the women of
Utah have a chance to let their voices be
heard and their views known. I am sur-
prised that there are so many sisters who do
not take the EXPONENT. I have sent some
of mine to Manti to be circulated among the
sisters there. All parents who are desirous
of placing a paper of truth in the hands of
their children should have a chance to do so,
and the EXPONENT is certainly worthy to be
called such. I had much rather my children
would read but one paper like WOMAN'S
EXPONENT than all the novel stories that
were ever manufactured.
				MARIA J. WOODWARD.
				   —
				SALEM, UTAH CO.,
						Nov. 28th 1872.
DEAR EDITOR:—
	I am a reader of the EXPONENT, one of
its first subscribers; watch for its coming
with interest, delight in its perusal, think
every one the best, desire to see it prosper
and enlarge with choice matter selected from
the many who write for its beautiful col-
umns. May God bless the WOMAN'S EXPO-
NENT and all connected with it or who fee
an interest for it.
						M. P. KILLIAN.


﻿CORRESPONDENCE OF MISS SNOW.
						—
			STEAMER “Minnesota,”
				800 miles from Liverpool,
					November 5th, 1872.
MY DEAR MISS GREENE:—
	Here I am in the midst of the ocean. We
embarked, as we had anticipated, on the 6th.
The sea was calm and sailing pleasant till
towards night, when the winds became too
strong for the sails—they were all furled,
which produced a rolling motion of the ves-
sel, conducive to sea-sickness with some of
the passengers. We have had considerable
rough weather, but now all is calm. I have
experinced a slight touch of that disquieting
malady called sea-sickness, just enough to
make my appetite inexpensive for two or
three days, and to cause a little cleansing of
the stomach for my future benefit.
	The short period of time since leaving my
Mountain Home has introduced me to much
that has been interesting; my health has
been good, and thus far my endurance of
the fatigue (if anything I have experienced
is worthy to be called such) has been beyond
my expectations. 		*	*	*	*		
	On entering Davenport I remarked that
they could not run street cars there, but I
was mistaken, for presently I saw one de-
spite the narrowness of the streets, some of
which are very handsome. Davenport is
a pleasant, stirring town, but, like all other
towns and cities I have seen since I left Salt
Lake, it seems destitute of sufficient land to
give elbow-room to the streets.


﻿We stopped two hours in Buffalo—a rainy
morning, but I wanted to see Buffalo and
took a long ramble for that purpose. From Buf-
falo we went to Niagara—crossed into Canada
on the new suspension bridge, over which
the R. R. trains pass, to view the Falls; and
after witnessing those scenes of nature in
her display of the grand and beautiful, we
recrossed on the old bridge. From descrip-
tions by various sight-seers, my imagination
had been whetted to an appreciative idea, so
far as the majestic grandeur of the scenery is
concerned; but its beauty—so chaste so in-
describably delicate, I had not anticipated.
In fact, without actual, personal view, I
never could have comprehended such a
harmonious combination of power and deli-
cacy. Imagine for a moment a body of
water falling in a magnificent volume, to a
great distance and forming in its descent a
mantle of pure, sparkling white, of ever
changing folds, over a robe of the most
delicate shade of green that either the dye
of art or of nature can produce—at times the
green appearing as if approaching the sur-
face—at other times seen obscurely—the
chaste and beautiful snowy white ever pre-
ponderating; while from the depths below,
springs are constantly ascending, apparently
as far as the eye can reach, and then drop-
ping in heavy mists on the ever humid
surroundings. But after so many able pens
have descanted upon this subject, it seems
like folly for mine to attempt it. I leave it,
after drawing the conclusion that Mark
Twain was induced to speak indifferently
respecting this celebrated curiosity, lest he
should be suspected of doing or thinking for
once like somebody else.
	As yet I have had but little opportunity
for writing, the passage being rough makes
it difficult.
						E. R. SNOW.
	P. S. Still pleasant, and all right.
Nov. 17th, 180 miles from Queenstown.
								E. R. S.


﻿[FOR the EXPONENT.
			TEMPERANCE.
				  —
	I know that many readers will glance
with almost contempt at this heading. Yet,
it is the very essence of happiness, and is
but very little understood by three-fourths
of the human family. Even those who
speak and write in behalf of temperance,
very many of them, abuse it shockingly.
The strictly temperate person will live to be
old. This is a bold assertion but the truth of
it can be proven any, or every day. Tem-
perance enters into the smallest minutiae of
life, and beautiful indeed are the lives of
the strictly temperate. They may not have
genius or brilliant talent. But if by circum-
stances placed side by side with, or in
antagonism with Genius or Talent, for any
considerable length of time, Temperance
will be sure to win and wear the laurel
wreath of fame. Washington was temperate
in all things, if what we read of him be
true. Does history furnish a more beauti-
ful character or one more venerated? He
conquered more by the wisdom of his
counsels than by his sword. His mind, being
calm and clear was able to receive and retain
the most perfect impressions of what would
be best for the Country he loved so well!
And those impressions he carried into prac-
tice as perfectly as it was possible for fallen
nature to do, which was the foundation of
the famous Constitution under which all
should be free. Contrast with the history of
his life and death that of Napoleon Bonapart.
Bonapart had genius and talent unbounded,
could dazzle the minds of nations; could as


it where, hold nations in his hands. Yet
he lost all, and a man very much his inferior
was his conqueror.
	Wellington was temperate; calmly he
watched his brilliant antagonist, moving his
men from place to place, avoiding collissions
as often as possible. Finally he conquered
Bonapart, came out of the strife and lived to
be a very old man. Wellington, although
very wealthy, lived exceedingly plain; he
slept on a camp bedstead and was in no way
given to luxurious living.   It is the lack of
temperance that makes human beings unhap-
py. Think of the most temperate people with
whom you have been acquainted and do you
not know they have made you admire them
whether you would or not? They, command
respect without asking for it, by the force of
their example. In history we find but few
Washingtons, whose careers’ have been more
beautiful at the close than at the beginning.
	But let us turn to the history of the
development of this Territory. Temperance
has been the watchword of President Young,
and those associated with him. In all things
they advise to take things calmly. In the
late crusade did not President Young show
the most-perfect moderation? Calmly he
has led the people of this Territory, advanc-
ing little by little, growing greater and
greater. And if we, as a people, will be taught
by those whose right it is to teach, we must
become the greatest people on the face of
the globe. And if we will be temperate in
all things our lives will be beautiful and our
deaths but triumphs.


﻿MISTAKES.
					—
	Which should be first with us, our own
private interests, or the common cause of
humanity? When viewed in a true light,
neither need be neglected for the sake of
the other; for they are so closely inter-
woven that they both terminate in the same
thing. But unless we are vigilant and ever
on the alert we are apt to neglect the very
duty we should perform, to accomplish some-
thing else of less import, but which, at the
moment appears to be of greater magnitude.
	A short time ago a sister came to me to 
tell why she would not be able to attend
the Relief Society meeting that afternoon.
She must go up town for some cause, and
there were quite a number of little “every
day” concerns that she must look after, in
consequence of which it would be impossible
for her to be present at the meeting.   But 
not one of the hindrances which she related
was of really grave importance, or anything 
that could not be performed at another time
without danger of loss or inconvenience.
“And all this must be done, and only the
meeting neglected,” I remarked; that which
should be a first consideration is placed at
the last and”—“Say no more,” interrupted
my friend, “I can go as well as not, and I
will.” Thus we often mistake the least for
the greatest objects of our present mission.
								  M. S.
	Salt Lake City, Nov. 28th, 1872.


THE WORLD'S Fair, at Vienna is to have,
as one of its grand attractions, a huge “baby
show.” Baron Von Schwartzenboren was
the originator of the idea. Many fashionable
mothers will take a peep in the nursery at
hearing of this who have never-thought of
troubling themselves about it or its puerile
occupants for months before. But their pale,
pent-up, misused babies will not take the
prize; unless rosy, robust children, belong-
ing to mothers of sense, who allow them
to breathe the fresh, pure air, play in the
dirt and let the sun shine on them, are kept
at home.

given before a large and enthusiastic audience
at Tremont Temple, Boston.

having survived the milking of fifteen gen-
erations of cows, has died at the age of one
hundred and thirty-one years.
	IT will be interesting to the ladies to
know that the ex-empress Eugene now
dresses almost entirely in red and black, and
that her beautiful hair is beginning to show
threads of silver.
	THE death is announced of Donna Juana
Meria do los Delores de Leon, widow of Sir
Harry George Wakelyn Smith, K. C. B.,
the first baronet who, after distinguished
military service was appointed Governor of
the Cape of Good Hope in 1847.
	PRINCESS Louise has received her first
welcome to Balmoral as the Marchioness of
Lorn, having been escorted with the Mar-
quis from the lodge to the castle by a
torchlight procession of servants and ten-
antry of the estate.
	MILLE. Eardon, a dressmaker, who recently
tried to drown herself in the Siene, exclamed:
“We have no liberties!”   When she was
pulled out, instead of thaking her pre-
servers, she cried out: “They don’t even
allow us to drown ourselves.”
	A LADY, who says that her opinion is
based on close observance, says that men,
as a rule, regard their wives as angels for
just two months—namely, a month before
marrying her and a month after burying
her.
	A SITUATION-seeking young lady noticed
an advertisement for one to do light house-
keeping. She wrote immediately to the
advertiser, asking where the lighthouse
was, and if there was any way of getting to
the shore on Sundays.
	MRS. D. Clair R. D. Everse Spettigne, of
Boston, spoke in Washingford, Vt. recently
on “Love and Marriage.” The lecture was
set ten minutes earlier than the usual hour
in order to give the gentleman who intro-
duced the lecturer plenty of time to call off
her name.
	MATILDA Phillips, sister of Adelaide Phil-
lips, the singer, pluckily rescued a young 
American, who was drowning at Genoe, a
few weeks ago, reaching him just as he was
sinking for the third time, and helping him
to land, while several gentlemen stood by
and made no attempt to aid her—although,
as she naively added, in telling the adven-
ture, “they were all kind enough after he
was landed.”
	A STRONG minded woman in Detroit
made the following gentle reply to a politi-
cian who had called at her house to get her
husband to go to the polls to vote: “No,
sir, he can’t go. He’s washing now, and
he’s got to iron to-morrow, and if he wasn’t
doing anything he couldn’t go. I run this
'ere house, I do, and if any one votes it'll be
this same Mary Jane.”


WHAT MAKES A LADY.
				—
	The various interpretations which have
been given to the word “lady,” provoke a
little thought on the subject. 
	The uneducated ascribe the term to those
who are not compelled to labor for support,
and who know of no better way to spend the
hours than in idle luxury or self gratification;
when, in truth, they may be the very ones
who, above all others, foil to exhibit the
least indications of a true lady. They scoff
at honest toilers, and turn disdainfully away
from those on whom they are dependent for
the food they eat, and the fine clothes in
which they delight to array themselves.
	In the estimation of some it is only neces-
sary to dwell in a large and elegant house,
or be able to ride out on pleasant days in a
carriage, in order to be a lady.
	“Oh! she is a lady now,” is often sarcasti-
cally given as a reason why old acquaintances
are allowed to pass unnoticed. Thus often
are old friendships broken, because, on one
side, persons having been raised from
obscurity and poverty, to a condition of
greater material comfort, which naturally
gives them an air of dignity, are misjudged
by the friends of other days, as proud, when
every reflecting woman must admit that
the fault is frequently on the other side.
How often is there in this way an impassable
gulf between those who were once on terms
of pleasant intimacy! Conscious of inferiority
in many respects, the less fortunate shrink
from the customary salutation, and the other,
surprised by the unusual coldness, without
thinking what the cause may be, fails to
make the necessary advances. In conse-
quence, bitter feelings of envy and jealousy
are engendered, and they who are already
destitute of sufficient selfrespect, sink still
lower in the esteem of the others, even
while they assume an air of independence
and contemptuous forgetfulness of their
former acquaintances.
	It is to be regretted that the general ten-
dency is to become vain of newly-acquired
possessions, and that many forget early
poverty. But there are more exceptions to
the rule than we sometimes think. Worldy
prosperity, indeed, is not necessarily incom-
patible with true nobility of character; on
the contrary, is often most helpful in the
exercise of generous purposes. The true
lady realizes this, and governs herself
accordingly. Is she rich? She recognizes
real worth in every woman who manifests
it, and endeavors to maintain a kindly atti-
tude towards all. Does she happen to be
poor? She will not envy her neighbor who
is blessed with more comfortable surround-
ings, but will “rejoice for her sake,” and
strive to meet her with generous sympathy.
She is well aware that silks and laces do not
make the lady, be they never so costly, and
knows that she is not herself exempt from
fulfilling the requirements in a lady. *	*
A wife will be a better wife, a mother more
a mother, if intellectually her husband’s
equal.	*	*	*	*								
Ladies may be found among women 	*	*
who have had but few educational advan-
tages. 	*	*	*	*	*			
	We affirm that she who cultivates virtue,
and dares to exercise all her mental powers,
from the very symmetry of her being, is a
“lady,” or better still, a “noble woman.”—
“Woman’s Journal.”


AMONG the ancients, when persons were
newly married, they put a yoke upon their
necks, or chains upon their arms, to show
that they were to be one, closely united,
and pulling equally together in all the con-
cerns of life.


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
				—

residence at Saratoga,  A  dock 600 feet
long is to be constructed, fronting the house
and skirting the lake shore.
	JUDGE Ingraham, of New York, decides
that a person’s watch comes under the head
of necessary articles and connot be taken by
by creditors.
	PROFESSOR Baird, of the Smithsonian In-
stitute, has returned and commenced work
on his report to Congress in relation to the
food fisheries on the Atlantic coast.
	ADVICE from Paris say that two-thirds of
the priests in Paris are ready to follow 
Hyacinth’s example—as soon as they can
find the essential American widows with 
$75,000 a piece.
	RECENT submarine investigations, carried 
on under the direction of the British Associ-
ation of Science, have demonstrated the fact 
that the sea is affected by the heat of the
sun only to the depth of one hundred and 
twenty feet.
	EXTRAORDINARY EYE-SIGHT.—The “Ex-
aminer” favor the building of a telescope
of extraordinary power to bring Mars with-
in 4,000 miles, and as the works of man,
such as roads and cities, might be seen if
Mars is inhabited.	
	A VIGNETTE of the late Thaddeus Stevens, 
the “Great Commoner” of Pennsylvania has
just been completed at the Bureau of Engrav-
ing and Printing in the Treasuary Depart-
ment, and will be used upon either the 
national currency or revenue stamps. The
likeness is perfect, and the work is pro-
nounced as one of the finest pieces of en-
graving ever executed. 
	SOME time ago, M. Chevreul, having acci-
dentally taken up a forgotten dish in which
there was a hardened saline substance, found
to his surprise that the surface was inter-
rupted by what appeared to be a soft
vegetation. The little moisture contained
in the salt in a vicious state at the bottom
had filtered through, partly melting the 
adjacent parts, which had led to their form-
ing protruding crystals; these by capillary
attraction, had drawn up more moisture,
followed by a similar effect, so that in course 
of time a sort of shrub, composed of crystals,
had grown up. At a late sitting of the
French Academy, the learned gentleman 
returned to the subject, and produced a
watch glass on which a new arborescent
matter has been formed of a vitreous appear-
ance, and of the thickness of only one or
two milimetres, while the “shrub” which
had grown out of it was nearly two centi-
metres in altitude, and of an average
thickness of one centimetre, but instead of 
being transparent it was opaque. All round
there was on the surface nine small
“pimples,” which were likewise without 
transparency. In another watch glass there
was a similar substance which had assumed
the appearance of satin by the formation of
minute crystals on the surface. Reasoniag
on this matter, Mr. Chevreul admitted that
the vitreous substances contained moisture 
in the first two specimens, but added that 
the third which, presented a superficial crys- 
talization, must have had much less. At all
events, this was a new proof of the molecular 
motion which could take place even in solid
matter. And to the same cause must be
attributed the change of color which is ob- 
served in stained glass when it is exposed
for many years to the action of the sun.
Might not this explain the mellowness of 
tint we admire in old stained glass, and 
which our own artists are unable to imitate?
—“Galignani’s Messenger."


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿EXTRAVAGANCE AND MARRIAGE.
				   —
	Those who like, with Mr. Buckle, to
collate social phenomena and draw general
deductions from them, may just now gather
excellent material for the process. As an
example, we find the London journals dis-
cussing with earnestness the prodigious
cost of beef, bread, coal and female attire;
and looking at the law reports in some of
the papers, we find a most unusual number
of cases of breaches of promise in which the
man is the defendant. The significance of
this is probably beyond dispute. Among
a hundred men who have engaged them-
selves, a certain percentage of cautious
natures will be found who, on the contem-
plated step becoming imminent, are more
and more disturbed by the thought of its
pecuniary difficulties and responsibilities.
These frightened swains retreat, and a cer-
tain proportion—probably a small one—of
the outraged damsels seek such solace as the
law will accord them.
	There can be no question that a high cost
of living discourages marriage, or that the
discouragement of marriage is hurtful to
the state; but it is easier to point out the evil
than the remedy. We cannot put down
the price of butcher’s meat by special legis-
lation, or resort to sumptuary laws to regu-
late how many yards of tulle, or lace, or silk
a woman may be permitted to wear. Yet it
is true that the absence of all restraint in
these matters, and the excess threatened in
them, is equivalent to setting a premium on
immorality and a tax on wholesome domestic
life. The sight of men straining every
nerve to support an expensive family, and
often going behind hand in the struggle and
losing all hope and faith in the future,
is surely no strong incentive to part with
the immunities of celibacy. It is a sepctacle
which might be supposed to touch the heart
of any woman—but it does not. Doubt-
less there are circumstances attendant on
modern civilization, other than the expen-
siveness of living, to discourage marriage.
Many, however, who profess to have made a
close study of the subject pronounce this
consideration the paramount one in London
and it is fast becoming so in New York.
The “Daily News” of the former city, de-
clares that the proportion of actions for
breach of promise increases there daily, and
that a perusal of the evidence shows that
dread of the expense has usually led to the
defendant’s breach of faith. It also signifi-
cantly observes that a study of the cases
tried shows “that there are women quite
equal to laying a plot for a verdict of big
damages after drawing out the first letter of
endearment from their future victim.”
There seems to be less delicacy, less dread
of publicity on the part of forsaken damsels
than of yore, and some indeed in late cases
have behaved as if they thought that next
to being married, an explicit promulgation
of the fact that somebody at some time has
wished to marry them is a thing to be
accounted desirable.
	The journal we have quoted calls attention to
one odd feature which it would puzzle the au-
thor of the History of Civilization himself to
account for. This is that the breach of promise
epidemic, as raging of late in England, takes
the form of a run upon special classes of per-
sons. Thus, within three weeks of the time
its article was published, the "News" says
that more than twenty farmers have been
mulcted for faithlessness to their sentimental
engagements. At a period more remote,
the linen drapers came in for their share of
punishment—young ladies employed in
their shops  being usually the persons
aggrieved. Presently, no doubt, we shall
hear of a rush being made for the doctors
and—leaving out, of course, the vast body of
poor curates—a determined charge may be
expected on the ranks of the clergy. The
soldiers, whose profession it is, as General
Damas observes, “to make widows, not
wives,” will then come in for their share of
expiation, the sailors may follow, and so on,
until the whole round of society receives its
due share of attention. After this experi-
ence, extreme weariness will take the place,
we may be sure, of the easy confidence with
which men flirted and committed themselves
in the olden time, and ladies with a taste for
admiration will be put on lenten fare as a
vicarious atonement for the wounded hearts
of their litigious sisters.
	In all seriousness, the omens suggested
by these numerous suits for breach of prom-
ise form a subject for deep and careful
reflection. There are by no means so many
cases here, where the facilities for divorces
are greater; but the fear of marriage respon-
sibilities—the feeling that men of a certain
social position have no business with the
luxury of a wife unless possessed of a hand-
some competency—is unmistakably gain-
ing ground among us, and possibly the
practical objection set up of late by so many
in England may likewise become prevalent
in America. The subject is well worth the
best thought of students in social science,
and, indeed, of all right-minded people who
can make use of a new and powerful argu-
ment against that extravagance in modern
living which is confined neither to the Old
world nor the New.—New York “Times.”


﻿BOOKS AND CONVERSATION.
				—
	If half the time which is spent in idle
gossip and talking fashionable nonsense,
was used in gathering grains of gold, in
storing the mind with a certitude of things
as they are and have been and are likely to
become, very much more real enjoyment
than is experienced would be realized by
many. In selecting reading matter, some-
thing that will be beneficial in some form
should always be chosen; something that
will serve to enliven and enlighten the
powers of intellect. Not always the same
subjects, but a diversity of interesting and
instructive themes. And in like manner
should our associations with friends be con- 
ducted. And yet, often will a word or
sentence be dropped in light conversation
which will prove a coagent to numerous
brilliant and delightful strains of thought
which, but for the accidental stroke upon
the keynote, might long have slumbered
or never have been born. And in reading
simple anecdotes, people will frequently
happen upon some little dint of inspiration
which will prove the foundation to a great
and sublime infusion of meditations, and
may be given to the world in glowing rich-
ness and beautiful originality.
								L.


﻿MISUNDERSTANDING.
	“None are so blind ast hose who will not
aee.” How some people love to misconstrue
the words and motives of others! Why
can’t they have sense enough to realize
that they, like others, and others like them-
selves, are mortal beings, and all alike are
subject to mortal weaknesses and mortal
woes? I have but little charity to extend to
those extraordinary “good” people who
watch for iniquity in others and seek to
hide their own baseness under a cloak, so
black as to deceive almost themselves, and
make them fancy they are pure and


truthful when they are nothing but budgets
of the vilest cant and hypocracy! Only imag-
ine a woman who, over such a prayer as the
following, for an unfortunate fellow creature
who had become so desparate as to commit
suicide, and her friends, could write dark
misrepresentations concerning the teachings
of a man proven to be a firm maintainer of
truth, integrity, virtue and honesty! “May
the tender Father of us all, who saw all the
agony of her soul, gather and enfold her in
His merciful arms, and also extend to the
aged mother and sister, far away on the
shore of Michigan’s mighty lake, His tender
consolation in this hour of deepest sorrow.”
And the falsehoods told and written by
these foolhardy-people, in this instance—are
believed by many, in preference to the
truth! And yet we must not be annoyed,
because that is what they would like to
make us do and we must disappoint
them. Well!
							FRANK.

	LORD BACON says, “But little do men per-
ceive what solitude is, and how far it
extendeth; for a crowd is not company, and
faces are but a gallery of pictures, and talk
but a tinkling cymbal, where there is no
love.”
	WILL THE EARTH BECOME A SUN SPOT?
—Mr. R. Holms sends a paper intended to
prove that the earth is gradually solidify-
ing, and losing its acqueous vapor; also, that
it is steadily diminishing its distance from
the sun, as shown by the gradual shorten-
ing of the year or annual revolution of the
earth; also maintaining that the “solar spots”
are not excavations, nor breaks in the solar
atmosphere, but that they are matter such
as forms the planets in a state of combustion,
whose emissions supply the solar system
with heat, light and electricity. The planet-
ary, cometary and nebular systems are
considered in this view. We receive
occasional communications of this kind,
which we are unable to publish because they
deal with what must yet be questions of-
theory and speculation, rather than ascer-
tained facts of science.—“English Paper.”
	THEY, who sit in the light of so many
brilliant windows little know, perhaps, how
much labor goes; to the making up of them”.
They are first designed, then stained in
bits; their colors are burned in separately,
—first the blue, then the red—and each re-
quires three successive heatings in the oven.
They must be pieced into a whole, with
leadings connecting the pieces, then swung
into a large window frame to be examined.
If any defect be discovered, the part or parts
must be burned again in the oven, and then
the whole must be burned together. The
scene in Glasmalarie is remarkable enough.
Here is a man engaged upon a martyr’s
toes, while in another room the eyes of the
same saint cast upon you the last look of de-
spair as they are shoved into a furnace
heated seven-fold—just as if Herr Fortner,
who now presides, I believe, were another
Diocletian.
	A week later you will see the martyr,
purified by his ordeal, smiling down a whole
rainbow upon the workmen in their shirt
sleeves, in an apartment of chalk, old
planks, coke and all sorts of odds and ends.
These fellows are no respecters of persons,
either, Gods and saints, St. George’s dragon
and the chamois destined for some noble-
man’s hunting box, are cast together into
the oven and shine together on the walls.

	WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

	A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL.

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.
	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.
	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves?“ It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!”
	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.
	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.
	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.
	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.
	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.

	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:	
1 Copy one year,	    $2.00	1 Copy six months,    $1.00 
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months 9.00

	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting 
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


Woman's Exponent.
﻿Vol. I. 	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JANUARY 1, 1872.  No. 15.


﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.
			     ——
	Heavy floods in Cambridge and vicinity,
England.
	Lady notaries public are the latest
novelty in official life. Terre Haute, Indi-
ana, has got one.
	Murderers and convicts, who have
escaped the penalty of offended laws, con-
tinue to court notoriety, now, as lecturers.
	A singular death occurred in Vernon,
Iowa, a few days ago. A child fell and hurt
its gums, when bleeding commenced at the
place injured, and the child died of it.
	A religion without bigotry, which in-
culcates love, truth and mercy, and whose
believers practice these virtues, is sadly
needed throughout the Eastern States.
	People find it frequently more conve-
nient to see and act wrong than right,
because, while there are numerous wrong
ways there is but one right way to look at
or perform anything.
	A first class mathematician might find
considerable of a problem, and spend some
time in recconing the number and cost of
the disasterous fires which have occurred
within the United States, since the Boston
conflagration.
	A hint to young ladies who design enter-
ing into the house-keeping business might
be gleaned from the example of a wedding
which lately took place, at whcih each guest
paid 25 cents admittance, which was handed
over to the bride as a little start in the
way of “setting up shop.”
	Among the last sayings of Horace
Greeley was “The country is gone; the
Tribune is gone; and I am gone!” While
his last words were: “I know that my Re-
deemer liveth!” These will do to remember
and jot down when making memoranda of
the dying words of eminent men.
	The British Embassador at St. Peters-
burg is reported to have warned Prince
Gortchakoff that if Russian-troops enter the
countries between Khiva and Afghanistan,
England will intervene in support of Afghan-
istan. If this statement be true, a quarrel
between England and Russia, may next be
apprehended.
	ONE OF the questions agitating the country
just now is, whether the people of Louisiana
will control the affairs of that State; or
whether they will be governed by a set of
“carpet-baggers” who have no special inter-
est in the State except to rob it.  The
question is important enough for considera-
tion.
	In the new public school building, No. 5,
Jersey City, New York, which was partially
destroyed by fire on the morning of the 23
of December, were about one hundred and
fifty young ladies, all of whom escaped un-
harmed; and likewise members of the Board
of Education which was in session at the
time. With such good fortune, the loss of
$60,000 need not be complained of.
	A little over a week ago two compositors
skated down the Missouri river from Sioux
City to Omaha, over two hunnred miles;
being some two days making the trip;
[new colum]
which was more fool-hardy than sensible.
Frozen ears for both, with a frozen cheek and
heel for one of them, were the result. If
there is any daring, but foolish thing to be
done, American boys are ready to undertake
it.
	A great curiosity in the form of a stone
has been found by Mr. A. M. Stewart, a
locator of the gravel deposits on the summit
of the Sierra Nevada mountains, in Califor-
nia. This stone is said to be in shape like a
magnifying, glass thick in the centre, and so
hard that the hardest of files tried upon it
produces not even a scratch. Viewed through
a powerful magnifying glass, it shows the
most wonderful rays of light. The owner
believes it to be a rare gem, unknown to
the lapidaries.
	Some time ago, “Woodhull & Claflin’s
Weekly” charged Mrs, Theodore Tilton
with marital infidelity, and Rev. Henry
Ward Beecher as the other guilty party in
the alleged criminality. Mr. Beecher paid
no attention to the charge, nor did Mr. Tilton
up to the present. Now, it is said, he will
publish a denial, although it comes at a
very late hour. Evidently New York
“Society" must have some very hideous-
looking “skeletons” in its cupboards, if they
were exposed to view.
	Among the most terrible disasters of late
occurrence, is recorded the railroad accident
near Carry, Pennsylvania, on the afternoon
of December 24th by which the train, con-
sisting of one passenger car and one baggage
car, the engine and tender, was thrown off
the track; falling a distance of over twenty
feet; the cars were turned directly bottomside
upward, caught fire, and through the fearful
crushing and burning, some twenty souls
were immediately ushered into eternity and
about as many more were dreadfully injured.
But one person out of forty-six who were
on the train, is known to have escaped un-
harmed.
	About three hundred men, women
and children were assembled in the recep-
tion room, up stairs, of the Newberry Baptist
Church, Pennsylvania, on Christmas day,
having a Sunday School Christmas Celebra-
tion; when the floor gave way and the mass
of human beings were precipitated to the
floor beneath. Fourteen were killed out-
right and a large number, more or less
injured. This is one of the most heartrend-
ing occurrences reported of the late holiday;
and should give a text to the bigoted of
other denominations, who forget what the
Savior said concerning the falling of the
tower of Siloam.	
	Train, whose given name is George
Francis, has not reached the White House,
but he has the inside of a New York prison,
charged with publishing an obscene periodi-
cal. But it was not the obscenity in Train’s
publication that roused virtuous New York,
where the most degradingly disgraceful
publications of the age are circulated by
wholesale and are on the stand of almost
every newsvender. It was because he pub-
lished statements concerning prominent
New Yorkers which were hard to meet and
yet could not be passed over in silence. If
New York will rid itself of obscene publica-
[new column]
tions, professional murderers who kill life
ere it has passed from its embryotic condi-
tion, and of its public destroyers of chastity
and innocence, it will be in a better condition
to pronounce judgment upon Train and his
publications than it is at present.
	The Christmas season just passed was
prolific of horrors in the United States.
Destructive fires occurred in many parts of
the country; the upper floor of a Pennsylvania
Baptist church fell in, killing fourteen
persons and injuring nearly fifty more, a
number of them severely; a number of rail-
road accidents occurred, by one of which
some twenty persons were literally roasted
alive through the burning of an overturned
car on which they were, and in addition to
these and other horrors, one of the most
violent snow-storms, accompanied with in-
tense cold, swept over the Eastern, Middle
and Southern States, that has been known
for seventeen years. These are but signs of
the times on which the wise will ponder;
while the unbelieving and the foolish will
not heed the warnings given of an approach-
ing consummation in fulfillment of prophecy.
	The Credit Mobilier is exciting consider-
able interest at present. Speaker Blaine
demanded an investigation into the
charges made against a number of
the members and ex-members of Congress—
Senators and Representatives; the principal
charge being that certain members, Blaine
among the number, were bribed in 1864 by
the Union Pacific Railroad Company’s Credit
Mobilier, to support the measure of that
corporation and vote for the amendments of
1862, to increase the land grant and ex-
change the Government’s first mortgage
on the road for a second one, the first being
allowed to the company. The Chicago
“Evening Post” has the following hit con-
cerning the affair: “Enough has been devel-
oped in the Credit Mobilier examination
to make it desirable to develop more. We
trust the full-sized cat will be let out of the
bag, no matter what Senator she bites, or
what member she scratches, or what editor
she spits upon. So interesting an animal
cannot get her back up too soon.”
	Some time ago Professor Tyndall pro-
posed to test the efficacy of prayer as a
healing medium, by having prayer offered
up in certain wards of an hospital, while
other wards were to be left prayerless; and
if the patients in the wards where prayer
was offered would be restored to health more
quickly than those in the other wards, then
prayer was to be accepted as efficacious in
the healing of dseases. It is needless to
say that Professor Tyndall is an infidel. A
conference of Methodist preachers consid-
ered his proposition, and after some discus-
sion decided that it was blasphemous. Now,
as the Methodist preachers in question,
with the entire denomination, and the
Christian sects generally, repudiate the gift
of healing, or the scriptural mode of healing
the sick by faith and prayer, the question
is an open one, wherein they differ from or
are better than Professor Tyndall? And
whether is the infidelity of the Professor or
the preachers worse, seeing the former
avows his unbelief in the Bible and the
latter profess belief in it?


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿CROSSING THE ATLANTIC.

Written at sea by Miss Eliza R. Snow, Dedicated to the
				Ladies of Utah.

We’re on the ‘'Minnesota."
	A ship of “Guion Line,"
Which boasts her Captain Morgan,
	The gen’rous staunch and kind.

Amid the heaving waters
	That form the liquid plain;
"With four and twenty draft feet
	The steamer ploughs the main.

I'm gazing on the ocean.
	As on the deck I stand,
And feel the cooling breezes,
	With which the sails are fanned.

By sunlight, star and moonlight,
	And tranquil evening shade,
The ever varying featu es
	Of ocean I’ve surveyed-

At times with restless motion,
	As if her spirit grieves—
As tho' her breast were paining,
	Her mighty bosom heaves.

And then vast undulations,
	Like rolling prairies spread;
With wave on wave dissolving,
	With tumbling, dashing tread.

Upon the deep, dark billows,
	Broad, foaming white caps rise;
And sprays in dazzling beauty,
	Shoot upward to the skies.

'Tis now a plain, smooth surface,
	As tho’ in cozy sleep,
Were wrapped each wave and billow
	Upon the briny deep.

But hark! The Captain orders 
	The furling every sail;
Storm-clouds and headwinds rising
	Portend a coming gale,

Anon all Neptune’s furies
	Are on the Steamer’s path;
We mount the deck to witness 
	The ocean in its wrath.

The scene! What pen can write it?
	What pencil’s art could show
The wild, terrific grandeur
	Which reigns around us now?

The waving, surging waters,
	Like battle armor clash;
Tumultuous waves upheaving
	With foaming fury dash.

The steamer mounts the billows,
	Then dips the space below:
And bravely presses onward
	Tho’ reeling to and fro.

We’re sailing on the ocean
	With wind and sail and steam;
Where views of “terra firma”
	Are like the poet’s dream.

The God who made the waters—
	Who made the solid lands,
Is ours—our Great Protector;
	Our life is in His hands.

Subservient to His counsel—
	Confiding in His care—
Directed by His wisdom.
	There’s safety everywhere.


﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
			  —
	EPHRAIM CITY, Dec. 10th, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	At our last monthly meeting, by common
consent, in compliance with Sister Snow’s
suggestion, the word “Female” was dropped
from the name of our Society. We had an
excellent meeting, which indeed we always
do have; our President, Sister S. A. Peter-
son urged the subject of education very
strongly upon the minds of the sisters. She
also gave much good instruction upon the
Word of Wisdom. The Ladies’ Hall, a very
nice two story rock building, is ready for
the shingles; the lower story will be used
for a Co-operative Store.
	AGNESS S. ARMSTRONG, Secretary.
[new column]
	Wanship, Summit co.,
			December 10th, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	We are a little band of sisters here, but
though among the least, we do not wish to
be the last in any good word or work.
Although but few in number we feel united
and earnest in our endeavors to do all the
good we can, both by precept and example,
as well as in relieving the needy and com-
forting the distressed. At our last meeting
five new members were voted in. At a re-
cent meeting it was voted unanimously to
drop the word “Female” from the name of
the Society, in accordance with the expressed
wishes of Sister E. R. Snow.
	We value your paper highly and hope it
will continue to vindicate the cause of
“Woman’s Rights” in the truest sense of
the word, and we wish you prosperity and
happiness in your enterprise.
	MRS. MARY REYNOLDS President.
	SARAH S. RICHARDS
	ANN BRIZZEE, Counselors
	SUSAN S. RICHARDS, Secretary.
			   —
	LOGAN, Dec. 6th, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:--
	At our monthly meeting, held in the hall,
November 4th, the word “Female” was
dropped from the name of our Society by a
unanimous vote, according to the suggestion
made by Sister E. R. Snow. President
Mrs. Maughan imparted to us many good
instructions which she had received from
Sister Snow, making our meeting interest-
ing as well as beneficial. Our meetings are
not largely attended but there are always
some from each ward, so that reports are
made of the condition of the poor and none
are allowed to suffer for the necessaries of
life. Some of the wards hold their sewing
meetings regularly. We have not much
means on hand as we always manage to dis-
tribute our quilts and other articles as fast
as we get them made. We have one hun-
dred dollars in the Co-operative, Store the
interest of which helps us a little. At our
last monthly meeting, (the 2nd inst.) the
sisters expressed their determination to
renew their diligence.
	Accept my good wishes for the success of
your interesting paper, which I think is
highly prized by many of our Society.
				L. B. Benson, Secretary.
	
	CEDAR CITY, Iron Co., U. T.
	December 12 th, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	Our Society was organized the 4th of
June 1868, and since that time has disbursed
nearly $400 for the benefit of the poor, be-
sides donating liberally for the building of a
Temple in St. George and other laudable
undertakings. We have $50 in the Wash-
ington Woolen Factory; $50 in our Co-op.
Store and $50 in the Co-op. sheep herd.
	The sisters with their President—Mrs.
Rachel Whittaker, and her Counselors Mrs.
Annabella Haight and Mrs. Jane Birkbeck
all feel well and willing to assist in every-
thing that is required of them to do good.
At a late meeting, it was moved and unani-
mously carried that the word “Female” be
dropped from the name of the Society.
				ELLEN LUNT, Secretary.

				MENDON, Cache Co.,
					December 8th, 1872

	I forward a brief report to you of our last
meeting, held on the 5th inst. at which
a vote was taken to omit the word “Female”
from the name of the Society, as suggested,
which, was unanimous. Our Society is in a
[new column]
prosperous condition; we have given to the

treasury.

		HEBER CITY, Wasatch Co.,
			     December 15th, 1872.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	The Relief Society of this place was organ-
ized on the 7th day of June, 1869, under the
supervision of Bishop Abraham Hatch. The
following named ladies were chosen as
officers: Mrs. Margaret Muir President;
Mrs. Ann Murdock and Mrs. Mary McMil-
len Counselors; Mrs. Ann Harvey Secretary
and Mrs. Emily Harvey Treasurer.
	At a meeting held the 7th of November
1872, presided over by President Mrs. Mar-
garet Muir, a unanimous vote was taken to
omit, hereafter, the inappropriate word
“Female” in connection with the name of
the Society. The sisters bore testimony to
the truth of the principles of the Gospel
with which we are associated, and Father
Thomson, being present, gave us some im-
pressive remarks relative to tithing. Meet-
ing adjourned for one month and closed with
singing and prayer.
				    ELLEN LEE,
				    MELISSA REYNOLDS.
				   —
			RIVERDALE Weber Co.,
				December 16, 1872.
EDITOR EXPONENT:—
	President F. D. Richards and Bishop L. J.
Herrick held a meeting at this place on the 5th
inst. The school house was well filled and,
among other instructions, President Richards
recommended the organization of a Relief
Society at Riverdale. And calling for an
expression of the feelings of the congrega-
tion upon the subject, a unanimous vote was
cast in favor of a branch Society being
organized. The following named sisters
were then chosen and ordained to fill the
first offices belonging to the Society; Mrs.
Martha Bingham. President; Mrs. Ednah
Stimpson and Mrs. Nancy Bybee Counselors.
As the day was far spent it was decided
that the sisters meet on the following
Wednesday, to complete the organization.
Accordingly, a meeting was held on
Wednesday December 11th, 1872, at which,
President Mrs. M. Bingham presided. Af-
ter singing and prayer, the following named
sisters were chosen and unanimously sus-
tained to fill the under-named offices: Mrs.
Hannah A. Child Secretary; Mrs. Dye,
Treasurer; and sisters Jennette Mitchell,
Martha A. Fife, Mary M. Child, Catharine
Henchliff, Mary Stimpson and Dell Straton
for Visiting Committee.
	Meeting adjourned, prayer by Mrs. Stimp-
son.
				H. L. Child Secretary.

	The subject of Physiology was continued
at the General Retrenchment meeting, held
in the 14 Ward, on Saturday, December 21st
1872. President Mrs. L. Horn being unvoid-
ably absent, the meeting was presided over
by Counselor Mrs. Z. D. Young, who, after
the usual ceremonies, made a few very appro-
priate remarks, followed by Mrs. Margaret P.
Young, Counselor Mrs. Woodruff and others:
Mrs. W. East, believed that, in, as, much
as the wives and daughters of the Saints
had not hitherto been permitted to learn
the nature of the human system and to study
the laws that govern the same, on account
of the labors and duties which they have
been obliged to perform, in connection with
the brethren, for the sustenance of their


families, that if they humble themselves be-
fore the Lord and ask for wisdom concerning
these things that light will be given them.
The Lord is able to quicken our understand-
ings and to teach us those things which we
ought to comprehend for the benefit of our-
selves and our descendants.
	Counselor Mrs. Sararh M. Kimball dwelt
beautifully upon the structure of the spiritual
and physical faculties of human nature. In
childhood and extreme youth, she said, we
enjoy most that which we best comprehend;
the faculties of taste and smelling are, per-
haps, as finely developed in a young child
as in an adult, while other faculties of per-
ception yet remain quite dormant. As we
grow older, the physical form develops, and
so, also, the mental powers expand, ever
reaching forth for something yet beyond
the present. And the most delightful faculty
pertaining to mortality, or immortality, is
faith, that faith which gives us foretaste
and foresight of future and eternal intelli-
gence. Intelligence is happiness—we cannot
enjoy aught, of which we have no under-
standing. Mrs. Watmough made some
very useful explanations upon the formation
of the body, the position of the bones, their
proper treatment and the wisdom exhibited
throughout the entire arrangement of the
same. Remarks suitable to the occasion
were made by sisters Ann Neal, M. A.
Leaver and Eleanor Pratt.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks. Prayer
by Counselor Mrs. Pheobe Woodruff.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Persons in pinched or moderate circum-
stances have, often, as lively a love for the
beautiful as those who are better off. There-
fore, nothing is more becoming to a sensible,
woman of limited means, than the firmest
endeavor to keep on hand, through some
legitimate course, such articles of food and
clothing for herself and family, as will
secure health. For it is a well known fact,
that health and beauty go hand in hand, and
that deprived of health, true beauty never
did, never can exist. Ladies may go to all
the expense they please to purchase artificial
beauty, but what is it after all but a simple
fraud which exposes a weakness of character?
While real beauty can be procured and long
preserved at less than half the cost, by using
sound judgment in the promotion and mainte-
nance of health.
	The following from the pen of Faith
Rochester is worthy of being contemplated
and acted upon by mothers:

	“Under-Clothing for Cold Weather.
—I have written on this subject before, but
there seems to be need of precept upon pre-
cept. Of late I have been learning better
ways than I knew before.
	For children of three years and over there
is nothing better, perhaps, than an under-
garment, clothing the body from the neck
and wrists to the heels, much like the night-
drawers children wear. They may be made
with a plain, easy, high-necked waist, with
long, straight sleeves, and with open drawers
sewed to the waist, The drawers should be
rather full at the top, but small enough at
the lower part of the leg to go inside the
stocking, reaching to the heel, or leaving no
gap above the shoe. 'Doctors disagree'
about the material of this under-garment,
some recommending woolen and some cot-
ton. Taking into account the difficulty of
washing woolen without shrinking it, and
the disagreeable sensation many skins ex-
perience in wearing it, probably the best
way in most cases is to have this under-
garment made either of cotton-flannel or of
hin cotton-cloth, with a similar garment of
warm woolen material over it. Buttons at
the waist support the other under-clothing.
	There are many sensible ladies in these
United States now wearing under-garments
similar to those just described. Among
them the preference is usually given to thin
cotton material for the suit next the body,
with a suit made of woolen (white flannel,
gray ladies’-cloth, or red fireman’s-flannel)
in the same fashion worn over the cotton
suit in winter weather. With two such
suits for frequent changes, to give oppor-
tunity for thorough airing and sunning of
the woolens, they require very little wash-
ing. The cotton next the body takes up the
perspiration, etc., and this gets the good
weekly washing. The woolen suit when
taken off weekly for a change need only be
exposed to pure air and sunlight for a day
or two to purify it properly, washing it
only when it visibly requires it.
	“If men wear cotton under their woolen
shirts, the woolen may be treated in the
same way, and men who are not engaged
in dirty work, can wear their woolen shirts
all winter with very little washing, purify-
ing them by air and sunshine each week
when changes are made. Ladies’ cloth shirts
shrink less than common flannel.”
	Preserving stuffed animals with-
out Arsenic.—Rub the flesh side of the skin
with a composition of 1 lb of tobacco ashes,
half a pound of alum, two pounds of dry
slaked lime.
	Cleaning Oil Paint.—Whiting is bet-
ter than soap. Use warm water and a soft
flannel. Afterwards wash clean and rub
dry with chamois.
	To render Corks or stoppers air-
tight.—This can be accomplished by
covering with a cement composed of red
lead or finely powdered litharge mixed with
undiluted glycerine.
	Mucilage.—Glue, water and 3 per cent
of nitric acid adheres well to metalic sur-
faces.
	Linseed is recommended as a cure for
consumptive cough. The linseed should
be boiled to the consistency of a jelly, and
sweetened with honey or sugar. Two large
tablespoonfuls should be taken when the
cough is about to attack the sufferer.
	To Wash Straw Matting.—Take a
pail half-full of hot water, a perfectly clean
long-handled mop, and a dish of dry, sifted
meal. Sweep all dust off the matting, then
scatter the dry meal evenly over the room.
Wring the mop so dry that it will not drip
at all, and rub hard, one breadth at a time,
always lengthwise of the straw, and use
clean water for each breadth. When the
matting is dry, the meal can be swept off
easily; it should always be done on a dry
day.


﻿Written for the Exponent.	
			MARRY A TRADESMAN.
	Young ladies, never speak unfavorably of
the idea of becoming the wife of an honest
tradesman, for they are in reality the
most honorable and worthy gentlemen of
any community, taken as a whole. Obscure
labor often does much more for the cause of
humanity than do deeds of world-wide fame.
The head may work itself gray, inventing
plans for industry to enter upon, but unless
the hands are fouud, the sturdy, willing
hands, to bring the designs which genius
has devised, into practical use by practical,
diligent labor, what could the schemes and
revelations of distinguished mental power
ever accomplish?


﻿Many a girl foolishly declines the hono
of being the wife of a true son of toil,
because of what her fashionable friends say
about it.
	“O! I wouldn’t marry such a man for
the world” says one. And another re-
marks, “It is so beneath your position in
society! To think of your settling down to
such a fate as washing dishes, and doubt-
less, mopping floors! I wouldn’t marry him
if I never married at all!” Why not? I
would marry him, provided I knew him to
be honest and temperate and industrious—
and that he loved me, and I him.
	If he is a genius and a scholar, so much
the better; but if not, these qualities can be
cultivated to a very great extent, and an
educated, refined and loving woman, can
do more to assist that cultivation than the
whole world could do without her.
	If he has earned, or even inherited a for-
tune, so that he is rich in worldly goods as
well as in heart, so much the better; but if
not, go to work with him, you will never
find wiser or better employment than assist-
ing the man you love to rise in the world
and become as rich and great as your proud-
est ambition would prompt within you a
desire to see him. There is much satisfac-
tion in earning what we possess, much more
than in receiving blessings in any other
way. There is true dignity and honor in
labor, labor of hands as well as of brains, to
which those who consider themselves above
work will never attain, until they have risen
above that degrading superstition.
								Aunt Trudie.


﻿RELICS OF A PAST RACE.
				  ——
	On the farm of Mr. Harrison Whaley, near
Moorfield, in this county, is a skirt of woods
which possesses characteristics of deep in-
terest. About three inches beneath the
surface of the entire tract may be found
innumerable bones, evidently the remains
of an extinct species of the human race.
Several mounds are also in the woods, and
in one which has been partially explored
were found skulls and bones, which from
their size, must have belonged to a race of
beings far more gigantic than the race which
now inhabits the earth. In this mound
were also found many clay utensils; also
arrow-heads cut out of the solid rock, and
pipes of the same material. Such a mem- 
orial of the past starts many inquiries.
Was the place a battle-ground, where abo-
rigines fought to maintain the glory of their
respective tribes? Or was it a common
burial-ground? The first seems the most
plausible theory, in as much as the whole
tract, covering at least fifteen acres, has
multitudes of human bones but a few inches
beneath the surfaee. It is evident they
were never buried, but originally lay exposed
to view, until the accumulated deposits of
time formed the black, rich soil which
covers them. But whatever theory may
be adopted, it is certain that these bony
memorials ante-date history, and furnish
another proof of how little is known of the
races and tribes who flourished, it may be,
centuries ago.—“Carlisle (Ky.) Mercury.”


﻿One of the most able and honored of
French writers has justly said, in speaking
of the pleasure he derived from literature,
“I read all that antiquity has written except
the poets, with whom we had been surfeited
at school, and in whose verses our wearied
eyes saw but the cæsnra, and the long or
short syllable. Sad effect of premature
satiety, which withers in the mind of a
child the most perfumed flowers of human
thought.
