﻿SPLINTERS.	
				—
	An agent has been sent to Germany in
the hope of securing 1,000 new hands to
work in the Lake Superior mines......For
the third time within nine years, the Jack-
sonville, Illinois, female college was lately 
burned......A Mrs. Kendall, wife of an officer
at Fort Davis, Texas, shot and killed a negro
corporal in the act of entering her sleeping
room, through the window in the absence
of her husband, and was justly applauded
......Black small pox has been widely spread
through Toledo, by the theft by some
students, of a body from the grave-yard
which had been taken off by that disease.
......Upon the first reading in the Spanish
Cortes, of the bill to abolish slavery in Porto
Rica, the vote was given in favor of the
ministerial measure, 180 to 6......Lotta, the
actress, lately reported dead, denies the 
report by a published card......Eight board-
ing house masters have been arrested by the
United States authorities in San Francisco,
for shipping seamen and demanding pay 
of the United States Commissioner......The
heavy frosts of this season in San Francisco,
have burst quite a number of lead water
pipes......The English Court of Queen's
Bench, awards an actress $8,000 damages
for a scar inflicted upon the face by a rail-
way accident......The way in which Irish 
school masters receive the school inspectors
is to meet them outside the door and give
them a sound drubbing; at least one was so
received not long since......There are fifty 
or fifty one counties in the State of Califor-
nia......In the suburbs of Paris, near the 
river Seine, houses are standing in vast lakes
of water from the overflow of that river......
A distinguished member of the French In-
stitute has been struck from the jury list for
declaring his unbelief in the existence of a 
God......A boy in Georgia is reported to
have refused $30,000 for a patent watch......
A recent ball given to the patients in the
lunatic asylum at Blackwell's Island, by
their physicians, is said to have resulted in
much good......A prize of one thousand 
dollars, for the best history of the United
States since the Civil War, is offered by the
King of Denmark......Mrs. M. J. Lamb, of
New York, is preparing a history of the 
Empire City......An old farmer states that
he has cured his daughter of the Grecian
bend, by pouring water on her and holding
her in the sun till she warped back again......
Streams in the neighborhood of Ghent, 
Belgium, have overflowed their embank-
ments and the country is inundated three
feet deep with water......Washington street,
Boston, had a $75,000 fire on the 23rd of
December which caused considerable con-
sternation......George Colton, a celebrated
American Indian painter and traveler, died
at Jersey City, New York, two days before
Christmas, 78 years old......A young lady in
Owensberg, while handling a pistol the
other day. accidentally shot herself through
the heart......All portions of the country
northwest, give accounts of almost unpre-
cedented cold weather......Three passenger
cars were recently precipitated from the
track, near Tonah, Wisconsin, by the break-
ing of a rail on the Milwaukee and St. Paul
Railroad, killing one man and injuring
several......Stokes second trial opened on
December 23, result like the first—nothing
definite concluded......A diamond was re-
cently found in the African diamond fields,
of 288 carats......The Pope severely de-
nounces the conduct of the Italian and
German Governments, and declares that
church property sold by order of the Italian
Parliament conveys no title to the purchaser
......A fire at Portland, Oregon, a few days
[new column]
since, destroyed $350,000 worth of property.
......Thirty persons were washed away and
drowned, from the packet Germania, which
went ashore on a sand bank at the mouth of
the Gironde, during a violent storm on the
night of the 21st of December......The horse
disease has been very prevalent at Toronto,
preventing the running of stages......Rail-
road trains on some of the northern routes
have been delayed by snow......Among the
specimens of the Army Medical Museum,
which number about 15,000, are 897 human
skulls and 34 skeletons......On the 23rd of
December Jersey City, New York, had a
$60,000 fire; Helena, Arkansas, a $30,000 fire,
and East Canaan, New Hampshire, a fire
worth $50,000, on the 22nd of December.


﻿ANOTHER YEAR.

	A new year commences to-day. Eighteen
hundred and seventy-two is with the past. Its
acts are history. Some of them have been im-
portant ones for the Latter-day Saints. A year
ago to-day dark clouds seemed to hang Iower-
ingly over the people who settled these Moun-
tain Valleys. The gravest charges were made
against the leading men of the Territory, by
bad men, and sworn to by even worse ones; and
in a few days after, President Young was held
a prisoner in his own house, while other prom-
inent men were similarly charged. It looked
to those not sustained by an undying faith that
no power could rescue these servants of God
from the hands of their enemies. But those
who sought their destruction were discomfited,
for that Power which has so long and so often
preserved the Saints, made bare His arm for
their deliverance, yet the unbelieving would
not realize the hand of God in the overthrowing
of those who sought the lives of His servants.
	During that trying period thousands of the
women of Utah longed for a medium through
which they could give utterance to their feel-
ings, thair faith, their devotion to the cause of
truth, and their unwavering hope in the future.
It was during that period that the project of the
Exponent was first thought of, and its publica-
tion talked of by a few interested in the matter;
although it was not until June that the way
seemed clear to fairly usher it into existence.
Now, we commence another year, with the
mutterings of the enemies of truth, and their 
expressed determinations to do all the wicked-
ness that is in their hearts. But the same
Power that has always controlled will control,
and they will be able to accomplish nothing
more than they are permitted to do for working
out the purposes of the Almighty.
	But the women of Utah are deeply interested
in all these movements. Ostensibly any blow
that may be aimed at the Church will be
directed against the men; but can our husbands,
fathers, brothers and sons be made the objects
of calumnious attacks, or even worse, and we
not be participators? And while much that
may be attempted will be done in the alleged
behalf of the “down-trodden women of Utah,”
shall we not fling back the slander to those who
utter it.” And as we honor manhood, andhold


virtue and chastity sacred as life, and love 
liberty as we love existence, shall we not repu- 
diate the wolves in sheeps’ clothimg, that would 
fawn around our young and guileless maidens
that they might destroy them!	
	With the opening of the New Year we ask
the patrons of the Exponent to continue their
labors in its behalf, that its instrumentalty for 
good may be widened and extended; and we
shall endeavor to labor faithfully, with the aid
of our sisters in the same noble object, to make
it what its name imports, An honest exponent
of the views of the women of Utah.


﻿MIXED PARTIES.

	This is the season when parties are many
and numerously attended. Social enjoyment
is sought by all classes and by all races of
people, and the people of this Territory have,
since its earliest settlement, encouraged,
especially in the winter season when out-door
work was often almost impossible, innocent and
recreative amusements and social gatherings.
When in each town and settlement everybody
knew everybody else, and was sure of meeting
only friends or acquaintances in whom he or
she had confidence, there existed no necessity
for strictness of inquiry as to who would be
present at a party. But things have changed,
and strangers are often now admitted to parties
in this Territory who are not proper associates
for innocent and pure-minded young people,
especially young ladies. We refer to what has
been called “mixed parties," where the price
of admission is placed at a certain figure, and
where every person is welcome who will pay
the money at the door. To such places every
character, no matter how vile, can claim
admission; and, there, simple and guileless
young girls may be thrown into the society of
men practiced in all the wiles and arts by which
maidens are lured to destruction. No wise
father or mother will allow their children to
attend such parties if they are aware of their
character; and as some of this kind have been
already gotten up, with the invitations headed
“select,” and as it is presumable that others will
be so gotten up, we earnestly recommend
parents to make the strictest inquiries where
the slightest doubt rests upon their minds, that
they may be satisfied beforehand their children
are not exposed to blighting evil, nor thrown in
the way of insidious and dangerous temptation.


﻿REAL ETIQUETTE.

	In those homes where parents observe all the
little courtesies belonging to refinement of social
intercourse, in daily life, are reared and edu-
cated the true ladies and gentlemen of society.
The charms of beauty, of gracefulness and of
manners, are born of the inward soul, and are
qualities of the mind, not of the outward person.
Books on manners and etiquette are unnecessary
for children whose parents possess the simple
yet important tact of making themselves agree-
able and courteous in the family circle. For
young people will unconsciously imitate those
ways, tones and expressions with which they
are familiar, and that influence becomes a part 
of their natures. A woman who bears about
her a continuous care and thoughtfulness for
the happiness of others, gives her offspring
truer and more lasting lessons in etiquette, by
her daily example, than years of study and
countless lectures upon the subject would en- 
able them to acquire. A cordial and pleasant
manner bears a force of sunny magnetism, the
influence of which is felt by all who approach
the agent of such power; and true civility often
does good when most unconscious of its ability.
If we notice carefully that which is unpleasant


and disagreeable to us in the conduct of others,
and avoid making use of anything similar,
and observe closely those obliging, modest
merits in the characters of those whose com-
panionship is ever fascinating and desirable,
and cultivate like principles, we need waste
little thought besides on etiquette.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿HOME AFFAIRS.

	PRESIDENT B. YOUNG and party arrived
safely at St. George, in the early part of last
week, in excellent health, having held meetings
at the principle settlements by the way down,
much to the edification of the Saints.
	AS CHRISTMAS has passed and the merry
New year is again with us, we wish our friends
and patrons a very happy day of it, and many
returns of the season. Damp and disagreeable
as the weather has been during the holiday
week, we are pleased to admit that no one, so
far as we have learned, has allowed this incon-
venience to interfere with their enjoyment.
	ON THE 22nd ult., we received a brief note
from sister Amelia M. Frodsham, of Ogden—
one of the firmest and most faithful and
energetic friends of the EXPONENT—bearing the
melancholy iutelligence that her husband,
brother Frodsham, departed this life on the
morning of the 21st at a quarter past nine. Our
sympathies for the bereaved lady are, if pos-
sible, increased, from the fact that at the time
of the death of her worthy husband, and for
several days previous, she had also a little
daughter lying very seriously ill. That the
child has recovered, and that the family may
be well sustained and comforted, under the
heavy loss which has deprived them of so dear
and true a friend, we sincerely hope.
	JUST NOW we learn, or rather are reminded,
that at the Territorial Fair, held in this city
last October, the first prize on quilts was
awarded to the quilt belonging to the Eighth
Ward Relief Society. That Society received
the prize money offered for the same, but
through some mistake of the reporters, the
honor was given, through the papers, to the
Twentieth Ward Relief Society, which drew
only the second prize on quilts. The Eighth
Ward Society should also have been credited
with the third prize on ottoman covers, which
the papers all omitted, or published wrong. At
fairs nothing unfair should be countenanced;
we therefore take pleasure in rectifying the
mistakes made by the EXPONENT, in common
with other papers, as soon as apprized of them.
A month or two earlier would have been all the
better.
	A TERRIBLE snow-slide occurred in Little
Cottonwood canyon, on Thursday evening last,
December 26th, by which ten teams were car-
ried away from the road and clear over to the
other side of the creek, smashing sleighs and
wagons, killing and maiming animals, and
worst of all, destroying the lives of some eleven
men. It was a fearful catastrophe, and shows
the terrible power of a mass of snow, light and
feathery is it appears when it falls, as it is
hurled from the mountain sides into the
yawning canyon below, This is the greatest loss
of life yet entailed by one of these avalanches
in Utah, and was caused by a large number of
ore-hauling teams being in the path of the
avalanche. But two bodies were recovered up
till Saturday night; one, of a man from Camp
Douglas, named Peter Rahn; the other of Leroy
Dibble, from Erie County, New York. It is ex-
pected that some of the bodies will not be
recovered until Spring, though a large force has
been searching for them


﻿“ACROSS THE WATERS.”

	CORRESPONDENCE OF MISS SNOW.

			Liverpool, Nov. 19th, 1872.
MY DEAR LULA:—		
	l am now on “terra firma” but so highly
elevated that I almost claim to be filling an
aerial position, being located on the fourth
floor in the “North-Western Hotel,” a
spacious building. The room I occupy, in
company with Mrs. Thompson and Miss
Little, fronts St. George’s Hall, said to be
the largest edifice in the city.
	In my last, I left you with the Falls of
Niagara. I do not recollect anything of
special interest occurring between there and
New York, unless the presence of Hon. Ben
Butler should be so considered. Not having
seen him, I felt considerable interest when
it was announced that he was on the train,
and to my entire satisfaction, he entered
the car in which I was riding, and seated
himself where I had a good view of his
ample phiz. As we arrived at towns by the
way, the citizens no doubt having been ap-
prised of his coming, were out in masses, to
whom he delivered electioneering speeches,
which were listened to with the usual en-
thusiasm of such occasions. For my part, I
feel to respect any man who possesses suffi-
cient independenc of character, in this age
of political bribery, to speak as he thinks—
which I believe Ben Butler does.
	We arrived in New York on Sunday
morning—it was raining, but luckily for us,
the beautiful depot afforded us shelter,
while, after considerable delay, a carriage
(probably the property of some unpopular
infidel) was procured to carry ourselves and
our baggage to St. Nicholas’ Hotel. The
fact of our arrival on the Sabbath was the
cause of delay, this very religious people
have plenty of cabs and hacks to receive
strangers, on other days of the week, which
strongly reminds me of a class of religionists
referred to in New Testament history. I am
now speaking in general terms—there are
very excellent people living in New York,
I know of several although my knowledge
is very limited.
	St. Nicholas’ Hotel is a splendid establish-
ment—it contains 600 rooms and accommo-
dates from ten to eleven hundred occupants.
It is freely ventilated and so warmed with
pipes running in various directions as to
keep an equilibrium of temperature. Water
is conveyed to and from the rooms in pipes,
so that you are not stinted—you can wash
yourself and rinse too, if you choose. The
whole establishment is lighted with gas,
and you can have as much light as you
please; yet I think there is an extra charge
if you keep it burning all night. What I
consider an enormity is the charge of one
dollar per day for fire, however small it may
be; and then the porter graciously expects a
small fee for his condescension in making it
for you. But that is all very well—he is so
obliging and does it so much better than
any one else.
	I stayed three nights in New York—spent
two days in the city and one in visiting
Willard Young at West Point. In company
of President George A. Smith, my brother
and brother Dunford; I went to the office of
the Notary Public, the kind and affable Mr.
None, before whom I solemnly subscribed
to my allegiance to the United States Gov-
ernment, etc., according to due form of law,
and he made out my application which he
promised to send to Washington for my
passport, which I expect to meet in London
on my arrival. In conversation with Mr.
None, brother Smith spoke of his rejection
from the Philadelphia Convention, on
account of his religion, when Mr. N. ex-
claimed emphatically, “How ridiculous!”
he expressed much pleasure on meeting
President Smith, saying he was the first
official of our people with whom he had met.
We then called on the “Consul General of
the German Empire,” Mr. Kosing, where we
met the very gentlemanly Mr. Schleiden,
member of the German Parliament. Both
these gentlemen had visited Salt Lake,
where they made Br. Smith’s acquaintance.
They spoke of their visit in the highest
terms.
	Through the kindness of Br. J. W. Young,
I enjoyed a pleasant turn around Central
Park; and here, amid its shades, fountains,
equestrian statues, etc. etc., I leave you for
the present.
						Eliza R. Snow.


﻿CORRESPONDENCE.
				——
		FRANKLIN, Oneida Co., Idaho, 
				   December 10, 1872. 	
MISS L. L. GREENE:—
	I left Salt Lake City, November 2nd,
and arrived at Box Elder station in safety,
had a very pleasant trip. There I took
passage on the Utah Northern. When I got to 
Hampton’s Station, the terminus, in compny
with some friends I stepped on to the
engine and ran on to the end of the track,
about four miles; when passing some very
deep fills, sitting as I did where I could see
all the danger, on hearing the man at the
Engine say he had never run over that road
before, all at once my blood seemed to stop
in its course, I felt my heart stop its beat-
ing; I hardly knew where I was. But in a
few minutes I saw we were all safe on level
ground, which made all right again. There
we saw men laying ties and rails; as I stood
looking at those willing hands at that great
work, I said in my heart, “what could this
people do if they could be let alone!”
	We stepped on to our swift beast, and ran
back to the station, found the coach in wait-
ing, were soon seated and off for Logan,
where we arrived in safety. When I saw how
far I had come in, in so short a time, I
felt to thank the Lord for his kindness in
helping this people to build the Rail-
road. And when I arrived in this far off
northern clime and found all my children
and friends well and prospering I felt more
than thankful. I am stopping at present at
my son-in-law’s, A. Stolker, who is now, in
company with Bishop Lorenzo Hatch, at
Boise City, the Capital of this Territory,
attending the legislature, of which they are
members. .I have seen letters from them;
they speak well of the people of that place,
but say they had a hard time in getting
there. Were way laied by robbers and
commanded to stop, but the driver on the
coach put whip to his horses and was soon
out of their reach. Besides, the coach was
upset in going down a very steep hill, but
happily there were none severely hurt.
	Well, I find good Saints in this land. The
men are alive to the calls of the Bishops to
work on the Railroad, the sisters to looking
after the poor.
	I am well and feel first rate in spirits.
I expect to spend the winter in this north-
ern country. We have had some very cold
weather, but it is fine now. I have not for-
gotten you nor your labors; may the Lord
bless you in the same.
					AMANDA SMITH.


A GERMAN beggar girl in Boston, who
has no hands, is reported to be the owner of
a fine residence in that city, besides other
property, all purchased with money gathered
from foolish but charitable people in the
streets.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿			  For the Exponent.
		KATIE'S BIRTHDAY PRESENT.
				     ——
	Yesterday Katie was eleven years old.
As father was leaving the breakfast table to
go to his office he said to her, “Put on your
hat and cloak, Katie, and walk up town with
me, and we’ll see if we can’t find a bird and
cage that will do for you to bring home as a
'Birthday Present’.” Pleased beyond
measure, Katie did as directed, and half an
hour afterward, came home with a lovely
Goldfinch in a perfect palace of a cage.
	“Isn’t Papa good?” said the young lady,
as she proceeded to hang her new pet in
the parlor window.
	But all the remainder of the morning
Katie seemed troubled. She supplied her
bird plentifully with food and water, and
every few minutes she would go to it, as it
hopped about from one side to the other of
its magnificent prison, and putting her lips
close to the bars, say somthing in a half
whisper, which sounded like a tender, pity-
ing mother soothing a fretful child.
	When father came home to dinner, he
asked for Katie, and was surprised, he said,
that she was not at hand to meet him with
her merry prattle, as usual. “How is the
'Birthday Present,’ my little girl?” asked
he as she came silently and thoughtfully to-
ward him. The child’s face crimsoned, as
she put up her mouth for the accustomed
kiss, without speaking.	
	“What is it Katie, doesn’t the Goldfinch
suit you? Would you have preferred a
Canary?” father continued. “Well, no,
dear Papa, I realy am not satisfied with my
'Birthday Present,’ though I ought to be
ashamed to acknowledge it, I know,"
answered Kate demurely. “Not if you
mean it, my frank darling,” answered our
indulgent parent, “let Mat have the Gold-
finch and I will bring you home a Canary
this evening.” “But I don’t want a Canary
Papa! and had rather the Goldfinch were
taken back; for although it is very beauti-
ful, and its singing delightful, I cannot look
at it or listen to its music without feeling as
though I must cry. I don’t want a bird,
Papa; I cannot think it is fair or just or
right, according to the laws of nature and
the statutes of creation, to keep the little
creatures housed up thus, when the broad
firmament and free, wild woods are their
native elements. I should never feel com-
fortable to have one of the little captives in
the house.”
	Thus philosophized our sensitiev hero-
ine, and who shall argue that ! those
girlish reasonings were not correct? Father
did not, but yielding to her childish persua-
sions, he carried the bird and cage back to
the store, and sent his sympathetic daughter
a splendid volume on ornithology instead,
with which she is highly delighted. Papa
thinks Katie will make a fine “Woman’s
Rights” woman, one of these days, and that
she ought to belong to the “Society for the
Prevention of cruelty to Animals.”
						LITTLE SISTER.
S. L. CITY, December 1872.,


A FARMER of Alvarado, Alameda county,
some months since, put in fifty acres of seed
for an experiment in chicory culture. He
went on a visit to the East.		On his return
recently he found that instead of fifty acres
of chicory, he had fifty acres of German
salad, the result of a trifling mistake in
seed.


RECTOR'S daughter (to Sunday scholar)—
"Oh, you have an elder brother; well, how
old is he?"	School-boy—"Dunno, miss, but
he just started o' swearin."


MISCELLANEOUS. 
			   ——
	A MISSING minister of the gospel is
wanted in Howwood, Scotland. He left his
charge about two months ago, for a holiday
trip to America, and has not returned. A
young lady disappeared at the same time,
but of course this fact has no connection
with his “lighting out.”
	A DOLLY VARDEN PIE:—Take about four
yards of light dough, gather it up in light
flounces, crimp the edges, and fill up with
fruit; then lay on the overskirt, fasten it;
with buttons of dough connected with frills
of the same, and you will have a tasteful
and elegant pie, only you must eat it, not
wear it.
	THE willow that bends to the tempest
often escapes better than the oak which re-
sists it; and so, in great calamities, it some-
times happens that light and frivolous spirits
recover their elasticity and presence of mind
sooner than those of a loftier character.—
“Sir Walter Scott.”
	LIKE flakes of snow that fall unperceived
upon the earth, seemingly unimportant
events of life succeed each other. As the
snow gathers together so are our habits
formed. No single action creates, however
it exhibits man’s character; but as the
tempest hurls the avalanche down the
mountain, so passion may overthrow the
edifice of truth and virtue.
	Dr. Franklin recommends a young man,
in the choice of a wife, to select from a bunch, 
giving as a reason, that when there are many
daughters they improve each other, and
from emulation acquire more accomplish
ments, and know more, and do more, than
a single child spoiled by parental fondness.
This is a comfort to people with large
families.	
	CHISELED FEATURES.—The features may
be common and characterless, but if they are
soft and uncertain, and the spaces around
them ample, they are safe.		But of the
defined forms and scanty quantities let us
beware though, fond mothers call them
“chiseled features” and “regular profiles.”
What is admired as delicate and precise
now, may be too likly to turn out sharp and
mean by-and-by.
	SELF-EXAMINATION. — Few sufficiently
practice the habit of self-examination.
Through life man is liable to error and
requires check, rebuke and counsel. He
should personify hisi own conscience. He
should be his own good spirit, hovering
over himself in moments of passion, temp-
tation and danger, and reminding himself
that he owes a duty to his Maker with which
the opinions and consequences of the world
have nothing to do.
	WOMAN'S DRUDGERY.—The hygienic
system is pre-eminently the cause of woman,
both as respects the preservation of health
and the treatmeut of disease. A simpler
and more wholesome plan would relieve her
of one-half of her present hard toil and
drudgery, while it would promote the health
of her household. At least four-fifths of all
the money expended for medicines and
medical advice, are paid because of the
diseases of women and children. And nine-
tenths of all the care, nursing, night watch-
ing and privation of sleep and rest because
of sick children, are performed and suffered
by women. The Medical Society in New
York, which, on one of its festive occasions,
toasted women in the following words, had
truth, if not poetry, as the basis of the
sentiment:
	“Woman”—God’s best gift to man, and
the chief support of all the doctors.”


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
				—
	ENGLAND allows its paupers two shillings
and sixpence (62c.) a week to live on.
	THE Bishop of Philadelphia proposes to
cure the horse disease with prayer.
	THE Knights of St. Patrick of St. Louis
talk of a $25,000 monument to the memory
of Daniel O’Connell.
	TWO hundred thousand gallons of wine
were made at Yountville this season and
100,000 gallons of brandy.	
	THE statute of Chief Justice Taney was
upon the pedestal at Annapolis, Md., on the 
21st of November. It represents Taney in 
a sitting posture.
	THE "Papal Index,” contains the names
of nineteen books which the Holy Father
prohibits. Among them is Dr. Buchner’s 
“Force and Matter.”
	THE Boston “Pilot,” twice burnt out,
says: “We are just about tired of this 
phoenix business. We don’t want to rise
nobly from our ashes any more.”
	EX-President Hopkins, of Williams Col-
lege, has been invited by the graduates of
that institution resident in Troy, N. Y., to
lecture in that city on Professor Tyndall’s 
“Prayer Gauge.”
	THE working girls in the Arlington woolen 
mills have sent to Jennie Collins eighty-one
dollars for the sewing girls of Boston, who
have lost their work by the fire.
	INDIANAPOLIS boasts of a contractor who
can neither read, write, nor figure, and yet
can accurately estimate the number of bricks
for a wall of given dimensions, and the cost
of construction.
	CARDWELL, the British War Minister, 
has sanctioned the introduction of playing
cards into the soldiers’ recreation room as
an experiment in the way of weaning soldiers
from the public houses.
	COLONEL Thomas A. Scott has completed
contracts for transporting supplies, provi-
sions and laborers, and 25,000 tons of iron to
be used in building the transcontinental
railroad, which begins at the end of the
Missouri, Kansas and Texas Railroad.
	A MAN’S total outward work, his whole
effect opon the world in twenty-four hours,
has been reckoned about 350 foot-tons. That
may be taken as a good “hard day’s work.”
During the same the heart has been working
at the rate of 120 foot-tons. That is to say,
if all the pulses of a day and night could be
concentrated and welded into one great
throb, it would be enough to throw a ton of
iron 120 feet into the air.
	IF the following is not a “Fact,” it ought
to be. We are personally positive of many
facts not half so good; and the figuring is
certainly excellent, showing that Ohio,
with all its intemperance has still some true
husbands, and it is to be hoped they are
justly appreciated:  “The epizootic has de-
veloped in Cleveland, Ohio, among other
things a vast deal of marital devotion. The
wife of one gentleman desired to attend the
Patti concert, but was in feeble health and
unable to walk. The husband put her into
a stout baby carriage, and proceeded straight-
way to the hall where Patti was to sing; and
at the close of the entertainment he trundled 
his wife home in the same style.  Another
wife desired to attend the wedding of a
friend, some distance away from her resi-
dence; but the horses were all sick and a
carriage could not be had for love or money.
Carefully wrapping her up and placing her
on the seat of a high buggy, the husband
took his place between the thills, and for
the nonce ‘played horse’ most successfully.”


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Written for the EXPONENT.
	IMPORTANT EVENTS OF ONE YEAR.
				    ——
		A HOLIDAY SKETCH BY L. L. 
				      —
	It was New Year’s Eve, 1863, and we were
having a regular nice time.  There had
been a shooting match, father was on the
winning side, and was to take supper with
Mr. W., who was to pay for both tickets to
the ball that evening as well. We were all
going to the ball, all but mother and the
little folks, and with those exceptions my
“all,” includes my father, three sisters, a
brother-in-law and myself.
	How clear and bright the hall was, and
how happy every one appeared! It seemed
to me, my sisters had never looked so well
as upon that New Year’s Eve, and they
enjoyed themselves so much and were so
unconscious of self, that I delighted in
watching them. It was not strange, either,
that I should very much admire the taste of
an intelligent lady, who remarked to me,
that the figure of my sister Maud was really
the finest in the room. I wonder if Mr. A.
remembers how I danced a reel with him
that evening? He was so awkward and un-
managable that, beginning at the head of
the set, when “down the centre” came for
us, I marched him to the foot and there
remained, and he still imagines, for ought I
know, that we went through the whole change
as did the others. My sister Eva, who was
dancing in the same set, laughed so much
that I almost became plagued over it. The
only unpleasantness that occurred, was that I
had to go home before I wanted to—I was
only fourteen,
	Three hundred and sixty-five days sped
by us, one after another, bringing each its
varying scenes. During that little period
one sister gave to earth a tiny, new inhabit-
ant; Eva was wooed, wen and wedded, and
another sister left us to return no more. In
less than six months time, birth, marriage
and death were all visitants of our family
circle.
	Yet there was no gloom around our fire-
side, as we sat and listened to father’s grave,
or humorous stories; and Maud and I 
repeated dialogues and sung songs and
hymns, on New Year's Eve, 1864.


ABUNDANTLY PROTECTED.—It was one
evening last Summer when a lady who be-
longs on the editorial staff of one of the
leading dailies of New York, had been
detained by office duties until rather a late
hour. Living on the Heights in Brooklyn,
but a short distance from Fulton Ferry, it
was not much of a venture to go home with-
out an escort, and so she started. On the
boat, standing outside enjoying the breeze
after the day’s toil, she perceived a gentle-
man (?) in rather close proximity to where
she was, but said nothing.
	“Are you alone?” said he, as the boat
neared the shore.
	“No, sir,” said the lady; and without
further interruption, when the boat touched,
she stepped off.
	"I thought you were not alone,” said the
fellow, stepping to her side again.
	“I am not,” replied the lady.
	“I don’t see any one. Who is with you?”
	“God Almighty and the angels; I’m never
alone.”	
	“You keep too good company for me,
madame; good night!” and he shot for a
Fulton Avenue car, then nearly a block
away. The heroic woman was permitted
to keep to “the right as the law directs,”
and enjoy that full measure of quiet satisfac-
tion one always feels from keeping good
company.


﻿THE SEX.
			    ——
	THE Grecian ladies counted their age
from their marriage, not their birth.
	FOUR hundred Brooklyn teachers, women,
have petitioned for an increase of salaries.
	JENNY Lind has been singing in the choir
of the American Episcopal Church in Flor-
ence.
	GRACE Greenwood has met with success
in her reading in Colorado.	
	THE story goes that the Empress Carlotta
will recover her health of body, but never
strength of mind.
	MISS Dixon, a Southern lady of admired
skill in penmanship, writes with both hands
simultaneously.
	THE young ladies of the Jackson Presby-
terian Female College have resolved them-
selves into a “Cozy Club.”
	A WOMAN named Mary Tabor has acted
as mate on a Missouri steamboat for two
years, earning forty-five dollars per month.
	FULLER states that Henry VIII, of Eng-
land, gave a woman the whole revenue of a
convent as a reward for making a pudding
which happened to gratify his taste.
	THE wife of Marshal Guebriant acted as
representative for France, and was acknowl-
edge as such, at the court of Ladislas IV.,
King of Poland, in 1646.
	HARRIET Martineau is a confirmed inva-
lid, and sees no visitors at her cottage,
"The Knoll,” at Ambleside, near Lake
Windermere, in England.
	A THOUSAND American girls, for whom
the schools on this side of the water are not
considered good enough, are receiving their
education in France and Italy.
	THE funeral of Fanny Fern took place in
New York, and in accordance with the
wishes of the deseased, was private. The
remains were taken to Boston.
	A REMARKABLE incident is related of a
young girl in Dundee, whose hands and
feet were all amputated in consequence of
embolic gangrene and for whom artificial
hands and feet were specially devised,
which she uses with very little difficulty
and much satisfaction.
	THE Princess Louise of Hesse—Alice of
England--has been chosen first Prsident of the
International Association for the Protection
of the Rights of Women; and the Congress
of Darmstadt, which made this election, has
chosen several English women as corres-
ponding members.
	MADAME Von Goethe, widow of the great
German poet’s only son, recently died at
Weimar, Saxony, very old. During the
lifetime of her father-in-law, she did the
honors of the house, and presided at many a
social gathering marked by the presence of
the most eminent authors and politicians of
the age. Goethe loved her as a daughter,
and admired her writings, and at the time
she edited the Mitternacht “Zeitung,” used
to speak of her as one of the ablest critics of
the age
	THE girls employed by Macullar, Wil-
liams & Parker in their tailoring establish-
ment, Boston, declined to accept their wages
for one week’s work, thinking that the sum
in the aggregate might be acceptable in
consideration of the heavy losses of the firm.
On the other hand, the members of the firm,
solicitous for the welfare of those whose
losses might be small but sufferings great,
told Jennie Collins to send to them any
work girls, and particularly their own, who
might be in need and they would provide
for them.


﻿FLOWERS.
			      ——
	WHO lives to fancy, rarely can be rich, 
who lives to nature never can be poor.
	WALK worthy of your vocation! You have
a noble escutcheon, disgrace it not by folly.
	WHEN you write, or get a friend to write
on your own business or for your own 
advantages, be careful to pay the postage
yourself.
	THE best property a man can be possessed
of, since happiness is the boon for which all
wish to bargain, is a clear conscience and 
a contented mind.	
	A HIGH sense of honor in all things, an 
adherence to truth, delicacy and politeness
are the essential and	distinguished charac-
teristics of a gentleman.
	TRUTH dreads no scrutiny; shields herself 
behind no breastwork of established custom
or of respectable authority, but proudly
stands on her own merits.
	TO an active and an ingenious mind there
is no pursuit more fascinating than the
pursuit of knowledge, no pleasure more ex-
quisite than the discovery of truth.
	LABOR allied with virtue may look up to
heaven and not blush, while all worldly 
dignities, prostituted to vice, will leave 
their owner without a corner of the universe
in which to hide his shame.
	DON'T laugh in company without ex-
plaining why you laugh. There are always
some sensitive ones who will believe them-
selves ridiculed without the slightest occa- 
sion.
	VIRTUE and truth constitute true nobility, 
these can be your’s as well as another’s, you
have only to maintain them. The poorest	
and lowliest can be as truly noble as the
rich and great.
	THERE is more than one reason why "Ye
cannot serve God and mammon;” there is
not time to do both even if both could be
kept in remembrance; who ever heard of a
rich person (“one who trusted in his riches,”)
who could find time to keep holy the Sab-
bath?
	A GOOD action however small should
never be omitted, a kind word, no matter
how simple should never be suppressed. 
One little deed or word, often saves one
from dishonor, sometimes from destruction,
and the actors or speakers are never the
poorer for having given what cost them
nothing.
	IF darkness obscures your way, think of
those whose lives are darker still, and be
comforted; If sunshine and gladness is your
portion, try to infuse golden drops of the
same, into hearts less fortunate and thus
increase your store; for like a spring of
water is the human heart; the more you
draw out of it, the more it is capable of re-
ceiving.


﻿NECESSARY EVILS.
					—
	The other evening, being out in company,
I heard a gentlemen (?) declare that women
were necessary evils. Now was not that
brilliant? Quite original. In down East
phraseology, let me exclaim “due tell.”
Being only a woman, I was covered with a
due sense of my degraded position. A
necessary evil!
	Will anybody define man’s position on
this sublunary sphere?
	Let me whisper in your ear—this gentle-
man has had three wives—think he is
rather fond of said evils, but don’t know.
	Wonder if his sons with such training
won’t become unmitigated causes of regret to
some poor necessary evils.
						ONE OF THEM.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿CHILDREN’S VOICES.
			       —--
	On the 15th of December, 1872, there was
a sort of Sabbath School exhibition in the
14th Ward Assembly Rooms. Music, sing-
ing and recitations were offered by children
of all sizes, from the “we wean” that gave
its little story in a perpect baby tone, to
adult manhood. There is something so
winsome, so benignly sweet in the voice of
innocence, that the same words spoken or
sung by persons of more mature years seem
to convey a different impression, and to have
a less desirable effect upon the heart. And
on this particular Sabbath it might have
been a fancy, but if so it was a very happy
one—it seemed as though the very voices of
the children had really become like those of
the angels, especially in their singing.
“Children may come to the Savior” and
“Hearts and Homes,” were rendered with
so much pathos that tears, grateful, pleasant
tears might have done honor to the eyes of
a soldier who had been a listener; but
“Music”—
	“There’s music by the fireside,
	“There’s music in the hall;
	“There’s music in the school-room,
	“There’s music for us all;
	"There’s music in the fountain;
	“There’s music in the air;
	“There's music in the true heart,
	“There’s music everywhere:”
How shall the rendering of this immortal
eulogy on the divine art be described !
The very touch of the tiny, child fingers,
upon the keys of the organ sounded as if
softened by the influence of sweet souled
messengers from choirs above!
	The neately arranged little dialogue and
song “What was witnessed in the Heavens?”
was also very chastely and sweetly executed.
	The singing was all delighful, the recita-
tions splendid.	Where order is so well ob-
served, who could ever tire of “children’s
voices?” Let us have more music.


THE seal can be readily tamed, and be-
comes very affectionate. It may be taught
many things, such as shaking hands, bowing
and kissing. The cry of the seal is soft and
flute-like, and resembles certain sounds
common to all languages, “pa pa” or “ma
ma.”

[new column]

		WOMAN’S EXPONENT.
				 —
	     A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL.
				 —
	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral 
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani- 
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.
	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.
	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? “It is bet- 
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented 
by others !"
	For these reasons, and that wemen may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica- 
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by, 
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been 
commenced.
	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.
	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.
	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In Woman’s Exponent a department
will be devoted to reports of their matings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and secretarics of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.
	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each  number containing eight pages, quarte. 
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah. 
	TERMS, Strictly in Advance :
  1 Copy one year,       $2.00	1 Copy six months,    $1.00
10 Copies one year,    18.00     10 Copies six months,    9.00
	No subscription received for less than six months. 
	It is earnestly desired that the bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a 
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine. 

	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City, 
Utah. 

Dr. W. H. GROVES,
						DENTIST, 
	Office Second South Street. Three doors west of
Great Western Hotel, half a block east of Elephant
Store, Salt Lake City. 
		Office Hours from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m.

Woman's Exponent.
VOL. I.	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JANUARY 15, 1873.	NO. 16.


EDITORIAL NOTES.
			     —
	THE "EPIZOOTIC" has reached Denver, 
Colorado. 	That is getting uncomfortably
near. 
	AN EASTERN paper says Susan B. An-
thony stands up for her rights like a man;
and considers it a compliment. 
	ITALY CONTINUES to reduce the number
of her brigands and professional murderers
by sending them as emigrants to the United
States. 
	PARDONING CONVICTS has become a popu-
lar pastime for State governors. The
goveror of Ohio was asked the other day to
pardon a batch of three hundred at a sitting,
but he took time to think it over.
	TWO EXPEDITIONS are to start simultan-
eously from England for extreme opposite
points; one to attempt the discovery of the
North Pole, and the other the South Pole.
They are likely to find something; ice, cer-
tainly, if nothing of greater public interest
	BARNUM HAD his great menagerie and
circus destroyed by fire in New York, and
immediately telegraphed to Europe for half
a million dollars worth of curiosities. Three
times burned out, he rises each time with
the occasion—and gets the newspapers to
tell of it. Shrewd advertising.
	THE ILLINOIS State woman’s suffrage
association will assist Susan B. Anthony in
her trial by every means in their power.
They consider the indignity to which she
has been subjected, in being imprisoned
because of casting her ballot as an American
citizen, as offered to the entire women of
America.
	AN ORDINANCE was introduced in the St.
Louis City Council, to repeal what is known
as the "Social Evil” act, under which fallen
women are licensed to pursue their fearful
traffic; and it was almost unanimously re-
jected. An ordinance requiring men who
patronize the places and persons thus
licensed, to be also licensed, would have
been rejected as unanimously. Such is a
specimen of governing “justice.”
	THE DRIVER of a New York street car,
seeing a young girl grossly insulted by a
ruffian, a couple of weeks ago, and noticing
that neither the passengers nor the con-
ductor interfered, stopped his car, thrashed
the scoundrel, threw him into the street,
and gave him in charge of a policeman.
That car-driver should have a monument,
subscribed for by defenceless ladies who
know what it is to be insulted by “gentle-
men” of the stamp that he punished so
promptly and justly. 
	ICE GORGES, in some parts of the eastern
rivers, such as the Missouri and Ohio, hav-
ing collected during the terrible storm
which raged in the latter part of December;
in their breaking up with a cessation of the
extreme cold, did immense damage in
several places, sweeping away docks, crush-
ing down coal barges, and crashing through
river steamers. Over half a million dollars
worth of property was thus destroyed in the
last eight days of December. This might
be reckoned among “the signs of the times.”
	IT IS reported that a Connecticut clergy-
man has resigned because his pious flock
[new column]
wouldn't let him play croquet. If he wished
to make a woman or “a tall girl” of himself,
why not let him? Or did they think that a
little healthy muscular exercise would drive
away misanthropic feelings, and that he
would not be able to picture the nethermost
pit with sufficiently harrowing vividness to
suit them, if he indulged in a game of
croquet?
	“SPEAK NOT thou evil of dignitaries” is
the rule to be observed in the French re-
public. A man in Lyons, some little time
ago, called Thiers “a stupid fool," and he
was promptly “jerked up” and sent to
prison. Had this rule of swift punishment
been followed during the late Presidential
campaign in this country, there is scarcely
sufficient hemp in the Union to have made
ropes enough for the necks of those who
would have had to be hanged. France is a
unique republic, of the old Venetian stripe.
	AN EASTERN paper—the Washington
“Star”—under date December 27, 1872,
says: “Sidney Rigdon, author of the Mor-
mon Bible, is dead.” It will be news to the
Latter-day Saints to learn that Sidney Rig-
don was the author of the Old and New
Testament, which form the “Mormon
Bible;” or that he was the author of the
“Book of Mormon,” which is probably what
is meant; or, indeed, that he was the
author of any really good book, although he,
at one time, held a high position in the
Church, but fell from grace through ambi-
tion, as did Lucifer.
	PLANT TREES. Thirty years ago, says an
exchange, Mrs. Lydia Wilson, of Raisin,
Michigan, stuck a willow-riding whip in
the ground and now it measures ten feet in
circumference and spreads over eighty-four
feet. Plant trees; fruit trees; shade trees, mul
berry trees to feed silk worms, or forest trees.
You leave a blessing to posterity which they
will appreciate in years to come. The
Oriental custom of planting a fruit tree on
the birth of each child is one that could be
adopted in the western world to advantage,
and no place to greater advantage than in
Utah; for our timber in the mountains is 
fast disappearing.	
	SIX GIRLS and a boy were the victims of
the Centre street fire, New York; roasted
alive; yet their bodies were allowed to re-
main for nearly a week under the ruins, in
the most populous city in the country,
because the authorities did not know who
would pay the expense of having the ruins
removed, and the lessees of the building
would not touch it for fear of injuring their
insurance. How intensely humane and
charitable New Yorkers have grown, where
a little money is involved!	This disgrace-
ful action of the police authorities and the
lessees of the burned building, should be
repudiated in some manner by the city, or
the odium will rest upon all.
	FIFTEEN WOMEN were brought before a
United States Commissioner in the State of
New York, charged with illegal voting, and
held to bail: That is, fourteen were so held,
and furnished the required security; but
the other, Miss Susan B. Anthony, refused
to furnish the bail, and was remanded to
prison. Now, the question at issue, is, does
the 11th Amendment to the Constitution
[new column]
confer upon women the right of suffrage
without State or Congressional enactments,
or the passage of a 16th Amendment to the
Constitution? As well-read lawyers and
as clever jurists as General B. F. Butler and
others hold that it does; and this Miss An-
thony declares her determination to test.
From the rapid advance made, towards
securing the recognition of this right to
women, during the past year, and the frank,
open manner in which it is advocated by
prominent men throughout the country,
there is little doubt that, whether, Miss
Anthony should succeed in this effort or not,
public sentiment would oppose her being
punished, and that in 1876 women in nearly
if not all the States, will vote for President
and Vice-President.
 A NEW United States District Attorney
has been appointed for Utah, in the person
of William Carey, of Galena, Illinois, and his
appointment was confirmed by the United
States Senate on the 8th inst. The rabid
anti-Mormons look for special legislation by
Congress against the Latter-day Saints, to
follow immediately, and that all their
desires for the destruction of those, who
settled and made this Territory what it is,
will be gratified; and yet they talk about
Christianity and profess belief in God and
in the Scriptures, while gloating, in antici-
pation, over scenes of desolation and blood-
shed brought upon an innocent, God-fearing
community! But there is an old French
proverb that will apply: “l’homme propose,
et Dieu dispose”—man proposes, and God
disposes. They have felt equally as san-
guine before, only to be disappointed as they
will be again. There are two other little
proverbs they might study to advantage;
one is, that “truth crushed to earth will
rise again;” the other, that “the blood of
the martyrs is the seed of the church.” Let
“Mormonism” alone and it will grow; perse-
cute it, and it will grow tenfold faster.


﻿PEN AND SCISSORS.
				—
	A VETERINARY surgeon who lives at
Buffalo, N. Y., lately tried to “founder” his
mother-in-law by dancing a polka with her
until she got warmed up, and then giving
her all the iced lemonade she could drink.
	THE indolent habits of the Orientals are
well illustrated by the following “bon mot,”
which in the regions of the East has
assumed the character of an adage: “No
man should run when he may walk, walk
when he may stand, stand when he may
sit, or sit when he may lie down.”
	ALWAYS pleasant is a compliment to edi-
torial ears. “I saw three excellent things
in your last paper ” said a wag to an editor.
“Ah, indeed! what were they?” inquired
the delighted knight of the quill. “Mince
pies,” replied the wag, who escaped the
paste-pot by slipping out of the door. 
	A JOLLY fisherman was asked in the
presence of a spinster, what sort of a wife
he would choose, if he were going to marry,
to which he promptly replied “An 'Ale-
wife.’ ” The spinster increased the merri-
ment which this occasioned by dryly adding,
“I like wine best, and should prefer a
grape ‘Husband-man.’”


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿[Written for the EXPONENT.
		PRAYERFUL CONFESSION.

	O God, when grief and pain beset,
	When hope and joy my soul forget,
		I bring my woe to Thee;
	I come to Thee and humbly pray
	That Thou wilt smooth my thorny way,
		And bid my sorrows flee.

	I ne’er forget to breathe Thy name,
	When o’er my pathway grief and shame
		In dreary darkness brood;
	I cry to Thee when in distress,
	Ask Thee to lift from off my breast
		Its weary, weary load,

	But when Thou healest all my woe,
	And when my soul is all aglow
		With golden beams of peace,
	My fickle heart, contented well
	'Mid transient gayeties to dwell,
		Bids all devotion cease.

	Dear Lord! assist my feeble faith,
	That not alone it prove its worth
		'Neath sorrow's blighting dew—
	That not alone I seek for aid,
	When treading paths of fearful shade,
		But make me grateful too.
								  LU DALTON,


﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
				  —
			  BEAVER, Dec. 22nd, 1872.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	We send you a brief sketch of the Relief
Society in Beaver. It was organized Janu-
ary 11th, 1868 and since that time has been
steadily and constantly struggling on in our
humble sphere. We have bought a lot on
a block northwest of the public square; fenced
it; and built a house on it to meet in for
work, and to instruct and be instructed,
which has been dedicated and held sacred
for that purpose, with the exception of a day
school being taught in it about five months.
Our deeds of charity commenced in our
own city, and were extended to our sisters
on the Muddy, who were burned out; to the
Emigration fund, and to snndry other calls.
We have two shares in the Co-operative
store, have donated to the St. George
Temple, and intend to continue until it is
completed, that we may reap the reward of
our labors therein. Our exact reciepts and
disbursments I am not in possession of. On
November 17th the Society met, according
to previous appointment, when a vote was
taken to drop the word “Female,” from our
Society’s name which was carried unanimous-
ly. That our labors may be directed in the
right channel, and the right spirit attend them
is our humble desire. May God bless the
Editor of the Exponent, and all who con-
tribute to its columns; may their pens be
inspired, that the world may know that we
are not the “down-trodden” women that we
are represented to be! The Gospel has made
us free, free as the pure air we breathe in
our Mountain Home! May we ever defend
its pure principles!
				MRS. LUCINDA HOUD,
							President.
				MRS. RUTH TYLER,
					"  JANE FILLIS
							Counselors.
				    —
				Fillmore City,
				     December 24, 1872.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	Our Society was reorganized on the 20th
of July, 1870, Caroline Callister, President;
Delilah Olson and Melissa Samson Coun-
selors. The latter removing to her former
home, in Salt Lake City, Belinda Pratt was
chosen to fill her place. A spirit of union
exists among us, and we are trying to the
best of our knowledge to fill the mission
which has been assigned us.
	At a recent meeting the word “Female,”
in connection with “Society,” was dropped
[new column]
by a unanimous vote as suggested by sister
E. R. Snow. Our finances are as follows:
	Aggregate receipts, $1,360; disbursments,
$460. We have invested eighteen shares of
twenty-five dollars each in the Co-operative
store; three shares in the Stock herd, and
the balance consists in building material,
articles of clothing, etc.
	We hold meetings regularly semi-monthly,
and we intend during the "winter to increase
our means as far as possible for the further-
ance of our building, which we have com-
menced for the benefit of our Society.
Your very interesting paper is highly
appreciated by those who subscribe for it,
and is gaining great favor as it becomes
circulated. We are just sending a list of
new subscribers and we will do all we can
to sustain it.
					Delilah Olson.
				 —
		1st Ward, Salt Lake City,
					December 25th, 1872.
Dear Editor:—
	We will never forget the lessons received
at our last Relief Society meeting. Surely
kind angels ministered unto us, and the
Spirit of God was in our midst. Sympathy
with human suffering is certainly increased
in the hearts of the sisters through these
meetings being held. And is there ought
else that distinguishes man from the beasts
that perish, so much as that eternal principle
of fellow feeling? True, we find sympathy
among the beasts of the field and forest; that
sympathy which exists in nature’s self
between parent and offspring; that quality
than which there is no deeper, higher or
holier attribute, which extends from the
bosom of nature’s God throughout the whole
of His vast creation. But the difference
manifested, to which I refer, is in the finer,
stronger grade of sympathy which reason
awakens, and which the realizing sense of
our own weaknesses, our own liabilities to
err, helps us to exercise for all mankind.
It is this that kindles in our souls the in-
finite desire to see the nations of the earth
repent and turn from their wickedness, that
they may have peace and joy and eternal
life. And it is this which makes us grieve
that in our beautiful city, even here, we
behold the degradation and hopeless condi-
tion of our fallen sisters, whom, were it in
our power, we would raise up to new, bright
life and trust and blessedness. Does it not
belong to us and to the cause we have
espoused, by our sympathies to divide the
sorrows of others and to double their joys?
And sympathy excites to action—we lean
not on faith without works. We would
extend the hand as well as the voice of
charity to all who are in need, and who are
not so blinded by their ignorance as to re-
ject our proffered aid. 
Your visits here have done a great deal of
good. May God bless you in your endeavors
to promote His glorious cause—the cause of.
eternal truth. 
			Truly yours,
				Elizareth Harrison.
				  —
				Salt Lake City, Utah,
						January 8th, 1872.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	The following brief report of our Annual
Meeting, held December 18th, should have
reached you in time for your last issue, but
a multiplicity of causes prevented; yet,
remembering the old maxim of “better late
than never,” I send it now, trusting you
will excuse.
	Several invited guests were present,
among whom were Elder John Taylor and
our bishop, Brother Thomas Taylor, both of
[new column]
whom addressed us in a most pleasing and 
instructive manner.
	Our Quarterly and Yearly Reports were
presented after which a brief summary of
the doings of the Society, since its organiza-
tion, was read.
	We have now been organized five years,
and our esteemed President, Mrs. M. I
Horne, has deemed it proper to give a slight
summary of our labors, etc., during that
time.
	We have had on our books over two hun-
dred names, still our average attendance
has not been above seventy-five.
	We have labored under many disadvan-
tages, and our succes demonstrates what
may be accomplished by the united, indi-
fatigable efforts of a few.
	We have administered to the poor in
every case which has been brought to our
notice, to the extent of our means; and
have furnished suitable burial clothes for
some six or eight persons. We have also
held a Fair which proved quite a success.
We have assisted to erect our building at a
cost of $2,354, which is now owned by the
Relief Society, except about $500. Our
donations have amounted to $2,013:19(1/2), of
which $l,206:73(1/2) have been distributed to
the poor. We have also collected and paid
into the P. E. Fund $264:50, and made and
donated to the Tabernacle twent-six and one
half yards of rug carpet.
	I think we can truly report that our
Society has accomplished much good, and is
still in a flourishing condition.
		Elmina S. Taylor, Secretary.
				—
		American Fork, Jan. 7th, 1873.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	At a recent meeting held in this place,
Mrs. Mary Hindley presiding, a unanimous
vote was taken to dispense with the word
“Female” in connection with the name of
the Society. We had long thought it a
superfluous prefix.
	Our Society has done well so far. The
sick and needy are cared for and supplied
with the necessaries of life. We have a full
share in the Provo woolen factory, two
shares in the Co-operative store, a building
lot, also a lot, five rods by eleven, planted
with mulberry; besides having donated to
the Emigration Fund and other laudable
institutions.
	The sisters still desire to go on in this
great and good work, that they nmay ot only
be blessed themselves but prove a blessing
to all with whom they are connected, in
the building up of the Kingdom of God upon
the earth.
		    Mary Hindley, President. 
E. Eastmond, Secretary.
				—
	The General Retrenchment meettng in
Salt Lake City, was held January 4th, 1873.
Singing; prayer by Counselor Mrs. Zina D.
Young; Singing: Minutes of last meeting
were read by the Secretary, Miss Mary A 
Jenkins, and accepted. Counselor Mrs.
Elizabeth Howard read the Revelation given
to the Prophet Joseph Smith, on Celestial
Marriage. President Mrs. I. Horne wished
the sisters all a happy New Year, and hoped
that the ending of the year might find all
farther advanced in usefulness, wisdom and
goodness than its beginning does. Thought
the Revelation just read was appropriate,
and believed that the law of Celestial mar-
riage might be properly classed as a principle
belonging to physiology. She had not read
the Revelation for some time herself, and
felt benefitted from listening to it; her
memory was strengthened and she felt well
concerning plural marriage. She had lived
in it a good while, and had always got along


first-rate; had no desire to shun any trial
calculated to purify and exalt. Hoped the
present year might prove the brightest and
best of their lives.
	Counselor Mrs. E. Howard said she had
enjoyed the reading of the Revelation.
They should be charitable one towards an-
other, and say and do all in their power to
aid and encourage, as none were exempt
from trial; and seek also to govern them-
selves, for if they gave way to the Tempter,
they would become fretful, grumbling,
miserable creatures. Mothers should speak
plainly to their daughters, teaching them
to shield, honor and maintain their glory,
as true daughters of Zion. If they neglected
seeking to the Lord for guidance, they would
very soon be left to themselves, which was
a fearful fate.
	Mrs. Harriet Cook Young said she felt at
home among her sisters, though circum-
stances had prevented her meeting with them
for some time. On the obedience which
they render to the law of Celestial Marriage
depended, not only their own salvation, but
that of their children, and indeed, of the
whole human family to some extent. Those
who enter not into the Celestial law in this
life, cannot obtain a Celestial glory hereafter,
but will be servants to others, who, through
obedience and faithfulness have become
rulers themselves, and lawful heirs in the
Celestial Kingdom. As mothers and
daughters in Israel, how were they bearing
their portions? They should study the dis-
positions of their children, the circumstances
under which they were begotten, and try to
understand how best to instruct and influence
them.
	Counselor Mrs. B. W. Smith thought
they should be ever watchful and prayerful,
as the records of their lives were probably
of their own keeping; and from them they
would be judged, whether they be good or
evil. It was well to view the bright side
of a picture, and not anticipate trouble.
	Mrs. Howe said she could not speak
scientifically on physiology, but she believed
the law of Celestial marriage to be the only
principle upon which they could be exalted,
or the nations of the earth obtain salvation.
	Counselor Mrs. Z. D. Young said she
hoped that they might accomplish more
good in the present year than they had
ever before done. She heard an outsider
remark that when a Mormon apostatized he
went to nothing, for there was more reason
and sound philosophy in Mormonism than
in all the wisdom of the world beside.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks. Sing-
ing. Prayer by Counselor Mrs. E. Howard.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS. 
				——
	We are often asked for a prescription for
preternaturally wakeful persons. The “high
pressure” principle on which many of our
business men work their brains and abuse
their bodies, begets an irritable condition of
the nerves, and a morbid state of mind, very
antagonistic to quiet and refreshing sleep.
Such persons will often go to bed weary
and exhausted, but cannot sleep; or sleep
dreamily and fitfully; or lie awake for hours,
unable to sleep at all. We have tried many
experiments to induce sleep with more or
less success, and have read many recipes
which proved better in theory than in prac-
tice. The very best method we have yet
discovered is that of counting. Breathe
deeply and slowly (without any straining
effort) and, with every expiration, count,
one, two, three, etc., up to a hundred. Some
persons will be asleep before they can count
fifty in this manner. Others will count ten,
twenty, and thirty and then forgot them-
[new column]
selves and cease counting. In such cases
always commence at once. Very few per-
sons can count a hundred and find themselves
awake; but should this happen repeat the 
dose until cured. — "Waverly."
	Brown Bread. —One pint of sour milk,
one-half cup of molasses, one teaspoonful of
soda, and graham flour to make it a little 
stiffer than cake.
	Spoiled Dairy Produce.—Within the
last year we have had occasion to note
several instances where butter, cheese and
meat have been injured or spoiled from no
apparent cause unless by the impurity of the
salt used.
	Loaf Cake Doughnuts.—One cup of
sugar, one-half cup of milk, one cup of yeast,
spices and salt to taste. Stir as stiff as pos-
sible with a spoon and let it rise as bread,
then mould them up and cut, and let rise
an hour, or till they seem light, by the fire.
	Cure for Bone Felon.—The following
is said to be a good remedy for this cause of
suffering: “Take common rock salt, such as
is used for salting down pork or beef, dry it
in an oven, then pound it fine and mix with
spirits of turpentine in equal parts. Put in
a rag and wrap it around the parts affected,
and as it gets dry put on more, and in
twenty-four hours you are cured; the felon
is dead.”
	This led to various experiments, which
have fully proven the presence of sulphur in
much of the salt used throughout this Terri-
tory. Salt may be purified from the sulphur
it contains, by heat;—place the salt in a
dripping-pan, say about an inch or so deep;
place it in an oven under a hot fire and the
sulphur will evaporate, or it may be roasted
out on top of a hot stove or over a fire. No
salt should be used for fresh meat, butter, or-
cheese without testing it in this way, There
has been a loss of many hundreds of pounds
of dairy and butcher produce, the present
season within the range of our knowledge,
where salt both from Salt Lake and the mines
towards the Colorado has been used. One
dairyman east of this place, alone lost nearly
a ton of butter and cheese.—“Utah Pomol-
ogist.”
	Wearing Flannel.—Put it on at once,
winter or summer, nothing better can be
worn next to the skin than a loose red flan-
nel shirt—“loose,” for it has room to move
on the skin, thus causing a titillation which
draws the blood to the surface and keeps it
there; and, when that is the case, no one
can take cold; “red,” for white flannel fulls
up, mats together, and becomes tight, stiff,
heavy, and impervious. Cotton merely ab-
sorbs the moisture from the surface, while
flannel conveys it from the skin and deposits
it in drops on the outside of the shirt, from
which the cotton shirt absorbs it, and by its
nearer exposure to the exterior air it is soon
dried without injury to the body. Having
these properties, red woolen is worn by
sailors even in midsummer of the hottest
countries. Wear a thinner material in sum-
mer.—“Hall's Journal of health.”


[FOR THE EXPONENT.
	LIFE.

	The other day a poor man was thus con-
soling himself on future prospects. “Well”
said he, “I shall, eventually be as well off as
any of them; for, if this life is all—if when
we come to die, that really is the end of our
existence, the richest man can only have a
quiet resting-place beneath the sod, and that
I shall have as well. My bed will be as soft
and smooth as his. But if there is a future
life, there I shall come in for a much hand-


somer share than some of these lucky rascals,
whose wives look down on mine now, will
ever be able to obtain."
	Now, are not the majority of mankind
laboring under grave mistakes in rushing
through life, as though to build themselves 
up regardless of the wants of the rest of the
world, was their chief aim, and the one great
object for which they were created? Why
not make the most and best of life as we go
along, taking things calmly and quietly?
Not to sit down with folded hands, as if to
eat and drink were the only real comforts
we should seek for in life. But in looking
about us, find blessedness in blessing others;
living not for self alone, but for the good we
may do. There is no true satisfaction in
anything else. We may gain riches, power
and influence, but unless they are wisely
made use of for the benefit of mankind, as
well as for our own, they are but sources of
anxiety and care, and the soul starves for
want of pleasurable fruits upon which to
feast. If, as the poor man said, this life is
all, it is wisdom to enjoy it as much as
possible while it lasts, and not spoil all its
sweetness by running wild after the vain
things of earth. But, trusting that there is
a glorious future awaiting the wise and just,
it is worth while to prepare for the reception 
and appreciation of such a hereafter by 
living this life as wisely, justly, and cheer-
fully as we can.
								L.


﻿[For the Exponent.
			DEBT OF GRATITUDE.
	What’s this that romance repeats so often?
A brave, handsome, young fellow, honest as
the day is long, and twice as generous, risks
his own life to save that of a surpassingly
lovely and divinely beautiful maiden; whose
sweet voice and sweeter disposition at once
win the ardent, deep and deathless devotion
of her gallant preserver. Gratitude, and a
sudden reciprocity of hitherto unawakened
affections, pure as the “beautiful snow,”
which was going to bury her in a terrific
slide, had not our hero interfered; or, per-
haps mighty and ceaseless as the huge
stream of water in which she must have been
drowned but for his timely approach and
adventurous dexterity in rescuing her,
makes her his own forever. But I have
something else to say about causes for grati-
tude. What great thing is it, after all, for
one person to save the life of another? That
might cost but the exertion of a moment,
and is an act which common respect for
humanity would prompt any individual to
perform. A wretch may render services in
saving the life of an innocent child; and I
admit for the good he does he should be
thanked and rewarded. But still that isn’t
anything like stopping at home, doing the
work and letting some one else go to a
Christmas and New Year’s jollification.
Should it awaken half the gratitude, since it
calls not for half the sacrifice? Then, there
are other tokens of lasting love and generous
fidelity to be brought in. Think of a
family’s going without butter for a week, for
the sake of sparing to a favored one, the
new milk wanted for two days. Should
such things be forgotten? Get out with
your fated maidens and life preserving
heroes! For my part, I’d rather spend one
happy, happy week of holidays, than to risk
my life a dozen times for the sake of having
it saved; and am really more grateful to my
dear, good friends whose friendship proves
itself continuously, than I ever could be for
one deed of humanity.
							Frank.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿THE WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

 				LOUISE L. GREENE, Editor.

	Published semi-monthly, in Salt Lake City, Utah. Terms:
One copy one year, $2; one copy, six months, $1. Ten copies
for the price of nine. Advertising rates: Each square, eight
lines of nonpareil space, one time, $2; per month, $3.

Salt Lake City.
