﻿SPLINTERS.

	An American lady in Rome has a school
of two hundred pupils......£400 has been
contributed to the sufferers from the late in-
undations in Italy, by Queen Victoria......
The combined fortunes of Horace Greeley’s
daughters is estimated at $100,000.....The
Queen of Holland is said to be a good
French schollar, a successful writer, a hater
of the Germans, and a firm supporter of the
Bonapartes......A railroad was voted against
by the people of Jasper county, Mississippi,
lest the deer should be frightened away by
the locomotive......Stokes’ counsel tried to
prove that Fisk shot and would have stab-
bed himself, but was prevented from the
latter by the presence of Irwin.......Salem,
Oregon, has the measles, and prospects of a
new woolen factory......About a third of a
percussion cap would be anything but
an agreeable visitant in the eye, as a boy at
Brighton, Sacramento, can testify since
last Christmas morning......General Schenck
and his daughter are in Paris.........The
question of flogging in public schools is being
agitated in England, and the wretched system
is defended by some of the learned gentle-
men as a promotion of dicipline......Miss
Swayze is lecturing on “Free Hate.”.......
Paymaster Hodge, the army defaulter, has
been pardoned by President Grant.....One
of the leading bankers in Munich is a lady,
Miss Adele Spitzides....The “Temperance
Daughters” of Philadelphia have $18,000 in
the Treasury'......‘The Gossip,” a new humor-
ous London paper, will be edited by W. H.
Garrett......A lady pedestrian is said to be
preparing for a long western walk, from
Long Island to Omaha......Gladstone, it is
rumored is about to honor journalism, by
confering a baronetcy upon one of the pro-
prietors of the “Daily Telegraph,”......Gail
Hamilton is spending the winter at Wash-
ington, with Speaker Blaine’s family......
President Juarez of Mexico, left a minute
account of all sums of money receive
him from his elevation to the Presidency
......Mrs. Livermore lectures on “What
shall we do with our daughters.”......A new
nickle coinage is to be adorned with the
portrait of the late Thaddeus Stevens.....
“The Ladies Sociable,” is the name of a
society lately formed in Wyoming.......
Governor English, of Connect
icut, gives
$10,000 worth of law books to the library of
the New Haven Bar.......Horace Greeley is
not likely to be soon forgotten, as the litiga-
tion relative to his will has not the
appearance of an early settlement..........
The rivers in the valley of the Po, are again
rising and fresh inundations are threatened
.......Two brothers by the name of Locke,
one at Gould Station, near Swedesboro, the
other at Camden, both died the same morn-
ing, December22d......The internal revenue
receipts since July 1st have been $61,518,922.
......The Crown Prince Humbolt was
thrown from his carriage, while riding, in
Rome, a few days since, and sustained some
slight injuries. Had he been the heir ap-
parent to a royal peasant, not a word
would have been said about it.......They
stew cherries with much juice, in Berlin,
and make a dish called cherry-soup......Darwin
has published a new book, entiled “The Ex-
pressions of the Emotions in man and ani-
mals.”.......A steamer which recently arrived
at New York, had, among its passengers, a
sixteen-foot anaconda, a five-foot gorilla,
and a, Bengal tiger......Joaquin Miller has
completed his poem of the “Isles of the
Amazon.".....Three California Archbishops
have issued a bill prohibiting the intermar-
riage of Catholics and Protestants........
Having successfully demonstrated that he
can live on an average of fifty-fonr cents a
week.......Dr. Dio Lewis has started a news-
paper......The death of the popular English
poetess, Eliza Cook, which English papers
have been chronicling of late, we are happy
to record was a mistake......Paris newsboys
are to be uniformed in dark blue clothes and
scarlet cape.


﻿UTAH AND THE MORMONS.

	For a couple of weeks past we have scarcely
opened a newspaper that had not something to
say about Utah, the Mormons, polygamy, and
so on; some calling for stern, repressive legisla-
tion by Congress, against the “enormities of
the Mormons,” and others advocating a more
charitable and humane policy.
	Utah is unpopular with a certain class, and
they indulge in prolonged pharisaic howls
against the people who built up this Territory,
until the masses, who kuow nothing of the
actual state of affairs in Utah, are led to believe
that the “Mormons” are a horrible people
whom it would be doing God’s service to annhi-
latc, destroy, kill off, or in some way put them
out of existence to prevent them from galling
a sanctimonious but corrupt set of Aminidab
Sleeks, who profess morality, yet whose hypoc-
risy is glaringly exposed by the moral,
industrious and temperate habits of the Latter-
day Saints.
	The great outcry is raised against polygamy,
stigmatized as the sole remaining relic of bar-
barism which afflicts or disgraces the country.
Oh great and glorious country! unequalled
people who have no fouler stain upon their 
national escutcheon than tins, and it confined 
to a few valleys in the heart of the continent!
What a proud spectacle does that land present
where nothing but the purest chastity can be
found; where no places devoted to vice exists
in its great cities or smaller towns; where civil-
ization has elevated the people until they have
gained the complete mastery over every passion
and appetite; and where there is nothing to
disturb the serenity of their souls but the faith
of a single and not very numerous community;
that faith, too, inculcating the strictest chastity,
and holding virtue as a pearl above price, while
contending that plural marriages are consonant
with morality, physiology, and the revealed
will of the Almighty as expressed in the Scrip-
tures! But it may be that those who bestir
themselves so actively in this matter really
discard every principle and practice of the patri-
archal times, called by them “the age of
barbarism.” Honor and honesty were con-
sidered worthy the respect of all in those
barbaric times, and they are evidently but
lightly regarded now. Men were temperate
then and lived to a good old age; now they
boast of their intemperance, and hurry to
premature graves. People then were simple in
their tastes, moderate in their habits, and easely
satisfied. Now, wealth is eagerly sought after
to minister to a thousand idle fancies, or worse
still, to gratify appetites that revel in debauch-
ery. Honesty, industry, temperance and frugal
habits being relics of barbarism, the present
"civilized" age, it would appear, desires to be
rid of them, lest they, too, with that other bar-
baric practice of plural marriage, should stain
the proud escutcheon of progress which flaunts
so loftily on the breeze inscribed "Excelseor,"
	An apostle of old strongly inculcated charity
as the greatest of the virtues, and a proverb of
less ancient date says charity should begin at
home. We recommend to these very pious
people throughout the country, who are so
strongly exercised over the condition of Utah,
to open their eyes and look around them, and
see what a vast field there is, within sight of
their very doors, for the philanthropist, the re-
former and the man or woman of real charity
to labor in. And we can assure those at a dis-
tance, that the men in Utah who make the
loudest outcry against the immorality of the
"Mormons," are themselves the most immoral
men in the country, and openly boast that they
only use this cry as a means of stirring up pre-
judice to enable them to accomplish designs
which it were flattery to characterize as base.


﻿WORK.

	In the design of mortal existence, whatever
else might have been contemplated, we,
evidently were intended to work. No matter
what we are, or how situated, there is no get-
ting around the one inevitable fact that we must
labor, if we would be any thing of ourselves
in and to the world. The wives of farmers,
who are believed by some of our industrious
cityladies to have less care and responsibilities
upon them than they could possibly have
living in the city and striving to “keep up ap-
pearances,’ will always assert that they have 
more to do than they know how to manage,
and that it seems as though no one else could
be so pressed with work; cooking, perhaps, for
a half a dozen or a dozen, with eight or ten cows
to milk, pigs and chickens to feed, churning,
washing and ironing to do and the making and
mending of clothes for the family. Is not all
this enough to keep the country woman busy?
Is it any marvel that she finds but little time
for reflection or improvement of mind? But if
she is a young wife and mother, with but one
child, and property around her accordingly, it
is the same; she will always find something to
busy herself about, something that must be
done. True it is that we weary, and that pain
is produced by toil in a majority of cases; yet
we must work. The actual scholar, to acquire
the vast amount of intellectual power which is
striven for with quite as much zeal as money
seekers employ in hunting after gold, must
labor most assiduously in discipling the
mental faculties and keeping them in hourly
use. And it is certain that no treasure can be
so much enjoyed as that which is fairely earned,
and that no good thing is earned without exer-
tion; while the happiest remedy there is for the
sorrows and ills of life, is generous and wise em-
ployment in which they are forgotten.


﻿EXITS.

	The record of the recent past bears off its page
the names of an unusually large number of dis-
tinguished characters, who have retired behind
the scenes on the grand stage of mortal exist-
ence. The spectacle is one in which almost a
generation of prominent actors seem to have
been represented as passing away, and their
histories alone remain, to remind us that “we
may make our lives sublime.” Eighteen hun- 
dred and seventy-three begins its round of noble 
exits with that of the ex-emperor Louis
Napoleon, who died in exile at Chiselhurst, 
England, on the morning of Thursday, 9th inst.


The career of this man has been so interwoven
with the history of Europe for the last twenty
years, that those who have a knowledge of the
one cannot be ignorant of the other; for he
shaped for years the policy that governed that
continent. His life was an eventful one, full of
light and shade; and in elevation as in exile he
found his truest friend in a woman, she who
shared his imperial splendor and knelt by his
dying bed at Chiselhurst, the empress Eugiene.
But death, when it comes, strikes alike the
highest and the lowliest; and in the tomb,
whether it be a mighty mausoleum or an
humble grave, emperor and peasant are but
equal remnants of mortality.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿THE CHANGE OF NAME.

	The Relief Societies of the following named
settlements have sent us reports containing
accounts of their properly renouncing the prefix
“Female” in connection with the name of the
Society: Smithfleld, Farmington, Ogden, Wil-
lard City, Brigham City, Kamas Prairie,
Alpine City, Santaquin, Salem, Nephi, Big
Cottonwood, 1st Ward In Provo City, Bounti-
ful, Ephraim, Wanship, Logan, Cedar City,
Mendon, Heber City, Fillmore, Beaver, and
American Fork—twenty-two. And the newly
organized branch at Riverdale, which takes the
correct term without making use of the super-
fluous word, makes the number twenty-three.
But these do not constitute a majority of the
branch Societies throughout the Territory,
Send on your minutes, Secretaries; we are
anxious to leave the “F” off the heading of the
Society reports, but cannot do so legally while
only a minority of those concerned have sanc
tioned the proceeding.


﻿HOME AFFAIRS.

	THE SABBATH School entertainment, referred
to in our issue of January 1st, was given in the
Thirteenth instead of the Fourteenth Ward in
this city. The figure in the notice was a typo-
graphical error.
	A NUMBER of those who subscribed for the
EXPONENT with its first issue, for six months,
have not renewed their subscriptions, although
the paper is now in its eight month since
started. Will they please renew at once, or
notify us that they wish their papers stopped?
	A NEW Associate Justice for Utah, in the
person of Wm. W. Mitchell, has been appointed
in place of Judge Strickland, who presided over
the first judicial district, holding court in
Provo. Judge Mitchell must be a very poor
specimen of a just judge if he is not an improve-
ment on the man he succeeds.
	NEWS OF our friends, the Palestine party,
come almost daily to hand, and only agreeable
accounts of good health and enjoyable seasons
have been yet received. They were at Nice on
Christmas day, and, doubtless, had a nice time;
on New Year’s day they reached Milan, and if
their expectations have been so far successful,
they are to-day at Pisa.
	WE ACKNOWLEDGE the receipt of a pleasant
invitation to attend a Relief Society Party of
the First Ward, held in the neat, new brick
building of that Ward, which, although not
yet completed is so far advanced as to be an
agreeable place of entertainment for an even-
ing’s enjoyment. We understand that the
proceeds are to be devoted to the finishing of
the meeting house, which is certainly credit-
able, and we wish the sisters much success in
the undertaking.
	THE PRESENT winter, so far, has been an un-
usual one, for the extreme and sometimes
sudden changes from frost to rain, and from


intense cold to mildness of atmosphere. As a 
result colds, catarrhs, bronchial affections and
pneumonia have been prevalent, and a number
have died of the last-named disease. Parents
should be especially careful of their children,
in such sudden changes of weather, and endeavor
to so arrange their clothing that the body will
be kept, as nearly as possible, at an even tem-
perature.


﻿CORRESPONDENCE OF MISS SNOW.

			London, Nov. 25, 1872.

MY DEAR LULA:—
	In my last, I left you at Central Park in
New York. Early next morning, (5th of
Nov.,) in company of brother Feramorz Lit-
tle, sister Little, Clara L. and her little
brother Frank—also brother Spencer Claw-
son, I took the cars for West Point where
we arrived at 10 a. m., after crossing the
beautiful Hudson river on a steam ferry
boat.
	West Point is a delightful point. The
natural scenery is variegated and imposing.
Having one hour to while away before we
could have an interview with the object of
our visit, Mr. Willard Young, we strolled
around the premises, viewing the objects of
interest, and some things, which to us,
promoters of peace, were not particularly
interesting. Great numbers of cannon, of
various forms and sizes were to be seen in
different places,—some from Mexico, others
from the Crimea, preserved as specimens;
also a portion of the ponderous iron chain
which the Americans stretched across the
Hudson, in the Revolutionary War, to im-
pede the British ships. I admired the
beautiful green area, with its fine shade
trees, surrounded by rugged hills; on the
top of the highest, in the distance, Putnam’s
Fort is to be seen, which is said to remain
precisely as the veteran General, whose
name it bears, built, and left it. The next
hour was very pleasantly spent in “tete a
tete” with the young military student,
whom we found in good health and spirits,
and returned to New York at 4 p. m.
	That evening Mr. Richardson and lady
called on us—he has been a traveler in Pales-
tine and gave us much interesting informa-
tion respecting it. Before we left New
York, the number in the hotel, from Utah,
amounted to twenty.
	On the morning of the 6th we went on
board the steamer Minnesota and through
the kindness of brother W. C. Staines, were
very comfortably quartered, and soon
launched forth into the Hudson and from
thence into the broad ocean.
	I saw the ocean for the first time, and was
in nothing so disappointed as its color,
which is, when calm, dark, very dark, yes,
positively black, and to me appeared like a
mass of lava. I wanted to look at it poetic-
ally, and watched intently for “blue waves,”
or even “green waves,” but to no purpose
except the waters were stirred, then, be-
neath the white foam the blue, and more
particularly the green were observable. I
was but little sea sick, and, although our
passage was rough, I rather enjoyed it. It
was raining when we arrived in Liverpool
on the morning of the 19th. The North-
western Hotel, where we stopped, is a finely
constructed building, with many accommo-
dations, but is conducted altogether too
ceremoniously to consult convenience and
comfort. We staid two days in the dense,
interminable fog of Liverpool, where they
have more light by night from gas than the
sun affords them by day.
	The first day I spent in writing, with a very
agreeable and interesting visit to the sanc-
tum of President Carrington; on the second,
a very severe cold prevented my visiting
the Workhouse as I had anticipated—a sketch
of which my brother Lorenzo has written to
the Deseret News.
	We left Liverpool for London by train, at 9 a.
m. and arrived at 2 p.m.—two hundred and
three miles. The cars in England, instead of
being open from end to end and the seats all
in one view, as in America, are partitioned
into boxes containing two seats, the first
class seating six persons, three on each seat,
face to face, and the second class, eight—
four on each seat. These boxes open only
on the outside, so that there is no communi-
cation between the different sections. Here
the distances are so short compared with
those of America that sleeping cars are not
in use.
			Yours, with love to all,
							E. R. Snow.


WOMAN’S AGENCY.
			—
	A temperate life and a prayerful heart
produce a fruitful mind, well stored with
useful knowledge. There has been a great
deal said and written concerning woman’s
rights. It is a subject that I feel interested in,
enough so to attempt to express some of my
views in relation to it, through the columns
of the EXPONENT. There is one fact that I
wish to prove, by reference to my own ex-
perience, having been eighteen  years a
“Mormon.” When I first heard a “Mor-
mon” missionary preach, I was twenty-eight
years of age; and I believed every word he
said. Why? Because I believed the Bible,
and he did not preach anything but what
could be proved from the sacred book. Now,
what was I to do? I had a husband, father
and brothers, all intelligent, honorable
men. Was it my duty to ask them if the
Gospel was true, and whether I must obey
it or not? Or had God given me my own
agency, to think and act in such matters for
myself? My father was a deist, and that
was the tradition under which I was
brought up. The “Mormon” Elder told the
people if they believed and obeyed the
requirements of the Gospel, they should
know of the doctrine, whether it was of God
or of man; and that the signs followed the
believers as in ancient times. Well, I
thought if God is the same yesterday, to-
day and forever, I will go to Him on my
bended knees and ask him if he has revealed
Himself from the Heavens and sent forth
messengers to gather His Elect from the
four quarters of the earth. I covenanted
with the Lord to be a faithful witness to
this testimony, provided He gave it to me
as I had read of its being given on the day
of Pentecost. I would bear testimony to all
the world that I received it in answer to my
prayer, leaving no room for doubt. I would
like to fulfill that promise to the Lord. And
if any person wishes to know why I am here
in Salt Lake City, living in polygamy, I
think this is sufficient reason; and my
prayer is that I never may live to be
ashamed of this testimony, for it is the
testimony of Jesus. And it is this that has
placed me in the position I occupy to-day.
All the men in Christendom could never
have made me submit to live with a man
that had more than one wife, had I not be-
lieved and become convinced that God
approved it, as much now as He did when
Jacob had Rachel and Leah. But to say
nothing of revelation, the system of plurality
of wives is far superior to that practiced by
the world. I have more to say of woman’s
agency.
							W. E.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿EMPTY.
			    —
Your cozy crib is in the corner yet;
	I sit and watch it, just as day is dead; 
You cannot press again, my vanished pet.
	Its pillow with your drowsy golden head.

You cannot reach plump arms to get my kiss,
	Or dart about with rosy naked feet,
Babbling soft syllables of that and this,
	A tiny nightgowned fairy, blithe and sweet.

Once and for all you have lain down to rest,
	Not to rise up because of birds or beams,—
Once and for all, wi h white flowers on your breast,
To slumber coldy and to dream no dreams.]

Empty the home where, frolicsome and fair,
	Your precious presence made so bright a part;
Empty your little crib, your clothes, your chair,
	But emptiest of all your mother’s heart!
						[“Atlantic Monthly."


﻿WOMAN’S VOICE.

EDITOR EXPONENT:—
	Believing that all who have been blessed
with the light of truth, with a knowledge of
the Gospel of Christ as restored to the earth
in this generation, by the power of God,
through His servant Joseph, should take
active and earnest part in seeking to build
up and establish the Kingdom of Righteous-
ness, I wish to offer a few words to my
sisters through the columns of your paper.
I would ask why we are here in these
valleys, we who have left homes and friends
in other lands, where we could have lived
and labored as our forefathers did before us,
without the trouble, expense and fatigue of
emigrating to Utah? The answer is trite,
but nevertheless true, with many of us; we
came here for the purpose of serving God.
And what is it to serve Him as He requires?
As we understand it, it is to “live by every
word which proceedeth from His mouth.”
	Among the many commandments which
He has given for the instruction and salva-
tion of His people, there are numerous
instances where we are directed to dili-
gence and warned against slothfulness. The
prophet Ezekiel reckoned idleness one of
the three great sins of Sodom. “Behold 
this was the iniquity of thy sister Sodom:
pride, fullness of bread, and abundance of
idleness was - in her.” The Creator has so
constituted His children that they cannot be 
happy and idle at the same time. The most
irksome task which can be imposed on man
is that of doing nothing; while not alone in
the possession lies the charm of that which
is valuable, but the chief delight is in honest
acquirement. “Things won are done—joy’s
soul lies in the doing.” There is labor for
the head as well as the hands. and it is noble
and God-like to exercise and improve the
mental gifts with which we have been blest,
to cultivate our intellectual, immortal parts,
and to increase the power of thought, and
consequent individuality, as well as to bring
theories into practice, and work with our
hands. Permit me to say that I think if many
of our ladies would do a little more brain
work of the right kind, instead of using so
freely those frivolities which are ever pre-
sent in the idle mind, and are brought forth
without exertion, it would be of great worth
to themselves and also to the community.
				Respectfully, 		NORA.


﻿YOUNG ladies of this city, going home
from Sabbath School, on the Sunday
between Christmas and New Year’s, were
heard singing a very appropriate parody
on “Beautiful Snow,” commencing with “O!
the mud, the tractable mud! Filling the
streets like a halcyon flood! Splashing to
cover a fair lady’s feet; Slopping about o’er
the people you meet. Fresh as an idea nip-
ped in the bud—There’s nothing so real as
the tractable mud!”


FLOWERS.
					—
	The ties of friendship are stronger and
more certain than those of kindred.
	Rejoice in having done well, but leave
others to tell of it; self praise spoils the
effects of a generous and noble action.
	Real modesty and affectation are so
different in their natures, that while one is
commendable the other is contemptible.
	There is nothing purer than honesty—
nothing warmer than love—nothing richer
than wisdom—nothing brighter than virtue
—and nothing more steadfast than faith.
These united in one mind, form the purest,
sweetest, warmest, brightest, and most
steadfast happiness.
	You may become what you will, if you
only work for it with all the powers God has
given you. Choose then, some worthy am-
bition. Do not let it be riches or honor
among men, or anything the Lord does not
esteem. Choose some great, noble purpose
on which you can ask His blessing. Then
work for it with all your might, and you
will not fail of the victory.
	Many people imagine that courage is
confined to the field of battle. There could
be no greater mistake. Even contentions
with men, unavoidable contentions, are not
by any means limited to public battle-fields.
And there are other struggles in private life
—struggles with adverse circumstances—
struggles, it may be, with habits of appetites
or passions—all of which require as much
courage, and more of perseverance, than the
brief, exciting encounter of battle.
	What spectacle more pleasing does the
earth afford than a happy woman, contented:
in her sphere, ready at all times to benefit
her little world by her exertions, and trans-
forming the briars and thorns of life into
roses of Paradise by the magic of her
touch? There are those who are thus happy
because they cannot help it; no misfortunes
dampen their sweet smiles, and they diffuse
a cheerful glow around them, as they pursue
the even tenor of their way. They have
the secret of contentment, whose value is
above the pilosopher’s stone; for without
seeking the baser exchange of gold which
may buy some sort of pleasure, they convert
everything they touch into joy. What
their condition is makes no difference. They
may be rich or poor, high or low, admired
or forsaken by the fickle world; but the
sparkling fountain bubbles up in their hearts
and makes them radiantly beautiful.
	Life bears us on like a stream of a mighty
river. Our boat glides down the harrow
channel through the playful murmurs of the
little brook and the winding of the grassy
borders. The trees shed their blossoms
over our young heads; the flowers on the
brink seem to offer themselves to our young
hands; we are happy in hope, and grasp
eagerly at the beauties around us; but the
stream hurries on, and still our hands
are empty. Our course in youth and man-
hood is along a deeper and wider flood, amid
objects more striking and mahificent. We
are animated at the moving picture of enjoy-
ment and industry passing around us—are
excited at some short-lived disappointment.
The stream bears us on, and our joys and
griefs are left behind us. We may be ship-
wrecked—we cannot be delayed; whether
rough or smooth, the river hastens to its home,
till the roar of the ocean is in our ears, and
the tossing of the waves beneath our feet,
and land lessens from our eyes, and the floods
are lifted up around us, and we take our leave
of earth and its inhabitants, until of our
farther voyage there is no witness save the
Infinite and the Eternal.


THE SEX.

	Anna Dickinson lectures on “Things
Hoped For.”
	Laura Ream is candidate for the office of 
State Librarian of Indiana.
	A daughter of the Rothschilds is about
to be married to a poor man, and this poor
man is a Christian.
	The married ladies of Hannibal, Missouri
have formed a “Come Home Husband Club.”
It is about four feet long and has a brush at
the end of it.
	Miss Sarah W. Barton, an American girl,
now studying music in Florence, is to be
prima donna of opera in Warsaw, Poland, the
coming winter.
	Miss Hotchkiss sensibly thinks it high
time that women should try to invent some
graceful and becoming style of dress which
would be consistent with health and free-
dom.
	Miss Mary Thurman; eldest daughter of
the Ohio Senator, is aoon to be married to a
naval officer named Herrman, which goes
to show that their domestic differences will
be over their T.
	Miss Rhoda Bou ghton, the author o
“Goodbye, Sweetheart,” etc., (says a corres-
pondent,) is young, pretty, fascinating, and
rather wild. She is the eldest daughter of
au English clergyman, and at present resid-
ing with an uncle in Devonshire, England.
“Cometh up as a Flower.” was written by
her when only sixteen.
	Miss Olive Risley Seward will hereafter
make her home at Fredonia with her father,
H. A. Risley, The old homestead has been
handsomely fitted up, where with the hand-
some income of fifty thousand dollars be-
queathed to her by Seward, she can live in
elegant retirement—if she has the notion to
live that way.
	The Queen of England has reversed the
gallantry of Sir Walter Raleigh, who spread
his rich plush coat out for his sovereign to
tread upon. The Duke of Sutherland is
having a shaft sunk in his estate to improve
some mines, and being told of these opera-
tions while there, the Queen expressed a de-
sire to see them. The Duke escorted her
thither, and while her Majesty was standing
on the bank, inspecting the work, it com-
menced to rain. A few yards off one of the
men was sawing timber for the shaft, and,
heedless of the rain, continued his work
without a coat. Presently he was surprised
to receive a light touch, and on looking up
perceived the Duke, who laid a costly rug,
over his shoulders, at the same time ex-
claiming. “The Queen requested me to
present you with her own rug, you may
keep and wear it.”
	A Brussels correspondent says that re- 
cently a poor crippled girl, to save her sister
from a week’s imprisonment, to which
she had been condemned for some slight
offence, herself confessed the crime, and
went there instead of the offender. The
fraud was detected after she had suffered-
the punishment, and when brought before
the judge she was sentenced to three months
imprisonment, which, however, she will
now escape. Her answer to the question
what induced her to substitute herself for
her sister was, that being a cripple, she
could not sustain her aged mother, which,
her sister had done for a long time, “and,
therefore,” she added, “it was better for me
to go to prison and let her be free.” A sub-
scription has been started for the girl, and
much has been subscribed to aid her and the
family of so devoted a heroine.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿[Written for the Exponent.
 			MY FRIEND.
				—
	She is not at all like other people; she is
her own singular, primitive self. Every
person has peculiarities sacred to the one
and possessed by no other; but her original-
ities are unprecedented, and entirely her
own. Her good sense is a safe and sure
backing for her in all circumstances. She
always dresses in good style but with such
superior taste that whatever she puts on is
becoming to her. Nothing is ever new to
her; she seems to have a presentiment of
occurrences before they take place, to antici-
pate and be prepared for just what transpires,
and consequently to understand the precise
method of undertaking and managing any-
thing that comes along. She knows too
much to be dull and miserable, and can
make herself at home, at ease, and agreeable
wherever she may be placed. And yet,
withal, she entertains an intense longing to
pass from this life into one of higher intelli-
gence, where the mind may have broader
scope and freer access to the inexhaustable
fountains of pure and eternal knowledge.
She seems to have drawn forth so much of
the finer grade of excellence which belongs
to earthly substances, that her mind now
reaches forward, grasping at something
beyond, which, while clogged with this
mortal coil, it cannot fully comprehend. It
is not in the daily routine of busy, careless
life that the real sublimity and grandeur of
her strangely beautiful intellectual powers
are clearly and unmistakably visible, though
in common conversation she expresses much;
and much in her expressive language which
would be self conceit and egotism in another,
portrays only self knowledge and absolute
strength of character in her. She is indeed,
a strange composition, wonderfully endowed.
But to understand what she is, one must
become familiar with her ways, intimate
with herself, and hear her in a private ram-
ble, back over some of the quiet windings,
twistings and twinings of the threads of her
past life. Then her singularities are plain
to be observed and comprehended. She
may be still an anomaly, but it is clearly ap-
parent why; and the sympathies which her
soul stirring words awaken are ever plea-
sant and intelligent, though of a strangely
solemn and dignified nature.
								 G.


﻿AN AFFECTING INCIDENT.
			   —
	An affecting incident of the sufferings,
of working women rendered houseless and
penniless by the Boston fire, is told thus:
	During the first week after the fire a very
pretty young girl, evidently not far from
twenty, came shyly in and took a seat away
from the crowd. She was quite noticeable,
both from her face and manner and her
dress, which, although it was only black
alpaca, was worn with a genuine lady-like
style and daintiness; everything about her
was scrupulously neat, and there was an air
of refinement about her that told of better
surroundings and different modes of life
from most of the women there assembled. A
member of the committee, wondering at
her appearance, went to her and accosted her
rather brusquely:
	“Well, what are you here for?”
	A painful flush rose quickly to the sensi-
tive face, and she replied:	
	“I heard—some one told me,—that all who
needed temporary assistance were to apply
here.”
	“Well,” was the answer, “why do you
come? you don’t look as though you needed


anything, you are dressed well enough,
and—”
	But the girl waited for no more. She
sprang to her feet, the tears streaming from
her eyes, her face crimsoned with shame,
and turned towards the door. Several ladies
who had been watching the scene, and who
appreciated the situation, instantly were at
her side. One of them led her to a seat,
and putting motherly arms around her,
soothed her as she would a grieved child.
Then came the question, quick and sympa-
thetic:
	“What is it, my dear? What can we do
for you?”
	Through the sobs came the answer:—
	“I never had to ask for help before, and I
had to struggle so hard with myself before I
could come this morning. I lost my place
by the fire, but I am to have another soon,
but, I must live until then. I cannot stay
in my boarding-place unless I pay my board
every week, and there is no place for me to
go, and I don’t know what I shall do.”
	“How much do you require?”
	The sum was told, and little enough it
was; in an instant it was in her hand, with
an addition beside from the pocket of her
new-found friend: and she went away happy
and safe. This is but one of the incidents
of a like character that are constantly occur-
ring, giving these women such insight as
they never had before into the lives of that
large class of their sex, of whom Henry
Ward Beecher once said: “There is only a
cambric needle between them and hell.”
This phase of poverty is a new revelation to
them, and they are learning to meet it
properly, and to feel a tender pity for those
who strive to hide it, only revealing the
truth at last when it becomes a bitter neces-
sity. That they did not understand it at
first is not but the fault of the persons them-
selves as the effect of their education. There
are traditional manners of managing the
poor that they quickly found were not prac-
tical, and they learned the ways fast as
possible, doing what they did with their
whole hearts if their tongues and fingers did
bungle a little- at first—“Pioneer.”


﻿WOMAN LOAFERS.
			—
	The following, furnished by a correspon-
dent of the Cincinnati “Commercial” is good
enough to read and reflect upon. Will some
of our young ladies who are accustoming
 hemselves to “look down on labor” give
the subject a little consideration?
	“Sometimes it seems to me that the whole
social life of woman is based upon two ideas
—aping and envy. The woman who lives in
two little back rooms up stairs, apes and en-
vies the woman with the large house, and
the woman with a large house is in a steady
fever of fear and fretting lest she be not
recognized by the woman with the stone
front house and a circus chariot, with a clown
dressed up in buttons. No matter how high
up a woman climbs upon the social ladder,
there is always still some higher hight
which she can’t scale, some other over her
head who looks down upon her, and gives
her days and nights of jealousy and head-
ache. Not a blessed woman of them all is
happy and content in the soil wherein fate
has planted her, but must needs spend her
time and strength in frantic efforts to pull
herself up by the roots and transplant her-
self somewhere else. The snobs who ride
circus chariots curl up their noses at the
schoolmistresses, the schoolmistresses look
down from their infinite hights upon the
clerks and dressmakers, and the clerks and
dressmakers in turn pay it off with interest


upon the kitchen girls. What nonsense,
what tom-foolery it all is! Is a schoolmis-
tress any better or higher than a washer-
woman? Not a bit. And are not school-
mistresses and washerwomen both infinitely
better and higher up than the lady loafer,
the Steve Moores of the female sex, in short?
Yea, verily.
	Women are the most-absurd creatures, all
owing to the silly fancy that it is lady-like
to be a loafer. A little time ago one of my
small busy housekeeper friends asked a lady
loafer caller what had become of her sister.
	“She goes down town every day, now,”
said the lady loafer, hesitatingly. 
	“Is she at work?” inquired the house-
keeper.
	“Well, ye—yes,” said the lady loafer, still
more hesitatingly.
	“What does she do?” inquired my friend.
	The lady loafer stammered and backed off
painfully two or three times, and finally
made a clean breast of it, thusly:
	“She—she—she has charge of the trim-
mings in a—dressmaking establishment.
She has nothing to do with the dressmaking.
She has charge of the trimmings. It’s not
a common dressmaking establishment. It’s
one of the fashionable dressmakers. She
won’t make a dress for a poor person.”
	“Oh, glory! Fan me with a cabbage
leaf!" quoth my friend, the small busy house-
keeper.
	The idea of a body’s dressmaker setting
herself to be “highstocracy.” The laurels of
the lady loafers in the circus chariots are
quite withered after that. And yet, why
not a dressmaker? Isn’t money made at
dressmaking quite as aristocratic as money
made with pork, whisky, hides, soap, tallow
and tobacco? Those are the material foun-
dations upon which our American nobility
found their airy castles of aristocracy, you
know, and I’m sure dressmaking is quite as
high and honorable as any of them.


﻿Gentle oxen, suitable for hauling a
basket phaeton about town, are in request
among the ladies of Titusville. 
	A New-fashioned square dance is called
the Pyramid, and being similar to the quad-
rille, is likely to become quite as popular.
	A pair of ladies’ rubber overshoes were
lately found in the stomach of an ox at
Orange, Vt. What became of the lady is
not known.
	“I’ve always heard the world was round
and now I know it,” were Seward’s first
words to a concourse of friends who welcomed
him to Auburn after his circumnavigation
of the globe.	
	“Six feet in his boots!” exclaimed Mrs.
Beeswax; “what will the imprudence of this
world come to, I wonder? Why, they
might as well tell me the man had six heads
in his hat.”
	Dickens says: “I have known vast quan-
tities of nonsense talked about bad men not
looking you in the face. Don’t trust to that
conventional idea. Dishonesty will stare you
out of countenance any day in the week, if
there is anything to be got by it.
	Miss Knight, of Boston, has invented a
machine for making paper bags, and is hav-
ing a number of them manufactured at
Chicopee under her own supervision. The
workmen employed were at first skeptical
as to her mechanical ability, but she cured
them of this by going daily and working
among them, detecting mistakes and im-
proving plans with a keener eye than any
man in the works. Her invention is said to
be an invaluable one, and she will make a
fortune by it.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


As a colored boy was following a whistle
of great volume he met an inebriated individ-
ual, who, taking umbrage at the boy’s
musical jollity, gave him a round buffet on
the side of the head. Unluckily for him,
however, the liquor he was carrying made
him top heavy and he was thrown from his
feet by the momentum acquired in deliver-
ing the blow. The boy, half crying, took
advantage of the opportunity and repaid in
kicks the cuff he had received, taking care,
however, to get out of the way before his
opponent regained his feet.
	In his recent talk with Gambetta, Senator
Sumner lamented as a great defect of France
the want of sincere and rational religion,
and expressed his opinion that one of the
most harmful things ever said by a great
man in a position to influence his contem-
poraries and posterity was the famous “mot”
of Henry IV., “Paris vaut bein une messe;”
the free translation of which is that it is well
worth while to change one’s religion and
become a Catholic instead of a Protestant in
order to be master of Paris. The profound
immorality of such skepticism, coming from
a king reputed one of the best that ever
reigned, had, in Sumner’s opinion, struck
deep root in France, and produced immense
evil by its example.
	When I was a boy, says a distinguished
man, my first saving of ten cent pieces,
earned by Saturday afternoon work—for
school kept half a day on Saturday.then—
were expended in buying a heifer calf.
Then I worked on, and paid my father a cer-
tain sum each month for keeping. When
the calf was one year old I traded it for two
steer calves, and now had to put in good and
strong to pay for their keeping; but I
occupied all my spare time in teaching these
calves to work in the yoke, and at one year
old they would gee and haw as well as
old oxen, and my father paid me for their 
use in leading the team for breaking his two
three-year-olds. Again, I had a piece of
ground each year, after I was fourteen, that
I could plant and work on shares; and if I
wanted help, why I had to give two days of
my time to the hired man’s one day. I grew
just what my fancy and reading dictated
and from the proceeds I dressed as well as
any of the boys at the present time. I always
had some time, to play, time to read, and now
look back, with love and pleasant thoughts,
to the old farm and the firm-hand which
taught me to use tools, and whipped me
when I neglected to drive the team out
straight at the end of the furrow in plowing.
The remembrance of my boyhood has always
iduced me to favor all items of encourage-
ment at home on the farm; and I believe, if
it were more generally practiced, we should
have: more good farmers and less broken-
down merchants or loafing, hanging-on,
time-serving clerks, ready for anything ex-
cept honorable labor and usefulness.

[new column]
			WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

			A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL.

   The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani- 
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.
   They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply
   Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!'’
   For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.
   The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief graphic summary of current, news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on lending topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.
   It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.
   Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connect d with their workings;
and to this end  he Presidents and Secretaries of the
various societies thr ughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
   Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female 
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal, 
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.	
   WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto. 
   The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in 
Utah.	
	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year,	    $2.00	1 Copy six months. $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months, 9.00 
   No subscription received for less than six months.
   It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting 
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.

   Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,
Utah.

DR. W. H. GROVES,
						DENTIST,
   Office Second South Street. Three doors west of
Great Western Hotel, half a block east of Elephant
Store, Salt Lake City.
			Office Hours from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

﻿Vol. I. 	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JANUARY 31, 1873.	 No. 17.


﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.

	THE WOMAN papers are divided in their
allegiance to the dominant party of the
country.
	THE DEATHS of nine eminent editors were
chronicled last year; seven in the United
States and two in France.
	ANOTHER EMINENT man gone! Lord
Bulwer Lytton, poet, novelist and dramatist,
reposes in Westminster Abbey.
	HUNGARY SHOWS its respect to the name
of the Emperor Francis Joseph, by im-
prisoning James L. Watson, an American,
traveler, for speaking derogatory of him.
	AN ALAMEDA California, mother, it is
said, has named her infant Laura D. Fair.
Tastes differ. Would she like the little
one to earn as much unenviable notoriety as
the one it is named after?
	WHAT IS the price of a seat in the United
States Senate? and, do honorable Senators
expect to steal enough during their terms to
repay the outlay and interest upon it? are
questions pertinent to the hour.
	THERE IS one officer in the United States,
who has served in the same calling thirty-
two years, and has not been found guilty of
defalcation or forgery; it is Charles S. Clark,
veteran fireman of Boston, now aged sixty-
three years.
	NAPOLEON IS said to have died of a large
clot of blood in one of the blood vessels near
the heart, and the learned call it "embo-
lism.” It was sufficient to kill him, even if
the heavy word is derived direct from the
Greek “embolus.”
	MILAN IS subscribing money for the erec-
tion of a monument to the memory of the
Emperor Napoleon III. Italy gratefully
remembers the efforts of the late French
Emperor to solidify the country, if not to
secure Italian unity.	
	THE STATE Temperance Convention of
Connecticut adopted a straight temperance
platform, with a full ticket of State officers.
No subject calls more loudly for attention,
or more necessarily demands it at present,
than that of temperance, and its friends have
need to be stirring themselves.
	SEVENTY-FIVE thousand men and many
millions of dollars are said to have been
sacrificed by Spain during the last four
years, to surpress the insurrection in Cuba,
which looks as formidable now as at
almost any other time. So much for forcing
on a people an unpopular government.
	THE VOORHEES anti-Mormon bill, of the
last session of Congress, it is said, has been
resurrected with a slightly changed form,
and presented in the Senate by Senator
Logan. Its object is to vest in the hands of
three persons—a U. S. judge, a district at-
torney and a U. S. Marshal—all power in
Utah; when the game of plunder can begin.
	MRS. GREELEY, whose ambitions are
reported to have been more intense than
those of her husband, is said to have made
the assertion that if she possessed the health,
the youth and the advantages of her
daughters she would shake the world!
With such a mother and the inimitable
Horace for a father, what may not be ex-
pected of the daughters? 
	THE HEADS of the Orleanist and Legiti-
mist aspirants to the French crown are 
reported to have come to an understanding,
and Chambord, chief of the Legitimists, is
quibbling over the question of the royal
standard. This is not waiting for dead
men’s shoes, but quarreling over who shall
wear them, and what kind of a shoe-string
they shall be tied with, before possession of
them is obtained. Napoleon is dead, but
the Bourbons do not yet rule France.
	LOUISIANA HAS two governors, each claim-
ing to be the lawful one; and two legislatures
with similar claims, that have elected two 
men to the United States Senate to fill the
seat of one. A great curse of the world 
is being over much governed, and Louisiana
seems to be in a fair way to have more than
her share of it; for there is danger, that in
addition to her double-headed monster at
home, federal authority may step in and set
both aside, or recognize one to the rejection
of the other, regardless of the will of the
majority of the citizens.
	ANOTHER MONARCH is about setting out
on his travels. The Shah of Persia pro-
poses to leave Teheran, the capital of Persia,
in April, with the intention of visiting
Constantinople, St. Petersburg, Berlin,
Vienna, London and Paris. He might add 
to the interest and attractiveness of his
journey by lecturing on what he knows
about famines, and telling how many of the
two millions of his subjects who died of
starvation might have been saved by the
use of the money which his European tour
will cost.
	IT IS reported that the Pope says the
bodies of the Apostles Philip and James
were discovered on Wednesday, January
5th, last, in the Church of the Apostles at
Rome. Were they embalmed? And if so
how did the poor followers of the despised
Savior secure such expensive treatment for
their bodies eighteen centuries ago? And
if they were not embalmed, how were they
preserved? Miraculously? And how were
they identified when found? It sounds
like a nice little religious romance, or what
in olden times used to be called “a pious
fraud;” which even Popes were supposed to
occasionally participate in.
	WOMAN SUFFRAGE is winning its way,
notwithstanding the ridicule with which it
is met, and which has to do duty for argu-
ment. A few days ago, on a debate in
Congress on the admission of Colorada as a
State, Mr. Sargent, of California, offered an
amendment that in the admission of new
States the right of suffrage should not be
abridged because of sex. Nothing may
come of this during the present Congress,
which expires on the 4th of next March;
but when the Forty-third Congress meets
there is good reason to believe that the
question of woman suffrage will receive
more attention than it has yet done. The
Republican party has given a semi-pledge
in favor of it, and Republicans in the next
Congress will be strong enough to carry any
measure they may choose. It is probable,
therefore, that during the present year this
question will advance so far that its agita-
tion will be not only more popular, but may
reach a successful consumation.
	FOR SOME weeks past the country has
been ringing with what is called the "Credit
Mobilier Scandal." Prominent members 
of Congress have been charged with having
received stock of the Credit Mobilier 
and the Union Pacific railroad to induce
them to vote for certain measures which
were expected to benefit the companies
named. Emphatic denials have been given
in public addresses, and have been sworn to
before the Congressional Committies inves-
tigating the affair; and yet the charges have
been just as emphatically sworn to, with the
details given in a manner that looks very
like convincing truth. But whether the
charges be true or false, one thing is plain;
members of Congress and prominent poli-
titions, on some side, are guilty of the
grossest perjury as they distinctly contradict
each other on oath, and that where there is
scarcely room to doubt that they know
exactly whether they are swearing truly or
falsely. This is a painful showing for the
legislators of a great country like this, and
is a strong argument in favor of the proposi-
tion that a few truthful, honorable women
in Congress wouldn't hurt but would help it.


﻿SCIENCE AND RELIGION.
			  —
	Truth is science, and science in its legiti-
mate form embraces all truth; and as “true
science and true religion walk hand in
hand,” nothing that is unscientific can be-
long to true religion, nor can anything that
is irreligious be classed with true science.
There are people whose ideas of religion 
lead them to pray a very great deal, and do
but little else, for fear of doing wrong, and
who dare not read other than spiritual
works; while many scientists, who demon-
strate great truths, being “unbelievers,”
take all the glory to themselves instead of
rendering it to God, the great Originator of
all intelligence. It may be unnecessary to
state here the obvious fact that these two
extremes are both wrong. In whatever form
the principle of divine truth is illustrated it
is sacred, and also scientifiic, and is an
established, fundamenal rule or portion of a
rule pertaining to the one true religion—the
Gospel of Jesus Christ.
	There are simple yet essential truths 
which appertain to daily, domestic life, as
lofty in their natures and more necessary to
be understood, than those of a grander and
more imposing character, which often startle
the world with their singular yet manifestly 
natural delineations. There are scientific 
and beautiful truths in the “Cookery Book” 
as well as in the Bible; and they all belong
to the true religion. It is quite as impor-
tant for present welfare and happiness to be 
considered, wherever it may be done in 
honor and wisdom, as that future blessed-
ness be sought for, regardless of the priva-
tions and inconveniences which may be
unnecessarily borne to-day, in consequence
of a lack of genuine faith and knowledge. 
Salvation is as good to-day as it will be in
days to come, if correctly viewed; and there
is no part of true science or true religion in
looking ever for future blessedness and
passing recklessly by those which should be
appreciated and enjoyed to-day.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿[Written for the Exponent.
					LONDON.	
					     ——
	Written while President G. A. Smith and party, consisting 
of L. Snow his sister E.R. Snow, F. Little, his daughter Clara, 
P. A. Sc ettler, G Dunford and T. Jennings were in London
“en route” for Palestine, 
			BY ELIZA R. SNOW.
				 —
	Far. far away from our dear native land,
	In England’s great Metropolis we stand;
	Where art a' d skill—labor and wealth combine
	With time’s co-operation in design
	Of superstructure’s bold and beauteous form,
	With all varieties of strength and charm.

	Here massive columns—stately towers, arise.
	And lift their spires in greetings to the skies; 
	Fine parks and gardens, palaces and halls.
	With scu ptured [niches,—frescue-painted walls; 
	Where no expense is spared to beautify,
	Nor time, nor toil, to captivate the eye,
	We saw, and viewing, continuously admired 
	The master strokes by Genius’ hand inspired.

	To “New Westminster Palace” we resort,
	Where the Chief Justice holds his august court;
	Twas then in session, and the Exchequer too—
	In wig and gown—a grand, imposing view!
	The House of Lords and Commons, too we saw,
	But not those grave expounders of the law.

	With deferential thought we fixed our gaze,
	There, in “The Prince’s Hall.” where face to face,
	On either side, on caved projections stood,
	With features varied as in life’s warm blood,
	White marble statues, from the sculptor’s hand,
	Of British Statesmen, men who could command
	The power of eloquence—the force of mind,
	A mighty nation’s destinies to bind—
	Chatham, Pitt, Granville. Walpole, Fox, beside
	Others who’re justly England's boast and pride.

	We visited the "Abbey" where repose in state,
	The effigies of many good and great,
	With some whose d eds are well deserving hate.
	Group’d in “ The Poet’s Corner,” here, we found,
	With rich, artistic sculpture trophies crown’d,
	The mem’ries of the muse’s world renown’d.

	In some compartments where old, massive stones
	Comprize the flooring, lie their mouldering bones,
	And we with reverential footsteps tread
	Above the ashes of the illustrious dead.

	  Great London City, mart of wealth and power,
	home for the wealthy—charnel for the poor!
	And here amid its boasted pomp and pride,
	Some faithful Soldiers of the Cross reside—
	A few choice spirits whom the watchman’s care,
	By humble search, found scatter’d here and there,
	“Like angels visit, few and far between,”
	As patient gardeners sep’rate clusters glean,
	  They barter earth’s allurements and device
	To gain the Pearl  of great and matchless price,
	And what to them the honors, pride and show
	That perish with their using, here below?
 	Their hopes are high—their noble aims extend
	Where life and peace and progress never end; 
	Where God’s own Kingdom, Time’s last-knell
		survives,
	Crowned with the gifts and powers of endless lives


F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.

			Harrisville, Weber Co.,
					January 5th, 1873.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	We forward a brief report of our Society,
which is in a prosperous condition. We
have a good organization, and good attend-
ance at our meetings. On account of our
scattered condition we only meet once
a month during the winter season; but the
sisters of the Society are willing to do all
the good they can. At a meeting in Decem-
ber last, a vote was taken to omit the word
“Female” from the name of the Society,
which was unanimous. With our best
wishes for the success of your interesting
paper which we peruse with pleasure and
from which we gain a great amount of in-
struction, and with much respect, we remain
yours, etc.,
	MRS. E. SHIRTLIFF, President,
MRS. E. TAYLOR, Secretary.

	Wellsville, Cache Co.,
		January.	7th,	1873.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	I forward a brief synopsis to you of the
[new column]
minutes of our last meeting, held on the 2d
inst, at which a vote was taken to omit the
word “Female” from the name of the
Society as suggested, which was unanimous.
Several brethren were present and gave us
good counsel; a good spirit prevailed. We
hold meeting regularly once a month, which
is pretty well attended. Our Society is
prosperous, all being desirous to assist in
doing good. Accept our best wishes for the
success of the “EXPONENT,” which we read
with great pleasure.
		MARIA PARKINSON, Secretary.

			     HYRUM, Cache Co.,
					January, 13, 1873.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	At a meeting of the Relief Society held
on Thursday November 6th, 1872, a motion
to have the word “Female” dropped from
the name, according to Sister Eliza R.
Snow’s suggestion, was carried by a unani-
mous vote.
	CHRISTINA LILJENQUIST, President.

	          KAYSVILLE, Jan. 20th, 1873.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	At a meeting of the Female Relief Society,
held in this place on the 9th of December
last, a vote was taken to drop the word
“Female” from the name of our Society,
which was carried unanimously.
	The Society in this place numbers about
150, through whose labors much good has
been accomplished. The poor have been
looked after and their wants in a measure
supplied. Fifty dollars have been given to
the P. E. Fund, and one hundred dollars for
other laudable purposes. We have two
hundred and twenty-five dollars in the Kays-
ville Co-operative store, and at present,
over one hundred dollars in the Treasury.
We are now making an effort to build a
house for the use of the Society. The cellar
and foundation walls are already completed.
Most of the material for the building is on
hand and paid for, and we expect to see
the walls go up early in the spring.
	Your valuable paper is much esteemed by
those who patronize it here.
			MARY A. HYDE, President.
HELLEN E. HYDE, Secretary.

	14 WARD ASSEMBLY ROOMS,
			          Salt Lake City.
	General Retrenchment Meeting, held
January 18th, 1873, presided over by Mrs.
Mary I Horne.
	After singing, and prayer by Counselor
Mrs. Zina D. Young, the minutes of the
preceding meeting were read by assistant
Secretary, Miss Maggie Mair, and approved.
The minutes of meetings of the Young
Ladies Retrenchment Associations, of the
9th and 11th wards, were also read. Presi-
dent Mrs. Horne spoke a short time. She
felt well in spirits, but on account of a bad
cold, contracted the evening before, was not
able to speak long; thought the sisters
should strive to be awake to their duties,
and be energetic in performing the same;
they should not trust to the Lord to do for
them what He had endowed them with
faculties to do for themselves.
	Counselor Mrs. S. M. Kimball spoke con-
cerning sympathy, true and false: When
she was in the midst of afflictions, she did
not want her friends to tell her how sorry
they felt for her, or to utter words of a nature
calculated to draw forth the emotions of
grief which she was striving to overcome;
she considered such, mistaken sympathy,
and preferred, when she paw a friend weighed,
down with sorrow, to offer something that
might serve to strengthen and build up
[new column]
faith and trust, rather than to incite and
prolong suffering, by speaking words, meant
for consolation, but only wringing more
effectually the heart already tried.
	Counselor Mrs. Elizabgth Howard read
the letter from Sister E. R. Snow, which
appears in this number of the Exponent,
addressed to President Mrs. Horne and her-
self. After which, remarks were made by
several members of the Association, and
meeting adjourned for two weeks, “Faith”
being chosen for the subject to be treated
upon at the next meeting.

				SUGAR HOUSE WARD,
				       	January 15th, 1873
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	At our monthly meeting, held in the
School House, November 3rd, 1872, Mrs.
Sarah Gibson presiding, a unanimous vote
was taken to omit the word “Female” from
the name of the Society as suggested by
Sister E. R. Snow.
	Our Ward is widely scattered, nevertheless
we do the best we can with our surroundings,
administering to and blessing the poor.
There are always a few good, faithful sisters
on hand who are ready and willing to do all
that is required of them. Our President,
Mrs. S. Gibson, gives us good and valuable
instructions. May she long live to fill the
position she now occupies
	I have been a member of this Church for
18 years, and I still desire to do right and be
faithful to the end.
	With kind wishes for the success of your
paper, I am yours affectionately,
			SARAH WHEELER, Secretary.

			RICHMOND, Jan. 12th, 1873.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	At our regular monthly meeting, held the
first Thursday in November, we unanimously
agreed to drop the word “Female” from the
name of the Society, according to Sister
Eliza R. Snow’s suggestion. This report
has been sent before, but as it has not
appeared in the Exponent, I suppose it has
not reached you, and I accordingly send
another.
	As a society we are doing our best to bless
those in trouble, sickness and poverty; of
the latter class, thank God, but few have
need of our assistance.
	Your excellent paper is a most welcome
visitor in my home, and it is highly appre-
ciated—as it seems—by many of our sisters.
God bless and prosper you in your noble un-
dertaking.
		SARAH A. MERRILL, President..
			OGDEN, Jan. 20th, 1873.

EDITOR EXPONENT:	
	Since my last account of our Society’s
doings, we have passed its third Anniver-
sary; on which occasion the report showed
the amount received since January 1st, 1872,
$636:24, and the amount disbursed,$599:15.
Our Treasurer, Sister R. Canfield, finding
her duties too laborious in the Institution,
desired to be released, and tendered her
resignation; whereupon Sister H. M. Ballan-
tyne was elected to that office. Sister Josie
Richards was also chosen assistant Secretary.
	On the 19th of December the Society held
its first meeting in the New City Hall. It
was a time of unusual interest to all who
were present. Elders Richards, Herrick,
Farr, Penrose and Ballantyne met with us
and each addressed the meeting, giving
much excellent instruction and encourage-
ment, a report of which you have, no doubt,
read in the columns of the “Junction.” On
the evening of the same day the Society had


a party which, as far as I have heard, all
agree was one of the best in Ogden this
winter. Our meetings are held regularly
on each alternate Thursday afternoon, in
the New City Hall. President Richards,
Bishop Herrick and the brethren generally
seem to take pleasure in advising and assist-
ing us to get along so as to make our labors
pleasant to us as well as beneficial to our-
selves and others. We think there is a
gradually increasing interest in the affairs,
of the Society in this place, and we hope to
continue to have that feeling and influence
until all the worthy of our sex are happily
engaged with us.
				Amelia M. Frodsham.

	From a report of a meeting held by the
Society of the 1st Ward of this city, on
November 7th, last, which is now too old to
be published at length, we learn that a vote
was taken by those present to drop the word
“Female” from the name of the Society.
Mrs. M. Chase is president, and Mrs. Eliza-
beth Thayne secretary. The Society is in a
healthy condition.	

	Sister Hannah Carter, Secretary of the
Relief Society of Springville, forwards a
cheering report of that Society, which has
also taken the necessary vote to dispense
with the word “Female” in connection with
the name.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
	
	A Poor, patient man, of my personal
acquaintance, is a bachelor, wandering from
place to place, without one gentle, loving
word of sympathy ever being uttered for
him by cherished lips; without a hand to
clasp as dearer than any other in the world
to him, or a trusting heart to beat in unison
with his own. I am also acquainted with a
lady who is moving rapidly on towards
thirty; pale and rather pensive, who stands
behind a counter instead of taking charge of
a household, as she should do at her present
age, leaving the shop for some one younger
or older to mind. And all this because she
did not understand cooking. She would not
learn to cook, and the gentleman, now a
bachelor, would not "pop the question." To
this some of our "strong minded women,"
would say, "Serves him right, silly 'brute'—
let him learn to do his own cooking!" But I
believe in "woman's rights." And did not 
our dear, old great-grandmother, Eve, set
us a worthy example in looking after what
she and Adam were to eat? And did not
Adam partake of what she had prepared for
him, without interfering with her domestic
arrangements? thus proving that a woman
naturally understands better what is good
for herself and her household than a man
does. Gentlemen wish, when they marry,
to get wives who know enough to manage
the affairs of the kitchen without their over-
sight and opinion about everything. Only
intermeddlers think they know enough for
themselves and the rest of the family too,
and so trusting, marry indolent, ignorant
girls, who are only fit for parlor ornaments,
and never know or care to know whether
the food they eat is calculated to promote or
injure health. Now, if you want to marry a
gentleman, young lady, be wise and make
yourself worthy of being a gentleman's wife
—learn to cook.

						Aunt Trudie.
	Making Bread.—Milk is always prefer-
able to water for mixing. Accurately
measure or weigh everything used in mak-
ing bread. Experience has shown that
baking pans should be about seven inches
long, four inches deep, and four inches wide
on the bottom, flaring and fitted with covers.
This fact is worthy of attention. Every
cook should consult the taste in the use of
salt. A quart of sifted flour, loosely put in,
will weigh a pound.
	When Storing away dried fruit, put in
small twigs and leaves of the China tree.
A large quantity is not required, and the
leaves, etc., can easily be taken out when
the fruit is to be used. This is said to be a
certain preventative against worms in all
kinds of dried fruit. 
	A Cement said to stop cracks in glass
vessels to resist moisture and heat:    Disolve
caseine in cold saturated solution of borax
and with this solution paste strips of hog's 
or bullock's bladder (softened in water) on
the glass and dry at a gentle heat; if the 
vessel is to be heated, coat the bladder on
the outside before it has become quite dry, 
with a paste of a rather concentrated solu-
tion of silicate of soda and quick lime or 
plaster of Paris. 
	Plain Apple Pie.- For the crust boil
white, mealy potatoes, mash smooth with
milk as for the table, and let it cool. Knead 
in graham or unbolted flour to mix well, and
roll out.    Put in a good quantity of apples
prepared as for sauce.    Put, in place of an
upper crust, bars of crust across. 
	Cooking Raisins.-It is well to cook
raisins before putting them into pies, cakes
or puddings. Soaking them is not sufficient.
Steaming them, by pouring a small quantity
of boiling water amongst them in a tightly 
closed dish, and allowing them plenty of 
time to cook before opening, is a good plan.
When rasins are rightly cooked before using
they are plumper and more palatable, and
can be eaten without injury by most dyspep-
tics.


﻿[For theExponent.
		EXERCISE FOR GIRLS. 
			     ——
	Since the body is not only the “temple of
the soul,” but also its servant and friend,
the means by which it is able to make im-
pressions on tangible things, it has claims to
care and culture, education and development,
only secondary, to those of the soul itself.
If it be true that a cramped body can only
give imperfect impressions to the desires
and capabilities of the soul, how manifestly
important that no pains should be spared to
unfold every latent power, and train to the
utmost extent of useful action, every bone,
muscle, organ and nerve which the body
contains!
	This article is written especially in the in-
terest of girls, for custom has long demanded
for boys vigorous and healthy exercise,
which is the legitimate means of giving
harmonious vigor to the body. But custom
has also forbidden for girls such exercise,
because forsooth, it is ungenteel to wrestle,
run and row; and scarcely dare the slaves of
sex and fashion indulge in equestrian exer-
cise; certainly not without various trappings
and restrictions which fetter free movement.
It is time that more common-sense views
were entertained on this subject, expecially
by the people of Utah, whose aim is the
renewal of the primeval strength and
longevity of the human race. There can be
no valid reason why we may not break
through the trammels of foolish and ground-
less customs, such, for instance, as the deep
prejudice against whistling. If it is proper
for a boy to whistle, why be shocked so 
deeply when under similar circumstances a
girl indulges in the harmless pastime! Not
that to be debarred from whistling would be
a great privation, but little things exemplify
[new column]
great ones; as the Bostonians argued respect-
ing three cents a pound on tea.
	It is not enough that one sex be trained to
bodily strength and symmetry; that is doing
the work by halves. Let the girls also have
due attention on this point. And do not 
let all their exerciese be drudgery, either.
Washing, sweeping and churning are all
very well in their place; but if "all work and
no play make Jack a dull boy," I contend
that it will also make Jill a dull girl. She
should learn to skate, to swim, to play ball,
and—yes, to shoot! Self protection demands
this last in these wild, western countries,
and to my mind it would be far more pleas-
ing to see our girl going out on shooting ex-
cursions with her brothers and sturdily
bearing home at night the game she has
captured, than laced up in corsets, with 
smelling bottle at nose, giving little plain-
tive screams if she should spy a mouse, or a 
frog, or any of God's harmless creatures
which should awaken admiration for His
boundless wisdom, instead of fear or even
disgust. It is high time that such nervous
timidity, not to say such affectation, should 
pass into disfavor. And the very best way
to accomplish this desirable end, would be 
to give the girl such confidence in her phy-
sical prowess that she would feel herself
equal to any small contingencies.
	It would be a pleasant sight to see com-
modious play-grounds attached to every
schoolroom in the land; where both boys 
and girls should be encouraged tp engage on
active games suitable to their age and 
strength. And more; well ordered and well
conducted calistheniums should be estab-
lished where girls might hoard strength of
nerve and spine, which shall enable them to
defy the ailments which to-day afflict
their mothers and almost make their lives a
burden. Like all good things, exercise
must be used with due moderation; but so
long as it does not pass that boundary, it is 
the great tonic which will give lithe limbs,
bright eyes, and straight spines to the next
generation. No better physicians can be
found than pure air, regular habtis, cheer-
fulness and —judicious exercise. Give these
to the girls as well as the boys, and see how
soon feeble, incapable, wornlooking mothers
will be replaced by rosy, smiling matrons,
whose well-ordered households and beauti-
ful, sweet-tempered children, bear witness 
to their great capabilities.
							L.L.D.


A LETTER FROM ELIZA COOK.—The
“Western Mail” states that a gentleman
residing in South Wales, having written to
Miss Eliza Cook expressing the sympathy 
felt by him and other local bards for the
poetess, in respect of the rumors circulated
as to her death, has received the following
acknowledgement:—“Beech-house, Thorn-
ton-hill, Wimbledon, S. W., near London,
Nov. 30, 1872.—I am too worried to write
much, my dear friend, for such I feel you to
be—but I must acknowledge your kind and
manly letter. The ‘people’ are, indeed, most
sympathizing with me, and their generous
support goes far to soften the bitterness of
the cruel and unfounded reports lately
issued about me. You may be glad to learn
that I have lived during the last ten years
with a nephew and his family; occasionally
visiting my brother. The last three years
we have resided here, and a most happy
home I have, where I hope to really die,
when it pleases the Great Father to call me
to my eternal rest. I am a sufferer, but my
brain has never lost its perfect power, and I
hope it never will.—With warm thanks to
yourself and your circle, I am, my dear Sir,
yours truly ever,—ELIZA COOK.”
