SPLINTERS.
	Two of the pension agencies of this
country are managed by women...........Iowa
laws now make no distinction between hus-
band and wife in their control over their
own property.......An old man in Caldwell,
who was not strong minded himself, took
with him his better half, when he went to
vote, to see that he was not coaxed into
voting the Democratic ticket.......Milan is
cheered by the singing of Brignoli.......The
“Pall Mall Gazette” is reported to have paid
Hepworth Dixon one farthing on his suit
for libel......The Irish orator, Father Burke,
is sick......A school at Geneva, R. I., was
lately broken up by a lively bull-dog......An
old lady, nearly eighty years of age, recently
hanged herself in Kentucky. It was a pity,
having lived so long to leave such an exam-
ple on record......Lexington, Ky., claims
the largest conservatory in this country......
It is suggested that babies taken to theatres
should be checked at the door and hung up
like umbrellas; would poorly dressed ones
be liable to get exchanged for those of better
appearance, as umbrellas frequently do?......
Stanley has been given a brilliant banquet
at Washington......Senator Sumner is still
feeble, though now able to sit up......Jenny
Lind lately sang in the American chapel at
Florence......Ben Wade’s daughter-- if he
has one—is about to marry a carpenter......
The Kansas Legislature has four women
clerks......Emerson, with his daughter Ellen,
has left Rome for Egypt......A Methodist
minister in Massachusetts being absent on
Sunday, his wife performed the pulpit
services satisfactorily to the people and,
doubtless, to herself......More than 25,000
persons viewed the remains of Napoleon
lying in state, though none, it was stated,
but particular friends of the family and dis-
tinguished representatives......A hurricane
from the north, recently vistied Minnesota,
lasting more than two days, freezing nearly
one hundred persons to death, and block-
ading all the railroads and common roads
with huge banks of snow.......Boston is said
to claim in May or Pierce its first bachelor
chief magistrate.....Lord Edward Lytton
Bulwer, whose death occurred on the 18th
of January, was sixty-seven years old......
Perteet, a colored wife murderer of Chicago,
is sentenced to be hanged.....It was a girl
of the period who said, when going to be
married, she took a “spoony” who had no
mind of his own that she might always have
her own way; and it was the Bible he threw
at and struck her with the other evening,
because his supper wasn’t ready when he
came home.......A lady writer pronounces
fashion at a standstill, and says everything
seems to be a modification of last year’s
style. If fashion has really run the full
length of her chain she can do no better
perhaps, than to retrace her steps back as
she first trod them.......The fact of Mrs. N.
B, Gardiner being recognized as a voter in
the 9th Ward of Detroit, Michigan, is said
to have caused considerable excitement
thereabouts......In some of the Eastern
schools and seminaries, much just indigna-
tion has been aroused by the discovery that
opium venders are in the habit of sending
opium surreptitiously prepared iu candy and
lozenges into the schools.......Mrs. Bloomer,
who has been accused of having pledged
allegiance to the Woodhull, denies the charge
openly.......The first organized county dairy
market in the United States, was commenced
at Little Falls, New York......Nova Scotia
and New Brunswick are to be connected by
a canal....A letter carrier in Cleveland,
Ohio, received, on New Year’s day, $627:75.
......Mrs. Livermore says that five-sixths of
[new column]
the applications for divorce come from
couples who have not been married over
five or six years; if they tide over that
period they generally keep on to the end.
......George Francis Train has been a
prisoner in thirteen jails......A lady at
Council Bluffs who lighted her fire with
kerosene, leaves a husband and one child.
......There are seven woolen mills in Oregon
which used 4,191,000 pounds of wool last
year......Parson Brownlow has written the
inscription for bis tombstone......In 1848,
Rev. Miles Grant, the adventist, gave away
a fine property in Connecticut, thinking the
final day was at hand, and has lately been
preaching in the same place......Ffty years
ago, blue eyes for ladies were the rage in
America; then black and dark brown ones
were considered the most attractive, and
since, gray ones have taken the place for
beauty; but now it matters little what the
color of the eyes are so the hair is flaxen
and the face has its proper amount of paint
......Illinois is nearly free from debt.


EXCITEMENT CONCERNING UTAH.
			  —
	﻿The periodical excitement in regard to Utah
is being worked up in the East. Scarcely a lead-
ing journal is now received from that part of the
United States that does not contain something
sensational from Washington, relative to what
is going to be done with Utah. At one time
ten thousand troops are to be sent here imme-
diatly; another time, petitions are stated to
have been received from the “Gentiles” of this
Territory, asking assistance; next, it is averred
Congress will pass some sweeping measure to
solve the “Mormon problem;” and then it is
announced that President Grant has deter-
mined to have the law enforced in Utah if it
requires the entire force of troops in the United
States to do it.
	While reading all this we involuntary ask
what place is the subject of so much comment?
And can scarcely realize that our moderately
quiet home, where, there seems nothing to
attract such attention, can be the object of all
the excitement we read of. But the fact forces
itself upon us, that there are a few men—bad
men, we may say; unscrupulous men, who aim
at carrying out the destructive policy of “rule
or ruin” who are the authors or circulators of
nearly all the falsehoods published concerning
Utah. They assert that the “Gentiles” are re-
stricted in their constitutional rights in this
Territory, but they fail to point to a single illus-
tration; they allege that all elective offices in
the Territory are tilled by candidates of the
“Mormons,” conveying the impression that
the elections are not fairly conducted, but sup-
pressing the fact that the Mormons, being in
the majority of eight to one, will naturally do
as people do elsewhere, elect their candidates
peacefully and lawfully; they demand that the
elective franchise be taken from the women of
Utah, because those women, understanding
them and their purposes, refuse to be led by
them or be their dupes; and they publicly
charge immorality upon the people of Utah,
while privately acknowledging that the Mor-
[new column]
mons are the most moral people they have
ever lived among.
	The astonishing part of the matter is, that
the people of the Eastern States, having had
such calumnies refuted so often, can be
deceived by them again. Why, it is only a
couple of days since we read a telegram from
Washington, in the “Ogden Junction,” which
reminded us of the sensational stories which
used to be sent broadcast over the world, of
President Young opening all the letters sent
from Utah, to see that no person told the truth
concerning it; and of the hairbreadth escapes
which some people had in fleeing from “the
horrors of Mormondom.” Few could be found
now to believe such trash, but a leading paper
published in Washington, largely owned by
Senator Harlan—who is a special favorite of
Dr. Newman—actually says that the “Mor-
mons” have “sought to exclude from their midst
all who fail to conform to the Mormon faith,
and practice; a system of persecution that
ought to have been rebuked long since!” It is
not supposed that this friend of Dr. Newman,
who personally had the privilege of denouncing
"Mormonism” in the large Tabernacle in this
city, did not know any better than this, for
such ignorance would be inexcusable; but he
does not hesitate to violate truth and trample
honesty in the mire, that he may deceive those
who are no better informed than to believe
him, that bitter feelings may be excited against
a community whose industry, temperance,
probity, sincerity and virtue are a standing
reproach against the hypocrisy and prominent
vices of the age. But while the old adage may
be correct, that a falsehood will travel twice
round the earth while truth is putting on its
boots, this should also be remembered, that
“Truth crushed to earth will rise again,”
for it is sustained by an inherent power which
cannot alway be overcome.


LEGAL INCONSISTENCY. 
	
	Some of the lawyers of this city—twenty-six,
indeed—have signed a document, published last
week in the “Salt Lake Herald,” which is at
once an accusation against the Territory and a
memorial to Congress, setting forth some of the
usual charges against the people of Utah which
ought to be too stale now to be rewarmed and
served up again for the thousandth time, by
these gentlemen. The “Herald” has been
handling the document without gloves, and
showing in plain terms its unfairness, weak- 
ness, unjust statements and bitter partisanship;
but one portion of it demands notice by the
EXPONENT. It covertly attacks the right of the
women of Utah to the elective franchise, by
attacking the law conferring the franchise upon
them:  We say covertly, and might add that
the entire document is gotten up in such style,
is not straightforward, nor honorable, but reads
more like the plea of a pettifogger in one of the
lower courts than a composition such as would
be expected from twenty-six individuals who
are entitled by law and usage to be styled
“learned gentlemen.” The document submits
“that the act of February 12th, 1870, prostitutes
the right of suffrage by conferring it upon an
alien woman without even qualification of time
of residence.” The simplest and most complete
way of refuting this is by quoting the act itself,
which being short, we here append in full:
				AN ACT.
Conferring upon women the elective franchise.
	(Approved February 12, 1870.)
	Sec. 1. Be it enacted by the Governor and
Legislative Assembly of the Territory of Utah:
That every woman of the age of twenty-one
years who has resided in this Territory six
months next preceding any general election,


born; or naturalized in the United States, or
who is the wife, widow, or daughter of a native-
born or naturalized citizen of the United States,
shall be entitled to vote at any election in this
Territory.
	Sec. 2. All laws or parts of laws conflicting
with this Act are hereby repealed.
	The act is so plain that it seems impos-
sible any one would attempt to dispute the fact
that it convicts these gentlemen of willful and
deliberate misstatements, with intent to de-
ceive.
	In looking over the names we note some who
were at one time very anxious that the women
of Utah should possess the elective franchise.
They were then, as they are now, aspirants for
notoriety and power; and they imagined that
women were weak, pliable beings, who could
not withstand their wonderfully eloquent
sophistries, but would rush to the polls enthusi-
astically determined to give these wordy
champions the desired elevation to office and
emoluments. But when the women of Utah
received the elective franchise, it was speedily
apparent that they could think and act for
themselves, and that they would vote for the
candidates whom they believed most suitable;
and they actually did vote for the men who had
conferred upon them the legal right to vote.
Then came a change with these before-time
self-proclaimed champion of the  "down-trod-
den women of Utah.” They found they had
made a mistake, and reckoned without suffi-
cient acquaintance with independent woman-
nature; and they have since been more loudly
urgent that Congress should step forward and
annul the Territorial statute than they ever
were that the women of Utah should receive
the elective franchise.
	As it is always well to know our friends, and
as some of our readers or their husbands, fathers,
or sons, may, unfortunately, have occasion to
require the services of a lawyer, we append the
names of the twenty-six who urge upon Con-
gress legislation inimical, to Utah, and the
taking from the women of Utah the right of
suffrage.
	J. B. Bosborough,		R. H. Robertson
	D. Cooper,			C. W. Bennett,
	Wm. Haydon,		C. K.Gilchrist,
	Frank Tilford,			C. H. Morgan
	D. J. Toohy,			Sol. P. McCurdy,
	E. P. Johnson,		J. N. H. Patcick,
	Wm. Hyndman,		Wm. P. Appleby,
	James M. Carter,		M. Kirkpatrick,
	Thomas Marshall,		Jno. R. McBride,
	Jonathan C. Royle,	Warren Earll,
	Wm. C. Hall,			George E. Whitney,
	Jas. G. Spratt,			Wells Spicer,
	W.W. Gee,			R. N. Baskin.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.				141


HOME	AFFAIRS.
 				      — 
	There is some excitement in the East con-
cerning Utah, but the people here seem un-
affected by the contagion.	
	A Correspondent in Alta, Little Cotton-
wood, informs us that for several weeks her
bedroom has been perfectly dark, from the win-
dows being under an enormous snow drift.
	Attention is again respectfully called to
the terms of clubs on the last page; with a hope
that the friends of the Exponent will continue
to use their endeavors in behalf of the paper.
	Mrs. Willmarth East, as local Agent
for the EXPONENT, is on hand to receive sub-
scriptions for the same, wherever it may be
more convenient for subscribers to see her
than the Editor or general Agent.
	The latest news from President Smith and
the Palestine party, among whom is Sister
Snow, reports them in Italy enjoying good


health. President Albert Carrington, of the
European Mission, was about to join them.
	Encouragement is offered to the Exponent
in many instances from friends whose judge-
ment and candor, as well as their open hearted-
ness we esteem very highly, and we are happy
in acknowledging our obligations and in tender-
ing our thanks for the same.
	A large number of patrons of the Expo-
nent, who subscribed for six months, have not
renewed their subscriptions, which expired
some time ago. Will they please renew im-
mediately, or we shall be compelled to stop
forwarding their papers?
	The number of restless and bad men, that
are now on the streets of Salt Lake city at
night, render it necessary that ladies should be
careful how they travel the streets after dark,
unless accompanied by a male protector. Time
was it was not so, but changes have occurred.
	That terrible disease, smallpox, has lately
visited a few of the wards of this city and some
of the settlements of the Territory, especially
in Morgan County. In regard to this as to
other diseases which spread infection easily, a
few facts should be more widely known than
they are. Woolen clothes will carry contagion
more readily than cotton, and cotton even
more readily than linen. The harder the
thread of which any fabric is woven and the
closer the texture, it is the, less likely to carry
infection. Cleanliness should characterize
every dwelling at all times, but double care
should be taken where there is danger from
contagious diseases. In such cases disinfect-
ants, such as chloride of lime, should be kept
in the house to be used as occasion might require;
while a little vinegar sprinkled around in the
room where a patient is suffering, will be found
valuable. To these precautions should be added
the avoidance of undue fear or excitement, and
the use of every precautionary measure to
prevent the spread of infection.

﻿A TEACHER’S STORY.

	“When I was on a mission in Scotland,”
said he, “I one day picked up a piece of a
newspaper, which contained a story of a
great king, of olden times, when the kings
of the earth used to keep ‘fools’ to make
sport for them. This king once gave his
staff to his fool saying to him as he did so,
'Take this staff and keep it until you find a
greater fool than yourself, then give it to
him.’ The fool took the staff and went
his way, and in the course of time the king
was taken very sick. He was, indeed 'sick
unto death and when he knew that he
must die, he sent for his fool to come to him
that he might bid him a final farewell.
When the fool appeared before him, the
king told him he was going a long journey
into an unknown land, from whence he
should never return. The fool then asked
his master how he was going. The king
said he did not know. The fool inquired
how the king would be supported in the
new home he was going to, where he should
remain forever, what provisions had been
made for his journey and his stay. The
king answered, none, and that he knew not
anything concerning the way or the place.
'Then,’ said the fool, ‘take this staff—for in
all my life, I have not been guilty of things
as foolish as these.’ ”
	“Now,” continued the teacher, “let us
not be worse than the fool; but let us day by
day make some wise preparations for that
long journey, which all must take, and that
now home which all must enter.”


﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
			—
	THE new directory of Des Moines indicates
a population of 15,000.
	THE Omaha, Neb. “Dispatch" is dead. It
run the race with dispatch.
	A SOLDIER'S monument is to be erected at
Dubuque, and the war graves then fenced.
	THE HUDSON river ice crop is estimated at
a million and a half tons, and was all to be
gathered by the 1st of February.
	McPherson, the county seat of McPher-
son, contains six houses and a newspaper—
the “Messenger.”
	A LIVE dog arrived in Lansing in a
United States mail bag on the 24th ult., as a
Christmas present for the postmaster.
	FOR the Centennial of American Indepen-
dence, Philadelphia will erect a building
that will cover fifty acres of ground.
	THE greatest mass of solid iron in the world
is the great Iron Mountain in Missouri. It
is three hundred and fifty feet high, and
two miles in circuit.
	THE statistical tables of England show that
less than one-third of the marriageable
women of that country were living in mar-
riage at the time of the last census.
	THE New York “Herald” says, the crime
of outraging female chastity in the South
appears to be especially the province of the
liberated negro.
	CORNELIUS Miles of Appanoose county,
took up his ashes, set them against the
house, and with his family went a visiting.
When he returned his home was gone.
	SERVANT girls are scarce in Topeka, Kan-
sas, and will continue to be so, says the
“Commonwealth,” as long as wives main-
tain prejudice against good-looking help.
	CEDAR Rapids gets the State Presbyterian
college for $100,000. Two men give be-
sides $25,000 each for endowment. It will
open next September.
	THE greatest cave in the world is the
Mammoth Cave, in Kentucky, where one
can make a voyage on the waters of a
subterrannean river, and catch fish without
eyes.
	THE Rev. John Tecumseh Jones, an in-
dustrious, economical Indian, of the Ottawa
tribe, died lately, and left his whole estate,
$60,000, to the Baptists in the State of Kan-
sas, for the education of ministers.
	AT a Georgia revival meeting, they search
all applicants for admission to the “mour-
ner’s bench,” and the proceeds, in the shape
of bowie knives and revolvers, are raffled off
at the close of the services, for the benefit of
the missionary fund. 
	A WICHITA man was found carrying
concealed about his person two navy revol-
vers, three pocket pistols, two billies, some
brass knuckles, an air gun and a sword cane.
He said he was going on the plains to fight
Indians, but the Cadi sent him to fight the
vivacious flea and enterprising bed bug in
the calaboose.
	Brain work costs more food than hand
work. According to careful estimates and
analyses of excretions, three hours of hard
study wear out the body more than a whole
day of severe physical labor. Another evi-
dence of brain work is obtained from the
fact that though the brain is only one for-
tieth the weight of the body it receives
about one-fifth of all the blood sent by the
heart into the system.


﻿If the whole world should agree to speak
nothing but the truth, what an abridgement
it would make of speech.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


A CHAPTER IN THE LIFE OF NAOMA.

	Naoma was the daughter of———of Ro-
chester, New York, where some of her
family still reside. She married. Her
husband was a moral man, a skillful me-
chanic, and a good financier; she an indus-
trious, frugal, and loving wife. A few short
years of labor and love, crowned with the
blessing of home, with all its endearing sur-
roundings and appertunances, caused her
heart to swell with uncontrollable joy as she
contemplated the haven of domestic bliss
in which she might now repose.
	But a missionary came. He said, “My
friends, I come to you in the name of my
Master, to offer you a commission to labor in
a the vineyard of the Lord. But know that
the Alter of sacrifice accompanies it—that
you will be required to sacrifice all that you
now hold dear. The conditions imply that
you, of your own free will, give yourselves,
your affections, your time, your all to Him
to whom all persons and all things rightfully
belong."
	They accepted the commision with its
conditions, laid their friends and their pos-
sessions on the alter, and journeyed to Kirt-
land, Ohio, where a few who had accepted
the same commission, had preceded them.
Freely they had received, freely they must
give. God was no respecter of persons; this
commission must be offered to all people,
The harvest was great and the laborers few.
Naoma’s husband must take a mission to
Europe. She must do the best she could in
a little rented house, with three little
mouths to fill, six little feet to shoe, and all
the etceteras thereunto belonging. No
matter. She had a light heart and a ready
hand; her husband was on duty, he would
honor his commission, and God would bless
his labors. Her duties would be made light;
they would be happy. And they were
happy, as their letters filled with affection,
confidence, and good cheer constantly testi-
tied.
	Years sped swiftly by, and sacrifice was
almost forgotten in the joys of the return.
But once enlisted, the armor must not be
laid off; sin must be combatted at home and
abroad. A mission to the New England
States came. Their neighbors did not like
their commission.
	Missouri would not be told that, if she
sinned, the hand of an avenging God would
be stretched out over her. These commis-
sioners were driven into Illinois.
	With an increased family, increased
poverty, and decreased bodily health, our
heroine was here. Her wifely confidence,
motherly devotion, and Christian resignation,
were subjects of remark and admiration for
her acquaintances. The light which they
had received, must not be hid. The com-
mission must be held up here. The world
must be reproved for sin, and warned of a
judgment to come.
	Naoma’s husband must fill another mission
among the European nations. The peaceful
trust that pervaded her bosom, made the
little log-cabin with its shake floor, in
which she and her sick children were left,
a kind of paradise to her; she could make the
rough places smooth for his sake, and the
cause they had espoused.
	Again the husband returned. He erected
a commodious dwelling, which they soon
filled with home comforts. The blessed
reunion had almost removed the traces of
anxious care that were becoming visible on
Naoma’s countenance. She thanked God
that he had enabled her, if not joyfully,
uncomplainingly, to take the spoiling of her 
goods, to endure the scoffs of friends, and
breast the storms of adversity.
	She felt that she had passed through the
furnace of affliction, and no more could be
required of woman.
	Poor Naoma, she knew not all. The
dregs of her cup were not yet drained. Her
husband—he to whom she could ever trust-
ingly confide any thoughts, and who had
ever thus trustingly confided in her, became
suddenly silent and thoughtful. His steps
became heavier and his head more bowed. If
she surprised a look of tender compassion
bent upon her, it was instantly averted. Her
heart was pained with the weight that she
could see and feel, resting upon her hus-
band’s mind; but she could not discern its
nature. She sought his confidence, he
evaded her inquiries. She sought again, he
said, “I cannot reveal to you the burden of
my thoughts to make you unhappy!”
Months rolled by. She sought his confi-
dence the third time. He repulsed her.
She tried to reason; her husband was a good
man, and therewith she ought to be content.
She would not be an exacting wife; she
would try to discipline her mind to rest
satisfied. Such a victory was easier sought
than achieved. Her unconquerable heart
would rebel; it would yearn for that recip-
rocal trust, which she felt was essential
to preserve the oneness that constituted
connubial bliss.
	Was the equilibrium of her mind, which
had been so long disturbed, never to be
restored?
	One morning in early spring, her hus-
band went to a Southern settlement to
preach. Susan Dean called; Naoma received
her kindly, even cordially, but the sadness
that pervaded the atmosphere of her person,
was sensibly felt by Susan. She said, “my
dear friend, you are not well to-day, let me
do something for you.” Naoma tried to
smile, said her ailment was only a fearful
foreboding of an undefinable something that
made her “so miserable” that she sometimes
feared the Evil One had put his finger in
her pie. But she could not eat in the
morning; they would have a cup of tea, and
then she would feel better. The water in
the polished tea kettle was soon boiled, and a
snow white napkin spread on the teaboard,
on which was placed some temptingly nice
bread, butter and plum preserves. Susan
pronounced the tea just the right flavor,
but Naoma could not eat. Susan marveled,
and bade her friend good bye. 
	Naoma opened a book, it seemed mockery;
no writer had ever felt as she felt—she
hoped not—and if not, they could have no
sympathy with her. What could she do?
she folded her arms and gave her mind a
few moments to a new train of thought.
Why had she not brought the burden of her
soul to the feet of Jesus? There she could
be understood; there she had ever found
succor, comfort, strength, as her circum-
stances had required. There she would
bring this, her great sorrow.
	She retired to a silent recess. Tears of
contrition filled her eyes, while with low
and tremulous voice, she pleaded with the
Father in the name of the Son to show her
wherein she was unworthy, that she might
repent, make a just restitution, and be
enabled to discharge the duties she owed to
herself, to her husband, family and to the
world, in a manner acceptable to God.
	A sweet voice from the Celestial Court
notified her that her petition was heard,
that the offering of her contrite heart was
accepted, and that the channel of communi-
cation was now open. On the holy commu-
nion I draw the curtain for half an hour.
	Naoma appeared in the midst of her
household with a new peace in her soul,
which spoke its own language through her
countenance. Her husband returned from
the neighboring settlement where he had
been to preach, and she met him with
smiles and tears. He took her hand carefully
in his, and asked, “what has happened to
you during my absence.” And she an-
swered, “Only that the Lord has deigned to
reveal to me the secret you sought to keep.”
They both wept. At length he said, “Naoma,
could you bear it?” she replied, “The
subject is so sacred, so solemn, that I cannot
converse upon it now!” he said, “I will
leave you for an hour, during which time
we will pray for ourselves and each other,
then peradventure, we may talk together.”
	There came an end to even “that” hour.
Her husband entered her apartment and
said, “How do you feel by this time,
Naoma?” She replied, “I cannot define my
feelings, farther than this; I know you to
be a servant of the Most High; I know that
the God of Abraham is your God; I know
He is a wise dictator; and wherein His ways
are not as my ways, I wish you to heed His
dictations, regardless of me. In spite of
all my good resolutions I formed before the
Lord, I find myself weak before you. Bear
with me, dear husband, a few months, and
I trust you will find me stronger.”
	Three months later, Naoma caressed her
fifth child. And her husband said, “can
you now kneel with me at the sacred Alter
and there, in the presence of God, Angels,
and witnesses, of your own free will and
choice give to me wives?” Naoma breathed
a little shorter, but answered unhesitatingly,
“yes, sir, and bless you, and bless them, and
thank God that I have the privilege.”
	Did Naoma still love her husband? Her
own words were that she “reverenced" him
now. 
									B.


﻿THE THREE last elected United States
Senators are accused of having bought their
seats: Bogg, of Missouri; Ingalls, of Kansas;
and Jones, of Nevada.
	“NO CAKE—no cards—no divorce,” was
appended to a Vermont marriage notice
lately. It is to be hoped the last condition
will be valid during life.
	LOCKMAN, the philosopher, was once
asked who had given him his first lesson in
wisdom. “The blind,” he replied, “who
do not move a step in advance until they
make sure of the ground with their stick.’
	CHARACTER is higher than intellect.
Thinking is the functionary. The stream
retreats to its source. A great soul will be
strong to live as well as strong to think.
Living is a total act. Thinking is a partial
act. Let the grandeur of justice shine in
his affairs. Let the beauty of affection
cheer his lowly roof. The scholar loses no
hour that the man lives.
	EYES.—The eyes of men converse as
much as their tongues, with the advantage
that the ocular dialect needs no dictionary,
but is understood the world over. When
the eyes say one thing and the tongue an-
other, a practised man relies on the language
of the first. If a man be off his centre, his
eyes show it. You can read in the eyes of
your companion whether your argument
hits him, though his tongue will not confess
it. There is a look by which a man shows
he is going to say a good thing, and a look
when he has said it. Vain and forgotten
are all the fine offers and offices of hospitality
if there be no holiday in the eye. How
many furtive inclinations are avowed by the
eye though dissembled by the lips!
							EMERSON.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿A LESSON LEARNED IN SLEEP.

Last night when wild winds shook my window pane,
And my sad heart as wildly beat within,
My dear, dead love, whom I had called in vain,
My ’raptured eyes beheld, through tear-mists dim.

He laid my tired head upon his breast
And gazed with far-off eyes beyond my ken;
“And does my darling weep when she should rest,
And long for me,” he said, “to come again?”

He clasped my willing fingers in his own,
And pressed upon my brow a holy kiss;
Then whispered low. with soft, indulgent tone
“Thou know’st not, love, what is real bliss.

"In mercy I am come to thee to-night.
To ope thine eyes, and ease thy breaking heart;
That sorrow nevermore may dim thy sight—
I tell thee why ’twas best that we should part.

“Dids’t never dream that I might turn away,
And from the paths of honor widely roam?
That thou might’st rue our happy bridal day,
And dread the hour that brought thy husband home?

“Temptation’s chains are strong and doubly tried;
The wine-cup was my master from my birth;
The fearful curse was born with me!” he cried,
“Look at that picture of our might-be hearth.”

And far into deep rolling mists and haze
The shadowy fingers pointed as he spoke;
And, as the dim clouds rifted for my gaze,
A sad, sad sc ne upon my vision broke,

A wan-faced woman sat beside a bed
Whereon a wasted infant form was laid;
I heard an eager, feeble cry for bread,
And knew for food that wretched mother prayed.

The scanty fagots which comprized the hoard,
Seemed but to mock her cry for warmth and light;
The meager furniture and empty board,
With fearful meaning dawned upon my sight.

A noble form, revealed by fire-light pale,
Lay prone, in heavy slumber near the hearth;
A crushed decanter told the fearful tale
Of fallen manhood and degraded worth.

“Could that have been thy fate and mine?” I said,
And from the fearful sight turned with a groan—
I started, for my visitant had fled;
I sat beside my window all alone.

The drear winds moaning at my window pane
Had lulled my tired senses into sleep;
I know my woeful vision was a dream
But from that moment I have ceased to weep.
										Emma.


[For the EXPONENT.
				PRAYER.
				    ___
	The person who has never tested the
power and efficacy of prayer is poor, indeed!
Where misfortune and suffering crowd upon
the stricken soul, when no human eye can
see and no human hand assuage its heavy
grief, how great the consolation and the
renewal of courage derived from prayer!
How divine the privilege of kneeling low
in solitude and pouring our griefs into an
ear which is not turned coldly away, and of
asking assistance where we have the blessed
assurance that we shall surely receive!
	The wayward may scoff, the wise and
strong in their own conceit may sneer, those
who cheat themselves into a disbelief in
God’s ability to stretch out His merciful
hand over His children on particular occa-
sions, may smile at the humility and faith
which prompt the act; but let the most
scornful of them be placed in sudden and
deathly peril, where no human power can
aid them, and faith and prayer are immedi-
atly vindicated; for the sufferer almost
unconsciously calls imploringly on the name
of God, and looks to Him for the assistance
which he knows can nowhere else be ob-
tained.
	And many come from such trials with
softened and humbled hearts; but some,
attributing their deliverance to whatever
means was made God’s instrument, still
refuse to believe because the universe was
not shaken to its centre and Nature’s har-
mony broken. But let them know that
God works always “with” and "through”
the laws which Himself has laid down, and
not “against” them.
	If the chief ruler of a nation finds himself
beset with intrigue, danger and difficulty,
the cultivation of a meek and prayerful
spirit is his best safe, gaurd. His bearing
will not be haughty and confident like that
of him who believes his own wisdom suffi-
cient for every emergency; but he will wear
a mild and quiet air as of one who leans upon
a superior strength; his hands will grasp
the difficulty just in the right place, and his
words, though few, will be as golden bands
to bind all sundered parts together.
	And yet, where is the miracle? No man
can acceptably present himself before the
Eternal until his heart is cleansed of all dark
and crooked purposes, his haughty spirit
humbled, his whole desires centered upon
what he deems his purest duty. Thus he
prepares himself to receive the blessings of
keen foresight, sound judgment and the
support and confidence of all pure minded
compeers. That very frame of mind which
is necessary to produce true prayer, is in
many cases the answer to prayer—the start-
ing point from which we may accomplish
the good we desire. In His wisdom God so
ordained it that many prayers should thus
answer themselves.
	And many a time when we lament that
our prayers are not answered, only our
shortsightedness prevents us from knowing
that they are answered even more fully and
completely than we could have imagined,
and much more for our good. Or, if actually
refused, ’tis only as a tender parent refuses
the glittering keen edged-blade for which
an inexperienced child holds up its hand
To cultivate this prayerful resigned state of
mind is the very best preparation agains
adversity, for we can believe our seeming
misfortunes to be blessings in disguise, and
generally prove them such. And prayer is
an armor fitted to all wearers.
	The child which desires the approbation
and confidence of its friends, may, through
this simple means bring itself into a state of
mind and manner which is sure to please.
	The busy mother, whose duties follow
upon each other as unintermittingly as the
changing seasons, may envelop herself in
this garb and surround her dear ones with
this atmosphere, until she will find the
wearing cares of the day reduced to less
than half their former dimensions. The
calm, peaceful, yet ardent spirit flows out
from her soul, as light diverges from the
sun, and permeates every object within the
circle of its benign influence. Not only will
her strength be renewed and her fainting 
heart revived, but her holy guiding influence
will be more widespread and enduring
than it could otherwise be.
	The maiden just planting the first elastic
steps upon the field of life, which, though
wreathed with roses, is also pierced with
thorns, may cling to this friendly hand, and
enveloped with prayer as with shadowing
wings, walk unharmed a midst the num-
berless dangers which enviorn the path of
youth and innocence.
	The youth upon the threshold of life, may
interpose it as a shield between him and all
his foes, and bear it in his breast as a faith-
ful monitor to guide him away from all evil.
	Oh, ye scoffers! try it “with pure mind
and feeling” and find yourselves nearer the
gates of heaven than you ever were before.
BEAVER
								L. L. D.


﻿THE SEX.
					-
	ONE hundred women are said, to be study- 
in law in American colleges.
	AGNES Ethel is to marry a wealthy New 
Yorker.
	THE Principal of Vassar College is Miss 
Perry; and Maria Mitchel is Professor of 
Astronomy in the same institution.
	THE piano sent to the future Empress of 
China is of gold and laquer, keys of mother- 
of-pearl, and plays itself like a music-box.
	TWO daughters of the Earl of Portsmouth 
have taken certificates at the London uni- 
versity, in connection with the Cambridge
local examinations.
	MISS Mary Nolan, of the St. Louis “Cen-
tral Magazine,” has applied for a patent for
a portable reservoir, of her invention, which
is to be used in preventing large fires.
	Marie Antoinette’s work-table has been
placed in the Louvre. The Empress Eugenie
bought it at a sale some years ago for $8,600,
and it was fortunately saved from the Tuil-
Ieries before the fire.
	MISS Eliza Lyman has Contracted to fur-
nish the lumber for all the water-tanks and
depot buildings of a new railroad to be built
in Vermont, and is going to superintend the
work herself.
	THE little coquettish curls or frizzes, now	
so commonly employed to adorn the brows 
of ladies were much worn by the belles of
the last century, and were called heart-	
breakers.
	IT is a singular fact that many ladies who
know how to preserve everything else, can’t 
preserve their tempers. Yet it may easily 
be done on the self-sealing principle. It is 
only to “keep the mouth of the vessel tightly	
closed.”
	MISS Ransom of Cleveland, Ohio, an artist 
of merit, who painted the first picture over 
bought by the government of a woman, has
fiuished a full-length portrait of General	
Thomas, which is spoken of as a work of 
great merit.
	EMILY Faithfull’s face, as described by a 
friend; “A pair of dark and very piercing
eyes over hung by a massive forehead and
brow, nose somewhat masculine, and mouth
large, firm, and backed by a set of good
teeth, make a face in which manly intellect
is intimately blended with manly firmness.”
	IN Strasburg, wearing the French tri- 
color having been forbidden by the Germans, 
the French ladies walk the streets by threes,
one dressed in red, the next in white, and
a third in blue. Engravings of these groups 
of fair promenaders, passing puzzled and
angry Prussians, are conspicuous in the
windows of the French print shops.	
	MRS. Virginia T. Minor has brought suit
in St. Louis, against the registration officer,
for refusing to place her name on the voting
lists. Mrs. Minor is the wife of Francis
Minor, Clerk of the supreme Court of
Missouri, who is determined to carry the
case to the United States Supreme Court,
claiming that women are entitled to the right
of suffrage under the Fourteenth Amend-
ment.
	It is related that in Gaylordsville, in Fair-
field county, Connecticut, there resides an
old lady who is so deaf that a large bell
rung immediately by her ear, cannot be
heard by her. Despite this absolute deaf-
ness, she will go to church, listen to a
lengthy sermon and then return home and
be able to repeat the discourse word for
word. Her entire knowledge of the sermon
being gained from the motion of the speaker’s	
lips.

					DUNFORD & SONS,
						SALT LAKE CITY.
	An ounce of practice is better than a pound
of theory.
	HE who sows courtesy reaps friendship,
and he who plants kindness gathers love.
	HE who never sacrificed a present to a future
good, or a personal to a general one, can
speak of happiness only as the blind do of
color.
	TRUE bravery is sedate and inoffensive;
if it refuses to submit to insults, it offers
none; it begins no disputes; enters no need-
less quarrels; it is above the little trouble-
some ambition to be distinguished every
moment; it bears in silence and replies with
modesty, fearing no enemy and making
none; and it is as much ashamed of insolence
as cowardice.
	TO live long—eat and drink and be com-
fortable. Never go to excess, and always be
hopeful. Let the world wag as it will, be hap-
py. If it goes well, laugh; if it goes ill, never
cry, for that makes it no better. Be cheerful,
contented, simple in habits, active in pur-
suits, wish well to man and have faith in
God. Then shall your years stretch out like
a summer’s day, and your youth shall be
perpetual.

		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

	     A UTAH LADIES' JOURNAL.

	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.
	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.
	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? “It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"
	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.
	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dross, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.
	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound 
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.
	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.
	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.
	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year,      $2.00	  1 Copy six months,    $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	10 Copies six months,   9.00
	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine. 

	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,
Utah.

Woman's Exponent.

﻿VOL. I. 	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MARCH 1, 1873.	 NO. 19.


﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.

	MAINE PROPOSES to give women the
ballot; progressive Maine!
	"DON'T TOUCH me; hit him;” is the way
Idaho talks of legislation for the Territories,
pointing to Utah.
	“THE HILL to be-devil the Mormons” is
how a leading Eastern paper talks of the
Frelinghuysen bill.
	THE WINTER just closing has been in
several parts of the country the severest
known for many years.
	INDIANA is gratified over the fact that it
has as many marriages as divorses. It could
scarcely divorce couples until they were
united.
	THE WORLD moves, annually, diurnally,
religiously, socially and politically, besides
the downward movement now painfully
conspicuous.
	WERE PIETY and patriotism as highly
prized and eagerly sought after as pelf,
what a happy, blessed country these United
States would be!
	THE SPANISH republic is threatened by
the Carlists, Bourbonists, Federalists, and
general disruption. The prospect is not
very encouraging.
	A PREMIUM of one hundred dollars was
offered for the prettiest baby at an Iowa
fair. A “nigger baby” won the prize. So
the world moves
	THE PRESENT session of Congress and the
present Congress—expires on the 4th inst.
at noon. It will be known in history as the
very discreditable Credit Mobilier session.
	INSTEAD OF a bitter bill by Congress
against Utah this session—which expires on
the 4th at noon—there is talk of a Commis-
sion to come to Utah and investigate
matters here.
	CALIFORNIA HAS an incorporated State
Woman’s Hospital, the trustees being com-
posed conjointly of prominent men and
women of San Francisco.	It was incorpor-
ated on the 18th ult.
	THE WOMAN suffragists of Oregon form
a State Society which is to meet once a
year. They will not be likely to meet
many times ere they meet the desired end
for which the society is organized.
	THE PROPOSAL of a commission to inves-
tigate matters in Utah meets with favor
from all quarters, except where there exists
a desire to push a crusade against the “Mor-
mons,” regardless of law, right or justice.
	A WISCONSIN singing master recently
flogged a young lady pupil, seventeen years
of age, breaking a wooden ruler on her per-
son. He is under arrest, and should have
the full benefit of the law in such cases made
and provided.
	THE ERA of fires in the United States is
not over, and now it is said the Interna-
tionalists, with branch societies in different
parts of the country, are threatening to burn
the leading cities. The scenes enacted in
Paris by the Internationalist Commune,
are not yet forgotten.
	NEW YORK imprisons George Francis
Train for publishing a paper containing
extracts and sentiments more pointed than
fashionable, calling it obscene literature; and 
allows the vilest illustrated papers to be
circulated freely, while its haunts of vice
are admittedly sustained by many of its
leading men and officials. Virtuous New
York!
 	FROM THE rapid spread of Spiritualism
and infidelity in the United States, it is a
question what fractional part of the commu-
nity nominal Christians form, and whether
a half of the clergymen have any belief in
what they preach.
	AFTER A few newspaper correspondents
had startled the country from its propriety
by the announcement that ten thousand
troops were to be sent to Utah, it was stated
on authority from official sources that there
was no truth in the report. A tithe of the
falsehoods published in a year concerning
this Territory, would make a large-sized
volume.
	THE WOMAN'S Suffrage Association, of
New York, at a meeting about two weeks
ago, adopted a resolution and protest, entreat-
ing the House of Representatives of Congress,
to defeat the bill offered by Merritt, dele-
gate from Idaho, which included the disfran-
chisement of the women of Utah. And for
this the members of the New York Woman’s
Suffrage Association have the warmest
thanks of the women of Utah.
	MRS. DUNIWAY wishes the charming
actress, Mrs. G. B. Waldron, “would flourish
her own feminine name, instead of the mas-
culine one of her husband.” Why? We
think it an honor to the lady to evince that
respect for her husband which she does in
wearing his name with dignity. And it is
certainly a credit to the profession they
follow, that there truly exists between the
gentleman and his wife an affinity of feeling,
said to be rarely met with among people of
their profession.
	DURING THE last presidential election,
Henry Wilson, the successful candidate for
Vice-President, authorized a telegraphic
dispatch to the New York “Times,” denying
that he owned any Credit Mobilier stock.
He admits that he did authorize the dis-
patch, and says he did not own the stock, as
he gave it to his wife. Our next Vice-
President for the four years commencing
March 4th, is a thoughtful man and a model
husband. Congressman Brooks turned over
his Credit Mobilier stock to his son-in-law,
but Mr. Wilson's plan of getting rid of it was
much better, for it kept it still closer in the
family.
	ONE OF the most quiet and important
revolutions of the century was that by
which Spain ceased to be a monarchy
and became a republic. The young King
Amadeus, with rare wisdom in so young a
monarch, seeing that his occupancy of the
throne was the cause of internal dissensions
in his kingdom, resolved to abdicate and
did so. The Cortes, or national congress,
after receiving the formal decree of abdica-
tion passed a vote establishing a republic
and immediately a provisional government
was formed. So far the new republic has
conducted itself with dignity, and with the
adhesion of the masses may become in a
short time more firmly rooted than is the
present French government, that seems
dependent on the will of Thiers for its
existence.
	THE NEWSPAPERS continue full of sensa-
tions concerning Utah; and one hardly
wonders that people traveling between the
East and West should feel a little tremor
when they approach Salt Lake City, as the
“news” widely circulated will lead honest
people who are not accustomed to have
wholesale fabrications palmed upon them
for truth, to imagine that there was a dread-
ful state of society here. And yet, with the
exception of a lawlessly disposed class that
has recently come to this city, especially
since a “vigilance committee” compelled
some of them to flee from Pioche, the city is
one of the most quiet on the continent. But
by and by summer will be here, travel will
increase across the continent, and as it was a
couple of years ago, when people were half
frightened to come here and were astonished
at the magnitude of the published false-
hoods when they arrived, so it will be
again.
	PARSON NEWMAN, chaplain of the U. S.
Senate, has again let his “piety” run loose
in stirring up a crusade against Utah. His
eloquence having failed to force poor argu-
ments on sensible and better-informed 
people, he seems determined, if possible, to
make them feel that there is a strong arm
of power which can be invoked in his favor.
Failing In logic and debate he would try
bayonet and rifled cannon. Frederick the
Great, of Prussia, was wont to enforce his
arguments, it is said, by heavy kicks on
the shins of those who discussed with him.
One day he remaked to an officer: “You
cannot stand up before my arguments.”
“How can I” was the response, “while you
wear such heavy boots?” Parson Newman
wishes his arguments sustained by the
machinery of perverted courts, infantry 
regiments, cavalry cohorts, and artillery
batteries. He desires the people of Utah
to understand that the sword is at hand to
force his syllogisms down the throats of the
Latter-day Saints. And he is a clergyman,
too! 
	SUPPOSE A crusade should be urged
against the Roman Catholics in this country,
because they believe in the infallibility of
the Pope and the invocation of Saints; or
against the Second Adventists because they
believe the Lord Jesus Christ will speedily
come and rule over the earth; or against the
Methodists for imagining that the simple
action of the mind in believing will secure
their salvation; or against the Presbyterians
for holding that a portion of the human
family are born to be saved and cannot be
damned, while another portion are born to
suffer eternal tortures and cannot secure
salvation, and what a howl would resound
throughout the entire country! Yet this is
precisely what is being urged against the
Latter-day Saints because they believe in
Prophets and present revelation; for the cry
against polygamy, it is admitted, is merely
raised for effect,the immorality of the age
caring but little in regard to morality, and
perfectly satisfled that the people of Utah
are the most moral of any community on
the continent. The onslaught against the
“Mormons” is merely an attack on religious
liberty, stirred up by a few who either do
not comprehend what they are doing or are
entirely reckless as to its results, if they can
only accomplish their purposes.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿[Written for the Exponent.
			THE YEAR 1872.

	The Year is stepping out regardless of
My long, long distance from my Mountain Home.
It leaves me in Italia's “sunny clime,"
Where verdant foliage, gentle breezes kiss,
And balmy zephers fan the evening tide.

	The year now passing out, has, in its course
In lib’ral portions, meted out to me
The wide extremes of deep bereavement, and
Munificence in richly flowing streams,
Which I acknowledge freely ere we part.

	All grateful reminiscences, the old,
Expiring Year inscribes indelibly
On mem’ry's sacred tablet, richly wreath’d
With choice mementos of the good produced—
Of vict’ries truth and justice have achieved—
Improvement’s progress in the march of mind,
And every aid to poor humanity;
While its successor treads upon its heels

	Good bye old Year. We both are moving on:
You, to the cloister of the mighty past,
To join it to the future yet unborn,
I, to the farfamed land of Palestine,
Which has a hist’ry of the past, that bears
With a momentous and eternal weight,
Of destiny to all of human kind,
Upon the future which the passing years
With hurried tread ere long will introduce
With bold, magnificent developments.

	I go to place my feet upon the land
Where once, the Prince of Peace, the Son of God
Was born—where once he lived and walk’d and
	preach’d,
And prayed, admonished, taught, rebuked and blest;
And then, to answer justice’ great demand,
And seal his mission of Eternal Love,
Upon the cross poured out his precious blood —
Arose to life triumphant o’er the tomb;
And after being seen and heard and felt,
Ascended up to heaven; and as he went,
These who stood looking, heard an angel say,
"Ye men of Gallilee, why stand ye here
Gazing to heaven? The selfsame Jesus, whom
Ye see ascending, in like manner will
Again descend.’'

				Each year that passes on,
Clips from the thread of time, a portion of
Its intervening length, and hurries up
The coming great and grand fulfilment of
That strange prediction—strange and strangely true.

That most moment’ous period for the great
Event, is fast approximating, and
The moving of the waters now, amidst
The nations of the earth, like deepes; shades
Of pencil drawings, seem foreshadowing
The world’s great crisis.

						Human policy
Grows tremulous; while human governments,
With tender care are fondly fostering,
And feeding with their life’s best nourishment,
The seeds of their own dissolution.

								France
Is poising on a pivot. England rests
On her broad pedestal, but resting moves
With vaulting tendencies. The tamed
Italia stands in leaning posture from
The Papal Chair to King Emmanuel;
While Russia, beckoning to Austria,
To Germany, or whosoever will;
Solicits help to lift the balance
Of Power, now lying just beyond her reach.

The wires of destiny are working on,
To consummate eternal purposes,
And bring results of change, that must precede
“The Second coming of the Son of Man;"
When, unto Him, “whose right it is to reign,"
All human pow’rs and governments will bow.
								Eliza R. Snow.
Milan, Italy, Dec , 31, 1872.


﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.

			TOOELE CITY, Feb. 10th, 1873.
DEAR EDITOR:—
    According to request, I write to inform
you that as a society we unanimously agreed,
some time ago, to drop the word “Female,"
from our appellation. I think it was a very
sensible move of Sister Snow’s,
    We feel, as a branch of the Relief Society,
thankful that we have sisters to lead us who
possess the Spirit of God and are able to give
us meat in due season.
    The EXPONENT, I think, is a paper that
is bound to succeed; it is “filled” with good
counsel, good ideas, and carries with it a
saving influence; and every family ought to
have it.
	I believe our Society in Tooele can com-
pare favorably with that of any other
settlement. It is now nearly three years
since we were organized, and we have been
trying ever since to do our best. We have
excellent meetings. The sisters are alive
to their duties, and I believe will accomplish
much good. We held our annual ball last
week, to assist in raising means for the
Emigration Fund; it was quite a success.
We have room enough to do a very large
amount of good, both at home and abroad;
and that God may strengthen and direct us
in all our ways is the humble prayer of your
sisters,
	MRS. MARY MEIKLEJOHN, President;
EMILY WARBURTON, Secretary.

		    SECOND WARD, S. L. City,
				February 13th, 1873.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
    At our meeting on the 2nd of January,
1873, we were favored with the presence of
our Bishop, Brother Leach, and his Counsel-
ors; also sisters Zina D. Young, Elizabeth
Howard and Martha B. Young; who ad-
dressed the meeting, exhorting to faithful-
ness and perseverance in good works. Our
financial report gave a good showing for the
year 1872. A motion to drop the word
“Female” from the name of the Society
was presented, and the vote to sustain the
proposition was unanimous.
    We held another excellent meeting on
the 6th of February, at which sister Sarah
Wheeler was appointed and unanimously sus-
tained as President of the Society, in the
place of sister Nicolona Oleson, who has been
faithful and energetic in her calling, but has
lately left the Ward.
			JANE RICKARD, Secretary.

		RELIEF SOCIETY, 10th WARD,
		   Salt Lake City, Feb. 21, 1873.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
    At a meeting held November 7th 1872, it
was agreed upon by our Society to omit the
word “Female,” as suggested. Our report
comes thus late on account of our failing to
get the understanding that each Society
was desired to report.
    Although our subscription list to your
excellent paper is yet small, it will increase;
for many are pleased with it. Those inter-
esting letters from Sister E. R. Snow are
splendid. May God bless, preserve and
inspire her to write us many such is the
prayer of her faithful sisters. The Society
here is prospering. Our monthly business
meetings are moderately attended, and
good feelings pervade; our semi-monthly
meetings, for testimony, prayer and etc.,
are doing much good. Our Retrenchment
Society has been reorganized and our young
sisters have made a very good beginning.
We have the confidence of our Bishop and
his counselors who are well pleased with our
exertions. Our President has been very
sick but is now better.
    May success attend the EXPONENT and
may it go faithfully and steadily onward.
    Respectfully yours,
			SARAH STRONG, President.
    SUSANNA M. NAYLOR, Secretary.

EDTIOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
    I feel that I owe you an apology for neg-
lecting to forward our mutual approbation in
regard to dropping the word “Female,” as
it is considered superfluous in connection
with the name of the Society.
    The Society of the 4th Ward (Provo,) is
in a healthy condition, new members being
added at nearly every meeting. It could
not well be otherwise with such a President
as Mrs. Holden, who delights in the order
of the Kingdom of God, often referring to its
beauties and grandeur. And she has the
assistance of a “live Bishop,” whose ex-
ertions are unceasing in looking after and
providing for the poor. I have never known
his individual interests so pressing that he
could not sympathize with the bereaved of
his Ward, and accompany them to the last
resting place of the deceased friends. His
presence at our meetings, his counsel and
instructions, are ever cheering and highly
appreciated. So much for Bishop Wm. A.
Follett. Our much esteemed Treasurer,
Mrs. M. J. Smith, in consequence of ill
health, has not been able to meet with us
this winter, yet she has exerted herself far
beyond her strength for the good of the
Society. A person of less “vim” would
long ago have yielded to another the laborious
position of Treasurer, but with energy like
her’s, there is no shrinking while the vital
spark burns.
    With kind love to yourself and all inter-
ested in the great cause of regeneration,
        I am truly yours,
			L. W. KIMBALL, Secretary,
4th Ward Relief Society, Provo.

    At the Retrenchment meeting in the 8th
Ward, Salt Lake City, held February 12th,
1873, Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball said she was
present in the interest of Physiology. If
she had a “hobby,” it was the importance
of having a knowledge of one’sself and the
proper uses of one’s powers. Self knowledge
was the first step toward self improvement.
Science taught that pain was a penalty and
did notbelong to nature. If one was dys-
peptic, there was a cause for it, either
inherited, or through abuse of some of the
powers. Human bodies were not forlorn,
disagreeable objects and should not be
subjected to causes that would make them
such. Every correct lesson learned of self,
made one better acquainted with her fellow
beings. “We give ourselves the wounds we
feel.” Health was productive of happiness,
which was the legitimate pursuit of all
flesh. Ladies’ skirts should not be fastened
with bands but the weight of their clothing
should be suspended from the shoulders.
Tight lacing was a sin against humanity.
The natural shape of the human body was a
little flattened and not round, as it was
forced to appear from the pressure it
received by the way in which many ladies
dressed. The body should never be clothed in
a manner to prevent the breath being drawn
in deeply and freely. The breath was the
life. When weary and feeling depressed,
hardly aware of what the cause might be,
one would often find relief from long, deep
breathing, there being too little oxygen in
the system. Professor Bronson, a learned
physician, while suffering with consumption,
from which, it was believed, he never could
recover, had gained a “thought,” while
watching a dying child, which was the
means of his restoration to health. He saw
that the child’s breath grew shorter and
shorter as death approached it, and saw also
that if the child could only continue to
breath, it could live. He thought, then,
that he would not cease to breath; and
he would go into the open air and draw long,
deep breaths for some time; and that simple
remedy cured him. Professor Fowler had
also told of once being so completely ex-
hausted that he felt it an impossibility for 
him to fulfill an engagement to lecture.
He threw himself upon the bed and began


drawing long, heavy breaths, which he felt,
rested and restored his fatigued body, and
he was soon able to rise and attend to his
business.
	The speaker asked if we could not apply
these and similar lessons to ourselves, and
answered that we could; she had proven it;
that twenty, thirty or even thirty-five years
ago she had seemed older than now, had never
enjoyed better health or been half so happy
in her life before, as at the present time.
	Sister Zina D. Young remarked that no
one had any right to trifle with existence
God had given. A short time ago a young
lady had died suddenly, and after her
death it was ascertained that by lacing, her
ribs had been turned in, so that the front
ends almost met with the back-bone. Good
sense should once more get the upper hand
of pride and vanity, and then woman could
live long and fulfill the measure appointed
unto her with rejoicing. It was well to
always cultivate a happy, cheerful spirit.
If the tears of the daughters of Zion were
to be wiped away, the present was as good a
time as there would ever be to begin it.
	Mrs. Willmarth East said, that to contem-
plate the degeneracy of the human family
was enough to convince any one that it was
necessary to study and learn the laws of life
and health. Dieting was one thing that too
little notice was taken of; and going with
damp feet was one of the worst things a
woman could do to produce desease and
suffering. For the sake of future genera-
tions, mothers should endeavor to preserve
health and longevity that the same might
be implanted in their offspring.
	Miss L. L. Greene was interested in the
subject of Physiology; the laws of nature
were the laws of God; it was impossible to
violate the one without sinning against the
other. In order to reasonably exercise
faith in God—men and women should live
in accordance with the laws of their beings.
The principles of the Gospel were the prin-
ciples of eternal life; a proper knowledge
and use of the faculties, physical and mental,
would make gods and godesses of the
children of men.
	Sister Mary A. Leaver, President of the
8th Ward R. Society, was grateful to the
sisters who had visited the Ward—not to
benefit themseles, but others. Hoped the
daughters of Zion might become one in
gaining useful knowledge and live by the
same.
	Sisters Tufts, Needham, Chamberlain
and Leaver, (daughter-in-law to President
Mrs. Mary A. Leaver,) bore testimony to the
truth, as revealed in the Latter-days, and
asked blessings upon the sisters who were
visiting and instructing them.
	Mrs. Lona Cross Spencer, President of the
Young Ladies’ Retrenchment Association,
thought that the faith of the sisters was
strengthened by their joining their testi-
monies and speaking words of comfort and
encouragement to each other. Brother
Heber C. Kimball used to talk very plainly
to the sisters about their manner of dress.
She thanked sister Kimball and the sisters
who came with her for their good words.
	Sister Kimball said the grateful feeling
was mutual—she and he companions also
returned thanks

			Salt Lake City, 13 Ward,
						January 25th, 1873.
Dear Editor:—
	We intended sending you a report of our
Society long ere this, but sickness and other
unavoidable hindrances have prevented.
Our meetings, as in most instances, have
been attended by the faithful few, though
many who do not meet with us have
donated liberally. Our receipts for dona-
tions since the Society was organized
amounts to $1,597:50. The sick and needy
have been cared for; we have assisted in
building a house for an aged couple, besides
expending means to aid repairs on the As-
sembly Rooms; donated to the P. E. Fund
$161; towards building the Temple $270;
have invested in the 13th Ward and Saving
Bank to the credit of $230 and in the
Treasury, in cash, clothing, etc. $72:50. Our
meetings are held the first Thursday in each
month; the Spirit of the Lord is much
enjoyed by those attending, who realize
that they are times of refreshing and tend
to strengthen and stimulate us to increased
energy and diligence in our duties as Saints
of God, and in the good cause in which we
are engaged. I believe I can say, there is
an increasing interest taken in contributing
to the good of the Society, and that it is in
a flourishing condition; trusting that the
demands and necessities of the poor will
continue to enlist our sympathies and efforts
to do them good, until it can be said “There
are no poor in Zion.” The Woman’s Ex-
ponent is highly appreciated by many of
our members, who pray that it may continue
to increase in interest and circulation.
		ELIZABETH H. Goddard, Secretary.

	Saturday the 15th of February, being a
fine and favorable day, a very fair represen-
tation of the members of the Retrenchment
Association were present at the 14th Ward
Assembly Rooms, in this city—their usual
place of meeting.
	After the ordinary preliminaries, Presi-
dent Mrs. Mary I, Horne made excellent
remarks concerning faith, which she testified
was indeed, in the language of the Apostle,
“The substance of things hoped for—the
evidence of things not seen.” If by faith
the sick had been healed, mountains re-
moved, the dead raised to life, and many
other marvelous things accomplished, what
might not the Saints hope for if they but
lived to secure to themselves that faith
which is obtained through obedience to the
requirements of the laws of God? If we
had but faith as a grain of mustard seed, it
had been written, that we might say to
that mountain “remove hence” and it would
be done. The Saints had nothing to fear if
they would live their religion so as to keep
the faith, and also gain continual increase
of the same. But without faith their power
would amount to nought.
	Sister Percinda L. Kimball said, every-
thing that was necessary to be done, could
be done through faith. If the people lived
faithfully and humbly they should not fail
in faith, but their faith be strengthened
constantly. They should exercise faith for
each other and for the Kingdom of God,
and if they could but live worthy of faith,
such as God was able and willing to bestow
upon them, no power would be able to harm
them, but all things would work together
for the good of the Saints.
	Sister Hannah W. Brinton was happy to
see the countenances of her sisters beaming
with intelligence and light, which bespoke
good hearts and pure minds. She believed
it impossible to accomplish any good thing
without faith.
	Sister Eleanor J. Pratt saw that there was
much to weaken the faith of the Saints, yet,
she believed the women present, if united,
could have sufficient faith to avert any evil
that might be aimed against Israel.
	Sister Sarah Groo said that it required
considerable faith to induce her to arise and
address an intelligent congregation like that
before her. She was glad that faith had
been chosen as a subject for consideration
in the meetings; she had been edified and
felt that she understood more concerning
faith than ever before.
	Counselor Mrs. E. Howard gave a very 
interesting account of her early experience
in the Church, and of the peculiar manner
in which the light of truth was first made
manifest to her mind, and showed what
power there was in the prayer of faith.
After which she said that the enemies of
the Saints were preparing to give them 
another lift up the hill; as they were—
many of them—too careless and indolent,
about the things of their religion, to keep in
the right path, except they were urged on
occasionally.
	Other remarks were made, and there ap-
peared to be a general impression that the
subject of faith had been introduced into
the meetings at the right moment, that its
exercise, with earnest prayer in behalf of
the Church, was highly requisite.	
	The same subject was to be continued at
the next meeting.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


A COOL-HEADED YOUNG WOMAN.

	A short distance this side of Union, on the
Union and Titusville Railroad, there is a
very long and very high trestle, and one
upon which nobody ventures who is at all
inclined to be light-headed. Immediately
this side of the trestle there is a sharp curve
in the road, so that a person walking on it
can not be seen by the engineer of an
approaching train until it is nearly upon
him. On Friday last, as Mr. Wm. Toles,
engineer of Mr. Holmes’ train came around
the curve at a good rate of speed, he was
horrified to discover a lady about the mid-
dle of the trestle, and hardly a train’s length
from him. Quick as thought “Billy”
whistled “down brakes,” gave her sand, and
threw back the reversing lever, while at
the same time he knew that it was an utter
impossibility to check the heavy train before
the victim would be overtaken and crushed
to death, and with fixed eyes he awaited
the catastrophe. The lady heard the
warning Whistle, and turning her head saw
the iron monster almost upon her. Escape
seemed impossible, to remain was certain
death, to jump to the ground beneath, a
distance of thirty or forty feet, equally cer-
than death, and to attempt to run ahead and
escape was out of the question. Unlike
ten thousand young ladies—and she is said
to have been young and fair—out of ten
thousand and one, she did not scream or
faint or indulge in any nonsense of any kind,
but realizing the situation in an instant, and
taking the chances all in, she proceeded to
an action that saved her life. About thirty
inches below the ends of the ties, and im-
mediately under the stringer which supports
them, there is a joint five inches wide run-
ning from one support of the trestle to
another, and to this the clear-headed girl
resorted for safety. Stepping to the end of
the ties she swung herself down to this
narrow thread with the apparent case of a
gymnast, and with her arms clasped around
it stretched herself at full length along it as
the train thundered by almost over her. As
soon as the engineer saw her action he threw
off his brakes, and putting on steam hurried
past as soon as possible, when she nimbly
sprang to the track again and pursued her
journey as though nothing had happened.
—“Titusville (Penn.) Herald.”


﻿THERE can certainly be no greater felicity
than to be able to look back on a life usefully
and virtuously employed, and to trace our
progress in existence by such tokens as
excite neither shame nor sorrow.
