		SALT LAKE CITY, MARCH 1, 1873.


SPLINTERS.
	Gail Hamilton has the small-pox......Mrs.
Stowe has a new book called “Palmetto
Leaves.”......Laura Keam is candidate for
the office of State Librarian in Indiana.
Mr. Conklin is alluded to as the thin-haired
Apollo-Belvidere of the Senate......The Arch-
Duke Charles of Austria has lately completed
his seventieth year......The income tax in
India is to be abolished......Nellie Grant
will visit Europe again this year......The
Crown Prince of Germany is an artist......
Fred Douglas talks of going to live in Eu-
rope......A Terre Haut, Indiana, belle stands
six feet one inch in her stockings......Mrs.
Henry Wood has a new story entitled “The
Master of Greyhounds”.......Barnum lately
had his pockets picked and a diamond pin
stolen......The balance of trade against the
United States last year was in round num-
bers $118,000,000......Michigan produced 118 1/2
pairs of boy twins during 1870, the other
half was a girl......A St. Louis paper says
that fashionable wedding trips in Chicago
consist of a foot race to the nearest
divorce court......The Japanese Embassy was
received with military honors at Calais......
The Sisters of Notre Dame have offered
their services as nurses at the small-pox
hospital in Boston......A premium was
offered at a late fair in Montgomery, Ala.,
for the “young woman who would make
the best wife for a poor man.”......The new
head-quaters of the Pullman Palace Car
Company, in Chicago, are finished and
occupied......Powell’s new painting of Per-
ry’s Victory has been placed in the Capitol
at Washington......A woman in Delaware
recently died at the age of 72, after serving
fifty years in one family as a farm hand.
The lecture field will soon be abandoned by
Olive Logan....Froude’s return to England
was much sooner than he expected.........
Oysters are being taken from Norfolk, Va.,
to be planted on the British coast......Ice at
St. Paul has been fourteen feet thick......
Chicago lost 800 horses by the distemper.
......Connecticut has 114 Baptist churches.
A locomotive shop in Russia employs 4,000
men......Small-pox is spreading in New
England......Brooklyn, Long Island, had
twenty-six fires in December......Lake
George was frozen over in December last, the
third time in thirty years at so early a date.
......Wisconsin is said to be more than two
million dollars in debt.....Lancaster, N. H.,
has had a touch of 42 degrees below zero......
The University at Geneva has, in a gentle-
manly manner, opened its doors to women.
......Philadelphia proposes a tower 1,000
feet high, to illuminate the grounds of the
great Centennial Exposition......The Chicago
public library is kept in a large iron water-
tank......A monster nettle of Australia
grows to the height of forty feet....Yale
College is to have a new chapel to cost
$100,000......The Boston flats are being filled
up with rubbish from the burned buildings.
......Massachusetts has $184,797,313:92 in
her savings’ banks......It is said that free
use of the Atlantic cable has been offered to
the Smithsonian Institute......Ohio men get
fined for opening their wives’ letters......
During the past year, within the limits of
the United States, 7,478 miles of railroad
have been completed, and 6,514 miles put
under construction, making a total of 13,992
miles on which work has been done.........
New Jersey has thirty-three savings banks.
......The abreviatory letters, M. C. for Mem-
ber of Congress, may now be appropriately
transversed, and read C. M. for Creditors
Mobilier......The amount of bonds which
the Government issued to the Union Pacific
Railroad Company, is pronounced $27,237,512
currency, bearing interest at the rate of 6
per cent per annum......Comodore J. D. Mc-
Kenny, of the United States Navy, lately
died, sixty-six years old, and leaving
property valued at more than $100,000......
Miss Rothschild, a daughter of the well
known family, was married in London, on
the 12th of February, to Mr. Elliott of New
York......Another Arizona diamond hunter
comes to Virginia City with several pounds
of what are pronounced “geuine rubies.”......
The President of Harvard, Mr. Elliott, has
given up his own residence to a student
who is down with the small-pox......Ken-
tucky wants to establish a law to have
separate schools for its white children and
colored ones.....Rose Porro, arrested in
Naples for child murdering, has confessed
the terrible crime of having starved and
poisoned, as near as she can recollect, about
forty infants, to satisfy her greed for money.
......More money is said to be made in
America by the cheap story-weeklies than
by the entire daily press.......Frauds, law-
suits, and false telegrams are so common
that they no longer create the slightest
surprise.
......A notorious Corsican—bandit, Antonio
Suzzoni, who has four times been
sentenced to be hanged and each time made
his escape, once from the very scaffold, was
lately shot and killed......Two gentlemen
in Virginia, Nevada, have adopted a plan
for prospecting for ore, similar to that em-
ployed in sinking artesian wells......A young
lady in Indiana, a few days since, fell into
the fire in a fainting fit, and burned to
death before the accident was discovered.
......Tennie C. Clafflin has lost her Colonel-
ship......Ben Butler is said to write sympa-
thetic letters to Mrs. Woodhull......Speaking
of the Credit Mobilier, a contemporary says,
“Who steals will lie!”....Newbern, N. C.,
eats shad at $1:80 per pair......In some
portions of France and Spain Woman’s
Rights are wonderfully recognized; she may
chop her own wood, fodder her husband’s
stock, and all that sort of thing, besides
doing the house-work......Colorado has
seven daily papers, six more than Florida.
......The euphonious name of a suburb of
Dubuque is “Dirty Holland.”


﻿NEED FOR COURAGE AND UNITY.

    To believe, or even to know a thing, no mat-
ter how important, amounts to very little
unless that which is believed can be tested, or
the knowledge existing be put to use. It is
true faith which gives a person courage to
speak or act in defense of right, at the risk of
being considered unpopular; to be honest,
though it bring but poverty and neglect; to be
loyal, though misjudged, misrepresented and
frowned upon; to stand firm and steadfast for
the truth, though all is dark, save where the
thought of God and hope of brighter days shed
one small ray of light—just enough to keep the
soul from sinking utterly. True faith gives
strength and heart to act, even in the face of op-
position; to tell the truth, however harsh it may
seem, if necessary. Though friendships be turned
to gall, though kindred desert and cherished
ones turn away in derision—still, true faith
will have the power to stand, and even walk
into the wilderness, if so directed by the voice
of God.
    When has there ever been a time since the
“Morning Stars sang together,” when to
exercise faith in the true and living God, and 
to manifest that faith by its legitimate repre- 
sentative—earnest work in the behalf of virtue 
and integrity—has not aroused the ire of Satan
and those who do his work?  Yet, when ever 
was the world so full of corruption, so crimsoned
with crime, so fully ripe in iniquity as at the
present time? When ever was unity among
the Saints of God so requisite as now, when all
the powers of the Evil One seem to be arrayed
in arrogance and stubborn, jealous enmity
against the cause of right and justice?
    Mothers should be united in teaching to the
youth who look to them for aid and direction,
the principles of everlasting truth; and the
young should be as one in seeking after that
faith and knowledge and divine revelation,
which will keep them safe from falling into
temptation, and give them the courage to
think and to act in spite of the taunts and
jeers of the vain and wicked.  The Saints
should begin to see that the unity which they
have been long counseled and urged to adopt, is
highly necessary to their safety and their
peace.

	HISTORY REPEATING ITSELF.

    Some eighteen centuries ago the world
saw the spectacle of the most powerful
nation on the earth attempting to crush out a
handful of believers in a new faith. That
power was Rome; that faith was the Gospel, as 
revealed by the Savior of mankind.  Pagan
Rome had no objections to the few who be-
lieved in Christ elevating him to a niche in the
Pantheon among their other deities, and being
recognized as one of the gods to whom temples 
were reared; but would not acknowledge, nor
permit others to proclaim, his superiority to
the deities before whom the Romans bowed in 
reverence. And edicts went forth to compel
the Christians to sacrifice to the Pagan deities,
or to inflict upon those that refused the severest 
penalties. And not alone on account of religion 
were those edicts to be enforced, for as one 
doctrine of the Christian faith was that their
Lord would come as a Messiah to deliver Israel 
and establish his Kingdom, this was held to be 
treason against Rome, punishable with death. 
So the Christians were hunted like wolves,
were thrown into the arena to combat with
wild beasts, were crucified, tortured in the
most horrible manner, and martyred, to satisfy
the vengance of those whodemanded that they
should turn away from the faith they had
received, deny Christ, and acknowledge the
supremacy of Rome and the gods worshipped
by the Romans.
    People now look back to those terrible 
seasons of persecution as presented on the
pages of history, that the Christians had to
pass through, and thank God that such scenes 
cannot be enacted now; that  people, especially
in this enlightened Republic, are not persecuted
for conscience’ sake; and, perhaps in the next
breath, will urge the passage by Congress of 
some proscriptive measure against the Latter-
day Saints. Point out to them the incon-
sistency of their so doing, and they will appear
amazed.  It is not the religion of the Latter-
day Saints that they are opposed to; oh, no!
But they object to "a theocratic govermment”
—that is, a government where God rules; they


object to the “absurd idea” of there now being
Prophets and Apostles; and they object to
the Celestial order of marriage, as it has been
revealed in this dispensation. In fact, these
people are willing the “Mormons” should
believe just as they please, it being understood
that such belief will be in accordance with the
views entertained by these very consistent
individuals as to what forms a proper kind of
belief. The Second Adventists, like the ancient
Christians, believe in the Second Coming of
the Lord Jesus Christ, and but little is said of
it; but the Latter-day Saints believe the same
thing, and they are accused of teaching trea-
sonable doctrines, in consequence.
	Viewed in the light of calm, philosophic
and unprejudiced reflection, when the attempt
is made by the United States, to-day, to compel
Latter-day Saints to change their religious be-
lief, discard doctrines held sacred, repudiate
their Prophets and Apostles, and deliberately
shut the gates of communication opened by
the Almighty between the heavens and the
earth, at the risk of imprisonment, despoiling
of property and perhaps death, then this
Republic aims at accomplishing what Rome
tried to do through many bitter and bloody
persecutions, and failed. Nor are we exagger-
ating the prospect.  Were some of the bills
lately introduced in Congress to become law,
and the desired power be conferred on certain
men in this Territory, it would be stretched
until the gallows would rise up, black and
ghastly, against the sunlight of heaven, and
they would compass, if possible, the death of
some of the best men in the community, for
alleged crimes which they never commit-
ted, yet, really for religion’s sake.  But so
far the Almighty has preserved His people;
yet, that history is repeating itself, is none the
less clear.  Here we have the mightest power
on earth—for with its boundless resources and
rapidly growing population this country is so
—having its authority directed by evil-minded
and pitiless bigots to crush out the people that
pioneered and settled this vast interior region;
and the cry goes up "Root out the Mormons
from Utah." Wiser men, better men, the more
conservative element of society, even if they
cannot coincide with the belief of the Latter-
day Saints, deprecate all such proscriptive and
extreme measures as are urged.  But the fanat-
ical class is active, and the numerous class does
not reflect while the conservative element is qui-
escent; and the whole nation ts permitting
itself to be driven to persecution, to the destruc-
tion of religious liberty, and to the overthrow
of republican principles, unless the best citizens
of the country bestir themselves to avert the
danger.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	The Utah question and the woman question
should change corners during one session of
Congress.
    IT IS worth noteing, that the heaviest snow-
storm which has fallen in this valley for years
commenced on Monday night last, 24th ult.,
and continued until Wednesday morning.  A
very large amount of snow fell during the time.
    PRESIDENT BRIGHAM Young has returned
from Southern Utah, where he has spent
the winter so far. It is gratifying to state that
his health is good, and that he enjoyed himself
very much while south; while the Saints there
appreciated his society.
    WE LEARN from a private communica-
tion from Fountain Green, Sanpete County,
that considerable sickness in those parts is
occasioned by a disease which some pronounce
Small-pox. Our correspondent had heard of
no deaths resulting from it at the time of writ-
ing.
    THROUGH COURTESY of President Mrs. Mary
I. Horne, of this city, and President Mrs. Jane
S. Richards, of Ogden, we are enabled to fur-
nish our readers in this number of the
EXPONENT, with two highly interesting
communications from “the Representative of
the Ladies of Utah, abroad"—Sister Eliza R.
Snow.
    THE EXPONENT has once stated that all
communications, addressed to its columns, to
receive attention must be accompanied by the
signature of the writer.  Again it becomes
necessary for us to request contributors to send
us their names—not for publication, except
they desire it—as was before stated, but because
it is proper for us to know who our correspond-
ents are.
    THE PALESTINE party, when last heard from,
were at Naples, Italy, on January 29th, and
were to leave the next morning for Egypt.
On the 27th they visited the excavation of
Pompei, buried for eighteen centuries; the
next day they visited Herculaneum, also buried
at the same time by an eruption of Vesu-
vius; and on the 29th they ascended the
burning mountain, President George A. Smith
being carried up in a chair, and looked down
the awful crater which has so often belched
destruction on the towns and villages at its
base.
    DURING THE week between the 15th and 22nd
of last month two deaths occurred, news of which
was sent all through the Territory, and from
which our young people, particularly the male
portion of the youth of our communities, may
draw striking examples of a highly beneficial
character. We refer to the dreadful, horrifying 
doom of the outlaw and murderer, young Ben-
son, of Logan; and the calm, hopeful, holy
death of the trusty servant and follower of
God, Major William Pitt, of this city.  Of the
former, little need be said; the gloomy circum-
stances connected with a life so dark, a death
so terrible, stand without comment, a fearful
warning to persons of unrestrained passions, or
those who are given to vicious habits.  But a
death and funeral like that of Elder Pitt, can
only be pronounced a triumph, for which, it
was worth living an honest, virtuous useful
life. To witness such a gathering of friends,
and to listen to the cheerful, light-producing
testimonies of inspired men on such an occa-
sion, bring up the hopeful thoughts of one of
old, “Let me live the life of the righteous,
and die like this just man.”


WOMAN'S VOICE.

EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
    As a lover of Truth, I take great pleasure
in advocating the same. I have been in the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
twenty-two years, and have had much
experience in that time.  It is over twenty
years since I left father, mother, brothers
and sisters for the Gospel.  I am a believer
in polygamy, have lived in it sixteen years
and have never once wished to be out of it.
I thank God my Heavenly Father, for His
goodness in enlightening my mind to know
the Truth when I heard it; and that I have
embraced it.  And I do know that the Lord
has heard and answered my prayers many
times.  I take the EXPONENT and like it
much; think two dollars well spent in sub-
scribing for it, and would not be without it
for anything.
						      M. E. W.
Salt Lake City.


﻿DIVERSIONS.

	A RISING man—A lamplighter.
	SHOCKING—A magnetic battery.
	HOW to serve a good dinner—eat it.
	A “YOUNG SHAVER.”—A barber’s boy.
	A TRUISM.—An expensive wife makes a
pensive husband.
	BOYS are like vinegar: the more “mother”
there is in them, the sharper they become.
	THERE are some things it won’t do to
trifle with—for instance, a woman’s opinion
and the business end of a wasp.
	WHAT is the difference between a mur-
derous attack and pig killing? One is
assault with intent to kill, and the other is a
kill with intent to salt.
	SOMEBODY inquiring at the Springville, Ill.
post-office for a letter for Mike Howe, re-
ceived the gruff answer that there was no
letter there for anybody’s cow.
	AN exchange tantalizes its readers with
this atrocity: “Have you heard of the man
who got shot?” “Got shot? No; how did
he get shot?” He bought them.
	A YOUNG lady who was rebuked by her
mother for kissing her intended, justified
the act by quoting the passage: “'Whatso-
ever you would that men should do unto
you, do you even so unto them.”
	A LITTLE four-year old sat alone in the
parlor, when a new doctor came suddenly
to see his sick mother. The doctor naturally
wished to make his acquaintance, and
asked, “How old are you, my son?” “I’m
not old—I’m new,” said the boy.
	THE man who sat up all night weighing
a ton of coal with a pair of steelyards, and
by the pailful, to see that he had received
full weight, thought he was a-head a few
pounds until he remembered that he had
not deducted the weight of the pail.
	A LARGE room in the accountant’s office
at the East India House was divided into
boxes or compartments, in each of which
six clerks were accustomed to sit, of whom
Lamb once was one. They were called
“Compounds,” and, the meaning of the
word being asked one day, Lamb explained
it as a “collection of simples.”
	“WHAT are you bellowing about?” cried
an irate mother at the foot of the stairs one
evening, after her two boys had been put
to bed. “Please, mother,” said bellowing
Bill, “Jim wants half the bed.” “Well,”
said she, “let him have half the bed and
you take the other half.” “Yes, mother,”
said Bill, “but he wants his half out of the
middle, and make me sleep on both sides of
him.”
	THE day before Washington’s birthday,
in February last, a lady teacher, in giving
notice of the coming holiday to her pupils,
said something about the good Washington,
and then asked the question:
	“Why should we celebrate Washington’s
birthday more than “mine?”
	“Because he never told a lie! shouted a
little boy.
	This was rather hard on the teacher, but
the boy did not see it.


JUDGE POLAND'S Congressional Credit
Mobilier investigating committee, in its
report, laments the painful conflict of testi-
mony—which is a mild way of saying
perjury among prominent politicians. No 
wonder people are asked to admire the
lofty character of the modern gentleman 
who makes law a profession and polices a
trade.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿CORRESPONDENCE OF MISS SNOW

			Milan, Italy, Jan. 1st, 1873.
	Mrs. Jane S. Richards,
Dear Sister:—
	How very changefull are the events of
human life! One year ago, the idea of
addressing you from Italy at the commence-
ment of this year would have seemed almost
an impossibility. But here I am, a long
distance from what to me is the dearest
spot on earth—the home of the Saints of the
living God. The lapse of time, since part-
ing with you, and the many dear ones
associated with you in Ogden, has been a
constant routine of change combined with
deep interest. Our party consists of Presi-
dent George A. Smith, Elders Lorenzo
Snow, Paul A. Shettler, Feramorz Little,
George Dunford, Thomas Jennings, Miss
Clara Little and your humble servant. So
far as society is concerned, we are indepen-
dent wherever we are, and enjoy ourselves
as well as possible for people abroad, feeling
assured of the faith and prayers of the
righteous; and I assure you that I never for-
get a tribute of gratitude to those through
whose love, kindness and generous energies
the privilege of this tour is extended to me.
I have, so far, enjoyed it far beyond my
anticipations. Two months and one week,
and I am between seven and eight thousand
miles from home, having traveled through
England—visited the most prominent cities
in Holland and Belgium; and in France the
cities of Paris, Versailles, Lyons, Marseilles
and Nice. After leaving Nice we enter the
dominions of Victor Emanuel—our first
stopping place in Italy, according to our
programme, would be Genoa, but in conse-
quence of heavy rains having made a break
in the railroak, we stopped one night in
Rome, and went the next day to Genoa, the
birth-place of Christopher Columbus. From
Genoa we went to Turin and came thence to
this place.
	The national religion, we found since
leaving England to be Catholic. I had
never witnessed the service until in Brus-
sels. This being New Year’s Day, “High
Mass”' was performed in the grand “Cathe-
dral of Milan,” which we attended. The
Cathedral is a magnificent Gothic structure,
containing fourteen chapels; it is, by many,
considered the finest in the world. We
were informed that the service on the
occasion was of the highest order; certainly
much of it was sensless form and unmean-
ing, though dazzling display. I enjoyed the
singing and the sweet music of the two
organs, which played alternately. The
Archbishop, who represented the Pope in
all respects except that his hand instead of
his toe was kissed, was most gorgeously
dressed—bearing, at times, upon his head a
supurb mitre, and at other times, according
to the requirements of the service, it was
taken off by an officiate and held until again
required and then replaced. The burning
of incense was profuse. Great numbers,
variously attired, officiated on the occasion,
and the congregation was immense. The
services were performed in Latin and it is
presumable, as little comprehended by the
majority of the church members as by us.
I readily understood that many of the people
present were, like ourselves, spectators.
But when I looked upon the congregation and
saw some crossing themselves, some rever-
ently kneeling and others bowing, first to a
golden crucifix, then to the Archbishop, my
heart responded—How long, O Lord, shall
these, thy children be bound in the dwarf-
ing chains of traditional superstition and
ignorance? It is true the powers of earth
are shaking, but at present, l can see no hope
for millions of people under the training of
the Mother of harlots, and the influence of
Priestcarft, but through the ordinances for
the dead. Such were my reflections while
I remained a spectator in the midst of a
great multitude, in the stately and superb
Cathedral of Milan, which is described as
being four hundred and eighty-six feet in
length, and its total breadth three hundred
and fifty feet.
	As we approached Marseilles, the face of
the country, which had been level, assumed
a very uneven appearance, hills approximat-
ing to the dimensions of mountains; and for
the first time on our route, we saw groves
of olive trees, and occasionally oranges; and
when we reached the Mediterranean, they
became very common, ornamenting the
cities along the coast—the orange and lemon
trees being loaded with golden fruit; some
of them, very large, must have been planted
long ago.
	Our present location is inland from the
sea, and the olive, orange and lemon have
all disappeared, save as the orange is placed
on our dinner table every evening for desert.
On this side the Atlantic, I have not seen
apples that would compare with those of our
mountain home—most of those we have
seen are very ordinary.
							January 2nd.
	Yesterday was rainy—to-day being favor-
able, we ascended five hundred and twelve
marble steps, which brought us to the plat-
form of the great cupola of the Cathedral
above mentioned, where we had a most
delightful view of the whole city; and but
for a fog which encircled the distant horizon,
we might have seen the surrounding chains
of snow-capped mountains in the distance.
	To-morrow morning we leave for Venice.
	Yours with love.
							Eliza R. Szow.

						Venice, Italy,
							Jan. 5th, 1873.
Beloved Sister Horne:—
	We arrived here on the 3rd, the same
evening I received a note with your signa-
ture attached. In acknowledging the
receipt I would give expression to my feel-
ings were it in my power to do so, but I
leave you to imagine. 	*	*	*	*		
Venice is a very interesting city, both on
account of its history and its singular loca-
tion, Approaching it, for a considerable
distance, the railroad track is laid on artificial
ground, with water lying on each side.
When we arrived at the commodious depot,
gondolas were waiting to take us to “Grand
Hotel Victoria;” and with two gondolas for
our persons—four in each—and one for our
baggage, we landed on the threshold of our
hotel.
	This city is said to have been built on
seventy-two islands. Pedestrians can go into
all parts of the city, but gondolas instead of
horses and carriages are used, the people
having no other means for riding. Yester-
day afternoon, our whole party took a gon-
dola ride, and visited a factory for the
manufacture of glass, where they were
making artificial eyes for men and animals,
together with every fancy ornamental thing
imaginable. The most beautiful looking-
glass I ever saw, with a white glass frame
most delicately wrought, was prized at three
thousand five hundred francs; We were
told that three of the kind were in America,
purchased in Venice.
	Our intelligence from Salt Lake has not
been very profuse. The only paper received
from there is the Salt Lake “Herald,”
which comes occasionally and is a rich treat
to us all. Arrangments are made with brother
Carrington to forward all trans-Atlantic
mail matter from Liverpool to different
points as President Smith designates to him
from time to time. *	*	*	*	By the
“Herald” I learned the sad news of the
death of your lovely, precious daughter. I
can think of no one better prepared to go—
although so young she was ripened for a
higher state of existence. For her there is
no cause for mourning, but when I think of
her father and mother, her young, bereaved
husband, the loss to her brothers and sisters
and the loss to the community where her
noble example has had a purifying influence;
my heart overflows with sympathy and
sorrow. How you will miss her! But
trained as you have been in the Gospel of
Jesus, you know where to look for consola-
tion. God alone can breathe comfort to the
deeply stricken heart. When I think of the
bereaved ones—I feel to mourn, but when I
think of the pure, noble, lovely Julia, whose
earthly mission is so soon accepted, she
spared a longer period of toil, struggle and
suffering, my feelings are buoyant and my
imagination, if I may call it so, follows her
triumphant spirit to the happy abodes of
purity and eternal peace. May the daughters
of Zion imitate her worthy example? God
bless you my dear sister and fill your heart
with his comforting Spirit.
					   Eliza R. Snow.


﻿[For the Exponent. 
		MORAL TEMPERANCE.

	What is temperance? It is a question
often asked and answered by each person
according to his individual views. And 
there is a mental as well as a physical 
side to the question.
	Abstaining from intoxicating liquors, is 
one kind of temperance, but is not broad
enough to comprise the whole. There is
also a temperance in diet, a temperance in
labor, a temperance in speech, and a temper-
ance in thought. But there is one word
which will comprise its full meaning, and
that is—moderation.
	Moderation is the true law of life. "When
this just boundary is passed, then the action
or attribute degenerates into unworthiness;
as a lean traveling to a fixed point of the
compass, may pass the boundary of distance
and lessen instead of increasing the space
between himself and his starting point.
	Thus, “Eat but little” is a wise precept;
and, followed with due moderation, will 
give its disciple health, strong nerves, a clear
intellect and a cheerful, happy temper; and,
used thus is true temperance. But it is 
plain to any child, that carried to excess, it 
will weaken the parts even unto death. 
	Speech is a physical power, and how often
are we exhorted to use it temperately.
The person who uses mild, temperate 
language, is far more readily believed than 
one who raves.
	And in the exercise of the mind, if we are 
moderate, we are temperate. The pursuit 
of knowledge is most noble and praise- 
worthy; it develops our mental powers to
great strength and activity; but over-exert
them, and reason is hurled from her throne,
or the golden thread of life is snapped asunder.
	So it is with the moral nature. Cultivate 
any of the emotions prudently, and they
will be as hands and feet to us, or even as 
wings to bear us to realms of happiness; but
press them too warmly, or pursue them too 
eagerly and they wither in our grasp or 
elude us entirely, leaving behind miserable
caricatures to mock us.
	Thus, humility is a heavenly grace in
man or woman, but beyond a certain degree,
its abject and contemptible.
	Pride, that pride which disdains to stoop


to anything low and mean, is a most en-
obling virtue; but follow it beyond the verge
of moderation, and it degenerates into
haughtiness and even vanity.
	Love, that heavenly radiance which glori-
fies the meanest surroundings, lifts the
weakest of mortals to a level with the
angels, nerves the most timid to do and dare
where the bravest might shrink, and, like
the "little leaven which leavens the whole,”
purifies and sanctifies hearts which nothing
else could reclaim, is a name sorely misap-
plied. Alas, that it should be so! but many,
by giving unrestrained liberty to all the
emotions which it awakens in their peculiar
natures, and pursuing the object with head-
long speed, miss it altogether and grasp,
instead—passion; that adder to sting the
heart that warms it.
	Hate when kept within due bounds and
directed against sin, but not sinners, may be
a faithful pilot to warn us of moral rocks and
shoals; but once give it command of the
bark of life, and prejudice, obstinacy and
revenge are soon placed in high positions,
and the wages they award are misery and
ruin.
	We are even told that "Fobearance ceases
to be a virtue” at times.
	And ambition, when directed in proper
channels and pointed to a noble aim, sustains
the human heart through many and bitter
reverses, strengthens hope and patience,
rouses the latent energies of the soul and
nerves it to brave battle with difficulty; but
it must be subjected to the rules of temper-
ance or it will degenerate into a base greed
of power or notoriety, and, like a consuming
flame, devour every gentle impulse, kind-
ness, mercy, love and even justice; grasping
all advantages without remorse or shame.
	Without the aid of temperance, the soft
and gentle attributes of the soul would lead
us to driveling imbecility; and the strong,
aspiring sentiments to recklessness, ruin,
torment.
	How urgent, then, the necessity for culti-
vating temperance in all things, physical,
mental and moral; that just discrimina-
tion between right and wrong, which shall
lead us onward and upward, temperately,
moderately, steadily, until we conquer all
weaknesses of the flesh and mortality and
so win the fadeless crown of immortality
which is—pefection.
							L. L. D.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

		WHY WASH MONDAY?

	"THERE'S ne’er a bit of comfort took
	Upon the washing day"
Has been verified in thousands of homes
where steam, suds, confusion, a “picked up”
dinner and cross looks, if not words, are
always the accompaniements of that dreaded
Monday. But why do you select Monday as
the washing day? If you have no better
reason than because your grandmother
always did, it would be about as sensible for
your husband to go to mill with the grist in
one end of the bag and a stone in the other, to
balance, because that was his grandfather’s
way. If you keep the Sabbath and do no
work on that day that can be dispensed with,
you will find more to do on Monday than
any other day of the week. Then the
Saturday’s baking has vanished and needs
to be renewed, and there are always little
things to be done preparatory to washing
—garments to be finished or mended and
clothes to be put to soak.
	Tuesday is a much more covenient day for
washing, and the work will be rendered
lighter by the preparations of the previous
day--preparations which ought not to be made
on the Sabbath. Then don’t exert yourself
too much for the sake of getting the washing
done by some particular hour. I know
whereof I speak; for haven’t I arisen while
it was yet night, just late enough to escape
the sin of the good deacon’s wife, who
washed on Sunday, and accomplished the
washing before breakfast; sustained through
the unaccustomed hours of labor, and for the
loss of sleep, by the thought that my long
line of snowy clothes would excite the envy
and admiration of my neighbors when they
first looked out in the morning. Then be-
cause the clothes were dried, spinkled, and
folded in the morning, I have done the
ironing in the afternoon, and have finished
that Monday’s performances by going to bed
and staying there the rest of the week sick.
	If you cannot get the washing done in
time to have the clothes dried the same day,
leave them in the rinsing-water till the
next; or if your washings are very large,
take two days for it,—anything rather than
have the washing-day so filled with hard
work and confusion for which the clean
clothes it produces are no compensation, nor
to the tired-out house-wife for the severe
labors of that ever to-be-dreaded day.—
Moore’s “Rural New Yorker.”
	ARSENICAL POISONING.—Persons inhabit-
ing rooms, the walls of which are covered
with paper colored with Schele’s green, or
in which are kept objects of natural history
preserved with arsenical preparations, are
alike liable to suffer from poisoning. Arsen-
ious gases are developed by the reaction of
arsenious acid upon the organic compound
with which it comes in contact, and the air
and dust of the room become dangerously
charged with poisonous matter.
	A CHEAP DISINFECTANT.—The following
is highly commended by those who have
used it:—Dissolve a bushel of salt in a barrel
of water, and with the salt water slack a
barrel of lime, which should be wet enough
to form a kind of paste. For the purpose of
a disinfectant, this home-made chloride of
lime is nearly as good as that purchased at
the shops and drug stores.    Use it freely
about sinks, cellars, gutters, and outhouses,
and in this way prevent sickness, suffering,
and expense.
	TO COLOR SHEEP SKINS.—Unslacked
lime and litharge equal parts, mixed to a
thin paste with water, will color buff—
several coats will make it a dark brown; by
adding a little ammonia and nitrate of silver
a fine black is produced. Terra japonica
will impart a “tan color” to wool, and the red
shade is deepened by sponging with a solu-
tion of lime and water, using a strong
solution of alum water to “set” the colors; 1
part crystallized nitrate silver, 8 parts car-
bonate ammonia, and 1 1/2 parts of soft water
dyes brown; every additional coat darkens
the color until a black is obtained.
	LEMON TWISTS.—Take a pound of flour,
half a pound of butter and twelve ounces of
loaf sugar, with enough essence of lemon to
flavor and a teaspoonfull sal volatile; pour
in beaten eggs and sufficient milk to moisten
the paste; roll it out, cut it into strips, plait
them together in threes, brush the outside
with milk, strew beaten sugar upon the tops
and bake the twists upon buttered tins about
twenty minutes. Grated lemon peel and
juice may be substituted for the essence.

﻿	FACTS AND FIGURES.

	A GRAND central depot is to be built in
Boston.
	St. Louis erected 1,500 new buildings
last year.
	THE Maine Agricultural College has 71
students.
	THE new chapel for Yale College is to cost
$100,000.
	BRAZIL is the best customer for New-
foundland codfish.
	THE ice crop on the Hudson will reach
1,500,000 tons this season.
	SENATOR Patterson has been offered the
Presidency of the Ohio Agricultural College.
	SAN Francisco has made 7,000 kangaroo
hides into fine boots.
	“ZION'S Herald,” of Boston, has celebrated
its fiftieth birthday.
	THE present is the worst hunting season
England has known for years.
	SOME 500 horses have been clipped by
machinery in Boston this Winter.
	THE number of emigrants who left Liver-
pool for America in 1872 was 164,000.
	MR. Poole, the famous London tailor, is
about to retire from business with £500,000.
	MME. de Perinot has left 20,000 francs to
the French Academy to found a prize for
astronomical researches.
	INDIANA farmers band together and run
saur kraut factories on the same principle
that cheese factories are conducted.
	OVER thirty brave and sensible girls in the
high school of Portland have resolved to
discard overskirts, laces, jewelry, and other
expensive articles of dress while attending
school.
	CONNECTED with Mr. Spurgeon’s Sunday 
school, in London, is a Bible class, taught by
Mrs. Lavinia Harkness, which enrolls nine 
hundred women, and has an average attend-
ance of over five hundred.
	MRS. General Sanderson, of Washington,
Ohio, owns a box, said to have been brought
to America by Cortez, when he came to con-
quer Mexico. It Is inlaid with ivory, and
its ago is thought to be about four hun-
dred years.
	Fisk University, the colored educational
institution at Nashville, Tenn., has profited
so largely by its band of “Jubilee singers,”
who have been traveling through the North,
that it will erect a fine college building from
the proceeds.
	A GERMAN Countess, who is an earnest
Catholic, has made a curious vow, to present
to the Pope every year a purse of gold coin
and a baptized Jew. The first of these
offerings was made a short time since, the
Jew being made a member of the church at
Basilica, with imposing ceremonies.
	SOUTH African papers tell of a farm of
240,000	acres, on which is a flock of 10,000
sheep—besides 10,000 Angora goats—which
are said to be far more profitable there than
sheep, and there is a promise of a rapid
extension of these flocks. The Angora goat
wool, as produced there, is much finer than
that of the sheep.
	FRANCE not only lost provinces, armies,
and an immense sum of money through the
late war with North Germany, but by the
session of Metz, she lost a military library,
of which there is not the equal left in the
Republic. This celebrated institution con-
tained 40,000 printed volumns, many of
which were extremely rare, besides numer-
ous manuscripts and drawings, many of
which were of a highly precious character.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿THE SEX.

	GENERAL Schenck’s youngest daughter is
to marry an English nobleman.
	M. M. POMEROY’S first wife offers the La
Crosse Opera House for sale.
	TWO American female physicians, both
from Chicago, are practising in Edinburgh.
	MADAME Hyacinthe is “at home” Satur-
day evenings at her residence in Brussels.
	QUEEN Victoria’s signature is elegantly
formed, with each letter beautifully cut.
	MR. Greeley’s daughters are spending the.
winter in New York, with their aunt, Mrs.
Cleavland.
	MISS Lydia A. Whitney has purchased
one-half of the "Presque Isle (Me.) Sun-
rise."
	THE mother of Rubinstein, the pianist, is
a celebrated music-teacher in Moscow, and
said to be very wealthy.
	MISS Hosmer is said to have sufficient
orders to keep her busy with chisel and
mallet for the next five or six years.
	SALLIE Ada Vance has become editor of
the Lexington, Mississippi “Advertiser.”
Her Salutatory is said to be well written.
	THE Crown Princess of Germany is mak-
ing a design for a monument, to be erected
on the field of Weissenburg by the officers of
he third army.
	A FEMALE pharmaceutist, Miss S. M
Harding, has been appointed Dispenser to
the Hospital for Women, at Birmingham,
England.
	CELIA Burleigh says that one of the most
singular things in woman’s nature is the
naturalness with which they gravitate to-
wards “cussedness” in the opposite sex.
	A KEROSENE lamp exploded at Rush
River, Wisconsin, recently, a piece of the
glass striking a young lady on the throat,
cutting it deeply, and causing speedy death.
	A MAN at Janesville, Minnesota, was re-
cently beaten by his wife. The woman was
arrested and fined fifty dollars for the assault,
which the husband was obliged to pay.
	A MODERN writer says:	“In Germany,
woman has a sad life, she seems to be of a
lower order than man. It is nothing but
work, work, work, for her, while the men
are visiting Beer Gardens.”
	MISS Lena Clark, daughter of a banker in
Newton, Iowa, walked seven miles from
the Junction to Jefferson, when the therm-
ometer marked thirty degrees below zero,
because the train was belated. She holds
the position of cashier in the bank.
	THE Swiss “Times” reports that Mont
Blanc has been successfully ascended by
three English young ladies, named Murray,
aged respectively twenty-one, seventeen and
fourteen. The youngest of the party a girl
of twelve, had to relinquish the attempt.
	MISS Lizzie Duffer, Miss Alice Hagey and
Miss Kate January, at the Murfreesboro,
Tenn., Agricultural Fair contended for pre-
eminence In the high art of cookery, the
prize being a fine cooking stove. Each
young woman came at the appointed time,
provided with provisions of her own selec-
tion, and in turn took charge of the cook
room. Miss January was the winner.
	LASt summer a party of girls were
directed by the driver of a street car in
Boston to get on by the front platform,
although getting on in that way is contrary
to the rules of the company. One of the
girls was thrown under the wheels and
severely injured. She brought suit against
the company and recovered $5,000 damages,
a verdict since confirmed by the Supreme
Court.

			WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

			A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL.
	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.
	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.
	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"
	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.
	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.
	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.
	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal, 
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from 
her numerous duties.
	WOMAN'S EXPONENT will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the 
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.
	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
  1 Copy one year,	    $2.00	    1 Copy six months.  $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00 	  10 Copies six months, 9.00
	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.
	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers WOMAN'S EXPONENT, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


Woman's Exponent.

VOL I. 	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MARCH 15, 1873. ﻿   No. 20.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	MISS R. L. HOYT carries off the prize
awarded for excellent drawing in the Lon-
don Kensington Art-School; so the ladies
may "hurrah for our side" again.
	MISS KATIE Barton, of Boston, has in-
vented an improvement on sewing machines,
for the marking of sails, tents, awnings, etc.;
she should be acknowledged a representative
of women.
	REPUBLICAN Massachusetts refuses wo-
man the right to vote, by direct action of
the State Legislature. What about the
woman suffrage plank in the State Repub-
lican platform? 
	A YOUNG lady in Maine who had taken
no interest in the cause of woman suffrage,
has been converted to it by hearing it abused
by a young unmarried minister. Preachers
should be cautious how they arouse femi-
nine combativeness.
	SEVEN thousand women of St. Louis
petition the legislature of Missouri to repeal
the law licensing the social iniquity in St.
Louis. It would be better to compel the
men, under whose patronage the vice
flourishes, to take out licenses as well as the
women, who are often their victims.
	ONE CENT a day is to be contributed by
each lady member of the Congregational
churches in Iowa, for five years, with the
view of an endowment fund of $20,000 for
the female department of the State College.
The object is a laudable one, and worthy of
encouragement and success. 
	THE COMMITTEE to investigate charges
against U. S. Senator Patterson unanimously
reported a resolution that he be expelled
the Senate for corruption and perjury.
Grave as are the charges, thus unanimously
endorsed, the Senate has not yet expelled
Patterson. But it passed the Frelinghuysen
bill.
	EX-VICE PRESIDENT Colfax has delivered
several speeches lately to show how inno-
cent he was of the charges made against him
in regard to the Credit Mobilier business;
and he has succeeded in convincing a very
large number of people that he was guilty
as charged, while his friends, cling all the
closer to him. Perverse human nature!
	“AN OBJECT of interest” is what more
than one worthy (?) Utah office, provision
and raiment-seeker has been determined to
become, ere the offices should be all supplied
or Congress should adjourn; and ought they
not now to be satisfied? For this they have
nearly all succeeded in making of them-
selves-"simply this, and nothing more!”
	ON THE 26th of February a Convention
was held in New York for the purpose of
securing a Constitutional amendment,
acknowledging the existence of God in that
instrument. No better amendment could
be made than the adopting of the original
Constitution of the United States, which
has been utterly disregarded by many of
the descendants of its framers.
	IN A late discussion in Congress, on in-
creasing the pay of the members, General
Banks said, arguing in favor of increased
pay because of the increased price of living,
that it was Edward Everett's advice to him
"never to be separated from his family."
This or anything that would have a tendency
to reduce immorality among national legis-
lators and at the federal capital, would be a
public benefit.
	THE REV. J. D. Fulton regards polygamy
as far more scriptural than woman suffrage;
and the agitation of the latter, he alleges,
causes a great decrease in the number of
boy babies. This is a serious matter; for if
the theory be correct, the agitation will
have to be stopped by granting the right of
suffrage to the women or polygamy may
become compulsory in another generation
or so, to prevent the race from dying out.
	AMONG THE late applications for office we
note that of Mrs. Anne Savery, of Des
Monies, Iowa, who is a candidate for
United States Consul at Havre. How this
practical exhibition of woman’s rights would
take in France, should her application be
successful, it is difficult to say; but were
she to seek and obtain the same appoint-
ment for Kiel, or any other German town,
it certainly would not be relished by the
authorities and officials of "Fatherland."
	THE FOLLOWING extract from the Wash-
ington "Star" is of more than usual interest:
"Woman's rights have at length been
recognized in high quarters in England. A
department of the civil service, known as
the local govermment board, has just ap-
pointed a lady poor-law inspector at the
very handsome salary of $2,000 a year to
report upon the domiciles and the treatment
of children who are "boarded out," after
the system which is generally adopted in
Scotland. Mrs. Tassau Senior is the lady
upon whom this distinction has been con-
ferred.” 
	AN EASTERN paper says: "A bill has
been favorably reported to the New York
assembly to incorporate the Young Ladies'
Christian Association of the city of New
York. The object of this corporation is
stated to be the improvement of the
temperance, social, mental, moral and
religious condition in New York. The fifth
article forbids these ladies to manage their
property except with the consent of an advis-
ory committee, named in the charter, and
composed of ten men. The same section
prohibits the associated young ladies from
indulging in wine, whisky, ale or lager
beer, and from gambling in the rooms and
on the premises of the association."
	PRESIDENT GRANT was inaugurated with
great festivities on the 4th inst., the com-
mittee on inauguration having refused to
accede to the petition from certain parties
in the State of New York, urging that a
grand inauguration prayer meeting be held
instead of a ball. The idea of commingling
prayer and dancing is evidently not a
familiar one with the petitioners, any more
than with the inauguration committee.
When one reads the account of the festivi-
ties, the magnificence and the display of
wealth, the mind naturally wanders back to
the days of republican simplicity, and the
thought comes questioning where that
simplicity has gone, and whether such
splendors are not more fitting for an im-
perial court than for "a government of the
people, by the people, and for the people,"
which ours is supposed to be.
	TO HEAR some men talk it would seem
that the chief end of existence was to enjoy
the appetites, and that the greatest charms
of life were whisky and tobacco. On this
theory, the more disgusting and filthy they 
make themselves the happier they are; and
their course would indicate that they be-
lieved in the theory. Is it any wonder that
pure women would cling to a pure man who
shunned and despised such vile and degrad-
ing habits?
	THE SEASON for tourists and travelers will
soon commence, and clergymen will be
relieved for a time from their "arduous
labors" of preaching a couple of times in the
week, and furnished with money to take a
little rest and recreation. It is strange that
this class of men should be so troubled
with bronchitis; strange how women who
have to use the larynx and bronchial tubes
more, are not so easily affected; strange how
"Mormon" Elders, and editors, who work
infinitely harder, have so little solicitation
bestowed upon their health; and strange,
too, how there should be such great neces-
sity that clergymen should travel so much
for their personal benefit—at half rates on
the railroad. Oh, things are wonderfully
changed since the ancient Apostles of the
Savior fished all night for a living;
hawked their fish to market, themselves
barefooted; and then preached in the even-
ing! Who ever heard of Peter, James and
John; or of Brigham, Orson or Wilford in
these latter-days, being sent out on a tour
by steamship and cabin, for the benefit of
their health, and to seek relief from a
bronchial affection?
	THREE YOUNG ladies were lately mur-
dered, two in New Hampshire and one in
Maryland, under most revolting circum-
stances and in a  horrible manner. The
Maryland murderer subsequently threw
himself under a passing freight-train and
was crushed to death. The New Hampshire
one is now in prison awaiting his trial.
Such reports are almost of daily occurrence,
from large cities and country places; and
show that with all the progress made in art,
science and education, crime keeps pace
with the boasted advance of the age, if it
does not lead it. Were the self-proclaimed
philanthropists who are so anxious to con-
vert the people of Utah to a belief in the
necessity of vice, corruption, outrage and
bloodshed, to exert their influence towards
repressing crime nearer home they might
be of some service to society. And if they
wish us to pattern after the society in which
they are shining examples, it would be well
to present us with a pattern for which they
would not have just cause to hide their
faces in burning shame. But while un-
bridled passion, bloodthirstiness and brutal
desires are so rampant, we beg to be per-
mitted to work out our own social problem
in peace, and see if we cannot produce a
better condition of society than is to be
found anywhere else.


GREAT BRITAIN will send an artisan com-
mission of eighty members to Vienna.
	PROF. Tyndall will give all the money he
makes in this country to some scientific
institution.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
﻿


﻿ANSWER TO A VALENTINE.

			FROM J. R.

Dear Sir:—
	Your counsel wise and good,
Is kind, if never needed;
And being clearly understood
Shall be as strictly heeded.

I know 'twas said by Solomon,
“There is a time for all things;"
But 'tis not written, every one
Shall do b th great and small things:

And those who seek the world’s applause,
And love vain approbation;
Have not the spirit of the Cause
Of Truth, and true Salvation.

l know it were not wise to yield
To worldy, false ambition:
I'd labor in a richer field—
Fulfill a holier mission.	
							L. L.


F. R. Society Reports

		Mill Creek Ward 
			Salt Lake Co., Feb. 18, 1873.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—	
	At a meeting of the Relief Society of this
Ward, held in the Ward-house on the 11th
inst., the word “Female” was dropped from
the name of the Society by unanimous
vote. This Society is in every way, in a
healthy and prosperous condition. We hold
our  meetings monthly, which are well
attended. In a short time we shall have
ready a very suitable hall for meetings and
the business operations of the Society,
		Rhoda Ann Miller, President,

			Salem, Utah County,
				February 16th, 1873.
Editor Woman’s Exponent;—
	The Salem Relief Society was organized
January 7th, 1869, with Mary E. Reed,
President; Mrs. Lucinda Jackman and Mrs.
Sarah W. Curtis, Counselors; Matilda P.
Killian, Secretary and Esther M. Reed,
Treasurer. During the four years since our
organization, our means has been very
limited, yet, we have been able to meet
every demand upon our charity. We have
thirty dollars in the Co-operative store, and
a little sum, (seventeen dollars) which we
are enlarging every week, to aid in gather-
ing the poor; then we still have in the
treasury, besides the above, cash and
property to the amount of one hundred and
twenty-two dollars. We are prospering and
feel to thank God for the wisdom and
knowledge that has enabled us, thus far, to
fill the mission that has been given us as
honorably as we have done. The sisters are
united in their efforts to do good and help
to build up the Kingdom of God upon the
earth that they may receive the reward of
the faithful.
			M. P. Killian, Secretary.

		Newton, Cache Co., U. T.
			February 12th, 1873.
Editor Woman's Exponent:—
	At a meeting of our Relief Society, held
January 3rd, 1873, a motion to have the
word “Female” dropped from the name,
according to Sister Eliza R. Snow’s sugges-
tion, was carried by a unanimous vote.
	Although our Society is not so forward as
others, our desire is to continue to do all
the good we can until circumstances will
admit of our doing more. Our settlement
is new; we have labored under various disad-
vantages. The means accumulated, has
been used in relieving the needy, and feed-
ing the Indians. We hope to be able to do
more in the future than we have done in
the past.
	Your valuable paper is much appreciated
here, and it is the expressed desire of many
to subscribe for it as soon as circumstances
will permit.
		Isadora Beck, President.
	A. M. Young, Secretary.

	The General Retrenchment meeting at
the 14th Ward Assembly Rooms, in this
city, Saturday, March 1st, was very well
attended. The spirit of unity and the desire
to improve in knowledge of a useful char-
acter appear to increase with the sisters.
And from the many impressive and sanguine
testimonies borne, it might well be inferred
that the doings of Congress at that critical
time, or other worldly missiles which might
be aimed to overthrow the Kingdom of the
great God, had but little weight on the
minds of the faithful daughters of Zion.

	Third Ward Relief Society,
		Provo City-, March 9th, 1873,
Dear Exponent:—
	At a meeting in November, our Society
voted unanimously to drop the word "Fe-
male,” and a report was at once forwarded,
together with money for four numbers of
the Exponent; which, owing to some mis-
take, carelessness or dishonesty in some
quarter, you did not receive,
	Our Society is doing well; our ward is
small and not well off, but we have no in-
digent poor. We give assistance occasionally,
but have no steady pensioners. We feel an
interest in the welfare of our excellent paper,
and in the rolling forth of the work of God.
	M. J. Tanner, President.

			WOMAN'S VOICE
Editor Exponent:—	
	Dear Sister:—I think it the duty of
mothers, who have been long in the Church,
to write for the instruction of their
daughters to give counsel to the young,
and try to impress them with the love of
chastity and goodness; to teach them not
to follow the vain fashions of the world, but
to seek to be prudent and temperate in all
their ways; not to covet the empty pleasures
and treasures of earth alone, but to secure
to themselves peace and joy everlasting—
the riches of eternity.
	We have examples among the mothers
in Israel worthy of imitation, and which, if
we follow, we shall not be led astray. I feel
that mothers should be awake to their duties
in training their children in the love of
truth and the ways of rectitude; and I pray
that the daughters of Zion may have wisdom
to so direct their offsprings that they may
have power over all evil and overcome the
ways of darkness.
				Sarah Roberts;
	Goshen.

				Springville,
					Feb. 20th, 1873.
	In looking over the Exponent my mind
has been led through many scenes, some of
joy and some of sorrow; and I find it con-
tains sympathy for every heart. It is a
heavenly work, when we can do so, to bind
up the broken heart. If we are rich in
knowledge, and God has blest us with the
light of His Holy Spirit to do good, it is
joy to us to seek out the depressed, the poor,
the widow and orphan and impart to them,
both of our heart’s sunshine and of our sub-
stance. The Gospel we have received
teaches us to be full of charity to mankind,
and let our love for each other flow as a
fountain of living waters. I can truly say
since I have been a member of the Relief
Society, it has been a school of improvement
to me, for which I thank God. True happi-
ness is offered to every faithful son and
daughter. If we live as peace-makers we
are filling our missions, if not, we live below
our calling and will be accountable for our
folly. If mothers will live in the light of
the Gospel they will have influence with
and great blessings for their children.
This the Lord requires at their hands. We
have the same fountain of light and intelli-
gence from which to gain a fullness of
the blessings of life, that has been enjoyed
by the Saints in every age, when God has
had a prophet to reveal His mind and will
to the children of men on earth. With an
experience of 31 years in the Church, I can
testify that the Gospel made known to
brother Joseph Smith, by the Angel that
John saw was to come forth in the last days,
is true; and the powers of darkness can not
stay the progress of the work of our God.
This is something to live for. It keeps the
hopes and affections bright and lends addi-
tional charms to all in nature that is amiable
and lovely. And all who labor in this
glorious cause, have their reward.
				Hannah Harrison.

		Cottonwood, Feb. 28, 1873.
Editor Exponent:—
	I have read your paper with pleasure and
interest; and now, have a desire to mingle
my testimony with those of my sisters who
testify to the sacred truth of the Gospel-
work in which we are engaged. To-day I
know this is the Church of Christ; that
plural marriage, as well as the other prin-
ciples which it contains, was ordained of
God, for the blessing and exaltation of the
Saints. And I believe it to be the duty of
every Latter-day Saint to teach and practice
that truth as well as the rest. And that
every mother should teach her children, as 
well by example as precept, to seek out
and understand for themselves, correct
principles. The Lord has given us a
glorious light, and except we follow it
steadily and prayerfully, we may expect to
be soon left in the dark, and that we will
become more blind than we were ere the
light beamed upon us.
	Daughters of  Zion, let us be mindful.
						Theresa.

		Ogden City, Feb. 3rd, 1873.
Dear Editor:—
	Having been a subscriber to your excel-
lent paper since its commencement, and
seeing the wide scope you have provided
the "Mormon" ladies, for expressing their
sentiments and portraying their position, I
venture to address you a few words. In
the first place, I feel thankful that the time
has come when the women of this Church
can publish their views, and that too, through
a woman's Journal. And I hope they all
feel as I do, that they would not be without
the paper.
	Now, for myself. For fifteen years I lived
in monogamy—bound to a "drunkard," who
used to come home and curse and swear,
and with whom my life was in constant
jeopardy. I cannot tell what I endured,
but I thank my Heavenly Father for my
deliverance, for my happy home in these
valleys, and for my good, faithful husband
in the Kingdom of God, who is a "polygam-
ist," honorable and worthy, and who has
not tasted of any kind of strong drink for
twenty-five years. This I consider, speaks
volumes. I am now living in "polygamy"
of my own free will and choice.
	It is woman's mission to do good—to per-
form, each one her faithful work, which
will bring its own reward. Any woman


can do good every day of her life if she
chooses. To her it is given to create sun-
shine in her home, however lowly it may
be; to comfort those that mourn, and to
bless with cheerful smiles and words of
encouragement all with whom she is associ-
ated.
	I am glad to read of the success of the
Relief Societes in different parts of the Ter-
ritory, and think the EXPONENT a splendid
means of making the sisters in the Societies
acquainted with each other.		
	I will conclude with kind wishes and
earnest prayers for all who engage in the
great work—the Emancipation of Woman
and the establishment of Truth.
							E. McKay.

		MILL CREEK WARD,
			Salt Lake City, Feb. 18, 1873.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	I do not purpose to say much. It is not
my calling to write for a public journal, yet
I desire to say a few words in an humble
way. 
	I am well advanced in years; am the
mother of ten children, and have had an ex-
perience in Celestial or Patriarchal marri-
age for the last twenty-five years, an experi-
ence that many of my sisters have not had.
	It is then of this great and eternal princi-
ple of truth that I wish to bear testimony.
	The rulers of our nation are moving "en
masse to abolish Plural or Patriarchal Mar-
riage, by enactments of stringent and severe
laws, which are not founded in justice,
truth or mercy, but are at war with our
sacred Republican institutions, and with the
only true order of marriage known in the
law of God, or that has ever been revealed
to man by the revelations of Heaven.
	When you take away, in the resurrection,
or in the life which is to come (in the great
plan of man’s redemption,) our sacred marri-
age ties, our family relations, “the Patri-
archal order, that God has ordained,” what
is left? What kind of a heaven is there for
us to inherit? Certainly none worth living
for; but the pious rulers of our nation, and
such “divines” as Parson Newman are
welcome to inherit such—the Latter-day
Saints do not want it.
	The first marriage celebrated on the earth
was for time and for all eternity. When
Adam took his wife he did not know of any
death. There was no “man-made” sectarian
ceremony about this union; no simple
“you take this woman to be your wife
until seperated by death,” No! the bond
reached from earth to the eternities and the
union was as eternal as God himself.
	Through the great atonement of Jesus
Christ, and the keys of authority in the
Everlasting Priesthood that Peter held, will
evreything be restored to man that was lost
in the fall. And Father Adam will have
his wife, or wives. And the faithful Latter-
day Saints, in the resurrection, by the same
Authority, that "binds on earth and it is
bound in heaven," "will have every wife
and child" that is faithful and adheres to 
the laws of God in the days of their proba-
tion. And the rulers of the nations cannot
prevent it. Their power may be felt by the
Church, and by individuals. This has been
the case in other ages of the world's history,
yet the eternal principles and purposes of
God remain the same. Man cannot abolish
what God has ordained. 
					RHODA ANN MILLER.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WORDS of cheerful encouragement often
do more good than costly presents.
	LORENZA Silva, a Mexican lady, died
with the weight of 150 years, less eleven
days, on her head.


(For the EXPONENT,
HONEST HEARTS vs. POLlSHED MAN-
				NERS.

	“Give me the honest heart and the rough
bark;” says Bro. Gruff, and—“Mark my
words, that fellow is too polite to be honest.”
	Wherefore, brother? Is not politeness
truly a christian grace? and is it absolutely
necessary to sacrifice honesty in order to
achieve politeness? If so, by all means
choose honor as the greater virtue; but I
doubt if a person can be truly and thoroughly
honest without being polite. As I under-
stand the term, “politeness” signifies “con-
siderate kindness of manners.”
	Is there anything wrong in being consid-
erate of another’s rights, feelings, or even
whims? or rather, does not the absence of
such consideration betoken dishonesty?
	Because it is pleasant and graceful, is it
any the less honest to say “I beg pardon,”
after slightly injuring another’s rights or
feelings? And are not smiling “Thank
you’s” for little favors received, as much
more honest as more charming than grim
silence? If our sons stand in easy, graceful
attitudes, are capable of executing captivat-
ing bows, are never culpable of wearing hats
nor whistling indoors, and never talk slang,
is it any shadow of an argument that their
morals are not good? If so, I am too blind
to perceive it.
	“But” argues Bro. Gruff, “a polished man-
ner is so good a cloak for rogues to hide
their wickedness under.” Now that would
imply that rogues are generally polished;
but I venture the assertion, that for one
polished rascal you will find ten rude ones;
but, admitting that it is not so, why con-
demn a virtue in order to reach a vice?
Discriminate, and condemn insincerity, in-
stead of the virtue counterfeited.
	There never was a counterfeit without
a true coin to copy from, and if rogues try
to hide their villainy behind a pleasing
address, that of itself is a proof of its worth;
for no one would take the trouble to copy
anything so intrinsically worthless as to be
inadequate to their aims. Besides, there
is no counterfeit so clever as to defy all
tests, and if polished rogues, by their fair
exterior lead any astray, it is through the
victims' vanity, greed, envy or other weak-
ness, and herein is an all-sufficient test; If
any one addresses himself to such feelings
he is thus far dishonest and should be
shunned; but so long as his urbanities appeal
to our nobler and better sentiments, there
is no harm in him, let his secret principles
be what they may. But the moment his
fingers strike a key, which does not cord
with honor, beware!
	One wiser than we, said, “Be courteous,”
and to do so, does not require us to be in-
sincere or crafty. Honesty and polished
manuers are not antagonistic; they are
individual flowers in the ambrosial bouquet
of graces; and because one may be fairer
or sweeter than another, is no reason for
discarding the other. Beloved youth, fear
not to cultivate a pleasant addess, for, thus
draped, your pure integrity will be doubly
alluring and effective. ’Tis but a choice
in habits, after all, and since we cannot
avoid forming habits of some kind, pure
gratitude for the choice, should lead you
to choose those which are at once the more
pleasant and the more profitable.
	All things are to be judged by that which
they effect; and civility and grace never
yet injured their possessor, and when not
linked with dishonesty, they just as richly
benefit those who come in contact with
them. A favor asked in a courteous manner
is far more readily granted than one which
savors of demand; and one granted gra-
ciously, gives far more happiness to the
recipient.
	Well bred ways, will secure for you respect
and favor, when your friend of churlish
manners would be severely slighted. 
	Politeness shall be the pure gold setting
for your priceless pearl of honesty.
								L. L. D.


﻿HOUSEHOLD HINTS. 

	CARROT SOUP.—Two pounds of carrots,
three ounces of butter, seasoning to taste of
salt and cayenne, two quarts of stock or
gravy soup.
	STEAMED BREAD AND BUTTER PUDDING.
—Lay the bread and butter in a basin with
apricot and other jam between; add a custard
as for a baked pudding, and steam it.
	ALDERLY PUDDING.—Two tablespoon-
fuls of ground rice, the same of arrowroot,
the yolks of two eggs, a little grated lemon-
peel, sugar to taste, and one pint of milk
mixed together. Lay a little orange-marma-
lade or other preserve at the bottom, of a
dish, pour over it this mixture, and bake.
	GINGER PUDDING.—One cup not quite full
of suet, two cups of bread-crumbs, two
teaspoonfuls of powdered ginger; mix with
warm molasses, butter a mould or basin,
put in the pudding, and bake; or, if pre-
ferred, steam it in place of baking.
	HOME is the realm in which the wife is
the sovereign, and her sovereignty, will
bring her honor and love according as her
rule over the matters committed to her
trust is wisely and beneficently ordered.
That realm, limited as it is, demands for its
government a much greater and a more
unremitting effort of thought than is always
given to it; for when a wife, intent on mak-
ing the home of her family all that it is  
possible to make it, studies daily how best
by skillful management she can gratify
preferences, please tastes, anticipate wishes, 
keep out of sight little worries, twine pri- 
vations with decorations of love, and lift off
for a while, burdens of care, all of which
appeal to her for thoughtful and graceful
intervention, she seldom fails of securing
her reward which she most dearly prizes.
	A PERSON who has suffered from, or is
subject to, quinsy, should, after an attack
has passed off, use every means of strength-
ening the throat; by this the attacks may
be greatly prevented. Any of the astringent
gargles may be used after quinsy, but none
perhaps answers better, or is more accessible
to the poor, than the decoction of oak bark.
This ought to be used night and morning
for some weeks, and when its use is drop-
ped, the person should acquire the habit of
gargling the throat with cold water at the
above times, “as a regular practice,” and
at the same time should, habitually, lave 
freely with cold water, or salt water at first,
at least, all around the throat. In this way
a tendency to quinsy may be greatly over-
come. Further, all unnecessary muffling,
either by fur or otherwise, about the neck,
should he avoided, for there is no more 
fertile source of sore-throat. Doubtless
many cases of simple soar-throat may be
managed domestically; but the contingen-
cies of crysipelatous or croupy sore-throat, 
or of quinsy, which may suffocate, must
not be lost sight of.

	SCOTT should be read in the Spring,
Shelly in the fervid Summer, Wordsworth
in Autumn, Cowper and Byron in Winter,
and Shakespeare all the year around.
