﻿SPLINTERS.

	In the forests of Maine, in the latter part
of February, snow was four feet deep......
The manner in which Poppleton drew tears
out of the eyes of Oaks Aames, is pro-
nounced “beautiful (!)”......There are 1,000
alligators in a hole on James’ Island, near
Charleston, South Carolina......Philadelphia
has contributed a block of marble for the
Washington monument........The standard of
admission to Yale College is to be raised,
doubtless in view of woman’s rights being
universally acknowledged......The Empress
Eugenie is, for her age, young looking;
wears a sweet, pensive expression, and is
beloved by all who have had a chance of
becoming attached to her......The remains
of 117 steam-boats lie on the bottom of the
Arkansas river......Eighteen Cadets, at West
Point, failed to pass and were discharged......
The exhibition building at Vienna was
reported completed in February......Rhode
Island has 3,000 militia......The inaugural
ball given in Washington, on the 5th inst.,
was the grandest ever given in this country.
......Eastport, Maine, has had an earth-
quake......Iron ore is found in twenty-eight
counties, inTennessee...Michigan City claims
to be the second lumber-point on the Lakes.
A boy in Mound City, Kansas, was lately
chased into the river and killed by a cata-
mount......A fever is raging in southwest
Missouri—the “mining fever.”...........The
horse-disease has attacked the cows in Sny-
der county, Pennsylvania......An elegant
boulevard is contemplated at Concord, New
Hampshire......Cold weather in Louisiana
has been destructive to bees in more than
ordinary style this season......Boston fired a
hundred guns on inauguration-day......San
Jose, California, boasts that in its balmy
climate, tomatoes and cats flourish in the
open air all winter.......A Minnesota judge
decides that shaving on Sunday is illegal.
.......A youth in the same State tried to start
his horse by hitting it with his gun which
went off, carrying three of his fingers with
it......Prince Gortschakoff, it is reported,
will settle the boundaries of Afghanistan by
a special Anglo-Russian committee......A
royal decree has been issued in Rome, by
which the Italian Government takes formal
possession of the convents in that city......
Bass, an English beer-brewer, is to be made
a baronet.......Prince Albert of Russia is to
be married in April.......The Russian Czar-
owitz accompanies his mother to Italy for
the benefit of his health......Returns of the
London Board show, that for the year 1872
the foreign trade of Great Britain reached
the enormous total of $3,046,690,000......The
new President of Mexico, Lerdo de Tejada,
is 45 years old, noted for eloquence as an
orator, possessed of great energy and cour-
age, and a lawyer......A new combustible,
said to give more heat and be cheaper than
coal, has lately been discovered in Italy......
It is reported that the Italian Government
has issued imperative instructions to the
Prefect of Turin to impede all emigration to
this country, and to permit only those
Italians to leave who have documentary
assurances of their employment when they
arrive here......A school-teacher in Minne-
sota procured food and bed-clothing from a
neighbor, and kept his entire school in-
doors, refusing any one permission to
attempt to go home during a late, long and
fearful storm......Pope Pius IX  has wit-
nessed the death of 100 Cardinals during his
pontificate of a quarter of a century......Miss
Emerson, of New York, claims the author-
ship of “Betsy and I are out.”......A ninety-
mile walk on snow-shoes was recently
performed by a woman in Michigan......Two
English sisters, in Berlin, have just put up
their sign as dentists......Listz, the music
professor, pronounces a young lady his most
promising pupil. It is Miss Anna Finken-
staedt, who has lately entered the concert-
field......Report says an attempt is being made
to reduce Queen Victoria to poverty by
cutting down her income to the sum paid
the President of the United States......
Ohio has three million acres more of culti-
vated land than Pennsylvania......Congress
prohibits gift photographic establishments
in Washington......Great damage has been
done by recent floods on the Delaware river.
......Montana is considered the best watered
region west of the Mississippi river......A
man in Cleveland has sued that city for
$5,000 for catching the small-pox there......
George Eliot made $40,000 by her "Middle-
march.”......Thirty-nine medals struck in
honor of Benjamin Franklin are owned by a
Bostonian......Miss Emily Faithful has had
large and attentive audiences in Washing-
ton......Mrs. Kate N. Doggett, of Chicago, has
lately delivered a series of Art-lectures
before the Christian Union Association.
......Delaware is to have a Constitutional
Convention on the 20th of May......The facts
and theories of Darwinism are to be lectured
on and explained by Rev. Rowland Connor,
of Florence.


﻿A BLOW AIMED AT WOMAN SUF-
				FRAGE.

	Congress adjourned without the bill directed
against Utah becoming law, although it passed
the Senate in a modified form, but still retain-
ing the provision depriving the women of
Utah of the ballot. This is a point in which
all the women of the United States should be
interested, as far, at least, as they desire to
occupy a political equality, not simply with
men, but such men as are to be found among the
most degraded class of the colored race,
or the most ignorant foreigners that may seek
our shores and desire citizenship. Does any
one think that if Congress were to deprive the
women of Utah of the elective franchise by
repealing the Territorial statute conferring it
upon them, that the same body would imme-
diately turn around and undo its own work,
by again conferring the suffrage on them
through any measure extending that right to
all American women? Those who are agitat-
ing the question, who are working for the
extension of the elective franchise to women
all through the United States, are directly
interested in this matter, for they may rest
assured that the Congress that strikes at the
principle of woman suffrage, by depriving the
women of a Territory of the right to vote, will
not stultify itself by passing or recommending
any measure, nor any Constitutional amend-
ment, recognizing woman’s right to this
political status.	
	The Woman’s Suffrage Association of New
York saw this, and passed a resolution, which
was presented to Congress, protesting against


the blow aimed at woman's rights in the Frel-
inghuysen bill. And advocates of woman’s
cause throughout the entire Union should be
alive to the fact that, if a similar attempt is
made next session of Congress, it will have a
double object, as it had before: to further the
plans for plunder of political adventurers in
this Territorty, and to strike a blow at
the woman’s suffrage cause.
	Most of the men who urge with the most
strenuousness the crusade against the Mormons,
are avowed enemies to the woman’s rights move-
ment in its every aspect; and while they are
without influence to effect anything against it
in the States, they know very well that if they
can get any measure through Congress in
opposition to it and attacking it in a Territory,
they will have successfully made a point.
	Every lecturer, every editor, every person
advocating or interested in the cause of woman,
should make this matter his or her own, in
the event of another attempt to deprive the
women of a Territory of the right of suffrage.


TWO DANGERS.

	There are two dangers to which the Latter-
day Saints are exposed. One is a want of 
that strong faith in the Almighty, necessary to
secure His blessings, often the result of a neg-
lect of duty, a want of prayerfulness, or a
disregard of revealed truths, in consequence of
which the Spirit of God is grieved. The other
is an over confidence in the protection of the
Lord, and acting on the belief that He will
preserve His people from all danger or affliction,
no matter what course they may persue. They
have the promise that He will sustain them
and bear them off triumphantly if they will be
faithful, humble, prayerful, and keep His com-
mandments; yet they have no promise of such
protection if they neglect their covenants and 
disregard the commandments of the Lord. The
Saints have been so visibly preserved from
many threatened dangers, and have so often
been borne in safety over the troubled waters of
affliction, that some seem to imagine they will
continue to be so sustained, under all circum-
stances. Yet the promised blessing is only to
those who seek to sanctify and purify them-
selves before Heaven.
	These are principles which mothers should
inculcate upon their children, upon their sons 
and daughters, that from childhood to maturity
the youth of the community may be trained
in ways and in the practice of principles of
righteousness, that will give them a faith and
a power only received and enjoyed as a result
of living honestly and uprightly before the
Lord, and observing every truth of the Gospel 
of salvation.


﻿HISTORY AND ROMANCE. 

	A young mother wishes to be informed what
difference it can make whether she reads novels
or history to her children, as either will tend
to inspire thought which is the foundation of
mental greatness? She asks how the acts of
individuals in former generations can make
any difference with the people of to-day? Of
what intrinsic importance it can be to her or
her children whether Henry VIII, of England,
divorced Catharine of Arragon and had Ann
Boleyn beheaded, or otherwise? Whether Na-
poleon was longer remembered and more deeply
mourned by Josephine, or Maria Louise?
Whether Mary Queen of Scots was dethroned 
and imprisoned or not? Whether Hannibal
crossed the Alps, or did not leave Africa? Or
if the city of Thebes had one gate or one hun-
dred?


The facts contained in history should not
possess, of themselves, so great interest for
present or future generations, as the motives
which prompted individuals or kingdoms to
work out such facts. Every deed that is, or
has ever been, accomplished, must, in accord-
ance with the laws of nature, be, or have been,
actuated by some design. The histories of
wise and good men and women, tell us of bright
designs wrought out in noble deeds, worthy of
our imitation; those of ambiguous and repre-
hensible characters show us the tedious and
fraudulent way of walking in darkness with no
pure and exalted ambition to instigate to high
and noble purposes.
	History is solid food, in the shape of facts,
from which intellectual and philosophical sus-
tenance may be drawn; romance is a stimulant,
which excites the mental powers only to leave
them weakened, and stirs up passion to seek
gratification, even by unwise if not by un-
righteous means.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	On February 9th, Miss Eliza R. Snow,
with President George A. Smith and party,
was at Alexandria, in Egypt, and the party ex-
pected to start next day for Cairo, the capital
of Egypt. From thence they proposed going
to Suez, Ismailia, and Port Said, taking
steamer at the last named point for Jaffa, in
Palestine.
	An hour spent pleasantly in Professor Mor-
gan's College, a few mornings since, gave us
an assurance that the gentleman or lady who
has the privilege of regular attendance there,
can hardly fail of becoming so much engaged
in whatever branches of study may be pursued,
as to render school-life dry and irksome in no
respect. The management of the commercial
department and the styles of penmanship
exhibited, were particularly interesting. Evi-
dently, the Principal understands his business.
	As if to apologize and make restitution, for
the heavy snow-storms of the middle and latter
part of February, the grand old weather-king
has been smiling so benignly, through the first
days of March, as to give the beginning of
Spring months almost the lovely appearance of
May’s delightful self. As early as the 10th
inst., birds were singing merrily about the
upper windows; an enterprising honey-bee
danced in and out of doors which it happened
to find ajar, warming its glossy wings in the
sunshine; young ladies skipped about the
streets, which were positively becoming dusty,
with light shawls or none at all on; and older
ones sighed over the near approach of the dread-
ful heat of July and August.
	A very interesting meeting of the mem-
bers of the Physiological class was held
at the Social Hall, their regular place
of meeting, on the afternoon of the 11th
inst. After the usual instructions had
been given and received, Miss Martha
Horne, on behalf of the class, made a presenta-
tion to Miss Mary E. Cook, whose services as
teacher of the class have been gratuitously
given since its organization. The gift, which
had been purchased by the class as a small
token in acknowledgement of their thanks for
and appreciation of Miss Cook’s generous
endeavors was a very handsome dish of artificial
fruit, artistically formed and arranged by Mrs.
Wilkinson of this city. Mrs. Sarah M. Kim-
ball, President of the association, made a
short, appropriate address, which was re-
sponded to in suituble terms by Msis Cook,
It was then moved, seconded and carried,
that the name of the Society be changed from
"Phisiological Class" to “Ladies Educational
Association,” which was considered more
appropriate; also that for various individual
and general causes, the meetings be postponed
for one month.
	There was a time when woman’s chastity
was protected in Utah, and the crime of viola-
tion was unknown here. But with the advent
of a class of lawless men there is a sad change;
and women when alone and unprotected are in
hourly danger. A short time ago a lady ar-
rived at Ogden from Piedmont, on the U. P. R.
R., where she had been teaching school. The
train had been delayed by snow, and did not
reach Ogden until a late hour. She was offered
a room, used as an office, near the track, as a
shelter for herself and a baby in her arms, until
morning, the person who made the offer hand-
ing her the key, and saying that he stopped at
a hotel up in the city. She accepted the offer
with thanks, lonely as the place was, locked
herself in the room, and was preparing to seek
what rest she could for the night, when the
villain who had given her the key forced an
entrance into the place, and with leveled pistol
in hand and by violence committed a crime so
black that the only fitting punishment for it
should be death. He escaped, was captured at
Toano, and brought to this city; and is now
committed for trial, being held in bonds of
$5,000 for his appearance. That is: the federal
Judge who committed him, estimates a wo-
man’s outraged chastity and a life-long horror,
at the price indicated. A wealthy scoundrel
may thus commit crime with impunity, of the
most infamous character, if he can pay for it;
while a poor one may have to suffer imprison_
ment. And this is the manner in which
woman, is “protected” by the administrators
of the law who clamor so loudly against “Mor-
mon” men for caring for, preserving, and really
protecting their own wives, while honoring
chastity in all, and guarding it as a priceless
jewel!


﻿THE Prince of Wales, the Emperor of
Germany, the Hospodar of Montenegro and
M. Thiers will be among the royal and other
distinguished guests at the approaching
Vienna Exposition.	
	THE Shah of Persia wishes to make his
European trip without ostentation, so he
will take with him only three princes of the
blood, three ministers of the first class, seven
of the second, and thirty attendants.
	A RECENT calculation relative to the prin-
cipal European languages shows that Eng-
lish is spoken by 90,000,000 of persons,
German by 55,000,000 Spanish by 55,000,000
and French by 45,000,000.
	AN immense Catholic emigration from
Germany is said to be impending, in conse-
quence of the difficulties between the
Imperial government and the dignitaries of
the Roman Church. The statement seems
to be that the young Catholics will come to
America.
	MINISTER Bancroft and the Greek church
patriarch at Constantinople wagged their
grey beards at each other in mutual compli-
mentary visits, in the city of the Sultan
the other day. The patriarch is ninety-two
years of age, and the minister is believed to
be not more than forty or fifty years older.
	THE World’s exhibition at Vienna will
open in May. The American Commission
is very active in securing space and provid-
ing accommodations for exhibitors from the
United States. A sharp competition Is
expected between British and American
manufacturers of mowers, reapers, and
other improved agricultural machinery.


THE	SEX.

	THE Vienna Exposition will have a special
department for “Woman’s Work.”
	ALICE Wellington, Boston’s coming poet-
ess, is a teacher in the Girl’s High School.
	MISS Sarah W. Barton, an American girl,
is to be the prima donna in Warsaw, next
season. 
	A WOMAN in Birmingham, England, was
lately fined $200 for giving a good character
to a bad servant.
	AND now, rumor says, it was lung fever
instead of small-pox, with which Miss
Dodge, (Gail Hamilton,) has been afflicted.
	THE dowager Empress, of Brazil, died in
Lisbon, a few days since, aged 61. She was
married to the Emperor Pedro I., in 1829.
	CARLOTTI Patti was on a train in Georgia
when a severe accident occurred, and was
very active in attending to the wants of the
sufferers.
	A FEW admirers of the brilliant talents pos-
sessed by Mme. Clara Schumann, have pur-
chased an annuity for that lady of a thousand
thalers.
	A SAN Francisco editor thinks Oregon and
Washington must be good places for women
to live in if they have time there to attend
to “Mass Conventions.”
	THE eldest of General Schenck's daughters,
who are so pretty, well-educated, and taste-
ful, is about to be married to the son of
a member of Parliament.
	MADAME de Schweinitz, wife of the Ger-
man Ambassador, and daughter of Minister
Jay, was lately received by the Emperor of
Austria, at Vienna. 
	MRS. Mayor of Jurupa, lately saved her
child from drowning by decending a well by 
bucket and rope; She sustained herself and
child until assistance arrived.
	A HUSBAND in Rome, N.Y., lately sued
his wife for $30 he entrusted to her before
marriage. Nothing like it was recorded in
the law books, and the jury, with chivalric
promptness, decided In favor of the wife.
	MISS Hosmer’s monument to Miss Fal- 
coner, a young English lady who died in 
Rome, has been placed in the Church of St.
Andrea delle Fratte, in that city, and is the
only sculpture by an American artist to be
found in any of the Italian churches.
	A YOUNG sharper says, that “a school-
ma’am who has never been in love hasn’t a
bit of mercy; but take one that’s got some-
body to look for, and she’s the best in the
world. She always keeps a smile and a
pleasant word on hand, for fear of a
surprise.”
	A WOMAN fell into a well in a Western
town the other day, and an editor, in com-
menting upon it, remarked that he never
heard of a commotion in his life that hadn’t
a woman at the bottom of it. It is supposed
that the poor fellow had been married once
or twice and spoke from the card.
	THE fashions of the Japanese women are
thus spoken of by Mr. Lanmann: “The
hair of the women is gathered in bunches,
and tied with beautifully dyed crapes,
and secured by a long four-cornered tortoise-
shell pin. The dresses are made very long.
The sleeves are flowing, and only sewed
about half way up, thus forming a pocket.
A species of sash, four inches wide and four
feet long, is worn around the waist. The
women always wear white socks, in which
the big toe is separated from the rest, to
admit of a thong by which the shoes are
tied to the feet. They have ‘rainy weather’
shoes and ‘fair weather' shoes, which are
made of wood and of straw.”


﻿				WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿CORRESPONDENCE OF MISS SNOW

		ROME, ITALY, Jan. 24, 1873.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	From Genoa, where I wrote you last, we
went to Turin—from Turin to Venice. The
time has been when Venice exercised a
powerful influence at home and abroad, and
claimed to be “mistress of the seas.” That
day has gone by. The hotel at which we
put up appeared to be completely imbedded
in water; but we found on the opposite side,
from where we entered, stepping from our
gondolas on to stone steps it claimed a narrow
strip of "terra firma.” There is not a car-
riage in the place—in the public garden
three horses are kept on exhibition—all
travel and business, except that of pedes-
trian capacity, is done on the water by the
means of oars propelled by human muscles.
	The once boasted silk manufactures of
Venice have dwindled into a solitary one,
and that, I was informed, is worked by
hand. In the manufacture of glass, I think
they are nowhere excelled, especially in that
of the ornamental kind. We visited one
establishment. It is a curiosity to see into
how many forms and textures glass can be
worked. We saw women spinning and reel-
ing it into fine threads, while others wove
it into fancy plates, necklaces, etc., which
seemed elastic and strong.
	The “Grand Hotel Victoria,” our stop-
ping place, is a fine establishment, and
affords good accommodations for travelers.
	But I must hasten on, and leave Venice
with its palaces, palazzes, prisons, “Bridge
of Sighs,” gondolas and canals, for another
consideration; and from thence to Bologna—
thence to Florence, where for the first time
since leaving home, we are located on the
ground floor of the hotel.

			FLORENCE. 

	Beneath high, villa-dotted hills 
		That in succession rise 
	Like rich gemm’d parapets around; 
		The lovely Florence lies.

 	The Arno, broad and gentle stream, 
		That flows meand’ring through, 
	Divides, but in unequal parts, 
		The city platt in two. 

	I’ve seen its princely palaces  
		Where wealth and ease reside— 
	Where independence fills her sails 
		With luxury and pride. 

	I see you, Florence, all the while, 
		So beautiful and gay; 
	I ask, Is this your common dress, 
		Or, this your holiday? 

	Be wise; and while their golden show’rs 
		The bounteous heav’ns distil;
	Avoid debasing luxury, 
	Prolific source of ill.

	The crown of Peace is on your head— 
		Its wreath around your brow; 
	The royal carpet, newly spread, 
		Adorns your threshold now.

From Florence we went to Pisa—from thence
to Rome.
	Of the much I have seen here, I can say
but little in one letter. We are traveling
under the direction of Mr. Cook, the Lon-
don tourist. He furnished a guide to take
us out five days, and show us the most
interesting portions of the city. Our pro-
gramme concluded last evening; and feeling
that I have “done Rome,” I am doing up
matters to-day, preparatory to leaving for
Naples to-morrow—writing you is one of
the items.
	Among the many curiosities witnessed, I
will mention one remarkably singular, al-
though not the most pleasing. It is the
“Cemetery of the Capuchins,” in the Fran-
ciscan Monastery. Our guide took us to the
door, where we were met by a monk who
conducted us through four apartments in
which five thousand and four hundred
monks had been buried, and nearly all of
the bones of these persons were in plain
view. A narrow path on one side of the
room afforded a passage, parallel with which
was a railway over a very low wall to pre-
vent intrusion into the main portion of the
room which the bones occupied. It is
impossible to describe the sensation pro-
duced by the sight of millions of human
bones assorted and arranged—some on
arches from four to five feet high, under
which a body apparently in a similar
state to that of an Egyptian mummy, was
standing, dressed in a black gown, others
were symmetrically formed into niches where
other bodies were placed in a reclining
position; others were arranged in fanciful
forms and figures on the celings above.
The long bones were packed by themselves
—the arches being mostly formed by them,
and some of them crowned with several
rows of sculls—the small ones being used
in making ornaments. Every thing, the
chandeliers, candlesticks, etc., were made
of bones! The arrangement of them re-
quired much care and study, and no very
slight artistic skill.
	The manner of these monks is, when they
bring a body, to take up the bones of the
one that had been the longest buried, and
place the fresh one in the vacancy, and in
this way the bones are, accumulating from
time to time.
	Those bodies which do not decompose,
are placed in the arches and niches of these
bony structures, and preserved in this state.
We saw a number of them—one who died
as late as 1867, with whom our guide had
been well acquainted.
	With all the seemingly ludicrous arrange-
ments, these bones are sacredly prized by
the living monks. On being asked if they
would sell a skull, the answer was decidedly
“no." The strange curiosity of this exhibi-
tion interested me much, but I was glad to
turn my face in the direction of fresh air, as
soon as the novelty of the scene was ex-
plained.
	We visited the palace of Victor Emanuel
—passed through halls, reception rooms,
antechambers, state rooms, dining rooms,
etc., very beautiful—the furniture rich, and
altogether magnificent. We should have
been better satisfied had we interviewed
the occupant, but that was not included in
the programme.
						ELIZA R. SNOW.

				PROVO CITY, Feb. 22, 1873.
MY DEAR MISS GREENE:—
	I forward to you a letter which . I have
received from our beloved and highly
esteemed sister E. R. Snow, thinking it of
enough interset to grace the columns of
the EXPONENT. If you think proper, there-
fore, you are at liberty to publish all, or a
portion of it. as your judgement may direct.
I believe everything that appears in your
paper from that noble lady, gives satisfaction
to your patrons. With kind love to your-
self, and all the dear sisters in your city,
and best wishes for the EXPONENT, which
I sincerely hope may continue to increase in
worth and interest until it shall be second to
no publication in Woman’s Editorial depart-
ment—praying God to bless and endow you
with wisdom and light from on high, I am,
your sister in the bonds of truth and love,
					MARGARET T. SMOOT.
						ROME, ITALY,
						  January 18, 1872.
MY DEAR SISTER M. T. SMOOT:—
	You in Provo, and I in Rome! Who
would have anticipated this wide separa-
tion? Yesterday, Mr. Wood, Mr. Cook’s 
agent, with whom arrangements were pre-
viously made, to show us the most
important points here, conducted us to some
portions of the ancient ruins of this ancient
city. We were shown the remains of the
temple of Venus, Jupiter, etc., the ancient
Forum, the Rostrum so famous for speechi-
fying—portions of gigantic columns in a
reclining position, several standing in their
original places and in a good state of pre-
servation, considering their great age. We
were informed that the late Popes have
exercised their influence in preserving these
ruins which are now attracting the attention
of great numbers of people from other parts,
particularly from England and America.
	At the expense of the government, many
hands are now employed in excavating a
portion of the great Forum of the ancient
Emperors, the Caesars, etc., which by
gradual changes and usages, having been at
times occupied by shepherds, has been buried
to the depth of several feet by accumulations
of “debris.” There is a paling around the
ruins, and no one is allowed to enter with-
out permission. As we stood on a portion
that has been unburied, we saw the stalwart
guard-officer hurry a man off from the
premises, who, do doubt, had stealthily
intruded. We were all right, for our guide,
an Englishman, seems to be of considerable
consequence; he is agent and sub-Editor of
the “Swiss Times,” a lecturer, etc. When
he conducted us to the “Holy Stair Case,”
said to be the identical one that the Savior
ascended in the Court of Pilate; I asked him
if he would swear to it. He said emphatically,
“I will not swear to anything.” But directly
afterward, he said I will swear to the acque-
duct, and I will swear that the stairs
were brought from Jerusalem, and I will
swear to the “Arch of Titus,” which was
before us. We saw a man and woman
slowly ascending the stairs which no one is
allowed to ascend except on their knees;
our incredulity prompted us to accept the
suggestion of our guide and ascend by a side
flight which was considered less sacred; we
found a representation of Christ on the cross
in front of the “Sacred Stairs.” We visited
the celebrated Colliseum, which must have
been a most magnificent structure in earlier
days, being capable of seating eighty-seven
thousand, with standing room for twenty
thousand more.
	It was St. Anthony’s day, and we wit- 
nessed the ceremony of blessing animals,
which was the most amusing of all. On
that day the priest blesses horses, mules, 
donkeys and all kinds of animals. A fine
span of horses, richly caparisoned, were
driven in front of the church—the priest,
with several attendants, came out, dressed 
in black gowns with white tunics. Stand-
ing in front of the horses, with grave pom-
posity, he read a service in Latin, which,
probably the animals understood as well as
himself; after which he took a long handled 
swab from a kettle, in the hand of a boy
standing by his side, and flirted water
(probably “holy,”) In the direction of the
horses’ faces, but not far enough to reach
them; and then, after pocketing his fee, the
priest returned into the church. The coach-
man, a young man, could hardly hold his
countenance, looking as if he were witness-
ing a farce, and felt that the spectators all
sympathized with him.
						MONDAY, 20th.
	Yesterday afternoon Mr. Wood took us


through, around and under many of the
ruins of the palaces of the Emperors—
portions of the walls built by early Romans.
Many, of the specimens show vestiges of
beauty and magnificence. In several in-
stances, trees have grown in crevices ,of
ponderous edifices, which were constructed
of rock, brick and cement, their growth
having made large openings, forcing the
structures apart and thus producing dilapi-
dation. I will enclose you a leaf which I
plucked from a shrub growing on the remains
of the ruins of Caligula. In exhuming these
ruins, inscriptions are found which identify
many of them.	
	Last night brother Carrington joined us,
and brought what of our mail had arrived
in Liverpool. 	*	*	*	We are all
in good spirits; my health was never better,
although “sight-seeing” is not the easiest
work in the world. Clara and I get along
just as well as two persons could do, and I
feel no lack of companionship. *	*		
	I get but little time to write, have done
this by “piece-meal.” With much love, I
am as ever
						Eliza R. Snow.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


﻿	[Written for the EXPONENT.
	LETTER FROM BEAVER.
			—
	On the afternoon of the 20th ult., Presi-
dent Young’s party arrived in Beaver, and
notice was circulated that a meeting
would be held, and although it was
then after four o'clock, and very cold, the
house was filled to overflowing.
	Bro. Staines first addressed the assembly,
comparing the ease and comfort of the
present day, with the privations of former
times; demonstrating to the Saints that
labor, and not money, was the essential of
success in building up Zion—that labor, and
not capital, redeemed this fair land from a
howling wilderness. He urged the Saints
to improve their own homes and build up
their own cities, instead of selling their
handicraft to others for money. He also
spoke about the ways and manners of the
young; urging them to cultivate sober and
steady habits; saying that mothers confessed
hat although poverty and hardship had
stung them, and death had torn their child-
ren from their arms in infancy, they never
knew what sorrow was until their sons
approached manhood and disregarded good
advice, plunging into dissipation, wasting
time, health and self-respect in a certain
kind of enjoyment, which leaves a sting
behind.
	After him, Bp. Lorenzo D. Young occupied
a few minutes, but excused himself from much
speaking on the ground that his brother
was going to speak, and did not want to
hold the meeting late.
	President Young said he did not rise to
preach, he had done preaching enough
thirty years ago, to convert the world, had
the world listened; but he wished now to
converse a little with his friends.
	He spoke about the wilful blindness of the
world, its readiness to believe a falsehood,
and its outcry without investigation, against
this people. “But” he said, “we have no
cause for uneasiness, so long as we live our
religion and are not carried away by the
things of the world.” He also said that the
hatred and scorn of the world should be
nothing to the Saints; their delight should
be in the consciousness that God approved
of them, and that when the brief span of life
was over their reward was sure. The blows
aimed at him all fall short of their mark;
they were powerless to cause him a moments
uneasiness; the source of his joy and comfort
was as high above their influence as the sun
[new column]
was above the earth. He had merited the
hatred of the world by being instrumental
in the hands of God in forwarding His great
work; that blessing His people spiritually
and temporally, was to him a joy, a comfort,
a consolation, which the world could neither
give nor takeaway.
	Speaking of the desire to obtain wealth,
he said every one had a perfect right to get
all the wealth he could, honestly; provided
he made proper uses of it afterward. He
acknowledged himself only a steward over
the wealth which God had placed in his
hands, and accountable to Him for the use
of the least portion of it. That no one on
this earth owned anything; but those who
proved faithful and profitable stewards over
what was intrusted to their care, should be
real owners hereafter. He wished all to be
able, as he was, to say that in getting wealth
they had never wronged the widow and
orphan, never defrauded the poor nor the
rich, never over-reached their neighbor in a
bargain, nor in any way stained their souls
to get it. 	
	Referring to his reputed power and in-
fluence over the people, said he was often
tempted to wish he really had it, that he
might cause them to cease their folly, their
envy, backslidings, running after the things
of the world; and to fix their hearts and
eyes on God and the great future which is
surely theirs if they live for it. But he
added that it would not be right for him to
have such power—God himself did not
possess it, having delegated it to His
children in  the form of free-agency, to choose
between good and evil and receive the
appropriate reward for the choice made.
“Brethren and Sisters,” remarked the
speaker, “I am for God and righteousness!
and I would gladly know the sentiments of
your hearts to-night. Let all who are will-
ing and determined to serve the Lord to the
best of their ability, even though His enemies
hate, persecute and even kill them, now do
as I do!” and his right hand pointed heaven-
ward. Instantly hundreds of hands were
uplifted, and breathing was almost sus-
pended in reverential awe. His voice had
not been raised above conversational pitch,
his manner was calm, mild and kindly, as
that of a father conversing with his chil-
dren; but his words were like pure gold, and
seemed to sway the hearts of his hearers as
the summer wind sways the meadow grass.
He broke the silence with “Are you all
for God? Then I am glad; all is well.”
	He then referred to some parts of Brother
Staines’ remarks, advising the young to write
a history in daily deeds of honor, that if so
be, in the future they are called to occupy
high places, they need not blush to have
their early history sought out. Advised
parents to pray and teach their children to
pray; saying that children begotten, nur-
tured and taught in the fear and admonition
of the Lord, would never spurn their early
teaching and love corruption.
	I have seldom listened to so simple, plain,
unassuming, yet impressive a discourse.
And the effect was deepened on my mind
by a remark I overheard from a a stranger
who passed me on my way home, and whom
I took to be an outsider. He was saying to
his companion, “Well, it cannot be denied
that Brigham Young speaks with power,
and has a great influence over his listeners.
Why I felt it myself while he was talking.”
	I longed to answer, “Yes, the power of
good authority for what he says; the in-
fluence of a strong, noble, philanthropic
spirit over all corresponding good in the
minds of his hearers."
	I feel to say, as did that congregation,
God bless our President and spare his life
[new column]
long to bless his people with his loving
care, and to enjoy the golden harvest of the
priceless grain and fruits which he has
labored so faithfully to plant.”
								L. L. D.


RELIGIOUS PERSECUTION. 

	Ladies have assisted materially in the 
election of President Grant, some of us, to 
be sure, considering it “a choice between
the two evils.” Then should we not protest
against any unwise, or cruel act proposed by
the Executive, lest we share in the oppro-
brium?
	Surely, no woman can believe, in a relig- 
ious persecution, in this day, or, indeed, in 
any day. The principle is manish. Yet 
we are informed that President Grant in- 
tends to resume his disastrous course toward 
the Mormons. It is hard enough not to be 
free ourselves. But to see those who have
earned a right to live according to the dic-
tates of conscience by such sufferings, labors
and successes as the Mormons have proved, 
is harder still.		
	Were their women forced to share the 
love of a husband with others, as many 
nearer home are, it might be chivalric in  
President Grant to coerce the men. But  
even then he might better attend to greater 
evils nearer home.	
	The Mormons found a religious belief in 
the Old Testement, men and women alike, 
and put it in practice. Otherwise their
morals are pure, their industry, enterprise
and loyalty are unquestioned; they have
“made the desert to rejoice and blossom as 
the rose.” They are courteous and tolerant 
toward those who differ with them. Until
Eastern States, cities and people are equally 
praiseworthy, let them attack the “beam in
their own eye.”		
	If the President is to give people their 
religious views, why not overhaul the 
Oneida community, or attend to the “Social
Evil?”	
	Even some of the President’s most influen- 
tial male supporters are opposed to his
narrow impolicy in this matter; certainly 
all women must prove their sense of justice,
and belief in equal rights, by protesting
against persecution. Argument and exam- 
ple would finally overthrow polygamy.
Persecution will rouse the martyr spirit, 
and the blood of martys will prove the seed 
of that Church also, as it has for those of
purer faith.
					SHAWANEBEKE.
				In “The Woman’s Journal.”


JAMES Havens of Lynne, Connecticut, has 
fasted for fifty-seven days, it is said.	
	ONE-half of the false hair worn by Ameri- 
can women comes from prisons and lunatic
asylums.
	VERDI brought out Don Carlos, lately, at
Naples, and the audience brought him before 
the curtain thirty times.		
	Jo. Jefferson’s four nights at Baltimore 
produced $6,500. He will play in all the
leading southern cities.
	A MAN named Jackson, who escaped from
Vermont penitentiary, returned and claimed
the fifty dollars reward, and it was given to
him.
	THE Prince and Princess of Wales recently 
lunched with the Duke of Rutland. The
central dish was a boar’s head, and the
second a peacock pie.	
	GEORGE Sand’s translation of the Psalms
is pronounced by the critics to whom she
has read specimens of it, the noblest work
she has ever written.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT


A CHAT CONCERNING UTAH,
				—
WITH AN EX-UNITED STATES ATTORNEY, A
	POLITICIAN OF FORTY YEARS STANDING.

	SAN BERNARDINO, CALA., Feb 26th, 1873.
DEAR EXPONENT:—-
	I have been reading the news concerning
the proposed course to be taken at Washing-
ton in relation to Utah, and also the reasons
given by the twenty-six attorneys of your
city. Not being well posted in politics I
concluded to ask a few questions of the
Colonel, “my better half;” and thinking
you might be amused as I was, I concluded
to give you the “chat” in full.
	Question.—What is the cause of the ex-
citement against Utah at Washington?
	Answer.—Oh! there’s Clagget, and Axtell,
and Washburne, and Merritt, and a host of
others that want offices, or a pull at govern-
ment pap, and they think that the preference
will be given to the one who gets off the
most gas. 
	Q.	Don’t you think they are honest in
the objections they make concerning Utah?
	A. Well, we politicians, about election
times, don’t care anything about a man’s
morals if we can only get his support.
	Q. Don't you think the twenty-six attor-
neys at Salt Lake City had good cause for
taking the course they did?
	A. I Don’t know any of them but Ap-
pleby, and I can’t think he had any just
cause, for signing such an instrument, The
most of the others, I suppose, are floating
adventurers, and you know how it is with
we attorneys. “If business doesn’t come to
us, we go to business.”
	Q. But don’t you think they had some
cause for objecting to woman suffrage? 
	A. I think I could find five women in
Utah that know more of the rights of
parties than the whole of them.
	Q. What is your opinion in regard to the
case of Judge McKean?
	A. I have examined his rulings, and
read them to some of the most able attor-
neys in California, and we were of the
opinion that he should resign or be im-
peached.		
	Q. What course would you have taken,
had you been defending in his court?
	A. It is hard to make points before a
Judge that is prejudiced, but I think I
should have plead in bar President Polks’s
contract, and President Buchanan’s pardon.
	Q. What bargain did President Polk
make?
	A. He contracted with the Mormon
agents that if they furnished a battalion and
assisted to take a portion of Callifornia they
should select a part of the country, and he
would protect them in their peculiar relig-
ion.
	Q. What did he mean by “peculiar?
	A. Polygamy, I suppose.
	Q. What was President Buchanan’s
pardon?	
	A. Forgiveness for all past offences
against the United States.
	Q. Did the Mormons accept the terms?
	A. They did, and the negotiation was be-
tween the Mormons and Ben. McCullough
and Governor Powell, United States Com-
missioners. 
	Q. What course do you think President.
Grant will take in regard to Utah?
	A. I think the President has too much
good common sense to be influenced by
tricksters, or religious fanatics.
	This last question put a stop to the chat,
for the Colonel was a strong supporter of
Grant in the last election.
	With many wishes for your success, I
sign myself your friend,		MINNIE.

		    WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

		  A UTAH LADIES' JOURNAL. 
	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which 
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable 
sources the views honestly entertained by them on 
these questions. 
	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them : and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and 
countrywomen in reply. 
	Who are so well able to speak for the women of 
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented 
by others!"
	For these reasons, and that women may help each 
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of WOMAN'S EXPONENT, a journal owned by, 
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced. 
	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every 
subject of interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence, 
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading. 
	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge. 
	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best 
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but 
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In WOMAN'S EXPONENT a department 
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other 
matters of interest connected with their workings, 
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the 
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive 
due attention. 
	Miss Eliza R. Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numorous duties.

monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto. 
	The following low rates will place it within the

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. I	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, APRIL 1, 1873.	No. 21.﻿


﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.
	New York proposes to allow women own-
ing $250 in property to vote. Liberal New
York!
	Corrupt nations march to ruin with
more certain tread than they ever do to
victory or greatness.
	The Congressional whitewashing sea-
son is about over; but several darkened
reputations yet show discoloring stains.
	A very sensible lady, speaking of the
follies of fashion, remarks, concerning the
“Grecian Bend:” “Had the Creator desired
to see women with humps on their backs,
He would so have created them.”
	An eight year-old “country lassie” is re-
ported to have named one of her chickens
after a prominent Utah official, “’cause,”
she says, “that old hen is always cackling
when there’s nothing to cackle about.”
	Utah has received two new Associate
justices. If they are an improvement upon
their predecessors, the Territorial Supreme
Court quorum of three would not be injured
by another similar change.
	The friends of Woman Suffrage in
Massachussetts, are grieved at the action of
the Legislature of that State, in which, not-
withstanding the Republican platform, the
prohibition of equal political rights for
women was voted down by a large majority.
They declare themselves “defeated but not
conquered;” and they will eventually gain
the victory.
	“Strikes,” on a large scale, are threat-
ened again, in the East. Those of last
spring cost the employers and workmen of
New York over thirty-three millions.
That is the way capital and labor cut each
others’ throats. Both desire to make money,
and yet by these “strikes” prevent the
accomplishment of the object. Were there
less pride and stubborness among them,
arbitration might be successfully resorted to
for the settlement of their difficulties.
	The hunt for offices has been, so great
that President Grant has lamented the pres-
sure upon his time and patience from this
cause. It seems that the glory of the aver-
age masculine American, throughout the
States, is to become a politician, and the
aim of the politician is to secure office. The
country is viewed as a fat oyster which it is
the precious privilege of every member of
the winning party to open and enjoy.
	George Francis Train is confined in
the Tombs, the principal prison in New
York, for publishing the “Train Ligue,” on
the charge that it contained obscene litera-
ture; and a physical who has examined
him says he is insane. We have not seen
the “Train Ligue," and consequently cannot
speak as to its contents; but it is possible
Train’s peculiar style of telling the truth
has considerable to do with this medical
opinion as to his insanity.
	Six out of ten vacancies in the Internal
Revenue Department, at Washington, to be
filled by competition, were gained by
women, who doing the same work as men
receive the same pay. This is gratifying,
yet the right thus conceded should have
been recognized long ago. It was in Utah
that the right of women to support them-
selves as clerks, telegraph operators, and so
on, was first publicly and practically
acknowledged, and now it is being admitted
all over the country.
	The Editor of the San Francisco “Pio-
neer”does “not believe that Celestial mar-
riage; or polygamy, is an agreeable, natural
condition for men and women.” Let her
honestly and intelligently contrast polygamy,
as found in Utah, with that condition of
society impotently fought against, which
exists so largely and but semi-covertly in
San Francisco and elsewhere; and say which
social system she considers the more agree-
able and honorable.
	Foster, the “car-hook murderer,” was
hanged on Friday, March 21st, in New
York. His crime was the killing of one
Avery D. Putnam some two years ago, in a
street car, because Mr. Putnam endeavored
to protect two ladies from insult. On the
same day McElhaney, the Massachussets’
wife murderer, was also hanged; he had
brutally killed his wife. Charles Mortimer
has been found guilty of murder in the first
degree, in Sacramento, California, for the
killing of Sarah Gibson. These three cases
are enough for one issue of the Exponent,
to convince its readers how fast the world
that berates the Mormons for their “peculiar
views”’ on the woman question, is traveling
toawrds woman being protected and treated
as she should be.
	The special session of the United States
Senate closed on the 26th ult., not one of the
resolutions for expulsion of corrupt members
of Congress having passed either branch of
the national Legislature. Senator Patter-
son, of New Hampshire, accused of corrup-
tion and perjury, escaped because his term
of office had expired; Senator Pomeroy, of
Kansas, was declared innocent in the face of
very positive evidence of his guilt; Senator
Caldwell, from the same State, resigned,
although it was believed, that a vote to
expel him would have failed; Senator Clay-
ton, of Arkansas, was declared innocent;
while in the House, Ames and Brooks were
censured for admitted corruption, but not
expelled.
	If every, man in the country could be
paid as well for his services as the Rev. Dr.
Newman, palatial residences and comfortable
carriages should be more plentiful than they
are. Dr. Newman receives his salary as
pastor of the Methodist Episcopal Metropo-
litan Church, in Washington; he also re-
ceives $7,500 for being chapalin of the Senate
of the United States; and he has lately
received the appointment, from President
Grant, of Inspector of Consulates, as he pro-
posed a pleasure trip around the world, by
which he has the expenses of the trip paid,
and receives besides, $6,000, in coin. Un-
doubtedly he is a fortunate man, and his
good fortune is multiplied upon him because
he was defeated in discussion by Elder
Orson Pratt, in the New Tabernacle of this
city, when he came to overthrow Mormon-
ism, but was himself overthrown,
	The house of Representatives of the
New York Legislature, in session at Albany,
on Monday, March 24th, instructed the
Judiciary Committee to bring in an amend-
ment to the State Constitution, providing
that tax paying women citizens shall have
the right to vote. There are two anomalies
in this country that would bear explanation,
considering that “taxation without repre-
sentation” is held to be a grievous and
radical wrong. One is, why the people of
the Territories should be taxed by Congress
without a vote, by representative or dele-
gate, in imposing the tax, or disposing
of the money thus raised after it has been
collected. And the other is, why women
who own property and are taxed, all over
the United States, are refused representation
of any kind, except in two of the Territories
thus unjustly dealt with. The women and
the Territories only ask their just rights
from government and from the States.
	The question “are women citizens of
the United States within the meaning of
the Fourteenth Amendment to the Consti-
tution?” is expected to be shortly decided
by the Supreme Court of the United States,
in a suit brought by Mrs. Myra Bradwell
of Chicago, against the State of Illinois, for
refusing her permission to practice law.
Should the decision be in her favor, it will
make woman suffrage a fixed fact in every
part of the Republic, and will not then be
in the power of any demagogues to take the
right of suffrage from the women of Wyo-
ming and Utah, the only portions of the
United States where the elective franchise
is enjoyed by the sex. With this principle
sustained by the judicial branch of the
government, it might be said that the
builders budded more firmly than they
knew when framing that amendment, which
was intended to confer certain rights upon
the colored population but would be found
unwittingly to extend to women as well.
That this would work a greater revolution
in politics than the emancipation and enfran-
chisement of the blacks no one can doubt;
and some of the best jurists in the country
contend that the decision of the supreme
tribunal is certain to confirm it.
	“Was it a Ghost?” is the question agi-
tating a portion of Minnesota at present,
over the following rather curious item
which we clip from an Eastern paper: “In
the great storm which proved fatal to so
many people in Minnesota, one John
Weston, of Worthington, was overtaken on
his way home through the woods. Three
days after, his wife, who was anxiously
awaiting his coming, heard as she
thought at the door the voice of a neighbor
saying, ‘Mrs. Weston, John is frozen to
death.’ She went at once to the door, but
nobody was there. So, six days after, an-
other neighbor saw the missing man stand-
ing about fifteen feet from, the stable door.
He addressed him with, 'why are you
here, we thought you were frozen?’
When the ghost answered, ‘So I am, and
my body lies a mile and a half northwest of
Hersey.’ With this the ghost vanished.
The depth of the snow prevents searching
for the body at present.” Should the body
be found, at the place thus indicated, the
question now agitating that community will
be as far as ever from settlement; for the
world is unwilling to believe the truth in
regard to such phenomena, and prefers to
ascribe them to hallucination of the mind, or
accept the first theory that meets approval.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[For	the	Exponent.
		THE WORLD IS PROGRESSING.

The world is progressing! the sunlight of science.
	With new luster sparkles to-day,
And keen, searching intellect wins an alliance
	With secrets long hidden away;
Bright genius unfolds new and great combinations
	Of power to bless and serve man;
While muses and graces forsake not their stations
	Adorning the structure so grand.

The world is progressing! O womanhood arouse
		thee. 
  Nor in the swift race be outrun;
The great wheels of progress will never turn
		backward,	
  So roll them right bravely, each one—
In the proud halls of state, at the hearthstone so
		 lowly,
	Let your voice for improvement be heard,
Strengthen virtue and honor, disdain things un-
		holy
And bid thought soar as free as a bird. 

The world is progressing! O manhood, waken
	And join in the jubilant strain!
The fetters which bound thee now surely are
		breaking
	And never shall gall thee again.
No more in the dust shall thy meek head be
		trodden;
	No more shall injustice o’erwhelm;
Thou shall rise above ignorance, fashion and folly.
	And reign as a queen in thy realm.

The world is progressing! old fabrics of error
	Fall, moldered and crumbling to earth:
While structures, far statelier, nobler and fairer,
	Rise pillared  with power and worth.
Old Prejudice withers 'neath the blows that are
		dealt her.
	And for power, wild struggling she keeps;
But her terrible sway shall forever be broken—
	Humanity no longer sleeps.

The world is progressing! yet all is not stainless
	Grim evils yet flourish abroad—
False teaching false swearing and falsehearted
		statesmen,
	Black intrigue, and blackest of fraud.
But the world is progressing! thank God we’re
		progressing!
	The day of dull torpor is past;
Brave hearts and strong hands are their ardor
		expressing;
The millions are waking at last!
							Lu Dalton.
Beaver.


F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS!

			Pleasant Grove,
				February 26th, 1873.
Dear Exponent:—
	We have been silent so far to the public;
yet we have been busy at home, trying to
promote the interests of our Relief Society,
which has, in the proper way, discarded a
portion of its former appellation—the word
"Female." We have plenty of faithful
sisters who evince a spirit characteristic of
our noble callings. Our Society is in a 
healthy state financially. By our generosity,
coupled with economy, we have succeeded
in gathering and saving quite a handsome 
amount of means which we have invested
in Co-operative institutions where we have
a perpetual benefit in dividensd.
	There is a unanimity of feeling among
our members to assist in building up the
Kingdom of God upon the earth. Our
monthly meetings are well attended. We
have rendered assistance to many public
improvements, as well as giving temporal
relief to those in our midst who are needy.
	We are of the opinion that it is very
opportune that we have such an organ as
the Exponent, for while Congress and 
President Grant are devising some means,
no matter how unconstitutional, against us
as a people we, the "weaker" portion of the
community, can speak boldly for the noble
institution with which we are so closely
interwoven that it will take more wisdom
than men can conceive of to cause us to re
linquish one iota of our holy religion.
"Fear not little flock, it is my good will to
give you the kingdom."
	God bless the right.
					Rosalie E. Driggs.

				Salt Lake City,
					March 13th, 1873.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	The Relief Society of the 17th Ward is
doing well, has a sufficiency for its poor,
and some to spare. The annual report,
which was read on the 6th of February,
showed the Society to be in a flourishing
condition financially.
	On the suggestion of our President, Mrs.
O. Hyde, the Society donated $80 to
the Emigration Fund last year. At a meet-
ing held in December, Secretary Mrs. Alder
moved that the word “Female” be dropped
from the Society, in accordance with the
views of Sister E. R. Snow, which was
passed by a unanimous vote. At the same
meeting President Mrs. Hyde requested the
visiting Committees to visit their blocks
and solicit for a Christmas dinner, for the
poor, which resulted in the donation of $30
in cash, chickens, pies, cakes, groceries, etc.
being collected, and our needy had a sub-
stantial reason to be glad that it was Christ-
mas.
	There are less needy in the ward at
present than at any time since its organiza-
tion, and a feeling of perfect unity exists in
our Society. We also feel that President
Mrs. Hyde and her Counsel have blessed the
Society, wisely guiding it, and the good
feeling we have always enjoyed is the
natural result.
	Our Bishop and the brethren have decided
to build a new meeting house this Spring,
and have taken active measures towards it,
for which we are thankful; and at a recent
meeting voted to help them all that lay in
our power; for we have sadly felt the need
of a comfortable meeting house in the Ward; 
and with it, we feel we will increase in
wealth, both as a Ward and as a Society.
					Mrs. O. Hyde, President,
	Mrs. G. A.  Alder, Secretary.

		19th Ward, Salt Lake City,
				March 20th, 1873.
Editor Woman's Exponent:—
	Having been rather remiss in forwarding
you a report of this branch of the Relief
Society, we will endeavor to offer one as
concise as possible now. During the five 
years the Society has been organized, the 
donations in cash and merchandise have 
amounted to $1,400. Our disbursements 
are $900, having donated $100 to the Emi-
gration Fund, $75 to missionaries, made
various other donations of small amounts
and used means for the benefit of the poor
of the Ward; which leaves a balance on
hand of $500 designed to be devoted to
building a hall for the benefit of the Society.
We hold our sewing meetings semi-monthly,
and our meetings for receiving donations
and making disbursements, monthly. Al-
though but few, comparatively, of our
members meet together, yet the people of
the Ward donate liberally to sustain the
Society. When we realize the blessings of
this great institution, organized in our
midst, we are led to exclaim Surely our
Father in Heaven careth for his children!
We have declined the use of the word
"Female," as being superfluous in connection
with the name of the Society. We think
very highly of the EXPONENT and wish it 
much prosperity.
		Respectfully yours,
				A. A. Pratt, President;
	Maria W. Wilcox, Secretary.

	A meeting of the Relief Society of the
10th Ward in this city, held March 6th, was
attended by Sisters Mary L. Horne, Zina D.
Young, Elizabeth Howard and Louise L.
Greene. That branch of the Society appears
to be active and wide-awake in relation to
the many responsibilities which the sisters
are learning to take upon themselves as
legitimately belonging to them. The re-
ports given in concerning those who are
needing assistance, and the general interest
exhibited in their behalf, showed the sisters
of the 10th Ward to be in possession of the
true spirit of their calling, and united in
their desires and efforts to do good.

	The tenor of spirit manifested at the
general meetings of the Co-operative Re-
trenchment Associations, held on the 15th
and 29th of March, in the 14th Ward As-
sembly Rooms, showed that retrenchment
in cooking and in dress, as first introduced
in the associations, had not been lost sight
of by a portion of the members. The im-
portance of self-culture was also warmly and
intelligently vindicated.

	The sisters in Heber City send us cheerful
reports of the Relief Society in that settle-
ment.


﻿THE JOYS OF THE POOR.
			  ——
	A woman was washing outside the door.
Her arm was slender, her eye dark and
quick, her forehead high and white, not-
withstanding exposure to the sun and wind.
There are some brows that never will
darken, no matter what the chances.
	The room was the last and least in the
row, at the further end of the alley. Seeing
the approach of a stranger, flashes of feeling
ran over the washerwoman's face, showing
she would have liked a moment to adjust
her toilet.
	Listen! There is music within! An in-
strument in the hands of a boy, apparently
about ten years of age, is leading the
infant voices in melting strains of simple
song.
		"Let me kiss you father, kiss you,
			I am very tired of play;” 

	There sat little Susie on the wood, the
baby on a rude stool near by, while Willie,
the eldest of the three, stood as if leading the
singing, and on the other side, the boy
playing the instrument with very superior
skill. I moved not another step, spoke not
a word, scarcely breathed, so exquisite
was the thrill that ran through my whole
being! Listen, angels! Harken children
of earth! This is heaven, though it be in a
hovel! All in unison; not the slightest
discord from beginning to end; not one in-
distinct syllable or misplaced accent! Now
they have ceased;	
	“Why madam, are these all your child-
ren?” I asked; and received the following
reply:
	"Yes, ma’am; they are all mine! and
don’t they sing prettily? Don’t you think
the poor have their joys as well as the rich?
I go out daily to wash that they may have
bread to eat; and when I return and hear
their sweet voices I forget that l am weary
or poor.”
	“But where is their father?” I queried:
“Ah! ma’am, he has wasted his life in the
drinking saloon, and we have left him in our
native country. He comes no more to
abuse the dear children, nor to humble me 
with threats and blows; and now we are
happy, though we are poor."
							BETTY PINE.
Salt Lake City, March 16,1873.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
				—
	WOMAN'S WORK IN THE GARDEN. —Don't
say you have no time.  Leave off a ruffle on
Susie's apron, or the rows of braid on Tom-
my's jacket.  If the kitchen floor be not
daily polished to whiteness, your husband
and his troop of hungry men will not mind
it; so, just ge tthings in trim for dinner, and 
steal the time for some pleasant work in the
garden every day.  Pay no regard to John's
sly  hint that a lively race with him down
through the potato patch, hoe in hand,
would do just as well; but coax or hire some-
body to spade a little plot of ground, and 
the work of planting and weeding will be a
penance for numerous physical ills.  A sun-
bath in your own dooryard might be just as
potent, and certainly less costly,  than if
prescribed by a physician to be taken on the
hillside,  at some fashionable water cure.
Try it, ye worn and exhausted toilers, who,
in the never-ending round of houshold 
labor, are fast losing that buoyancy which is
such a powerful lever in lifting heavy bur-
dens.
							RUTH DANA.
	A USE FOR RUSHES.— When I was quite
a little girl, I remember paying homage to
an egg shell which hung in the cottage
window of an old lady whom I visited.  It
was ornamented with a particular design at
nothing, I think, only the blending of
bright colors.  This was effected by dyeing
the pith of common rushes in various bright
colors and glueing them on in rings.  Since 
then, I have discovered that very beautiful
imitations of flowers like the camelia, 
tuberose, etc., may be made by their skillful
use.  Make each petal separately and fasten
the center with wax.  Once commenced,
you can scarcely fail to make pretty flowers
—if you are patient.
						ROSE GERANIUM.
	IF YOU wish to have your pictures show
to greatest possible advantage, avoid all
figured wall paper, or have the figures
as small and  quiet as your taste will allow.
—"The Beautiful Home."
	GINGER SNAPS.—One pint of molasses;
two thirds of a cup of butter; one teaspoon-
ful of ginger; a small half-cup of sweet milk;
one measure each of Horsford's preparation.
Mix well in flour enough to roll out
	SOFT GINGERBREAD.—One and a half cup
of molasses; two thirds of a cup of sour milk;
half a cup of butter; one teaspoonful of
soda; one teaspoonful of ginger.
	SWEET SAUCE (for all kinds of pud-
dings).—Half a cup of butter; one cup of
sugar; one cup of milk.  Stir the butter and
sugar to a cream; boil the milk, and while 
boiling stir in one teaspoonful of corn-starch
previously mixed in a little cold milk; pour
the milk while boiling, over the butter and
sugar.
	STEAM PUDDING.—Two cups of sour
milk; two cups of flour; two cups of corn-
meal; half a cup of molasses; two small
teaspoonfuls of soda; a little salt.  Steam
one hour and a quarter.
	DELICATE CAKE.—The whites of five
eggs beaten to a froth; two cups of white
sugar; half a cup of butter; one cup of milk;
three cups of flour, and one measure each of
Horsford's preparation mixed well in the 
flour.
	MOUNTAIN CAKE.—Two eggs; one cup of
sugar; half a cup of butter; half a cup of 
sweet milk; two cups of flour; one teaspoon-
ful of cream of tartar; half a teaspoonful of
soda.  A little nutmeg and fruit impoves it.
	APPLE JAM.—Weigh equal quantities of
sugar and good sour apple; pare, core and
[new column]
chop the apples fine; make a good clear
syrup of the sugar; add the apples, juice and
grated rind of three lemons, and a few
pieces of white ginger. Boil it until the
apples looks clear and yellow. On no
account omit the ginger.
	WHILE SMOKING meat with chips or saw-
dust, put on the fire a few red peppers;
the fumes will prevent all insects from at-
tacking your meat.
	WINDOW-BOXES—Plants may be success-
fully raised in these, where only a few are
required for early crops, and are often more
convenient than a hot-bed.


[For the EXPONENT.
			THE EVIL OF SLANDER.
					 ——
	There is probably no one thing in which
the Saints sin so much as in speaking evil
of their brethren and sisters, and attending
to other people's affairs.  The poet says:
"We've faults enough at home to mend,"
and we all know this is true.  Yet how 
prone we are to meddle with our neighbors'
failings, to desire to pull the motes out of
their eyes when probably, at the same time,
there is a beam in our own.  There is one
things we should all bear in mind: if our
brother or sister does wrong we are not the 
ones to put him or her right; that belongs
only to those who are placed over them.
And another thing, their faults have noth-
ing to do with us, unless they sin against
us individually.  When they do so, it is our
duty to go to them privately and tell them
of their error, instead of going to those who
have no right to know anything about it.
If we will do this with the right spirit we 
will be tolerably sure of receiving satisfac-
tion.  In all we undertake we should have
the Spirit of God to dictate us; then we are
certain of doing right; for that Spirit never
leads anyone into difficulty.  The Lord
never forsakes us, it is always we that for-
sake Him when we are unhappy.  He is
always ready and willing to pour out
blessings upon us when we are willing to 
receive them.  We should not watch for 
iniquity, for if we look for evil we will
certainly find it; if in no other way, by mis-
construing something that was meant for 
good.  You know there is a woe pronounced
upon those that make a man an offender for 
a word.  Let us remember what the Savior
said.  "Whatsoever ye would that men
should do to you, do ye even so to them."
I don't think many of us carry this out in
the true sense of the word; for we would
not like to think that our friends were
criticising our talk and actions in our
absence.  We would think they were both
unjust and unkind, when at the same time
we have scarcely a friend in the world but
we can find some fault with, and are very
apt to do it.  It is a good rule never to say
a thing in persons' absence that we would
not be perfectly willing for them to hear.
If we adhered strictly to this rule, it would
be very little evil we would ever speak of
anyone; if we told them of a fault we would 
be as lenient as possible.  Girls, let us strive
to do right, and be forbearing with each
others' weaknesses, and the Lord will bless
us, for He is desirous to bless and comfort
those who seek Him with full purpose of
heart.
								M.F.


IT IS astonishing to see how readily bad 
habits may be overcome and good ones
grown into by a person whose mind is fully
made up to be wise. That attempts at re-
form so often prove futile, is simply because
of a lack of resolution and determined
courage.


[For the Exponent.
		"DOES HER OWN WORK."
				    ——
	"Does her own work! Does she!" I
heard this uttered in a sneering con-
temptuous tone, as if it was a disgrace to
the girl in question; as if she was less a
true lady, less worthy of respect than those
who sit with their hands in their laps and
never know or realize aught so blissful or
satisfactory as the sense of real dignity
which is the happy attendant of honest,
healthful labor. And I could not help
thinking the expression of such feelings
evinced a selfish, narrow mind, better fitted
for almost any other community than ours,
whose institutions are based upon honorble
labor as one of the most essential corner
stones.
	Such utterances show the detestable spirit
of caste, which should be foreign to any but
the vain and ignorant, but which is estab-
lishing itself too forcibly among certain
classes in our midst. They should reflect
that manners and morals, as well as dress
and occupation, enter into the grand mak-
ing up of a character. If a woman is able to
live without actual labor, let her do so if
she so desires. But to be ashamed of work,
or of a knowledge of how to manage the
shop, or the kitchen, or to sneer at those
who do work, betrays the presence of a
"false pride." Were ouw mothers ashamed
to work, or are we ashamed of them because
they did work? It is an honor to any woman,
any society, to be able and willing, when
necessity requires it, to do her own work.
							M.   R.   W.
		EDUCATION OF WOMEN.
				  ——
	George W. Curtis thus closed his lecture
on the education of women: Now, as to
the question of a collegiate education for
women, it is not proposed that women shall
be compelled to go to college but only that
they shall have precisely the same opportu-
nity that men have, if they wish to improve
it. There is in England at this very day
a lady lecturing at the Young Women's 
Institute upon domestic questions, such as
"Would you prefer to use an earthen or an
iron pot upon your kitchen stove?" or "How
would you lay out ten shillings if you had a 
sick husband and six children too young to
work?" and "How would you make bread?"
This is precisely the kind of education; the
sooner it becomes universal, the sooner we 
shall have better food and better health. We
have our agricultural fairs and colleges, and
certainly if it is worth while to teach us how
to raise wheat it is as wise to teach us
how to convert it into bread. If there are
branches of knowledge improper for wo-
men to learn or duties unfit to perform, they
will know it a thousand times better than
men, so give them their choice. If there
be some women in whom the love of learn-
ing extinguishes all other love, then the
heaven-appointed sphere of that woman is
not the nursery. It may be the library, the
laboratory, the observatory. Not the happi-
ness of Romeo and Juliet. but star-eyed
science is her place.  Does such a woman
prove that perfect liberty of education un-
spheres woman?  On the contrary it has
enabled that woman to percieve exactly
what God meant her to do. We have left
the petted slave in the Greek-household, to
go onward and forward.  We have left
the idle toy and the painted doll with
Chesterfield and the clubs, and we still go
forward.  God lead us to find the true
woman in the free American home.


﻿THERE are 50,000 gold-hunters now in
Africa.
