SPLINTERS.
			      —
	Dr. Feard joins his opinion with the senti-
ment which is becoming popular, that
modern brain-workers sleep too little..........
The Iowa legislature gives too much credit to
its lady clerks and commends women for
such employment to future General Assem-
blies......Mrs. Woodhull's lecture on "Naked
Truth" is said to contain very indecent and
blasphemous language......Maria A. Elmore
is writing a series of articles on "Woman's
Dress," a subject which demands more
serious and sensible attention than it re-
ceives......Six lady physicians graduated in
one day lately at the "Woman's Hospital
Medical College."......A woman admirably
oversees the binding in one of the largest
printing-houses of St. Louis......George W.
Curtis has been dangerously ill with typhoid
fever, at his home on Staten Island, occa-
sioned by overtaxation in his civil service
and other duties......Mrs. S. B. Chandler
has lately concluded a series of lectures in
Washington on "Education and the duty of
parents."......The Queen of Belgium is said
to own the finest horses in the world......A
German housekeeper in New York, Mrs.
Lawrence, about six feet high and weighing
250 pounds, captured a house-breaker the
other night, without any assistance, taking
from him an offensive looking knife and a
chisel of considerable magnitude......Ten-
nessee employs its female prisoners in
breaking rock for street pavement......Pro-
fessor Fowler is reported to have said, "If
you would marry a good wife, get a romp."
It is reasonable to presume he meant a care-
ful, studious, virtuous "romp," who can
enjoy work as well as play......Again report
says, that the winsome little actress, Lotta,
has departed the stage—of life; this time it 
is in France; but may possibly be another
mistake of the telegraph......A man in Illi-
nois has lately found out, to his great
astonishment, that there is a newspaper
published "away out in that wild country,
Oregon.”.....Vienna has a type-setting
school for girls. Other schools should intro-
duce this art, for girls and boys too......
California gives equal pay to male and female
teachers, according to the abilities mani-
fested; that is fair play......Since the death
of her father, Miss Ella Chapin fills the
position he formerly held as Register of
Deeds for Gratiot County, Michigan......Two
practical artizans, two scientific men and a 
large number of honorary commissioners have
been appointed by President Grant, to  attend
the  Vienna Exposition......Printers in Swit-
zerland think they have a "fat" situation
when it affords them $3:60 per week......
Mrs. Snyder, a Syracuse shop-lifter, stole
$1,500 worth of dry goods in an hour. The
Leavenworth "Commercial" thinks she
should go to Congress......Paris has a band
of robber composed partly of females......
Brooklyn gives a detective, convicted of
perjury in a divorce suit, six years and 
three months in the State's prison......San
Ignacia, Mexico, has witnessed a remarkable
phenomenon in a shower of quicksilver......
The German Geographical Societies are going
to send explorers to supplement the discov-
eries of Dr. Livingstone......A man in Ohio
was lately literally ground up by being
caught in the cogwheel of a flour mill......
The past winter, particularly the latter
portion of it, is very generally admitted to
have been an old fashioned one......Brick
Pomeroy's divorced wife is married again....
The President's salary having been doubled,
it is intimated that he will now be able to
pay off the national debt with greater ease
and rapidity than at any former period......
[new column]
The prince of Lichtenstein has placed his 
castle at Vaduz at the service of the nuns
expelled from Germany......An English
Chief Justice decides that the writer of a
novel has no more right to dramatize it
after publication than any other person has.
......Pike County, Indiana, has a famous
medical spring......The grounds and sewer-
ages are being prepared in Philadelphia, on
the site of the proposed buildings for the
Centennial celebration......In Taylorville,
Polk County, Iowa, poor men recently be-
came rich in one day; oil was discovered
and the price of property instantly trebled.
......Somebody stole a Maltese cat from the
Superintendent of the New York Police
Department, lately; he offered a reward of
fifty dollars for its recovery, and the place
is lively  with cats since that time, though
he has not discovered any trace of the real
stolen one......A couple were married on the
cars in Missouri, a few days since, thus
entering into marriage at railroad speed......
Mr. Beecher's salary is $20,000....A daughter
of George Sands is going to marry an ex-
Catholic priest whose home is in Tours,
France......Seven thousand women belong
to the Belgian Internationals...Mrs. Knowles,
widow of the heroic captain of the North-
fleet, is to receive, by order of Queen
Victoria, a pension from the civil list of £50
a year........A woman 74 years old, in Mary's-
ville, California, pursued and shot a thirty
pound wild cat, recently, for having de-
stroyed her pet lamb.


ONE PROMINENT REASON. 
					—
   Said a person a few days ago, who has made
himself somewhat conspicuious in pressing a
crusade against the Mormons, when closely 
questioned on the subject: “The fact is, I care
nothing about how many wives a man has;
I make no pretentions to being a moral man
myself, and I care very little whether other
men are moral or immoral; but I’m bound
to break up th the ocracy and religion that’s in
this thing.” This was not his exact language,
although we have placed it in quotation
marks for we would not transfer to these
columns any of the vile and blasphemous in-
vectives with which his speech was interlarded;
nor would our informant, a gentleman whose
honor and veracity are unquestioned, repeat
them in our hearing; but the ideas expressed
are here produced. The person is thus, himself,
confessedly an immoral man, yet he declaims
against the Latter-day Saints and their faith,
not because he cares whether their practices
are moral or otherwise, but because he aims at
overthrowing their religion and breaking up
their Church; and he finds “polygamy” a word
that can be used to rouse the prejudices of the
bigoted and the ignorant. There is nothing
new in this. It is the old story—as old as the
contest between truth and error; as old as
the revelation of gospel principles to man.
Those who hold to the truth dare not resort to
falsehood to defend themselves, for if they did 
the Spirit of truth would not abide with them;


but those who oppose right, employ every
means, no matter how false, dishonorable or
vile to secure a triumph. This man had some
honesty left. He was fighting to accomplish
an object, Though licentious himself, he
assumed an air of virtue and endeavored to
affix the stigma of licentiousness where it did
not belong, to arouse a public feeling against
the Latter-day Saints; and had honesty enough
to admit as much. The subject needs no
elaboration; it is very plain; and contains a
truth and a moral that should be self-evident
to all.


WOMAN SUFFRAGE IN UTAH.
				—
	Under this heading the Woman's Journal
congratulates the friends of Woman's Suffrage
that the "Utah Bill," better known as the 
"Frelinghuysen Bill" did not pass the House,
and that through the efforts of the friends of
Woman's Suffrage, it failed to become law.
The Journal's article says: “This abominable
bill, framed by Senator Frelinghuysen, of New
Jersey, and approved by the Senate Judiciary
Committee, was entitled 'A bill in aid of the
execution of the laws in the Territory of Utah, 
and for other purposes.' But it was especially
designed to remand the women of Utah into
political slavery, and even went so far as to
guard against their future emancipation by
Constitutional prohibitions."
	In support of this the Journal quotes the act
of the Territorial Legislature, conferring the
elective franchise; and the part of the "Frel-
inghuysen Bill" which repeals that act." It 
further shows, after referring to the fact that
20,000 women  were thus to be disfranchised,
that the men of Utah were to be prevented from
again conferring the right of suffrage upon
women; and in this connection the provision
of the bill might have been cited which re-
quired all future laws made by the Territorial
Legislature to be enacted in conformity with
the spirit and letter of this bill, thus attempt-
ing to prohibit the leading Territory of the 
United States from exercising the legitimate
powers which the very fundemental principles
of our government vest in every legislature in
the United States—State, Territorial or muni-
cipal—by which laws shall be made as liberal
as the public good demands; and in conformity
with the Federal Constitution. The concluding
portion of the article we give in full:	
	No content with thus crushing the political
rights of the women of Utah, with the strong 
arm of the Federal Government, Senator Fre-
linghuysen actually proposes to rob every 
married woman in the Territories of her person,
property and earnings.
	"Sec. 23. That the common law of England,
in force in the colonies of America, at the date
of the Declaration of Independence, is hereby
extended over and declared to be in force in
the Territories of the United States, so far as
the same is applicable: 'Provided, that nothing
herein shall be construed to prevent the Terri-
torial Legislatures of the respective Territories
from modifying the same."
	Remeber, ladies, that "The common law of
England,” thus extended over your sisters in
Utah, expressly gave "The custody of the wife's 
person absolutely to her husband," gave the
husband a right to use "gentle restraint" to
keep her, and to administer "moderate cor-
rection," to punish her; deprived her of the
power to sue or be sued, or to make a will.
Made her the "servant" of her husband; gave
him all her personal property and all her earn-
ings, and the life-use of all her real estate, if
they had ever had a living child, and subjected 
her to despotic control.
	To this infernal law, almost as bad as chattel
slavery itself, Senator Frelinghuysen has
actually induced a Republican Senate to suject
the women of Utah. Thus has the Republi-
can Senate shown itself mindful of its obliga-
tions to the loyal women of America! This is


its “respectful consideration," for the rights of
the women of the Territories.
	Thank God the bill has failed. But this
failure may prove only a respite. The snake
is scotched not killed. Let the women of New
Jersey call a public meeting and denounce tlie
action of their Senator, who has thus insulted
Womanhood. Let them see to it that Mr.
Frelinghuysen is never re-elected to the
Senate of the United States.,
	This question, as the EXPONENT has before
contended, is one not confined to the women of
Utah, for had the “Frelinghuysen Bill” become
law it would have acted adversely to the Wo-
man’s Suffrage agitation throughout the United
States; and it is gratifying to note that this
fact is being recognized throughout the
country.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


TERRITORIAL FAIR.
				    ——
	The Board of-Directors of the Deseret Agri-
cultural and Manufacturing Society are already
arranging premiums for the forth-coming
Territorial Fair. As it will not be held until
October, this may seem early to direct atten-
tion to it; but there was complaint made—and
we think reasonably so—by the ladies of the
Relief Societies last year, that they had not
sufficient notice to prepare for the Territorial
exhibition so as to make such a showing as
they desired to do, and hence we refer to the
next Fair ,thus soon. One of the most interest-
ing features of the last one was the department
devoted to ladies’ work, in which several
of the Relief Societies made very liandsome ex-
hibitions. When it is understood a few months
in advance that a Territorial exhibition will be
held, the ladies and Societies that design partic-
ipating in it will commence in time and exert
themselves to have such a representation as
they will be satisfied with.
	Since the above was written the following
address was placed in our hands for publication
in the Exponent, which we take much plea-
sure in laying before the ladies of the Relief
Societies throughout the Territory:
		Salt	Lake City, March 25th,	1873.
To the Relief Societies of Utah:
	Ladies:—The Board of Directors of the
Deseret Agricultural and Manufacturing
Society propose holding another Exhibition,
or Fair, in October 1873. 
	In order that ample time may be given to all
who contemplate taking an active part in the
Exhibition, the Directors have already pre-
pared a premium list offering to all competitors
a fine opportunity of displaying their skill,
tact and genius in producing from the mater-
ials at their command, beautiful specimens of
the workmanship of their own hands.
	We cheerfully accord to the Ladies an ac-
knowledgement of the fact that in their
Department the Fairs have invariably been a
success, and when time has been given them
the number of the specimens have never been
meagre, and have generally exceeded that of
any other department.
	The dignity of labor is conceded by all
intelligent beings, and the adage that there Is
no excellence without labor has passed into an
axiom, or proposition to which assent is con-
ceded without proof.   We feel therefore to ask
your honorable associations to unite with us,
from Rich County in the north to Washington
County in the south, in an endeavor to make
the exhibition of 1873 a truthful exposition of
our progress in material developments.
				Wilford Woodruff, President,
	Robert. L, Campbell, Secretary.


﻿HOME AFFAIRS.
				    —
	Two exceedingly interesting letters from
Miss Eliza R. Snow, will be found in the pre-
sent number of the Exponent.
	We shall be pleased to see our friends, who
may come in to Conference, at the office of the
Exponent, west of the city market.
	The Annual Conference convenes on Sun-
[new column]
day next, April 6th, when a large attendance
is expected. Presidents Young and Wells are
at home and most of the Twelve Apostles will
be present. The First Presidency have deemed,
it expedient to telegraph throughout the Terri-
tory,. to the various Bishops, enjoining the
necessity of strict quarentine in regard to small-
pox, and the importance of persons, who have
been exposed to the contagion not coming to
Conference.
	PREPARATIONS are being made at the
theatre in this city to provide attractive enter-
tainments during. Conference times, including
the spectacular play of “Cherry and Fair
Star,” and the drama of “Stage and Steam,”
written by Mr. E. L. Sloan, of this city, which
when first produced a few weeks ago was so
well received.
	Small-pox has continued to spread in this
city, and the City Council has deemed it neces-
sary to order the quarantine regulations to be
rigidly enforced. Much danger would be
avoided if people, who are exposed to the con-
tagion, would take proper precautions not to
carry it to others; and were all those who need
not be exposed to carefully keep away from
places where, while doing no good they would
be liable to catch the infection.
	Hons. W. H. Hooper and George Q. Can-
non arrived home from Washington, D. C. on
the 15th ult., just too late for their arrival to be
noticed in our last issue. They had passed
through a winter of much excitement in Wash-
ington over Utah affairs, and had been able to
prevent the passage of the special legislation
directed against this Territory. They were
received at Ogden by a large number of their
constituents, with a special train and music
from this city; and welcomed home in the
warmest manner.
	A morning newspaper published in this
city, which gives its attention to abusing
“Mormonism” and “woman's rights,” cham-
pions. some of the worst places in town, and
advocates anything and everything that would
increase here the evils so long fought against
by this community, devoted a little editorial
space lately to the “Woman's Journal” pub-
lished in Boston, confounding it with the
Exponent, and atiempting to berate both.
We merely allude to the fact, as an evidence
of how poorly informed some of these editorial
persons are, relative to matters of which they
treat so flippantly.
	The subject of home manufactures, of
various kinds is again receiving considerable
attention.  This is a matter which the ladies
of the Relief and Retrenchment Societies can
materially aid by precept and example, by
encouraging the wearing of home manufactured
fabrics instead of those imported, by throwing
an influence in the way of wool being woven
up in the Territory instead ef being exported,
and many other ways. And so far as the
fabrics woven in the factories in this Territory
have been exhibited at the last two Territorial
Fairs, they have compared favorably with any-
thing imported. We can all encourage home
manufactures, for all have influence, in a greater
or less degree; and in so doing we promote
prosperity, an increase of wealth to the com-
munity at large, and independence, which is
only truly enjoyed by a people when they are
self-sustaining.


﻿President Grant has subscribed $100
and Vice-President Wilson $50, to the
Greeley monument fund. How much will
be subscribed by the proprietors of Harper’s
Weekly, which helped to kill the over-
worked old man, is not yet announced.


“MANLY" OCCUPATIONS.
				  —
	Some individual has kindly prepared the
following, and  offers it as advice to young
men:
	The young man who has an ambition to
make a great noise in the world should learn
boiler-making.  He can make more noise
in that trade than anything else he can
engage in. 
	If he believes a man should “strike for
wages” he should learn blacksmithing—
especially if he is good at “blowing.”
	If he would embrace a profession in which
he can rise rapidly he should become an
aeronaut. He couldn’t find anything better
“for high.”	
	He certainly could do a staving (and per-
haps a starving) business at the cooper trade.
	If he believes in “measures, not men,” he
will embark in the tailoring business. 
	If the one great object of his life is to
make money, he should get a position in
the United States Mint.
	If he is a punctual sort of a chap, and
anxious to be “on time,” he should put his
hands to watchmaking.
	If he believes in the chief end of man to
have his business largely “felt,” why of
course he will become a hatter.
	If he wants to “get at the root of a thing”
he will become a dentist—although if he
does he will be often found “looking down
in the mouth.”
	If a man is a bungler, at his best, he
should become a physician—and then he
will have none of his bad work thrown upon
his hands. It is generally buried out of
sight, you know.
	Should he incline to high living, but pre-
fer a plain board, then the carpenter trade
will suit him. He can plane board enough
at that.
	If he is kneady and well-bred, he will be
right at home as a baker.
	He shouldn’t become a cigar-maker. If
he does, all his work will end in smoke.
	The young man who enjoys plenty of
company and is ever ready to scrape ac-
quaintance, will find the barber business a
congenial pursuit.
	The quickest way for him to ascend to the
top round of his calling is to become a hod-
carrier.		
	A very “grave” young man might flour-
ish as an undertaker.
	Don’t learn chair-making, for no matter
how well you please your customers, they
will sooner or later, get down on your work.
	And don’t become an umbrell-maker, for
their business is “used up." 
	If he would have his work touch the
heads of the nation, we know of no way he
could sooner accomplish such an object than
by making combs.
	The young man who would have the
fruits of his labor brought before the eyes of
the people will become an optician. The
work being easily seen through, cannot be
difficult to learn.
	Cannot some one prescribe as many good
pursuits suitable to the different tastes and
abilities of young women?


﻿Parsnip Wine.—Cut twelve pounds of
parsnips in slices and boil them in four gal-
lons of water till they become soft, then
press the liquor well out of them, run it
through a sieve and add to each gallon three
pounds of loaf sugar. Boil the whole three
quarters of an hour, and when, it is nearly
cold add a little yeast, let it stand ten days
in a tub stirring it every day from the bot-
tom, then put it into a cask for twelve
months. As it works over fill it up every
day.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


CORRESPONDENCE OF MISS SNOW.
				—
			Alexandria, Egypt, 	
				   February 9th, 1873. 
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	My Dear Lula :—I wrote you last in
Rome. We found very much in that
ancient capital of the world strikingly
interesting. The unremitting work of time
is nowhere more apparent than in the ruins
of that once magnificent city. The excava-
tions commenced at the expense of Napoleon
Third, and which are continued by the,
present government of Italy, have brought
to light, most astonishing specimens of
former grandeur and architectural skill.
Although much has been unburied, much
more remains, not only many feet beneath
the surface of the earth, but also below
spacious palaces and temples of compara-
tively modern structure, which have been
erected upon the ground formed by accumu-
lations through the lapse of ages, that
constituted a sepulchral arch over Rome of
the Caesars. While there, we were informed
of a design to remove those modern build-
ings in favor of perfecting the work of
excavation, and since leaving, have read a
published article announcing that the work
of demolishing had commenced.
But I must leave Rome for the present
and hasten on to Naples, where we arrived
in the evening of the 25th of January.
That is a city of pomp and beggary; in
fact, there, begging appeared to be more a
necessity than in Rome, where children
well clad, fat and apparently well fed,
seemed to beg for mere amusement, no
doubt being trained to the business. But
in Naples we saw poverty strikingly ap-
parent, and beggars clustered in droves.
Thousands of men, women and children
were on the side walks and in the streets,
lounging about as though they had no
abiding place; most of the men smoking
pipes and cigars—some of the women knit-
ting, others spinning flax in the most simple
manner, with a distaff from which they
drew the thread, and a bobbin or spool
which they twirled to give a twist to the
thread and then wound upon it.
	Many of these people have no resort for
the night only as they huddle into the
porches of the churches, on the steps, or
in niches in the corner of the street. Re-
turning rather late from Pompei one even-
ing, we saw groups of these houseless people
on steps leading into churches. They are
accustomed to this manner of existence, it
can hardly be called living. .Warm climates,
in which people easily exist, foster indo-
lence; and it is doubtful, whether any
inducements could prompt these people to
industry.
	President Smith and most of his party
ascended to the crater of Vesuvius, my ambi-
tion was satisfied with excellent views of
this natural wonder, at a lower point. Con-
tinually throwing out volumes of smoke,
and frequently ashes and stones, it is a
marvel that people residing near and at the
base of this fiery mountain can feel the
serenity they manifest.
	From Naples wo went by rail to Brindisi,
from there by steamer to Corfu, one of the
Grecian Isles thirty miles in length and
from one to fifteen in breadth. It seems
like a cluster of high hills and low dells,
mostly covered with olives and grape-vines,
with here and there a cypress. Varieties
of vegetables are growing in some parts,
also flax, which we saw nearly in bloom.
The Grecian women, living in the country,
are, many of them, at least, “beasts of bur-
den.” I never saw such gigantic bundles
[new column]
carried by human beings as these poor
women carry on their heads. In the city of
Corfu, as in most of the cities we have
visited, America and England are repre-
sented in the circles of fashion. The grand
promenade and public gardens were very
gay, especially in the afternoon of the Sun-
day we spent there.
	At 12 (midnight) of the 2nd of February
we embarked on the “Saturno,” an Austrian
steamer, and arrived in Alexandria the
morning of the 6th. We had a delightful
voyage, no one of our party was sea-sick;
the sea was calm, although the day before
and the day after our voyage were very
boisterous.
		SUNRISE ON THE MEDITERRANEAN.
		We mounted "Saturno's" deck to see
			A grand, magnificent scene,
		The rising sun in its majesty,
			Diffusing its golden sheen.

		A charming precursor first appear’d 
			In volumes of golden rays, 
		Increasing their splendor till all around
			The horizon seemed to blaze.

		Anon, the disk of the "king of day,”
			O’er the wat’ry main arose;
		Now upward and onward he makes his way,
			Till the canopy gaily glows.

		The morning is fine the air serene,
			And the sky above is clear, 
		Except where a tiny cloud
			Like a floating nymph appears.

		The sparkling waves of the sea below,
			The blazonry over head,
		The horizon wrap’d in a burning glow,
			A thrilling enchantment spread.
	No place that we have visited makes one
feel so thoroughly from home as Alexandria;
and in no place have we had more comfortable
quarters and better fare than here. Yet
the medley of nations, religious costumes,
colors, dialects, etc., etc., and the dilapi-
dated appearance of the city, constitute a
scenery at once interesting, ludicrous and
extremely amusing. But my sheet is full,
and I will leave Alexandria, with this intro-
duction, for a future opportunity. We go
to Cairo to-morrow
							E. R. Snow.
			Cairo, Egypt, Feb. 14, 1873.
Editor Exponent:—
	Dear Lula:—We are now in the
flourishing city of Cairo, but I will take
you back to Alexandria, to which I intro-
duced you in my last.
	Although the buildings generally, in that
once celebrated, antiquated city, are speci-
mens of age and decay, it contains some
respectable appearing ones, recently built
and owned by foreigners. Most of the
streets are unpaved, narrow and filthy.
Our hotel in Alexandria occupied a pleasant
position ; our rooms, on the third floor, front-
ing a public square eight or ten rods in width,
affording us full views of two streets and
four sidewalks, which were most of the
time thronged with people and animals.
From our balcony and windows, which
opend at full length, we had an excellent
opportunity for studying national peculi-
arities. The sight was at once intensely
amusing and interesting. It would be im-
possible to give more than a faint idea of
the strange varieties to be seen at a glance;
men, women and children of every shade of
complexion from the fairest blond to the
most glossy jet, with every variety of
feature, and in every imaginable costume.
In five minutes I counted twenty different
styles of covering on the heads of that
number of male bipeds, saying nothing of
those of the feminines. Mixed up with
pedestrians may be seen men, women and
children on donkeys, men on horses and
[new column]
mules, camels laden with enormous masses
of straw, grass, lumber, rock, and frequently
with a man on the top of the load; people pf
all classes in carriages and buggies,rudelycon-,
structed vehicles containing twenty persons
—men, women, and children; and to com-
plete the medley, dogs and monkeys.
	The most comically amusing practice, and
one of constant exhibition, is a person,
either Turk or Christian, on a donkey, with
a man or boy in gown or turban, running
in the rear, and with a stick punching or
striking the animal to quicken its speed.
Our highly respected cashier and inter-
preter, Brother P. A. Schettler, adopted this
fashionable style, but much to the regret of
Miss Little and myself, he disappointed us
of the gratification we anticipated in wit-
nessing the interesting and undignified
exhibition, by performing it clandestinely.
These Arab runners outstrip donkeys, and
equal horses at pretty good speed. It is
quite customary for people of consequence to
have one of these runners in front of their
carriage-horses, clearing the street before
them. President Smith and party rode out
this forenoon in two carriages, with a runner
in white gown and turban, in advance of
each. They felt themselves to be men of
authority—stick in hand, ordering people,
donkeys, etc., aside to clear a passage.
	On the streets, Americans, English,
French, Germans. Italians, Greeks, Turks,
Abyssinians, Jews, etc., etc., mix, and it is
impossible to discover from appearance
who feel most at home. Several of an
English party who crossed the Mediterra-
nian with us, have already adopted the
neat, little, Turkish cap, which, with
turban and gown, is the predominant style
in Egypt. In Alexandria, most of the hotel
servants appeared in no other. I had tried
to persuade some of the gentlemen of our
party that this costume might become them,
but, up to date, they have only donned the
cap—the gown is only yet in prospect.
	The custom of veiling the face, which is
much practised here by Egyptian women,
appears to be very inconvenient. I have
already seen thousands with only the eyes,
and sometimes the forehead exposed; some
with white veils and dressed in white;
others with black, and dressed in black, and
also in various colors; some very richly dad.
Most of the veils are short, made of thick
lace, and fastened across the face just below
the eyes. The outside garb, or covering,
nearly half-square in form, is laid over the
head, fastened under the chin, falling loosely
over the shoulders and reaches to the instep;
and frequently parting in front, exposes to
advantage a beautiful, rich underdress.
Walking in the public garden, seatedi n an
arbor, on the sidewalk, riding on a donkey
or in a carriage, every Turkish or Egyptian
woman you see, looks as though she had a
large shawl over her head. Considering
the outside appearance of the den-like
houses of the Arab Mahometans, it is very
surprising to see how neat they look. Many,
both men and women, dress in white—and
really white; their religion enjoins cleanli-
ness. They have fountains in front of their
Mosques, where the people wash before
prayer. We frequently see them washing
themselves beside the street, probably pre-
paratory to praying, for wherever a true
Mahometan is when the hour of prayer
arrives, he bows to service. We have seen
several where we were passing, bowing
their heads to the ground while their lips
moved as in silent devotion.
	But to Alexandria. The first morning after
our arrival, the early and frequent discharge
of cannon, reminded us of our “Fourth” and
“Twenty-Fourth.” On inquiring the cause,


we were informed the pilgrims were then
ascending the mountain in commemoration of
the Hegira, or flight of Mahomet, to Mecca.
It was also the time for the“Wailing for the
dead” in the Mahometan cemetery. Our cur-
iosity prompted us to walk a tiresome distance
through dust, filth, crowded streets, to the
place designated, where we found thousands
of people of all ages, and, with very few
exceptions, all in Mahometan costumes.
	The graves are covered with clay moulded
into a compact oval form, raised from one
to two feet above the surface, and many of
them surrounded with one, two and three
steps. Beside many of the graves were
those, apparently, who were relatives of
those interred, weeping, mourning or pray-
ing, while another class, with no mourning
sympathies whatever, was exhibiting the
greatest mirth; playing on rude instruments,
dancing-monkeys, buying, selling, eating,
drinking, smoking and having a gala time.
Not comprehending their words and doings,
the entire scene seemed to us a master-piece
of confusion. The throng was so dense it
was difficult to wend our way into the
midst sufficiently to gratify our curiosity,
which we did as soon as possible lest we
might be thought intrusive; and in coming
out, we were beset with importunities to
purchase all kinds of trinkets, bread, cakes,
figs, oranges, dates and many other things
altogether anonymus to us,; but it being
Sunday we were out without any “back-
sheesh” in our pockets—of course were not
prepared to trade.
	In Cairo we have visited a Turkish
Mosque, a Jewish Synagogue, a Greek
Church in which we were shown “where
Joseph, Mary and the Savior resided when
they fled from the wrath of Herod;” a Church
of the Copts, “Joseph’s Well,” which is two
hundred and fifty feet deep,the Pyramids and
“Sphynx,” the Museum of Antiquities, the
Gardens and Palace of Gizeh, which belong
of the Khedive, of whom we had a good view
as he passed us in his carriage while prom-
enading on his premies. He has four wives
and quite a number of children, four of
whom were married during the past month,
which has been a continued holiday festival!
Many thousands of lamps have gorgeously
illuminated, it is judged more than fifty
acres in extent, hung in arches, over gate-
ways, in arbors, most tastefully arranged in
every direction. Yesterday the festival
closed and many were engaged in remov-
ing the lamps and other costly ornaments.
During the festivities firing of cannon was
frequent, and the exhibitions of fireworks
in the centre of the illuminations wore ex-
ceedingly grand. Three of the recently
wedded, are sons; the daughter’s nuptials
were since our arrival. It is said that her
intended had seen her photograph previous
to marriage, but not her face. If that is so,
it is to be hoped he has met with no un-
favorable disappointment.  She has the
reputation of being very pretty and amiable.
On the 17th we leave Cairo for Suez.
					Eliza R. Snow.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


﻿LIBERTY is self government—not govern-
ment “by” ourselves merely, but govern-
ment “of” ourselves—strict and unrelent-
ing, healthful and cheerful.
	SCATTER flowers on the young maiden's
coffin, ye blooming friends!  Ye used to
bring flowers on her birthday feasts; she is
now celebrating the greatest of them, for
the bier is the cradle of heaven.
	LOCKMAN, the philosopher was once
asked, who had given him his first lesson
in wisdom.  “The blind,” he replied, who
do not move a step in advance until sure of
the ground with a stick.


WOMAN'S VOICE.
					—
Dear EXPONENT:—
	I wish to say a few words about the
women of Utah and others. It is an old and
threadbare story, I know, but I still wish to
have my “say.” I believe it is a fact, well
demonstrated, that women were created for
wives and mothers; and I believe, too, that
most women prefer filling the measure of
their creation. I do not believe that women
naturally desire to become the creatures
that some men would make of them; yet,
man made in the image of his Creator,
wearing the form of Godliness though sadly
devoid of its attributes, will deceive, humil-
iate and degrade woman; and her sister
woman will pass her by with contempt,
stinging her with scorn, and sinking her
still deeper in dagredation and misery.
And such persons will raise their hands in
pious horror at the thought of the man who
dares to take two or more at a time, as law-
ful wives, and raises them to a station of
respect and honor. God ordained that
woman should marry; why should she be
restricted in the choice of a husband, or get
none at all? If bachelors will not marry,
women must needs choose from the married
men, or remain single. We are told again
and again that women are slaves; perhaps
they are, but how is it, where is it, and
why is it? Surely the statement cannot be
considered true of us, since we chose to
follow our husbands to a new country, a
thousand miles from “civilization,” to brave
the hardships and perils of a journey across
the great American desert, where the roads
were new, and ox teams instead of palace
cars were our means of conveyance!
	True, for years, and while the hardy
pioneer was tilling the soil or working in
the canyon, what kind of a wife must it
have been who would not have done her
best to help provide for the comfort of home?
Was this slavery? Perhaps it was; but the
master was stern necessity.   Spinning,
weaving, and toiling in every possible way
for herself and loved ones, were the labors
of the day; but when they were over, she
could commend her soul to God, and sleep
peacefully, to awaken refreshed for the bur-
den and toils of another day.   All this was
done cheerfully and happily; and I say, God
bless the wives and mothers who have
striven thus nobly for their husbands and
little ones, and for the Kingdom of God!
Their names will be handed down to future
generations and their children will rise up
and call them blessed.
	Sensation writers have tried to represent
them as degraded and oppressed. If so,
who were their oppressors? Not the hus-
bands and fathers, whose portion was
poverty, in which the wives and daughters
shared alike its toil and inconveniences.
Often the cry has been raised that the
women of Utah could not escape from these 
valleys. Had our “sympathizers!” come
here as we did they would not have been
anxious to have tried to leave in like man-
ner. The prospect was not tempting. But
now that their way is open, the opportunity
does not seem to be improved by many.
	The women of Utah, “fallen and de-
graded!” They have been cherished and
respected, and woe to the villains who would
seek to degrade them! In Church affairs
they have walked side by side with their
husbands, and their votes have often been
called for in matters of importance. They
have held the same power as their husbands,
to sustain the authorities. 
Bloated demagogues, standing in high
places, have often called a blush to the cheeks
of modesty; but thank God, they were a min-
[new column]
ority, and their influence  appears as if
becoming “beautifully less.” Their cow-
ardly insults and blatant demonstrations of
power have carried a weight among
strangers and caused the men of Utah to be
misjudged; but civilization is opening its
way through our country and that matter
with many others will have a fair chance
for investigation.	
	The women of Utah have never clam-
ored for rights. They have always found
as many rights as they have been capable
of attending to, and as great tasks in their
own sphere as they have had strength to
accomplish. Unasked for, but not unwel-
come, came to them the elective franchise,
by which they were called upon to aid
in choosing their rulers, which though a
matter of importance, they had been well
contented to leave in other hands. But
since this privilege has been lawfully
granted them, they seek to manifest their
appreciation in modesty and union, and
to perform the duties that are placed before
them with care and forethought, and again I
say, God bless the noble women of Utah!
								M. J. T
Provo. 
			  —
			Provo, March 3rd, 1873.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	As so much is being said at this time
concerning the marriage relations of the
Saints, I feel desirous of taking part in the
grand controversy,
	I am not a theorist, but one who has had
quite an experience during a period of
fifteen years, and feel pleasure in testifying
that when people will be guided by the
Spirit of Truth and the word of the Lord,
that no state or condition of society is so
well calculated to foster purity and social
advancement, as the doctrine and practice
of plurality of wives; and I am certain, that
if I am always privileged to continue in the
faithful discharge of the duties and obliga-
tions connected therewith, that I shall be
numbered among the redeemed of all
nations. As one of our poets has lately
sung:
	“Hearts and homes in union dwelling
		All the wives of one good man; 
	Peace and love our bosoms swelling
		Guided by the Gospel plan;
	In one home we raise our offspring
		In the nurture of the Lord; 
	Of the Spirit’s power partaking
		And the Gospel’s power restored.,
	Praying the Lord to bless you in your
truly womanly efforts to overcome evil and
establish righteousness I remain yours truly,
							A. M. Jones.


THE ladies of Boston propose to give Rev.
George MacDonald a testimonial.
	THE King of Holland has disinherited
his eldest son.
	THE French Assembly has made intoxi-
cation a penal offense. 
	THE Queen of England has subscribed
$800 to the fund in aid of the survivors of
the North-fleet disaster.


﻿MANY flowers open to the sun, but only
one follows him in his course.  Heart, be
thou the Sun-flower; be not only open to
thy God, but obey Him too. 	
	IF love’s sunshine fall upon the bare, cold
rocks, in the restless sea of desolate human
life, flowers will spring up even there, and
fill the place with gladness.
	Make up your mind what you intend to
do.  Sit down and count the cost.  Do not
undertake more than you can perform. It is
unwise to commence work that you are not
able to finish in due season.


﻿WOMAN'S  EXPONENT.


A WARNING TO YOUNG LADIES.
					 —
	A  lesson and a warning to the young
ladies of Utah contained in the scandalous
conduct of the man Anderson, who was
arrested here a few days ago, and the folly
of the girl Mitchel, who fell into his snares.
Flattered by his notice, and deceived by his
plausible tongue and “gentlemanly” man-
ners, the girl left her home in Salt Lake
City and came to Ogden, where her sorrow-
ing mother discovered her with her seducer,
after a long search, aided by the police.
Anderson promised to marry the girl, but
subsequently tried to decamp without ful-
filling his contract.   He was arrested and
taken back to Salt Lake, where, to avoid
the punishment of the law he married the
victim of his wiles.
	How many times have our young ladies
been cautioned about associating with
strangers, and warned against the arts of
polite but designing scoundrels? What
respectable girl would think it any consola-
tion to be wedded to a man who only con-
sented to marriage through the force of law
and the fear of punishment? And when
will the odium of the disgrace attending
such a scandal be chased away from the
guilty parties? 
	Our girls are brought up in innocence of
the vices and corruptions of the great world,
and enjoy great freedom in companionship
with their juvenile friends, male and female.
They think no evil of those in whose com-
pany they may be cast, and unless willing to
heed the advice of their friends, are apt to
be led astray. The only path of safety for
them is to avoid the intimate society of all
persons about whom they know little or
nothing, and associate only with those in
whom their parents, guardians, or trusted
friends, have confidence. By taking this
course many will save themlselves from
shame, and, perhaps, from present and
everlasting ruin,—“Ogden Junction.”


﻿The Law of True Marriage.—“Wher-
ever,” says Gail Hamilton, “man pays
reverence to woman—wherever any man
feels the influence of woman, purifying,
chastening, abashing, strengthening him
against temptation, shielding him from evil,
ministering to his self respect, medicining
his weariness, peopling his solitude, winning
him from sordid prizes, enlivening his
monotonous days with mirth, or fancy, or
wit, flashing Heaven upon his earth, and
mellowing it all for spiritual—there is the
element for marriage. Wherever woman
pays reverence to man—wherever any
woman rejoices in the strength of any man,
feels him to be God’s agent upholding her
weakness, confirming her purpose and
crowning her power wherever he reveals
himself to her, just, upright, inflexible, yet
tolerant, merciful, benignant, not unruffled
perhaps, but not overcome by the turbulence
and responding to all her gentleness, help-
ing her to hold her soul steadfast in right;
to stand firm against the encroachment of
frivolity, vanity, impatience, fatigue and
discouragement; helping to preserve her
good nature, to develop her energy, to con
solidate her thoughts, to utilize her benevo-
lence, to exalt and illuminate her life—
there is the essence of marriage. Its love
is founded on respect, and increases self-
respect at the very moment of merging self
in another. Its love is mutual, equally giv-
ing and receiving at every instance of action.
There is neither dependence, but inter-
dependence. You cannot, weaken its bond;
distance cannot sunder them; it is a love
which vanquishes the grave and transfigures
death itself into life.

			WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
			A UTAH LADIES' JOURNAL.	
	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.
	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.
	Who are so well able to speak for the women of 
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"
	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of Woman's Exponent, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.
	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.
	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.
	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In Woman's Exponent a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R. Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.
	Woman's Exponent will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.
	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year, 		$2.00	1 Copy six months, 	$1.00
10 Copies one year,     18.00	10 Copies six months,   9.00
	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a 
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.
	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers Woman's Exponent, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

VOL. I. 		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH,  APRIL 15, 1873.		No. 22﻿


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	POPE PIUS Nono has been very ill the
past week. He is old, and his death may
occur at any time.
	THE LATEST agony in New York journal-
ism is a daily illustrated newspaper; and the
wood cuts show all the evidences of being
hurriedly got up.
	THE SECOND Adventists of Groton, Ver-
mout, fixed upon April 12, of this year, as
“the last great day;” but as it wasn’t, they
will have to refix the date.
	INDIANA proposes another “new de-
parture,” by allowing women the suffrage.
Should the proposition succeed where will
Massachusetts progress be?
	A DAUGHTER of President Thiers of the
French Republic, died at San Francisco
during the last week of March, and the
remains were taken to France for inter-
ment.
	AN INDIANA parson determined that his
discourses shall not be wanting in both
rhyme and reason, delivers them in rhyme.
His congregation should escape future pun-
ishment after having to endure such present
torture.
	MICHIGAN UNIVERSITY has graduated
twelve lady physicians and two lady law-
yers this year; and yet some of the New
England States querulously question wo-
man’s ability to practice either medicine or
law. The West keeps in the front!
	WILL ANYTHING frighten fashionable
ladies out of purchasing and wearing false
hair? Here is another “ghost” from Cali-
fornia:	“Somebody sold a dealer in human
hair some hair that had been cut from the
head of a small-pox patient, and the dealer
took the disesse and died.”	
	KENTUCKY HAS a town named Elizabeth,
with a population of 2,500, which spends
$200 a day in whisky; $73,000 a year for
that twenty-five hundred souls, male and
female, infant, youth, adult and aged, to
swallow some ninety thousand gallons of
liquid poison! Very probably they think
themselves temperate people, too! 
	AND NOW word comes from Minnesota
that the “foreman” of one of the leading
newspaper offices in that State is a woman.
There are people still who say women are
not competent to become printers; but the
world has never been rid of that kind of
people from whom even practical elucida-
tion will not remove such unbelief.
	NEW JERSEY legislative corruption bids
fair to outrank oven Congressional Credit
Mobilierism and New York legislative
venality. The question is not, did the leg-
islators sell out to railway magnates; but
how much was paid for them. As the
people elect their representatives they may
thank themselves if they are twice so
afflicted with the same men.
	THERE HAS been a very general outcry
through the press against the action of the
late Congress in passing the increased salary
bill, and making it retroactive so that each
member could draw the advance for the two
back years; and in some instances members
of Congress that voted for it have been
asked to resign. The feeling appears to be,
not that the country should pay more for its
legislation, but that it could do with a great
deal less of the kind of legislation it has
been favored with for some time. It is held,
too, that no act of the last Congress became
it half so well as that of dying.
	THE WOMAN Suffragists of California
were in convention in San Francisco last
week, but the brief telegraphic news re-
ceived concerning it by the daily papers,
contained so little information on the subject
that we must wait for due course of mail to
bring a fuller report.
	IT MAY be considered sad to record that
a number of women assisted, lately, at a
case of “lynching” at Fort Erie, Canada;
yet the foremost in deeds of daring among
the Paris Communists were women. It only
shows how the gentleness of the sex can be
turned to fierceness by association with
rough and bloodthirsty men, or by famili-
arity with scenes of violence.
	DIVORCE, WHICH was at one time deemed
cause for painful reproach has now become
fashionable, and the lady of the period can
meet with the utmost nonchalance the former
husband, while she hangs on the arm of
the present one. Our grand-parents looked
at things differently; but they had a kind
of old-fashioned faith in honor, honesty,
esteem and love, that is little admired now.
	OHIO HAS a Farmer’s Club for Women,”
the members of which meet regularly to
discuss practical questions applicable to farm
life; such as making bread, best methods of
curing fruit, how to make and keep pickles,
both sour and sweet, how to clean house
with the least confusion, least destruction
to furniture and most expedition, and the
like. This, certainly, is more becoming and
more profitable than the idle gossiping
which is fashionable.
	MISS PHOEBE Cousins, who was admitted
to the bar as a practicing lawyer in Salt
Lake City, as she could not secure such
recognition in St. Louis, is an applicant for
postmistress of the latter city, a Washing-
ton paper says. Why should she not be
successful? Unless, indeed, it may be
shown that her services in electioneering
last Fall were not so valuable as that of
some men whose persuasive powers, or
whisky expenditures, were more potent
with the “sovereigns” on election day.
	DR. NEWMAN, Chaplain of the United
States Senate and noted as coming to Salt
Lake to shear but going away with but little
fleece, having been appointed Inspector of
Consulates at a handsome salary and ex-
penses paid, was authorized to take a clerk
with him at $3,500 a year; and knowing
that Mrs. Newman would also like to travel,
sent her name in as clerk. This was
keeping the place in the family, which
might as well draw the money as there will
be little, if any, work to do. Dr. Newman,
however, is not on general principles, an
advocate of woman’s rights; only as far as
Mrs. Newman’s drawing her salary may go
he possibly is.
	PRESIDENT GRANT has appointed Mrs.
Cunningham postmistress of Provo, Utah,
and we understand the choice is acceptable
to the people, as the lady performed the duties
of the oflice very efficiently for some time
prior to the death of her late husband, who
was postmaster. In connection with post-
offices, we might say, while applauding the
commissioning of Mrs. Cunningham, that it
would seem a little singular anywhere else
than in Utah that all or nearly all the im-
portant post offices, where any pecuniary
benefit is derivable, are placed in the hands
of comparatively recent settlers in the Ter-
ritory; while the old settlers have the
chance to run post offices where the pay
does not cover the expenses, and the work,
consequently, has to be done “free, gratis,
for nothing.” We say this might seem
strange anywhere else than in Utah, but
here, impartiality, or old settlers receiving
paying offices, would be matter for surprise.
	HORACE GREELEY soon followed to the
tomb the woman who had been his partner
in life so long; and now Mrs. James Gordon
Bennett, wife of the other great New York
editor, follows her husband. She died
recently in Germany, and of her an eastern
paper say:	“She	 was formerly Henrietta
Agnes Crean, who kept a small hosiery and
fancy goods store in William street, New
York, when lower Broadway was still a
place of residence. She was a bright, viva-
cious woman, of large frame and great
vitality. At the time of her marriage, which
occurred at St. Peter’s church, in Barclay
street, on the 6th of June, 1840, Mr. Ben-
nett made the occasion conspicuous in the
columns of his newspaper, and afterward
faithfully recorded the birth of his children
in the same manner. Four children were
born to them, two of whom, a son and a
daughter, survive. Mrs. Bennett at one
time wrote a number of letters from Europe
for the ‘Herald.’ and they were distin-
guished by much vivacity and dash.”
	THERE HAS been a surfeit of horrors
during the last two weeks. The Atlantic
steamship, from Liverpool for New York,
with over a thousand souls on board, went
ashore on the coast of Nova Scotia, some
twenty miles from Halifax, and nearly six
hundred of the number were drowned or
dashed to pieces. About two hundred
were instantaneously killed, crushed or
mangled, by a boom breaking loose and
sweeping over the decks; hundreds were
drowned in their beds; many more in their
efforts to escape; while numbers died of cold,
exhaustion, and exposure in the rigging.
One lady, after hanging to the rigging for
six hours, partly sustained by one of the
officers, died still holding on with a death
grip, her clothing nearly torn and washed
from her person, and the jeweled rings that
glittered on her tender fingers, shining with
mocking glare on the hand of death. What
a fearful sight must have been presented!
And not a woman or child escaped; they all
perished; men, only, finding their way to
shore. A mere contemplation in fancy of
the awful scene is sickening; but what must
it have been for the fated participants to
pass through, before death kindly came to
their relief! Since then heavy floods in the
east have done great damage; destructive
conflagrations have devoured property and
life; and disasters have been of almost daily
occurrence. These are signs of the times,
if the world would but open its eyes to note
their intent.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


LEFT ALONE AT EIGHTY.

			BY ALICE ROBBINS.

	What did you say, dear, breakfast?
		Somehow I’ve slept too late;
	You are very kind, dear Effie,
		Go, tell them not to wait;
	I’ll dress as quick as ever I can,
		My old hands tremble sore,
	And Polly, who used to help, dear heart!
		Lies t’otter side o’ the door,

	Put up the old pipe, deary.
		I couldn’t smoke to-day;
	I’m sort o’dazed and frightened,
		And don’t know what to say.
	It’s lonesome in the house, here,
		And lonesome out o’ door—
	I never knew what lonesome meant,
		In all my life, before.

	The bees go humming, the whole day long,
		And the first June rose has blown,
	And I am eighty, dear Lord, to-day,
		Too old to be left alonel
	Oh, heart of love! so still and cold,
		Oh, precious lips ! so white—
	For the first sad hours in sixty years,
		You were out of my reach, last night

	You’ve cut the flower. You’re very kind.
		She rooted it last May.
	It was only a slip; I pulled the rose,
		And throw the stem away;
	But she, sweet thrifty soul, bent down,
		And pla[-]ted it where she stood,
	“Dear, maybe the flowers are living,” she said,
		“Asleep, in this bit of wood.”

	I can’t rest, deary—I cannot rest;
		Let the old man have his will.
	And wander from porch to garden post—
		The house is so deathly still;
	Wander, and long for a sight of the gate
		She has left ajar for me—
	We had got so used to each other, dear,
		So used to each other, you see.

	Sixty years, and so wise and good,
		She made me a better man,
	From the moment I kissed her fair young face,
		And our lover’s life began.
	And seven fine boys she has given me,
		And out of the seven, not one
	But the noblest father in all the land
		Would be proud to call his son.

	O, well, dear Lord, I’ll be patient,
		But I feel sore broken up;
	At eighty year, it’s an awesome thing
		To drain such a bitter cup
	I know, there’s Joseph and John and Hall,
		And four good men beside,
	But a hundred sons couldn’t be to me
		Like the woman I made my bride.

	My little Polly; so bright and fair!
		So winsome and good and sweet!
	She had roses twined in her sunny hair.
		White shoes on her dainty feet;
	And I held her hand—Was it yesterday
		That we stood up to be wed ?
	And—No, I remember, I’m eighty to-day,
		And my dear wife, Polly, is dead.


﻿F. R. SOCIETY REPORTS.
	
	Sister Priscilla Evans, Secretary of the
Spanish Fork Relief Society, writes as fol-
lows:
	The Spanish Fork Relief Society, was
organized September 14th, 1857, in 4 wards.
Mrs. Rhoda Snell, President of the first and
Mrs. Lucretia Gay, President of the second
ward. We have had opportunities in the
last 16 years of doing much good, in help-
ing the poor, and assisting to build up this
Kingdom. I would especially mention the
time of the Echo kanyon expedition; when
among other things, we were called upon for
lariats. Some of our faithful mothers
worked day and night, carding and spin-
ning cattle’s hair for that purpose; they
made and brought in twenty-two lariats.
I don’t know but some of our daughters
would blush to read of such a thing in this
enlightened age; but, why should they?
I consider every trial we pass through in
this Kingdom, if done with an eye single to
its progress or defence, will give us experi-
ence and help to bring us conquerors
through, if we are faithful to the end. I
find in looking over the old accounts—
donated to the poor at different times, $1,227,
to the Emigrant fund and building of the
Temple, $294, to the meeting-house and
donations of labor $841; total, $2,362.
Through the exertions of a few, a great deal
can be accomplished.
	Sister Snell worked faithfully for 14 
years as President of 1st Ward Society,
until sickness in her family compelled her
to resign her office. Sister Gay also resigned,
on account of age and a large family to
attend to; but they are always ready with
their good counsel, and to assist when there
is a call. Mrs. Letitia A. Davis, (deceased,)
acted as Counselor to sister Gay until her
resignation, when sister Davis was called to
to the Presidency, which office she filled
honorably until ill health caused her also to
resign. The Spanish Fork F. R. Society
was reorganized in 1871 into 1st and 2nd
Districts, Mrs. C. A. Williams, President;
Mrs. Ruth Davis, and Mrs. J. Warren, 
Counselors, Mrs. Thirza Thurber, Treasurer
of 1st District. Since that time we have
donated to the poor, emigration and other
funds $225, and have in Co-operative shares,
cash and goods, $130. A meeting was called
March 11th, at which it was proposed that
we dispense with the word “Female” in
the name of the Society, which was seconded
and carried. It was also proposed that we
continue our meetings, and have a meeting
for prayer and testimony every Fast-day in
the afternoon. Your valuable paper is re-
ceived and appreciated by many. Success
to it, and every other herald of truth.

	From Providence, Cache County, we re-
ceive the following:
	Our Society was organized, April 27th,
1868. From that time, we have prospered,
a good spirit prevailing among the members.
It is a satisfaction to us to know that we
may lighten the burdens of our brethren in
helping to roll forth the Latter-day work.
Last year the sisters here donated to help to
gather the poor from the nations, in cash,
stock and grain, to the amount of $171:50.
We have also assisted our brethren towards
erecting a fine hall that is being built, and
donated means for the benefit of the Sabbath
school; have shares in the Co-operative
store, and the wants of the poor among us
have been supplied. The teachers visit the
members every month and find out the con-
ditions and feelings, and see that none
suffer for the common necessaries of life.
We hold meetings every month, and fre-
quently we have sewing meetings at private
houses, making clothes for the poor. At a
meeting held last October, the word “Fe-
male” was dropped from the name of our
Society by a unanimous vote.
	Mrs. Margaret Mathews, Prest.
	Mrs. Louisa Hammond, 	}	
	Mrs. Margaret Fife 		} Counselors
	Mrs. Emily Maddison, Secretary.

	The following letter comes to hand, from
Mrs. Caroline A. Jackson, Secretary of the
Relief Society of St. George, Washington
County:
	At a meeting in November our branch of
the Relief Society voted to drop the word
“Female” as recommended by Sister Eliza
R. Snow. During the past winter our
meetings have been well attended, and at
our first meeting in January 1873, President
Anna L. Ivins suggested to omit work and
substitute prayer and testimony as a fitting
beginning of the New Year, which was
done, and the spirit of the Lord was made
manifest in a remarkable manner.
	On the 14th of February, at a dinner
given to President Young and party, much
good instruction was given to the members
of the Society and a new interest was
awakened which has resulted in many new
members being added to our number, and a
prompt attendance of old members. We
feel honored in having our names enrolled
in this organization, and desire with our
sisters in the more favored “North,” to
help build up the Kingdom of God on the
earth.

	From South Cottonwood Ward, we have
received the following report:
	The Relief Society of this place is in a
flourishing condition, although there are
but few that attend our meetings. There
is a united desire with the sisters who are
faithful, to aid in building up the Kingdom
of God. At a recent meeting the word
“Female” passed into disuse with us in con-
nection with the Society’s name, in the
proper manner. We have additions to our
Society quite frequently; and have an inter-
est in almost every enterprise in this ward.
We are very much pleased with your inter-
esting and valuable paper, which a good
portion of the sisters take at present; and
we wish you prosperity in the enterprise.
	Mrs. Jenett Cahoon, President;
	Mrs. Lucy Bullock } Counselors;
	Mrs. Eliza Greene }
	Mrs. Ann E. Labrum, Secretary.
	Mrs. Francis Litson, Treasurer.


INFLUENCE OF A SMILE.—There is noth-
ing which makes the human face so
attractive as a bright smile. It breaks over
the countenance like the sun-light through
the rain clouds after a shower, and it enlivens
all upon whom it rests. A countenance,
ever so stolid and impassable and hard,
often becomes softened, vivacious and
charming under the influence of a smile.
There is something indescribable about a
smile, and a great deal of character is often
exhibited in it. No man or woman with a
really bad heart ever possessed an open,smil-
ing face, which brought peace and gladness
with it. Beware of the man who never
smiles, for “the motions of his spirit are as
dull as night, and his affections are as dark
as Erebus.”
	The smile of tenderness and affection
from the mother’s face greets us upon our
entry on the journey of life, and the recol-
lections of it lingers sweetly in the heart
while reason lasts. The smile of encourage-
ment in distress or sickness, is often worth
more than money or medicines. In the
domestic circle it lightens the burden of
life, and makes the hardest lot cheerful and
endurable. It is the offspring of Hope, and
the pledge of amity between friends. A
broad smile was never inspired by a bitter
thought or an uncharitable desire. In the
face where smiles prevail, happiness reigns
and a gentle human charity dwells in the
bosom of such. A smile is the language of
kindness and forbearance, and it emanates
only from the soul in harmony with nature,
and at peace with all the world.


﻿Corn is cheaper than wood for fuel in
Iowa lime kilns.
	That was a beautiful idea in the mind of
a little girl, who, on beholding a rose-bush,
on the topmost stem of which a rose was
fading, whilst below and around it three
beautiful crimson buds were just unfolding
their charms, at once and earnestly ex-
claimed to her brother: “See William these
little buds have awakend in time to kiss
their mother before she dies.”


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Woman was created for a helpmate for
man, and while it is his duty to provide for
and protect her, it is no less her duty to
assist him in all the minor affairs of life.
Her province is home; there she should
reign supreme, her busy steps never flag-
ging, her watchful eye never wearying;
the welfare of her children, the happiness of
her husband, her constant care. She should
strive to make her home the most delight-
ful place on earth to him, that when the
toils of the day are ended, and he seeks it
for repose, he may find in it his cares light-
ened, his sorrows soothed; that he may go
forth again to the contest with renewed
vigor and with cheerfulness and encourage-
ment in his heart.
	Domestic duties, and even, as is often the
case, household drudgery must necessarily
fall to her lot, and though she does not
shrink from the performance of these, there
are those of a different kind which have
no less claim upon her. God has given her
a mind, has bestowed upon her intellect,
and it is her duty to cultivate it—it is a
religious duty, for it was given her for a
noble, purpose that she might be her hus-
band’s pride, a fit instructress of her child-
ren, an ornament in her home. She should
not suffer the common details of everyday
life to render her manners coarse, or sour
her temper, but always strive to be mild,
refined, and polite.
	But woman—“the weaker vessel”—frail,
fragile woman, does she not need encourage-
ment and assistance? Is her life, though
spent in obscurity, exempt from trials,
snares, and temptations. Are there no
rough places in her pathway, no difficulties
to surmount?
	Her task is great, very great, and while
she earnestly tries to perform it, should she
not be strengthened and encouraged by words
and tones of affection and approval? This
is due her. If her husband desires a plea-
sant home he should prove to her that he
can appreciate one. If she must soothe his
troubled spirit let him not be impatient
should he sometimes detect a cloud upon
her brow; let him remember that poor
human nature connot always bask in the
sunshine, and that one cheering, loving
word from him might dispel the gloom
which rests upon her spirits. He desires
her to be delicate and modest, refined and
elegant, mild and affectionate, then let him
not shock her by coarse and unkind manners
and profane language.—“Magazine.”
	A cure for morbid thoughts.—Eat
mush and milk for supper, go to bed at nine
o’clock in the evening, rise at six in the
morning, and walk three miles every day.
	Sick Headache.—This is attended
with nausea and sometimes vomiting, and
is attributed to a disordered liver. Take
frequent sips of warm water until the
nausea subsides, and then occasional sips of
cold water.
	Pearl Barley, with water, simmered
to a jelly, is a valuable food for infants.
Strain all seeds, etc., before it gets cold,
Mix with due quantity of milk, or with-
out milk if preferred, and give it warm.
This is a happy medium between oatmeal
and arrowroot.
	Cleaning.—All saucepans, copper or
otherwise, and all bright tins, dish covers,
etc., ought if possible to be cleaned every
week. Spanish whiting and sweet oil,
made into a paste, rubbed on and allowed
to stand a little, then wiped off, and the
things polished with a leather and dry
whiting, will make tins and dish covers
look very nice.
	Whitening Ivory. — An, approved
method of whitening ivory that has turned
yellow, consists in heating a thin chalk paste
in a vessel over a fire, and then immersing
the ivory and leaving it until it has reached
the proper degree of whiteness. It is then
to be taken out, dried and polished off.
This method is preferred by a German
writer to one that has been a good deal in
use, namely, placing the ivory in a saturated
solution of alum for an hour, and afterward
rubbing it off with a woolen cloth, and then
wrapping it in a linen one, in which it is to
be allowed to dry.
	To clean Paint.—Provide, a plate with
some of the best whiting to be had, and
have ready some clean warm water and a
flannel, which dip into the water and
squeeze nearly dry; then take as much
whiting as will adhere to it, apply it to the
painted surface, when a little rubbing will
instantly remove any dirt or grease. After
which, wash the part well with clean water,
rubbing it dry with a soft wash-leather.
Paint thus cleansed looks as well as when
first laid on, without any injury to the most
delicate colors. It is far better than using
soap, and does not require more than half
the time and labor.


﻿ THE GOOD SPIRIT.
				—
	The Prophet Joseph Smith told the people
to “get the Spirit of God, and keep it.”
What a sermon there is in these few words!
If we always had the Spirit of God, we
would never do a wrong thing; for that
Spirit leads into all truth and goodness.
When any trial or temptation besets us,
if we possess that Spirit, we are sure to
come off victorious; and have strength to
withstand all the shafts of the adversary
which are directed against our peace and
happiness. But how prone is human nature
to give way, and let some little trial bring
wretchedness for hours, and perhaps days,
when it is our duty to be happy all the
time. When we are unhappy ourselves,
everyone around us partakes of the same
feeling, more or less, and they feel bad too.
Consequently we see that it is a duty we
owe to our friends as well as ourselves to
keep the Spirit of God continually, which is
the spirit of peace. I think if we could
realize how much easier it is to keep that
Spirit, than to regain it after we have
grieved it from us, we would try harder
than we do to overcome evil; for I am sure ;
there is no one that enjoys being miserable
so well as being happy. There are two
things that we should seek and pray for,
namely, patience and charity. Without
these there can be no true happiness. Then
let us my young sisters, strive with our
might to keep the good Spirit always with
us, to help us to be Saints in deed as well as
in name, and set an example worthy of im-
itation.	
							M. F


﻿We are always complaining that our days
are few, and acting as though there would
be no end of them.
	Though a taste of pleasure may quicken
the relish of life, an unrestrained indulgence
is inevitable destruction.
	The odor of flowers is never so sweet and
strong as before a storm. Beautiful soul!
when the storm draws nigh thee, be a
flower.
	Our true acquistions lie only in our chari-
ties. We gain only as we give. There is
no beggar so destitute as he who can afford
nothing to his neighbors.


DELICACY IN CHILDREN.
				   —
	Dio Lewis relates a story of a wealthy
gentleman and lady whose family he was
called upon to attend. Several children had
been born to them, and each had lived to a
certain age and had then been taken away
by death. At the time when the aid of the
noted physician was required, their last two,
a son and a daughter, had reached the ap-
pointed age, at which all the former child-
ren of the house had been snatched away,
and the consumptive decline by which the
others had been carried off was irresistibly
and unmistakably fastening upon them.
The doctor entered, the house and spent
some time in watching the little invalids.
He then asked the mother if she would
allow him to take  the little girl into the
garden. She assented, and accordingly,
the child was carried out into the beautiful
garden which belonged to the rich home-
stead. “Which, of all the shrubs and
flowers do you think the most lovely?’,
enquired the physician of the child. After
careful consideration she selected a moss
rose bush in full bloom, as being, to her
mind, the most beautiful of all in the gar-
den. Then the Doctor asked the child if
she could not return to the house and tell
her mother he wanted a suit of her—the
child’s—clothes—One of every article that
she wore, he said to her. When the little
girl had told her mother, the latter went to
the Doctor to ascertain if the child had
understood aright; and when informed that
she had, did as requested, bringing a little
suit of her daughter’s clothes; shoes, stock-
ings, chemise, drawers, petticoats, dress,
pinafore and even to the “shaker” which
the child had worn in the garden. The
Doctor took the garments, and with them
dressed the rose-bush which the child had
chosen. Every thing except the shoes, he
made use of, cutting the stockings open and
fastening them to the bottom of the bush.
When this was done, “There,” said he, “I
will visit you again in two weeks madam.
Do not give the child any kind of medicine,
and let the bush remain as I leave it.”
	In a fortnight he called again, as he had
promised. Going with the mother to the
garden he undressed the rose-bush, which
was drooping and withered and almost life-
less.
	“See,” he said; but even now the anxious
mother did not understand the meaning of
the exemplification, but wanted to be told
still more plainly the cause of her children’s
malady. “Look at this bush,” said the
Doctor; “a little while longer being thus
clothed, and shut in from the air and sun,
it would have been dead. Now there is
still life remaining sufficient to build upon,
and have health restored by proper treat-
ment. It is the same with your children.”
And still the mother queried what she could
do for them. “Give them air,” said the
physician, “undress them, and let them
bask in the sunshine. But at first with
their heads covered.”
	His directions were followed, proper
screens being prepared in two different
corners of the garden, where the children
were daily undressed and placed for a season
to gambal in the sunlight, while the Smmer
lasted. And the remedy proved effectual,
both children commencing to convalesce at
once.
	This story, told here almost precisely  as
it was repeated to us a few days since,
we believe very many mothers would do
well to draw references from, both for their
own and their offspring's good.
