			SALT LAKE CITY, APRIL 15, 1873.


SPLINTERS.
					—
	The “Danbury News” wonders that “Epi-
zootic hats” have not yet appeared......Colo-
rado is going to send her ores to the Vienna
Exhibition......Senator Frelinghuysen, by
his course on the Utah bill, has managed to
make himself unpopular with the ladies
every where; a pleasing position for a gen-
tleman of refined taste(?)......A vicious colt,
in Kansas, bit a little girl the other day,
who died a few hours after......Japanese
children, between six and twelve years of
age, are to be compelled to attend school....
The bill emancipating slaves in Porto Rico
has passed the Spanish Assembly......It is
asserted that woman as a voter is an accom-
plished fact in England....The Woman
Suffragists of Michigan have asked the
Legislature to strike out the word “Male”
in the State Constitution wherever it re-
stricts the right of suffrage; and they believe
in the final success of their movement......
Hon. S. W. Hale, gallantly got out his own
team, after midnight, and carried home all
the lady passengers who came to Keene one
night, and he is now on the track for the
next Republican nomination for Governor.
......In Ireland every woman who pays any
sum as a poor-rate, has a vote in the election
of Poor Guardians......The women of Massa-
chusetts petition for a separate prison for
women; can there be women criminals
there, sufficient to justify such an act?.....
An exchange says the Beechers take to the
pulpit and to heresy as naturally as ducks
to water; Mrs. Isabella Beecher Hooker
contemplates devoting herself to the minis-
try......All habitual water drinkers were
recently ordered by a vigilance committee
to leave Pioche City......Caldwell is spoken
of as the “weak-eyed, red whiskered child
of corruption, from Kansas.”......It is twelve 
years since Madame Adeline Patti left this
country, and she contemplates returning.
......The will of Napoleon III is sworn at
under $600,000, left without reserve to the
Empress; the Prince Imperial having but
one bequest, the Imperial crown......The
coal famine in England has led to the dis-
covery that chalk can be used for fuel......It
is estimated that nine million wooden to-
bacco pipes are manufactured in this country
yearly, and it is presumable that there are
blockheads enough to use them up......The
same postmaster has acted at Grayson
Springs in Tennessee, for forty years......
Iowa boot-blacks resolve to charge by the
square foot for shining-up customers...... 
Rome’s reigning beauty for the moment, is
said to be an Austrian, Princess Furstem-
burg......A Troy paper asks, “Shall lunatics
be licensed as murderers?”......Not a single
member of General Grant’s Cabinet is now
a candidate for President......A Vermont
dog was heart-broken at the recent death of
a horse-belonging to its master......Mrs.
Mary Monoghan died recently in New York, 
aged 102 years......A little girl in New Jer- 
sey fell into a cistern the other day; and
although she was in the water fifteen 
minutes before being rescued, she was
revived and recovered after two hours ex-
ertions in her behalf......Mrs Stanton says
the aristocracy of sex is the worst in the
world......Little boys in Oregon prevent
their rocking horses from taking epizootic
by fastening bits of red flannel around their
necks......A daughter of Zachary Taylor,
late President of the United States, Mrs.
Wood has been in destitute circumstances,
but is now to receive a pension of $50 per 
month......A Kansas youth set fire to a
school-house, because his mother would not
allow him to go to the circus......A little
[new column]
western girl, who is very strictly kept by
her parents says she would like to be a
minister that she might “holler on Sun-
day.”......A number of students in the State
University of California lately made their
appearance in last spring straw-hats and
last year dusters......A man in the cars on
the first of April whispered to the conductor
that another man present had just recovered
from small-pox; there was a general scat-
tering, but it proved to be only an “April
Fool."......The 31st ult. was celebrated in
San Francisco by the Spiritualists as the
twenty-fifth anniversary of modern “Spirit-
ualism.”......California prides herself on
the reputation of her '49ers, and grieves
that some of her first missionaries are get-
ting into trouble......One contemporary, by
reading the editorials in another, has found
out that “pain and misery have driven
many a person to desperation.”......Virginia
school girls learn to swim.


THE EXPONENT. 
		    —
	Two numbers after the present will close the
first year of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT. So far
its success has been gratifying, the subscription,
list having grown encouragingly–through the
assistance of the friends of the paper in different
parts of the Territory. Yet it is desirable that
it should be still more widely read; and im-
provements which we have contemplated mak-
ing in it cannot be made without a still larger
list of subscribers. We would like to see the
printing of the EXPONENT done in an office
owned by and used for itself, supplied with all
the necessary appliances; where young women
could learn the “art preservative”—printing—
and qualify themselve for some other of the
duties and employments referred to by Presi-
dent Young at Conference. But a printing
office, to be self-sustaining, in connection with
the EXPONENT, would require to do more work
than this paper now demands, and a larger
amount of capital. We therefore ask the ladies
throughout the Territory to kindly use their
endeavors to send in clubs before the 1st of
June, for the second volume of the EXPONENT;
so that the paper may commence its second
year with increased patronage, in every sense
self-sustaining and capable of accomplishing
more good than it is at present; and with
brightened prospects for the future. A list of
premiums will be found on the last page of the
paper; and to that list we would add,
that any person, or Society, sending in
a hundred names, with two dollars a name,
will receive a sewing machine, valued at
seventy-five to ninety dollars, as we can make
arrangements. This we offer not as a prize,
but holding that those who exert themselves
in this respect should have their exertions sub-
stantially recognized, while we can procure
machines on more advantageous terms than
perhaps any of our subscribers could do.
	We would like to receive club-lists as early
as possible, so as to know the number of papers
to have printed, for it is not wise nor economic


policy to print off a large number on specula-
tion, and probably have them left on hand
—waste paper.


WORK FOR WOMEN-
			      —
	One of the most interesting and most im-
portant features of the late general Conference
was the many excellent instructive suggestions,
made by President Brigham Young, to the
mothers and daughters in Israel. He called
upon them, and especially upon the Relief
Societies, to assist in the people becoming self-
sustaining, and counseled them to save and teach
the children to save “paper-rags;” and thus
aid in supplying the paper-mill with material,
so that the paper used by the Latter-day Saints
might be made by themselves, thus keeping
at home the means expended in procuring it
abroad. He then touched upon the impro-
priety of large, healthy, able-bodied men, who
should be engaged in farming, quarrying, stone
cutting, and other suitable pursuits, being em-
ployed as type-setters, proof-readers, book-
binders, clerks in stores, tailors; and in all
such branches of labor, which were legiti-
mately work for women, and should be largely
performed by them. He wished the women
and girls among the Saints to be educated to do
all such work as belonged to women. He was
desirous of seeing the school books used by
children in Utah, made by the people here, and
this also should be done to a great extent by
the women. The female mind was naturally
better fitted for such pursuits than that of the
male. Generally speaking, girls would mem-
orize spelling, grammer, arithmetic and almost
anything taught in books, more readily than
boys. It was a mistake to have girls taught
nothing but to play the piano, and when tired
of that to go to reading novels; they should be
taught cleanliness, neatness of person, and the
duties of a house-wife; with such branches of
useful knowledge as might be desirable, as well
as music and other accomplishments; while
the useful and practical should always take
precedence, and their education should be
wisely directed in all things. The speaker
urged that measures be taken to provide means
by which the women and girls of Utah might
obtain an understanding of and make them-
selves useful in type-setting, book-making and
such other occupations as justly belonged to
them; and that they should take in hand to
furnish the schools and families of the Saints
with suitable, intelligent and truthful reading
matter; that the vast outside expenditure, for
the purchase of books, which were no better,
nor so good as many of the women of Zion
were capable of preparing, might be stopped.
The officers of the Relief Societies, and, after-
ward, all the sisters, who were interested in
the welfare of Zion, were called upon to testify
their willingness to sustain the proposed under-
taking by raising their right hands. A fair
vote was given; and unless the wives and
daughters of the Elders in Israel, prove them
selves less faithful to the trust reposed in them
than they have hitherto done—which, there is
no cause to apprehend—much good will be the
result of the suggested enterprise.
	President Young proves himself to be the
most genuine, impartial and practical “Wo-
man's Rights man” upon the American conti-
nent, as he has ever done; his counsels,
instructions and advice to women being always
directed towards their progess and advancement
in usefulness and the possession of valuable
knowledge. And as these facts are well under-
stood by the large majority of the women of
Utah, we look for a prompt response to this
direct call for united and energetic action on


their part, in furtherance of the great object
of the community becoming entirely self-sus-
taining.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HOME AFFAIRS.
	
	The Annual Conference was largely at-
tended and was a season of refreshing. Presi-
dent Brigham Young spoke several times,
giving much, excellent instruction and admon-
ition.
	A pleasing indication of Spring time anp
Spring weather is the sight of little maidens
culling the earliest blossoms which peep mod-
estly up from under the edge of fences and
along the brooklet’s, brink.
	Those who are called to form a settlement in
Arizona, south of the Colorada river, are mov-
ing to get there, some having started while
others will shortly do so. They expect to ren-
desvouz at Kanab and travel south from that
point together.
	The last letter received in this city from
President George A. Smith and the Palestine
party, up to the time of going to press, was
written from Jerusalem, where they had
arrived. Sister Eliza R. Snow had stood the
journey and was proving herself an excellent
horsewoman.
	In few places in this city can an hour or
two be more profitably or enjoyably spent
than in the Deseret Museum, Mr. J. W. Young
proprietor and Professor Barefoot superinten-
dent. The collection is large and varied, and a
wonderful amount of information can be
gained in it in a short time. 
	The Deseret Teachers’ Association held its
semi-annual meeting in this city during Con-
ference, and was largely attended; while the
programme of exercises elicited much valuable
information. An address by School Superin-
tendent R. L. Campbell reached us too late to
be given in this number of the Exponent, but
will appear in our next issue.
	The meeting of Sunday School Superinten-
dents, in this city, on Monday evening, April
7th, was exceedingly interesting, nearly, if not all,
the counties in the Territory being represented,
while a report was also handed in from Malad,
Oneida County, Idaho. From the reports of
the Superintendents this medium of good is
flourishing throughout the entire community.
Much interest is manifested in the schools by
parents and children, the average attendance
is large, and great good is resulting from the
labors of the superintendents and teachers of
the various Sabbath Schools. It is a noble
work, blessing those who teach as well as those
who are taught; and has the prayers and best
wishes of all who seek the welfare and growth
in grace and in sound principles of the rising
generation.


﻿LUCCA has taken out her preliminary nat-
uralization papers.
	OVER 1,000 American girls are now re-
ceiving their education in English schools.
	A female student of medicine out West,
wrote home to her friends for $28, “to buy a
man to cut up.”
	A LADY thinks it very strange that when-
ever she goes to balls with an escort, he al-
ways goes out between the dances to get
some cloves to chew.
	A PROJECT is on foot among the numerous
New York ladies for forming an insurance
company having only women for officers,
agents and policy holders.
	ETERNITY has no grey hairs. The flowers
fade, the heart withers, man grows old and
dies, but time writes no wrinkles in eternity.


﻿WHAT MOTHERS CAN DO
			       —
	To none is given greater responsibilities
than those resting on mothers in training
their children in the fear of the Lord. We
are all aware that when we receive these
precious darlings, from the hands of their
Maker, they are perfectly innocent and
pure. Now, mothers, we have these dear
little ones committed to our charge to mould
and fashion, as it were, to our own liking.
They receive their first impressions from
us, and how important it is that those early
impressions should be directed in the right
channel. We wish to see our children grow
up to be noble men and women, honorable,
virtuous and intelligent; we wish them to
be affable and kind in their feelings to each
other, and to all with whom they associate.
And it seems to me that we can have the
desires of our hearts with regard to our
children in a great measure, if we are faith-
ful in the discharge of our duties to them
during their young and tender years. If
we instil into their minds the importance
of being obedient, truthful, virtuous and
obliging, and are kind and affectionate to
them in all our acts, and thereby twine them
around us, so that they will feel that we are
their best friends and advisers, they will be
willing to listen to our counsel; and then
if they ever see that they have done any-
thing that has grieved their parents, they
will be sorry for it, and try in the future
to do better. We should not fail to instruct
them in the principles of faith, that as the
Savior, when he was upon the earth, loved
little children and took them in His arms,
and blessed them, He still loves them,
and loves to listen to their prayers. Our
children thus taught, when they are sick,
will call upon their parents, or the Elders
to lay their hands upon them that they may
recover. And so with all the principles of
the Gospel; they will grow into them, hav-
ing had them impressed upon their minds
in infancy, and they will not fight against
any of the commandments of God; but will
be like those two thousand young men
commanded by Helaman, that we read of in
the Book of Mormon, Alma, 26th chapter.
They had been taught faith in God by their
mothers, and they did not fear to go into
battle, for they had been taught that if they
did not doubt, God would deliver them.
	Not only can mothers mould the minds of
their children in relation to religious and
moral matters, but, according to the care,
thought and judgment they use for the
purpose of fashioning and developing the
physical organization, will depend very
much, the strength or weakness, good or ill
health of their offsprings during life.
	For the sake of their children, if not for
themselves, mothers are in duty bound to
learn as far as possible, and strictly observe
the laws of health. This subject will bear
much further consideration, and I hope to
see it largely treated upon in the EXPO-
NENT.		   A MOTHER IN ISRAEL.


﻿DUNLOP says that in all European litera-
ture there are not more than three hundred
distinct plots, and two hundred and fifty of
these are earlier than Christianity, and had
their origin in Asia. Almost all the news-
paper jokes have reached a venerable age;
the Irish bulls are Greek.
	NEVER give up when you are right. A
frown is only a muscular contraction, and
cannot last long. A laugh of derision is but
the modified bark of a cur. If you can
laugh out of good, or the good be laughed
out of you, you are weaker in intellect than
the fool, whose argument is guffaw and
whose logic is a sneer.


FACTS AND FIGURES.
			——
	A VINTON man brought sixty-one suits
in a justice court in one day.
	THERE is a widow in England who enjoys 
an income of $2,500,000 a year. 
	THE “Sunday Journal” of Toledo Ohio,
gives three of its columns to be used by the
Woman Suffrage Society of that city.
	AUSTRALIA proposes a free school system, 
and compulsory education, in the interest
of civilization and free institutions.
	NEVADA pays her male teachers $110:75
per month, and her female teachers $92:16.
Louisiana pays respectively $112 and $76.
	THE deepest sea-sounding ever taken
showed a depth of eight miles and three
quarters, double the height of the Andes.
	A BRAZILIAN savant has discovered that
men were on the banks of the Amazon
29,000 years ago.
	A THOUSAND American girls for whom
the schools on this side of the water are not
good enough are receiving their education
in France and Italy.	
	A COLLECTION of twenty five well-made
pins has been found in the subterranean
vaults of Thebes—showing that the modern
pin is only a re-invention. 
	A RECENT medical writer of good authority
says: “It is certain that in our times, among
our brain workers, there are 999 who do not
sleep enough to one who sleeps too much.
	A CEDAR Rapids woman has invented a
machine for making lace, consisting of 100
bobbins, and on this has manufactured hand-
kerchiefs and collars and lace equal to any
imported from Paris. 
	DANIAL Webster once said, “I well re-
member a marked difference between those
of my schoolmates who had, and those who
had not, access to newspapers. Other things
being equal, the first were always superior
to the latter in debate, composition; and
general intelligence.”
	A MIDDLING sized man would weigh as
much as 4,000 wrens, and as much as 10,000
locusts. The note of a locust can be heard
1-16th of a mile. If a man of moderate size
could make his note heard at a distance
proportionate in comparison to his size, his
voice would be heard at a distance of 1,000
miles.
	EX-Governor Merrill, J. P. Casady, John
A. Elliott, S. F. Spafford and ex- Lieut.
Gov. Gue, have formed a stock company
with a capital of $40,000, under the name
of State Printing Company located at Des
Moines. The object of this association is to
manufacture patent “insides” and “out-
sides” for the newspapers of Iowa, either
Republican, Democratic or neutral in poli-
tics.
	THE number of women who aspire to the
baccalaureate of belles letters, increases from
day to day in France. Madame Ribard,
wife of a young physician of Nantes, has
passed successfully the examinations for the
degree of Bachelor of Arts. After having
presented excellent written compositions
the young woman was questioned orally in
the presence of a large audience and passed,
after a series of interrogations, brilliantly
sustained.
	A TOWN meeting was to be held at Dan-
bury, Conn., recently, to decide whether or
not to license the sale of intoxicating
liquors. On the preceding day the ladies of
the town voted on the question in regular
form, with the following result:—For
license, 2, against license, 457. On the 
following day the men voted—for license,
452, against license, 487.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.﻿


﻿FEMALE PHYSICIANS.
			—
	At Steinway Hall last evening a very iutel-
ligent appearing audience assembled to
assist at the commencement exercises of the
Women’s Medical College of the New York
Infirmary. On the platform were several
well-known physicians of this city and other
distinguished citizens, among whom were
Judge John R. Brady and Nathaniel James,
Jr. Mr. Samuel Willetts, President of the
college, presided at the exercises, which,
after a voluntary on the organ by Mr. G. W.
Morgan, were opened with the conferring
of degrees of the following named graduates:
—Miss Abbie Luelle Fox, of Illinois; Miss
Charlotte Warner Ford, of New Jersey; Miss
Ellen Currie Leggett, of New York; Miss
Jeanette Taylor, of New Jersey; Miss Emily
Hellen Wells, of Pennsylvania, and Miss
Kate Cecilia Woodhull, of New York:	The
obligation was read to each of the graduates
by Mrs. Emily Blackwell, M. D., Secretary
of the Faculty; after which the diplomas,
were presented by Mr. Willetts.
	Prizes were then awarded as follows:—To
Miss Emily H. Wells, for the best report of
the medical clinique, “Nehmeyer’s Practice
of Medicine;” to Mrs. Sarah E. Crocker, for
the best report of the surgical clinique, a
splendid case of surgical instruments; to
the same for the best report of the eye and
ear clinique, a handsome opthalmoscope.
For report of the eye and ear clinique, Miss
E. Baldwin recived “honorable mention.”
	The valedictory was then read by Miss
Emily H. Wells, after which Judge Brady
delivered one of his characteristic humorous
and philosophical addresses, with which the
exercises closed.—“New York Herald,
March 26.


﻿FAMILY MEETINGS.
		     —
	No gathering can be more sacred and yet
jubilant, or should be more conscientiously
recorded in the memories of the participants,
than the gathering together of the “family
circle,” after seasons of separation have
elapsed; changes having taken place since
the old, happy days when the parents were
young and the children small; new associa-
tions having been formed, new branches
woven in, individual cares and responsibili-
ties having been linked with the funda-
mental household cause, which is dear alike
to each member. There are they all—the
gray haired sire and grand-sire, with his
best of all God's gifts to him, the partner
of his life, sharer of his every joy and hope,
counsoler of his griefs, his faithful wife; who
hath borne for him that group of kindred
hearts. Brothers and sisters and their little
ones all congregated once more at “Father’s
house”—fair, typical display of grander
scenes, the blest, bright future in Eternal
Worlds.
	No timid spirit, fearing to be seen or
heard is there, nor restraint is felt; one is
not higher, greater than the rest, but all
alike are free and equal.
	With all the different casts of character
comprising a medley of entertaining lights,
with all the varying tasets and impulses,
imaginations, fancies, whims, and thoughts
inspired; there still exists with all, in all,
one natural sentiment uniting heart to
heart, and making each one’s interests the
common cause of all. Love, is that senti-
ment, love which reigns supreme within
the walls of every true home. For no
abode, however richly graced or proudly
ornamented or brilliantly illumined, should
bear the sacred name of home, where love
is not, or not considered chief of all attrac-


tions. And in large families, will be found
such vast varieties from nature’s store, that
the admiration, fervency of devotion and
warmth of affection in every soul, will find
some rich enchantment where, upon to feast.
	Some natures are best pleased with their
own like, as some thoughts call for echoes
of themselves; while others most adore
their opposites. But in the “family meet-
ing,” every desire for congeniality, of a
lofty nature, can be gratified. There will
be found the jocund, happy boy; the shy,
coy maiden; genius riper grown; and pleas-
ing innocence of tender years. There music,
mirth and joyous wit abound; and holy
aspirations are awakened and expressed;
a father’s prayer, a mother’s grateful smiles;
brothers’ and sistefs’ confidence exchanged;
aunts’ kindly sympathies for childish way-
wardness; uncles’ brave, manly generosity;
nephews and nieces—all are pets with all—
each is in some particular respect a favorite
with each, and every one delighted with
the “Family Meeting at Father’s House.
								  L.


﻿SABBATH SCHOOL TEACHERS.
				—
	It is often remarked that the teacher in
the Sabbath School, can do as much to help
in sustaining the cause of Truth as can the
missionary to foreign climes, preaching the
Gospel. The same sentiment was expressed
by one of the Elders who made an address
at the late Sabbath School Concert, held in
the old Tabernacle in this city, and a new
meaning came with the words as they were
uttered there. Women, as well as men,
may be efficient teachers. How many
young ladies, belonging to the Church of
Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, have felt
that if would be a joy and great gratifica-
tion to them if they could have the privilege
of proclaiming the glad tidings of Truth and
salvation to those who are afar off, held in
darkness and in ignorance! And some 
times has not the feeling arisen that the
greater power for obtaining a fulness of glory
had been bestowed upon man, while his
faithful helpmate was destined to follow in
the rear? Here we find all cause for envy
or jealousy in that respect obliterated.
Woman may teach, by word and by example,
the fullness of the Everlasting Gospel, as
plainly, as faithfully as man. What more
can be done for the gaining and maintaining
of the highest exaltation?
	But to truly teach a principle, it should be
practically understood by the teacher. Hence,
no pains should be spared by a Sabbath-
School teacher, by which acquaintance may
be formed with any branch of learning that
may be made beneficial to a pupil or a class.
It matters not how simple a truth may be, it
is worth impressing upon the mind of youth.
It is only by a grand compilation of divine
truths, each one small in itself, that great
wisdom is wrought; and no teacher of youth
should be above explaining the simplest
fact which might puzzle the young learner.
	There are greater responsibilities resting
upon teachers of Sabbath Schools than many
are aware. As their missions are no less
than those which the Elders are called to
perform in going forth to teach the nations
of the earth, and warn them to repent of
their sins, should their condemnation be less
if they allow themselves to be slack and
careless in performing their duties in the
Sabbath School, than that of the unfaithful
servant who is sent abroad? The Sabbath
School teacher should seek as constantly and
fervently, the spirit of faith and revelation
to direct his efforts, as should the mission-
ary upon the seas or in foreign lands.


THE SEX.
				—
	Mrs. Sarah E. White will hereafter
manage the Bloomfield, Mo., “Republican.”
	Charlotte Cushman’s health seems to be 
quite broken down. She has finally given
up her public engagements in the South and 
West.	
	IN Rockford, Illinois, Mrs. Wilder the
young wife of a Chicago capitalist, publicly
horse whipped John White, proprietor of
one of the principal hotels in that place.
	A WHITE marble statue of Queen Vic-
toria, weighing seven tons, has arrived at
Windsor Castle, and is to be placed in the
vestibule of the state apartments. The 
Queen is represented sitting on a chair, with
a dog lying by her side. 		
	THE author of a memmorial of Alice and 
Phoebe Cary, states that, looking through
the entire mass of their papers, she found
not a single word of their own, refering in 
any personal way to themselve. They 
shrunk from writing letters; they kept no 
diaries of thought or incident.	
	A LADY of Harwich, Mass., attended the
town-meeting, and presented her claims in
opposition to a road which was to pass 
through her land. As she ascended the 
platform, the house was hushed to silence, 
she was listened to with interest, and 
secured the vote in her favor. So much for 
Cape Cod. 
	IN several parts of England, there appear 
to have been established Mechanic’s Insti-
tutes for mutual improvement, in which
young women have the same privileges as
men, and in one of these, Miss Jewsbury,
an authoress, well esteemed, attends the
lectures to a class of young women, who 
take the name of mechanics also.
	CHARLAMANT has a revolutionary pen-
sioner, in the person of Mrs. Thirza Wing,
for services rendered by her late husband
in the campaign of 1777, at the time of
Burgoyne’s surrender at Saratoga. She
is now ninety-five years old, enjoys good
health and all her mental faculties. Her
pension was increased in 1868 from one-
fourth to full pension. 
	When Laplace, met the late Mrs. Somer- 
ville for the first time, he  said, in his lofty 
way, “Madam, there have been only three 
women who have undersood me—yourself,
Caroline Herschel and a Mrs. Creig, of whom 
I have never been able to learn anything.”
“I was Mrs. Creig,” said the modest little
woman. “So, then, there are only two of
you,” exclaimed the philosopher.
	A NUMBER of young women have united
to form the “New York Business Woman's 
Union,” and are now occupying a large house,
at No. 222 Madison Street. The design of the
association is to secure good lodging accom-
modations for respectable women whose
incomes are small. There are about eighty
rooms in the house, which are to be let fur-
nished, at very low rates. 
	A DRIVER, angry with his horses for some
fancied offence, was about to lash them 
severely. A lady interrupted him by in-
quiring the way to a certain street, to a cer-
tain man’s house, both of which she knew 
very well. But the driver too gallant not 
to answer the lady’s questions, had oppor-
tunity for his temper to cool, and restored
the whip to its socket without striking a 
blow.


IF the beautiful and the good are not
always united, we can only say that they
ought to be so, for they belong to each other
as the blossom and the fruit.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


]For the EXPONENT.
	A WARNING.
		—
	Ladies, “beware of wolves who come to
you in sheep’s clothing.” Do you not
know their object when they come to you to
get you to do some fancy work for them?
They wish to form an acquaintance with
you so that they may tell you how smart
you are, and that your “husbands do not
appreciate you, nor furnish you money
enough, and it is altogether too bad,” etc.
Perhaps they will treat you to some little
niceties very frequently, until your judgment
and good sense fail you, if you are not aware
of their motives and do not shun every
appearance of familiarity with them. It is
not worth while to barter your peace of
mind and bring discontentment into your
homes, for the sake of receiving attentions
from a set of worthless, heartless scamps.
Yet I have known wives and even mothers,
who would thus sell themselves and dis-
honor their households, by reason of their
vanity. And I could name more than one
woman, who stands on dangerous ground
to-day, and who, on account of a lack of
firmness and the circumstances surrounding
her, had better beware. Often it has been
repeated and proven, some women do not
comprehend that polished manners and rich
clothing do not make gentlemen.
							L. M. SMITH.


[﻿For the Woman’s EXPONENT.
	    AN INCIDENT IN PLURALITY.
				     —	
	When that true woman and gifted lady,
“Grace Greenwood,” was here, she delivered
a lecture on that inexhaustible and interest-
ing theme “The heroes and heroines of
humble life. Several communications have
of late appeared in the EXPONENT on the
subject of plural marriage. A Gentile
writer, lately puts forth the idea, that the
solution of the “Mormon Problem,” will be
effected through the growing dislike of the
rising generation of “Mormondom,” to this
principle. The foolish, extravagant display,
and inconsiderate expressions of many of our
young people, partly justify this conclusion;
but the surface expression of a portion of
society, is not the heart-index of the people
at large, in their different avocations and by
their firesides.
	When first I came here from the outside
world, there was much that being new,
struck with a strange thrill, and surprised
me. In a settlement some distance from
here, was a large family whose clothing was
chiefly of home manufacture. The third
wife, had two little girls who really needed
new dresses; but how to pay the weaver,
was the serious question. Said an elder
daughter of the first wife; “Get the yarn
ready and I’ll take it home with me” (thirty
miles away) “and weave it for you.” “O!
no Maggie! I couldn’t think of it! You
have so much to do, and need the pay for
your work,” responded the mother of the
little girls with a burst of feeling. The young
woman replied; “I think it a pity if I can’t
do that much for my father’s children! and
I will.” Noble, generous girl! It touched
me then, and has never lost its first effect,
when I have thought of it since. She had
not the money to purchase dresses to present
to those children, but she gave them what
money could not buy—the labor of love
from her own hands, and a place of equality
and honor in her own heart, as, “my father’s
children.”
	I hope this true “heroine of humble life”
will forgive the liberty taken; for it serves
to illustrate that such writers as I have
mentioned, have not seen all; and the picture


is justice due to many of the rising genera-
tion of “Mormondom.”
								A. J. C. 
Salt Lake City, April, 1873.


﻿	WOMAN’S VOICE.

	       	     Farmington, Davis Co.,
				         April 8, 1873.
Editor WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	As a lover of the true principles which
God in His great mercy has again restored
to the earth, I feel, with many of my sisters,
to offer my “mite.” I have been a member
of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day
Saints from my childhood, and can realize
that the hand of the Lord has been over His
Saints. He has caused streams of water to
burst forth, and the desert to blossom as the
rose. I was in Missouri at the time of the
mobbing and driving of the Saints from
that land and I realize they were miracu-
lously delivered from their enemies; and
the same God has led and guided us to the
valleys of the mountains. I have been in
polygamy for eleven years, and feel thank-
ful to my Father in Heaven, I have never
felt like saying anything against that pure
principle. We know, of course, it is a trial;
but has not God said He would have a tried
people? “After much tribulation cometh
the blessings.” I can truly say I am a hap-
pier woman since I have obeyed this com-
mandment. If we would strive to be
humble and prayerful, it will be a lesson in
the great school which will prepare us for
an exaltation in the Celestial Kingdom of
God.
	I take the EXPONENT and feel amply paid
in reading its interesting pages, especially
the spirited letters of Sister E. R. Snow.
				       	Mary S. Clark.


﻿WOMEN LOVERS.
		      —
   Perhaps you do not know it, but there are
women who fall in love with each other.
Woe be to the unfortunate she, who does the
courting! All the cussedness of ingenuity
peculiar to the sex is employed by “the
other party” in tormenting her. She will
flirt with women by the score who are
brighter and handsomer than her victim.
She will call on them oftener. She will
praise their best bonnets and go into ecsta-
cies over their dresses. She will write
them more pink notes, and wear their “tin
types,” and when despair has culminated,
and sore-hearted Araminta takes to her bed
in consequence, then only will this conquer-
ing “she” step off her pedestal to pick up
her dead and wounded. But then, women
must keep their hand in. Practice makes
perfect.
					     Fanny Fern.


﻿CHRISTIAN graces are like perfumes—the
more they are pressed, the sweeter they
smell; like stars, that shine brightest in the
dark; like trees, the more they are shaken,
the deeper root they take, and the more
fruit they bear.
	GOVERN the appetites, or they will become
tyrants, under whose bondage all that is
noble in human character, will be crushed
out of existence. Besides, indulgence of
the appetites in time destroys health; and,
what is life without health?
	NEVER go back, unless you find you are
wrong. What you attempt, do with all
your strength. Determination is omnipotent.
If the prospects, are somewhat darkened, put
the fire of resolution to your soul, and kindle a
flame that nothing but death can extinguish.


﻿MISCELLANEOUS.
				   —
	SMIGGLES says  that the most thrilling
tale he ever listened to was that of a rattle-
snake.
	A PAYING vein of silver has been dis-
covered on the head waters of the Arkansas
river, in Colorado.
	THE New Orleans “Times” says that the
daily interest on what that city owes is four
thousand dollars.
	THE best indication of a let up of hard
times, according to the Miami “Republi-
can,” is the large increase to marriages.
	THE directors of the Commercial National
Bank of Cleveland have voted to add
$260,000 to the bank’s capital.
	A SOUTHERN Kansas paper thinks it can
increase the population ot the town by offer-
ing to insert birth notices free.
	IT costs $80,000 per annum to keep up the
city government of Denver and supply the
aldermen with whisky straight.
	Beecher had an audience of one hundred
and ninety-eight at Rochester, and the
society realized $1:17 after paying expenses.
	IT is the general belief that a decent,
respectable man is safer on the outskirts of
the Modoc camp that in a New York street
car.
	It is believed that in less than two cen-
turies English will be the dominant lan-
guage, and will be almost exclusively
used in scientific works.
	THE Jewell “Clarion” man says he is able
to keep afloat by drinking his coffee without
sugar and turning his collar three times a
month. He thinks that’s economy.
	THE first Russian newspaper was pub-
lished over one hundred and sixty years
ago, under the special patronage of Peter
the Great, one of its principal contributors.
	SEDALIA wants to give the cold
shoulder to 1,100 colored folks, citizens of
the town, by asking the Missouri legislature
to cut off the Ward containing them from
the city limits.
	THE table on which the Declaration of
Independence was signed is still in exis-
tence in South Carolina. It has always
remained in the Laurens family, one of
whose ancestors was a “signer.”
	THE foreman of the Newborn “Journal
of Commerce” is only sixteen years old.
He and his brother, aged fourteen, set up in
a day fifteen full columns of bourgeois.—
“Eastern Paper.”
	THE “Bridge of Sighs” was a passage
in the gloomy bridge connecting the ducal
palace at Venice with the prisons, through
which condemned prisoners were led to
their death.
	THE Cathedral of St. Peters at Rome, is
four hundred and sixty-three feet; St.
Paul’s, London, four hundred and four feet;
The Monument, Lundon, is two hundred
and ten feet high.
	THE foreman of the “New Northwest,”
Portland, about as neatly gotten up a paper
as comes to this office, is but 16 years old.
the paper is set up in brevier and made
ready for the press each week by the fore-
man and two younger brothers.—“Lafayette
Courier.”
	IN Louisville some kindly disposed people
buy nuts and scatter them in the park for
the squirrels, and it has been discovered
that the tiny quadrupeds are regularly
robbed by boys. When we consider the
depravity of the juvenile heart we wonder
where all the good men come from.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[For the EXPONENT.
	INTEMPERANCE.
		    —
	Intemperance is taking a dreadful hold
upon some of our young men. To see the
young going to destruction, is very, very
painful to those who have the interest of
this people at heart. But what can be done?
	Our leaders have faithfully warned the
lovers of stoong drink of the consequence
of giving way to this fatal appetite; fatal
alike to body and mind, to interest here
and hereafter.
	What can a woman say against this in-
sidious disease, (for it is nothing short of a
disease,) when those having authority to
teach have failed to arouse the young to a
sense of their danger? Who can care for
the confirmed inebriate? Is he not a hiss
and a by-word, to both old and young, to
the wise and the foolish, to the religious
and even the wicked who follow not his
ways? Who can trust a drunkard? It
looks as though the fathers of our young
men are much to blame; those who set the
example by sometimes getting drunk which
is worse than habitual drunkenness. Young
men often appear to think it is something
to laugh at. They try it once, twice, thrice,
and so on till the demon fairly enters and is
seldom if ever dislodged. The beastly
habit becomes a deadly Upas tree, blasting
every good principle and virtue in them,
sinking them beneath the level of the brute
creation. It is sad to see men who might be
godlike sinking themselves lower and lower
in such degradation; but who can stay them?
The Lord Himself reasons with them, but
to no purpose, tells them the consequence,
yet still they go on reckless of everything.
Every effort to reclaim seems lost on the
inebriate. Anger, tears, or prayers, all, all
are wasted. There is, in London, a picture
of a Pandemonium. Standing before it one
can imagine the effects of strong drink.
Drunkards in tbeir madness might have
been taken by the artist for his model, the
likeness is so terribly striking. Strong
drink is like a serpent; it fascinates its
victims, throws its folds around them, fold
upon fold, until it has completely crushed
them, and leaves them a shapeless mass,
loathsome to behold. Kind mother Earth
opens her pitying bosom to receive the
wreck intemperance has laid low, she is
his last and only friend, for no one, however
near by nature’s ties, can really grieve for
the drunkard when he is on the earth no
more.
	Not alone in strong drink is intemperance
fatal. It is destructive in every form,
especially in dress. Here it attacks the
young girl, ever crying “give, give!” In-
temperance in dress has filled more brothels
than any other passion, has caused more
desolate homes than any other female vice.
Girls, beware of this passion; indulged in,
it is as fatal as strong drink; it has driven
kind fathers and husbands to ruin, shame
and death. Study to dress well, but never
beyond your means. A calico dress may be
made as graceful as one of satin or velvet;
it is not the cost of the material but the
goodness of the wearer, that makes a dress
beautiful. Many will dress costly yet never
look well.
	Love of money is often without one spark
of generosity, and like the prison whose
walls closed by mechanism, it slowly and
silently crushes out every thing that is noble
or beautiful in the soul of its victims, leav-
ing nothing but dust and ashes.
	What a vast field intemperance covers!
It is the first and last cause of sin, misery
and death. Beware, Oh! beware of intem-
perance!						     MARY.

		    WOMAN’S EXPONENT.	
		A UTAH LADIES’ JOURNAL.
	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.
	They have been grossly misiepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply
	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves ? “ It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!”
	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of Woman’s Exponent, a jou[-]nal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.
	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general, household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.
	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.
	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little Is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In Woman’s Exponent a department
will be devoted to roports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the journal,
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.
	Woman’s Exponent will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.
	TERMS, Strictly in Advance:
1 Copy one year,	     $2.00   	1 Copy six months,  $1.00
10 Copies one year, 18.00	      10 Copies six months, 9.00
	No subscription received for less than six months.
	It is earnestly desired that the Bishops, Presidents
of Female Relief Societies and others interested in a
work of this kind, will interest themselves in getting
up clubs; ten copies for the price of nine.
	Address all communications and subscriptions,
Publishers Woman’s Exponent, Salt Lake City,
Utah.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

VOL. I	SALT LAKE CITY, ﻿UTAH, MAY 1, 1873. 	No. 23.


EDITORIAL NOTES. 
		     —
	"ANOTHER WIFE murder” might be
almost the terrible daily cry now. Edgewoods,
near Boston, furnished one of the latest
victims.
	THERE ARE indications of wide-spread
troubles with the savage Indians, and the
savage white man howls madly for exter-
mination.
	MORE OFFICIAL defalcations, and an
occasional pardon for a criminal against the
government, continue to stir the stagnant
flow of public life.
	UTAH AFFAIRS continue to occupy public
attention throughout the country, and dis-
cussion on them shows a plentiful lack of
information on the subject.
	JAPAN WANTS Christianity for state
reasons, though. Many people pretend
they have it, when it is only policy that
dictates the pretense.
	A VIGOROUS political editor inquired, when
informed that Governor Kellogg had lately
been hanged in effigy in New Orleans, why
the people didn’t hang him in earnest in-
stead of in effigy.
	ANOTHER HORRIBLE railroad accident
has occurred, this time in Connecticut,
with “nobody to blame” as usual. Unlike
the steamer Atlantic horror, which was a
prolific topic for over a week, this occupied
public attention for only a day or two!
	AMERICAN COMMISSIONERS to the Vienna
Exposition are charged with the gravest
corruption and venality, and the scandal is
the talk of two continents. Honesty in
public officers would seem to be only “a
relic of barbarism,” like purity and earnest
piety.
	INSTANTANEOUS PHOTOGRAPHY is a late
discovery of importance. The fast horse
“Occident,” owned by ex-Governor Stan-
ford, of California, was photographed while
running at a speed of thirty-two feet a
second. The value of this discovery will
be better understood in the future.
	THE LAVA Bed, where the Modoc In-
dians have their hiding place, is said by
geologists, to be an outflow of volcanic
matter, forming a sea of melted rock which
has hardened and again become like rock,
covering probably a hundred square miles,
and honeycombed with caverns, where it is
impossible to get at them by those unac-
quainted with the nature of the locality.
	HERE IS how “Gath”—or George Alfred
Townsend—in the Chicago Tribune, alludes
to the travels of Dr. Newman and his clerk
—Mrs. Newman. “Gath” has a nice way of
putting it:  “The days of the itinerant
ministry have returned, and the Rev. Dr.
Newman will go on circuit—to Japan. He
travels, like Lorenzo and Peggy Dow, with
his wife, M. F.—,on a free steamship pass.
I refer to the M. F. because under these
modest initials the lady was concealed in
the appointment.”
	A COLORED student of a law-school in the
District of Columbia, was, the other day,
admitted to practice before the Supreme
Court of that Territory. The Supreme
Court of Illinois refuses Myra Bradwell the
right to practice law, because she is a
[new column]
woman, and the Supreme Court of the
United States affirms the decision, because
it is a matter in which the State is supreme;
but in Utah both the colored law-student
and white woman could have been admitted
to the bar on terms of equality. Will some
one tell us what peculiar kind of slavery this
then is, which so manifests itself in Utah?
	THE SUPREME Court of the United States
having decided in the case of Myra Brad-
well against the State of Illinois, that a
woman is not a citizen under the 14th
Amendment to the Constitution, and there-
fore cannot vote, it becomes the duty of all
women, and of all who desire they should
possess their just rights, to earnestly and
persistently agitate for the passage of the
16th Amendment, to secure a recognition of
woman’s right to earn honestly and honor-
ably the means to live, and have a voice as
the political equal of man, in saying how
the taxes, of which she pays her share, shall
be disposed of.
	LOUISIANA IS still terribly unsettled; and
less than a couple of weeks ago a battle
occurred between the whites and blacks, in
which over a hundred of the latter were
shot down. This is veritably a war of races,
and is the result of forcing, by law, the
freedmen into positions of social equality
with and political superiority over their
former owners. It is affirmed that the votes
of the last election have been ignored, and
the Kelogg government, or that sustained
by the colored citizens, is upheld by the
Federal government. Such a condition of
affairs ill accords with the claim of prosper-
ous peace all over the country; and indicates
a condition of feeling which may work much
evil and misery before it is removed.
	ONE OF the Associate Justices of the
Supreme Court of this Territory, T. J. Drake,
sold out his position to O. F. Strickland,
who held the office of Judge here for nearly
four years; and now the whole matter has
been exposed by the commencement of a
suit for tbe recovery of the price of the judi-
cial position. Yet such men have not a
sense of shame sufficient to even try and
cover up their disgrace! No wonder poli-
ticians talk of the incorruptibility of public
men, when here was a Federal Judge, an
officer supposed to be immaculateness per-
sonified, who valued his honor, his official
position and his reputation at $2,800; and
he found a buyer who is charged with be-
coming rich in four years on $3,000 a year,
but who was never wealthy enough to pay
the bill he contracted in buying his office.
And these are the men whose voices have
been so potent against the people of Utah.
	“CAPTAIN JACK,” chief of the Modoc
Indians, on the borders of California and
Oregon, after having failed to receive ac-
ceptable terms from the Peace Commis-
sioners treacherously invited another “peace
talk,” and at the council thus held he and
the Indians with him shot and killed Gen-
eral Canby and Dr. Thomas, one of the
Commissioners, and seriously injured Mr.
Meacham, another Commissioner. Then a cry
rang through the land for the utter exter-
mination of the Modocs, men, women and
children; a cry so barbarously savage, as if
the vengeful Indian spirit had swept over
the country with the utmost fierceness. A
[new column]
noble people, a Christian people, a truly
civilized people, seek justice, not revenge, but
this wild and implacable thirst for revenge,
leveling death against a whole people, youth
and age, manhood and infancy, male and
female, because their leaders, driven to
desperation and goaded by repeated wrongs,
planned and brutally perpetrated a foul
murder, is neither noble, Christian nor civi- 
lized.	
	WE CLIP from the Washington Star, of
April 16th, the following, which settles
negatively woman’s claim to equal rights
even with the colored masculines, under the
14th Amendment to the Constitution of the
United States: “The case of Myra Brad-
well, plaintiff in error vs. the State of Illi-
nois, has been decided in favor of defendant
by the United States Supreme Court, the
Chief Justice alone dissenting. Mrs. Brad-
well, who is the editor of a Chicago law
journal, applied for permission to practice
law in the Supreme Court of Illinois; sub-
mitting the requisite certificate as to charac-
ter and qualification. The application was
denied on the ground that a woman is not
entitled to admission. The applicant
appealed to the Supreme Court of the United
States, claiming that she is a citizen of the
United States, and, therefore, is entitled to
the privilege of practising law in the
State of Illinois, under the provisions of the
Fourteenth Amendment. The United States
Supreme Court affirm the judgment of the
Illinois Supreme Court. Mr. Justice Miller
delivered the opinion of the court. Justices
Bradley, Swayne, and Field concurred in
the judgment, but not in the opinion.”
	AS AN illustration of the quickness of
the eye and delicacy of touch which woman
undoubtedly possesses in a superior degree
to man, we take the following from a
Washington paper; merely adding, that as 
a result of numerous experiments woman
experts are exclusively employed in this
department of the government offices: “Miss
Patterson, of the United States Treasurer’s
office, with two female assistants, has been
at work every bright day for two weeks 
past in overhauling, for the purpose of
identification, the packages of bank notes
and securities which were so badly burned
by Anderson, the insane cashier of Lamber-
ton’s bank, at Franklin, Pennsylvania, about
a month since. Many of these papers were
almost wholly destroyed, in fact, in some
instances bills and other papers were com-
pletely consumed, nothing remaing but
small portions of the ashy fabric, which a
breath of air would have broken into minute
particles. Notwithstanding this, so deli-
cately and carefully have these vague
vestiges of what was once money been
manipulated by the experts that almost all
the notes and securities have been identified,
and those issued by the government will be
redeemed by Uncle Sam. The railroad and
other corporation bonds will doubtless be
redeemed by the companies issuing them.
Ex-Representative Gillfillan, son-in-law of 
Mr. Lamberton, the president of the bank,
and who has been in Washington for several
weeks watching the process of identification,
is greatly pleased with the result, and
speaks in high terms of the skill of General
Spinners’s experts.”


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


OUR MOUNTAIN HOME.
				   —
	The following sweetly expressive lines, full of the
simplicity of nature, were written by the second wife
of an Elder in Israel, addressed to the first wife of
her husband.	

	In the old log cabin in the hills, Mary,
		We have spent many happy hours;
	Our children p'aying about the door,
		As bright as the summer flowers.
	And though we have sighed for social life,
		And the joy which friendship brings,
	We have found there are pleasures in duty’s path,
		Pleasures which leave no stings.

	Sometimes we have felt we were banished, Mary,
		And as days and weeks rolled by,
	We looked to the Eastern mountains,
		With longing, wishful eye.
	We sometimes feel as if shut out
		From friends and kindred dear;
	Yet this is but a moment’s thought—
		For duty calls us here.

	To teach and instruct our boys, Mary,
		To guide their footsteps aright;
	Will do much for us to accomplish,
		If we do it with all our might:
	How, then, can we ever be lonely,
		With this great mission to fill?
	By the Spirit of Truth directed,
		Teach them Jehovah’s will.

	How we may spend our future,
		Or where our lot be cast.
	We know not, may we ne’er regret
		The unforgotten past;
	And may memory turn with pleasure, Mary,
		To the lonely days we spent,
	In the old log cabin away in the hills, 
		On "every-day duties” intent.
										E. A. B.
April 16th, 1866.


R﻿ELIEF SOCIETY REPORTS.
			    —
	By especial invitation, sisters Precendia
L. Kimball, Sarah M. Kimball and Zina D.
Young attended the Annual meeting of the
Relief Society of West Jordan, held in the
school-house on the 14th of March. There
was a full attendance of the sisters and
a goodly number of the brethren present.
The singing by a juvenile choir, trained and
led by the President, Sister Pixton, was ex-
cellent. It was a delight to see the officiat-
ing of Sister Pixton, in her presidency
—acting, as she herself expressed it, as a
“Shepherdess” over the flock entrusted to
her care. As such she trains the choir,
watches, over the young, and comforts the
poor, the aged, and afllicted all around her.
Indeed, her example is worthy of commen--
dation to all. The Society numbers forty-
eight members. The sisters had donated
and prepared a number of articles for a Fair
which certainly was most creditable to
them. These articles were disposed of to the
amount of $43:65, to be appropriated to the
building of a house for the Society. Besides
this, there was considerable donated by the
brethren, with the promise of liberal assist-
ance in future. Much valuable instruction
was given by the sisters from the city as
well as the brethren and sisters of the
Ward. Sister S. M. Kimball suggested and
advocated the formation of a Physiological
class, for the benefit of the community in
general in that locality. This suggestion
met with general approval and a resolution
to form a class was adopted. By the re-
quest of the President and Secretary of the
Society I have prepared, and forward this
report.
			PRECENDIA L. KIMBALL.
Salt Lake City, March 20th, 1873.

	Meeting of the Relief Society, 16th Ward,
held in the new school house on the 6th of
March, 1873. Bishop Kesler and Counselor
Riser were present and addressed the sisters,
commending them for their faithfulness,
and tendering to them the use of a room
[new column]
in the new house for their meetings, which
was most gratefully received by the Society.
	PRECENDIA L. KIMBALL, Secretary.

	Sister Melissa Reynolds writes from Heber
City, Wasatch County:—“There is a unity
of feeling among the faithful sisters here,
and improvement in grace and the know-
ledg of the truth is the general aim. And
our sincere desires are that we may be
instrumental in achieving much good in
our day.”
	Accompanying this pleasant report, comes
a list of subscriptions, which is a positive
proof that the EXPONENT is acceptable in
the Society, and corroborative of the senti-
ments expressed.

	From Paris, Oneida Co., Idaho Territory
comes the following, written April 10th,
1873: The annual meeting of the Relief
Society of Paris, was held in the Second
Ward School House, last Thursday at 2
o’clock p. m., and as our sisters in Utah,
have not heard much from those who live In
this northern country, I take pleasure in
writing to the EXPONENT concerning our
condition and feelings. Our meeting was
not so well attended by members, the roads
being very bad, as it otherwise would have
been; but we had the company of President
C. C. Rich, and Bishops Budge and Horne.
The meeting being called to order by our
respected President, sister Julia Lindsay,
Bishop Budge engaged in prayer, after
which the Secretary read a financial state-
ment of last year’s business, which showed
that the Society had collected two hundred
and eighty-three dollars and forty-four
cents and spent in assisting the poor, one
hundred and fifty dollars and seventy-nine
cents, leaving a balance in the Treasury of
one hundred and forty-two dollars and sixty-
five cents. This was considered very satis-
factory, and the sisters felt thankful, that
they had been able to help the needy so
much. President Rich, being invited, occu-
pied some time in giving instructions in
his usual kind and fatherly manner, impart-
ing much encouragement to the sisters.
He expressed great confidence in the
perpetual success of the Society, if the
members would continue to cultivate the
spirit of union. Bishop Budge followed
with suitable remarks, and advised care and
instruction by mothers to the children, thus
laying the foundation for improved lives.
President Rich asked the members if they
were still willing to sustain their President,
Mrs. J. Lindsay, and her Counselors, Mrs.
Caroline Kimball and Mrs. Elizabeth Col-
lins in their callings, and received a hearty
response in the affirmative, Sister Nancy
Rich was appointed Treasurer in place of
sister Stucki, whom circumstances prevented
from continuing in that office, which she
had faithfully filled. During the meeting
an excellent spirit prevailed, and the
Teachers reported a very good feeling in
the settlement towards the Society. It was
arranged to have a Society Social Party,
which came off last night, and was a com-
plete success,—only, so great was the general
confidence that it would be a good one, that
the hall was rather crowded. The best of
feelings prevailed, however, and the Com-
mittee, sisters Mary Oakey and Emeline
Rich were highly complimented for the
neatness and convenience of every thing.
I must not forget to say that we were
advised to increase the circulation of the
EXPONENT which we intend doing.
	I remain your sister in the Gospel,
					    ALICE MARGETTS.

	Extracts from minutes of the 55th meet-
[new column]
ing of the Senior and Junior Co-operative
Retrenchment Association of Salt Lake
City, held April 12th 1873, in the 14th
Ward Assembly Rooms. Counselor Mrs.
Zina D. Young, presiding in the absence of
President Mrs. Mary L Horne, suggested
that as there was no particular subject to be
that day considered, the meeting be one for
testimony, and that each one speak as she
might be led by the Holy Spirit.
	Counselor Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball did not
wish to feel, on such an occasion that she
had nothing to say; it was very important
that all weigh themselves occasionally
And see if they really had faith in God
and were worthy of a place in His Kingdom.
Women and girls should be instructed in
branches of learning appropriate for them to
put to use, that they might assist in sus-
taining themselves. There was nothing
improper, unlady like or undignified in a
woman’s taking charge of a post office, or
any such light business; but it was right
and proper for women to qualify themselves
for such occupations which were more suit-
able for them than the heavy washing and
other kinds of work which some of them do
now, and which should be performed by
men.
	Mrs. Harriet C. Young and several other
sisters bore impressive testimonies to the
truth and validity of the Latter-day work.
Counselor Mrs. Elizabeth Howard said it
was a great comfort to her to have the con-
fidence of her sisters; that they should be
careful and not do anything to shake the
confidence of those they loved; exhorted
her sisters to keep the word of wisdom;
their daughters should be taught to make
themselves useful and to be self sustaining.
	Counselors Mrs. Phoebe Woodruff and B.
W. Smith, and sisters Hampton, P. L. Kim-
ball and others made suitable remarks,
giving much valuable instruction and ask-
ing blessings to rest upon Israel.
	Meeting adjourned for two weeks to meet
at the same place at 2 o’clock, p. m. Sing-
ing—benediction by Mrs. Eleanor Pratt.
	M. A. JENKINS, Secretary.

	Farmington, Davis County, under date of
April 17th, sends the following:
	In reading the EXPONENT I see reports
from Relief Societies in other settlements,
and feel that I have been neglectful in not
reporting how we prosper as a Society in
Farmington  I think I can safely say that
we are improving; for although our regular
meetings, which we hold monthly, are not
so well attended as they should be, yet from
the reports given by the Teachers, after
visiting their districts, the sisters generally
feel well in their religion, and are willing,
when called upon, to donate what they can
for charitable purposes. It is only very
lately, that, by the suggestion of our Presi-
dent, Mrs. S. J. Holmes, the Society donated
seventy-five dollars for the Temple, and
received a receipt for that amount, besides
helping our Bishop in other public matters.
But we think it a duty as well as a pleasure
to assist him when we can. We meet to
work every two weeks; and, from what I
can learn, are about like other Societies in
general. We have a faithful President and
Counselors to take the lead. I have
lived in Farmington twenty-two years and
do not think there is a community where
the ladies are more willing to assist the sick,
and administer to their wants than in this
place; which I think is something in our
favor; for where and when is the true friend
needed more than in times of sickness and
distress? I prize your paper very much
and hope your subscribers will increase and


all who have a desire to, may be, able to
purchase it. 
			Yours Respectfully,
				A. S. Rogers, Secretary. 

	This comes from Wallsburg, Wasatch
County:
	Wallsburg is a small place containing
only about forty-five families; consequently
our means are limited; but the Relief
Society has always been able to meet the
call for charity and have a surplus on hand.
We hold meeting once a month, and all feel
well towards the cause engaged in.
			Sarah Glenn, Secretary.

	We receive the following from Meadow
Creek, Millard County: At a meeting held
by our Relief Society, March 8th, 1873, the
word “Female” was dispensed with, accord-
ing to suggestion. We are not a wealthy
Society, but with the help of the Lord we
intend to improve in all good as the time
advances. The Society has been organ-
ized three years. We have not many poor
among us; are thankful that we have a
kind and faithful President, and feel to
sustain her with our faith and prayers, and
with our hands also.
				Martha Adams, President. 
	Selina Adams Secretary.

		Salt Lake City, April 23rd, 1873.
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	President B. Young, in some of his re-
marks during Conference advocated the
cause of woman most kindly and wisely, for
which the young ladies of the 8th Ward
Retrenchment Association return him a vote
of thanks, and wish him to live long to see
them prosper under his judicious guidance.
	Louisa C. Spencer, President.
	Sarah Jane Chamberlain, Counselor.
	Anne Loss, Secretary pro. tem.

	The annual meeting of the Relief Society
of Harrisville, Weber County, is reported as
follows: Our annual meeting, which, to
agree with former dates, should have been
held some weeks before, but was postponed
on account of the prevalence of the measels
in the settlement, we held on the 20th of
April.. It was well attended by the sisters
and a goodly number of the brethren who
gave much good instruction. The financial
report, presented by the Treasurer, Mrs.
Louisa Harris, showed that in the past year
there had been given into the Society $61:60,
$18:10 having been disbursed, there was
remaining in the treasury, in goods $19:20,
and in cash $24:30.
		Mrs. Melissa Shirtliff, President.
	Mrs. Mary Taylor, Secretary.

	The sisters write from Gunnison, April
23rd, 1873: We have had a Relief Society
in this place since September 7th, 1867.
On the 17th of May, 1868, we were reorgan-
ized as a Society, according to the pattern
set by sister E. R. Snow. Although our
“mites” put together, may not amount to
more than “a drop in the bucket,” compara-
tively, yet, we feel that we are doing our
best. Our Treasury has disbursed, to the
Emigration Fund, the Temple, the poor at
home, and other investments, about $500.
	Knowing, that with our means, our power
to do good will increase, and that “He that
giveth to the poor lendeth to the Lord,” we
cheerfully join, heart and hand with our
sister Societies, in the grand object for
which we were organized. The Exponent
is a valuable companion in our Semi-monthly
sewing meetings. In our last meeting we
agreed to omit the word “Female” from the
[new column]
name of ouir Society.   We feel there is an
important work before us, destined to make
a link in the chain of the great reformatory,
plan in these Latter-days.
			  Lydia Horne, President.
	Helena Madsen, Secretary.

Sarah M. Kimball, Mrs. Zina D. Young,
Mrs. Phoebe Woodruff; and sisters Willmarth
East, Pixton from South Cottonwood, Mag-
jgie C. Shipp, and Sarah Wheeler from the
Sugar House Ward.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿WOMAN’S VOICE.
			       —
Editor Woman’s Exponent:—
	Having read the beautiful testimonies of
many of my sisters in the Gospel, that have
appeared from time to time in your paper,
I feel that I, too, should not neglect to add
my own to the list. It is more than twenty-
six years since I first became associated
with the Saints of God.   I crossed the Plains
in the first company of “Hand-carts; I
realized the blessings promised to those who
would start relying onl Israel’s God for
strength and protection, and I can truly say,
that in all, the varied and trying scenes I
have had to pass through, I have never been
forsaken by my Heavenly Father; but
through all I have enjoyed that peace of
mind which none but the Saints of God can
know. I feel very glad that there is a
channel through which woman can speak her
true feelings, for I consider that however
much the women of Utah may be pitied or
ridiculed by their sisters in the outside
world, there are thousands who would be
glad to occupy the position we hold, for are
we not the respected, and honored wives of 
heaven’s true nobility? Do not our chil-
dren have the privilege of acknowledging
their fathers, and fathers their children,
honorably sustaining them? I feel thankful
that God in his mercy has revealed a princple
by which our sons and daughters can be
reared in virtue, and free from the contam-
inating evils of celibacy. “Better, far
better,” our position to-day, as the wives of
polygamists, than that of millions of women
in the world, who are daily the victims of
men who have a “holy horror” of polygamy,
but yet hesitate not to ruin the fair charac-
ter of women, and then with scorn, trample
her under their feet—simply because she
trusted in their boasted honor! How long
shall the strong triumph over the weak?
Surely, we have cause to pray that the time
will soon come when righteousness shall
cover the earth as the waters cover the
mighty deep, and the voice of the oppressed
be no more heard in sorrow and lamenta-
tions.
	praying God to hasten the time of their
redemption.
	I remain your sister in the Covenant of
peace,					Mrs.	 A. Randal.
	Spanish Fork, April 22nd, 1873.


﻿The fact of one Federal Judge in Utah
selling out his position and getting the
purchaser appointed in his place, while the
latter refused to pay the purchase money,
is creating considerable comment throughout
the country. Evidently the character of
the men who have often been appointed to
such important positions in the Territories,
and notably in Utah, is not yet knonw to
the country. If it were, there would be less
sympathy with many of their lawless and
tyrannous acts.


﻿WHAT-NOT. 
				—
	Nothing is so reasonable and cheap as 
good manners.
	A LOUISIANA operator makes soap out of 
cotton seed. 
	LOUISVILLE policemen are provided with
wheelbarrows.	
	TUNEFUL lyre—a music teacher who does
not keep his engagements.
	A CONNECTICUT horse has acquired the
habit of chewing tobacco. Poor horse!
	A DETROIT youth has no forehead, the
hair growing down close to the eyes. Poor 
boy!	
	A CROSS-eyed man in Newberg has
gathered around him seven cross-eyed
servants.
	THE stocking manufacturers of Newark
N. J., say that the largest sizes are shipped
to Chicago.
	A YOUNG lawyer in North Carolina was 
recently paid a pound of butter for legal 
services.
	THACKERY says that a critic is a Solo-
mon that sits in judgment before us and
chops up our children.		
	A LOUISVILLE man is so opposed to	
capital punishment that he can’t even
tolerate paper hanging. 
	If you should be asked when a cat is like a
teapot, you might reply, “When you’re
teasin’ it.”
	A MAN out West says he moved so many 
times during one year that when ever a 
covered wagon stopped at the gate his 
chickens would fall on their backs and hold 
up their feet, in order to be tied and thrown
in.
	A GENTLEMAN was complimenting a pretty	
young lady in the presence of his wife. "It 
is lucky I did not meet Miss Hopkins before 
I married you my dear.” “Well, yes, it is
extremely lucky—for her,” was the dry
rejoinder.
	WHAT a glorious world this would be, if 
all its inhabitants could say, with Shake-
speare’s shepherd: “Sir, I am a true laborer!  
I earn what I wear; I owe no man hate,
envy no man’s happiness, glad of other
men’s good: content with my farm.” 
	MRS. Moore, an industrious woman of
Topeka, Kansas, earned money enough to
support a good-for-nothing husband and
bring a niece to live with them. The
husband eloped with the niece and she is
now nicely rid of both.
	“Mother,” said a little girl, who was
making her doll an apron, “I believe I will
be a dutchess when I grow up.” How do
you expect to be a Dutchess, my daughter?” 
“Why, by marrying a Duthman, to be
sure,” replied the girl.
	SINCE their only schoolma’am eloped
from Green Bay, Wis., the school directors
advertise for a teacher that is over forty
years of age, has had the small-pox, with
poor teeth, and a glass eye, and wooden leg,
if possible. Any girl answering these re-
commendations can secure a permanent
job by applying early.
	A LITTLE boy was playing with a couple
of five cent pieces, the other evening, which
a friend had given him, and putting his
finger on one of them, said, “This one I am
going to give to the heathen.” He kept on
playing till at last one of the pieces rolled
away, and he could not find it.   “Which
one have you lost?” asked the friend.
“The one I was going to give to the hea-
then,” replied the cherub.
