﻿ 	SPLINTERS.
				     —	
	George Francis Train protested against
the attempt to place him in better quarters
than those he previously occupied in the
Tombs......It is estimated by eminent phy-
sicians that nearly one-third of the luxuries,
under the name of “ice cream,” manufac-
tured in France, is composed of poisonous
substances......“Elm Orlou” thinks it un-
generous for “the fellow” to be blamed with
every silly thing said that every one else is
ashamed of, by every bodys saying “as the
fellow said.”......Edward Eggleston, once
“Reverend Divine,” writes novels now......
Church singing in Santo Domingo, a writer
pronounces good in no way but the zest
with which it is participated in......Eighty-
five young doctors lately graduated at Ann
Arbor, Michigan; and twelve of them were
women......Maria Hawley, of the “Balance,”
Chicago, thinks though it is more unlawful
it is not more unrighteous for robbers to
take wealth at midnight, than for wealthy
people to take the labor of others at unfair
prices, which is a good and just thought......
Two lady deacons officiate in the Clinton
Avenue Congressional Church, Brooklyn......
A machine for making French lace, contain-
ing one hundred bobbins, has been invented
by a lady at Cedar Falls, Iowa......Ladies are
to be admitted to the Boston University
upon the same condition as gentlemen......
Grace Greenwood lectures on “In-doors.”....
A Boston mother complains of a lecturer
who talks only to the boys, while her poor
children, being girls, are not addressed......
Charlotte A. Barber soon begins a lecture
tour in Michigan......A lady writer says
“sin is so convenient, man wants to keep it;”
it is a great pity that once in a while, woman
interferes with his wants......The Board of
Education in Yorkshire, England, has as
coadjutors, a Lady’s Honorary Council, to
aid, by practical work and suggestion, the
better education of women.......General J.
W. S. Browne, one of the founders of the
Cincinnati “Commercial,” lately died at
Memphis......A petition for the repeal of
the social evil-law of St Louis, has been
presented to the Legislature of Missouri, and
is reported to have been signed by over
7,000 women......The Arkansas House of
Representatives has passed a bill, fixing the
pay of teachers at the same amount, without
regard to sex......Columbia, Maine, has F. C. &
C. H. Nash, husband and wife, for a law firm.....
The Bloomfield, Missouri “Republican,”
is to be managed hereafter by a woman,
Mrs. Sarah E. White......It is announced
that Joaquin Miller is writing “By the Sun-
down Seas.”......There is to be a “Woman’s
Journal,” published in Washington......Miss
Smiley, the preacher, is said to be a small
woman with wonderfully beautiful, expres-
sive eyes......The Emperor William of Ger-
many has conferred the Cross of Honor upon
Mary Von Olnhousen, of Lexington, as a
recognition of her services as nurse in the
German hospitals during the war with
France......April storms, this year, in Ne-
braska, were the most severe ever witnessed
there at any season of the year......Woman
Suffragists are rapidly increasing; many
who cared nothing about it a year ago begin
to clamor loudly.....Liquor laws are exten-
sively talked of; may the right ones be
rigidly enforced......Billerica, Massachusetts,
was honored the other Sunday by having
the minister's daughter to conduct the
church services, her father being unable to
attend......Beecher says it should not only
be the privilege of every woman to vote
but her duty as well......Royalston, Massa-
chusetts, Superintendent of Common Schools
[new column]
is a woman......The Pope praises the pro-
tection that Catholics enjoy in the Burmese
Empire......A young lady in Ohio recently
fell dead while on her way to be baptised.
......A half dozen new papers have lately been
started in Paraguay......The Duchess of In-
verness, wife of the late Duke of Sussex and
aunt to Queen Victoria, is pronounced in a
serious state......A rascal in Lowell, Massa-
chusetts, knocked a gentleman in the head
with a bludgeon the other night, and rob-
bed him of thirty-five dollars; the gentle-
man proved to be his father......Miss Ann
Wallace, a lineal descendant of Sir William
Wallace, recently died at Glasgow, at the
age of one hundred and three years......
It is again reported that Maria Alexand-
rovna, only daughter of the Czar, is to
marry the Duke of Edinburg......Turkish
Journals state that Halim Pacha, uncle of
the Egyptian Khedive, has been appointed
Ambassador at St. Petersburg......A Mrs.
Hutchins, in Maine, is eighty-seven years
old, has forty-seven grand-children and has
never yet seen a steamboat or a railroad car.
......A Boston bakery is said to have been
set on fire by some piping-hot Graham
crackers......A gentleman in the same city
keeps the small-pox hospital plentifully
supplied with flowers; this should be the
work of a gentle woman......The people in
a Florida town don’t like the arrangements
of keeping pigs in the cellar beneath the
church, because they sqeal during service.
......A young lady in Minneapolis lately
fell so desperately in love that, her affec-
tions being unrequited, it dethroned her
reason and drove her to the insane asylum.
......A woman in Fairfield County, Conn.,
only thirty-two years of age, has three
grand-children.


﻿WOMAN ON WOMAN’S NEEDS.
				—
	Miss Emily Faithful, before her departure
from New York on her return voyage to Eng-
land, delivered a farewell lecture in Steinway
Hall, of that city, which treated largely on the
Woman Question, and contained many pointed
truths and touching facts. The following ex-
tract we take from a report of her lecture in
the New York Tribune, of April 4th, which will
convey the drift of her position:
	In England there are now nearly three
million women dependent on their own exer-
tions. To tell such as these that woman’s
proper sphere is home is mockery, for they are
forced from their homes to get bread. Though
many a barrier to woman's livelihood has been
broken down, there are still terrible difficulties
in finding employment for women. Specially
onerous is the effort in the case of those of
fallen fortunes, members of genteel classes.
To relieve such Miss Faithful had founded a
“Fund for Destitute Gentlewomen,” to which
she would devote the proceeds of the lecture.
True it is that young men now find it hard
to get suitable work; they often have to go
West. But thereis no an alogy among them to
the wholesale yearly destruction of consciences,
bodies and souls among women—destruction
too often brought about by destitution. How
can tender-hearted people fold their hands
[new column]
while so many of their sisters are driven to the
gates of hell by want of bread? Statements
are published that capable women, willing to
work, can get employment at good wages.
Good, steady, skilled labor is wanted in just
those departments where women have gained
position. The unremitting, earnest applica-
tion required to acquire skill in these depart
ments, is hard for women to go through. In
them love of work for its own sake is no more
inherent than in men. Moreover, women are
always looking for the possible emancipator.
Men have nothing but their work to look to for
dependence. The greatest evil of all is the
lack of the right early training, and for this,
the family, the parents, society in general,
must be impeached. Society casts a stigma on
women who earn their own livelihood, and
parents pray that their daughters may never
be brought so low. As to education; a girl’s
training stops just where the main part of a
boy’s begins. Men are allowed full opportunity
to devote themselves to their chosen work,
and are not diverted by social demands. Wo-
men are at the beck and call of everybody, as it
were, and have so many society duties, so many
distracting little trifles to attend to, that the
wonder is not that there has not been a female
Shakespeare, Raphael, Newton, but that women
have done so much.
	The problem, what we shall do with our
redundent women in England, is answered by
some philosophers by proposing emigration
and marriage. But emigration has already
been tried, and Scotch, English, and Irish
have been sent to Australia and America in large
numbers without much diminishing the gravity
of the problem; while as for marriage there
is yet to be found the woman to say “No” when
the right man appears. As long as the num-
ber of women in Great Britain exceeds that of
the number of men by six per cent., marriage
will not wholly do away with the difficulty
unless Mormonism is tried. True marriage is
the crown and glory of a woman's life; but it
must be founded on love, and not on the desire
of a home or of support, while nothing can be
more deplorable, debasing, and corrupting than
the loveless marriages brought about in our
upper society by a craving ambition and a long-
ing for a good settlement. Loveless marriages
and a different standard of morality for men
and women are the curses of modern society.
The dignity of labor is not properly appreciated.
We agree that work is honorable in a man,
but are not yet convinced that idleness is dis-
honorable in a woman. A contempt for work
is at the bottom of the mind of a fashionable
young lady. Frivolity is so general that it is
surprising that so much good survives in spite
of neglect. So long as we frown down aud
sneer at the efforts to enlarge woman’s sphere,
we are encouraging frivolity and idleness in
women. We hear the interests and rights of
women spoken of as if these could be separated
from those of man, as if men and women were
creatures of a different kind. A most common
and mischievous error is that which would
make woman the mere shadow and attendant
of her lord, as if a shadow could be a true
helpmeet, We have long heard the man’s
sphere is the world; woman’s is home. But
women have a part in the world, while men
are not ciphers in the home circle.
	This is the language of a true woman; of one
who comprehends that woman has a nature of
her own, distinct of itself, but one which is
never wholly satisfied unless she is fulfilling
her designed mission on earth, by becoming a
loved and honored wife and occupying that
position wherein the responsibilities of mater-
nity are hers. She should have the right to
work, and receive fair pay for it; she should
enjoy representation with taxation; she should
be allowed to fill every honorable position in
society for which she has capacity; but she
should not have her womanly feelings crushed
out, nor her woman's heart deadened; for above
all she should enjoy the right to a home and a
husband, to have a voice in the choiceof the man
with whom she unites her destinies, and fill
the noblest, holiest and most exalted position
occupied by humanity on the earth—that of
moulding pliant, tender beings for lofty pur-
poses in time and an exalted immortality here-
after. 
	In England, Miss Faithful says, are three


millions of women dependent on their own ex-
ertions. They have no husbands, or perhaps
worse than none—men who degrade their
manhood, cease to be beings whom woman
can love or respect; and instead of being
woman’s protectors, guides and supporters,
become burthens upon the weaker sex and a
disgrace to humanity. Even suppose these
three millions had work, and suppose all the
women in this and every other country who
are dependent upon their own exertions were
also supplied with work and the means to live,
how are the holy upwellings of woman’s wifely
and motherly nature to be met, by any
recognized means palatable to the world of
Christendom? We put the query in all sin-
cerity, as forming a part of a whole as known
as the “Woman Question;” and add, after
years of acquaintance with that system—
“Mormonism,”—to which Miss Faithful refers,
that there will be found more in her remark
regarding it, thus apparently dropped at ran-
dom, than society outside of Utah is to-day
willing to recognize.
	The subject is worth recurring to again.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


DAILY BATHING.
				  —
	With many intelligent women, among the
Latter-day Saints, the thought seems to pre-
vail that if the drinking of tea and coffee, and
the extravagant use of meats are dispensed
with, the Word of Wisdom is faithfully kept
in every particular.
	Yet, it is plainly apparent to all who will
take time to reflect upon the subject, that these
little matters, though it is very essential that
they be carefully observed, constitute but a
small portion of what may be justly considered
words of wisdom; living according to which,
cannot fail to produce longevity, health, and
consequent happiness and usefulness.
	Not the least or the last consideration which
should be entertained, pertains to timely, and
proper bathing. If mothers wish their children
to be healthy, active and intellectual, this duty
must not be neglected, either for themselves
or their little ones. It is often the case that
women feel they have not time to take the
morning bath, so necessary to produce for them
lively, energetic thoughts and feelings, and so
generative of clear judgment, upon which all
the systematic arrangements of the household
depend. But they will find it so great an ad-
vantage, if they but adopt the habit, that, very
soon it will be realized that time is gained
rather than lost by the operation.
	Another hindrance with many, is, that small
houses and large families are apt to afford no
suitable place for bathing; and although this
cannot always be avoided, It should not alto-
gether prohibit any woman, especially if she
be delicate in health, from bathing, or at least,
sponging the body every day. A screen may
be prepared in one corner of any room, for the
purpose; and compared with the benefits, to be
derived, the trouble will be nothing. From,
the effects of this treatment, very delicate
women have become comparatively robust and
healthy.


HOME AFFAIRS. 
				   —
	A Special Conference will convene in the
New Tabernacle, in this city, on Saturday 3rd
inst.
	ANOTHER INTERESTING letter from Miss
Eliza R. Snow will be found in the present
number of the EXPONENT.
	THE NEXT issue of the EXPONENT closes its
first year. Need we again request an early
sending of clubs for the second volume?
[new column]
	A LADY who has been in attendance at the
Third District Court held in this city, for some
weeks past, pronounces it a “first-rate medical
school,”' A  medical case has been on trial.
	WE ARE requested to say that the name of
the President of the First Ward Relief Society of
Spanish Fork, is Catharine A. Wilkins, instead
of Williams as it appears in a report of our last
issue.
	A CONDUCTOR on one of the street cars in this
city made himself notable and notorious in the
estimation of some ladies, a short time since,
by severely kicking and cuffing an urchin
who, he said, was trying to steal a ride. The
ladies ask if a man should reprove a child for a
wrong action by himself committing one of
greater magnitude?
	PARENTS WHO are desirous of seeing their
children grow in wisdom, and grace as they ad-
vance in years, cannot be too particular now-a-
days in relation to the associations which they
keep, and the influences under which they are
liable to be placed; as has been repeatedly
evinced of late, by shameful proceedings which
have occurred in the streets of this city.
	IMPROVEMENTS IN various forms are being
rapidly carried on in different parts of this city,
which, from present indications, will soon be
extensively lighted by gas. Gardening and a
general trimming up of things out of doors, has
been going on for some time, and Salt Lake
City seems to be prepared, with the rest of the
world, to welcome once more the, fresh, sweet
face of May.
	NOTWITHSTANDING ALL the admonitions
given to teamsters to secure their horses before
leaving them standing in the streets, daily
papers are continually chronicling accidents
occasioned by teams being left carelessly, and
taking fright and running away; which,
though not generally of a serious nature, should
be sufficient warning to prevent so much heed-
lessness on the part of those having them in
charge.
	EVEN HERE, in our once quiet Utah, it
is difficult now-a-days to distinguish the
rogue from the gentleman. A lady of this
city rode in the cars to Ogden, a short
time since, opposite a respectable appearing
individual, not having the slightest suspicion
that he was other than he seemed. The next
day, however, while returning home, the same
man was seated directly in front of her, being
brought to the city in charge of an officer by
whom he had been arrested in Ogden, as a gov-
ernment defaulter from Idaho.
	LITTLE BOYS being urged into a fight, by
those who consider themselves men; and big
boys running over little ones with wheelbar-
rows, and rolling them into ditches, for the fun
of hearing them cry, and seeing them scramble
out; and other similar practices, are not such 
grand entertainments for persons who care
anything about morality or good behavior,
either in children or adults, as they seem to be
for some of the rude, ill-bred persons who now
constitute a portion of the community in dif-
ferent parts of the Territory.
	THE MANY terrible calamities occurring from
railroad accidents, occasioned frequently by
recklessness, of which, news comes from all parts
of the country, should make the people of Utah
appreciative of the careful and judicious man-
ner in which the management of the railroads
belonging to them is carried on by the gentle-
manly, polite and obliging Superintendents,
Agents and Condutors of these roads. Although
some few inconveniences are now to be met
with in going from this city to Logan in the cars,
even these are, comparatively, very slight, tak-
ing into consideration the short time the Utah
[new column]
Northern cars have been running. Any lady
who has a proper idea of taking care of herself,
may feel perfectly at home and at ease while
traveling by rail on either the Utah Southern,
Utah Central or Utah Northern railway.


THE SEX.
				—
	Mrs. Merriman Loyson, in addition to
her $75,000 and accomplishments, is a
painter and sculptor.
	Queen Isabella weighs about two hun-
dred pounds, and is the jolliest old ex-Queen
who ever saved her head.
	A Young Irish girl in Jacksonville, Ill.,
has refused $100 for her hair. It reaches
the floor when she stands erect.
	Mrs. Anna A. Pomeroy, former wife of
“Brick” Pomeroy, of La Crosse, has married
James H. Reed, of Fulton, Illinois.
	Miss Julia Seeley, daughter of the old
postmaster, has been nominated by the
President for postmaster, at Great Barring-
ton.
	The widow of the late Secretary Stanton
is residing at German town, near Philadel-
phia and is said to be in rapidly declining
health.
	Lady Otway, widow of the late colonel of
the Eighty-fourth British regiment, who
died recently, was a lineal descendant of
Edward III.
	A BRILLIANT belle may make a loving
wife, but you will be far more apt to find
the virtues of a perfect woman in retiring
modesty.
	Report of a Kentucky wedding—“The
bride wasn’t remarkably handsome, but her
father threw in seven mules, and the hus-
band was satisfied.”
	Many old women in New Hampshire
wear strings of beans around their necks
to keep off the rheumatism, and the charm
is said to work splendidly.
	Mary Warren, of Madison, Wisconsin,
has eight fingers on each hand. May the
Lord pity her husband when she has one,
when she begins to gridiron his face. 
	The ocean cable announces the death of
Countess Guiccioli, an Italian woman of
world-wide notoriety as the mistress of
Byron. She died at Rome.
	Miss Emily L. Wyman has returned from
her Art pilgrimage in Europe bringing,
among her treasures, exquisite copies of
several of Turner’s masterpieces executed
by herself.
	Mrs. Alice C. Clymor, the favorite niece
and principal, legatee of the Carey sisters;
died, recently at Jacksonville, Florida. She
was one of the last surviving members of
the family.
	A La Crosse, Wis., girl has been turned
out of house and home for rubbing her
cheeks against her sister’s husband’s beard
when she wished to get up a healthful glow
for a ball.
	A woman in destitute circumstances in
Indiana, cut off her hair, the other day,
and sold it to a charitable lady, who gave
her seventy-seven cents for it to buy food
for her children.
	The lady teachers of the Detroit public
school have issued an admirably written
address to the tax-payers of that city, ask-
ing for an increase of their salaries, which
are now shamefully inadequate. 
	The Art Schools of the National Acad-
emy admit pupils without regard to sex or
color, granting every student access to all
its advantages. The excellence of these
schools is of a very high order.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿THE WORLD IS PROGRESSING.
					      ——
	This poem, which was written for, and appeared
in the twenty-first number of, the EXPONENT, April
1st, contained there several typographical errors,
which so completely changed the sense that we
deem it due to the talented author to republish it,	corrected.
The world is progressing! the sunlight of science
	With new luster sparkles to-day,
And keen, searching intellect wins an alliance
	With secrets long hidden away;
Bright genius unfolds new and great combinations
	Of power to bless and serve man;
While muses and graces forsake not their stations
	Adorning the structure so grand.

The world is progressing! O manhood arouse thee!
	Nor in the swift race be outrun;
The great wheels of progress will never turn back-
		ward,
	So roll them right bravely, each one—
In the proud halls of state, at the hearthstone so
	lowly,
	Let your voice for improvement be heard,
Strengthen virtue and honor, disdain things unholy
	And bid thought soar as free as a bird.

The world is progressing! O womanhood, waken
	And join in the jubilant strain!
The fetters which bound thee now surely are break-
	  ing.
	And never shall gall thee again.
No more in the dust shall thy meek head be trodden;
	No more shall injustice o’erwhelm;
Thou shall rise above ignorance, fashion and folly
	And reign as a queen in thy realm.

  The world is progressing! old fabrics of error
	Fall, moldr'ed and crumbling to earth;
While structures, far statelier, nobler and fairer,
	Rise pillared with power and worth.
Old Prejudice writhes 'neath the blows that are
	dealt her,
And for power, wild strugg'ling she keeps;
But her terrible sway shall forever be broken—
	Humanity no longer sleeps.

The world is progressing! yet all is not stainless,
	Grim evils yet flourish abroad—
False teaching, false swearing, and falsehearted states-
		men.
	Black intrigue, and the blackest of fraud.
But the world is progressing! thank God, we’re pro-
		gressing!
	The day of dull torpor is past;
Brave hearts and strong hands are their ardor ex-
	  pressing;	
	The millions are waking at last!
								LU DALTON.
   BEAVER.


HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
	
	What to do in case of accident.—
Prof. Wilder of Cornell University, gives
the following short rules for acting in case
of accident, which it will be found useful to
preserve or remember:—If choked, get
upon all fours and cough.—If in water, float
on the back, with the nose projecting.—For
apoplexy, raise the head and body; for faint-
ing, lay the person flat.—If any artery is
cut, compress above the wound; if a vein is
cut, compress below.—For slight burns, dip
the part instantly in cold water; if the skin
is destroyed, cover with varnish.—Remove
wax from the ear by tepid water; never
put a hard instrument into the ear.—In
case of poisoning, excite vomiting by tick-
ling the throat or by warm water and
mustard.—For dust in the eyes, avoid rub-
bing; dash water into them; remove cinders,
etc., with the round point of a lead pencil.—
For acid poisons, give alkalis, for alkaline
poisons, give acids—white of egg is good in
most cases; in case of opium poisoning give
strong coffee and keep moving.—Smother
a fire with carpets, etc water will often
spread burning oil, and increase danger.
Before passing through smoke, take a full
breath and then stoop low; but if carbonic
acid gas is suspected, walk erect,—Suck
poisoned wounds, unless your mouth is sore;
enlarge the wound, or, better, cut out the
part without delay; hold the wounded part
as long as can be borne to a hot coal or end
of a cigar
	The Pomegranate.—It is perhaps not
generally known that this beautiful, rich
and wholesome fruit will keep well through
the winter. Let it be wrapped and packed
away in a box or laid among clothes in a
chest and it will keep sound until spring—
try it.
	Farmer’s Fruit Cake.—One cup of sour
dried apples, soaked over night and chop-
ped fine; simmer two hours in a cup of
sugar; one-third of a cup of butter; one-half
cup of sour milk; one teaspoonful soda; one
of cloves and nutmeg; two eggs; beat well
together; add the apples stirred up in a cup
of flour; add flour till quite stiff.
	Scent Powder.—A charming recipe for
scent powder, to be used for wardrobes,
boxes, etc., far finer than any mixture sold
at the shops, is the following:—Clove gilla,
sweet peas, mignonette, orris root, rose
leaves, and aromatic calamus each one
ounce, lavender-flowers, two ounces; rho-
dium wood, one-fourth of a dram. These
are to be mixed and reduced to a coarse
powder. This scent on clothes is as if all
fragrant flowers had been pressed in their
folds.
	Pudding.—One quart of milk. While
boiling stir in one pint of flour, after it is
sifted, six eggs, six tablespoonfuls of white
sugar, one spoonful of butter, the grated
peel and juice of two lemons. All the in-
gredients must be well beaten together
before they are stirred into the milk. Stir
one way without stopping for a minute or
two, take it off and turn into your pudding
dish. It is to be eaten cold, with sugar and
cream if you like.
	Make Strawberry Wine.—Nothing can
be more delicious; nothing can be more
healthful. In all cases of dyspepsia, or of
slow, nervous fevers, or of impure blood, or
of ill-regulated bilious apparatus, it will be
found a great palliative, and a powerful aid
to other remedies of a more decided charac-
ter. Three quarts of strawberries make
one quart of juice. Add three quarts of
water and three pounds of the best sugar.
Use a clean, sweet cask, leave the bung
out for fermentation. When the fermenta-
tion subsides close the cask tightly and the
wine is ready for use. Tightly stopped it
will keep ten years, if necessary, but in my
experience it has always been found too
attractive to be kept quite so long a time.—
“Shakespearian Almanac.”
	Cold Water Drinking.—Cold baths of
the skin are good, but it is doubtful if flood-
ing the stomach on going to bed and on
rising is not, on the whole, the most profit-
able form of cold bathing. Costiveness,
piles and indigestion are uniformly relieved
by this morning and evening stomach
douche. The quantity must be determined
by each one for himself. Two or three
swallow's will generally do to begin with,
but the quantity will soon grow to a tum-
blerful, and I have known persons to use
much larger quantities with marked benefit.
If wisely managed, every dyspeptic will
be greatly improved by this cold stomach
bath.—Ex.
	A Cheap and Delicious Soup.—Save
the liquor in which the nice leg of mutton
was boiled to-day. It would be wicked
to throw it away, for a large fraction of the
succulence and nutriveness of the meat is
in it. Remove the fat to-morrow, and then
put it over the fire. Add onions if desired,
carrots and turnips, sliced thin, and some
lime beans, sweet corn and slised potatoes,
with bits of celery; add in either case rice
or barley, a tablespoonful of them dry, to a
quart of liquor is a good rule.  Do this and
at a trifling expense you may have a tureen
of soup fit to set before a King.


﻿CORRESPONDENCE OF MISS SNOW.
				Jerusalem, March 4, 1873.
Editor Woman's Exponent:—
	I wrote you last in Cairo, Egypt, from
whence we went to Suez, where we paid our
respects to the Red Sea of antique celebrity.
I walked up and down its low bank, admired
its beautiful, calm surface, as it lay in sweet
repose with wave unstirred; and reflected
deeply on the past of its interesting history.
	From Suez we went by rail back to Is-
maila, which we passed on our way from
Cairo to Suex. From Ismaila we went by
steamer on the great Suez Canal, one of the
modern “wonders of the world,” to Port
Said, a town built on artificial ground,
made of material taken from the bed of the
canal. On the evening of the 22nd of Febru-
ary, by the aid of boats we went on board
the Vesta, of the Austrian Lloyd line of
steamers, and early the next morning an-
chored off the coast of Jaffa, which we
reached in boats in safety, although this is
considered the most dangerous of seaports.
The Mediterranean washes the foundations
of the buildings.  We ascended a few steps
and entering a narrow, muddy street, were
met by a host of natives, clamoring for our
satchels in anticipation of a fee of “Back-
sheeh” for relieving us from carrying them.
Presently a smiling corpulent biped in tur-
ban and gown demanded our passports, at
the same time giving us a hint that he
would compromise his demand, for a fee; to
which our cashier readily assented, and
thereby also protected our baggage from
being overhauled at the Custom House.
	We walked directly to our encampment;
found our tents in readiness to receive us;
and were delighted with the many conve-
niences provided by Mr. Howard, agent of
Mr. Cook of London, under whose arrange-
ments we are traveling.
	Thus, under as clear a sunshine as had over
shone on Jaffa, and in as bright a morning
as ever dawned on the land of Palestine,
we commenced tent life, and, to render it
more impressive, it was Sunday morning.
	There is nothing attractive in the appear-
ance of the interior of this ancient town.
It is said to contain about five thousand
inhabitants. As we saw them in crowds
in the narrow, crooked streets, they are the
representatives of low life and degradation.
We walked a long, roundabout distance to
visit the “house of Simon the tanner,”
situated by the sea side, and were glad to
return to our tents in the suburbs, where we
breathed the pure fresh air. The surround-
ings of the city are pleasant and richly
decorated with groves of the lemon and
orange. With me, the reflection that we
really were in Palestine, the land rendered
dear to the Saints of God by some of the
most interesting associations of mortal life
—the history of the past in connection with
the anticipations of the future, which no
other people than Latter-day Saints can so
fully appreciate—was the all absorbing
thought.
	Early on Monday morning our camp
equipage, which consists of every thing
necessary for genteel boarding houses, was
packed and in monstrous bundles lashed to
the sides of donkeys, mules and horses; our
tents struck and packed; the laden animals
and their drivers started off; when we
mounted our horses and following our drag-
oman, went in the same direction leading to
Jerusalem.
	For a considerable distance after leaving
the city, the street on both sides, is lined
with the most luxuriant orange groves we
have seen, the limbs bending with the


weight of large clusters of the golden fruit,
and protected by hedges of cactus of an
enormous size.  We traveled till noon over
the beautiful plain of Sharon, and stopped
to lunch in the vicinity of Ramleh; where
the attendants who had our mid-day refresh-
ments in charge, consisting of light bread,
cold boiled mutton, chicken, a boiled egg
for each, an orange each, and bottles of
water, had preceded us, and opened our
lunch on mattresses provided for the purpose.
The majority of the attendants proceeded
ahead in time to pitch tents, put up for each
an iron bedstead, (which by the way is
rather a small pattern for President Smith,
but very comfortable for the rest) tables,
seats, etc., and get the camp stove and cook-
ing apparatus all into working order
preparatory to evening dinner served with
soups, meats, vegetables and pastries.
	The country through which our afternoon
ride led, is very uneven; some portions
rocky and barren.  We encamped for the
night on a lovely green, called the entrance
to the valley of Ajalon, conspicuous in the
history of Joshua.  Next day we rode
through the valley of Ajalon; up mountain
ridges, where we had delightful views of
the plains of Sharon and the Mediterranean
—the country is very picturesque, the road
zigzag, winding around and on the sides of
mountains—pass Kirjath Jearim, famous in
the history of David, lunch in the valley of
Elah and in the afternoon in view of the
Mount of Olives, which we leave at our left,
and arriving at the memorable city of the
ancient Jewish nation, the once favored of
the Most High, the site of the Jerusalem
from which the “Word of the Lord” shall yet
go forth, we found our tents in readiness
and entered them with hearts of thankful-
ness to God for His protecting care and
blessings in bringing us to this destination
in safety.
	But I must hasten to a close. The next
day, the 26th, we spent in and about Jerusa-
lem, visited the “Church of the Holy
Sepulchre;” had a fine view of the Garden
of Gethsemane; the next morning struck
tents, and spent three days in visiting the
Pools of Siloam, the Tomb of Ratchel, Beth-
lehem, the Dead Sea, Jordan river, Gilgal,
Jericho, Bethany, etc.,—have not time for
description.  On the evening of the 1st of
March returned to this city, visited the
Mount of Olives and the Mosque of Omar.
To-morrow we leave for Syria.
						Eliza R. Snow.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿MORAL TRAINING IN SCHOOLS.
		OPENING ADDRESS
By Superintendent R.L. Campbell, before the Utah
	Territorial Teachers’ Association, in the Deseret
	University, April 3, 1873.
	I take pleasure in congratulating the
Teachers and friends of education in Utah
on having the happy privilege of meeting
together, under such favorable auspices.
Can we contemplate a meeting the objects
of which are more important to the rising
generation? Shall good grow out of our
thus assembling? I answer, assuredly, if
we have met with single hearts, devoid of
every selfish or otherwise wrong feeling
and devoted to the cause we represent.
	Do we understand how much depends on
the course we pursue—upon how faithfully,
how wisely, how unitedly we act? If we
can answer affirmatively, and our actions
are in accordance therewith, success will
crown our efforts. Perhaps there is no asso-
ciation the meeting of which is more needed,
more timely or more important than this.
To be sure, we do not claim to be laboring at
the top of the tree, but amongst the roots.
[new column]
We are helping to lay the foundations of
society; and that our work be well done is
a primary matter.
	There is one object that has been impres-
sed on my mind lately, and to which I
respectfully call your attention, and that is
the subject of religious and moral training
in our schools. I would ask, can any system
of education be acceptable to this people,
that ignores religion or morality, or that
dwarfs these essential elements? Our
schools should be opened by prayer to God.
We were all born in lands favored with the
Bible, we have been taught from our infancy
that “the fear of the Lord is the beginning
of wisdom,” and shall our common schools
be the first to ignore this sentiment?
	To reverence the Supreme Being should
be one of the first lessons imparted by the
parent to the child. The precept of the
parent should be perpetuated and indorsed
by the teacher, that the educational founda-
tion may be laid aright. Man’s nature is
complex. That man has a religious nature
is illustrated by the fact that where the
true religion does not exist humanity will
be found bowing to idols. Man has an in-
tellectual, a physical and a moral nature.
	Are we not apt to narrow in our educa-
tional ideas, and to give undue weight to
intellectual culture? Cultivate the intellect
and neglect the heart and you may usher
into the world an intellectual monster. As
has been frequently observed, some of the
greatest scourges of our race have been men
of powerful and cultivated intellects. That
“knowledge is power” none will gainsay, and
that it is equally potent for evil as for good
all will readily admit. Where the intellectual
qualities have been cultivated but to enable a
mortal to inflict misery upon his fellows,
ignorance would Indeed have been bliss.
Any educational system that fails to give
due prominence to religious and moral
training is defective. Education has been
defined to proper training of the “whole
man.” If he were endowed with a physical
nature only, all he would need would be
physical training. On the other hand if he
were a purely intellectual being, Intellectual
culture would comprehend all that could be
included in a perfect education.
	It has been truthfully said that “the ex-
altation of talent, as it is called, above virtue
and religion, is the curse of the age.
Education is now chiefly a stimulus to learn-
ing, and thus men acquire power without
the principles which alone make it good.
Talent is worshiped; but if divorced from
rectitude, it will prove more of a demon
[-]han a god.”
	Education essays to elevate the child to
an honorable position in society, to assist in
making him a competent, intelligent and
virtuous citizen, a good patriot. If this be
the aim, it is palpable that too little stress
is often laid upon the moral and religious
training of our youth. It is patent to us all
that man is susceptible of fervent and per-
manent religious feelings. The child is in-
fluenced by hope and fear and early can be
made to judge of that which leads to do evil
and that which leads to do good. That he
has a conscience—a heart susceptible of
cultivation, is soon apparent. This leads us to
the conclusion that religious training should
be a part of his education. “Train up a
child in the way he should go and when he
is old he will not depart from it.” This is
the counsel of the Almighty. Can webet-
ter it?
	Dr. Humphrey has well said:—“A more
Utopian dream never visited the brain of a
sensible man than that which promises to
usher in a now golden age by the diffusion
and thoroughness of what is commonly
[new column]
understood by popular education. With all
its funds, and improved school-houses, and
able teachers, and grammars, and maps,
and black-boards, such an education is
essentially defective. Without moral prin-
ciple at bottom to guide and control its
energies, education is a sharp sword in the
hands of a practiced and reckless fencer. I
have no hesitation in saying, that if we
could have but one, moral and religious
culture is even more important than a
knowledge of letters; and that the former
can not be excluded from any system of
popular education without infinite hazard.
Happily, the two are so far from being
hostile powers in the common domain,
that they are natural allies, moving on
harmoniously in the same right line, and
mutually strengthening each other. The
more virtue you can infuse into the hearts
of your pupils, the better they will improve
their time, and the more rapid will be their
proficiency in their common studies. The
most successful teachers have found the
half hour devoted to moral and religious
instruction more profitable to the scholar
than any other half hour in the day, and
there are no teachers who govern their
schools with so much ease as this class.
Though punishment is sometimes necessary
where moral influence has done its utmost,
the conscience is, in all ordinary cases, an
infinitely better disciplinarian than the
rod. When you can get a scholar to obey
and to study because it is right, and from a
conviction of accountability to God, you
have gained a victory which is worth more
than all the penal statutes in the world, but
can never gain such a victory without lay-
ing great stress upon religious principle in
your daily instructions.”
		PEN AND SCISSORS. 
	A, flush of hope renders toil and trial
beautiful, as the sunbeams give a sparkle to
the winter’s frost.
	Sickly people, it is said, live longer
single than they would if married, and
healthy people live longer in pairs.
	Because a man does not see as you do is
not a proof that he is blind or near sighted;
and because he does not think with you on
all subjects Is no sign that he is a fool. The
eyes of the pigeon answer its purposes per-
fectly, but would not do for the hawk; and
why should a cow eat raw meat in prefer-
ence to corn or cabbage because a tiger
considers it a better diet?
	It is the wealthy and independent who
scoff at poverty; never the learned and wise
who mock at crude simplicity; never the
pure and good who evince no charity for
frail, erring humanity. And none but the
exceedingly ignorant are guilty of these
things.
	A Nautical School, presided over and con-
ducted by a lady, who has herself, within
the last ten years, educated in Navigation
and Astronomy, and fitted for active service
at sea, more than two thousand young men
—the most of whom were officers in the U.
S. Navy during the rebellion—is now in ac-
tive operation in the Rooms of the New York
Port Society, on the corner of Madison and
Catharine Streets, under the supervision of
Mrs. Capt. Brownlow—late Principal of the
N. Y. Nautical School, established by her
father, Capt. William Thomas, more than
twenty years ago.


﻿Six simultaneous applications for divorce
emanate from one family in Maysville,
Kentucky.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[For	the Exponent.	
	“AND MURDERS INCREASE IN THE
				LAND.” 

	Almost every passing day brings to our ears
some fresh recital of blood and crime, which
fills us with sickening horror. Every year
adds hundreds to the list of Cains, accursed
of God and abhorred of men.
	No man can say he is safe from the ball
and steel of the assassin, and neither matron
nor maid can divine how soon she may fall a
victim to redhanded violence. Even the
infant in its cradle is not exempt from this
terrible doom.
	Our criminal cells are full of felons; the
sword of justice hurries hundreds uncalled
into eternity; and still the awful cry ascends
that earth is reeking with the blood of her
children, which blood cries out for redress.
	Whence comes this demon of ferocity and
disregard of the sacred rights of human
life? Why is the fair page in the history
of human life thus foully blotted? Who are
responsible for the fierce and sanguinary
impulses which prompt one frail mortal to
take the life of another?
	Oh, woman! Oh mothers! when you see
a darkbrowed, bloodstained criminal led
forth to answer the question addressed to
Cain—“Where is thy brother?” does relent-
less conscience accuse you of attempting
to take that man’s life before he saw the
light of day? Did you meditate release
from the restraint of his presence by raising
your hand against the life of the helpless
being whose only safeguard against your
cruelty, was the care of Him who forgets
not the least of His creatures?
	If so, then, mother, hide your face in
shame and wring your hands in despair; for
at your door, that responsibility lies. You
have deliberately given your child over to
perdition. You have steeped his soul in
crime and violence, and planted in him the
deadly seeds of rashness. Your dark and
criminal meditations, even if they never
assumed the magnitude of actual attempts,
have been impressed upon his plastic mind
and have given form to the motive powers
which govern his walk in life; and tears of
efface shed by you in after years, could not
blood, their blighting influence.
	When the man is born into the world,
you have no more power to shape his origi-
inal disposition; you may, indeed, with
much watchfulness and care, cultivate and
modify his character, but the leading traits
will ever remain to bless or curse the world
and himself.
	Oh, woman! though so frail in body
and seeming, how mighty! how lasting! is
your influence! Peeress, almost of God
Himself in shaping the destinies of this
world, through the dispositions you implant
in the children of men, think not your
sphere is narrow nor your influence unfelt.
	You may through prayer and watchful-
ness and strong determination, give your
child a soul and system unmarred by one
motive to crime, firm in integrity, upright
in every thought and deed, a blessing to his
kindred, his country and even the age which
gave him birth; or you may indulge in dark
thoughts and evil motives, fully assured
that, since from a child which is yet part of
yourself you can keep no secrets, every dark
design and crooked purpose will be mirrored
in thesensitive organization of the little
one, to bear bitter fruit in the years to come,
when your influence will be utterly unfelt,
or the grave separates you from him, leav-
ing him entirely at the mercy of his own
hateful passions.
	Then, mothers, pause and consider, for
one moment, the magnitude of the power
[new column]
and responsibility resting upon you. Re-
member, each one, you are arbitress of the
destiny of an immortal being; and try by
every means in your power to plant germs of
integrity and purity, instead of death and
destruction. Cultivate your own mind and
heart in the knowledge and admiration of
all things lovely and of good report, that
the blessing may descend to those you leave
behind you.
	Never even contemplate the possibility of
yielding your consent to a wrong, no matter
how small, if you would have your child
lead a blameless life; but above all things,
never, NEVER think of freeing yourself from
the incumbrance of an expected child; for
if you succeed your hands are stained with
innocent blood; and if you fail, your child
may grow up with dwarfed soul and
deformed principles, to be a source of an-
guish and despair to all who love him, and,
in a moment of madness, he may lift his
hand against the life of a fellow-creature;
thus adding one more horror to the blood-
curdling cry of “Murder!!”
							L. L. D.
Beaver, April 4th, 1873.


FACTS AND FIGURES.   
 			——
 CHICAGO now has a population of 380,000.
 FORTY-nine operas failed in Italy last
year.
 THE Shah of Persia is building a new ten-
acre palace.
 TWENTY-one editors have recently been
elected Mayors in Illinois.     
 BROADWAY, N. Y., is to be widened at a
cost of $3,800,000.
 SENATOR Pomroy owns 120,000 acres of
land. 	
 DES MOINES is to have a $50,000 opera
house. 	
 No fewer than 175 almanacs for 1874 have
been published in Paris.
 THE new United States Hotel at Saratoga,
will have a mile of piazzas.
 MICHIGAN has 148 legislators, not one of
whom was born in the State. 
 OHIO GIRL students play base ball, and
the newspapers talk of it. Well? 
 TEXAS is improving rapidly.   She has
944 convicts in her penitentiary now,
against 21 in 1862.
 SIX members of the German royal family
make up an aggregate stature of thirty-six
feet and two inches.
 LADY Burdett Coutts supports a sewing
school of two hundred pupils in Spitalfields,
and a night school in Shoreditch.

Woman's Exponent.
Vol. I. SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MAY 15, 1873.	No. 24.


﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.
	The Mormon question—How many
wives has he?
	The Pope still lingers in feeble health,
not expected long to survive.
	A Rhode Island man is under arrest for
the horrible crime of kicking his wife to
death. Was this worse than brute worthy
to have a wife!
	Mark Twain declares he would go
hungry two days rather than eat an old
personal friend. Such friendship should
be appreciated!
	The Tower of Babel style, is the name we
propose fora kind of head-dress now in vogue.
It rises toward heaven, but its owners will
never climb there by it.
	Fears of a general Indian war over the
west are widely felt, and may possibly be
realized if the cry for the extermination of
the aborigines continues and is sustained by
the country.
	Where women have the right to vote in
school districts, schools increase and grog-
geries decrease. The moral is obvious. Let
there be more schools and fewer dens of in-
temperance.
	The motto of the Good Templar girls of
Salem, New Jersey, is worth recording in
golden letters, and being hung up for gen-
eral admiration and adoption. It is: “The
lips that touch wine shall never touch
mine.”
	Official dishonesty has come to be
looked upon, in this country, as a rather
commendable kind of “smartness,” and as a
consequence, the public have frequent op-
portunities of applauding and rewarding
public robbers.
	A bridge gave way at a baptism at Dix-
on, near the Illinois State line, and precipi-
tating a crowd of people into the river, over
fifty of whom were drowned or killed, is
one of the latest horrors reported; and “no-
body to blame,” as usual.
	Two police captains of New York have
been represented to Mayor Havemeyer as
having pecuniary interests in gambling estab-
lishments. They will probably be promoted
to judgeships or be sent as Governors to
some of the Territories.
	A Marriageable young lady in Iowa
was lately locked in her room by her
father, but her lover got her out by a writ
of habeas corpus, and married her. That’s
what comes of understanding “business.”
More young men should go to studying law.
	Historians and novelists of this country,
will find their stores of foundation matter
for the present period largely increased
from the incidents recorded of the fierce and
bloody “war” with the Modocs. But small,
indeed, are the gains in such instances,
compared with the losses.
	The late claims of the women of Massa-
chusetts, and their petition to the Senate
and House of Representatives of the com-
monwealth of that State, to have plurality
of wives recognized and legalized, in order
that the surplus women may be provided
with homes and husbands, as it is every
woman’s right to be, shows that in this as
well as other matters, the intelligent and
[new column]
thoughtful women of the day, are, without
violating any order or law, superceding
the sterner sex.
	Robert B. Rosevelt holds the ladies
responsible for a drag on legislation on great
days, when they crowd the galleries of
Congress and “certain members air their
rhetoric just to let the ladies see what they
can do;” and he thinks this should be
remedied by the ladies visiting the halls of
Congress more frequently; a remedy which
many ladies would consider a good one.
	Civil war is actually raging in Louisiana,
as a consequence of there being two govern-
ments in the State, one supported by the
United States government, the other by the
majority of the people of the State. Armed
bands of men, supplied with artillery, are
in the field, and bloody contests are of
almost daily occurrence. Is this the begin-
ing of another tribulation?
	A writer from Paris describes a meet-
ing of French women, where she says a mob
was at the entrance, and as a woman rose to
speak, a chair was hurled at her; and as
another woman rose, chairs were hurled
over the heads of the audience. We have
read of the extraordinary politeness of the
“Messieurs,” but was scarcely aware of its
consistency being so similar to that of Chris-
tian purity.”
	It has been proved, by scientific re-
searches, that the perfume of flowers over-
comes noxious vapors that are often in the
atmosphere, especially the exhalations from
marshy grounds or decomposing animal
matter. In other words, they are healthful,
and add to life as well as minister to the
enjoyment of the senses. Plant flowers,
then, for their beauty; and perfume flowers
for their fragrance; and you will increase
the general health as a result.
	Great men are dropping off suddenly.
Chief Justice Chase, a truly great man, a
“pillar in the State of solid form,” a just
judge, a wise statesman, and a firm upholder
of the Constitution of our country, died in
New York last week; and his funeral ser-
vices were performed in Washington on
Monday, 12th inst. He will long be re-
membered as one of the most conservative
men in the government, at a time when the
centralizing tendencies of the age were
rapidly stripping the American people of
their sovereign rights reserved by them in
the Constitution. John Stuart Mill, the
eminent English political economist and
advocate of woman’s rights, also died last
week at Avignon, France; having left be-
hind him, in his works, a memory that will
be enduring.
	It has been discovered that chalk can be
used as fuel, by placing it in a grate with
alternate layers of coal. If, as some people
imagine, the coal supply of the globe is
rapidly becoming exhausted, some substitute
must be found, but the quantity of chalk in
the world, so far as known, would not meet
the demand for a great length of time. In
burning coal the inhabitants of the earth
now are using up the “preserved sunbeams”
of past ages, the heat then poured upon
vegetation yielding warmth and becoming
useful now, as that vegetation, in the shape
of coal, is returned to the elements by being
[new column]
consumed by fire. But people need not
worry themselves over the prospect of the
world’s being thrown back into barbarism
because of the failure of the supply of fuel;
the same beneficent Hand that formed the
coal beds from the flora of prehistoric ages,
can furnish another supply should this be-
come exhausted.
	One of the saddest things we have seen
recorded for a long time was the case of a
boy fifteen years of age, arrested and impris-
oned in New York the other day, on the
charge of being drunk, yet who had not
taken liquor. On an investigation of the case
it was discovered that before the boy was
born his father had been a habitual but
secret drunkard, and when the fact was
made known to the wife it almost deprived
her of reason. Her child was born, and
grow up bearing all the outward appearances
of being a drunkard though rigidly temper-
ate. He has the air, the manners, the look,
and the reeling gait of one intoxicated, and
when excited stammers, and speaks in
broken words, like a drunken man. Thus
his life has been blasted and his earthly ex-
istence cursed by his father’s sin. It should
be added, however, that the father, when
he learned how terribly the news of his
degradation affected his wife, made and
kept a resolution of reform, became an
altered and temperate man, and is now the
father of a family of bright, intelligent
children—all but the fated first-born.


﻿FRUIT FOR CHILDREN.

	Fruits have been marked as the special
enemies of childhood—the things to he
avoided at that age above all the rest.
Farmers, unable to keep their children out
of their orchards, have been known to cut
down their fruit-trees so as to place the
pernicious things beyond their reach; and
nothing is more common than to hear men
and women predicting that “the season is
going to be sickly because there is so much
fruit.” It would be difficult to conceive of
any notion more absurd than this; and yet
it has had for many years the sanction of
the highest medical authority. One of the
precepts that have come down to us from
the mostancient school of medicine founded
in Christendom contains a warning against
the enjoyment of these rich gifts of Provi-
dence—“Autumni fructus caveas, no sint
tibi luctus”—and well and faithfully has the
precept been inculcated by the successors of
the faculty of Salernum.  Instinct and
reason, however, have begun at last to pre-
vail over the heresy, and now physicians
generally when asked an opinion, say, “Eat
all the fruit you want—the more the better.”
And, indeed, nothing could be more sensible
than this answer. I am not sure the sense
of languor and lassitude so generally felt in.
the Spring is not owing to the want of fruit,
from which probably results a state of the
system bordering on scurvy.  By sub-acid
fruits and the vegetables of Summer, that
scorbutic tendency is corrected, and in
Autumn, as a consequence, the acme of
health is attained. On this account I should
place foremost among the culinary inven-
tions of our times the art of preserving
fruits in their fresh state all the year round.
—“Home and School."


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿[For the Exponent.
			TO ERNEST.

My own, these laughter-loving days, whose feet
Should gaily trip to sunshine’s merry horn,
Whose golden eyes should wake with smiles so sweet,
Till raptured eve from rosy, dewy morn,
Are all obscured with clouds so dim and gray,
Whose dun and trailing robes and falling tears
So over-shadow all my lonely way.
That hours seem surely length’ning into years.

The silv’ry tinkle of rain-cymbals overhead
(So like the patter of fairy feet in dreams,
Which o’er their smooth, green circles lightly tread,
While on the revels Luna softly beams,)
Fills my rapt soul with thoughts of that sweet night
When hand in hand, we wandered forth afar,
When only stars smiled out to give us light,
But benedictions beamed from every star.

Where no eye saw save His who reigns above,
In the hush and silence of that holy hour,
Apart in soul from all else living, Love,
Where God in Nature reigned in peace and power,
We lowly bowed before th’ Eternal throne
(But one in spirit, though two hands were joined)
And purely, truely, you confessed your own
True love and humbly sought the gift of mine.

You plead for guidance for our troubled souls—
O’er our coming lives a Father’s kindly care.
And that, when dread temptation darkly rolls, 
Our steadfast faith might keep our purpose fair.
The pure petition fell like summer rain
Upon the thirsty fields when parched and gray;
And doubts and fears that on my soul had Iain
Like cliffs of terror, melted soft away.

The waves of dread and trouble in my breast
Fell gently, ’neath that holy trusting prayer
Like some tired infant to its evening rest, 
And Peace spread over me her pinions fair.
Our souls were mingled then for evermore,
Like twin rills meeting in the mighty sea;
And still as one we’ll seek the shining shore
Of that fair life we call Eternity.
								Lu Dalton.


R. S. REPORTS.
	Report from Provo: The sisters in the
4th Ward Relief Society are faithful in the
discharge of their duties, and many of them
impart of their substance until I almost
think the receiver fares better than the
giver. Our meetings are not so well
attended as I could wish but the “faithful
few” seem to possess a good spirit and enjoy
our communion together. I believe the
Relief Societies have had a great influence
in uniting the hearts of the sisters and of
doing away, in some degree, with that ex-
clusiveness which has been too much in the
midst of the Saints. We have invested in
Co-operative institutions where we have a
small but perpetual benefit in dividends;
but in consequence of the many poor in our
Ward we have not been able as yet to build
a house but hope to do so in time.
		Repectfully yours,
		  Ruia A. Holden, President.

	The following is a brief synopsis of the
minutes of the 57th meeting of the Senior
and Junior Co-operative Retrenchment As-
sociation of Salt Lake City, held May 10th,
1873, at the 14th Ward Assembly Rooms.
	After singing and prayer, minutes of the
previous meeting were read and accepted;
minutes of meetings held in the 9th, 10 and
11th Wards were also read. The spirit of
the meeting was to impress upon the minds
of the sisters the importance of each one
seeking diligently to carry out the com-
mandments of the Lord, as given in words
of counsel and instruction by President
Brigham Young. They were admonished
to take interest in saving their paper rags
and jointly assisting in commencing and
maturing the plan suggested by President
Young, of making their own books. The
Exponent was also spoken of, and the
sisters were urged to renew their subscrip-
tions—those who had taken the first volume
—and those who had never subscribed for
[new column]
it, were advised and solicited to do so.
The subject of writing was introduced, and
the fact illustrated that practice and cultiva-
tion in composition would prove that true
talent and genius existed with many of the
daughters of Zion.  Sympathy and appro-
bation were expressed in relation to the
course being pursued by the women of
Lowell, Massachussets.  Faith and prayer
were spoken of as safe guards against
temptation and sin, and requisite auxiliaries
in accomplishing whatever was undertaken.
The speakers were President Mrs. Mary I.
Horne, Mrs. W. East, Counselors Mrs. B.
W. Smith, E. Howard and S. M. Kimball,
Mrs. Nancy Kennedy, Mrs. Leaver and Mrs.
Eleanor J. Pratt.
	“Medicine” was chosen as a subject for
the next meeting. Meeting adjourned; dis-
missed by singing and prayer.
			Maggie M. Groo, Secretary.
				 
	From the 12th Ward, Salt Lake City,
May 8, 1873:
	The 12th Ward Relief Society was organ-
ized April 29th, 1868, with Mrs. Priscilla
Staines, President, Mrs. Amanda Smith and
Mrs. Ruth Sayers Counselors. On April 7th,
1870, Mrs. Sayers was obliged to resign, on
account of ill health, and Mrs. Emily P.
Young was chosen to fill her place. The
Society is in a flourishing condition, although
but few in comparison attend our meetings.
The teachers are faithful in visiting on
their various blocks, instructing the sisters
and gathering their offerings; and we are
happy to state that the people of the Ward
have responded liberally to whatever call
has been made.  In looking over the books
I see that the disbursements for the poor and
other charitable purposes were for 1868, in
merchandise, $184:98; for 1869, $354:45; for
1870, $232:82; for 1871, $370:05; and for
1872, $399:25; with a balance on hand of
$105:55; making in all of cash and merchan-
dise, $1,647:10.  At a meeting held in the
Ward school house, May 1st, 1873, Counselor Mrs.
Amanda Smith made a motion that we drop the
word “Female” from the name of our Society, in
accordance with the views of our beloved Sister,
Miss E. R. Snow. Also that a report of the finan-
cial state of our Society be made out and forwarded
to the Exponent for publication; both of which
were carried unanimously. Our meetings are held
the first Thursday of every month at 2. p. m.
		   Mrs. Priscilla Staines, Pres.
			" Amanda Smith, }	Counselors.
			" Emily P. Young, }
			" H. A. Hardy, Secretary.
			" Sophia L. Hardy, Treasurer.

	 We are favored with the following from Spring
City, Sanpete County: Our Society was organized
May 10th, 1868, with Mrs. Mary Ann Hyde,President;
Mrs. Elizabeth Allred, first Counselor, Mrs. Ann
Larson, second Counselor, Mrs. Philindia Benson,
Secretary and Mrs. Mary Ann Frantzen, Treasurer.
Having suffered by Indian depredations, as well as
by being sorely scourged by the ravages of grass-
hoppers, and being a small settlement, we have not
accomplished as much as some others under more
favorable circumstances. We have at present $125
in the co-operative store; in the treasury $70;
some grain and other articles; besides a goodly
portion of building material towards building a
house. We have donated to the poor at home,
emigration and other public calls, during the last
year, $182:27. This is not a full account of all
that has been done, for some accounts have been
lost in changing Secretaries.  The word “Female,”
has been withdrawn from the name of the Society
by unanimous vote.
	Our President, Mrs. Hyde, is a faithful Saint,
striving to her utmost to carry out all the duties
which devolve upon her.
	We have suffered severely from the spotted
[new column]
fever; quite a number have died from its effects,
some twenty within the last six months.
	Wishing success to the Exponent,
		Respectfully,
 				Mrs. P. Benson, Secretary.

	This comes from Bountiful, May 2d, 1873:
Having been rather dilatory in forwarding
a report of this Branch of the Relief Society,
we will now endeavor to be as brief as pos-
sible. The Society was organized in 1868,
by Bishop Stoker and his Counselors, as
follows: Elizabeth H. Barlow, President,
Lucinda Sessions and Mary Jane Crosby,
Counselors; Mary Carter Secretary and Cor-
delia Carter, Treasurer. During the five
years the Society has been organized we
have received in cash $564:19; in merchan-
dise, $1,318:09 1/2. Disbursed to the poor
in cash $322:90; in goods, $1,025:07 1/2; have
helped the P. E. Fund; have shares in the
Co-operative store and design building a
hall for the benefit of the Society, which we
have already commenced. We feel it a
satisfaction to know that we can be co-
workers together with and lighten the care
of our brethren, in helping to roll forth the
kingdom of God; and that it is woman’s por-
tion to be full of charity, seek out the
oppressed, the poor, the widow and the
orphan, imparting to them of her substance
—and binding up the broken heart. We
have a great interest in the Exponent—
		Through which we rejoice to hear,
		From our sisters far and near;
		Stimulating ev’ry heart.
		To press on and bear a part.	
	Elizabeth H. Barlow, President.
	Mary Carter, Secretary.

	From Marriottsville, near Ogden: The
Relief Society of Lynn and Marriottsville
was organized April 21st, 1870. Since that
time it has disbursed to the poor of the two
settlements, $103;30; to the Emigration
Fund, $100; has also assisted in building the
Ladies’ Store in Ogden City, donated to the
Marriottsville Sunday School, has at present
two shares (ten dollars) in the Ladies’ Store
and $30 in the Treasury. On the 21st and
22d ult., we celebrated the anniversary of
our organization; held meetings both days,
well attended and favored with the pres-
ence and kind, cheering instructions of
our brethren; after which, we concluded
the little season of social enjoyment with a
"party" at  which good order and harmony
prevailed. We had previously voted unan-
imously to adopt the change of name for the
Society, omitting the word "Female." We
admire and highly appreciate the “Woman’s
Exponent” and design doing all we can in
sustaining that and every good cause.
			Ann Bickington, President:
			Lydia Gates, } 
			Nancy Tracy } Counselors:
			C. Parry, Secretary;


Agnes Goulding, for a number of years
matron of the home for discharged female
prisoners at Dedham, has been appointed
matron of the Clark Institution for deaf
mutes, at Northampton, and enters upon her
duties in June.	
	Carrie N. Forsythe, of Chicago, daughter
of Charles S. Forsythe of Illinois, deceased,
has brought suit in the Circuit Court,
against the National Life Insurance Company
of Chicago, placing her damages at $6,000.
From the bill filed it appears that the
deceased was insured in the “Commercial
League” of the company, which guaranteed
an indemnity of $4,000 at least, but although
proof of death has been duly submitted and
the funds demanded, the company has
refused to pay over the same.


WOMAN'S. EXPONENT.


[For the EXPONENT.
WORKING MEN AND WORKING WO-
			   MEN.
			    ——
— "O! Oh!” says Madam Grundy, “the
idea of our girls setting themselves up to be
some great big “I’s,” and attempting to be-
come ‘business women!' It's perfectly
absurd—it is awful!”
	Why, auntie, has not a woman the same
right to be a business individual that a man
has? She was created like him to live upon
the earth; like him she must eat and drink
and be provided with a habitation wherein
to dwell, and material wherewith she may
be clothed. Is it less honorable in her
to take proper measures to obtain, fairly
and justly, the little essentials of life, or even
luxuries, if she desires them, than to be
dependent on man, or apparently so, for
whatever her fancy or reason may require?
“Ah, but,” says madam, “she amply pays
for all she gets,if she stays at home and
attends to her household affairs with pro-
priety. There is no necessity for a woman
ever to leave the house to find employment;
there is always abundance of work to be
done in every family.” There you are
quite right, Mrs. G., and further, no girl
should be brought up without a knowledge
of house-keeping and cookery. But all this
does not prove that no woman should learn
and practice any suitable trade, art or pro-
fession. In our common schools, girls as
well as boys are required to study mathe-
matics and other branches which are but
little benefit to them, if all they are to be
allowed to think of is preparing meals and
keeping the house in order. But it is their
lawful and just privilege to progress in
knowledge and worth in every form, prac-
tically as well as mentally; and, as men do,
make choice among whatever legitimate
courses present themselves, of the one most
acceptable for gaining—not alone a liveli-
hood—but influence and recognized utility
in society as well.
	“Yes,” argues the old lady, “but it is so
out of place, so improper, for women to be
associated with men in business transactions,
as they inevitably must be, in order to
become acquainted with and successfully
carry on any of the employments which
have been so long controlled by men; such
as clerking, working in printing establish-
ments, etc.” Is it, then, to be considered a
disparagement to either, for men and women
to be in the society of each other? Yet
men who are habitually associated with
women of refined natures, are themselves
superior to other men. They lose their
rude, declamatory and pedantic ways, and
become more chivalric, courtly and elegant
in mind and heart, as in manner and address.
The woman, too, who becomes accustomed
to the society of educated, honorable men,
so that she is at ease and enjoys freedom of
thought and speech in such associations,
becomes also superior to her sex in general,
and to her former self. Her better senses
are awakened; she finds, by continued
conversation with the enlightened of
the opposite sex, that she possesses high
and noble faculties which brighten into
richness and glowing worth under the
influence of intellectual competition; and
grace and dignity take the place of frivolity
and timidity, without changing in the
smallest degree the delicate, chaste, and
gentle peculiarities of her woman's nature.
Did not the Eternal and Allwise Creator,
say it was not good for man to be alone, and
therefore made for him a help mate in the
form of woman?   Wherefore, then, should
there be so great a distinction placed be-
[new column]
tween man. and woman, since they were
created for each other?
	The cause of working men .and working
women being embarrassed and awkward in
the society of the opposite sex, is simply
force of habit. The rich man’s sons and
daughters, who give much of their time
to pleasure and amusement, are continually
brought in contact with companions of both
sexes. Hence, their superior grace and
seeming intellectual powers. The children
of laborers are less-connected and gain less
self-confidence; and then it is thought that
they are less gifted and naturally more
sluggish in mind. But they must not seek
to break the barrier which restrains them—
O no! it is “absurd” for a working woman to
undertake to establish for herself any thing
but the “humdrum” drudge life Madam
Grundy has been, pleased to mark out for
her. If she attempts to advance in the
scale of practical education any higher than
to have an understanding of how to knit and
sew, or scrub and cook, she is growing
“strong minded,” and that is “awful,”
	But after all, work must be done; and in
reality, a woman who will and must work,
has as just a right to work by the side of a
man, as another who will not work and
is able to live without it, has to walk, ride,
dance or sit on the sofa beside one.  Men
and women were not made to dwell separate,
but in the society of each other.	G.


﻿PETITION FOR POLYGAMY.
	
	The following petition has been prepared
and signed by a large number of ladies in
Lowell, Massachusetts, to be presented to
the Legislature of that State. The petition
has already appeared in a number of lead-
ing Eastern journals, the first to give it
place being the New York Graphic, from
which it was clipped into the Chicago Times
and other papers:
				Lowell, April—,1873.
To the Honorable the Senate and House of
		Representatives of the Commonwealth
		of Massachusetts:
	The undersigned, citizens of the State,
respectfully set forth the grievances under
which they suffer as women who are not
permitted to vote, hold any and all offices,
and engage in the occupations opened to
men, and are otherwise restricted in the
opportunity of earning a living, and here-
with beg your permission to suggest a
remedy for these evils, upon which they
pray your honorable body to act.
	The law which now governs society says,
practically, that women should be married,
should engage in work at their own homes,
and should look to their husbands for sup-
port. On the other hand, the census shows
that it is impossible to carry out this un-
written but recognized law, for the reason
that there is a large excess of women in the
commonwealth, and many of the men of
lawful age are idle, vicious, incompetent, or
otherwise unfit to be heads of households.
Yet your petitioners hold that the matter
is not without remedy. Prejudice and cus-
tom has decided in favor of restricting the
husband to a single wife, yet without justice
and authority, as we believe. In the Book
which lies at the foundation of all law recog-
nized in this country, there is no injunction
against a plurality of wives, while there are
many examples therein recorded in its
favor. Men’s wives appear to have in-
creased in number in proportion with their
flocks and riches. Such a rule even now
holds in the land from which the Christian
nations received their religion.
	Your petitioners have no desire to inter-
fere with the regulations of any existing
[new column]
household, but simply to present their claims
to the marriage state for your respectful
consideration. They deem it their privilege
and their duty to suggest the marriage of a
man to more than one wife, in cases where
the first wife does not object, and where it
is made evident that the man is able to sup-
port the burden laid upon his resources.
They are aware that it takes years to remove
prejudices, and that those who take a second
or third place in the household may be looked
upon with disfavor; but confident that their
proposed action will ultimately do away
with much of the social evil which afflicts
and distresses all communities, they are
willing to be the first to engage in this work
of reform. Society which now insists that
woman shall be married and look to her
husband for support, will, after mature
reflection, counteract this effort to carry out
the law practically.
	It is far from the design of your peti-
tioners to ask legislation in behalf of free
love or any loosening of the marriage bond.
We ask that the marriage of the second wife
shall be made as permanent and binding
as that of the first, and that all the children
of the household shall have equal honor.
And in asking this, we believe we have
taken a long stop toward doing away with
foundling asylums, preventing ante-natal
murders, and lessening the vagabond child
population of our large cities.
	For the reasons enumerated your peti-
tioners respectfully ask the passage of a law
permitting plural marriages in the cases
above enumerated, and under such other
conditions as to your judgment may seem
wise and proper.
	And your petitioners will ever pray, etc.


[For the Exponent.
		IMPORTANCE OF DECISION.

	Is it better to spend half a life-time
contemplating what is best to do, than to make
up your mind to some laudable course of
conduct and make a bold attempt to reach
the mark you set out for, even though it be
not the highest on record, and, even though
you come short of gaining the exact point
you started for? I think not. True, “it is
better to move moderately than to move
wrong;" and it is well to “look before you
leap;” but indecision holds many a man from
achieving the most praiseworthy results of
high and noble ambition. A young man
realizes that he might become a first class
architect, or a superior workman of any
kind; he would like the trade, has the
abilities and, probably, there is nothing in
the way to interfere with his commencing
to learn at once. But it will require time
and patience and practice to acquire a
thorough knowledge of it, such as he would
need, if he undertook it, and possibly
something else may be presented that will
be a little easier and pay better just now—
the future stands away off and can be looked
after when it gets here. He will wait a year
or two, and look around. he does not
really know that the trade would suit him,
it might not be as much to his taste as he
imagines.  So the chance passes without
being improved, and the time slips away
and is lost. And thus people waste the
best and most useful days of their lives in
very many instances; and, nothing being
decided upon, nothing is accomplished.
	There is no necessity for taking what
seems to be a wrong step in order to move
in some direction. The way chosen may
not always prove the wisest; but we may
dally in uncertainty half our lives, for fear
of running a dangerous risk, and then make
the worst of mistakes when we are too old to
rise above and outlive them. 	Mignona.
